advanced_techniques.description: Elevating CO2 from ambient (400ppm) to 1000-1500ppm increases photosynthesis rate by 20-40% when light and temperature are also increased.

methods.notes: Most common. Tank refills every 2-4 weeks for a tent.
Produces heat and humidity — better for large rooms with AC.
Produces ~100-200ppm boost. Only useful for small sealed spaces. Not worth it for most.

advanced_techniques.name: CO2 Supplementation

ppfd_co2_correlation.notes: Ambient CO2 sufficient at low light
Slight benefit from supplementation
Moderate benefit — cost-effective threshold
Strong benefit — diminishing returns above this
Maximum practical benefit for most grows
Commercial intensity — requires perfect environment

requirements: Sealed grow space (CO2 escapes quickly from leaky rooms)
High light intensity (>600 PPFD — plants can't use extra CO2 without sufficient light)
Higher temperature tolerance (82-86°F optimal with CO2 vs 75-80°F without)
CO2 controller or timer

advanced_techniques.description: Manipulating environmental and irrigation parameters to push the plant toward vegetative or generative (flowering/fruiting) growth.
advanced_techniques.name: Crop Steering
advanced_techniques.notes: Crop steering is most effective in hydro/coco where irrigation frequency is highly controllable. Soil growers have less precision due to soil buffering.

vegetative_steering.goal: More leaf/stem growth, larger plant framework

best_practices: Spray during lights-off or first/last 30 minutes of light cycle (stomata open, no light burn)
Use fine mist sprayer — coverage is key
pH foliar solution to 6.2-6.5 (leaf surface slightly higher pH than root zone)
EC should be very low (0.2-0.5) — leaves burn easily
Never foliar spray during flower (moisture in buds → mold/bud rot)
Best for micronutrient deficiency correction (CalMag, iron, magnesium)

common_foliar_products.frequency: Weekly preventive, 3-day intervals for active infestations

advanced_techniques.description: Spraying nutrient solution directly onto leaves for rapid uptake. Bypasses root zone entirely.
advanced_techniques.name: Foliar Feeding

advanced_techniques.description: Strengthens cell walls, increases stem thickness, improves heat/drought/pest resistance.
important_notes: ALWAYS add silica to water FIRST before other nutrients (raises pH significantly)
Let silica mix for 5-10 minutes before adding base nutrients
Silica is incompatible with concentrated nutrient solutions — never mix undiluted
Discontinue in late flower (last 2-3 weeks)
advanced_techniques.name: Silica (Potassium Silicate)

advanced_techniques.description: Introduces beneficial bacteria/fungi to the root zone to outcompete pathogens, improve nutrient uptake, and protect against root diseases.
important_notes: Do NOT use hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) with beneficial microbes — it kills them
Choose either sterile (H2O2) OR beneficial (Hydroguard) approach — not both
Beneficials need warmish water (65-75°F) to thrive
Reapply after any reservoir change or system cleaning
advanced_techniques.name: Beneficial Microbes & Root Inoculants

products.name: Hydroguard
products.notes: The standard for DWC/RDWC. Add at every res change.

products.dosing: 1 tsp/gal (water-in) or dust roots at transplant
products.name: Great White
products.notes: Best for soil/coco. Less effective in pure hydro.

products.name: Recharge
products.notes: Feeds existing soil microbes. Great for living soil.

products.name: Mammoth P
products.notes: Most effective in flower when P demand is highest.

advanced_techniques.description: Removing select fan leaves to improve light penetration, airflow, and direct energy to bud sites.
advanced_techniques.name: Defoliation (Strategic Leaf Removal)
rules: Never remove more than 20-30% of leaves at once (except schwazze technique)
Only defoliate healthy, vigorous plants — never stressed ones
Autoflowers: very light defoliation only — they can't recover from heavy removal
Always leave the top 2-3 fan leaves on each branch (they feed the bud below)

timing.method: Remove lower leaves that get no light (lollipop)
Major defoliation day 1 and day 21 of flower (schwazzing)
Selective tuck or remove leaves blocking bud sites

equipment.name: Mist nozzles (anti-drip preferred)
equipment.notes: 1-2 per plant depending on root zone coverage. Anti-drip nozzles prevent continuous dripping between cycles. HPA: Tefen/Dramm fogger nozzles (50-micron droplets). LPA: standard mist nozzles.

equipment.name: High-pressure pump (HPA) or submersible pump (LPA)
equipment.notes: HPA: diaphragm pump rated for 80-100 PSI continuous duty. LPA: submersible pump rated for 30-50 PSI. Size for total nozzle flow rate + 20% headroom.

equipment.name: Accumulator tank (HPA only)
equipment.notes: HPA ONLY. Pre-pressurized tank that delivers instant pressure on demand. Prevents pump cycling every mist event. Size: 1-2 gallons for most home setups.

equipment.name: Solenoid valve(s)
equipment.notes: Electrically actuated valve controlled by timer. Opens for mist, closes between cycles. Must be rated for your operating pressure. Food-safe materials. Normally-closed type (fails safe = no mist, not continuous mist).

equipment.name: Cycle timer (SECOND resolution)
equipment.notes: NON-NEGOTIABLE. Must handle seconds on / minutes off. CT-2 cycle timer, Arduino/ESP32 controller, or commercial aero controller. Standard minute-resolution timers CANNOT run aeroponics. Day/night separate programming preferred.

equipment.name: Inline filter (fine mesh, 100-200+)
equipment.notes: Between pump and nozzles. Catches precipitates before they reach nozzles. This alone prevents 80% of nozzle clogs. Clean weekly minimum. HPA: finer mesh (200+).

equipment.name: Pressure gauge
equipment.notes: Monitor system pressure. Pressure drop = clogged filter, failing pump, or leak. Essential for diagnosing problems before they become emergencies.

equipment.name: Pressure regulator
equipment.notes: Maintains consistent pressure regardless of pump output variations. More important for HPA. LPA can often operate without one.

equipment.name: Spare nozzles (20-30% of total)
equipment.notes: NON-NEGOTIABLE. More spares than any other method because nozzle failure is more catastrophic. Keep them within arm's reach of the grow. A clogged nozzle is a 5-minute emergency.

equipment.name: Root chamber (light-tight, food-safe)
equipment.notes: Must be 100% light-tight. Any light = algae = clogged nozzles. Food-safe plastic or stainless steel. Drain at bottom for runoff return to reservoir. Size for your root mass — roots grow FAST in aero.

equipment.name: Neoprene collars / net pot lids
equipment.notes: Seal around plant stems in the chamber lid. Must block ALL light. Neoprene is flexible and reusable. Have extra sizes for different stem diameters.

equipment.name: Root chamber thermometer
equipment.notes: Monitor root zone temperature continuously. Target 65-68°F. Above 72°F = pathogen risk. Below 60°F = slow growth. Wireless sensor is ideal.

equipment.name: Reservoir (opaque)
equipment.notes: Light-proof. Catches drain-back from root chamber. Size: minimum 2-3 gallons per plant. Aero reservoirs cycle faster than DWC — larger is more stable.

equipment.name: Air pump + air stone (for reservoir)
equipment.notes: Oxygenates nutrient solution in reservoir. Helpful but less critical than in DWC — the mist process itself oxygenates the solution.

equipment.name: Water chiller (strongly recommended)
equipment.notes: Keeps reservoir and root chamber cool. 65-68°F target. Strongly recommended for any climate where ambient temps exceed 75°F. In aero, warm roots = dead roots faster than any other method.

equipment.name: UPS / battery backup for pump + timer
equipment.notes: NON-NEGOTIABLE. A 5-minute power outage kills aero plants. UPS must power pump and timer for minimum 30-60 minutes. This is not optional — it is survival equipment.

equipment.name: Backup pump
equipment.notes: Pre-plumbed or ready to swap in under 5 minutes. If the primary pump fails, you have minutes, not hours. Test the backup monthly.

equipment.name: Audible alarm (pump failure / pressure loss)
equipment.notes: Pressure switch + buzzer that alerts you if pressure drops below operating threshold. For unattended grows, this can save the entire crop.

equipment.name: pH meter (accurate to 0.1)
equipment.notes: Calibrate weekly. Aero pH range is 5.5-5.8 (tighter than DWC). Bluelab or Apera recommended.

equipment.name: EC/TDS meter
equipment.notes: Aero EC is lower than other methods. Precise measurement matters more. Calibrate monthly.

equipment.name: Jeweler's loupe (60-100x)
equipment.notes: Trichome inspection during late flower/harvest.

equipment.name: Grow light (LED preferred)
equipment.notes: Size for canopy. 30-40 watts/sq ft LED. Dimmable for stage-appropriate PPFD.

equipment.name: Exhaust fan + carbon filter
equipment.notes: Odor control and air exchange. Size for room volume.

equipment.name: Oscillating fan(s)
equipment.notes: Canopy airflow. Don't blow directly on buds during flower.

equipment.name: Temperature/humidity controller
equipment.notes: AC Infinity or Inkbird. Climate control is critical — especially with a root chamber adding humidity to the room.

equipment.name: Dehumidifier
equipment.notes: Root chamber adds significant humidity to the grow space. Dehumidifier is essential during flower. Size for your room + root chamber moisture output.

equipment.name: Base nutrient system (Flora Trio or equivalent)
equipment.notes: SYNTHETIC ONLY for aero. No organic nutrients — they clog nozzles. Flora Trio, Athena Pro, Jacks 321. Clean-dissolving formulas.

equipment.name: CalMag supplement
equipment.notes: Recommended for all aero grows. Clean synthetic CalMag only.

equipment.name: pH Up/Down solutions
equipment.notes: Maintain pH 5.5-5.8. Tighter range than DWC.

equipment.name: Hydroguard (beneficial bacteria)
equipment.notes: Root zone protection. Essential in aero — warm, humid root chambers are Pythium incubators.

equipment.name: Hydrogen peroxide (3%)
equipment.notes: Nozzle cleaning, line flushing, chamber sanitization. Use between grows and for emergency cleaning.

flush_timing.coco: 7-14 days reduced nutrients → plain water last 3-5 days
flush_timing.notes: Flushing is debated — some studies show no difference. At minimum, reduce nutrients in final week.
flush_timing.soil: 14-21 days plain water (soil holds nutrients longer)

other_harvest_indicators: Most pistils (hairs) have darkened and curled in
Calyxes are swollen and dense
Fan leaves are yellowing and falling off naturally
Trichome stalks are visible and heads are enlarged
Breeder's recommended flowering time has passed

trichome_ratios.notes: Harvest early for more THC-dominant, less CBN. Energetic, racy high. Slight reduction in yield.
Standard harvest window. Mix of THC and CBN. Full-spectrum effects.
Extended harvest for max CBN conversion. Heavy body high. Good for pain/sleep. Slightly degraded THC.

detection: One plant wilting while others fine
immediate_response: Swap nozzle with backup immediately
Manually mist affected roots with spray bottle while fixing
failure_modes.prevention: Inline filter. Weekly soak. Keep 2x backup nozzles ready.

immediate_response: Switch to backup pump immediately
If no backup: submerge roots in bucket of nutrient water (emergency DWC conversion)
Mist manually with spray bottle
failure_modes.prevention: Backup pump wired to float switch or pressure sensor. UPS on pump circuit.
failure_modes.time_to_damage: 5-15 minutes in hot/dry conditions, 30-60 min in cool/humid

detection: Pump running normally but nozzles dry
immediate_response: Manually open solenoid (override)
Swap solenoid
Run continuous mist until fixed
failure_modes.prevention: Quality solenoids rated for cycling. Spare on hand. Test monthly.
failure_modes.time_to_damage: Same as pump failure — no mist reaching roots

immediate_response: UPS kicks in (if installed)
Manually mist roots every 5 min with spray bottle
Cover root chamber to trap humidity (buys time)
failure_modes.prevention: UPS rated for pump wattage × 2 hours minimum. Generator for extended outages.

cons: Zero failure tolerance (roots dry in minutes)
high_pressure_aeroponics.description: True aeroponics — atomizes water into fine mist. Maximum oxygen exposure to roots.

low_pressure_aeroponics.best_for: Hobbyists, first-time aero growers, budget builds
low_pressure_aeroponics.description: Uses standard pump with spray nozzles. Larger droplets — technically 'fogponics' or spray culture.

early_flower.notes: Peak mist demand. Roots are massive.

early_veg.notes: Increasing frequency as root mass develops.

flush.notes: Maximum misting with plain water to flush salts.

late_veg.notes: Aggressive root growth demands more frequent misting.

seedling.notes: Very light misting. Roots just emerging. Too much = stem rot.

day_vs_night.nighttime: Reduce frequency by 30-50%. Example: 5s on / 180s off at night vs 5s on / 120s off during day.
day_vs_night.reasoning: Transpiration drops at night. Roots need less water. Over-misting at night = root rot risk.

perfect: Roots hang freely with fine branching

cleaning_protocol: Soak in white vinegar or pH 3.0 solution for 1-2 hours
Use ultrasonic cleaner if available (best method)
Clear orifice with soft bristle brush or compressed air (never wire — damages orifice)
Test spray pattern before reinstalling — should be even cone/fan
Replace if spray pattern is uneven after cleaning
clog_prevention: Inline filter (50-100 micron) between pump and nozzles — MANDATORY
Use synthetic nutrients ONLY (no organics — they clog nozzles immediately)
Reservoir filter sock over pump intake
Weekly nozzle soak in pH-down solution (citric acid) or vinegar to dissolve mineral deposits
Run plain water flush cycle for 5 minutes after every reservoir change
Keep backup nozzles ready — swap and clean offline rather than downtime
nozzle_management.inspection_schedule: Visual check daily. Remove and soak weekly. Replace every 2-3 grows or when spray pattern degrades.

anti_drip.notes: Stops dripping between cycles. Cleaner operation.

ceramic_disc.notes: LPA only. Larger droplets but very clog-resistant.

ec_targets.notes: LOWER than all other hydro methods. Fine mist = high absorption efficiency. Less is more.

nutrient_approach.organic_warning: NEVER use organic nutrients in aeroponics. Organic particles clog nozzles within hours. No exceptions.
nutrient_approach.particle_filtration: 100-micron inline filter minimum. 50-micron preferred for HPA. Clean/replace filter weekly.
nutrient_approach.reservoir_change_frequency: Every 5 days in veg, every 3-4 days in flower. Aero systems have smaller reservoirs that concentrate faster.

root_chamber_design.drain_design: Sloped floor (1-2% grade) toward drain port. No standing water — roots must hang freely in air.
root_chamber_design.humidity_inside: 95-100% during mist cycles. Drops between cycles (this is the oxygen exposure window).
root_chamber_design.light_exclusion: 100% light-proof. ANY light leaks = algae on roots. Use black containers with white/reflective exterior.
root_chamber_design.material: Food-safe plastic (HDPE). No metal (corrosion). Smooth interior (easy to clean, no biofilm anchors).
root_chamber_design.sizing: Minimum 12 inches vertical space below net pots for root development. 18-24 inches preferred for large plants.

temperature.notes: Chamber temp tends to be cooler than ambient due to mist evaporation (evaporative cooling effect).

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Micro → Gro → Bloom (always Micro first)
nutrient_brands.name: General Hydroponics Flora Trio

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Part A → Epsom → Part B (dissolve each fully before adding next)
nutrient_brands.name: Jack's 321
nutrient_brands.notes: Jack's 321 is 3.6g Part A, 2.4g Cal Nit, 1.2g Epsom per gallon at full strength. Extremely cost-effective for large grows.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Core → Bloom
nutrient_brands.name: Athena Pro Line
nutrient_brands.notes: Commercial-grade. Simple 2-part with integrated CalMag. Popular in commercial cannabis facilities.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: A → B (separate for veg and bloom)
nutrient_brands.name: Advanced Nutrients pH Perfect (Sensi)
nutrient_brands.notes: Self-adjusting pH technology (claims no pH adjustment needed). Expensive but popular for beginners who struggle with pH.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Shake VERY well before use (thick concentrate settles). One bottle at a time.
nutrient_brands.name: General Hydroponics FloraNova
nutrient_brands.notes: Simplest option — one bottle per phase. Great for beginners. Shake well — concentrate separates.
nutrient_brands.type: 1-part (two bottles — one for veg, one for bloom)

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Dissolve powder in warm water, stir well
nutrient_brands.name: Greenleaf Nutrients Mega Crop
nutrient_brands.notes: One powder does everything — includes CalMag. Incredibly cost-effective at ~$0.02/gal. Community favorite on r/microgrowery.

environment.container: Glass mason jars (quart size). Fill 75% full.

humidity_packs.boveda_58: For drier preference or long-term storage.
humidity_packs.boveda_62: Maintains 62% RH. Set-and-forget. Replace when crispy.
humidity_packs.grove_bags: Heat-sealed bags with built-in humidity regulation. No burping needed.

indicators_of_good_cure: Smooth, flavorful smoke — no harshness or hay smell
Buds are slightly sticky but not wet
Strong terpene aroma when jar is opened
Ash burns to white/light gray (not black)
method: Trim buds to final shape (dry trim or finish wet trim)
Place in mason jars — fill 3/4 full to allow air exchange
Week 1: burp 2-3x daily for 10-15 minutes (release moisture and gases)
Week 2-4: burp 1x daily for 5-10 minutes
Month 2+: burp 1x weekly or seal completely
Cure for minimum 2 weeks, ideal is 4-8 weeks. Some strains improve for 6+ months.

common_mistakes: Drying too fast (>3 days) — harsh smoke, hay smell, terpene loss
Drying too slow (>14 days) — mold risk, diminishing returns
Fan blowing directly on buds — uneven drying, crispy exterior/moist interior
Temperature too high (>70°F) — terpene evaporation, harsh taste

environment.airflow: Gentle circulation — fan NOT pointed at buds. Just keep air moving.

method: Cut branches 12-18 inches long
Optionally remove large fan leaves (wet trim) or leave intact (dry trim)
Hang upside down on line/rack with 6+ inches between branches
Small branches should snap (not bend) when done — 5-7 days minimum
Stems should snap cleanly; if they bend, keep drying

long_term_storage.degradation: THC slowly converts to CBN over time. Light, heat, air, and moisture accelerate degradation.
long_term_storage.method: Sealed mason jars or vacuum-sealed bags with Boveda 58% in dark, cool place
long_term_storage.notes: Properly cured and stored cannabis can remain excellent for 12+ months. Freezing is debated — can damage trichomes if not done carefully.

failure_response_times.description: In aeroponics, every failure is time-critical. Know your response windows.

nozzle_clog.action: Replace nozzle immediately. Have spares within arm's reach.

power_outage.action: UPS should engage automatically. If no UPS: hand-mist roots immediately.

pump_failure.action: Switch to backup pump. If no backup, hand-mist roots with spray bottle while diagnosing.

solenoid_stuck_open.action: Continuous mist suffocates roots over time. Close valve manually. Replace solenoid.

timer_failure.action: Run pump manually on short cycles while replacing timer.

golden_rules: Nozzle check EVERY day. A clogged nozzle = dead plant in minutes.
Backup pump and UPS are NON-OPTIONAL. Not recommended — required.
Root chamber must be 100% light-tight. Any light = algae = clogged nozzles.
Root zone temp 65-68°F. Above 72°F = pathogen risk. Above 75°F = root death.
SYNTHETIC nutrients ONLY. Organic nutrients clog nozzles within days.
Spare nozzles within arm's reach at ALL times. 20-30% of total count.
Timer must handle SECONDS, not minutes. Standard timers cannot run aero.
Read your roots: glistening = perfect, dripping = too wet, dry = emergency.
Aero EC is lower than every other method. What's half-strength in DWC is full-strength in aero.
Power failure = 5-10 minutes to root damage. UPS is survival equipment.

hpa_vs_lpa.description: High-Pressure vs Low-Pressure Aeroponics — fundamentally different systems.

hpa.best_for: Experienced growers, commercial operations, maximum performance
hpa.cons: Most complex, most expensive, most fragile, smallest margin for error
hpa.equipment: Diaphragm pump, accumulator tank, solenoid valves, fine-mesh filters
hpa.pros: Maximum oxygenation, fastest growth, highest efficiency

lpa.best_for: Home growers, first-time aero, still want aero performance
lpa.cons: Larger droplets = less oxygenation, slightly slower growth than HPA
lpa.equipment: Submersible pump, standard mist nozzles, basic filter
lpa.pros: Simpler, cheaper, more forgiving, still excellent growth

mist_cycle_by_stage.description: Mist timing is the core of aeroponics. These are starting points — adjust based on root observation.

nozzle_maintenance.check_frequency: Daily minimum. Twice daily during flower.
nozzle_maintenance.cleaning_method: Soak in 3% H2O2 for 30 min. For mineral deposits: pH 3.0 citric acid soak 1 hour.
nozzle_maintenance.description: Nozzle management is the #1 maintenance task in aeroponics.
nozzle_maintenance.no_organics: NEVER use organic nutrients in aero. They clog nozzles within days. Synthetic only.
nozzle_maintenance.replacement_schedule: Replace all nozzles every 1-2 grows even if they appear functional. Degraded nozzles produce larger droplets.
nozzle_maintenance.spare_rule: Keep 20-30% spare nozzles on hand at ALL times. More than any other spare part.

quick_reference.nutrient_mixing_order: 1) CalMag → 2) Flora Micro → 3) Flora Gro → 4) Flora Bloom → 5) pH adjust. NO silica unless proven safe with your nozzles. NO organics.

ph_range.notes: Tighter pH range than DWC/drip. Aero mist absorption is pH-sensitive. Stay in the sweet spot.

quick_reference.reservoir_change_schedule: Every 5-7 days. Aero reservoirs are smaller and cycle faster. pH/EC drifts faster than DWC.

root_observation_guide.brown_slimy: Pythium / root rot. Treat with H2O2 flush, increase Hydroguard, check chamber temp.
root_observation_guide.description: Your roots tell you everything. Learn to read them.
root_observation_guide.dripping_wet: Too much mist. Increase off-time. Roots need air exposure.
root_observation_guide.dry_papery: EMERGENCY. Roots are drying. Decrease off-time immediately. Check for clogged nozzle.
root_observation_guide.glistening_moist: Perfect. Roots are coated in fine mist droplets. Ideal state.
root_observation_guide.matted_tangled: Root mass too dense for nozzle coverage. Reposition nozzles or add nozzles.
root_observation_guide.white_fluffy: Healthy. Dense, branching root system with fine root hairs visible.

change_schedule.top_off: Daily with plain pH'd water when EC rises. With dilute nutrient when EC drops.
change_schedule.top_off_rule: EC rising = plants drinking more water than nutrients → top off with plain water. EC dropping = plants eating more nutrients than water → top off with dilute nutrient mix.

reservoir_management.description: Reservoir management for active hydroponic systems (DWC, RDWC, NFT, Ebb & Flow, Drip, Aeroponics).

dissolved_oxygen.why_important: Roots need oxygen to function. Low DO → root rot, nutrient lockout, slow growth.

sizing_guidelines.notes: Larger reservoirs = more stable pH/EC, less frequent changes, more forgiving of mistakes.

beneficial.cons: Slightly cloudy water (normal). Doesn't work above 75°F water.
beneficial.pros: Continuous protection. Outcompetes pathogens. Set-and-forget per res change.

sterile_vs_beneficial.recommendation: Beneficial approach (Hydroguard) for most growers. Sterile only if you have persistent issues.

sterile.cons: Must reapply every 2-3 days. Kills beneficial microbes too. Degrades in hours.
sterile.pros: Kills all pathogens. Clean roots. No biofilm.

water_temperature.too_cold_risk: Slowed metabolism, nutrient lockout, stunted growth
water_temperature.too_warm_risk: Root rot, low dissolved oxygen, pathogen growth

scale_tiers.description: First-time grower or single-plant hobbyist. Manual management, learning-focused.
scale_tiers.name: Solo / Single Plant
scale_tiers.notes: Hands-on learning experience. Manual checks 1-2x daily. Ideal for beginners.

scale_tiers.description: Hobbyist tent grow. Light automation helpful but not required.
scale_tiers.name: Small Tent (2-8 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Timer-controlled lights, humidity controller. Sensor monitoring recommended.

scale_tiers.description: Dedicated grower with multiple tents/rooms. Automation saves significant time.
scale_tiers.name: Multi-Tent (9-24 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Multiple environments to manage. Automated alerts essential. Consider perpetual harvest.

scale_tiers.description: Licensed commercial operation. Full automation, compliance tracking, batch management.
scale_tiers.name: Commercial Room (25-100 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: SOPs required. Staff training. Regulatory compliance. Insurance requirements.

scale_tiers.description: Large-scale production facility. Enterprise automation, SCADA integration, dedicated staff.
scale_tiers.name: Warehouse Scale (100+ plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Multiple rooms, dedicated HVAC, industrial nutrient dosing, video surveillance.

common_problems.cause: Too cold, too wet, or bad seed
common_problems.solution: Ensure 75-80°F. Starter medium should be moist not soaking. Try a different seed after 7 days.

common_problems.cause: Clogged or defective nozzle, or pressure variation
common_problems.solution: Replace uneven nozzles now. Clean all nozzles (H2O2 soak). Verify pressure is consistent across all zones. Fix before plants go in.

common_problems.cause: Using a minute-resolution timer meant for drip or ebb/flow
common_problems.solution: Get a proper aero timer: CT-2 cycle timer, Arduino/ESP32 controller, or commercial aero controller. Minute-resolution timers cannot run aeroponics.

stages.description: Seed cracks open and taproot emerges. For aeroponics, start seeds in neoprene collars with Rapid Rooters or rockwool cubes inside a humidity dome — NOT in the aero chamber yet. Seeds need consistent moisture, not mist cycles.

environment.notes: Keep seeds in darkness. Heat mat at 78°F. No light until sprout emerges. Humidity dome over propagation tray. Do NOT put seeds in the aero chamber.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse germination: use heat mat and humidity dome. Root chamber temperature will be challenging to maintain in a greenhouse — plan insulation and cooling.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse aero: root chamber temp control is the primary challenge.

environment_overrides.notes: Aeroponics outdoors is NOT recommended. Temperature control of the root chamber is nearly impossible. If you must: germinate indoors.

outdoor.notes: Outdoor aeroponics is extremely challenging. Germinate indoors regardless.

health_checks: Is the taproot visible and white?
Is the starter medium moist but not soaking?
Is the aero system pressure-tested and leak-free?

mist_system.notes: Aero system NOT running yet. Germinate seeds off-system. While seeds germinate: pressure-test the entire system, verify all nozzles spray evenly, check for leaks, test timer/solenoid cycling.

stages.name: Germination

nutrients.approach: None — seeds contain all energy needed to germinate.

tasks.description: Soak seeds 12-24 hours in plain pH 6.0 water, then place in Rapid Rooters or rockwool cubes inside neoprene collars. Place in humidity dome.
tasks.name: Start seeds

tasks.description: After 24-72 hours, look for white taproot emerging. Do not disturb the seed.
tasks.name: Check for taproot

tasks.description: While seeds germinate: run the entire mist system. Check every nozzle for spray pattern, every fitting for leaks, every solenoid for cycling. Fix ALL leaks now — you cannot fix them with plants installed.
tasks.name: Pressure-test aero system

tasks.description: Aeroponics requires second-resolution timers (not minute-resolution). Test that the timer can do 3-5 seconds ON and 3-5 minutes OFF reliably. Cycle it 50+ times to verify consistency.
tasks.name: Verify timer resolution

tasks.description: Test the backup pump/power supply/UPS. Simulate a power failure and verify the backup engages within 60 seconds. In aeroponics, backup is NOT optional.
tasks.name: Test backup systems

tasks.description: Close the chamber, turn off room lights, then shine a flashlight around every seal and fitting from outside. Any light leak = future algae problem = clogged nozzles.
tasks.name: Check root chamber light-tightness

common_problems.cause: Off-time too long, or nozzle not reaching this plant's roots
common_problems.solution: Shorten off-time by 30-60 seconds. Verify nozzle spray pattern covers all root zones. Reposition nozzle if needed. In aero, dry roots = dead roots within minutes.

common_problems.cause: Off-time too short, or nozzle dripping between cycles
common_problems.solution: Increase off-time by 30-60 seconds. Check for dripping nozzles (anti-drip nozzles solve this). Roots need air exposure between mists — that's the entire point of aeroponics.

common_problems.cause: Root chamber too warm (>72°F), Pythium, or light leak causing algae
common_problems.solution: Check chamber temp — cool below 72°F. Add Hydroguard. Check for light leaks. Brown tips in the first week = environmental problem. Address immediately.

common_problems.cause: Neoprene collar too large or stem too thin
common_problems.solution: Use smaller collar insert. Pack a small piece of rockwool around the stem inside the collar. The collar MUST support the plant and block light.

stages.description: Seedling develops first true leaves. Transfer seedlings in neoprene collars into the aero chamber once roots are 1-2 inches long. Start with gentle, frequent mist cycles — seedling roots are fragile and need consistent moisture. LPA is more forgiving at this stage than HPA.

environment.notes: Gentle light. Seedling roots are just entering the mist zone. Keep mist cycles frequent — roots must NEVER dry out. If roots look dry between mists, increase frequency.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: insulate root chamber. Greenhouse temps can spike, overheating the root zone rapidly.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse aero: insulate chamber, consider chiller.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor aero seedlings: root chamber temperature is nearly impossible to control. Insulate the chamber heavily. Have a chiller.

extra_problems.cause: Direct sun on chamber
extra_problems.solution: Shade the chamber completely. Insulate. Use a chiller. Root zone above 75°F = root death in aero.

extra_tasks.description: Wrap root chamber in reflective insulation. Direct sun on the chamber will cook roots. Shade the entire chamber.
extra_tasks.name: Insulate root chamber

outdoor.notes: Outdoor aero: chamber temperature control is critical and difficult.

health_checks: Roots glistening between mist cycles (not dry, not dripping)?
No slime or discoloration on roots?

day_cycle.notes: 5 seconds on, 3 minutes off during lights-on. Seedling roots are short — keep them wet.

night_cycle.notes: 5 seconds on, 5 minutes off at night. Reduced transpiration at night means roots stay wet longer.

mist_system.notes: Frequent, short mist bursts. Roots should glisten with moisture between mists — never dry, never dripping. Observe roots after installing seedlings: if tips dry out before next mist, shorten the off period. Root chamber temp 65-72°F is critical for oxygen absorption.
mist_system.nozzle_type: Anti-drip mist nozzles preferred. Standard mist nozzles acceptable for LPA.

stages.name: Seedling

nutrients.approach: Very low strength. Aero roots absorb nutrients 3-5x more efficiently than submerged roots. What would be quarter-strength in DWC is full-strength in aero. Start very light.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root protection. Aero root chambers are warm and humid — ideal for Pythium. Hydroguard is essential.

tasks.description: Insert neoprene collars with seedlings into the chamber lid. Roots should hang 1-2 inches into the mist zone. Ensure the collar seals around the stem — no light leaks.
tasks.name: Transfer seedlings to aero chamber

tasks.description: Program timer for 5s ON / 180s OFF during day, 5s ON / 300s OFF at night. Run and watch the first 3-4 cycles. Verify all nozzles are misting the root zone evenly.
tasks.name: Start mist cycles

tasks.description: Open the chamber between mist cycles and check root moisture. Roots should be glistening, not dripping, not dry. Adjust off-time up or down based on what you see.
tasks.name: Observe root moisture between mists

tasks.description: Root zone temp must be 65-72°F. Above 72°F = low dissolved oxygen in mist, pathogen risk. Below 60°F = slow growth. Use a chiller or chamber insulation as needed.
tasks.name: Check root chamber temperature

tasks.description: Verify ALL nozzles are spraying. Even one clogged nozzle means one plant's roots are drying. In aero, this is a 30-minute emergency, not a 2-day problem.
tasks.name: Nozzle check

tasks.description: Small aero reservoirs swing fast. Check pH/EC daily. Top off with plain pH'd water to maintain volume. Full change every 5-7 days.
tasks.name: Check reservoir pH/EC

transition_signals: Roots 4-6 inches long and bright white

common_problems.cause: Root chamber too warm, or mist cycle not optimal
common_problems.issue: Root growth slowing despite good top growth
common_problems.solution: Check chamber temp — must be under 72°F. Adjust mist timing. Consider adding a small fan inside the chamber for air circulation (controversial but effective in some setups).

common_problems.cause: Partial clog, or pressure dropped below atomization threshold
common_problems.issue: Nozzle producing stream instead of mist
common_problems.solution: Clean nozzle immediately. Check pump pressure. For HPA: verify accumulator is charged. A stream instead of mist dramatically reduces oxygen in the root zone.

common_problems.cause: Light leak somewhere in the chamber
common_problems.solution: Find and seal the light leak immediately. Algae clogs nozzles and creates slime on roots. Clean chamber with H2O2. Re-seal all joints with opaque tape or silicone.

common_problems.cause: Silica precipitating in nutrient solution or nozzle
common_problems.solution: Reduce or eliminate silica. If using: add first, pH down, wait 30 min, then add other nutrients. Clean nozzles more frequently. Some growers skip silica entirely in aero — the fast growth compensates.

stages.description: Rapid root and leaf expansion. Aeroponics produces the fastest vegetative growth of any method — roots can grow 1-2 inches per DAY when conditions are optimal. Mist cycles can begin to lengthen (more off-time) as the root mass increases and retains moisture better. This is when aero's advantage becomes visible.

environment.notes: Ramp up light intensity. Aero plants grow FAST — be ready for rapid vertical growth. VPD management matters: higher VPD = more transpiration = roots absorb mist faster.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: root chamber insulation and possibly a chiller. Greenhouse temps spike fast.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse aero: chiller recommended for summer months.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor aero veg: hot days will overheat root chamber. Chiller is mandatory, not optional.

extra_problems.cause: Ambient temperature warming chamber
extra_problems.solution: Chiller, insulation, shade structure. If chamber consistently exceeds 75°F, aeroponics outdoors may not be viable in your climate.

extra_tasks.description: Peak outdoor temps can push root chamber to 80°F+ which is lethal for aero roots. Check at 2-3 PM daily.
extra_tasks.name: Monitor root chamber temp in afternoon heat

outdoor.notes: Outdoor aero: viable only with excellent temperature control.

health_checks: All nozzles spraying fine mist (not streams)?
Roots bright white and branching rapidly?
Rapid new leaf growth visible daily?

day_cycle.notes: 3 seconds on, 5 minutes off. Root mass is larger now and retains moisture better. Shorter on-time = finer mist coat (better oxygenation).

night_cycle.notes: 3 seconds on, 8 minutes off at night. Reduced transpiration. Roots stay wet longer. Longer off-time prevents waterlogging.

mist_system.notes: Shorter on-time, longer off-time compared to seedling stage. The growing root mass retains more moisture between cycles. Observe roots: they should go from glistening (just after mist) to slightly damp (just before next mist) — NOT dry. If roots dry before the next cycle, shorten the off-time.
mist_system.nozzle_type: Anti-drip mist nozzles. For HPA: Tefen or Dramm fogger nozzles.

stages.name: Early Vegetative

nutrients.approach: Still low by other-method standards. Aero roots absorb mist nutrients ultra-efficiently. EC 0.6-0.8. What looks like half-strength in DWC is full feeding in aero.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root zone protection. Non-negotiable in aero.

supplements.name: Silica (Armor Si)
supplements.purpose: Strengthen rapidly growing stems. Add FIRST before other nutrients. WARNING: silica can precipitate and clog nozzles — use low dose and monitor.

tasks.description: Transition to 3s ON / 5min OFF day cycle. Observe roots for 2-3 cycles after adjusting. If roots dry before next mist, reduce off-time. If roots are dripping, increase off-time.
tasks.name: Adjust mist cycle

tasks.description: EVERY DAY. Check every nozzle. In veg, plants can survive a clogged nozzle for a few hours. In flower, minutes. Build the daily check habit now.
tasks.name: Daily nozzle inspection

tasks.description: Aero reservoirs are typically smaller and swing faster. Check daily. pH 5.5-5.8 for aero (slightly lower than DWC for better mist absorption).
tasks.name: Check reservoir pH/EC

tasks.description: Aero roots grow fast. Check that roots are not hitting the chamber bottom or tangling in nozzles. Trim if necessary (rare).
tasks.name: Monitor root growth rate

tasks.description: The filter between pump and nozzles catches precipitates. Clean it weekly minimum. A clogged filter = reduced pressure = poor atomization = poor growth.
tasks.name: Clean inline filter

tasks.description: Run plain pH'd water through the system for 5 minutes to clear any nutrient buildup in nozzles. Do this before every reservoir change.
tasks.name: Flush nozzles

training.description: Begin bending and tying down branches. Aero plants grow FAST — start training early or they'll outgrow your space. 4-5 nodes is ideal to start.
Top above 4th or 5th node. Aero recovery is extremely fast — expect 2-3 day recovery vs 5-7 in soil. The plant barely notices.

common_problems.cause: Aero veg is 30-50% faster than other methods — common mistake to veg too long
common_problems.issue: Plant too tall already (grew faster than expected)
common_problems.solution: Flip now. Supercrop tall branches. Next grow: flip earlier. Aero plants need less veg time than you think.

common_problems.cause: Roots grew into the nozzle zone
common_problems.solution: Gently untangle. Consider repositioning nozzles or using root guides/barriers. Roots blocking nozzles = uneven mist distribution.

common_problems.cause: EC too high for aero efficiency — plant is getting more than it can use
common_problems.solution: Reduce EC by 0.2-0.3. Aero doesn't need as much nutrition as other methods. Err on the side of less — you can always increase.

stages.description: Plant reaches target size before flip. Aero plants grow so fast that veg time is often 30-50% shorter than other methods. The root mass is now substantial — mist off-time can be extended further. Final system checks before flower: clean all nozzles, verify backup systems, prepare for the highest-demand period.

environment.notes: Full veg light intensity. Plants may be significantly larger than expected — aero veg is fast. Flip earlier than you would with other methods (plant at 33-50% of target height vs 50-66% for other methods, because aero stretch is more aggressive).

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: light dep gives control over flip timing.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse aero: light dep recommended for photoperiod control.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor aero: natural photoperiod triggers flowering. Aero stretch outdoors can be extreme — plan for it.

outdoor.notes: Outdoor: flip timing is nature's decision.

health_checks: All nozzles freshly cleaned and spraying fine mist?
No algae or biofilm in chamber?
Canopy level and ready for flip?

day_cycle.notes: 3 seconds on, 6 minutes off. Large root mass retains moisture well. Longer off-time = more oxygen to roots.

night_cycle.notes: 3 seconds on, 10 minutes off at night. Plant transpires less at night. Roots need less frequent misting.

mist_system.notes: Extended off-times. The root mass is substantial and retains moisture between cycles. More off-time = more oxygen exposure = faster nutrient uptake. But never let roots go fully dry. Pre-flip: clean ALL nozzles, flush lines, verify backup systems.

stages.name: Late Vegetative

nutrients.approach: Half strength by other-method standards. EC 0.8-1.2. Aero absorption efficiency means you need less. Watch for tip burn — it comes fast in aero.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root protection throughout veg.

tasks.description: Remove and clean ALL nozzles. Soak in H2O2 (3%) for 30 minutes. Rinse and reinstall. You do NOT want a nozzle failure during flower — fix everything now.
tasks.name: Pre-flip nozzle cleaning

tasks.description: Simulate power failure. Verify backup pump/UPS engages within 60 seconds. Test backup timer. In flower, a 5-minute mist failure can damage roots permanently.
tasks.name: Test backup systems

tasks.description: Fresh reservoir with transition nutrients. Clean reservoir and pump intake. Replace inline filter if needed.
tasks.name: Pre-flip reservoir change

tasks.description: Aero plants grow fast — final topping, LST, SCROG net filling. Flip at 33-50% of target height (aero stretch is aggressive).
tasks.name: Canopy management

tasks.description: Check for tangling, browning, or slime. Trim any dead root tips. Healthy aero roots are brilliant white and fluffy (not matted).
tasks.name: Root mass inspection

tasks.description: If accessible: wipe down chamber walls with H2O2. Remove any biofilm or mineral deposits. Last chance before flower.
tasks.name: Deep clean root chamber

training.description: Install screen at canopy height. Weave branches through. Fill 70-80% of squares before flipping. Aero plants fill the net faster than other methods.
Remove lower 1/3 of growth. Redirect energy to top canopy.

common_problems.cause: Aero root systems drive more aggressive stretch than other methods
common_problems.solution: Supercrop immediately. Raise lights to maximum height. Increase negative DIF (cooler days, warmer nights temporarily). Next grow: flip earlier.

common_problems.cause: High nutrient concentration or biofilm in lines
common_problems.solution: EMERGENCY. Replace nozzle immediately. Run backup mist for affected zone. Clean all nozzles within 24 hours. Check inline filter.

common_problems.cause: Explosive growth consuming massive amounts of water
common_problems.solution: Normal for aero stretch. Top off 1-2x daily with plain pH'd water. Consider a larger reservoir. Automated float valve is ideal.

common_problems.cause: Rapid growth demanding more calcium than supplied
common_problems.solution: Increase CalMag to 2-3 ml/gal. Brown spots on new growth = calcium deficiency. Very common during stretch in all methods, especially aero due to growth speed.

stages.description: Light cycle flipped to 12/12. Explosive stretch — aero plants can TRIPLE in height during stretch (more aggressive than other methods due to the massive healthy root system). Mist cycles need careful adjustment: the plant is transpiring heavily and roots are demanding more nutrients. This is the most dangerous period for nozzle failures.

environment.notes: Flip to 12/12. Temperature DIF (day-night difference) of 10°F helps control stretch. Aero stretch is MORE aggressive than other methods — plan for 2-3x height increase.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: light dep tarps give photoperiod control. Pull consistently.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse: light dep recommended.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor: natural photoperiod triggers stretch. Light pollution is a hermaphrodite risk — audit the area.

extra_tasks.description: Any external light during the dark period risks hermaphrodites. Especially critical for stress-sensitive aero plants.
extra_tasks.name: Check for light pollution

outdoor.notes: Outdoor aero stretch: expect extreme height gain.

health_checks: ALL nozzles spraying fine mist (check twice daily)?
No light leaks during dark period?

day_cycle.notes: 4 seconds on, 5 minutes off. Increase on-time slightly — plant is transpiring heavily during stretch and needs more moisture. Heavy root demand.

night_cycle.notes: 3 seconds on, 8 minutes off at night. Night cycle stays similar to late veg.

mist_system.notes: Slightly more mist (longer on-time or shorter off-time) to support explosive growth. The plant is drinking heavily — reservoir level drops fast. Top off daily. Nozzle failures during stretch are CRITICAL — check twice daily.

stages.name: Transition (Stretch)

nutrients.approach: Transition formula. Shift from grow-heavy to bloom-heavy ratio. EC 1.0-1.4. Increase CalMag — stretch demands calcium for rapid cell wall construction.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root protection. Warm flower temps increase pathogen risk.

tasks.description: Ensure ZERO light leaks during dark period. Even brief light exposure can cause hermaphrodites. Aero plants are sensitive to stress.
tasks.name: Flip light cycle to 12/12

tasks.description: Stretch is the highest-demand period. A clogged nozzle during stretch = permanent damage to that plant's root zone within an hour. Morning and evening checks.
tasks.name: Nozzle check — TWICE daily during stretch

tasks.description: The plant is drinking heavily during stretch. Reservoir may drop significantly in 24 hours. Top off with plain pH'd water to maintain volume and prevent EC spike.
tasks.name: Top off reservoir daily

tasks.description: Shift from veg to bloom formula over 3-5 days. Increase CalMag for calcium-hungry stretch growth.
tasks.name: Transition nutrient ratio

tasks.description: Supercrop, tuck into SCROG net. Aero stretch is aggressive — stay on top of it daily.
tasks.name: Manage stretch height

tasks.description: Remove males immediately. Hermaphrodites from stress are possible — check every 2 days.
tasks.name: Check for preflowers / sex

training.description: Crush and bend tall stems 90°. Aero plants heal supercrop injuries in 2-3 days (vs 5-7 for other methods) due to the powerful root system.
Continue tucking into net daily. Aero growth is fast — miss a day and branches escape the net.

common_problems.cause: Chamber too warm, Pythium, or nozzle partially clogged (reduced mist coverage)
common_problems.issue: Root tips turning brown in flower
common_problems.solution: Check chamber temp (must be under 70°F). Increase Hydroguard. Check every nozzle. Brown roots in aero = emergency. Healthy aero roots are WHITE.

common_problems.cause: EC too high for aero efficiency — plant is getting more than it needs
common_problems.solution: Reduce EC by 0.2. Aero nutrient burn appears faster than in other methods because absorption is more efficient. Err on the side of less.

common_problems.cause: Humidity too high near the canopy (root chamber humidity escaping)
common_problems.solution: Ensure root chamber is sealed properly. Lower ambient humidity to 45%. Defoliate aggressively. Remove affected buds immediately.

common_problems.cause: Nutrient precipitate, biofilm, or mineral deposit
common_problems.solution: Replace immediately with spare. Clean all nozzles within 24 hours. Flush lines. Check inline filter. This is why spare nozzles are mandatory in aero.

stages.description: Stretch has ended. Buds forming and fattening. Mist cycle optimization is critical: slightly longer off-times give roots more oxygen, pushing energy into flower production (analogous to generative steering in drip). EC increases to support bud development. Nozzle reliability is now a SURVIVAL issue — a clog during flower development means permanent yield loss.

environment.notes: Peak light intensity. Humidity dropping to prevent bud rot. Day/night DIF of 10-12°F. CO2 to 1000-1200 ppm if supplementing.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: manage humidity aggressively. Root chamber adds significant moisture to the growing environment.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse aero: dehumidification critical during flower.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor aero flower: bud rot risk is extreme with moisture from root chamber + outdoor humidity. Seal chamber meticulously.

extra_problems.cause: Root chamber humidity + outdoor humidity + rain
extra_problems.solution: Seal root chamber perfectly. Cover plants during rain. Defoliate aggressively. Consider whether outdoor aero flower is viable in your climate.

extra_tasks.description: Moisture + outdoor humidity + aero root chamber moisture = bud rot cocktail. Shake plants and improve airflow.
extra_tasks.name: Shake plants after rain/dew

outdoor.notes: Outdoor aero flower: highest bud rot risk of any method/environment combo.

health_checks: Roots still white and fluffy (not matted or brown)?
Buds forming at all flower sites?
No bud rot or powdery mildew?

day_cycle.notes: 4 seconds on, 6 minutes off. Slightly longer off-time pushes generative response (more oxygen = more flower energy). Don't go too long — roots must never dry.

night_cycle.notes: 3 seconds on, 10 minutes off at night. Flower-stage night cycles can be extended as transpiration is minimal.

mist_system.notes: Generative misting: longer off-times give roots more air exposure, driving energy into flower production. This is the aero equivalent of dryback/crop steering in drip systems. But NEVER let roots dry — in aero, dry roots = dead roots in minutes. Monitor root tips: glistening = perfect. Dry = emergency. Dripping = too much mist.

stages.name: Early Flower

nutrients.approach: Full bloom formula. EC 1.4-1.8. Higher PK for flower development. Still lower than DWC/drip equivalent due to aero efficiency.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root protection throughout flower.

supplements.name: Liquid Kool Bloom
supplements.purpose: PK booster for early flower. Start conservative — aero absorbs supplements efficiently.

tasks.description: Increase off-time to 6 minutes day / 10 minutes night. This gives roots more oxygen, pushing energy into flowers. Monitor root tips — they must stay moist.
tasks.name: Extend mist off-time for generative push

tasks.description: A clogged nozzle during flower = permanent yield loss for that plant. Check every nozzle daily. Replace or clean any suspect nozzle immediately.
tasks.name: Nozzle check — daily minimum

tasks.description: Flower stage: keep root chamber at 64-70°F. Cooler roots during flower improve terpene production and bud density. Warmer roots = pathogen risk.
tasks.name: Root zone temp check

tasks.description: Remove large fan leaves blocking bud sites. Open up the canopy interior. Critical for bud rot prevention.
tasks.name: Defoliate for airflow

tasks.description: Dense canopy + high humidity in root chamber nearby = risk. Inspect flower sites every 2 days.
tasks.name: Check for bud rot / PM

tasks.description: Full reservoir change every 5-7 days during flower. Clean reservoir. Fresh bloom nutrients.
tasks.name: Reservoir change

training.description: Remove blocking fan leaves. One defoliation at start of early flower. Then only individual leaves as needed. Never more than 20% at once.
Remove all growth below SCROG net or lower 1/3. These sites won't produce quality buds in aero either.
training.timing: First week of early flower only

common_problems.cause: Off-time too long for current conditions (temp, humidity, root mass)
common_problems.issue: Root tips drying between extended mist cycles
common_problems.solution: Reduce off-time by 60-120 seconds. Different plants/genetics tolerate different off-times. If even one plant's roots are drying, the cycle is too long for that plant.

common_problems.cause: Dense root growth compacting. Mist not penetrating to inner roots
common_problems.solution: Gently separate matted sections. Ensure nozzles are positioned to spray into (not across) the root mass. Consider additional nozzle for coverage.

common_problems.cause: Root chamber humidity escaping near plant stems, creating a moisture microclimate around buds
common_problems.solution: Seal collar-to-chamber junction better. Add small fan blowing across the top of the chamber (where stems exit). This creates a dry boundary between root chamber humidity and the canopy.

common_problems.cause: High-EC nutrients precipitating, biofilm, or mineral buildup
common_problems.solution: EMERGENCY. Replace nozzle instantly. The affected plant's roots start dying in 5-10 minutes. Clean all nozzles within 24 hours. Flush lines. This is why spare nozzles MUST be on hand.

stages.description: Peak bud development. Buds fattening rapidly, trichome production accelerating. Mist cycles optimized for maximum generative push: extended off-times for root oxygenation. EC at its highest (still lower than other methods). Nozzle reliability is life-or-death — a failure now means catastrophic yield loss.

environment.notes: Peak light intensity. Humidity under 50% — dense buds trap moisture. Day/night DIF of 12°F+ for terpene production. CO2 at 1200-1500 ppm if supplementing.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: maintain aggressive dehumidification. Root chamber + greenhouse humidity = dangerous combo.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse aero: dehumidifier at full capacity during mid-flower.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor aero mid-flower: if you've made it this far, maintain vigilance. Autumn weather can be unpredictable.

extra_tasks.description: Power outages kill aero plants in minutes. Have battery backup or generator ready during storm season.
extra_tasks.name: Monitor for storms

outdoor.notes: Outdoor aero: power reliability is critical during mid-flower.

health_checks: Root tips moist between cycles (never dry)?
No bud rot in any cola?

day_cycle.notes: 4 seconds on, 8 minutes off. Maximum generative push — longest off-time of the grow. Roots get extended air exposure between mists, driving flower production. Monitor root tips obsessively.

night_cycle.notes: 3 seconds on, 12 minutes off at night. Very long off-times at night are safe because transpiration is minimal. Roots stay moist longer.

mist_system.notes: PEAK GENERATIVE MISTING. Longest off-times of the entire grow. Extended air exposure drives root oxygenation and pushes energy into bud production. But root tips must NEVER go dry — check between cycles. If any root tips look dry or papery, reduce off-time immediately. Cooler root chamber (62-68°F) during mid-flower improves terpene retention.

stages.name: Mid Flower (Peak Bloom)

nutrients.approach: Peak bloom. EC 1.6-2.0. This is the highest EC in aero — still lower than DWC/drip peak (2.6-3.0) due to mist absorption efficiency. PK-heavy.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root protection. Non-negotiable through harvest.

supplements.name: Liquid Kool Bloom
supplements.purpose: PK booster at peak dose. Conservative vs drip/DWC because aero absorbs more efficiently.

tasks.description: 8 minutes off during day, 12 minutes off at night. This is the limit — monitor root tips twice daily. Any drying = reduce off-time.
tasks.name: Peak generative mist cycle

tasks.description: Check EVERY nozzle EVERY day. Mid-flower nozzle failure = catastrophic. Have spare nozzles within arm's reach of the grow.
tasks.name: Nozzle check — daily, non-negotiable

tasks.description: Open chamber and inspect roots. Should be white, fluffy, extensive. Any brown, slimy, or matted sections = immediate action. Healthy aero roots look like cotton candy.
tasks.name: Root health inspection

tasks.description: Pull apart largest colas gently. Dense aero buds + root chamber humidity = bud rot risk. Catch it early.
tasks.name: Inspect buds for mold daily

tasks.description: Full change every 5-7 days. At peak EC, nutrient balance drifts faster. Fresh reservoir = balanced nutrition.
tasks.name: Reservoir change

tasks.description: Aero produces heavy, dense buds. Trellis nets, yo-yos, or stakes to prevent branch snapping.
tasks.name: Support heavy branches

common_problems.cause: Light too intense or too close. Heat stress.
common_problems.solution: Raise or dim lights by 10-15%. Late-flower buds are light-sensitive. Foxtails reduce bag appeal.

common_problems.cause: Temps too warm, or slow-finishing genetics
common_problems.solution: Drop day temps to 73-75°F. Check breeder's stated flowering time. Some cultivars take 10-12 weeks. Patience.

common_problems.cause: Natural senescence — plant is finishing and roots are aging
common_problems.issue: Root mass starting to brown at edges
common_problems.solution: Some browning at root tips is normal in late flower as the plant reallocates energy to buds. If the core root mass is still white, you're fine. If all roots are brown/slimy = Pythium, treat with H2O2 flush.

stages.description: Buds ripening. Trichomes transitioning from milky to amber. Begin reducing EC and mist frequency. The plant is finishing — it needs less. Mist off-times can extend further as the plant's water demand decreases. Some yellowing of fan leaves is natural and desired.

environment.notes: Slightly reduced light. Cooler night temps (58-62°F) bring out colors. Humidity at 35-45% — mature dense buds are highest rot risk.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: supplemental heating for cold nights. 60°F minimum.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse: heating during cold nights.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor: frost kills cannabis. Monitor overnight lows. Be ready to harvest early.

extra_tasks.description: Frost = instant death. Below 40°F = severe stress. Have emergency harvest plan.
extra_tasks.name: Monitor frost forecasts

outdoor.notes: Outdoor: weather dictates harvest timing.

health_checks: Root tips healthy despite reduced misting?

day_cycle.notes: 3 seconds on, 10 minutes off. Plant is drinking less. Extended off-times are safe — root mass is large and retains moisture well.

night_cycle.notes: 3 seconds on, 15 minutes off at night. Very long off-times as plant winds down. Monitor root tips but expect slower drying.

mist_system.notes: Tapering mist cycles. Plant is finishing and drinking less. Longer off-times are fine — the large root mass retains moisture well and the plant's transpiration rate is decreasing. Root tips should still never go dry, but they'll stay moist longer between cycles now.

stages.name: Late Flower (Ripening)

nutrients.approach: Reducing. EC 1.2-1.6. Taper nitrogen. Maintain PK for final ripening. Plant is consuming stored nutrients — fan leaf yellowing is normal.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root protection through finish.

supplements.name: Liquid Kool Bloom
supplements.purpose: Reduced PK. Tapering down from peak.

tasks.description: Drop EC to 1.2-1.6. Extend off-time to 10 min day / 15 min night. Plant is finishing.
tasks.name: Reduce EC and mist frequency

tasks.description: 60-100x loupe on buds (not sugar leaves). Clear → milky → amber. Target harvest ratio depends on desired effect.
tasks.name: Trichome checks with magnification

tasks.description: Still checking daily. A late-flower nozzle failure is still catastrophic — buds can't recover lost development.
tasks.name: Continue daily nozzle checks

tasks.description: Risk remains high through harvest. Daily inspection of largest colas.
tasks.name: Continue bud rot inspection

tasks.description: Based on trichome progression, estimate harvest. Plan flush to start 7-10 days before target.
tasks.name: Plan harvest date

common_problems.cause: Roots drying out, or flush started too early
common_problems.issue: Plant dying too fast during flush
common_problems.solution: Ensure mist system is still running (plain water). If leaves are crispy and buds look underdeveloped, the plant died rather than flushed. Keep roots moist.

common_problems.cause: Weakened plant defenses + humidity
common_problems.solution: Harvest immediately rather than risk more rot. A shorter flush is better than a moldy harvest.

stages.description: Final flush before harvest. Run plain pH'd water through the mist system — no nutrients. Aero flush is faster than media-based methods (5-7 days vs 7-14) because there's no media holding salts. The roots are misted with pure water and the plant consumes stored nutrients rapidly. Fan leaf yellowing accelerates dramatically.

environment.notes: Reduced light. Low humidity. Plant is finishing. Cooler temps are fine.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: maintain humidity management through flush.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse: keep environment controlled through flush.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor flush: stop nutrient solution, run plain water. Monitor weather for harvest window.

outdoor.notes: Outdoor: weather may force early harvest during flush.

day_cycle.notes: 3 seconds on, 10 minutes off. Plain water only. Maintain root moisture for nutrient transport out of tissues.

night_cycle.notes: 3 seconds on, 15 minutes off. Minimal night misting — just enough to keep roots alive.

mist_system.notes: PLAIN WATER MISTING. No nutrients. Keep roots alive and moist so the plant can transport stored nutrients from leaves to buds. Aero flush is faster than media-based flushes because there's no salt-loaded media to leach. 5-7 days is typically sufficient.

stages.name: Flush (Pre-Harvest)

nutrients.approach: Plain pH'd water only (pH 5.5-5.8). Zero nutrients. Flush is faster in aero than any other method.

tasks.description: Drain nutrient reservoir. Clean reservoir. Fill with plain pH'd water (5.5-5.8). Run through entire mist system.
tasks.name: Switch to plain water

tasks.description: Run clean water through the system for 10+ minutes continuously to clear all nutrient residue from nozzles and lines.
tasks.name: Flush nozzles and lines

tasks.description: Monitor daily. Harvest window approaching: mostly milky with 10-20% amber.
tasks.name: Continue trichome checks

tasks.description: Even with plain water, nozzles can clog from mineral deposits. Check daily.
tasks.name: Continue nozzle checks

tasks.description: Let roots dry slightly before chop. Less water weight in the plant. Some believe final stress pushes trichome production.
tasks.name: Stop misting 12-24 hours before harvest

common_problems.cause: Hidden rot inside dense colas
common_problems.solution: Cut away rotted sections plus 1 inch margin. Salvage clean buds. Do not dry or cure rotted material.

common_problems.cause: Long-term exposure to nutrient solution and pressure cycling
common_problems.solution: Replace degraded nozzles before next grow. Nozzles are consumables in aero — budget for replacements every 1-2 grows.

stages.description: Chop day. Shut down the mist system. Cut plants, wet trim or whole-plant hang. Clean and store all nozzles, lines, pump, and root chamber. Aero systems are cleaner at harvest than media-based methods — no media to dispose of.

environment.notes: Some growers give 24-48 hours of darkness before chop. Harvest in the morning. Room light only for working.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: controlled harvest timing.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse: use space for drying if possible.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor: harvest before frost. Have drying space ready.

outdoor.notes: Outdoor: weather may force harvest timing.

health_checks: No bud rot discovered during trim?

mist_system.notes: System OFF. After harvest: remove all nozzles, soak in H2O2 for 1 hour, rinse, dry, store. Flush all lines with H2O2 then plain water. Clean root chamber thoroughly. Dry everything before storage.

stages.name: Harvest

nutrients.approach: None. System shut down.

tasks.description: Confirm target ratio before chopping. 70-80% milky, 10-20% amber, minimal clear.
tasks.name: Final trichome check

tasks.description: Cut main stem at the collar. For wet trim: remove fan leaves and trim sugar leaves now. For dry trim: remove fan leaves only, hang whole plant.
tasks.name: Harvest the plant

tasks.description: Remove every nozzle. Soak in 3% H2O2 for 1 hour. Rinse with clean water. Dry completely. Store in a clean container.
tasks.name: Clean all nozzles

tasks.description: Run H2O2 solution through all lines for 15 minutes. Then flush with plain water. Drain completely. Coil and store dry.
tasks.name: Flush and clean lines

tasks.description: Remove root debris. Scrub chamber with H2O2 or dilute bleach. Rinse thoroughly. Dry completely. Biofilm and mineral deposits must be removed.
tasks.name: Clean root chamber

tasks.description: Drain, scrub, sanitize. Clean pump impeller and intake screen. Store dry.
tasks.name: Clean reservoir and pump

transition_signals: All material hung or in drying room

common_problems.cause: Humidity too low, temp too high, too much airflow
common_problems.solution: Raise humidity to 60-65%. Lower temp to 60°F. Reduce fan speed. Whole-plant hang slows drying.

common_problems.cause: Density + moisture trapped inside
common_problems.solution: Remove moldy buds. Lower humidity to 55%. Increase gentle airflow. Break dense buds into smaller pieces if needed.

common_problems.cause: Drying too fast — chlorophyll trapped
common_problems.solution: Slow down. Long cure (4-8 weeks) can partially recover.

stages.description: Slow, controlled drying. 60°F / 60% humidity / darkness (the '60/60 rule'). 10-14 days is ideal. Faster drying destroys terpenes. Aero-grown buds can be denser than average — they may take slightly longer to dry through.

environment.notes: DARK. 60°F. 60% humidity. Gentle airflow (not on buds directly). Hay smell = drying too fast.

environment_overrides.notes: Too warm and light for drying. Move to a separate dark room.

greenhouse.notes: Do not dry in greenhouse.

environment_overrides.notes: Dry indoors regardless of grow method.

outdoor.notes: Always dry indoors.

mist_system.notes: No aero system involvement. Drying is purely environmental control.

stages.name: Drying

nutrients.approach: None. Plant is harvested.

tasks.description: Hang upside down. Space so plants don't touch. Air must circulate around all sides.
tasks.name: Hang whole plants or branches

tasks.description: 60°F, 60% humidity, darkness. Monitor with hygrometer.
tasks.name: Maintain 60/60 environment

tasks.description: Bend a small stem. Snaps cleanly = ready for cure. Bends = keep drying. Aero buds may be denser — allow extra time.
tasks.name: Check drying progress

tasks.description: Mold can appear during drying. Dense aero-grown buds are higher risk. Inspect daily.
tasks.name: Inspect for mold

transition_signals: Outer buds dry but not crunchy

common_problems.cause: Buds too wet when jarred — anaerobic decomposition
common_problems.solution: Remove buds, dry 12-24 hours on screen, rejar. If smell persists, buds may be compromised.

common_problems.cause: Over-dried or too much burping
common_problems.solution: Add Boveda 62 pack. Reduce burping. Tortilla trick for emergency rehydration (remove promptly).

common_problems.cause: Too wet when jarred, or insufficient burping
common_problems.solution: Discard moldy buds. Dry remaining 12 hours. Sanitize jar. Rejar with more frequent burping.

stages.description: Slow cure in airtight containers. Terpenes develop, harshness mellows, quality finalizes. Minimum 2 weeks, ideal 4-8 weeks. Aero-grown flower is often noted for exceptional terpene profiles when properly cured — the high-oxygen root environment during growth promotes terpene precursor production.

environment.notes: Inside jar: 58-62% RH (Boveda 62 packs). Store in dark, cool place. Light degrades THC.

environment_overrides.notes: Do not cure in greenhouse. Move to stable indoor location.

greenhouse.notes: Cure indoors, not in greenhouse.

environment_overrides.notes: Same curing process. Store indoors.

outdoor.notes: Curing is identical for all grow methods.

health_checks: Buds improving in smell and smoothness?

mist_system.notes: No aero system involvement. Post-harvest processing.

stages.name: Curing

nutrients.approach: None. Post-harvest.

tasks.description: Trim sugar leaves. Place buds loosely in mason jars (75% full, not packed). If wet-trimmed: jar directly.
tasks.name: Trim and jar buds

tasks.description: Open jars 10-15 min, 2-3x/day first week. Once/day second week. Exchanges air and releases moisture.
tasks.name: Burp jars — first 2 weeks

tasks.description: Boveda 62% RH, 1 per oz. Maintains perfect humidity passively.
tasks.name: Add Boveda packs

tasks.description: Every 2-3 days after 2 weeks. Once/week after 4 weeks. Seal long-term after 8 weeks.
tasks.name: Reduce burping after 2 weeks

tasks.description: Inspect when burping. White fuzz = remove affected buds immediately.
tasks.name: Monitor for mold

container_progression.commercial_medium: Turkey bags in sealed bins, nitrogen-flushed
container_progression.industrial: Climate-controlled vault, nitrogen generators, automated monitoring

degradation_timeline.18_plus_months: Convert to extracts, edibles, or topicals.

testing_schedule.360_days: Full panel — sell, process, or destroy decision

common_problems.cause: Heat, light, oxygen, or time
common_problems.solution: Darkness, cool temps (60°F), minimal oxygen. ~5%/year baseline.

common_problems.cause: Temps above 70°F, oxygen, frequent opening
common_problems.solution: Below 65°F. Nitrogen-sealed. Minimize opening.

common_problems.cause: Humidity above 65% or improper cure
common_problems.solution: Verify 58-62% RH before sealing. Remove affected material.

common_problems.cause: Normal moisture equilibration
common_problems.solution: Boveda packs. Sealed containers.

stages.description: Post-cure long-term storage. Aeroponics produces some of the highest quality flower due to maximum oxygenation and precise nutrient delivery — storage must preserve that premium quality. Rapid aero growth cycles mean inventory accumulates fast. Proper storage preserves potency and terpenes for 6-12+ months.

environment.notes: DARK. Cool. Stable. Zero light — UV destroys cannabinoids and terpenes. Commercial: 58-62°F, 55-60% RH, nitrogen atmosphere.

environment_overrides.notes: Do NOT store in greenhouse.

greenhouse.notes: Climate-controlled indoor space only.

environment_overrides.notes: Storage is always indoor.

outdoor.notes: Indoor controlled environment.

stages.name: Long-Term Storage

nutrients.approach: None.

tasks.description: Home: mason jars with Boveda 58-62%. Commercial: nitrogen-sealed grove bags, CVault, or nitrogen-flushed drums.
tasks.name: Transfer to long-term containers

tasks.description: Strain, harvest date, storage date, weight, batch number. Commercial: seed-to-sale, FIFO rotation.
tasks.name: Label and track batches

tasks.description: Inspect for mold, off odors. Check humidity packs. Commercial: potency/terpene testing at 30/90/180 days.
tasks.name: Monthly quality checks

tasks.description: Monitor vault temp/humidity. No light leaks. Commercial: automated alerts.
tasks.name: Maintain environment

tasks.description: First in, first out. Flag batches approaching 12 months.
tasks.name: Rotate stock (FIFO)

tasks.description: Commercial: retain samples per regulations.
tasks.name: Compliance testing holds

autoflower.description: Ruderalis-hybrid strains that flower automatically regardless of light schedule. Fixed timeline.

light_schedule.flower: 20/4 or 18/6 (same as veg — no change needed)
light_schedule.transition: Automatic — plant decides when to flower (usually week 3-4)

autoflower.name: Autoflower
autoflower.notes: Fixed timeline — stress cannot be recovered by extending veg. Start in final pot (no transplant shock). LST preferred over HST.

training_suitability.defoliation: light only — auto can't recover from heavy defol
training_suitability.topping: risky — only if plant is vigorous

photoperiod.description: Standard cannabis strains that require 12/12 light to flower. Grower controls timing.

light_schedule.transition: Switch to 12/12 when plant is ~50% of desired final height

photoperiod.name: Photoperiod
photoperiod.notes: Can be vegged as long as desired. Cloneable. More forgiving of stress (can extend veg to recover).

training_suitability.defoliation: good
training_suitability.topping: excellent

issues.cause: Clogged nozzle serving that plant
issues.fix: Replace nozzle IMMEDIATELY (5-minute emergency). Clean all nozzles within 24 hours. Check inline filter.

issues.cause: Pressure too low, or nozzles partially clogged
issues.fix: Check pump pressure on gauge. Clean nozzles. For HPA: verify accumulator is charged. Streams = dramatically reduced oxygen absorption.
issues.symptom: Nozzles producing streams instead of mist

issues.cause: Standard nozzles without anti-drip feature, or worn anti-drip valve
issues.fix: Replace with anti-drip nozzles. Continuous dripping waterlogged roots and defeats the purpose of cycle timing.

issues.cause: Nozzle positioning, pressure variation, or partially clogged nozzles
issues.fix: Test each nozzle individually. Reposition for even coverage. Add nozzles to dead zones. All roots must receive mist.

issues.cause: No inline filter, or using organic/incompatible nutrients
issues.fix: Install/clean inline filter (200+ mesh for HPA). Switch to synthetic-only nutrients. Clean reservoir. Some nutrient brands are worse than others for nozzle compatibility.

issues.cause: Pythium (root rot) from warm chamber or contamination
issues.fix: Flush system with H2O2 (3%). Cool root chamber below 70°F. Increase Hydroguard. Remove dead root material. Severe cases: may need to restart.

issues.cause: Light leak in chamber
issues.fix: Find and seal light leak. Clean chamber with H2O2. Algae clogs nozzles and promotes pathogens. Every seal, fitting, and collar must be checked.

issues.cause: Off-time too long, nozzle clogged, or root mass outgrew coverage
issues.fix: Reduce off-time. Verify all nozzles. Add nozzles if root mass has expanded beyond current spray pattern.

issues.cause: Ambient heat, pump heat, or insufficient insulation
issues.fix: Insulate chamber. Add water chiller to reservoir. Move pump outside chamber if possible. Hot root zone is the #1 killer in aero.

issues.cause: Dense root growth in confined space
issues.fix: Gently separate. Ensure nozzles can penetrate the root mass. Consider larger chamber for next grow. Matted roots block mist penetration to inner roots.

issues.cause: Pump failure, power loss, or clogged intake
issues.fix: EMERGENCY (5-10 min to damage). Switch to backup pump. If no backup: hand-mist roots with spray bottle every 2-3 minutes while diagnosing. Check power, intake screen, pump impeller.

issues.cause: Grid failure, tripped breaker
issues.fix: EMERGENCY. UPS should engage automatically. If no UPS: hand-mist roots. Run generator. Do NOT leave aero plants without mist for more than 10 minutes.

issues.cause: Timer failure or programming error
issues.fix: Continuous mist: open solenoid manually on a schedule. No mist: run pump manually. Replace timer. Test new timer 50+ cycles before trusting it.
issues.symptom: Timer malfunctioning (continuous mist or no mist)

issues.cause: Solenoid failure or electrical connection issue
issues.fix: EMERGENCY. Manually open the solenoid valve. Check wiring. Replace solenoid. Keep a spare solenoid on hand.
issues.symptom: Solenoid not opening (no mist despite pump running)

issues.cause: Leak in system, pump degrading, or accumulator losing charge
issues.fix: Check all fittings for leaks (spray soapy water on connections, look for bubbles). Test pump output directly. Recharge or replace accumulator.

cons: RO system cost and waste water
water_source_profiles.name: Reverse Osmosis (RO) Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Gold standard for precision growing. Always add CalMag first when using RO.
pros: Blank slate — total control over mineral content

dechlorination.chloramine: Does NOT gas off — must use Campden tablets or activated carbon filter
dechlorination.chlorine: Let sit uncovered 24 hours (gasses off) or use dechlorinator drops
dechlorination.how_to_tell: Check city water report. Most modern cities use chloramine.

water_source_profiles.name: Municipal Tap Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Good enough for most grows. Dechlorinate and pH-adjust. Get a water report from your city.

cons: May contain iron, sulfur, or heavy metals
water_source_profiles.name: Well Water
water_source_profiles.notes: MUST get a water test before using. High iron or sulfur can cause lockout. Great if your well has clean water with moderate minerals.

water_source_profiles.name: Distilled Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Same as RO but purchased instead of filtered. Only practical for 1-2 plant grows.

advanced_techniques.description: Elevating CO2 from ambient (400ppm) to 1000-1500ppm increases photosynthesis rate by 20-40% when light and temperature are also increased.

methods.notes: Most common. Tank refills every 2-4 weeks for a tent.
Produces heat and humidity — better for large rooms with AC.
Produces ~100-200ppm boost. Only useful for small sealed spaces. Not worth it for most.

advanced_techniques.name: CO2 Supplementation

ppfd_co2_correlation.notes: Ambient CO2 sufficient at low light
Slight benefit from supplementation
Moderate benefit — cost-effective threshold
Strong benefit — diminishing returns above this
Maximum practical benefit for most grows
Commercial intensity — requires perfect environment

requirements: Sealed grow space (CO2 escapes quickly from leaky rooms)
High light intensity (>600 PPFD — plants can't use extra CO2 without sufficient light)
Higher temperature tolerance (82-86°F optimal with CO2 vs 75-80°F without)
CO2 controller or timer

advanced_techniques.description: Manipulating environmental and irrigation parameters to push the plant toward vegetative or generative (flowering/fruiting) growth.
advanced_techniques.name: Crop Steering
advanced_techniques.notes: Crop steering is most effective in hydro/coco where irrigation frequency is highly controllable. Soil growers have less precision due to soil buffering.

vegetative_steering.goal: More leaf/stem growth, larger plant framework

best_practices: Spray during lights-off or first/last 30 minutes of light cycle (stomata open, no light burn)
Use fine mist sprayer — coverage is key
pH foliar solution to 6.2-6.5 (leaf surface slightly higher pH than root zone)
EC should be very low (0.2-0.5) — leaves burn easily
Never foliar spray during flower (moisture in buds → mold/bud rot)
Best for micronutrient deficiency correction (CalMag, iron, magnesium)

common_foliar_products.frequency: Weekly preventive, 3-day intervals for active infestations

advanced_techniques.description: Spraying nutrient solution directly onto leaves for rapid uptake. Bypasses root zone entirely.
advanced_techniques.name: Foliar Feeding
