advanced_techniques.description: Strengthens cell walls, increases stem thickness, improves heat/drought/pest resistance.
important_notes: ALWAYS add silica to water FIRST before other nutrients (raises pH significantly)
Let silica mix for 5-10 minutes before adding base nutrients
Silica is incompatible with concentrated nutrient solutions — never mix undiluted
Discontinue in late flower (last 2-3 weeks)
advanced_techniques.name: Silica (Potassium Silicate)

advanced_techniques.description: Introduces beneficial bacteria/fungi to the root zone to outcompete pathogens, improve nutrient uptake, and protect against root diseases.
important_notes: Do NOT use hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) with beneficial microbes — it kills them
Choose either sterile (H2O2) OR beneficial (Hydroguard) approach — not both
Beneficials need warmish water (65-75°F) to thrive
Reapply after any reservoir change or system cleaning
advanced_techniques.name: Beneficial Microbes & Root Inoculants

products.name: Hydroguard
products.notes: The standard for DWC/RDWC. Add at every res change.

products.dosing: 1 tsp/gal (water-in) or dust roots at transplant
products.name: Great White
products.notes: Best for soil/coco. Less effective in pure hydro.

products.name: Recharge
products.notes: Feeds existing soil microbes. Great for living soil.

products.name: Mammoth P
products.notes: Most effective in flower when P demand is highest.

advanced_techniques.description: Removing select fan leaves to improve light penetration, airflow, and direct energy to bud sites.
advanced_techniques.name: Defoliation (Strategic Leaf Removal)
rules: Never remove more than 20-30% of leaves at once (except schwazze technique)
Only defoliate healthy, vigorous plants — never stressed ones
Autoflowers: very light defoliation only — they can't recover from heavy removal
Always leave the top 2-3 fan leaves on each branch (they feed the bud below)

timing.method: Remove lower leaves that get no light (lollipop)
Major defoliation day 1 and day 21 of flower (schwazzing)
Selective tuck or remove leaves blocking bud sites

fish_in.advantages: Simpler setup. Fish from day one.
fish_in.method: Add hardy fish at low density. Monitor daily. Water changes if ammonia/nitrite spike.
fish_in.risk: Fish stress/death if not managed carefully.

fishless.advantages: No fish at risk during unstable period.
fishless.method: Add pure ammonia to 2-4 ppm. Feed bacteria with ammonia until conversion is instant. Then add fish.

nitrogen_cycle.overview: The nitrogen cycle is the ENGINE of aquaponics. Fish excrete ammonia → Nitrosomonas bacteria convert to nitrite → Nitrobacter bacteria convert to nitrate → Plants absorb nitrate as fertilizer.

steps.notes: Toxic to fish above 1 ppm. Must be converted quickly.
Nitrite also toxic to fish. Intermediate step.
Nitrate is plant food! Safe for fish up to 200+ ppm.
Plants clean the water. Returns to fish tank filtered.

allowed_methods: Neem oil foliar (ABOVE water only — never in system water)
Diatomaceous earth (on soil surfaces, not in water)
banned_methods: Any systemic pesticide (enters plant tissue → falls in water)
pest_management.constraint: CANNOT use pesticides, fungicides, or any chemicals that will enter the water and harm fish.

potassium.use: pH buffer that doubles as K supplement

fish_to_plant_ratio.feeding_rate_drives_nutrients: Fish food is the ONLY input. More food = more waste = more plant nutrients.
fish_to_plant_ratio.for_cannabis: Cannabis is a heavy feeder. Use 1 lb fish per 1 sq ft of grow space for flowering.
fish_to_plant_ratio.rule_of_thumb: 1 lb of fish feeds 1-2 sq ft of grow bed (media bed) or 2-4 sq ft of DWC raft

high.notes: Commercial density. Needs excellent aeration and filtration.

low.notes: Hobby. Forgiving. Less nutrients for plants.

medium.notes: Balanced. Good for most setups.

dwc_raft.best_for: Leafy greens, herbs. Can work for cannabis with support.
dwc_raft.description: Floating foam rafts with net pots. Roots dangle in nutrient-rich water.

media_bed.best_for: Cannabis (root space + support for large plants)
media_bed.description: Flood and drain grow beds filled with expanded clay or gravel. Serves as grow space AND biofilter.

nft_channel.best_for: Small plants. Not recommended for cannabis.
nft_channel.description: Thin film of water flowing through channels. Roots in channel.

equipment.description: Main fish habitat. Size determines system capacity. 1 lb fish per 5-10 gal water.
equipment.name: Fish tank (100-300 gal)

equipment.description: Media bed (flood/drain), DWC raft, or NFT channel for plants. Media beds double as biofilter.
equipment.name: Grow bed(s)

equipment.description: Moves water between fish tank and grow beds. Sized for system volume.
equipment.name: Water pump

equipment.description: Dissolved oxygen for fish AND nitrifying bacteria. Critical for both.
equipment.name: Air pump + stones

equipment.description: Surface area for nitrifying bacteria. K1 media, lava rock, or grow bed media serves this role.
equipment.name: Biofilter media

equipment.description: The nutrient source. Species chosen based on water temp and local regulations.
equipment.name: Fish (tilapia, goldfish, trout, etc.)

equipment.description: Input that drives the entire system. Higher protein = more N output.
equipment.name: Fish food (high-quality pellets)

equipment.description: Ammonia, nitrite, nitrate, pH minimum. Test regularly.
equipment.name: Water test kit (API Master)

equipment.description: For media bed systems. Provides root support + bacterial surface area.
equipment.name: Grow media (hydroton/lava rock)

equipment.description: Controls flood/drain cycle in media beds. Bell siphon is gravity-powered.
equipment.name: Bell siphon or timer

equipment.description: Most common aquaponics deficiency. DTPA form is fish-safe.
equipment.name: Chelated iron (DTPA)

equipment.description: Raises pH (nitrification is acidifying) AND supplements K. Dual purpose.
equipment.name: Potassium hydroxide

equipment.description: Monitor fish tank temperature. Critical for fish health.
equipment.name: Thermometer (water)

equipment.description: For DWC/raft systems. Hold plants in floating rafts.
equipment.name: Net pots

equipment.description: Removes solid fish waste before grow beds. Prevents clogging.
equipment.name: Solids filter/settling tank

equipment.description: Fish die within hours without oxygen. Battery backup air pump is insurance.
equipment.name: Backup air pump

flush_timing.coco: 7-14 days reduced nutrients → plain water last 3-5 days
flush_timing.notes: Flushing is debated — some studies show no difference. At minimum, reduce nutrients in final week.
flush_timing.soil: 14-21 days plain water (soil holds nutrients longer)

other_harvest_indicators: Most pistils (hairs) have darkened and curled in
Calyxes are swollen and dense
Fan leaves are yellowing and falling off naturally
Trichome stalks are visible and heads are enlarged
Breeder's recommended flowering time has passed

trichome_ratios.notes: Harvest early for more THC-dominant, less CBN. Energetic, racy high. Slight reduction in yield.
Standard harvest window. Mix of THC and CBN. Full-spectrum effects.
Extended harvest for max CBN conversion. Heavy body high. Good for pain/sleep. Slightly degraded THC.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Micro → Gro → Bloom (always Micro first)
nutrient_brands.name: General Hydroponics Flora Trio

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Part A → Epsom → Part B (dissolve each fully before adding next)
nutrient_brands.name: Jack's 321
nutrient_brands.notes: Jack's 321 is 3.6g Part A, 2.4g Cal Nit, 1.2g Epsom per gallon at full strength. Extremely cost-effective for large grows.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Core → Bloom
nutrient_brands.name: Athena Pro Line
nutrient_brands.notes: Commercial-grade. Simple 2-part with integrated CalMag. Popular in commercial cannabis facilities.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: A → B (separate for veg and bloom)
nutrient_brands.name: Advanced Nutrients pH Perfect (Sensi)
nutrient_brands.notes: Self-adjusting pH technology (claims no pH adjustment needed). Expensive but popular for beginners who struggle with pH.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Shake VERY well before use (thick concentrate settles). One bottle at a time.
nutrient_brands.name: General Hydroponics FloraNova
nutrient_brands.notes: Simplest option — one bottle per phase. Great for beginners. Shake well — concentrate separates.
nutrient_brands.type: 1-part (two bottles — one for veg, one for bloom)

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Dissolve powder in warm water, stir well
nutrient_brands.name: Greenleaf Nutrients Mega Crop
nutrient_brands.notes: One powder does everything — includes CalMag. Incredibly cost-effective at ~$0.02/gal. Community favorite on r/microgrowery.

environment.container: Glass mason jars (quart size). Fill 75% full.

humidity_packs.boveda_58: For drier preference or long-term storage.
humidity_packs.boveda_62: Maintains 62% RH. Set-and-forget. Replace when crispy.
humidity_packs.grove_bags: Heat-sealed bags with built-in humidity regulation. No burping needed.

indicators_of_good_cure: Smooth, flavorful smoke — no harshness or hay smell
Buds are slightly sticky but not wet
Strong terpene aroma when jar is opened
Ash burns to white/light gray (not black)
method: Trim buds to final shape (dry trim or finish wet trim)
Place in mason jars — fill 3/4 full to allow air exchange
Week 1: burp 2-3x daily for 10-15 minutes (release moisture and gases)
Week 2-4: burp 1x daily for 5-10 minutes
Month 2+: burp 1x weekly or seal completely
Cure for minimum 2 weeks, ideal is 4-8 weeks. Some strains improve for 6+ months.

common_mistakes: Drying too fast (>3 days) — harsh smoke, hay smell, terpene loss
Drying too slow (>14 days) — mold risk, diminishing returns
Fan blowing directly on buds — uneven drying, crispy exterior/moist interior
Temperature too high (>70°F) — terpene evaporation, harsh taste

environment.airflow: Gentle circulation — fan NOT pointed at buds. Just keep air moving.

method: Cut branches 12-18 inches long
Optionally remove large fan leaves (wet trim) or leave intact (dry trim)
Hang upside down on line/rack with 6+ inches between branches
Small branches should snap (not bend) when done — 5-7 days minimum
Stems should snap cleanly; if they bend, keep drying

long_term_storage.degradation: THC slowly converts to CBN over time. Light, heat, air, and moisture accelerate degradation.
long_term_storage.method: Sealed mason jars or vacuum-sealed bags with Boveda 58% in dark, cool place
long_term_storage.notes: Properly cured and stored cannabis can remain excellent for 12+ months. Freezing is debated — can damage trichomes if not done carefully.

critical_rules: NEVER use pesticides, fungicides, or chemicals — kills fish
NEVER let ammonia or nitrite exceed 1 ppm — fish die
System MUST be cycled before adding fish at full density
Always have backup aeration — fish die in hours without O2
Plant something IMMEDIATELY after harvest — nitrate spikes without plants
Supplement iron (chelated DTPA) — universal aquaponics deficiency
key_challenges: P and K deficiency in flower (fish waste is N-heavy)
quick_reference.maintenance_level: High — living system requires daily fish care, water testing, and balancing two ecosystems simultaneously.
quick_reference.method_summary: Fish and plants in symbiosis. Fish waste → bacteria convert → plant food. Plants filter water → clean for fish. Closed-loop ecosystem.

scale_tiers.description: First-time grower or single-plant hobbyist. Manual management, learning-focused.
scale_tiers.name: Solo / Single Plant
scale_tiers.notes: Hands-on learning experience. Manual checks 1-2x daily. Ideal for beginners.

scale_tiers.description: Hobbyist tent grow. Light automation helpful but not required.
scale_tiers.name: Small Tent (2-8 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Timer-controlled lights, humidity controller. Sensor monitoring recommended.

scale_tiers.description: Dedicated grower with multiple tents/rooms. Automation saves significant time.
scale_tiers.name: Multi-Tent (9-24 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Multiple environments to manage. Automated alerts essential. Consider perpetual harvest.

scale_tiers.description: Licensed commercial operation. Full automation, compliance tracking, batch management.
scale_tiers.name: Commercial Room (25-100 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: SOPs required. Staff training. Regulatory compliance. Insurance requirements.

scale_tiers.description: Large-scale production facility. Enterprise automation, SCADA integration, dedicated staff.
scale_tiers.name: Warehouse Scale (100+ plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Multiple rooms, dedicated HVAC, industrial nutrient dosing, video surveillance.

common_problems.cause: Temperature too low, pH too extreme, or chlorine in water
common_problems.solution: Ensure water 72-80°F. pH 7.0-7.5. Dechlorinate all water. Add bacteria starter.

common_problems.cause: Added fish before cycle complete
common_problems.solution: 50% water change immediately. Stop feeding. Add bacteria. Wait for cycle to complete.

stages.description: Unique to aquaponics: system must be cycled (nitrogen cycle established) before planting. Ammonia→Nitrite→Nitrate bacteria colonies take 2-6 weeks. Germinate seeds separately during this period.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse warmth helps cycling speed.
Outdoor cycling is slower in cool weather. Shade tank from direct sun.

stages.name: Germination (System Cycling)

nutrients.approach: Fish waste provides all nutrition once cycled. No synthetic nutrients in aquaponics.

tasks.description: Fishless cycling: add pure ammonia to 2-4 ppm. Or add hardy fish.
tasks.name: Add ammonia source

tasks.description: Ammonia, nitrite, nitrate, pH. Track cycling progress.
tasks.name: Test water daily

tasks.description: Paper towel or starter plugs. System not ready for plants yet.
tasks.name: Germinate seeds separately

tasks.description: Ammonia drops → nitrite rises → nitrite drops → nitrate rises = CYCLED.
tasks.name: Monitor bacterial colonization

transition_signals: System cycled — ready for full stocking

common_problems.cause: System too new — not enough nitrate yet
common_problems.solution: Patience. Increase fish feeding slightly. Add supplemental seaweed extract (safe for fish).

common_problems.cause: Stress from new environment or water quality issue
common_problems.solution: Check ammonia/nitrite. Partial water change if high. Give fish 24h to acclimate.

stages.description: System cycled. Transplant seedlings to grow beds. Fish stocked and feeding. Bacteria converting waste to plant food. Start with light fish feeding.

environment_overrides.notes: Ideal controlled environment for aquaponics.
Protect from predators (birds, raccoons).

extra_tasks.description: Net over fish tank. Fencing if needed.
extra_tasks.name: Predator protection

stages.name: Seedling

nutrients.approach: Fish waste only. Light feeding schedule. System still maturing.

tasks.description: Place in net pots with clay pebbles. Roots will reach water.
tasks.name: Transplant seedlings to grow beds

tasks.description: 2x daily, only what they eat in 5 minutes. Avoid overfeeding.
tasks.name: Feed fish lightly

tasks.description: Ammonia, nitrite, nitrate, pH daily. System still stabilizing.
tasks.name: Test water parameters

tasks.description: Active, eating, no gasping at surface.
tasks.name: Observe fish health

common_problems.cause: Fish waste low in iron. Most common aquaponics deficiency.
common_problems.solution: Chelated iron (DTPA form is fish-safe). 2-3 ppm target in water.

common_problems.cause: Excess light on water + high nutrients
common_problems.solution: Shade fish tank. Increase plant mass to consume nutrients. Reduce fish feeding slightly.

stages.description: Plants establishing. Roots reaching water. Fish growing and eating more = more nutrients for plants. System reaching biological equilibrium. Increase fish feeding to boost plant nutrition.

environment_overrides.notes: Monitor water temp — greenhouse heat can warm fish tank too much.
Shade fish tank from direct sun. Plants can take full sun.

stages.name: Early Vegetative

nutrients.approach: Fish waste provides NPK. May supplement iron (chelated Fe safe for fish) and potassium (potassium hydroxide for pH up doubles as K supplement).

tasks.description: 3x daily. As much as they eat in 5 min.
tasks.name: Increase fish feeding

tasks.description: Ammonia, nitrite, nitrate, pH. System should be stable now.
tasks.name: Test water 3x/week

tasks.description: Chelated iron (DTPA) is fish-safe. Add if yellowing between veins.
tasks.name: Supplement iron if needed

tasks.description: LST or topping. Aquaponics plants grow vigorously.
tasks.name: Begin training

common_problems.cause: Too many fish for plant mass
common_problems.solution: Add more plants. Reduce fish numbers. Reduce feeding. Partial water change.

common_problems.cause: Nitrification is acidifying. Normal in aquaponics.
common_problems.solution: Buffer with potassium hydroxide (adds K!) or calcium carbonate. Never use pH down chemicals.

stages.description: System in full balance. Fish providing ample nutrition. Plants consuming nitrate aggressively. Aquaponics excels in veg — unlimited nitrogen from fish waste. Vigorous growth.

environment_overrides.notes: May need cooling for fish tank in hot greenhouse.
Peak outdoor growing. Monitor water temp in heat.

stages.name: Late Vegetative

nutrients.approach: Fish waste provides abundant N. Supplement Fe and K as needed. Consider banana peel tea for K (fish-safe).

tasks.description: Max sustainable feeding. More fish food = more plant nutrition.
tasks.name: Feed fish generously

tasks.description: If nitrate > 120 ppm, too many fish or too few plants.
tasks.name: Monitor nitrate

tasks.description: Remove solid waste from tank bottom. Clean biofilter media gently.
tasks.name: Fish tank maintenance

tasks.description: Aquaponics plants are often extra vigorous. Train aggressively.
tasks.name: Train canopy

common_problems.cause: Fish waste insufficient in phosphorus for flowering
common_problems.solution: Supplement with bone meal tea bags in system. Rock phosphate in biofilter.

stages.description: Flip to 12/12. Plants stretch. Aquaponics challenge: fish don't change with light cycle. Maintain fish feeding consistently. Begin phosphorus supplementation for flower.

environment_overrides.notes: Blackout covers if needed. Fish tank stays lit normally.
Natural photoperiod. Fish unaffected.

health_checks: Fish still healthy under reduced light?
stages.name: Transition (Stretch)

nutrients.approach: Fish waste + supplemental P (bone meal tea, rock phosphate in biofilter). K supplementation via pH management.

tasks.description: 12/12. Fish don't care — they're on their own schedule.
tasks.name: Flip light schedule

tasks.description: Fish waste is N-heavy, P-light. Add bone meal in biofilter bag or rock phosphate.
tasks.name: Supplement phosphorus

tasks.description: Don't reduce fish food — plants still need N during stretch.
tasks.name: Maintain fish feeding

tasks.description: LST/supercrop as needed.
tasks.name: Manage stretch

common_problems.cause: Insufficient P-K from fish waste alone
common_problems.solution: This is normal for aquaponics. Supplement P-K. Accept slightly smaller yields for superior terpene/flavor quality.

stages.description: Bud sites forming. Aquaponics challenge: sufficient P and K from fish alone is difficult. Supplementation needed without harming fish. Flower quality can match hydro with proper management.

environment_overrides.notes: Humidity management critical with water volume.
Pest management — no pesticides in aquaponics!

stages.name: Early Flower

nutrients.approach: Fish waste + P supplement + K supplement. Kelp extract (fish-safe) provides micronutrients and natural growth hormones.

tasks.description: Bone meal bag + potassium hydroxide for pH up.
tasks.name: Maintain P/K supplementation

tasks.description: Compare to hydro expectations. May be smaller but quality is excellent.
tasks.name: Monitor bud development

tasks.description: Remove lower fans. Aquaponics humidity can be high.
tasks.name: Defoliate for airflow

tasks.description: Don't reduce. Plants still using N.
tasks.name: Fish feeding maintained

common_problems.cause: Large water volume in grow room raises ambient humidity
common_problems.solution: Dehumidifier. Separate fish tank room from grow room if possible. Excellent airflow.

common_problems.cause: Lowering water temp for flower hurts tropical fish
common_problems.solution: Choose fish species that tolerate cooler water (trout, goldfish). Or separate fish and plant water temps.

stages.description: Peak bud development. Maximum P-K demand. Fish-powered nutrients produce unique terpene profiles — many aquaponics growers report exceptional flavor and smoothness.

environment_overrides.notes: Ventilation critical with water mass.
Water temp follows ambient. Choose fish accordingly.

stages.name: Mid Flower (Bulk Phase)

nutrients.approach: Fish waste + P-K supplements. Consider worm casting tea (fish-safe) for micronutrient boost.

tasks.description: Fish feeding, water testing, supplement P-K.
tasks.name: Maintain system balance

tasks.description: Flowering prefers cooler — may need to cool fish tank.
tasks.name: Monitor water temp

tasks.description: Trellis or stakes.
tasks.name: Support heavy buds

tasks.description: Aquaponics = high humidity environment. Be vigilant.
tasks.name: Inspect for bud rot

common_problems.cause: Fish waste still providing too much N
common_problems.solution: Reduce feeding more. Fish are fine on 1x/day. Or accept it — aquaponics often finishes green.

stages.description: Final ripening. Reduce fish feeding slightly to lower nitrogen availability. This encourages natural fade. Fish adjust easily to reduced feeding.

environment_overrides.notes: Night temp drops beneficial for ripening.
Fall temps help. Monitor fish in cooling water.

stages.name: Late Flower (Ripening)

nutrients.approach: Reduce fish feeding 25-50% to lower N. Stop P-K supplements. Let plant use stored nutrients.

tasks.description: Cut to 50-75% normal. Lowers N for natural fade.
tasks.name: Reduce fish feeding

tasks.description: 60x loupe. 70-80% milky, 10-20% amber target.
tasks.name: Check trichomes

tasks.description: No more P-K additions. Let system deplete.
tasks.name: Stop supplements

tasks.description: Fish handle reduced food fine. Won't harm them.
tasks.name: Monitor fish at reduced feeding

common_problems.cause: Habit from synthetic grows
common_problems.solution: Aquaponics is organic. No synthetic salts to flush. Smoke is already clean/smooth. Flush optional.

stages.description: Aquaponics doesn't flush traditionally — no salt buildup. Reduce fish feeding to minimum. Some growers temporarily move plants to plain water for final days. Others harvest without any flush (already organic).

environment_overrides.notes: Standard procedure.
Harvest timing based on weather, not flush schedule.

stages.name: Flush

nutrients.approach: Minimal fish feeding. No supplements. System naturally depletes. No salt flush needed.

tasks.description: Once daily, small amount. Just enough to keep fish healthy.
tasks.name: Minimal fish feeding

tasks.description: Continue checking ripeness.
tasks.name: Monitor trichomes

tasks.description: Some remove plants from system to plain water tub for final 3-5 days.
tasks.name: Optional: move to plain water

common_problems.cause: No plants consuming the fish waste nitrogen
common_problems.solution: CRITICAL: plant something immediately. Lettuce, basil, or next cannabis. Fish waste doesn't stop.

stages.description: Remove plants from grow beds. Fish continue as normal — resume regular feeding. System keeps running. Can replant immediately for continuous cycling.

environment_overrides.notes: Year-round cycling possible.
Harvest before frost. Plan for fish over winter.

stages.name: Harvest

nutrients.approach: None for plants. Resume normal fish feeding.

tasks.description: Pull from net pots. Remove all root material from beds.
tasks.name: Remove plants from grow beds

tasks.description: Wet or dry trim.
tasks.name: Trim

tasks.description: Back to normal schedule. Fish need consistent nutrition.
tasks.name: Resume fish feeding

tasks.description: System needs plants to consume nitrate. Plant lettuce/herbs as gap crop if not ready for next cannabis.
tasks.name: Plant next cycle (or companion plants)

water_params.notes: Resume normal fish feeding. System continues without plants temporarily.

common_problems.cause: Humidity too low
common_problems.solution: Raise humidity. Hang whole plants for slower dry.

stages.description: Standard hang dry. Aquaponics-grown flower often dries slightly differently due to organic nutrient profile — takes well to slow dry.

environment_overrides.notes: Dry in separate space.
Always dry indoors.

stages.name: Drying

nutrients.approach: None.

tasks.description: 60-65°F, 55-62% RH, complete darkness.
tasks.name: Hang branches

tasks.description: Check daily.
tasks.name: Monitor conditions

tasks.description: Small stems snap = ready.
tasks.name: Stem snap test

water_params.notes: Fish system running independently. May need water changes if no plants consuming waste.

common_problems.cause: Jarred too wet
common_problems.solution: Remove, dry 12-24h, re-jar.

stages.description: Jar cure. Aquaponics flower often has excellent flavor due to broad-spectrum organic nutrition from fish waste. Extended cure recommended.

environment_overrides.notes: Cure indoors.
Cure indoors.

stages.name: Curing

nutrients.approach: None.

tasks.description: Daily 2 weeks, then weekly.
tasks.name: Burp jars

tasks.description: 58-62%. Boveda 62 packs.
tasks.name: Monitor humidity

autoflower.description: Ruderalis-hybrid strains that flower automatically regardless of light schedule. Fixed timeline.

light_schedule.flower: 20/4 or 18/6 (same as veg — no change needed)
light_schedule.transition: Automatic — plant decides when to flower (usually week 3-4)

autoflower.name: Autoflower
autoflower.notes: Fixed timeline — stress cannot be recovered by extending veg. Start in final pot (no transplant shock). LST preferred over HST.

training_suitability.defoliation: light only — auto can't recover from heavy defol
training_suitability.topping: risky — only if plant is vigorous

photoperiod.description: Standard cannabis strains that require 12/12 light to flower. Grower controls timing.

light_schedule.transition: Switch to 12/12 when plant is ~50% of desired final height

photoperiod.name: Photoperiod
photoperiod.notes: Can be vegged as long as desired. Cloneable. More forgiving of stress (can extend veg to recover).

training_suitability.defoliation: good
training_suitability.topping: excellent

issues.cause: Dead fish, overfeeding, or insufficient bacteria
issues.solution: 50% water change immediately. Stop feeding 24h. Check for dead fish. Add bacteria starter.

issues.cause: System partially cycled or bacteria die-off
issues.solution: 50% water change. Add salt (1 ppt) to protect fish gills. Patience — nitrite bacteria will catch up.

issues.cause: Nitrification produces acid. Normal over time.
issues.solution: Buffer with potassium hydroxide or calcium carbonate. Small amounts frequently. Target 6.8.

issues.cause: New system, alkaline water source, or limestone in media
issues.solution: Patience — pH drops naturally from nitrification. Remove any limestone. Use rainwater.

issues.cause: Low dissolved oxygen
issues.solution: Emergency: add air stones immediately. Check air pump. Reduce feeding. Partial water change.

issues.cause: Stress, disease, or water quality issue
issues.solution: Test all water params. Partial water change. Look for visible disease signs. Salt bath (1-3 ppt).

issues.cause: Ammonia/nitrite spike, temperature shock, or oxygen crash
issues.solution: Emergency water change (50%). Test everything. Check air pump. Don't add chemicals.

issues.cause: Ichthyophthirius (common parasite)
issues.solution: Raise temp to 82°F slowly (1°F/day). Salt to 3 ppt. Do NOT use malachite green (kills plants).

issues.cause: Fish waste contains almost no iron
issues.solution: Add chelated iron DTPA at 2-3 ppm. This is safe for fish. Most common aquaponics deficiency.

issues.cause: Phosphorus deficiency — fish waste is N-heavy
issues.solution: Bone meal in mesh bag placed in biofilter flow. Rock phosphate. Fish-safe P supplement.

issues.cause: Potassium deficiency
issues.solution: Use potassium hydroxide as pH buffer (raises pH AND adds K). Kelp extract (fish-safe).

issues.cause: Insufficient fish biomass for plant load
issues.solution: More fish, larger fish, or reduce plant count. Rule: 1 lb fish per 1-2 sq ft grow space.

issues.cause: More fish waste than plants can consume
issues.solution: Add more plants. Reduce fish count. Reduce feeding. Partial water change.

issues.cause: Too many plants for fish load
issues.solution: Add more fish. Increase feeding. Supplement with fish-safe inputs.

issues.cause: Light hitting water + excess nutrients
issues.solution: Shade all water surfaces. Cover fish tank. More plants to consume nutrients.

issues.cause: Insufficient solids filtration
issues.solution: Add settling tank before grow beds. Clean beds periodically. Worm composting in media beds helps.
issues.symptom: Solids building up in grow beds

cons: RO system cost and waste water
water_source_profiles.name: Reverse Osmosis (RO) Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Gold standard for precision growing. Always add CalMag first when using RO.
pros: Blank slate — total control over mineral content

dechlorination.chloramine: Does NOT gas off — must use Campden tablets or activated carbon filter
dechlorination.chlorine: Let sit uncovered 24 hours (gasses off) or use dechlorinator drops
dechlorination.how_to_tell: Check city water report. Most modern cities use chloramine.

water_source_profiles.name: Municipal Tap Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Good enough for most grows. Dechlorinate and pH-adjust. Get a water report from your city.

cons: May contain iron, sulfur, or heavy metals
water_source_profiles.name: Well Water
water_source_profiles.notes: MUST get a water test before using. High iron or sulfur can cause lockout. Great if your well has clean water with moderate minerals.

water_source_profiles.name: Distilled Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Same as RO but purchased instead of filtered. Only practical for 1-2 plant grows.

advanced_techniques.description: Elevating CO2 from ambient (400ppm) to 1000-1500ppm increases photosynthesis rate by 20-40% when light and temperature are also increased.

methods.notes: Most common. Tank refills every 2-4 weeks for a tent.
Produces heat and humidity — better for large rooms with AC.
Produces ~100-200ppm boost. Only useful for small sealed spaces. Not worth it for most.

advanced_techniques.name: CO2 Supplementation

ppfd_co2_correlation.notes: Ambient CO2 sufficient at low light
Slight benefit from supplementation
Moderate benefit — cost-effective threshold
Strong benefit — diminishing returns above this
Maximum practical benefit for most grows
Commercial intensity — requires perfect environment

requirements: Sealed grow space (CO2 escapes quickly from leaky rooms)
High light intensity (>600 PPFD — plants can't use extra CO2 without sufficient light)
Higher temperature tolerance (82-86°F optimal with CO2 vs 75-80°F without)
CO2 controller or timer

advanced_techniques.description: Manipulating environmental and irrigation parameters to push the plant toward vegetative or generative (flowering/fruiting) growth.
advanced_techniques.name: Crop Steering
advanced_techniques.notes: Crop steering is most effective in hydro/coco where irrigation frequency is highly controllable. Soil growers have less precision due to soil buffering.

vegetative_steering.goal: More leaf/stem growth, larger plant framework

best_practices: Spray during lights-off or first/last 30 minutes of light cycle (stomata open, no light burn)
Use fine mist sprayer — coverage is key
pH foliar solution to 6.2-6.5 (leaf surface slightly higher pH than root zone)
EC should be very low (0.2-0.5) — leaves burn easily
Never foliar spray during flower (moisture in buds → mold/bud rot)
Best for micronutrient deficiency correction (CalMag, iron, magnesium)

common_foliar_products.frequency: Weekly preventive, 3-day intervals for active infestations

advanced_techniques.description: Spraying nutrient solution directly onto leaves for rapid uptake. Bypasses root zone entirely.
advanced_techniques.name: Foliar Feeding

advanced_techniques.description: Strengthens cell walls, increases stem thickness, improves heat/drought/pest resistance.
important_notes: ALWAYS add silica to water FIRST before other nutrients (raises pH significantly)
Let silica mix for 5-10 minutes before adding base nutrients
Silica is incompatible with concentrated nutrient solutions — never mix undiluted
Discontinue in late flower (last 2-3 weeks)
advanced_techniques.name: Silica (Potassium Silicate)

advanced_techniques.description: Introduces beneficial bacteria/fungi to the root zone to outcompete pathogens, improve nutrient uptake, and protect against root diseases.
important_notes: Do NOT use hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) with beneficial microbes — it kills them
Choose either sterile (H2O2) OR beneficial (Hydroguard) approach — not both
Beneficials need warmish water (65-75°F) to thrive
Reapply after any reservoir change or system cleaning
advanced_techniques.name: Beneficial Microbes & Root Inoculants

products.name: Hydroguard
products.notes: The standard for DWC/RDWC. Add at every res change.

products.dosing: 1 tsp/gal (water-in) or dust roots at transplant
products.name: Great White
products.notes: Best for soil/coco. Less effective in pure hydro.

products.name: Recharge
products.notes: Feeds existing soil microbes. Great for living soil.

products.name: Mammoth P
products.notes: Most effective in flower when P demand is highest.

advanced_techniques.description: Removing select fan leaves to improve light penetration, airflow, and direct energy to bud sites.
advanced_techniques.name: Defoliation (Strategic Leaf Removal)
rules: Never remove more than 20-30% of leaves at once (except schwazze technique)
Only defoliate healthy, vigorous plants — never stressed ones
Autoflowers: very light defoliation only — they can't recover from heavy removal
Always leave the top 2-3 fan leaves on each branch (they feed the bud below)

timing.method: Remove lower leaves that get no light (lollipop)
Major defoliation day 1 and day 21 of flower (schwazzing)
Selective tuck or remove leaves blocking bud sites

steps: Rinse coco thoroughly (remove dust, salts, shipping residue)
Soak in CalMag solution (5 ml/gal) for 8-24 hours
Drain and rinse again with plain water
Soak again in light nutrient solution (EC 0.5-0.8) for 4-8 hours
Drain to field capacity — ready to use
buffering_protocol.why: Raw/new coco must be buffered to pre-saturate the CEC sites with calcium. Otherwise, plants will show CalMag deficiency for weeks.

calmag_science.cec_explanation: Coco has a high cation exchange capacity (CEC). The negatively-charged coco fiber attracts positively-charged Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions, locking them away from roots.
deficiency_symptoms: Yellowing between leaf veins while veins stay green (magnesium)

dosing.new_unbuffered_coco: 5 ml/gal CalMag soak for 24h before first use

calmag_science.led_increases_demand: LED grow lights lack the infrared heat of HPS, making plants work harder to transpire. This reduces calcium transport (which moves passively with water flow), increasing CalMag demand by ~30%.
calmag_science.ro_water_compounds: RO water has zero minerals — combined with coco's CEC, plants get ZERO calcium without supplementation.
calmag_science.why_needed: Coco coir's cation exchange sites naturally bind calcium and magnesium, making them unavailable to plants. Every watering must include CalMag.

coco_specific.description: Coco-specific management sections for coco coir grows.

dryback_monitoring.description: Dryback % = how much the pot weight decreases between feedings. The #1 crop steering tool in coco.
dryback_monitoring.how_to_measure: Weigh pot immediately after feeding (saturated weight) and before next feeding (dry weight). Dryback% = (saturated - dry) / saturated x 100
dryback_monitoring.never_let_coco_dry_fully: Unlike soil, coco should NEVER fully dry out. Dry coco becomes hydrophobic (repels water), concentrates salts to toxic levels, and kills beneficial microbes.

danger_zone.effect: Salt concentration dangerous, anaerobic conditions possible, hydrophobic surface

vegetative_steering.effect: Keeps substrate wet = vegetative growth (leaves, stems)

fertigation_frequency.automation: Automated drip/pump timers make high-frequency fertigation practical. Manual watering 5x/day is unsustainable — invest in automation.

flush.notes: Flush with plain water for 3-7 days

late_veg.notes: Roots filling pot = more frequent feeds

mid_flower.notes: Maximum water/nutrient demand. High frequency essential.

seedling.notes: Keep coco moist but not soaked

fertigation_frequency.principle: In coco, every watering is a feeding. More frequent smaller feedings = better results than infrequent large ones.

pot_sizing.autoflower_exception: Start autoflowers in final pot — they don't recover well from transplant shock.

progression.notes: Small pot encourages roots to fill quickly
Transplant when roots visible at drain holes
Final pot for flower. 5gal most common.

reuse_protocol: Remove old root mass (shake/pull out large pieces)
Soak in enzyme solution (Hygrozyme, SLF-100) for 24h to break down dead roots
Drain and mix with 20-30% fresh coco to restore structure
reuse.when_to_discard: After 3 uses, heavy salt damage, pest contamination, or foul smell.

runoff_management.description: Input EC vs Runoff EC is the #1 diagnostic tool in coco growing.

interpretation.runoff_ec_higher_than_input: Salt accumulation in media. Solutions: increase runoff percentage, do a flush feed, reduce input EC.
interpretation.runoff_ec_lower_than_input: Plant is eating more than you're providing. Solution: increase input EC.
interpretation.runoff_ec_matches_input: Perfect balance — nutrients in = nutrients consumed. Ideal state.

runoff_management.when_to_flush: Runoff EC is >0.5 higher than input EC consistently, or visible salt buildup on pot surface.

equipment.name: Coco coir (pre-buffered preferred)
equipment.notes: Canna Coco, Mother Earth, or equivalent. Pre-buffered brands save the CalMag soaking step. Raw bricks need full buffering. Budget: ~$15-25 per bag.

equipment.name: Perlite
equipment.notes: Mix 30% perlite with 70% coco for ideal drainage. Coarse perlite preferred over fine.

equipment.name: Solo cups (16 oz) for seedlings
equipment.notes: Drill drain holes in bottom. First home for seedlings in coco.

equipment.name: 1-gallon pots (intermediate)
equipment.notes: Intermediate pot for early veg. Transplant from solo cup when roots fill it.

equipment.name: 3-5 gallon fabric pots (final)
equipment.notes: Fabric pots (Smart Pots, Root Pouch) for air pruning. 3-gal for small plants/autos, 5-gal for full-size photos. Fabric pots prevent root circling.

equipment.name: Saucers / drip trays
equipment.notes: Catch runoff from every pot. Size to hold maximum feeding volume. Empty after feeding — don't let pots sit in runoff.

equipment.name: Watering can or pump sprayer
equipment.notes: For hand watering 1-20 plants. Pump sprayer makes even distribution easier.

equipment.name: Drip system (optional for 10+ plants)
equipment.notes: Timer + pump + emitters for automated fertigation. Essential at scale. See drip grow type config for details.

equipment.name: Reservoir (opaque)
equipment.notes: Mix nutrients in advance. Opaque to prevent algae. Size for 1-2 days of feeding.

equipment.name: pH meter (accurate to 0.1)
equipment.notes: Calibrate weekly. Coco pH range: 5.8-6.2. Bluelab or Apera recommended.

equipment.name: EC/TDS meter
equipment.notes: Input AND runoff EC. The delta is your most important number.

equipment.name: Kitchen scale
equipment.notes: Weigh pots to track dryback precisely. The poor man's substrate sensor.

equipment.name: Jeweler's loupe (60-100x)
equipment.notes: Trichome inspection.

equipment.name: Grow light (LED preferred)
equipment.notes: LED lights increase CalMag demand in coco (less infrared heat = less transpiration). Account for this.

equipment.name: Exhaust fan + carbon filter
equipment.notes: Odor control and air exchange.

equipment.name: Oscillating fan(s)
equipment.notes: Canopy airflow.

equipment.name: Temperature/humidity controller
equipment.notes: Climate control.

equipment.name: Dehumidifier
equipment.notes: Essential during flower.

equipment.name: Base nutrient system (coco-specific preferred)
equipment.notes: Canna Coco A+B, GH Flora Trio, or Athena. Coco-specific formulas have boosted CalMag.

equipment.name: CalMag supplement
equipment.notes: NON-NEGOTIABLE in coco. Every feeding, every time. 1-3 ml/gal depending on stage, water source, and light type.

equipment.name: pH Up/Down solutions
equipment.notes: pH 5.8-6.2 for coco.

equipment.name: Hydroguard
equipment.notes: Root protection in warm moist coco.

equipment.name: Silica (Armor Si)
equipment.notes: Stem strength. Add FIRST.

equipment.name: PK booster (Liquid Kool Bloom)
equipment.notes: Flower supplement.

equipment.name: Yucca extract (wetting agent)
equipment.notes: Emergency: rewets hydrophobic coco. Preventive: improves water distribution. Thermo-stable, food-safe.

initial_buffering_protocol: Mix CalMag solution at 150 ppm calcium (approximately 5ml/gal of most CalMag products)
Soak coco in CalMag solution for 8-24 hours
Drain. DO NOT rinse after buffering (you want those CEC sites loaded).
This pre-loads the exchange sites so they don't steal calcium from your nutrient solution.

ongoing_supplementation.dose: Add CalMag to EVERY feeding at 1-3 ml/gal (200-400 ppm Ca target in final solution)
ongoing_supplementation.led_consideration: LED lights increase calcium demand. Add 1 ml/gal extra CalMag under LEDs vs HPS.
ongoing_supplementation.soft_water_consideration: If using RO or soft water (<100 ppm base), use maximum CalMag (3 ml/gal). Hard water (>200 ppm) may need less.
ongoing_supplementation.when_to_increase: If seeing calcium deficiency (brown spots on lower/middle leaves) despite adequate EC, increase CalMag.

calcium_magnesium_buffering.why_coco_needs_calmag: Coco coir has high Cation Exchange Capacity (CEC). It naturally binds calcium and magnesium ions, making them unavailable to plants. You must 'buffer' (pre-load) the CEC sites and continuously supplement.

50_50_coco_perlite.best_for: Very high frequency fertigation. Fast dry-back. Aggressive crop steering.

70_30_coco_perlite.best_for: MOST grows. The gold standard ratio. High frequency fertigation.

pure_coco.best_for: Autopot/bottom-feed systems where drainage doesn't matter as much

washing_protocol: Rinse with plain water 3-5 times until runoff EC is below 0.5
This removes excess sodium and potassium salts from manufacturing

high_frequency_fertigation.concept: Coco thrives on frequent, small irrigations with nutrients in every watering. Never plain water.

danger_zone.notes: NEVER let coco dry more than 20%. Causes salt concentration spike and root damage.

generative_steering.notes: Allow more dry-back overnight. Promotes flowering/ripening.

vegetative_steering.notes: Keep coco wet. Minimal dry-back. Promotes stretching/growth.

early_veg.notes: 1-2 times daily. Increasing as roots fill container.

flower.notes: High frequency. Peak demand. Smaller shot sizes.

late_flower.notes: Slightly reduce if plant is finishing. Still never plain water.

late_veg.notes: Multiple daily fertigations. Roots established. Push growth.

seedling.notes: Once daily. Small container stays moist. Light feed (0.4 EC).

high_frequency_fertigation.plain_water_rule: In coco, EVERY watering should contain nutrients. Plain water causes calcium/magnesium deficiencies because coco's CEC releases bound Ca/Mg when fresh water passes through (buffering effect).

shot_size_calculation.method: Target 3-5% of container volume per irrigation event.

reconditioning_protocol: Remove old root mass (shake/pull large roots, don't stress about fine roots)
Rinse thoroughly with plain water (3-5 times, target EC < 0.5 in runoff)
Add fresh perlite to replace any that was removed/degraded (coco compresses over time)
Mix in 10-20% fresh coco to restore structure
when_to_replace: If persistent pH issues despite proper input
If root disease occurred in previous grow (start fresh)
If coco is visibly degraded/muddy in texture

flushing_protocol: Use nutrient solution at 50% strength (never plain water in coco)
Check runoff EC. Repeat if still high.
Resume normal feeding once runoff EC within 0.3 of input

runoff_ec_monitoring.critical: Runoff EC >1.0 above input. Flush immediately. Multiple high-runoff irrigations until equalized.
runoff_ec_monitoring.ideal: Runoff EC within 0.3 of input EC. Balanced.
runoff_ec_monitoring.warning: Runoff EC 0.3-0.8 above input. Salt building. Increase runoff % next irrigation.

runoff_management.why_runoff_matters: Runoff flushes accumulated salts from CEC sites. Without runoff, EC builds in media regardless of input EC.

flush_timing.coco: 7-14 days reduced nutrients → plain water last 3-5 days
flush_timing.notes: Flushing is debated — some studies show no difference. At minimum, reduce nutrients in final week.
flush_timing.soil: 14-21 days plain water (soil holds nutrients longer)

other_harvest_indicators: Most pistils (hairs) have darkened and curled in
Calyxes are swollen and dense
Fan leaves are yellowing and falling off naturally
Trichome stalks are visible and heads are enlarged
Breeder's recommended flowering time has passed

trichome_ratios.notes: Harvest early for more THC-dominant, less CBN. Energetic, racy high. Slight reduction in yield.
Standard harvest window. Mix of THC and CBN. Full-spectrum effects.
Extended harvest for max CBN conversion. Heavy body high. Good for pain/sleep. Slightly degraded THC.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Micro → Gro → Bloom (always Micro first)
nutrient_brands.name: General Hydroponics Flora Trio

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Part A → Epsom → Part B (dissolve each fully before adding next)
nutrient_brands.name: Jack's 321
nutrient_brands.notes: Jack's 321 is 3.6g Part A, 2.4g Cal Nit, 1.2g Epsom per gallon at full strength. Extremely cost-effective for large grows.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Core → Bloom
nutrient_brands.name: Athena Pro Line
nutrient_brands.notes: Commercial-grade. Simple 2-part with integrated CalMag. Popular in commercial cannabis facilities.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: A → B (separate for veg and bloom)
nutrient_brands.name: Advanced Nutrients pH Perfect (Sensi)
nutrient_brands.notes: Self-adjusting pH technology (claims no pH adjustment needed). Expensive but popular for beginners who struggle with pH.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Shake VERY well before use (thick concentrate settles). One bottle at a time.
nutrient_brands.name: General Hydroponics FloraNova
nutrient_brands.notes: Simplest option — one bottle per phase. Great for beginners. Shake well — concentrate separates.
nutrient_brands.type: 1-part (two bottles — one for veg, one for bloom)

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Dissolve powder in warm water, stir well
nutrient_brands.name: Greenleaf Nutrients Mega Crop
nutrient_brands.notes: One powder does everything — includes CalMag. Incredibly cost-effective at ~$0.02/gal. Community favorite on r/microgrowery.

environment.container: Glass mason jars (quart size). Fill 75% full.

humidity_packs.boveda_58: For drier preference or long-term storage.
humidity_packs.boveda_62: Maintains 62% RH. Set-and-forget. Replace when crispy.
humidity_packs.grove_bags: Heat-sealed bags with built-in humidity regulation. No burping needed.

indicators_of_good_cure: Smooth, flavorful smoke — no harshness or hay smell
Buds are slightly sticky but not wet
Strong terpene aroma when jar is opened
Ash burns to white/light gray (not black)
method: Trim buds to final shape (dry trim or finish wet trim)
Place in mason jars — fill 3/4 full to allow air exchange
Week 1: burp 2-3x daily for 10-15 minutes (release moisture and gases)
Week 2-4: burp 1x daily for 5-10 minutes
Month 2+: burp 1x weekly or seal completely
Cure for minimum 2 weeks, ideal is 4-8 weeks. Some strains improve for 6+ months.

common_mistakes: Drying too fast (>3 days) — harsh smoke, hay smell, terpene loss
Drying too slow (>14 days) — mold risk, diminishing returns
Fan blowing directly on buds — uneven drying, crispy exterior/moist interior
Temperature too high (>70°F) — terpene evaporation, harsh taste

environment.airflow: Gentle circulation — fan NOT pointed at buds. Just keep air moving.

method: Cut branches 12-18 inches long
Optionally remove large fan leaves (wet trim) or leave intact (dry trim)
Hang upside down on line/rack with 6+ inches between branches
Small branches should snap (not bend) when done — 5-7 days minimum
Stems should snap cleanly; if they bend, keep drying

long_term_storage.degradation: THC slowly converts to CBN over time. Light, heat, air, and moisture accelerate degradation.
long_term_storage.method: Sealed mason jars or vacuum-sealed bags with Boveda 58% in dark, cool place
long_term_storage.notes: Properly cured and stored cannabis can remain excellent for 12+ months. Freezing is debated — can damage trichomes if not done carefully.

calmag_guide.description: CalMag is NON-NEGOTIABLE in coco. Dose depends on water source and light type.

ro_water_hps.notes: RO water has zero baseline minerals. Must supplement heavily.

ro_water_led.notes: Worst case: RO + LED + coco = maximum CalMag demand. Start high.

tap_water_hps.notes: Tap water has baseline minerals. HPS lights drive more transpiration.

tap_water_led.notes: LED = less transpiration = plant moves less CalMag through tissues. Supplement more.

coco_buffering_protocol.description: Raw coco MUST be buffered before use. Pre-buffered brands still benefit from a rinse.
coco_buffering_protocol.step_1: Rinse coco thoroughly with plain water to remove dust, salt, and shipping chemicals.
coco_buffering_protocol.step_2: Soak in CalMag solution (EC 0.8, pH 5.8) for 8-24 hours.
coco_buffering_protocol.step_4: Repeat soak with fresh CalMag solution for another 8 hours.
coco_buffering_protocol.step_5: Drain. Coco is now buffered and ready to use.
coco_buffering_protocol.why: Coco's cation exchange sites are loaded with sodium and potassium from the ocean. Buffering replaces these with calcium and magnesium. Without buffering, the coco steals CalMag from every feeding.

coco_reuse_protocol.description: Coco can be reused 2-3 grows with proper cleaning.
coco_reuse_protocol.step_1: Remove root debris by hand or sieving.
coco_reuse_protocol.step_2: Flush with plain water at 3x volume to remove old nutrients.
coco_reuse_protocol.step_3: Re-buffer with CalMag solution (same as new coco buffering).
coco_reuse_protocol.step_4: Optional: treat with enzyme product (Sensizym, Cannazym) to break down remaining root material.
coco_reuse_protocol.step_5: Dry and store, or use immediately.

fertigation_frequency_by_stage.description: Coco feeding frequency ramps throughout the grow. More frequent = better.

golden_rules: CalMag in EVERY feeding. Non-negotiable. The #1 coco mistake is insufficient CalMag.
Every watering is a feeding (except flush). Never plain water in coco.
Feed to 10-20% runoff every time. Without runoff, salts accumulate unchecked.
Never let coco dry completely — it becomes hydrophobic and repels water.
Buffer new coco before ANY plant touches it. Raw coco steals calcium.
Lift your pots. Weight = water content. The best moisture meter is your hand.
Multiple small feedings > one big watering. Frequency is king in coco.
Input EC vs Runoff EC delta is your #1 diagnostic. Check every 2-3 days.
Coco flush still includes CalMag (0.5 ml/gal). Coco never stops exchanging cations.
Fabric pots for air pruning. No root circling. Better drainage. The coco standard.
quick_reference.nutrient_mixing_order: 1) Silica (if using) → pH to 5.8 → 2) CalMag → 3) Flora Micro → 4) Flora Gro → 5) Flora Bloom → 6) Supplements → 7) pH adjust final

ph_range.notes: Coco is naturally pH-stable around 5.8-6.2. Less drift than other hydro methods.

photo_large.notes: For large photoperiod plants with long veg.

runoff_ec_interpretation.delta_0_3_to_0_5: Watch. Salts starting to accumulate. Increase runoff % or flush soon.
runoff_ec_interpretation.delta_above_0_5: Flush now. Salt buildup beyond safe levels.

scale_tiers.description: First-time grower or single-plant hobbyist. Manual management, learning-focused.
scale_tiers.name: Solo / Single Plant
scale_tiers.notes: Hands-on learning experience. Manual checks 1-2x daily. Ideal for beginners.

scale_tiers.description: Hobbyist tent grow. Light automation helpful but not required.
scale_tiers.name: Small Tent (2-8 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Timer-controlled lights, humidity controller. Sensor monitoring recommended.

scale_tiers.description: Dedicated grower with multiple tents/rooms. Automation saves significant time.
scale_tiers.name: Multi-Tent (9-24 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Multiple environments to manage. Automated alerts essential. Consider perpetual harvest.

scale_tiers.description: Licensed commercial operation. Full automation, compliance tracking, batch management.
scale_tiers.name: Commercial Room (25-100 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: SOPs required. Staff training. Regulatory compliance. Insurance requirements.

scale_tiers.description: Large-scale production facility. Enterprise automation, SCADA integration, dedicated staff.
scale_tiers.name: Warehouse Scale (100+ plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Multiple rooms, dedicated HVAC, industrial nutrient dosing, video surveillance.

common_problems.cause: Too cold, too wet, or bad seed
common_problems.solution: Ensure 75-80°F. Starter medium moist not soaking. Try different seed after 7 days.

common_problems.cause: Skipped or rushed CalMag pre-soak
common_problems.solution: Coco MUST be buffered. Unbuffered coco will steal calcium from every feeding, causing persistent deficiency that looks like every other problem. Buffer before ANY plant touches the coco.

stages.description: Seed cracks open and taproot emerges. Start seeds in Rapid Rooters, rockwool starter cubes, or directly in pre-buffered coco in solo cups. If using coco: the coco MUST be buffered with CalMag before the seed touches it.

environment.notes: Keep seeds in darkness. Heat mat at 78°F. Humidity dome. No light until sprout emerges.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse germination: heat mat + humidity dome. Temp swings may slow germination.

greenhouse.notes: Heat mat recommended.

environment_overrides.notes: Start seeds indoors. Outdoor coco dries unpredictably.

outdoor.notes: Always germinate indoors.

health_checks: Is the taproot visible and white?
Is the coco properly buffered (CalMag soaked)?

medium.notes: Seeds are in a humidity dome, not being fertigated yet. While seeds germinate: prepare coco by buffering with CalMag solution (EC 0.8, soak 8+ hours, drain, repeat). This step is NON-NEGOTIABLE for raw coco.
medium.pot_size: solo cup (16 oz) or starter plug

stages.name: Germination

nutrients.approach: None — seeds contain all energy needed to germinate. But the coco itself must be pre-buffered with CalMag.

tasks.description: Rinse raw coco thoroughly to remove salts and dust. Soak in CalMag solution (EC 0.8, pH 5.8) for 8-24 hours. Drain. Repeat soak. Drain. This charges the cation exchange sites so coco won't steal calcium from your nutrient solution. Pre-buffered brands (Canna, Mother Earth) need less prep but still benefit from a CalMag rinse.
tasks.name: Buffer coco coir

tasks.description: Mix 70% coco and 30% perlite by volume. This provides ideal drainage and air-to-water ratio. Some growers use straight coco — it works but drains slower.
tasks.name: Mix coco and perlite

tasks.description: Soak seeds 12-24 hours in plain pH 6.0 water. Place in Rapid Rooters, pre-soaked rockwool cubes, or directly in pre-buffered coco in solo cups. Cover with humidity dome.
tasks.name: Start seeds

tasks.description: After 24-72 hours, look for white taproot. Do not disturb.
tasks.name: Check for taproot

common_problems.cause: CalMag deficiency — coco is stealing calcium despite buffering
common_problems.solution: Increase CalMag to 1.5 ml/gal. This is the most common coco problem. If using LED lights and/or RO water, CalMag demand is even higher.

common_problems.cause: Feeding too often or too much volume for the tiny root zone
common_problems.solution: Let the solo cup dry down more between feedings. Lift to check weight. Seedlings in solo cups should dry in 1-2 days.

common_problems.cause: Solo cup too small, or environment too dry/hot
common_problems.solution: Normal as roots fill the cup. Transplant to 1-gallon when roots reach the bottom. Water more frequently if needed before transplant.

stages.description: First true leaves develop. Seedling is in solo cup with buffered coco/perlite. Begin very light fertigation — every watering includes nutrients in coco (never plain water). One feeding per day is enough. Water in a small ring around the stem, not the entire cup — encourage roots to search outward.

environment.notes: Gentle light. Remove humidity dome gradually over 3-5 days. Seedlings in coco dry faster than in soil — check daily.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: excellent for coco seedlings. Watch for heat-driven drying.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse coco: monitor drying rate in warm conditions.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor coco seedlings: rain disrupts fertigation schedule. Cover or bring inside during rain.

extra_problems.cause: Uncovered outdoor containers
extra_problems.solution: Cover containers during rain. Rain washes out nutrients and CalMag — you'll need to re-feed after rain.

outdoor.notes: Outdoor coco: rain management is essential from day 1.

health_checks: Runoff EC close to input EC?
No brown spots on cotyledons (CalMag deficiency)?

feed_frequency.notes: Once per day. Water in a small ring around the stem. Feed until you see slight runoff from the bottom of the solo cup.

medium.notes: EVERY watering includes nutrients in coco. Never plain water (except during flush). Water until slight runoff. Lift the cup to judge weight — heavy = wet, light = time to feed. Coco in solo cups dries fast.

runoff_ec_ratio.notes: Runoff EC should be within 0.3 of input EC. If higher, flush.

stages.name: Seedling

nutrients.approach: Quarter strength with CalMag in every feeding. Coco seedlings need less than soil seedlings because nutrients are delivered more efficiently.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root protection. Warm moist coco is a Pythium environment.

tasks.description: First feeding with quarter-strength nutrients + CalMag. Water in a ring around the stem. Feed until slight runoff from bottom.
tasks.name: Begin fertigation

tasks.description: Lift the solo cup. Heavy = still wet, skip feeding. Light = dry, time to feed. This is the most important coco skill.
tasks.name: Daily weight check

tasks.description: Collect runoff and check EC. Should be within 0.3 of input. Higher = salt building up.
tasks.name: Check runoff EC

tasks.description: Crack dome day 1, remove for a few hours day 2, full remove by day 3-5.
tasks.name: Remove humidity dome gradually

transition_signals: Roots visible at bottom drain holes
Cup drying in less than 24 hours

common_problems.cause: LED lights + RO water + coco = triple CalMag demand. Or coco wasn't fully buffered.
common_problems.solution: Increase CalMag to 2.0 ml/gal. If persistent: re-buffer the coco (flush with CalMag solution at EC 1.0). LED + RO + coco is the highest CalMag demand combination.

common_problems.cause: Insufficient runoff at each feeding — salts accumulating
common_problems.solution: Feed to 15-20% runoff for 2-3 days to flush salts. Then return to 10-15% runoff. Never feed without generating some runoff in coco.

common_problems.cause: Transplant shock or root zone too wet in oversized pot
common_problems.solution: Water only around the root ball initially, not the entire pot. Roots need to grow into the new coco. Over-wetting the entire pot before roots reach it creates an anaerobic zone.

stages.description: Rapid growth phase. Transplant from solo cup to 1-gallon pot when roots fill the cup. Increase fertigation frequency — coco should be fed 1-2 times per day now. Vegetative steering: keep dryback low (5-10%) with frequent feedings to push green growth. CalMag at every feeding.

environment.notes: Ramp up light. VPD rising. Plant growing rapidly in coco — expect faster growth than soil due to better air-to-water ratio.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: excellent for coco veg. Good light + protected from rain.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse is ideal for coco growing.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor coco: rain events flush nutrients and disrupt EC. Feed after every rain.

extra_tasks.description: Rain washes nutrients from coco. After any rain event, fertigate with normal-strength solution to restore EC.
extra_tasks.name: Re-feed after rain

outdoor.notes: Outdoor coco: rain is your biggest enemy.

health_checks: No CalMag deficiency signs (brown spots on new growth)?
Coco not drying below target dryback?

feed_frequency.notes: 2x/day as roots establish in the new pot. Feed to 10-15% runoff each time. Multiple small feedings > one big watering.

medium.notes: Vegetative steering: low dryback, frequent feedings. Never let coco in 1-gallon pots dry more than 10%. Multiple small feedings are better than one large one — this keeps EC stable in the root zone. Transplant to final pot (3-5 gal) when roots fill the 1-gallon.
medium.pot_size: 1 gallon → transplant to 3-5 gallon final pot late in this stage

runoff_ec_ratio.notes: Runoff EC within 0.3 of input. Monitor every 3 days.

stages.name: Early Vegetative

nutrients.approach: Half strength. Plant is growing fast but root zone is still expanding. CalMag at every feeding — increase if using LED + RO water.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root protection in warm coco.

supplements.name: Silica (Armor Si)
supplements.purpose: Strengthen stems. Add FIRST before other nutrients. pH down to 5.8 before adding Flora series.

tasks.description: When roots are visible at solo cup drain holes, transplant to 1-gallon pot with pre-buffered coco/perlite mix. Water in with quarter-strength nutrient + CalMag.
tasks.name: Transplant to 1-gallon

tasks.description: Ramp to 2x/day in the 1-gallon pot. Each feeding: nutrient solution to 10-15% runoff. Never plain water.
tasks.name: Increase fertigation frequency

tasks.description: Collect runoff from representative plants. Input EC vs runoff EC delta should be <0.3. Rising runoff EC = flush needed.
tasks.name: Check runoff EC

tasks.description: When roots fill the 1-gallon (pot dries in less than a day), transplant to final 3-5 gallon fabric pot. Pre-wet the coco in the new pot with CalMag solution.
tasks.name: Transplant to final pot (3-5 gal)

tasks.description: Start LST and/or topping once plant has 4-5 nodes. Coco plants recover from training quickly.
tasks.name: Begin training

training.description: Bend and tie branches. Start at 4-5 nodes. Coco plants respond fast to training.
Cut above 4th or 5th node. Recovery is 2-4 days in coco — faster than soil.

transition_signals: Root ball filling 50%+ of final pot

common_problems.cause: Salt accumulation from weeks of feeding
common_problems.issue: Runoff EC climbing despite good input
common_problems.solution: Flush with plain water + CalMag at 3x pot volume. Normal in coco — needs periodic flushes. Increase runoff target to 20% temporarily.

common_problems.cause: Vegged too long — coco grows fast
common_problems.solution: Flip now. Supercrop tall branches. Plan shorter veg next time.

common_problems.cause: Root-bound in plastic pot
common_problems.solution: Use fabric pots for air pruning. If already in plastic: transplant to larger pot or accept slightly constrained growth.

stages.description: Plant reaches target size in final pot (3-5 gal). Full vegetative fertigation: 2-3 times per day, feed to runoff every time. Dryback stays low (5-10%) for maximum vegetative push. Pre-flip flush to reset root zone salts. Last chance for heavy training.

environment.notes: Full veg intensity. Plant transpiring heavily. Coco drying faster now — may need 3x/day feedings.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: light dep gives flip control.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse: light dep recommended for coco.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor: natural photoperiod triggers flip. No manual control unless using light dep.

extra_tasks.description: Flower triggers when daylight drops below ~14 hours. Know your local sunset times.
extra_tasks.name: Monitor daylight hours

outdoor.notes: Outdoor coco: photoperiod controls the flip.

health_checks: Plant at target size (50-66% of final)?

feed_frequency.notes: 3x/day for vigorous late-veg plants. Each feeding to 10-15% runoff. First feed within 1 hour of lights on.

medium.notes: Maximum vegetative fertigation. Low dryback, frequent feedings. Plants in coco drink heavily in late veg — if pots are light within 6-8 hours, add another feeding. Pre-flip flush: run plain pH'd water + CalMag (no base nutrients) at 3x pot volume to reset salts before flower.

runoff_ec_ratio.notes: Check every 2 days. Pre-flip flush when runoff EC delta exceeds 0.3 persistently.

stages.name: Late Vegetative

nutrients.approach: Three-quarter strength. Heavy nitrogen for veg growth. CalMag at every feeding.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root protection.

supplements.name: Silica (Armor Si)
supplements.purpose: Stem strength for flower weight. Add FIRST.

tasks.description: Three feedings per day: within 1 hour of lights on, midday, and 2-3 hours before lights off. Each to 10-15% runoff.
tasks.name: Ramp to 3x/day fertigation

tasks.description: Check every 2 days. Delta should be <0.3. Climbing runoff EC = salt accumulation.
tasks.name: Runoff EC monitoring

tasks.description: 2-3 days before flipping to 12/12: run plain pH'd water + CalMag (1 ml/gal, no base nutrients) through pots at 3x pot volume. This resets root zone salt load.
tasks.name: Pre-flip flush

tasks.description: Complete topping, LST, SCROG net filling. No heavy training after flip.
tasks.name: Final training

tasks.description: Defoliate lower growth. Level the canopy. Lollipop lower 1/3.
tasks.name: Canopy management

training.description: Install screen. Weave branches. Fill 70-80% before flip.
Remove lower 1/3 of growth.

common_problems.cause: Rapid growth outpacing CalMag supply — extremely common in coco during stretch
common_problems.solution: Increase CalMag to 3 ml/gal. Brown spots on new growth = calcium deficiency. In coco, CalMag is ALWAYS the first suspect.

common_problems.cause: Genetics or environment (too much DIF)
common_problems.solution: Supercrop tall branches. Reduce day/night temp difference to 5°F. Increase light intensity.

common_problems.cause: Heavy feeding + ratio change concentrating certain salts
common_problems.solution: Flush with pH'd water + CalMag (no base nutrients) at 2x pot volume. Resume at 80% strength.

stages.description: Flipped to 12/12. Explosive stretch — plant may double in height. Maintain high fertigation frequency (3-4x/day) to support explosive growth. Begin transitioning nutrient ratio from veg to bloom. CalMag demand peaks during stretch (rapid cell wall construction). Begin increasing dryback slightly as you transition toward generative steering.

environment.notes: Flip to 12/12. DIF of 10°F helps control stretch. Humidity dropping toward flower targets.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: light dep tarps control timing.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse: light dep for flip control.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor: natural photoperiod triggers stretch. Watch for light pollution.

extra_tasks.description: External light during dark period = hermaphrodites.
extra_tasks.name: Check for light pollution

outdoor.notes: Outdoor coco: stretch timing follows the sun.

feed_frequency.notes: 4x/day during stretch. Plant is growing explosively and drinking heavily. Each feeding to 10-15% runoff.

medium.notes: Transitioning from vegetative to generative steering. Increase dryback slightly (8-12%) while maintaining high frequency. This signals the plant to start shifting energy toward flower production. CalMag peaks during stretch — increase to 2.5-3 ml/gal.

runoff_ec_ratio.notes: Stretch is heavy-demand. Allow slightly larger delta (0.5) before flushing.

stages.name: Transition (Stretch)

nutrients.approach: Near full strength. Transition ratio: reduce Gro, increase Bloom. CalMag at peak — stretch demands maximum calcium for rapid cell wall construction.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root protection through flower.

supplements.name: Silica (Armor Si)
supplements.purpose: Support rapidly elongating stems. Add FIRST.

tasks.description: Zero light leaks during dark period. Even brief light causes hermaphrodites.
tasks.name: Flip to 12/12

tasks.description: Shift from veg to bloom over 3-5 days. Increase CalMag to 2.5 ml/gal for stretch calcium demand.
tasks.name: Transition nutrient ratio

tasks.description: Stretch is the highest water-demand period. 4 feedings per day, each to runoff.
tasks.name: Maintain 4x/day fertigation

tasks.description: Check daily during stretch. EC swings are rapid in this high-demand period.
tasks.name: Monitor runoff EC

tasks.description: Supercrop, tuck into SCROG net. Stay on top of it daily.
tasks.name: Manage stretch height

tasks.description: Watch for sex expression. Remove males immediately.
tasks.name: Check for preflowers

training.description: Bend tall stems 90°. Coco plants recover fast — 2-3 days.
Tuck daily. Coco growth is aggressive.

common_problems.cause: EC too high for this cultivar, or salt accumulation
common_problems.solution: Reduce EC by 0.2-0.4. Flush. Resume at lower strength. Coco delivers nutrients efficiently — less is often more.

common_problems.cause: Still steering vegetative — dryback too low, EC too low
common_problems.solution: Increase dryback to 15-18%. Increase EC. Delay first feed. More aggressive generative steering.

common_problems.cause: Coco dried out completely at some point
common_problems.issue: Coco going hydrophobic (water sitting on top)
common_problems.solution: Add a drop of wetting agent (yucca extract). Water slowly from multiple angles. Once coco goes hydrophobic, it takes 3-5 slow waterings to fully re-wet. PREVENTION: never let coco dry completely.

stages.description: Stretch ended. Buds forming and fattening. Switch to full generative steering: increase dryback to 12-18%, increase EC, feed 3-5x/day. This is where coco shines — the ability to steer the plant through fertigation frequency and dryback is coco's greatest advantage over soil.

environment.notes: Peak light. Humidity dropping for bud rot prevention. DIF of 10-12°F for terpenes. CO2 to 1000-1200 ppm if available.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: protected from rain, excellent for coco flower. Manage humidity.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse: ideal for coco flower if humidity is controlled.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor coco flower: rain during flower is dangerous — dilutes nutrients, promotes bud rot. Cover containers.

extra_problems.cause: Unprotected outdoor containers
extra_problems.solution: Cover containers. Re-feed after rain to restore EC. Shake plants to remove moisture from buds. Defoliate for airflow.

extra_tasks.description: Rain washes nutrients from coco AND promotes bud rot. Cover or move containers under shelter during rain events.
extra_tasks.name: Cover containers during rain

outdoor.notes: Outdoor coco flower: rain management is critical.

health_checks: Buds forming at all flower sites?

feed_frequency.notes: 4x/day. First feed delayed 1 hour after lights on (allow morning dryback for generative push). Last feed 3 hours before lights off.

medium.notes: GENERATIVE STEERING. Increase dryback to 12-18%. Delay first feed 1 hour after lights on. Increase EC to 2.2-2.6. The plant responds to mild drought stress by pushing energy into flowers. This is coco's greatest advantage — precise control over vegetative vs generative growth through fertigation timing and volume. Never let coco go fully dry (hydrophobic risk).

runoff_ec_ratio.notes: Higher EC feeding = faster salt buildup. Check every 2 days. Flush if delta >0.5.

stages.name: Early Flower

nutrients.approach: Full bloom. Heavy PK. EC 1.6-2.0. CalMag maintained at every feeding.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root protection.

supplements.name: Liquid Kool Bloom
supplements.purpose: PK booster for early flower.

tasks.description: Delay first fertigation to 1 hour after lights on. Increase dryback target to 12-18%. Increase EC. This pushes energy into flowers.
tasks.name: Switch to generative steering

tasks.description: High EC = fast salt buildup. Flush immediately if runoff EC exceeds input by >0.5.
tasks.name: Monitor runoff EC every 2 days

tasks.description: Remove fan leaves blocking bud sites. Open canopy interior. Critical for bud rot prevention in dense coco-grown plants.
tasks.name: Defoliate for airflow

tasks.description: Inspect flowers every 2 days. Dense coco-grown buds are susceptible.
tasks.name: Check for bud rot / PM

tasks.description: Run plain pH'd water + CalMag at 2-3x pot volume to reset salts. Resume normal feeding next irrigation.
tasks.name: Flush every 10-14 days

training.description: Remove blocking fan leaves. One session at start of early flower. Max 20% of leaves at once.
Remove growth below SCROG net or lower 1/3.

common_problems.cause: EC too high + insufficient flushing
common_problems.solution: Emergency flush: 3-5x pot volume plain water + CalMag. Resume at 80% previous EC. More frequent flushes.

common_problems.cause: Dryback went too far — coco dried completely
common_problems.solution: EMERGENCY. Add yucca extract wetting agent. Water very slowly from multiple angles. May take 3-5 slow waterings to fully re-wet. This is why you never let coco dry completely.

common_problems.cause: EC too high for cultivar's tolerance
common_problems.solution: Reduce EC by 0.3-0.4. Some cultivars can't handle high EC. Better slightly lower EC than burned plants.

stages.description: Peak bud development. Maximum generative steering: highest dryback (15-22%), quality-focused EC (1.6-2.0), 4-5 feedings per day. Coco delivers nutrients efficiently so yields can match or exceed hydro. Flush every 7-10 days to prevent salt lockout at these high EC levels.

environment.notes: Peak light. Humidity under 50%. DIF 12°F+ for terpenes. CO2 at 1200-1500 ppm if supplementing.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: dehumidification at maximum during mid-flower.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse: humidity management is key.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor coco mid-flower: rain + dense buds = extreme rot risk. Shelter containers.

extra_tasks.description: Move under cover or use tarps. Rain during peak bloom destroys crops.
extra_tasks.name: Shelter containers during rain

outdoor.notes: Outdoor: shelter is critical during mid-flower.

feed_frequency.notes: 4-5x/day. Delay first feed 1-2 hours after lights on for maximum morning dryback. Last feed 3-4 hours before lights off.

medium.notes: PEAK GENERATIVE STEERING. Maximum dryback (15-22%), quality-focused EC (1.6-2.0), delayed first feed. Every calorie goes to bud production. Flush every 7-10 days — at these EC levels, salts accumulate fast. Never let coco go completely dry — hydrophobic coco during peak flower is catastrophic.

runoff_ec_ratio.notes: At peak EC, salt buildup is rapid. Flush if delta exceeds 0.5. Check every 2 days.

stages.name: Mid Flower (Peak Bloom)

nutrients.approach: Full bloom. Peak PK. EC 1.6-2.0. CalMag at every feeding.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root protection.

supplements.name: Liquid Kool Bloom
supplements.purpose: PK booster at peak. Drives bud density.

tasks.description: Dryback 15-22%, EC 1.6-2.0, first feed delayed 1-2 hours. Monitor plant response daily.
tasks.name: Peak generative steering

tasks.description: Run plain pH'd water + CalMag (1 ml/gal) at 3x pot volume. Reset salt accumulation. Resume normal feeding next irrigation.
tasks.name: Flush every 7-10 days

tasks.description: At peak EC, check every 2 days. Delta >0.5 = emergency flush.
tasks.name: Runoff EC monitoring

tasks.description: Dense coco-grown buds are rot targets. Pull apart large colas gently.
tasks.name: Daily bud inspection

tasks.description: Trellis, yo-yos, or stakes. Heavy coco-grown buds snap branches.
tasks.name: Support heavy branches

common_problems.cause: pH lockout or root problems, not natural senescence
common_problems.solution: Check runoff pH. Gradual yellowing = good. Sudden browning = problem. Flush if pH is off.

common_problems.cause: Light stress or heat
common_problems.solution: Raise/dim lights 10-15%. Late-flower buds are light-sensitive.

stages.description: Buds ripening. Trichomes transitioning milky to amber. Reduce EC and feeding frequency. The plant is finishing — it needs less. Some fan leaf yellowing is normal and desired (nutrient fade). Begin tapering toward flush.

environment.notes: Slightly reduced light. Cooler nights bring out colors. Low humidity for rot prevention.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: heating for cold nights.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse: supplemental heat during cold nights.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor: monitor frost forecasts. Frost kills cannabis.

extra_tasks.description: Below 32°F = death. Cover or harvest.
extra_tasks.name: Monitor frost forecasts

outdoor.notes: Outdoor: late season weather controls harvest timing.

feed_frequency.notes: Reduce to 3x/day. Plant is drinking less. Each feeding to 10-15% runoff.

medium.notes: Tapering. Plant is finishing. Reduce feeding frequency and EC. Fan leaf yellowing is natural — plant is consuming stored nutrients. Don't panic-feed a yellowing late-flower plant.

runoff_ec_ratio.notes: Continue monitoring. Drawing down salts before flush.

stages.name: Late Flower (Ripening)

nutrients.approach: Reducing. EC 1.2-1.6. Lower nitrogen. Maintain PK for final ripening. CalMag reduced to 1.5 ml/gal.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root protection through finish.

supplements.name: Liquid Kool Bloom
supplements.purpose: Reduced PK. Tapering.

tasks.description: Drop EC to 2.0-2.4. Reduce to 3x/day. Plant is finishing.
tasks.name: Reduce EC and frequency

tasks.description: 60-100x loupe on buds. Track clear → milky → amber.
tasks.name: Trichome checks

tasks.description: Risk remains high. Daily checks on dense colas.
tasks.name: Continue bud rot inspection

tasks.description: Start flush 7-14 days before target harvest based on trichome progression.
tasks.name: Plan flush timing

common_problems.cause: Flushing without CalMag in coco — cation exchange strips calcium from plant tissue
common_problems.issue: Lockout symptoms during flush (brown spots, leaf death)
