common_problems.solution: ADD CalMag back at 0.5 ml/gal. This is why coco flush is different — you always include minimal CalMag.

common_problems.cause: Insufficient runoff volume
common_problems.solution: Increase runoff to 30%+. More frequent flushes. Coco usually flushes well — if not, the salt load was extreme.

stages.description: Pre-harvest flush. Run plain pH'd water + CalMag ONLY (CalMag even during flush in coco to prevent lockout from cation exchange). The exception to 'every watering is a feeding.' Goal: force the plant to consume stored nutrients. Fan leaf yellowing accelerates. Coco flushes well — salts leach efficiently with 20-30% runoff.

environment.notes: Reduced light. Low humidity. Cooler temps fine.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: maintain humidity control through flush.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse: keep environment controlled.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor: rain helps flush coco. Monitor harvest window.

outdoor.notes: Outdoor: rain during flush is actually helpful.

feed_frequency.notes: 2x/day plain pH'd water + CalMag (0.5 ml/gal). Feed to 20-30% runoff to leach salts.

medium.notes: COCO FLUSH RULE: plain water + minimal CalMag (0.5 ml/gal). Even during flush, coco's cation exchange steals CalMag. Without it, you get lockout symptoms during flush that look like deficiency and confuse growers. High runoff (20-30%) to leach salts. Flush is complete when runoff EC drops below 0.5.

runoff_ec_ratio.notes: Monitor runoff EC — flush is complete when runoff EC drops below 0.3-0.5.

stages.name: Flush (Pre-Harvest)

nutrients.approach: Plain pH'd water + CalMag only (0.5 ml/gal). No base nutrients. The CalMag during flush is unique to coco — it prevents cation exchange lockout.

tasks.description: Plain pH'd water (5.8-6.0) + CalMag (0.5 ml/gal). NO base nutrients. This is the one exception to 'always feed in coco.'
tasks.name: Switch to flush solution

tasks.description: Collect runoff. Flush is working when EC drops below 0.5. Coco flushes efficiently — usually done in 7-10 days.
tasks.name: Monitor runoff EC daily

tasks.description: Harvest window approaching. 70-80% milky + 10-20% amber = peak.
tasks.name: Trichome checks daily

tasks.description: Plant defenses weakened during flush. Daily checks.
tasks.name: Continue bud rot inspection

common_problems.cause: Hidden rot in dense colas
common_problems.solution: Cut away rot + 1 inch margin. Salvage clean buds.

stages.description: Chop day. Cut plants, trim, hang to dry. Spent coco can be reused — flush heavily, re-buffer with CalMag, and it's good for another 2-3 grows.

environment.notes: Some growers give 24-48 hours darkness before chop. Harvest in morning.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: controlled harvest timing.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse: use space for drying if possible.

environment_overrides.notes: Harvest before frost.

outdoor.notes: Outdoor: weather can force harvest timing.

health_checks: No bud rot discovered during trim?

medium.notes: No more feeding. After harvest: spent coco can be reused. Flush heavily with plain water + CalMag (re-buffer), remove root debris, let dry, store for next grow. Good for 2-3 reuses.

stages.name: Harvest

nutrients.approach: None.

tasks.description: Confirm target: 70-80% milky, 10-20% amber.
tasks.name: Final trichome check

tasks.description: Cut main stem. Wet trim or whole-plant hang.
tasks.name: Harvest

tasks.description: If reusing: remove root debris, flush with plain water + CalMag at high volume, let drain, store dry. If composting: add to compost pile — coco is an excellent compost amendment.
tasks.name: Process spent coco

tasks.description: Clean pots, saucers, reservoir, pump. Sanitize with H2O2.
tasks.name: Clean equipment

common_problems.cause: Low humidity, high temp, too much airflow
common_problems.solution: Raise humidity, lower temp, reduce fan speed.

common_problems.cause: Dense buds + high humidity
common_problems.solution: Remove moldy buds. Lower humidity to 55%. Improve airflow.

stages.description: Slow controlled drying. 60°F / 60% humidity / darkness. 10-14 days ideal. Coco-grown flower dries similarly to hydro-grown — may be slightly denser than soil-grown.

environment.notes: DARK. 60°F. 60% humidity. Gentle airflow not on buds. Hay smell = too fast.

environment_overrides.notes: Too warm/bright. Move to dark room.

greenhouse.notes: Dry in separate dark room.

environment_overrides.notes: Dry indoors.

outdoor.notes: Always dry indoors.

medium.notes: No coco involvement. Drying is environmental control.

stages.name: Drying

nutrients.approach: None.

tasks.description: Space so they don't touch. Air circulates all sides.
tasks.name: Hang plants/branches

tasks.description: 60°F, 60% RH, dark. Monitor with hygrometer.
tasks.name: Maintain 60/60

tasks.description: Bend stem — snaps = ready. Bends = keep drying.
tasks.name: Check drying progress

tasks.description: Daily mold checks on dense buds.
tasks.name: Inspect for mold

common_problems.cause: Too wet when jarred
common_problems.solution: Remove, dry 12-24 hours, rejar.

common_problems.cause: Over-dried or over-burped
common_problems.solution: Boveda 62 pack to rehydrate.

common_problems.cause: Too wet when jarred
common_problems.solution: Discard moldy buds. Dry rest 12 hours. Rejar.

stages.description: Slow cure in mason jars. Terpenes develop, harshness mellows. Minimum 2 weeks, ideal 4-8 weeks. Coco-grown flower cures identically to other methods.

environment.notes: In-jar: 58-62% RH (Boveda 62). Dark, cool storage. Light degrades THC.

environment_overrides.notes: Cure indoors, not greenhouse.

greenhouse.notes: Cure in stable indoor space.

environment_overrides.notes: Same curing process. Store indoors.

outdoor.notes: Cure identically.

medium.notes: No coco involvement. Post-harvest processing.

stages.name: Curing

nutrients.approach: None.

tasks.description: Trim sugar leaves. Jars 75% full, not packed.
tasks.name: Trim and jar

tasks.description: 2-3x/day first week, 1x/day second week. Exchange air, release moisture.
tasks.name: Burp jars

tasks.description: Boveda 62%, 1 per oz.
tasks.name: Add Boveda packs

tasks.description: Every 2-3 days after 2 weeks. Weekly after 4 weeks.
tasks.name: Reduce burping

tasks.description: Check when burping. Remove affected buds immediately.
tasks.name: Monitor for mold

container_progression.commercial_medium: Turkey bags in sealed bins, nitrogen-flushed
container_progression.industrial: Climate-controlled vault, nitrogen generators, automated monitoring

degradation_timeline.18_plus_months: Convert to extracts, edibles, or topicals.

testing_schedule.360_days: Full panel — sell, process, or destroy decision

common_problems.cause: Heat, light, oxygen, or time
common_problems.solution: Darkness, cool temps (60°F), minimal oxygen. ~5%/year baseline.

common_problems.cause: Temps above 70°F, oxygen, frequent opening
common_problems.solution: Below 65°F. Nitrogen-sealed. Minimize opening.

common_problems.cause: Humidity above 65% or improper cure
common_problems.solution: Verify 58-62% RH before sealing. Remove affected material.

common_problems.cause: Normal moisture equilibration
common_problems.solution: Boveda packs. Sealed containers.

stages.description: Post-cure long-term storage. Coco-grown flower cures and stores exceptionally well due to clean root zone during growth. Fast coco growth cycles (especially with high-frequency fertigation) produce multiple harvests per year — storage capacity planning is critical. Proper storage preserves potency and terpenes for 6-12+ months.

environment.notes: DARK. Cool. Stable. Zero light — UV destroys cannabinoids and terpenes. Commercial: 58-62°F, 55-60% RH, nitrogen atmosphere.

environment_overrides.notes: Do NOT store in greenhouse.

greenhouse.notes: Climate-controlled indoor space only.

environment_overrides.notes: Storage is always indoor.

outdoor.notes: Indoor controlled environment.

stages.name: Long-Term Storage

nutrients.approach: None.

tasks.description: Home: mason jars with Boveda 58-62%. Commercial: nitrogen-sealed grove bags, CVault, or nitrogen-flushed drums.
tasks.name: Transfer to long-term containers

tasks.description: Strain, harvest date, storage date, weight, batch number. Commercial: seed-to-sale, FIFO rotation.
tasks.name: Label and track batches

tasks.description: Inspect for mold, off odors. Check humidity packs. Commercial: potency/terpene testing at 30/90/180 days.
tasks.name: Monthly quality checks

tasks.description: Monitor vault temp/humidity. No light leaks. Commercial: automated alerts.
tasks.name: Maintain environment

tasks.description: First in, first out. Flag batches approaching 12 months.
tasks.name: Rotate stock (FIFO)

tasks.description: Commercial: retain samples per regulations.
tasks.name: Compliance testing holds

autoflower.description: Ruderalis-hybrid strains that flower automatically regardless of light schedule. Fixed timeline.

light_schedule.flower: 20/4 or 18/6 (same as veg — no change needed)
light_schedule.transition: Automatic — plant decides when to flower (usually week 3-4)

autoflower.name: Autoflower
autoflower.notes: Fixed timeline — stress cannot be recovered by extending veg. Start in final pot (no transplant shock). LST preferred over HST.

training_suitability.defoliation: light only — auto can't recover from heavy defol
training_suitability.topping: risky — only if plant is vigorous

photoperiod.description: Standard cannabis strains that require 12/12 light to flower. Grower controls timing.

light_schedule.transition: Switch to 12/12 when plant is ~50% of desired final height

photoperiod.name: Photoperiod
photoperiod.notes: Can be vegged as long as desired. Cloneable. More forgiving of stress (can extend veg to recover).

training_suitability.defoliation: good
training_suitability.topping: excellent

issues.cause: Calcium deficiency — coco's cation exchange stealing Ca
issues.fix: Increase CalMag to 2-3 ml/gal. If using LED + RO water, go to 3 ml/gal. This is the #1 coco issue.

issues.cause: Magnesium deficiency — coco stealing Mg
issues.fix: Increase CalMag. Also check pH — Mg lockout occurs above pH 6.5 in coco.

issues.cause: Coco was not properly buffered before use
issues.fix: Re-buffer: flush with CalMag solution (EC 1.0) at 5x pot volume. Let sit 8 hours. Flush again. Stubborn coco needs multiple rounds.

issues.cause: Flushing without CalMag — coco's cation exchange strips plant of Ca/Mg
issues.fix: ALWAYS include 0.5 ml/gal CalMag during coco flush. This is unique to coco.

issues.cause: Feeding too frequently or too much volume for root zone size
issues.fix: Reduce frequency. Smaller shots. Lift pot to check weight. In small pots (solo cup), once/day may be enough.

issues.cause: Coco dried out completely — surface tension prevents reabsorption
issues.fix: Add yucca extract wetting agent. Water very slowly from multiple angles. Multiple slow passes. NEVER let coco dry completely.

issues.cause: Salt accumulation from insufficient runoff
issues.fix: Increase runoff to 20%. Flush with 3x pot volume. Resume at slightly lower EC. This is normal — coco needs periodic flushes.

issues.cause: Root zone staying too wet (overwatering) or dryback too aggressive
issues.fix: Check dryback range. Roots need oxygen — constant saturation suffocates. Increase perlite ratio to 40% for better drainage.
issues.symptom: Slow growth despite correct EC and pH

issues.cause: EC too high or salt accumulation
issues.fix: Reduce EC by 0.2-0.3. Flush. Coco delivers efficiently — less is often more.

issues.cause: EC too low, or nitrogen locked out by pH drift
issues.fix: Check pH (should be 5.8-6.2). Increase EC. Nitrogen uptake drops above pH 6.3 in coco.

issues.cause: Phosphorus deficiency — often pH or temperature related
issues.fix: Check pH (P lockout below 5.5 or above 6.5). Check root zone temp — cold roots can't absorb P. Ensure P in nutrient formula.

issues.cause: Calcium or potassium deficiency, or pH instability
issues.fix: Check pH. Check CalMag dose. Check runoff EC — rising EC can lock out individual nutrients.

cons: RO system cost and waste water
water_source_profiles.name: Reverse Osmosis (RO) Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Gold standard for precision growing. Always add CalMag first when using RO.
pros: Blank slate — total control over mineral content

dechlorination.chloramine: Does NOT gas off — must use Campden tablets or activated carbon filter
dechlorination.chlorine: Let sit uncovered 24 hours (gasses off) or use dechlorinator drops
dechlorination.how_to_tell: Check city water report. Most modern cities use chloramine.

water_source_profiles.name: Municipal Tap Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Good enough for most grows. Dechlorinate and pH-adjust. Get a water report from your city.

cons: May contain iron, sulfur, or heavy metals
water_source_profiles.name: Well Water
water_source_profiles.notes: MUST get a water test before using. High iron or sulfur can cause lockout. Great if your well has clean water with moderate minerals.

water_source_profiles.name: Distilled Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Same as RO but purchased instead of filtered. Only practical for 1-2 plant grows.

advanced_techniques.description: Elevating CO2 from ambient (400ppm) to 1000-1500ppm increases photosynthesis rate by 20-40% when light and temperature are also increased.

methods.notes: Most common. Tank refills every 2-4 weeks for a tent.
Produces heat and humidity — better for large rooms with AC.
Produces ~100-200ppm boost. Only useful for small sealed spaces. Not worth it for most.

advanced_techniques.name: CO2 Supplementation

ppfd_co2_correlation.notes: Ambient CO2 sufficient at low light
Slight benefit from supplementation
Moderate benefit — cost-effective threshold
Strong benefit — diminishing returns above this
Maximum practical benefit for most grows
Commercial intensity — requires perfect environment

requirements: Sealed grow space (CO2 escapes quickly from leaky rooms)
High light intensity (>600 PPFD — plants can't use extra CO2 without sufficient light)
Higher temperature tolerance (82-86°F optimal with CO2 vs 75-80°F without)
CO2 controller or timer

advanced_techniques.description: Manipulating environmental and irrigation parameters to push the plant toward vegetative or generative (flowering/fruiting) growth.
advanced_techniques.name: Crop Steering
advanced_techniques.notes: Crop steering is most effective in hydro/coco where irrigation frequency is highly controllable. Soil growers have less precision due to soil buffering.

vegetative_steering.goal: More leaf/stem growth, larger plant framework

best_practices: Spray during lights-off or first/last 30 minutes of light cycle (stomata open, no light burn)
Use fine mist sprayer — coverage is key
pH foliar solution to 6.2-6.5 (leaf surface slightly higher pH than root zone)
EC should be very low (0.2-0.5) — leaves burn easily
Never foliar spray during flower (moisture in buds → mold/bud rot)
Best for micronutrient deficiency correction (CalMag, iron, magnesium)

common_foliar_products.frequency: Weekly preventive, 3-day intervals for active infestations

advanced_techniques.description: Spraying nutrient solution directly onto leaves for rapid uptake. Bypasses root zone entirely.
advanced_techniques.name: Foliar Feeding

advanced_techniques.description: Strengthens cell walls, increases stem thickness, improves heat/drought/pest resistance.
important_notes: ALWAYS add silica to water FIRST before other nutrients (raises pH significantly)
Let silica mix for 5-10 minutes before adding base nutrients
Silica is incompatible with concentrated nutrient solutions — never mix undiluted
Discontinue in late flower (last 2-3 weeks)
advanced_techniques.name: Silica (Potassium Silicate)

advanced_techniques.description: Introduces beneficial bacteria/fungi to the root zone to outcompete pathogens, improve nutrient uptake, and protect against root diseases.
important_notes: Do NOT use hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) with beneficial microbes — it kills them
Choose either sterile (H2O2) OR beneficial (Hydroguard) approach — not both
Beneficials need warmish water (65-75°F) to thrive
Reapply after any reservoir change or system cleaning
advanced_techniques.name: Beneficial Microbes & Root Inoculants

products.name: Hydroguard
products.notes: The standard for DWC/RDWC. Add at every res change.

products.dosing: 1 tsp/gal (water-in) or dust roots at transplant
products.name: Great White
products.notes: Best for soil/coco. Less effective in pure hydro.

products.name: Recharge
products.notes: Feeds existing soil microbes. Great for living soil.

products.name: Mammoth P
products.notes: Most effective in flower when P demand is highest.

advanced_techniques.description: Removing select fan leaves to improve light penetration, airflow, and direct energy to bud sites.
advanced_techniques.name: Defoliation (Strategic Leaf Removal)
rules: Never remove more than 20-30% of leaves at once (except schwazze technique)
Only defoliate healthy, vigorous plants — never stressed ones
Autoflowers: very light defoliation only — they can't recover from heavy removal
Always leave the top 2-3 fan leaves on each branch (they feed the bud below)

timing.method: Remove lower leaves that get no light (lollipop)
Major defoliation day 1 and day 21 of flower (schwazzing)
Selective tuck or remove leaves blocking bud sites

equipment.name: Drip emitters / drip stakes
equipment.notes: Pressure-compensating emitters preferred. 1 per plant minimum, 2 for larger plants. Match flow rate to media type. Spare emitters are MANDATORY — emitter failure is the #1 drip system problem.

equipment.name: Drip manifold / mainline tubing
equipment.notes: 1/2 inch or 3/4 inch mainline. Size for your plant count. Include shut-off valves for sections.

equipment.name: Drip spaghetti lines (1/4 inch)
equipment.notes: Individual feed lines from manifold to each emitter. Keep runs under 6 feet to maintain even pressure.

equipment.name: Water pump (submersible)
equipment.notes: Size pump for total flow rate: sum of all emitter flow rates + 20% headroom. Submersible pump sits in the reservoir.

equipment.name: Irrigation timer / controller
equipment.notes: Digital timer with minute-resolution for shot timing. Commercial grows: use Trolmaster, Grodan, or Aroya controllers with substrate sensor integration.

equipment.name: Inline filter (sediment)
equipment.notes: 100-200 mesh inline filter between pump and manifold. Catches precipitates before they clog emitters. Clean weekly. This alone prevents 80% of emitter clogs.

equipment.name: Pressure regulator
equipment.notes: Maintains consistent pressure across all emitters. Essential for larger systems (20+ emitters). Without it, emitters closest to pump get more flow.

equipment.name: Spare emitters (10-20% of total)
equipment.notes: NON-NEGOTIABLE. Emitters clog. When one fails mid-flower, you need an instant replacement. Keep spares on hand at all times.

equipment.name: Reservoir (opaque)
equipment.notes: Light-proof to prevent algae. Size: minimum 2 gallons per plant. Larger is more stable. Include drain valve for easy changes.

equipment.name: Air pump + air stone (for reservoir)
equipment.notes: Keeps nutrient solution oxygenated and prevents stagnation. Especially helpful for recirculating systems.

equipment.name: Saucers / runoff trays
equipment.notes: Collect runoff from each plant for measurement and disposal. Size to catch maximum shot volume. For DTW: must be emptied to prevent root sitting in stagnant water.

equipment.name: Wet/dry vacuum or runoff pump
equipment.notes: For larger grows: pump or vacuum runoff from saucers rather than emptying by hand. Essential at 20+ plant scale.

equipment.name: Runoff collection tray (sloped)
equipment.notes: Sloped table or tray that channels all runoff to a single drain point. Commercial standard — eliminates individual saucer emptying.

equipment.name: pH meter (accurate to 0.1)
equipment.notes: Calibrate weekly with 4.0 and 7.0 buffer solutions. Bluelab or Apera recommended. Cheap meters drift.

equipment.name: EC/TDS meter
equipment.notes: Measures input AND runoff EC. The input-vs-runoff EC delta is your most important diagnostic number. Calibrate monthly.

equipment.name: Substrate sensor (Teros, Aroya)
equipment.notes: Measures volumetric water content (VWC), EC, and temperature inside the media in real time. Game-changer for crop steering. Essential for precision agriculture. Expensive but transformative.

equipment.name: Measuring cups/syringes
equipment.notes: For measuring emitter output, runoff volume, and nutrient mixing. Graduated cylinders for accuracy.

equipment.name: Jeweler's loupe (60-100x)
equipment.notes: For trichome inspection during late flower/harvest. Digital USB microscope works too.

equipment.name: Grow light (LED preferred)
equipment.notes: Size for your canopy. Target 30-40 watts/sq ft for LED. Must support dimming for stage-appropriate PPFD.

equipment.name: Exhaust fan + carbon filter
equipment.notes: Odor control and air exchange. Size for your room volume — exchange air every 1-3 minutes.

equipment.name: Oscillating fan(s)
equipment.notes: Airflow through canopy prevents mold and strengthens stems. Don't blow directly on buds during flower.

equipment.name: Temperature/humidity controller
equipment.notes: AC Infinity controller or Inkbird for automated climate control. VPD management is critical for drip efficiency.

equipment.name: Dehumidifier
equipment.notes: Essential during flower. Target 40-50% RH. Size for your room. Dump dehumidifier water — don't reuse.

equipment.name: Growing media (coco, rockwool, or perlite)
equipment.notes: Choose ONE: Coco (most forgiving, needs CalMag, reusable). Rockwool (precision steering, single-use). Perlite (cheap, drains fast, needs frequent irrigation).

equipment.name: Containers / pots
equipment.notes: Fabric pots (air pruning), plastic pots, or grow bags. Size: 3-5 gallon for indoor. Ensure adequate drainage holes.

equipment.name: Base nutrient system (Flora Trio or equivalent)
equipment.notes: Flora Micro, Flora Gro, Flora Bloom. Or Athena Pro, Jacks 321, etc. Consistency matters more than brand.

equipment.name: CalMag supplement
equipment.notes: MANDATORY for coco coir. Recommended for all media. Coco steals calcium — CalMag in every feeding.

equipment.name: pH Up/Down solutions
equipment.notes: Maintain pH 5.8-6.2 for all media types. pH Down (phosphoric acid) used more often than pH Up.

equipment.name: Hydroguard (beneficial bacteria)
equipment.notes: Root zone protection. Especially important in warm environments and recirculating systems.

equipment.name: PK booster (Liquid Kool Bloom or equivalent)
equipment.notes: Phosphorus/potassium supplement for flower. Start light in early flower, peak in mid-flower, taper in late flower.

equipment.name: Hydrogen peroxide (3% or 29%)
equipment.notes: Emitter cleaning, line flushing, sterilization. 3% for routine cleaning, 29% food-grade diluted for heavy cleaning. Safety: 29% is caustic — handle with gloves and goggles.

equipment.name: Citric acid
equipment.notes: Alternative emitter cleaning solution. pH 3.0 soak dissolves mineral deposits. Cheap and effective.

flush_timing.coco: 7-14 days reduced nutrients → plain water last 3-5 days
flush_timing.notes: Flushing is debated — some studies show no difference. At minimum, reduce nutrients in final week.
flush_timing.soil: 14-21 days plain water (soil holds nutrients longer)

other_harvest_indicators: Most pistils (hairs) have darkened and curled in
Calyxes are swollen and dense
Fan leaves are yellowing and falling off naturally
Trichome stalks are visible and heads are enlarged
Breeder's recommended flowering time has passed

trichome_ratios.notes: Harvest early for more THC-dominant, less CBN. Energetic, racy high. Slight reduction in yield.
Standard harvest window. Mix of THC and CBN. Full-spectrum effects.
Extended harvest for max CBN conversion. Heavy body high. Good for pain/sleep. Slightly degraded THC.

crop_steering_with_drip.description: Manipulating irrigation timing and volume to steer plant behavior (vegetative vs generative).

generative_steering.ec_input: Higher range for stage (+0.2-0.5 above normal)
generative_steering.effect: Promotes flowering, fruit set, tighter internodes, terpene production.
generative_steering.strategy: Allow dry-back. Fewer irrigations. Higher EC. Stress the plant slightly.

p1_p2_strategy.overnight_dry_back: Generative: let media dry back 10-15% overnight. Vegetative: maintain moisture (last irrigation closer to lights-off).
p1_p2_strategy.p1_first_irrigation: Critical. Should be timed to catch the dry-back at its lowest acceptable point. Too early = vegetative. Too late = stress.
p1_p2_strategy.p2_subsequent_irrigations: Smaller, frequent shots to maintain moisture without over-saturating.

sensor_requirements: Weight-based sensor (scale under pot — gold standard for precision)
Environmental sensors (VPD drives transpiration which drives dry-back rate)

vegetative_steering.effect: Promotes stretching, leaf development, vegetative growth.
vegetative_steering.strategy: Keep media wet. Frequent irrigations. Small dry-back.

drain_to_waste.best_for: Coco, perlite, small hobby grows, organic nutrients.
drain_to_waste.description: Feed → runoff discarded. Fresh solution every irrigation.
drain_to_waste.ec_management: Input EC is all that matters. Target EC based on stage.

recirculating.best_for: Rockwool slabs, commercial operations, environmentally-conscious growers.
recirculating.description: Runoff collected and returned to reservoir. Solution reused until changed.
recirculating.ec_management: Monitor reservoir EC constantly. Top off with adjusted solution. Full change every 5-7 days.

drain_to_waste_vs_recirculating.recommendation: Drain-to-waste for hobby growers using coco/perlite. Recirculating for commercial rockwool slab systems.

drip_rings.best_for: Large containers (5+ gal). Ensures entire root ball is wetted, not just one spot.
drip_rings.description: Ring around stem base with multiple drip points. Even distribution around root zone.
drip_rings.notes: Especially important in coco/perlite where lateral water movement is limited.

drip_stakes.best_for: Pots, grow bags, individual containers. Most common hobby setup.
drip_stakes.description: Emitter mounted on a stake inserted into media. Directs water to root zone.
drip_stakes.notes: Use arrow/barbed stakes for stability. One per small pot, 2-4 per large pot.

non_compensating.best_for: Small systems with equal line lengths. Budget builds.
cons: Uneven flow if lines differ in length
non_compensating.description: Flow rate changes with pressure. Simpler, cheaper, but less uniform.

pressure_compensating.best_for: Multi-plant systems with varying line lengths. Commercial setups.
pressure_compensating.description: Delivers consistent flow regardless of pressure variations or line length.

coco_coir.notes: Coco wants frequent, small irrigations. Never let it dry out completely. High-frequency fertigation is ideal.
coco_coir.shot_size_ml: Calculate: container volume × 3-5% per irrigation event

perlite.notes: Very low retention. Needs most frequent irrigation of all media. Perfect for crop steering.
perlite.shot_size_ml: Smaller shots, more frequent. Perlite drains fast.

rockwool.notes: Rockwool holds water well. Less frequent than coco. Watch for over-saturation.

soil_soilless.notes: Soil retains heavily. Drip in soil acts more like automated hand-watering. Longer intervals.
soil_soilless.shot_size_ml: Larger shots, less frequent. Let dry back significantly.

runoff_monitoring.collection_method: Saucers under pots or elevated pot stands with collection trays. Measure immediately — don't let runoff sit (evaporation concentrates it).

runoff_ec_analysis.action_threshold: If runoff EC is >0.5 above input EC consistently, perform a flush.
runoff_ec_analysis.runoff_ec_close_to_input: Media is balanced. Nutrients flowing through without accumulation. Good.
runoff_ec_analysis.runoff_ec_higher_than_input: Salt buildup in media. Increase runoff % or flush. Common issue.
runoff_ec_analysis.runoff_ec_lower_than_input: Plant consuming heavily. May need to increase feed strength.

runoff_ph_analysis.runoff_ph_dropping: Media becoming acidic. Often from over-feeding ammoniacal nitrogen. Raise input pH slightly.
runoff_ph_analysis.runoff_ph_rising: Media becoming alkaline. Could be calcium carbonate buildup. Lower input pH slightly.

flush.notes: Heavy runoff during flush to leach accumulated salts.

heavy_feed.notes: During heavy feeding stages, extra runoff helps prevent salt lockout.

standard.notes: 10-20% runoff ensures full media saturation without waste.

hybrid.best_for: Most hobbyist systems aiming for precision without full automation.
hybrid.description: Timer-based with sensor guardrails. Timer sets schedule, sensor prevents over/under-watering.

sensor_driven.description: Irrigations triggered by substrate moisture readings.

cons: Not responsive to plant needs or environment changes
timer_based.description: Fixed schedule. Irrigations at set times for set duration.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Micro → Gro → Bloom (always Micro first)
nutrient_brands.name: General Hydroponics Flora Trio

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Part A → Epsom → Part B (dissolve each fully before adding next)
nutrient_brands.name: Jack's 321
nutrient_brands.notes: Jack's 321 is 3.6g Part A, 2.4g Cal Nit, 1.2g Epsom per gallon at full strength. Extremely cost-effective for large grows.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Core → Bloom
nutrient_brands.name: Athena Pro Line
nutrient_brands.notes: Commercial-grade. Simple 2-part with integrated CalMag. Popular in commercial cannabis facilities.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: A → B (separate for veg and bloom)
nutrient_brands.name: Advanced Nutrients pH Perfect (Sensi)
nutrient_brands.notes: Self-adjusting pH technology (claims no pH adjustment needed). Expensive but popular for beginners who struggle with pH.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Shake VERY well before use (thick concentrate settles). One bottle at a time.
nutrient_brands.name: General Hydroponics FloraNova
nutrient_brands.notes: Simplest option — one bottle per phase. Great for beginners. Shake well — concentrate separates.
nutrient_brands.type: 1-part (two bottles — one for veg, one for bloom)

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Dissolve powder in warm water, stir well
nutrient_brands.name: Greenleaf Nutrients Mega Crop
nutrient_brands.notes: One powder does everything — includes CalMag. Incredibly cost-effective at ~$0.02/gal. Community favorite on r/microgrowery.

environment.container: Glass mason jars (quart size). Fill 75% full.

humidity_packs.boveda_58: For drier preference or long-term storage.
humidity_packs.boveda_62: Maintains 62% RH. Set-and-forget. Replace when crispy.
humidity_packs.grove_bags: Heat-sealed bags with built-in humidity regulation. No burping needed.

indicators_of_good_cure: Smooth, flavorful smoke — no harshness or hay smell
Buds are slightly sticky but not wet
Strong terpene aroma when jar is opened
Ash burns to white/light gray (not black)
method: Trim buds to final shape (dry trim or finish wet trim)
Place in mason jars — fill 3/4 full to allow air exchange
Week 1: burp 2-3x daily for 10-15 minutes (release moisture and gases)
Week 2-4: burp 1x daily for 5-10 minutes
Month 2+: burp 1x weekly or seal completely
Cure for minimum 2 weeks, ideal is 4-8 weeks. Some strains improve for 6+ months.

common_mistakes: Drying too fast (>3 days) — harsh smoke, hay smell, terpene loss
Drying too slow (>14 days) — mold risk, diminishing returns
Fan blowing directly on buds — uneven drying, crispy exterior/moist interior
Temperature too high (>70°F) — terpene evaporation, harsh taste

environment.airflow: Gentle circulation — fan NOT pointed at buds. Just keep air moving.

method: Cut branches 12-18 inches long
Optionally remove large fan leaves (wet trim) or leave intact (dry trim)
Hang upside down on line/rack with 6+ inches between branches
Small branches should snap (not bend) when done — 5-7 days minimum
Stems should snap cleanly; if they bend, keep drying

long_term_storage.degradation: THC slowly converts to CBN over time. Light, heat, air, and moisture accelerate degradation.
long_term_storage.method: Sealed mason jars or vacuum-sealed bags with Boveda 58% in dark, cool place
long_term_storage.notes: Properly cured and stored cannabis can remain excellent for 12+ months. Freezing is debated — can damage trichomes if not done carefully.

crop_steering_guide.description: Crop steering uses irrigation timing and volume to control plant behavior.

generative_strategy.effect: Pushes flower development, terpene production, bud density.

vegetative_strategy.effect: Pushes leaf growth, root expansion, vegetative vigor.

dryback_targets.description: Dryback % = how much the media dries between irrigations. Key crop steering lever.

generative_steering.effect: High dryback pushes generative growth (flowers, fruits, terpenes).

transition.effect: Moderate dryback during the shift from veg to flower.

vegetative_steering.effect: Low dryback pushes vegetative growth (leaves, stems, roots).

emitter_management.check_frequency: Every 2-3 days during active grow. Daily during flower.
emitter_management.cleaning_method: Soak in 3% H2O2 for 30 min, or pH 3.0 citric acid solution for 1 hour.
emitter_management.description: Emitter clogs are the #1 failure mode in drip systems.
emitter_management.prevention: Inline filter (100-200 mesh). Clean filter weekly. Use pressure-compensating emitters.
emitter_management.spare_rule: Keep 10-20% spare emitters on hand at ALL times.

golden_rules: Check EVERY emitter, EVERY 2-3 days. A clogged emitter = a dead plant.
Runoff EC vs Input EC is your most important number. Check it religiously.
Never let coco dry completely — it becomes hydrophobic and repels water.
CalMag in EVERY feeding for coco. Non-negotiable.
Flush every 7-14 days to prevent salt lockout.
Generative steering (high dryback + high EC) starts in early flower, not before.
First shot timing matters: early = vegetative push, delayed = generative push.
Spare emitters on hand at ALL times. You will need them.
Inline filter before the manifold prevents 80% of emitter clogs.
Lift your pots — weight tells you more than any sensor (until you have sensors).

irrigation_timing_guide.P1_morning: First 1-2 shots replenish overnight dryback. Largest volume shots of the day.
irrigation_timing_guide.P2_midday: Maintenance shots. Keep media at target saturation. Moderate volume.
irrigation_timing_guide.P3_afternoon: Last shots of the day. Smaller volume. Set up overnight dryback.
irrigation_timing_guide.description: Shot timing through the day follows a P1-P2-P3 pattern.

coco.best_for: Beginners, forgiving, most popular for drip.

perlite.best_for: Budget grows, fast-draining, mix with coco for best results.

quick_reference.nutrient_mixing_order: 1) Silica (if using) → pH down to 5.8 → 2) CalMag → 3) Flora Micro → 4) Flora Gro → 5) Flora Bloom → 6) Supplements → 7) pH adjust final

ph_range.notes: Measure input AND runoff pH. Runoff pH tells you what's happening in the root zone.

quick_reference.reservoir_change_schedule: Every 7-10 days, or when EC drifts >0.3 from target. For DTW, top off daily, full change weekly.

runoff_ec_interpretation.description: Input EC vs Runoff EC is the #1 diagnostic tool in drip systems.
runoff_ec_interpretation.runoff_higher_by_0_3_to_0_5: Caution — salts starting to accumulate. Increase runoff % or flush soon.
runoff_ec_interpretation.runoff_higher_by_0_5_plus: Problem — flush immediately. Salts are building up beyond plant tolerance.
runoff_ec_interpretation.runoff_higher_by_0_to_0_3: Healthy — plant is consuming nutrients well.
runoff_ec_interpretation.runoff_lower_than_input: Hungry plant — increase EC or shot volume. Plant is consuming more than you provide.

change_schedule.top_off: Daily with plain pH'd water when EC rises. With dilute nutrient when EC drops.
change_schedule.top_off_rule: EC rising = plants drinking more water than nutrients → top off with plain water. EC dropping = plants eating more nutrients than water → top off with dilute nutrient mix.

reservoir_management.description: Reservoir management for active hydroponic systems (DWC, RDWC, NFT, Ebb & Flow, Drip, Aeroponics).

dissolved_oxygen.why_important: Roots need oxygen to function. Low DO → root rot, nutrient lockout, slow growth.

sizing_guidelines.notes: Larger reservoirs = more stable pH/EC, less frequent changes, more forgiving of mistakes.

beneficial.cons: Slightly cloudy water (normal). Doesn't work above 75°F water.
beneficial.pros: Continuous protection. Outcompetes pathogens. Set-and-forget per res change.

sterile_vs_beneficial.recommendation: Beneficial approach (Hydroguard) for most growers. Sterile only if you have persistent issues.

sterile.cons: Must reapply every 2-3 days. Kills beneficial microbes too. Degrades in hours.
sterile.pros: Kills all pathogens. Clean roots. No biofilm.

water_temperature.too_cold_risk: Slowed metabolism, nutrient lockout, stunted growth
water_temperature.too_warm_risk: Root rot, low dissolved oxygen, pathogen growth

scale_tiers.description: First-time grower or single-plant hobbyist. Manual management, learning-focused.
scale_tiers.name: Solo / Single Plant
scale_tiers.notes: Hands-on learning experience. Manual checks 1-2x daily. Ideal for beginners.

scale_tiers.description: Hobbyist tent grow. Light automation helpful but not required.
scale_tiers.name: Small Tent (2-8 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Timer-controlled lights, humidity controller. Sensor monitoring recommended.

scale_tiers.description: Dedicated grower with multiple tents/rooms. Automation saves significant time.
scale_tiers.name: Multi-Tent (9-24 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Multiple environments to manage. Automated alerts essential. Consider perpetual harvest.

scale_tiers.description: Licensed commercial operation. Full automation, compliance tracking, batch management.
scale_tiers.name: Commercial Room (25-100 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: SOPs required. Staff training. Regulatory compliance. Insurance requirements.

scale_tiers.description: Large-scale production facility. Enterprise automation, SCADA integration, dedicated staff.
scale_tiers.name: Warehouse Scale (100+ plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Multiple rooms, dedicated HVAC, industrial nutrient dosing, video surveillance.

common_problems.cause: Too cold, too wet, or bad seed
common_problems.solution: Ensure 75-80°F. Starter medium should be moist not soaking. Try a different seed after 7 days.

common_problems.cause: Skipped CalMag pre-soak step
common_problems.solution: Coco MUST be buffered before use. Soak in CalMag solution (EC 0.8) for 8+ hours, drain, repeat. Unbuffered coco steals calcium from nutrients.

stages.description: Seed cracks open and taproot emerges. For drip systems, start seeds in Rapid Rooters, rockwool starter cubes, or coco plugs inside a humidity dome. Seeds are NOT under drip emitters yet — germinate separately.

environment.notes: Keep seeds in darkness. Heat mat at 78°F. No light until sprout emerges. Humidity dome over propagation tray.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse germination: use heat mat and humidity dome. Greenhouse temp swings may slow germination without supplemental heating.

greenhouse.notes: Heat mat recommended in greenhouse.

environment_overrides.notes: Start seeds indoors. Outdoor drip systems face rain dilution and temperature swings — seedlings are too fragile.

outdoor.notes: Always germinate indoors.

health_checks: Is the taproot visible and white?
Is the starter medium moist but not soaking?
Are all drip lines flushed and leak-free?

medium.notes: Seeds are NOT under drip emitters yet. Germinate off-system in humidity dome.

stages.name: Germination

nutrients.approach: None — seeds contain all energy needed to germinate.

tasks.description: Soak seeds 12-24 hours in plain pH 6.0 water, then place in Rapid Rooters, pre-soaked rockwool cubes, or coco plugs inside a humidity dome.
tasks.name: Start seeds

tasks.description: After 24-72 hours, look for white taproot emerging. Do not disturb the seed — just look.
tasks.name: Check for taproot

tasks.description: While seeds germinate: assemble manifold, run emitters, flush lines with plain water, verify all emitters flow evenly, check for leaks.
tasks.name: Prepare drip system

tasks.description: Buffer coco coir with CalMag (EC 0.8, soak 8+ hours). Pre-soak rockwool slabs in pH 5.5 water for 1 hour. Rinse perlite to remove dust.
tasks.name: Prepare growing media

tasks.description: Run all emitters into cups for 5 minutes. Measure output per emitter — should be within 10% of each other. Replace any outliers.
tasks.name: Test emitter uniformity

common_problems.cause: Too many shots per day, too much volume per shot, or media staying saturated
common_problems.solution: Reduce shot frequency or volume. Lift pots to check weight. If heavy, skip next irrigation. Seedlings need very little water.

common_problems.cause: Nutrient salt or biofilm blocking the emitter
common_problems.issue: Clogged emitter — plant wilting while neighbors are fine
common_problems.solution: Check emitter immediately. Replace or clean (soak in pH 3.0 solution or H2O2). Every emitter check should compare flow rates.

common_problems.cause: Drip stake too close to stem, or media staying too wet at surface
common_problems.solution: Move emitter 1-2 inches from stem. Add dry media (perlite, hydroton) on top to keep the stem-media junction dry.

common_problems.cause: Evaporation concentrating salts on top of media
common_problems.solution: Normal in drip systems. Flush periodically (3x normal volume). If severe, top-dress with perlite to reduce evaporation.

stages.description: Seedling develops first true leaves. Transplant into final media and place under drip emitters. Start with very low-volume, infrequent irrigations — the root zone is tiny and overwatering is the #1 killer at this stage. Hand watering may be easier than drip for 1-10 plants.

environment.notes: Gentle light. Remove humidity dome gradually over 3-5 days. If hand watering, water in a small ring around the stem — do NOT soak the entire container.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: higher light levels may increase transpiration, requiring more frequent irrigations than pure indoor.

greenhouse.notes: Monitor dryback more closely — greenhouse light spikes can dry media faster than expected.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor drip: rain dilutes nutrient solution and throws off runoff EC readings. Cover plants or account for rain in irrigation schedule.

extra_problems.cause: Uncovered outdoor containers
extra_problems.solution: Cover containers during rain or accept that outdoor drip requires manual adjustment after rain events.

extra_tasks.description: Rain disrupts drip irrigation scheduling and dilutes nutrients. Cover containers or pause drip during rain.
extra_tasks.name: Install rain cover or monitor weather

outdoor.notes: Outdoor drip is viable but rain management is essential.

health_checks: Emitter delivering solution near (not on) the stem?
No standing water in saucers after drain?
Runoff EC within 0.3 of input EC?

coco.notes: Coco holds moisture well. 1 small shot per day. Never let coco dry completely — it becomes hydrophobic. CalMag in every feeding.

perlite.notes: Perlite drains fast. 2 small shots per day. Roots dry quickly — monitor for wilting between irrigations.

rockwool.notes: Rockwool retains heavily. 1 small shot per day. Over-irrigating rockwool drowns seedling roots. Let slab settle to 60-65% saturation before next shot.

medium.notes: Tiny root zone — less is more. Water only where roots are (small ring around stem). Runoff should be minimal at this stage. Check emitter placement: drip stake should deliver water 1-2 inches from the stem, NOT directly on it.

stages.name: Seedling

nutrients.approach: Quarter strength. Seedlings are tiny. Each drip event delivers a small dose — no continuous feeding needed.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Protect young roots. Warm, moist media is ideal for Pythium.

tasks.description: Place seedling in starter plug into final container with chosen media (coco, rockwool slab, perlite). Position drip stake 1-2 inches from stem. Emitter should NOT drip directly on the stem.
tasks.name: Transplant into final media

tasks.description: Run drip manually. Verify emitter delivers solution where roots are. Watch for runoff — at this stage, minimal or no runoff is fine.
tasks.name: First drip irrigation

tasks.description: Program timer for 1-2 irrigations per day during lights-on period only. Each shot should be small (50-150ml depending on media and container size).
tasks.name: Set irrigation timer

tasks.description: Verify every emitter is flowing. A single clogged emitter = one dead plant. This is the #1 drip system failure mode.
tasks.name: Check emitter flow

tasks.description: Collect runoff from saucers. Check runoff EC vs input EC. Runoff EC should be close to input EC at this stage. If runoff EC is much higher, you have salt buildup — flush.
tasks.name: Monitor runoff

tasks.description: Lift container to gauge weight. Heavy = saturated (reduce irrigation). Light = dry (increase irrigation). With experience, weight tells you everything.
tasks.name: Check media moisture

transition_signals: Root tips visible at container edges or bottom

common_problems.cause: Salt accumulation in media — plant not taking up all nutrients
common_problems.issue: Runoff EC much higher than input EC
common_problems.solution: Flush with plain pH'd water at 3x container volume. Reduce nutrient strength 10-20%. Increase runoff target to 15-20% temporarily.

common_problems.cause: Plant is eating more than you're feeding — hungry plant
common_problems.issue: Runoff EC lower than input EC
common_problems.solution: Increase nutrient strength 10-20%. Increase shot volume. The plant is using everything and wants more.

common_problems.cause: Emitter flow rate variation — some plants getting more water than others
common_problems.solution: Test all emitters. Replace pressure-compensating emitters that are out of spec. Check for kinked lines or partial clogs.

common_problems.cause: Root zone too wet (overwatering) or pH drift in media
common_problems.solution: Check media pH via runoff. Increase dryback slightly. Roots need oxygen — if media is constantly saturated, roots suffocate.

stages.description: Rapid root and leaf expansion. The plant is now established and drinking more — increase irrigation frequency and volume. Vegetative steering: keep dryback low, irrigate more frequently to push green growth. Begin monitoring runoff EC closely as nutrient demand rises.

environment.notes: Plants are actively growing. VPD rising as humidity drops and temperature is maintained. Light intensity increasing toward full veg levels.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: excellent light but temperature spikes can dry media faster than expected. Automate irrigation if possible.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse drip: watch for mid-day temperature spikes drying media.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor: natural light provides excellent DLI in summer. Irrigation must account for rain and ambient temperature. Hot days = more frequent irrigations.

extra_problems.cause: High ambient temperature and wind
extra_problems.issue: Media drying too fast in heat
extra_problems.solution: Increase irrigation frequency. Mulch surface of containers. Consider shade cloth during extreme heat (>95°F).

extra_tasks.description: Hot/dry days: increase shot frequency. Rainy days: reduce or skip irrigations. Monitor media weight daily.
extra_tasks.name: Adjust irrigation for weather

outdoor.notes: Outdoor drip: daily weather adjustment is mandatory.

health_checks: Runoff EC within 0.5 of input EC?
Dryback within target range for the media?
No salt crust building on media surface?

coco.notes: Coco: 3 shots/day, keep dryback under 10% for vegetative steering. CalMag every feeding. Never let coco dry below 50% saturation.

perlite.notes: Perlite: 4 shots/day. Drains fast — roots dry quickly. More frequent, smaller shots. Monitor for wilting between irrigations.

rockwool.notes: Rockwool: 2 shots/day. Monitor slab weight — target 70-80% saturation. Dryback under 8% for vegetative push.

medium.notes: Vegetative steering: keep the plant well-watered with low dryback to push leaf and root growth. First shot should arrive shortly after lights on to replenish overnight dryback. Last shot early enough to allow some dryback before lights off. Runoff EC should be within 0.5 of input EC.

stages.name: Early Vegetative

nutrients.approach: Half strength. Plant is growing fast but roots are still expanding. Ramp up gradually — watch runoff EC to gauge if the plant is eating what you feed.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root protection. Especially important in warm media.

supplements.name: Silica (Armor Si)
supplements.purpose: Strengthen stems and cell walls. Add FIRST, before other nutrients, and pH down to 5.8 before adding Flora series.

tasks.description: Ramp from 1-2 shots/day to 2-4 shots/day based on media type. Plant is drinking more — watch for faster dryback as signal to increase.
tasks.name: Increase irrigation frequency

tasks.description: Collect runoff from 2-3 representative plants. Compare runoff EC to input EC. Delta > 0.5 = salt accumulating, run a flush. Delta < 0 (runoff lower) = plant is hungry, increase strength.
tasks.name: Check runoff EC

tasks.description: Walk the room and verify every single emitter is flowing. One clogged emitter = one stunted plant. This takes 2 minutes and prevents disasters.
tasks.name: Check every emitter

tasks.description: Run plain pH'd water through the entire system at 3x normal volume to clear salt buildup in lines and emitters.
tasks.name: Flush drip lines

tasks.description: Lift containers to judge weight change between irrigations. If using substrate sensors (Teros, Aroya), monitor dryback % directly. Target low dryback for vegetative steering.
tasks.name: Monitor dryback

tasks.description: Add a thin layer of perlite or hydroton on top of coco/media to reduce surface evaporation and salt crusting.
tasks.name: Top dress or mulch

training.description: Begin bending main stem and branches to create an even canopy. Start when plant has 4-5 nodes. Tie down branches with plant wire or clips.
Cut the main stem above the 4th or 5th node to create two main colas. Do this once, early in veg. Let the plant recover 3-5 days before further training.

transition_signals: Drinking noticeably more water each day

common_problems.cause: Salt accumulation in media over weeks of feeding
common_problems.solution: Flush with 3x container volume of plain pH'd water. Then resume at slightly lower strength or higher runoff target (20%). This is normal — drip systems accumulate salts and need periodic flushes.

common_problems.cause: Vegged too long or insufficient light intensity
common_problems.issue: Plant too tall (stretched) for the space
common_problems.solution: Flip to flower now — plant will stretch 50-100% more during flower stretch. Supercrop (bend and tape) any branches that are too tall.

common_problems.cause: Concentrated nutrient solution precipitating in lines or emitters
common_problems.solution: Clean all emitters with H2O2 soak. Run acid flush through lines. Consider inline filter before manifold. Use pressure-compensating emitters rated for nutrient solutions.

common_problems.cause: Salt accumulation changing media buffering, or coco not properly buffered initially
common_problems.issue: Media pH drifting despite correct input pH
common_problems.solution: Flush and re-buffer. Check runoff pH — if drifting above 6.5, run extra flushes. Persistent pH drift in coco usually means insufficient initial buffering.

stages.description: Plant reaches target size before flip. Irrigation at full vegetative capacity — maximum frequency and volume for vegetative steering. This is the last stage to push green growth before transition. Canopy should be filled and level. Root zone management is critical now — the plant is drinking heavily.

environment.notes: Full vegetative intensity. VPD rising toward flower levels. Plant is transpiring heavily — irrigation demand is highest in late veg.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: can use light deprivation to force flip, or let natural photoperiod trigger flowering.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse: light dep gives control over flip timing.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor: natural photoperiod change triggers flowering. No manual flip. Late veg ends when daylight drops below 14 hours.

extra_tasks.description: Outdoor flowering is triggered by photoperiod. Know your local sunset times. Late veg ends as days shorten.
extra_tasks.name: Monitor daylight hours

outdoor.notes: Outdoor: pre-flip flush still recommended as photoperiod shifts.

health_checks: All emitters flowing at rated output?
Runoff EC within 0.5 of input EC?
Plant at target size (50-66% of final desired height)?

coco.notes: Coco: 4 shots/day. Keep dryback under 10%. CalMag every feeding (1.5-2 ml/gal). First shot within 30 min of lights on to replenish overnight dryback.

perlite.notes: Perlite: 6 shots/day. Drains very fast at this size — plant is drinking heavily. Monitor constantly for wilting. Consider mixing with coco for better retention.

rockwool.notes: Rockwool: 3 shots/day. Monitor slab weight and EC. Target 65-75% saturation. Dryback under 8% for continued vegetative push.

medium.notes: Maximum vegetative irrigation. Low dryback, frequent shots, plenty of runoff (15-20%) to prevent salt accumulation. First shot should arrive shortly after lights on. Last shot early enough to allow 2-3 hours of dryback before dark period. Monitor runoff EC religiously — plant is eating everything.

stages.name: Late Vegetative

nutrients.approach: Three-quarter strength. Full veg formula, heavy on nitrogen. Plant is eating aggressively — watch runoff EC to confirm uptake.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root protection throughout veg.

supplements.name: Silica (Armor Si)
supplements.purpose: Strengthen stems for heavy flower weight. Add FIRST before other nutrients.

tasks.description: Ramp up to full veg irrigation schedule. 3-6 shots/day depending on media. First shot within 30 min of lights on.
tasks.name: Full irrigation capacity

tasks.description: Collect runoff from multiple plants. Input EC vs runoff EC delta is your most important number. Keep delta under 0.5. If runoff EC creeps up, flush before flipping to flower.
tasks.name: Runoff EC comparison — every 2 days

tasks.description: Flush all containers 2-3 days before flipping to flower. Run plain pH'd water at 3x volume. This resets the root zone and prevents salt stress during transition.
tasks.name: Pre-flip flush

tasks.description: Remove and inspect emitters. Soak in H2O2 (3%) or pH 3.0 citric acid solution for 30 minutes. Rinse and reinstall. Do this before flip — you don't want emitter failures during flower.
tasks.name: Check and clean emitters

tasks.description: Defoliate lower growth that won't receive light. Level the canopy with LST. The goal is an even canopy ready for flower.
tasks.name: Canopy management

tasks.description: Complete all topping, LST, and SCROG net filling before flip. No heavy training after flip.
tasks.name: Final training

training.description: Install screen/net at canopy height. Weave branches through to create perfectly even canopy. Fill 70-80% of squares before flipping to flower.
Remove lower 1/3 of growth that won't reach the canopy. This redirects energy to top colas and improves airflow.

transition_signals: Drinking heavily (containers light within hours of last shot)

common_problems.cause: Genetics, too much day/night temperature difference, or insufficient light intensity
common_problems.solution: Supercrop tall branches (bend stem 90°, tape to support). Reduce DIF (day-night temp gap) to 5°F. Increase light intensity. Some strains just stretch — plan for it.

common_problems.cause: Light leaks during dark period, stress, or genetic predisposition
common_problems.solution: Fix light leaks immediately. Remove pollen sacs with tweezers. If severe, remove the plant. Hermaphrodites pollinate the room.

common_problems.cause: Rapid growth demanding more calcium than you're supplying
common_problems.solution: Increase CalMag to 3-4 ml/gal. Calcium deficiency shows as brown spots on new growth. This is extremely common during stretch.

common_problems.cause: Heavy feeding + reduced water uptake as plant adjusts to new light cycle
common_problems.solution: Flush with plain pH'd water. Increase runoff target to 20% for a few days. Then return to normal schedule.

stages.description: Light cycle flipped to 12/12. Explosive vertical growth (stretch — plant may double in height). Irrigation strategy transitions from pure vegetative steering toward generative. Begin increasing dryback slightly while maintaining high frequency. The plant needs heavy feeding during stretch to support rapid growth. Switch nutrient ratio toward bloom.

environment.notes: Flip to 12/12. Temperature differential (DIF) between day/night helps control stretch: keep day temps steady, drop night temps 8-10°F. VPD climbing. Humidity dropping toward flower targets.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: light dep tarps can control photoperiod precisely. Pull tarps at the same time daily — consistency matters.

extra_tasks.description: If using light deprivation, pull tarps at the same time every day. Inconsistent timing confuses the plant.
extra_tasks.name: Pull light dep tarps consistently

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse: light dep tarps give photoperiod control.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor: natural photoperiod triggers stretch. No manual flip. Watch for light pollution from street lights or neighbors — can cause revegging or hermaphrodites.

extra_problems.cause: External light sources disrupting dark period
extra_problems.issue: Revegging or hermaphrodites from light pollution
extra_problems.solution: Block light sources, move plants, or accept the risk. Outdoor grows near buildings are high-risk for light pollution.

extra_tasks.description: Any light during the dark period (street lights, porch lights, even moonlight through clouds in extreme cases) can cause hermaphrodites. Audit the grow area.
extra_tasks.name: Check for light pollution

outdoor.notes: Outdoor: stretch timing depends on latitude and photoperiod.

health_checks: All emitters flowing under the thickening canopy?
Runoff EC stable and within 0.5 of input?
No light leaks during dark period?

medium.crop_steering: transitioning — vegetative early in stretch, shifting generative

coco.notes: Coco: maintain 4 shots/day during stretch. Increase dryback slightly (8-12%) as transition progresses. Heavy CalMag — stretch demands calcium.

perlite.notes: Perlite: 5 shots/day during stretch. Rapid growth means rapid water demand. Don't let dryback exceed 15% or you'll stress the plant during its most critical growth phase.

rockwool.notes: Rockwool: 3 shots/day. Allow slightly more dryback (8-10%) as you transition to generative. Monitor slab EC — stretch is a heavy feeding period.

medium.notes: Transition irrigation: still frequent (plant is growing explosively) but begin increasing dryback to start the shift toward generative steering. The goal is to maintain enough water for stretch growth while signaling the plant to shift from vegetative to reproductive mode. Runoff EC monitoring is critical — stretch is when salt problems can spiral.

stages.name: Transition (Stretch)

nutrients.approach: Near full strength. Transition from grow-heavy to bloom-heavy ratio. Increase CalMag — stretch demands extra calcium for rapid cell wall construction.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root protection. Warm flowering temps promote pathogens.

supplements.name: Silica (Armor Si)
supplements.purpose: Structural support for rapidly elongating stems. Add FIRST.

tasks.description: Change timer to 12 hours on / 12 hours off. Ensure ZERO light leaks during dark period — even brief light exposure can cause hermaphrodites.
tasks.name: Flip light cycle to 12/12

tasks.description: Shift from veg ratio (high Gro) to bloom ratio (high Bloom). Increase CalMag for stretch calcium demand. Do this gradually over 3-5 days.
tasks.name: Transition nutrient ratio

tasks.description: Stretch is the highest-demand period. Runoff EC can swing rapidly. Check daily and adjust. Input EC 2.0-2.4, runoff should be within 0.5.
tasks.name: Monitor runoff EC daily during stretch

tasks.description: Supercrop (bend and tape) any branches growing too tall. Tuck branches into SCROG net. This is your last chance to control height.
tasks.name: Manage stretch height

tasks.description: Watch for male pollen sacs or female pistils at nodes. Remove any males immediately. Hermaphrodite plants (male + female) should be removed unless you want seeds.
tasks.name: Check for preflowers / sex

tasks.description: As canopy fills in, emitter access becomes harder. Verify flow now — a clogged emitter during flower development is devastating.
tasks.name: Check emitters under canopy

training.description: Gently crush and bend tall stems 90° to control height. The stem heals into a knuckle that's actually stronger. Tape to support if needed.
training.timing: During first 2 weeks of stretch only

training.description: Continue tucking new growth under the net to maintain even canopy. Stop tucking when stretch ends.

transition_signals: White pistils appearing at bud sites
Flower sites visible at branch tips
Plant focus shifting from vertical growth to bud development

common_problems.cause: EC too high for the plant's tolerance, or salt accumulation in media
common_problems.solution: Reduce EC by 0.2-0.4. Flush with plain pH'd water. Then resume at lower strength. Some cultivars are more sensitive than others.

common_problems.cause: Still steering too vegetative — dryback too low, EC too low, or temperature too high
common_problems.solution: Increase dryback. Increase EC. Drop day temps to 77-78°F. More aggressive generative steering.

common_problems.cause: Humidity too high, poor airflow, or dense buds trapping moisture
common_problems.solution: Remove affected buds immediately (cut 2 inches below the rot). Lower humidity to 45%. Increase airflow. Defoliate to open canopy. Prevention is everything — once rot is inside a bud, that bud is lost.

common_problems.cause: High-EC nutrient solution precipitating in emitter
common_problems.solution: Replace emitter immediately. Clean all emitters with H2O2 soak during next flush. Consider inline sediment filter. This is why spare emitters should always be on hand.

stages.description: Stretch has ended. Buds forming and fattening. Shift irrigation strategy to generative steering: increase dryback, reduce shot frequency slightly, and increase EC. The goal is to stress the plant mildly to push energy into flower production rather than vegetative growth. This is where crop steering separates amateur from professional results.

environment.notes: Peak light intensity. Humidity dropping to prevent bud rot. Day/night temperature differential (DIF) of 10-12°F promotes terpene production and tight flower structure. CO2 supplementation to 1000-1200 ppm if available.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: excellent for flower quality if humidity is managed. Use dehumidifier or exhaust fans to keep humidity under 55%.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse: humidity management is key during flower.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor: natural humidity fluctuations make bud rot prevention harder. Airflow is weather-dependent. Consider shade cloth on extreme heat days.

extra_problems.cause: Outdoor moisture trapped in flower sites
extra_problems.solution: Shake plants after rain. Defoliate aggressively for airflow. Cover plants during extended rain if possible. Inspect daily.

extra_tasks.description: Water trapped inside developing buds = bud rot. Gently shake plants to remove moisture from flower sites. Improve drainage around plants.
extra_tasks.name: Shake plants after rain or heavy dew

outdoor.notes: Outdoor flower: weather awareness is critical for bud rot prevention.

health_checks: All emitters flowing under dense canopy?
Runoff EC within 0.5 of input EC?
Buds forming at all flower sites?
No bud rot or powdery mildew?

medium.first_shot_timing: 1 hour after lights on — allow morning dryback

coco.notes: Coco: 4 shots/day but push dryback to 12-18% for generative steering. First shot delayed 1 hour after lights on (let the plant work for it). Higher EC (1.6-2.0) supports quality flower development. CalMag still every feed.

perlite.notes: Perlite: 5 shots/day. Perlite makes generative steering harder — it dries too fast. Consider mixing with 30% coco for better moisture retention during flower.

rockwool.notes: Rockwool: 3 shots/day. Increase dryback to 10-15%. This is precision crop steering territory — substrate sensors (Teros/Aroya) give you exact dryback data. Target slab weight dropping to 55-65% before first shot.

medium.notes: GENERATIVE STEERING: The key shift. Delay first shot to 1 hour after lights on (allow morning dryback). Increase overall dryback target (12-20% depending on media). Increase EC (push harder). Reduce overnight moisture. The plant responds to mild drought stress by pushing energy into flower production. This is the difference between average and exceptional yields. Monitor closely — too much dryback = stressed plant, too little = continued vegetative growth instead of flowering.

stages.name: Early Flower

nutrients.approach: Full strength bloom formula. Heavy phosphorus and potassium for flower development. Maintain CalMag. EC 2.4-2.8 for generative push.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root protection throughout flower.

supplements.name: Liquid Kool Bloom
supplements.purpose: PK booster for early flower development. Start light, increase in mid-flower.

tasks.description: Delay first irrigation to 1 hour after lights on. Increase dryback targets. Increase EC by 0.2-0.4. This signals the plant to push energy into flowers.
tasks.name: Shift to generative steering

tasks.description: Generative steering with higher EC means salt buildup risk is higher. Check runoff EC every 2 days minimum. Flush if runoff EC exceeds input by more than 0.5.
tasks.name: Monitor runoff EC closely

tasks.description: Remove large fan leaves blocking bud sites and airflow. Open up the interior of the canopy. This is critical for bud rot prevention.
tasks.name: Defoliate for airflow

tasks.description: Inspect flowers for gray mold (botrytis) or white powdery mildew. With humidity at 45-55% and good airflow, risk is manageable. Catch problems early.
tasks.name: Check for bud rot / PM

tasks.description: Canopy is now very dense. Get under it and verify every emitter is flowing. Use a flashlight. A failed emitter during flower = major yield loss from that plant.
tasks.name: Emitter check under dense canopy

tasks.description: Flush entire system with plain pH'd water at 3x volume. High-EC feeding during flower increases clog risk.
tasks.name: Flush drip lines

training.description: Remove fan leaves blocking bud sites and airflow. Do ONE defoliation at the start of early flower, then only remove individual leaves as needed. Never strip more than 20% of leaves at once.
Remove all growth below the SCROG net or lower 1/3 of the plant. These sites won't produce quality buds and divert energy from top colas.
training.timing: First week of early flower only

common_problems.cause: Runoff EC far above input EC — salts have accumulated beyond plant tolerance
common_problems.issue: Salt lockout (yellowing, brown spots, stunted growth)
common_problems.solution: Emergency flush: 3-5x container volume of plain pH 5.8 water. Resume at 80% previous EC. Increase runoff target to 25%. More frequent flushes.

common_problems.cause: Humidity too high, insufficient airflow inside canopy, or water splashing on buds
common_problems.solution: Remove affected bud plus 2 inches below it. Lower humidity to 40%. Add oscillating fan aimed at canopy. Defoliate to improve airflow. Inspect remaining buds daily.

common_problems.cause: EC too high for this cultivar's tolerance
common_problems.solution: Some cultivars can't handle EC 3.0. Drop to 2.4-2.6. Better to have slightly lower EC than burnt plants. Watch leaf tips — crispy tips are the early warning.

common_problems.cause: Root zone issues — overwatering, root rot, or pH lockout
common_problems.solution: Check runoff pH (should be 5.8-6.2). Check for root rot smell. If roots are brown/slimy, add Hydroguard and reduce irrigation frequency. Healthy roots are white.

stages.description: Peak bud development. Buds fattening rapidly, trichome production accelerating. Full generative steering: maximum dryback, highest EC of the entire grow. This is where yields are made or lost. Runoff management is critical — high EC means salt buildup accelerates. Emitter reliability is non-negotiable.

environment.notes: Peak light intensity. Humidity must stay under 50% — dense buds trap moisture and bud rot risk is highest now. Day/night DIF of 12°F+ enhances terpene production. CO2 at 1200-1500 ppm if supplementing.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: dehumidification is critical. Vent during dry periods, close up during rain. Target 40-45% RH.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse: dehumidifier running constantly during mid-flower.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor: humidity control is impossible. Focus on airflow, defoliation, and daily bud inspection. Autumn rain is the enemy.

extra_problems.cause: Extended wet weather during peak bloom
extra_problems.solution: Cover plants if possible. Harvest early if forecast shows extended rain. A slightly early harvest is better than a moldy one.

extra_tasks.description: Water in buds = rot within 48 hours. Shake every morning and after every rain. No exceptions.
extra_tasks.name: Shake plants after rain/dew

outdoor.notes: Outdoor mid-flower: weather is the primary risk factor.

health_checks: Runoff EC within 0.5 of input EC?
No bud rot in any cola?

medium.first_shot_timing: 1-2 hours after lights on — maximum morning dryback

coco.notes: Coco: 4 shots/day with 18-22% dryback. Peak generative steering. EC 2.6-3.0. First shot delayed 1-2 hours after lights on. Higher runoff (20%) to manage salt accumulation. CalMag every feed.

perlite.notes: Perlite: 5 shots/day. Aggressive dryback is easy in perlite (too easy). Watch for over-stressing — wilting between shots means you've gone too far. Back off dryback 2-3%.

rockwool.notes: Rockwool: 3 shots/day. Dryback 12-18%. Substrate sensors are invaluable here — slab weight should drop to 50-55% before first shot. EC 2.6-3.0. This is precision agriculture.

medium.notes: PEAK GENERATIVE STEERING. Maximum dryback, highest EC, delayed first shot. The plant is working hardest now — every calorie goes to bud production. Runoff at 20%+ to prevent salt lockout at these high EC levels. Flush every 7-10 days. This is the highest-risk, highest-reward period of the grow.

stages.name: Mid Flower (Peak Bloom)

nutrients.approach: Full bloom strength. Peak PK (phosphorus/potassium). EC 2.6-3.0. This is the heaviest feeding of the entire grow.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root protection. Warm media + high nutrients = pathogen risk.

supplements.name: Liquid Kool Bloom
supplements.purpose: PK booster at peak dose. Drives bud density and weight.

tasks.description: Delay first shot 1-2 hours after lights on. Dryback 15-25%. EC 2.6-3.0. This is aggressive — monitor plant response daily.
tasks.name: Maximum generative steering

tasks.description: At this EC, salt accumulation is rapid. Runoff EC exceeding input by >0.5 means flush immediately. Do not let it spiral.
tasks.name: Runoff EC monitoring — every other day

tasks.description: Flush all containers with plain pH'd water at 3x volume. Reset salt accumulation. Resume feeding next irrigation.
tasks.name: Full system flush

tasks.description: Dense buds in mid-flower are prime bud rot targets. Pull apart the largest colas gently and inspect inside. Catch rot early = lose one bud. Catch it late = lose the plant.
tasks.name: Inspect buds for mold daily

tasks.description: Verify all emitters under the now-dense canopy. At this stage, losing an emitter for even 2 days means significant yield loss from that plant.
tasks.name: Emitter check

tasks.description: Buds are heavy. Use trellis nets, yo-yos, or bamboo stakes to support branches. A snapped branch at this stage is devastating.
tasks.name: Support heavy branches

common_problems.cause: pH lockout, over-drying, or root problems — not natural senescence
common_problems.solution: Check runoff pH. Ensure irrigation hasn't been reduced too aggressively. Gradual yellowing = good. Sudden browning/crisping = problem.

common_problems.cause: Light stress (too close or too intense) or heat stress
common_problems.solution: Raise lights or dim by 10-15%. Late flower buds are sensitive to light/heat stress. Foxtails reduce bag appeal but don't ruin potency.

common_problems.cause: Temperatures too warm, or genetics are slow-finishing
common_problems.solution: Drop day temps to 73-75°F. Some cultivars take 10-12 weeks of flower. Check breeder's stated flowering time. Patience.

stages.description: Buds ripening. Trichomes transitioning from clear to milky to amber. Begin reducing EC and irrigation frequency. The plant is finishing — it needs less food and less water. Reduce generative stress. Some growers begin a pre-flush reduction here, dropping EC gradually before the full flush stage.

environment.notes: Slightly reduced light intensity — plant is winding down. Cooler night temps (58-62°F) can bring out purple/red colors in some cultivars. Humidity at 35-45% — bud rot risk remains high with these dense, mature buds.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: supplemental heating may be needed for cool nights. Target 60-65°F minimum.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse: heating during cold nights enhances color development.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor: late season cold snaps can damage buds. Cover plants on frost nights. Be ready to harvest early if freeze is forecast.

extra_problems.cause: Overnight temperatures below freezing
extra_problems.solution: Harvest immediately if frost hits. Cover plants with tarps or blankets on borderline nights. Move containers indoors overnight if possible.

extra_tasks.description: Frost kills cannabis instantly. If overnight temps will drop below 32°F, cover plants or harvest. Below 40°F, plant metabolism slows dramatically.
extra_tasks.name: Monitor frost forecasts

outdoor.notes: Outdoor: late season weather dictates harvest timing.

health_checks: Trichomes progressing from milky toward amber?

coco.notes: Coco: reduce to 3 shots/day. EC dropping to 2.0-2.4. Dryback 15-20%. Plant is drinking less — overwatering now delays ripening. CalMag reduced to 1.5 ml/gal.

perlite.notes: Perlite: 4 shots/day (reduced from 5). Lower volume per shot. Plant is drinking less but perlite still drains fast.

rockwool.notes: Rockwool: 2 shots/day. EC 2.0-2.4. Slab weight dropping as plant drinks less. Allow more time between shots. Rockwool retains — don't overwater a finishing plant.

medium.notes: Tapering down. The plant is finishing — reduce shot frequency, reduce EC, maintain moderate dryback. Some yellowing of fan leaves is normal and desirable (plant is consuming stored nutrients). Watch for over-drying — a completely dry root zone at this stage can cause premature death and harsh, unripened buds.

stages.name: Late Flower (Ripening)

nutrients.approach: Reducing. Drop EC to 2.0-2.4. Reduce nitrogen (lower Flora Micro/Gro). Maintain PK for final bud ripening. Some growers begin the taper here.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root protection through finish.

supplements.name: Liquid Kool Bloom
supplements.purpose: Reduced PK booster. Tapering down from peak.

tasks.description: Drop EC to 2.0-2.4. Reduce shots per day by 1. Plant is finishing and needs less. Watch for fan leaf yellowing — that's normal nutrient fade.
tasks.name: Reduce EC and irrigation

tasks.description: Use 60-100x jeweler's loupe or digital microscope on buds (not sugar leaves). Track trichome progression: clear → milky → amber. Harvest timing depends on this.
tasks.name: Trichome checks with magnification

tasks.description: Risk remains high through harvest. Daily inspection of largest colas. Squeeze gently — rot feels soft/hollow inside.
tasks.name: Continue bud rot inspection

tasks.description: Continue monitoring but less aggressively. Goal is to draw down nutrients in the media before flush stage.
tasks.name: Runoff monitoring

tasks.description: Based on trichome progression, estimate harvest date. Plan flush stage to start 7-14 days before target harvest.
tasks.name: Plan harvest date

transition_signals: Trichomes mostly milky with some amber (5-15%)
Lower fan leaves yellowing and falling

common_problems.cause: Media heavily loaded with salts (especially rockwool)
common_problems.solution: Increase runoff to 30%+. More frequent irrigations. Consider flushing agent. Rockwool can take 10-14 days to fully flush.

common_problems.cause: Flush started too early, or plant was already stressed
common_problems.issue: Plant dying too fast (all leaves brown/crispy)
common_problems.solution: Check pH of runoff. If the plant has weeks of flower left, resume light feeding (EC 0.5-0.8) and flush later.

common_problems.cause: Continued moisture in dense buds with weakened plant defenses
common_problems.solution: Harvest immediately if rot is spreading. The flush can be shortened if bud rot is the alternative. Clean flower > perfectly flushed flower with rot.

stages.description: Final flush before harvest. Run plain pH'd water only — no nutrients. The goal is to force the plant to consume stored nutrients in the leaves and media, resulting in cleaner-burning, smoother-smoking flower. The flush debate is ongoing (some studies show no difference), but industry standard practice is 7-14 days of plain water. In drip systems, flush is straightforward: replace nutrient solution with plain pH'd water.

environment.notes: Reduce light intensity slightly. Maintain low humidity for bud rot prevention. Cooler temps are fine — plant is finishing.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: maintain low humidity during flush. Plant defenses are weakened during this stage.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse: keep humidity management active through flush.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor: flush by stopping nutrient feedings. If using drip, switch to plain water. Rain during flush is actually helpful — nature's flush.

outdoor.notes: Outdoor: rain helps flush. Monitor harvest window closely — weather can force early harvest.

health_checks: No nutrient solution accidentally mixed in?

coco.notes: Coco: 3 shots/day of plain pH 5.8-6.0 water. Higher runoff (25-30%) to leach salts. Monitor runoff EC — when it drops below 0.3-0.5, the media is flushed.

perlite.notes: Perlite: 3 shots/day. Perlite flushes fast — salts rinse out quickly. Runoff EC should drop to <0.3 within 3-5 days.
