rockwool.notes: Rockwool: 2 shots/day of plain water. Rockwool holds salts stubbornly — may need extra days to flush. Monitor runoff EC and keep flushing until <0.5.

medium.notes: PLAIN WATER ONLY. pH 5.8-6.0. No nutrients. High runoff (25-30%) to leach accumulated salts from media. Monitor runoff EC — flush is complete when runoff EC drops below 0.3-0.5. Fan leaf yellowing will accelerate dramatically — this is desired. The plant is consuming stored nutrients.

stages.name: Flush (Pre-Harvest)

nutrients.approach: Plain pH'd water only. Zero nutrients. Some growers use a flushing agent (Flora Kleen, Clearex) — optional.

supplements.name: Flora Kleen (optional)
supplements.purpose: Chelating agent that helps dissolve salt deposits in media. Optional — plain water works fine.

tasks.description: Drain nutrient reservoir. Clean reservoir. Fill with plain pH'd water (5.8-6.0). Run through entire drip system.
tasks.name: Switch to plain water

tasks.description: Run clean water through all lines for 15+ minutes to clear nutrient residue from manifold and emitters.
tasks.name: Flush drip lines

tasks.description: Check runoff EC from multiple plants daily. The flush is working when runoff EC drops below 0.5. Some media (rockwool) takes longer.
tasks.name: Monitor runoff EC

tasks.description: Monitor trichome color daily. Harvest window is approaching: mostly milky with 10-20% amber = peak potency/flavor.
tasks.name: Continue trichome checks

tasks.description: Final dryback: stop all irrigation 24-48 hours before planned chop. Let the media dry down. Some believe this final stress pushes trichome production. At minimum, it makes the plant easier to handle (less water weight).
tasks.name: Stop flush 24-48 hours before harvest

common_problems.cause: Hidden rot inside dense colas
common_problems.solution: Cut away rotted sections plus 1 inch margin. Salvage clean buds. Do not dry or cure rotted material — it contaminates everything.

common_problems.cause: Impatience or misreading trichomes
common_problems.solution: Check trichomes on buds, NOT sugar leaves (sugar leaves mature faster). If most are still clear, potency and weight are not maximized. Lesson for next grow.

common_problems.cause: Didn't flush system before shutdown
common_problems.issue: Emitters clogged with dried nutrients after shutdown
common_problems.solution: Soak in H2O2 or citric acid solution for several hours. Flush with warm water. Prevention: always flush lines before shutting down.

stages.description: Chop day. Cut plants, wet trim or whole-plant hang. The drip system is shut off. All emitters should be cleaned and stored. The most important thing: harvest at the right trichome ratio for desired effects (more milky = cerebral, more amber = sedative).

environment.notes: Some growers give 24-48 hours of darkness before chop (unproven but common practice). Harvest in the morning when terpenes are theoretically highest. Room light only needed for working.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: controlled harvest timing. Excellent conditions for wet trimming in the greenhouse.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse: use the space for drying if possible.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor: harvest timing may be forced by weather. Have drying space prepared in advance.

extra_tasks.description: If rain or frost is forecast, harvest early rather than risk losing the crop. Early harvest is better than no harvest.
extra_tasks.name: Harvest before rain/frost

outdoor.notes: Outdoor: weather can force harvest timing.

health_checks: No bud rot discovered during trim?

medium.notes: Drip system OFF. No irrigation. Clean and store all emitters, lines, and manifold after harvest.

stages.name: Harvest

nutrients.approach: None. System shut down.

tasks.description: Confirm trichome ratio is at target before chopping. Once you cut, there's no going back. Most growers target 70-80% milky, 10-20% amber, minimal clear.
tasks.name: Final trichome check

tasks.description: Cut the main stem at the base. For wet trim: remove fan leaves and trim sugar leaves close to buds now. For dry trim: remove fan leaves only, leave sugar leaves, hang whole plant or branches.
tasks.name: Harvest the plant

tasks.description: Remove all emitters. Soak in H2O2 (3%) for 1 hour. Flush all lines with H2O2 solution then plain water. Drain completely. Store dry.
tasks.name: Clean drip system

tasks.description: Coco can be reused (flush heavily, re-buffer with CalMag). Rockwool is single-use (dispose). Perlite can be rinsed and reused.
tasks.name: Dispose of media

tasks.description: Drain, scrub, sanitize reservoir, pumps, filters, saucers. Biofilm builds up over a grow cycle — remove it now.
tasks.name: Clean reservoir and equipment

transition_signals: All material hung or in drying room

common_problems.cause: Humidity too low, temperature too high, or too much airflow
common_problems.solution: Raise humidity to 60-65%. Lower temp to 60°F. Reduce fan speed. Whole-plant hang (no wet trim) slows drying. If buds are already dry, jar them and begin burping — moisture from stems will redistribute.

common_problems.cause: Humidity too high, insufficient airflow, or buds were wet at harvest
common_problems.solution: Remove moldy buds. Lower humidity to 55%. Increase gentle airflow. Separate dense buds from each other.

common_problems.cause: Drying too fast — chlorophyll trapped in buds
common_problems.solution: Slow down future dries. For this batch: a long cure (4-8 weeks) can partially recover the situation. Chlorophyll breaks down slowly during cure.

stages.description: Slow, controlled drying. The goal is 10-14 days in a dark room at 60°F / 60% humidity (the '60/60 rule'). Faster drying destroys terpenes and produces harsh smoke. Slower is almost always better. No drip system involvement — this is purely environmental control.

environment.notes: DARK. 60°F. 60% humidity. Gentle airflow (not blowing directly on buds). The drying room should smell like fresh cannabis, not hay. Hay smell = drying too fast.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: too warm and too much light for drying. Move to a separate dark room.

greenhouse.notes: Do not dry in the greenhouse — move to a controlled dark room.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor: must dry indoors. Outdoor drying is too uncontrolled. Any indoor space with environmental control works.

outdoor.notes: Always dry indoors regardless of grow method.

health_checks: No mold visible on drying buds?

medium.notes: No drip system involvement. Drying is purely environmental control.

stages.name: Drying

nutrients.approach: None. Plant is harvested.

tasks.description: Hang upside down on lines or hangers. Space plants so they don't touch each other. Air must circulate around all sides.
tasks.name: Hang whole plants or branches

tasks.description: 60°F, 60% humidity, darkness. Use AC/heater for temp, humidifier/dehumidifier for RH. Monitor with hygrometer — buy a good one.
tasks.name: Maintain 60/60 environment

tasks.description: Bend a small stem. If it snaps cleanly (not bends), buds are ready for trim and cure. If it bends without snapping, keep drying. Outer buds will feel dry but stems should still have slight flex.
tasks.name: Check drying progress

tasks.description: Mold can still appear during drying, especially in dense buds. Inspect daily. Remove any moldy material immediately.
tasks.name: Inspect for mold during drying

transition_signals: Small stems snap cleanly when bent
Outer buds feel dry but not crunchy
Larger stems still have slight flex

common_problems.cause: Buds were too wet when jarred — anaerobic bacteria decomposing
common_problems.solution: Immediately remove buds from jars. Spread on screen and dry for 12-24 hours. Rejar. If smell persists, the buds may be compromised.

common_problems.cause: Over-dried before jarring, or too much burping
common_problems.solution: Add Boveda 62 pack — it will slowly rehydrate. Reduce burping. A tortilla in the jar for a few hours is a folk remedy (adds moisture, remove promptly to avoid mold).

common_problems.cause: Buds too wet when jarred, or not burping enough
common_problems.solution: Remove all buds from jar. Discard moldy buds (do NOT smoke). Dry remaining buds for 12 hours. Sanitize jar. Rejar and burp more frequently.

stages.description: Slow cure in airtight containers. This is where terpenes develop, harshness mellows, and the final product quality is determined. A proper cure is the difference between good and great cannabis. Minimum 2 weeks, ideal 4-8 weeks. Some connoisseurs cure for 6+ months.

environment.notes: Inside the jar: 58-62% RH (use Boveda 62 packs as insurance). Store jars in a dark, cool place. Light degrades THC into CBN. Heat accelerates degradation.

environment_overrides.notes: Do not cure in the greenhouse — temperature fluctuations degrade quality. Move to a stable indoor location.

greenhouse.notes: Cure indoors, not in greenhouse.

environment_overrides.notes: Same curing process regardless of grow method. Store jars indoors in a cool, dark place.

outdoor.notes: Curing is identical for all grow methods.

health_checks: No mold or ammonia smell when opening jars?
Buds gradually improving in smell and smoothness?

medium.notes: No drip system involvement. Curing is purely post-harvest processing.

stages.name: Curing

nutrients.approach: None. Post-harvest.

tasks.description: If dry-trimmed: trim sugar leaves now. Place trimmed buds loosely in mason jars (fill 75%, not packed). If wet-trimmed: buds are ready to jar directly.
tasks.name: Trim and jar buds

tasks.description: Open each jar for 10-15 minutes, 2-3 times per day for the first week. Second week: once per day. This exchanges stale air and releases excess moisture. If buds feel wet when you open, leave lids off for 1 hour.
tasks.name: Burp jars — first 2 weeks

tasks.description: Place Boveda 62% RH packs in each jar (1 per 28g/1oz). They maintain perfect humidity passively. Replace when they feel crunchy/hard.
tasks.name: Add Boveda packs

tasks.description: After 2 weeks: burp once every 2-3 days. After 4 weeks: once per week. After 8 weeks: jars can be sealed long-term.
tasks.name: Reduce burping after 2 weeks

tasks.description: Inspect buds when burping. Any white fuzzy mold = remove affected buds immediately and increase burping frequency for that jar.
tasks.name: Monitor for mold during cure

transition_signals: Buds smell rich and complex (not grassy or hay-like)
Jar humidity stabilized at 58-62% without Boveda

container_progression.commercial_medium: Turkey bags in sealed bins, nitrogen-flushed
container_progression.industrial: Climate-controlled vault, nitrogen generators, automated monitoring

degradation_timeline.18_plus_months: Convert to extracts, edibles, or topicals.

testing_schedule.360_days: Full panel — sell, process, or destroy decision

common_problems.cause: Heat, light, oxygen, or time
common_problems.solution: Darkness, cool temps (60°F), minimal oxygen. ~5%/year baseline.

common_problems.cause: Temps above 70°F, oxygen, frequent opening
common_problems.solution: Below 65°F. Nitrogen-sealed. Minimize opening.

common_problems.cause: Humidity above 65% or improper cure
common_problems.solution: Verify 58-62% RH before sealing. Remove affected material.

common_problems.cause: Normal moisture equilibration
common_problems.solution: Boveda packs. Sealed containers.

stages.description: Post-cure long-term storage. Drip systems — especially commercial operations with crop steering — produce premium flower that commands top dollar. Proper storage preserves that premium quality. Commercial drip farms may store hundreds of pounds across multiple strain batches. FIFO inventory and degradation tracking are essential.

environment.notes: DARK. Cool. Stable. Zero light — UV destroys cannabinoids and terpenes. Commercial: 58-62°F, 55-60% RH, nitrogen atmosphere.

environment_overrides.notes: Do NOT store in greenhouse.

greenhouse.notes: Climate-controlled indoor space only.

environment_overrides.notes: Storage is always indoor.

outdoor.notes: Indoor controlled environment.

stages.name: Long-Term Storage

nutrients.approach: None.

tasks.description: Home: mason jars with Boveda 58-62%. Commercial: nitrogen-sealed grove bags, CVault, or nitrogen-flushed drums.
tasks.name: Transfer to long-term containers

tasks.description: Strain, harvest date, storage date, weight, batch number, crop steering profile used. Commercial: seed-to-sale, FIFO rotation.
tasks.name: Label and track batches

tasks.description: Inspect for mold, off odors. Check humidity packs. Commercial: potency/terpene testing at 30/90/180 days.
tasks.name: Monthly quality checks

tasks.description: Monitor vault temp/humidity. No light leaks. Commercial: automated alerts.
tasks.name: Maintain environment

tasks.description: First in, first out. Flag batches approaching 12 months.
tasks.name: Rotate stock (FIFO)

tasks.description: Commercial: retain samples per regulations.
tasks.name: Compliance testing holds

autoflower.description: Ruderalis-hybrid strains that flower automatically regardless of light schedule. Fixed timeline.

light_schedule.flower: 20/4 or 18/6 (same as veg — no change needed)
light_schedule.transition: Automatic — plant decides when to flower (usually week 3-4)

autoflower.name: Autoflower
autoflower.notes: Fixed timeline — stress cannot be recovered by extending veg. Start in final pot (no transplant shock). LST preferred over HST.

training_suitability.defoliation: light only — auto can't recover from heavy defol
training_suitability.topping: risky — only if plant is vigorous

photoperiod.description: Standard cannabis strains that require 12/12 light to flower. Grower controls timing.

light_schedule.transition: Switch to 12/12 when plant is ~50% of desired final height

photoperiod.name: Photoperiod
photoperiod.notes: Can be vegged as long as desired. Cloneable. More forgiving of stress (can extend veg to recover).

training_suitability.defoliation: good
training_suitability.topping: excellent

issues.cause: Clogged emitter on that plant
issues.fix: Check emitter immediately. Replace or clean (H2O2 soak). This is the most common drip system problem.
issues.symptom: One plant wilting while others are fine

issues.cause: Flow rate variation between emitters
issues.fix: Test all emitters into cups for 5 min. Replace outliers. Use pressure-compensating emitters. Add pressure regulator for large systems.

issues.cause: No inline filter, or high-EC solution precipitating
issues.fix: Install 100-200 mesh inline filter. Clean filter weekly. Lower nutrient concentration slightly. Use nutrient brands designed for drip systems.

issues.cause: Pump failing, filter clogged, or airline kinked
issues.fix: Check pump output. Clean filter. Inspect all tubing for kinks. Check pump intake for debris.

issues.cause: Gravity siphon through emitters below reservoir level
issues.fix: Install anti-siphon valve or check valve after pump. Or raise emitters above reservoir water level.

issues.cause: Salt accumulation in media
issues.fix: Flush with 3x container volume of plain pH'd water. Increase runoff target to 20-25%. Reduce nutrient strength 10-15%.
issues.symptom: Runoff EC much higher than input (delta > 0.5)

issues.cause: Plant consuming more nutrients than provided
issues.fix: Increase nutrient strength. Increase shot volume. Plant is hungry — this is good news, it means rapid growth.

issues.cause: Media buffering exhausted, or salt buildup altering pH
issues.fix: Flush media. Re-buffer coco with CalMag soak if pH is high. Check for root rot if pH is very low (<5.0).
issues.symptom: Runoff pH very different from input pH

issues.cause: Evaporation concentrating salts at surface
issues.fix: Normal in drip systems. Top-dress with perlite/hydroton. Increase runoff %. Flush more frequently. Surface salts don't directly harm roots but indicate overall salt load.
issues.symptom: White salt crust on media surface

issues.cause: Shot volume too low, or media absorbing everything
issues.fix: Increase shot volume until 10-20% runs off. Without runoff, you have no diagnostic data and salts accumulate unchecked.

issues.cause: Dryback too low, EC too low — still steering vegetative
issues.fix: Increase dryback to 15-20%. Increase EC. Delay first shot. Widen the gap between last shot and lights off.
issues.symptom: Plant still growing vegetatively during flower

issues.cause: Insufficient generative steering, or light intensity too low
issues.fix: Push dryback higher (20-25%). Increase EC. Check light — PPFD 700-1000 needed for dense buds. CO2 helps.
issues.symptom: Buds small and airy despite good genetics

issues.cause: Dryback too aggressive, or media dried too much overnight
issues.fix: Reduce dryback target by 3-5%. Add one more shot per day. Don't over-steer — stressed plants don't produce well either.

issues.cause: Overwatering or root zone staying too wet
issues.fix: Reduce shot frequency. Increase dryback slightly. Lift pots — if heavy for hours after last shot, you're overwatering.
issues.symptom: Slow growth in veg despite good conditions

issues.cause: Environmental changes (temp/humidity swings) affecting transpiration rate
issues.fix: Stabilize environment first. Use substrate sensors for real-time monitoring. Adjust irrigation schedule to match environmental conditions.

cons: RO system cost and waste water
water_source_profiles.name: Reverse Osmosis (RO) Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Gold standard for precision growing. Always add CalMag first when using RO.
pros: Blank slate — total control over mineral content

dechlorination.chloramine: Does NOT gas off — must use Campden tablets or activated carbon filter
dechlorination.chlorine: Let sit uncovered 24 hours (gasses off) or use dechlorinator drops
dechlorination.how_to_tell: Check city water report. Most modern cities use chloramine.

water_source_profiles.name: Municipal Tap Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Good enough for most grows. Dechlorinate and pH-adjust. Get a water report from your city.

cons: May contain iron, sulfur, or heavy metals
water_source_profiles.name: Well Water
water_source_profiles.notes: MUST get a water test before using. High iron or sulfur can cause lockout. Great if your well has clean water with moderate minerals.

water_source_profiles.name: Distilled Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Same as RO but purchased instead of filtered. Only practical for 1-2 plant grows.

advanced_techniques.description: Elevating CO2 from ambient (400ppm) to 1000-1500ppm increases photosynthesis rate by 20-40% when light and temperature are also increased.

methods.notes: Most common. Tank refills every 2-4 weeks for a tent.
Produces heat and humidity — better for large rooms with AC.
Produces ~100-200ppm boost. Only useful for small sealed spaces. Not worth it for most.

advanced_techniques.name: CO2 Supplementation

ppfd_co2_correlation.notes: Ambient CO2 sufficient at low light
Slight benefit from supplementation
Moderate benefit — cost-effective threshold
Strong benefit — diminishing returns above this
Maximum practical benefit for most grows
Commercial intensity — requires perfect environment

requirements: Sealed grow space (CO2 escapes quickly from leaky rooms)
High light intensity (>600 PPFD — plants can't use extra CO2 without sufficient light)
Higher temperature tolerance (82-86°F optimal with CO2 vs 75-80°F without)
CO2 controller or timer

advanced_techniques.description: Manipulating environmental and irrigation parameters to push the plant toward vegetative or generative (flowering/fruiting) growth.
advanced_techniques.name: Crop Steering
advanced_techniques.notes: Crop steering is most effective in hydro/coco where irrigation frequency is highly controllable. Soil growers have less precision due to soil buffering.

vegetative_steering.goal: More leaf/stem growth, larger plant framework

best_practices: Spray during lights-off or first/last 30 minutes of light cycle (stomata open, no light burn)
Use fine mist sprayer — coverage is key
pH foliar solution to 6.2-6.5 (leaf surface slightly higher pH than root zone)
EC should be very low (0.2-0.5) — leaves burn easily
Never foliar spray during flower (moisture in buds → mold/bud rot)
Best for micronutrient deficiency correction (CalMag, iron, magnesium)

common_foliar_products.frequency: Weekly preventive, 3-day intervals for active infestations

advanced_techniques.description: Spraying nutrient solution directly onto leaves for rapid uptake. Bypasses root zone entirely.
advanced_techniques.name: Foliar Feeding

advanced_techniques.description: Strengthens cell walls, increases stem thickness, improves heat/drought/pest resistance.
important_notes: ALWAYS add silica to water FIRST before other nutrients (raises pH significantly)
Let silica mix for 5-10 minutes before adding base nutrients
Silica is incompatible with concentrated nutrient solutions — never mix undiluted
Discontinue in late flower (last 2-3 weeks)
advanced_techniques.name: Silica (Potassium Silicate)

advanced_techniques.description: Introduces beneficial bacteria/fungi to the root zone to outcompete pathogens, improve nutrient uptake, and protect against root diseases.
important_notes: Do NOT use hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) with beneficial microbes — it kills them
Choose either sterile (H2O2) OR beneficial (Hydroguard) approach — not both
Beneficials need warmish water (65-75°F) to thrive
Reapply after any reservoir change or system cleaning
advanced_techniques.name: Beneficial Microbes & Root Inoculants

products.name: Hydroguard
products.notes: The standard for DWC/RDWC. Add at every res change.

products.dosing: 1 tsp/gal (water-in) or dust roots at transplant
products.name: Great White
products.notes: Best for soil/coco. Less effective in pure hydro.

products.name: Recharge
products.notes: Feeds existing soil microbes. Great for living soil.

products.name: Mammoth P
products.notes: Most effective in flower when P demand is highest.

advanced_techniques.description: Removing select fan leaves to improve light penetration, airflow, and direct energy to bud sites.
advanced_techniques.name: Defoliation (Strategic Leaf Removal)
rules: Never remove more than 20-30% of leaves at once (except schwazze technique)
Only defoliate healthy, vigorous plants — never stressed ones
Autoflowers: very light defoliation only — they can't recover from heavy removal
Always leave the top 2-3 fan leaves on each branch (they feed the bud below)

timing.method: Remove lower leaves that get no light (lollipop)
Major defoliation day 1 and day 21 of flower (schwazzing)
Selective tuck or remove leaves blocking bud sites

siphon_elbow.purpose: Maintains 1-2 inch reservoir in bucket bottom for continuous root moisture.

equipment.description: Individual containers with siphon elbow drain. 3.5 gal standard.
equipment.name: Dutch/Bato buckets (3-5 gal)

equipment.description: Shared nutrient reservoir. Size based on bucket count.
equipment.name: Reservoir (50-100 gal)

equipment.description: Pumps from reservoir to drip manifold.
equipment.name: Feed pump (submersible)

equipment.description: Mainline, branches, and drip stakes per bucket.
equipment.name: Drip manifold + emitters

equipment.description: Shared drain collecting runoff from all buckets.
equipment.name: Return/drain line

equipment.description: Maintains 1-2 inch water level in bucket bottom.
equipment.name: Siphon elbows

equipment.description: Controls feed pump cycles. Minute-precision.
equipment.name: Digital timer

equipment.description: Fills each bucket. Perlite most common.
equipment.name: Growing media (perlite/hydroton/coco)

equipment.description: Screen filter between pump and emitters.
equipment.name: Inline filter

equipment.description: Monitor reservoir and runoff.
equipment.name: pH/EC meters

equipment.description: Aerate reservoir. Prevents stagnation.
equipment.name: Air pump + stones

equipment.description: Auto-top-off reservoir from water line.
equipment.name: Float valve

equipment.description: Funnel runoff to drain line.
equipment.name: Drain catch trays

equipment.description: Elevated for gravity drain to reservoir.
equipment.name: Bucket stands/rail

equipment.description: For recirculating setups. Prevents pathogen spread.
equipment.name: UV sterilizer

equipment.description: Redundancy for critical feed system.
equipment.name: Backup pump

flush_timing.coco: 7-14 days reduced nutrients → plain water last 3-5 days
flush_timing.notes: Flushing is debated — some studies show no difference. At minimum, reduce nutrients in final week.
flush_timing.soil: 14-21 days plain water (soil holds nutrients longer)

other_harvest_indicators: Most pistils (hairs) have darkened and curled in
Calyxes are swollen and dense
Fan leaves are yellowing and falling off naturally
Trichome stalks are visible and heads are enlarged
Breeder's recommended flowering time has passed

trichome_ratios.notes: Harvest early for more THC-dominant, less CBN. Energetic, racy high. Slight reduction in yield.
Standard harvest window. Mix of THC and CBN. Full-spectrum effects.
Extended harvest for max CBN conversion. Heavy body high. Good for pain/sleep. Slightly degraded THC.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Micro → Gro → Bloom (always Micro first)
nutrient_brands.name: General Hydroponics Flora Trio

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Part A → Epsom → Part B (dissolve each fully before adding next)
nutrient_brands.name: Jack's 321
nutrient_brands.notes: Jack's 321 is 3.6g Part A, 2.4g Cal Nit, 1.2g Epsom per gallon at full strength. Extremely cost-effective for large grows.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Core → Bloom
nutrient_brands.name: Athena Pro Line
nutrient_brands.notes: Commercial-grade. Simple 2-part with integrated CalMag. Popular in commercial cannabis facilities.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: A → B (separate for veg and bloom)
nutrient_brands.name: Advanced Nutrients pH Perfect (Sensi)
nutrient_brands.notes: Self-adjusting pH technology (claims no pH adjustment needed). Expensive but popular for beginners who struggle with pH.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Shake VERY well before use (thick concentrate settles). One bottle at a time.
nutrient_brands.name: General Hydroponics FloraNova
nutrient_brands.notes: Simplest option — one bottle per phase. Great for beginners. Shake well — concentrate separates.
nutrient_brands.type: 1-part (two bottles — one for veg, one for bloom)

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Dissolve powder in warm water, stir well
nutrient_brands.name: Greenleaf Nutrients Mega Crop
nutrient_brands.notes: One powder does everything — includes CalMag. Incredibly cost-effective at ~$0.02/gal. Community favorite on r/microgrowery.

environment.container: Glass mason jars (quart size). Fill 75% full.

humidity_packs.boveda_58: For drier preference or long-term storage.
humidity_packs.boveda_62: Maintains 62% RH. Set-and-forget. Replace when crispy.
humidity_packs.grove_bags: Heat-sealed bags with built-in humidity regulation. No burping needed.

indicators_of_good_cure: Smooth, flavorful smoke — no harshness or hay smell
Buds are slightly sticky but not wet
Strong terpene aroma when jar is opened
Ash burns to white/light gray (not black)
method: Trim buds to final shape (dry trim or finish wet trim)
Place in mason jars — fill 3/4 full to allow air exchange
Week 1: burp 2-3x daily for 10-15 minutes (release moisture and gases)
Week 2-4: burp 1x daily for 5-10 minutes
Month 2+: burp 1x weekly or seal completely
Cure for minimum 2 weeks, ideal is 4-8 weeks. Some strains improve for 6+ months.

common_mistakes: Drying too fast (>3 days) — harsh smoke, hay smell, terpene loss
Drying too slow (>14 days) — mold risk, diminishing returns
Fan blowing directly on buds — uneven drying, crispy exterior/moist interior
Temperature too high (>70°F) — terpene evaporation, harsh taste

environment.airflow: Gentle circulation — fan NOT pointed at buds. Just keep air moving.

method: Cut branches 12-18 inches long
Optionally remove large fan leaves (wet trim) or leave intact (dry trim)
Hang upside down on line/rack with 6+ inches between branches
Small branches should snap (not bend) when done — 5-7 days minimum
Stems should snap cleanly; if they bend, keep drying

long_term_storage.degradation: THC slowly converts to CBN over time. Light, heat, air, and moisture accelerate degradation.
long_term_storage.method: Sealed mason jars or vacuum-sealed bags with Boveda 58% in dark, cool place
long_term_storage.notes: Properly cured and stored cannabis can remain excellent for 12+ months. Freezing is debated — can damage trichomes if not done carefully.

critical_rules: Every bucket MUST have a siphon elbow
Aim for 20-30% runoff to prevent salt buildup
Clean inline filter weekly
DTW for disease prevention in commercial settings

feed_schedule.flower: 15 min on / 15 min off or continuous

quick_reference.maintenance_level: Medium — daily reservoir checks, weekly emitter/drain inspection.
quick_reference.method_summary: Individual media-filled buckets with shared top-feed drip and common drain line. Recirculating or drain-to-waste.

scale_tiers.description: First-time grower or single-plant hobbyist. Manual management, learning-focused.
scale_tiers.name: Solo / Single Plant
scale_tiers.notes: Hands-on learning experience. Manual checks 1-2x daily. Ideal for beginners.

scale_tiers.description: Hobbyist tent grow. Light automation helpful but not required.
scale_tiers.name: Small Tent (2-8 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Timer-controlled lights, humidity controller. Sensor monitoring recommended.

scale_tiers.description: Dedicated grower with multiple tents/rooms. Automation saves significant time.
scale_tiers.name: Multi-Tent (9-24 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Multiple environments to manage. Automated alerts essential. Consider perpetual harvest.

scale_tiers.description: Licensed commercial operation. Full automation, compliance tracking, batch management.
scale_tiers.name: Commercial Room (25-100 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: SOPs required. Staff training. Regulatory compliance. Insurance requirements.

scale_tiers.description: Large-scale production facility. Enterprise automation, SCADA integration, dedicated staff.
scale_tiers.name: Warehouse Scale (100+ plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Multiple rooms, dedicated HVAC, industrial nutrient dosing, video surveillance.

common_problems.cause: Temperature too low or seeds too old
common_problems.solution: Use heat mat. Test seed viability.

stages.description: Germinate in starter cubes (rockwool or rapid rooters). System doesn't need to run yet — seedlings stay in propagation tray until ready for buckets.

environment_overrides.notes: Propagation bench with humidity dome.
Germinate indoors for control.

stages.name: Germination

nutrients.approach: Plain water in propagation tray.

reservoir.notes: System off. Propagation only.

tasks.description: pH 5.5 water. Rockwool or rapid rooters.
tasks.name: Soak starter cubes

tasks.description: One seed per cube, 1/4 inch deep.
tasks.name: Plant seeds

tasks.description: Look for taproot or sprout.
tasks.name: Check germination

common_problems.cause: Drip emitter not reaching root zone
common_problems.solution: Move emitter closer. Increase frequency. Hand-water supplementally.

common_problems.cause: Media too wet + poor airflow
common_problems.solution: Reduce drip frequency. Improve ventilation.

stages.description: Seedlings in starter cubes under mild light. Transplant to buckets once roots emerge from cube. Run system at low flow to keep media moist.

environment_overrides.notes: Ideal Dutch bucket environment.
System outdoors if frost-free.

stages.name: Seedling

nutrients.approach: 1/4 strength veg formula.

reservoir.notes: Very light feed. 15 min on / 60 min off timer.

tasks.description: Place cube in bucket media when roots show. Bury 1-2 inches.
tasks.name: Transplant to buckets

tasks.description: 15 min on / 60 off. Gentle flow to seedling zone.
tasks.name: Set drip timer

tasks.description: Should be moist, not saturated around seedling.
tasks.name: Monitor media moisture

tasks.description: Reservoir and runoff.
tasks.name: Check pH/EC

common_problems.cause: Clogged emitter or genetics
common_problems.solution: Check each emitter flow rate. Clean or replace.

common_problems.cause: EC too high or insufficient runoff
common_problems.solution: Increase runoff to 20-30%. Flush with plain water.

stages.description: Plants establishing in buckets. Roots growing through media. Increase feed strength and frequency. Each bucket operating independently.

environment_overrides.notes: Excellent light + controlled environment.
Protect reservoir from sun (algae).

stages.name: Early Vegetative

nutrients.approach: Half-strength veg. High nitrogen.

reservoir.notes: Increase feed cycles. 15 min on / 30 min off.

tasks.description: 15 on / 30 off during lights. Off at night.
tasks.name: Increase feed frequency

tasks.description: Runoff EC should be within 0.3 of input.
tasks.name: Check runoff EC/pH

tasks.description: All buckets draining freely.
tasks.name: Inspect drain lines

tasks.description: LST or topping at 4th-5th node.
tasks.name: Begin training

common_problems.cause: Large plants drink heavily
common_problems.solution: Larger reservoir. Float valve auto-fill.

common_problems.cause: Roots growing out of bucket
common_problems.solution: Ensure siphon elbows installed. Check drain fittings.

stages.description: Aggressive growth. Full nutrient strength. Dutch buckets excel — each plant gets abundant feed with individual drain monitoring. Train canopy.

environment_overrides.notes: Ventilate for humidity control.
Cover reservoir from rain/debris.

stages.name: Late Vegetative

nutrients.approach: 3/4 strength veg. Cal-mag if coco media.

reservoir.notes: Full veg strength. Multiple feed cycles.

tasks.description: Large plants drink 1-2 gal/day each.
tasks.name: Monitor reservoir consumption

tasks.description: Individual EC/pH. Identify problem plants.
tasks.name: Check runoff per bucket

tasks.description: Prevents emitter clogs.
tasks.name: Clean inline filter

tasks.description: SCROG or LST. Dutch buckets support large plants.
tasks.name: Train canopy

common_problems.cause: Genetic variation
common_problems.solution: Raise individual buckets or supercrop tall ones.

stages.description: Flip to 12/12. Plants stretch 50-100%. Transition nutrients to bloom. Dutch bucket advantage: individual plant monitoring during transition.

environment_overrides.notes: Blackout covers if needed.
Natural photoperiod trigger.

stages.name: Transition (Stretch)

nutrients.approach: Transition: 50% veg + 50% bloom.

reservoir.notes: 50/50 veg-bloom transition.

tasks.description: Switch to 12/12.
tasks.name: Flip light schedule

tasks.description: Begin bloom formula. Reduce N, increase P-K.
tasks.name: Transition nutrients

tasks.description: LST/supercrop individual buckets as needed.
tasks.name: Manage stretch

common_problems.cause: Emitter delivering more to that bucket
common_problems.issue: Nutrient burn on some buckets only
common_problems.solution: Equalize emitter flow rates. Check for partial clogs.

stages.description: Bud sites forming. Full bloom nutrients. Dutch buckets deliver consistent feed — ideal for uniform bud development across the row.

environment_overrides.notes: Airflow between buckets critical.
Pest pressure increases.

stages.name: Early Flower

nutrients.approach: Full bloom formula. High P-K ratio.

reservoir.notes: Full bloom. High P-K.

tasks.description: Complete transition to flower nutrients.
tasks.name: Switch to full bloom

tasks.description: Remove lower fan leaves blocking bud sites.
tasks.name: Defoliate for airflow

tasks.description: Each bucket 20-30% runoff.
tasks.name: Check drain consistency

common_problems.cause: High EC over weeks
common_problems.solution: Increase runoff to 30%+. Plain water flush weekly.

common_problems.cause: Multi-strain setup
common_problems.solution: Flush/feed individual buckets differently.

stages.description: Peak bud development. Maximum nutrient demand. Dutch buckets handle large flower loads — media provides root support for heavy colas.

environment_overrides.notes: Dehumidifier may be needed.
Rain cover critical.

stages.name: Mid Flower (Bulk Phase)

nutrients.approach: Full bloom + PK booster weeks 4-6.

reservoir.notes: Peak strength. Monitor for lockout.

tasks.description: Peak consumption. 2-3 gal/day per plant.
tasks.name: Monitor reservoir daily

tasks.description: Add PK booster weeks 4-6 of flower.
tasks.name: PK boost

tasks.description: Yo-yo supports or trellis per bucket.
tasks.name: Support heavy colas

tasks.description: Dense buds in humid environments.
tasks.name: Inspect for bud rot

common_problems.cause: Strain variation or excess nitrogen
common_problems.solution: Begin individual bucket flush early. Reduce N.

stages.description: Final ripening. Reduce nutrients. Dutch bucket advantage: harvest individual buckets at different times based on readiness.

environment_overrides.notes: Night temp drops enhance terpenes.
Fall temps help. Watch for frost.

stages.name: Late Flower (Ripening)

nutrients.approach: 75% bloom. Stop PK boosters.

reservoir.notes: Reduce strength. Preparing for flush.

tasks.description: 60x loupe. 70-80% milky, 10-20% amber.
tasks.name: Check trichomes

tasks.description: Drop EC. Simpler formula.
tasks.name: Reduce nutrients

tasks.description: Identify earliest-ready buckets.
tasks.name: Plan staggered harvest

common_problems.cause: Dense media holding salts
common_problems.solution: Extend flush. More cycles. Switch to perlite next round.

stages.description: Switch reservoir to plain pH'd water. Media flushes clean within 3-5 days due to frequent irrigation. Can flush individual buckets by disconnecting from main system.

environment_overrides.notes: Standard flush.
Continue timer-based feed with plain water.

stages.name: Flush

nutrients.approach: Plain pH'd water. No nutrients.

reservoir.notes: Plain water only.

tasks.description: Drain nutrient reservoir. Refill with pH'd water.
tasks.name: Switch to plain water

tasks.description: More cycles to flush media faster.
tasks.name: Increase feed cycles

tasks.description: Target < 0.3 EC = fully flushed.
tasks.name: Check runoff EC

common_problems.cause: Dense root ball in media
common_problems.solution: Soak bucket 30 min. Roots slide out.

stages.description: Chop plants. Dutch bucket advantage: staggered harvest — take ready buckets first. Remove root mass, media is reusable.

environment_overrides.notes: Harvest at convenience.
Harvest before frost.

stages.name: Harvest

nutrients.approach: None.

reservoir.notes: System keeps running for unharvested buckets.

tasks.description: Cut stem above media.
tasks.name: Chop plant at base

tasks.description: Wet or dry trim preference.
tasks.name: Trim

tasks.description: Pull roots from bucket. Rinse media for reuse.
tasks.name: Remove root ball

tasks.description: H2O2 rinse bucket + siphon elbow.
tasks.name: Clean bucket

common_problems.cause: Too warm or low humidity
common_problems.solution: Lower temp, raise humidity, hang whole plants.

stages.description: Standard hang dry. 60°F, 60% RH, darkness, gentle air movement.

environment_overrides.notes: Dry in separate controlled space.
Always dry indoors.

stages.name: Drying

nutrients.approach: None.

reservoir.notes: Clean system during drying for next cycle.

tasks.description: Whole plant or individual branches.
tasks.name: Hang branches

tasks.description: 60-65°F, 55-62% RH, darkness.
tasks.name: Monitor conditions

tasks.description: Small stems snap = ready to jar.
tasks.name: Stem snap test

common_problems.cause: Jarred too wet
common_problems.solution: Remove, dry 12-24h, re-jar.

stages.description: Jar cure. Burp daily for 2 weeks, then weekly. 4-8 weeks ideal.

environment_overrides.notes: Cure indoors.
Cure indoors.

stages.name: Curing

nutrients.approach: None.

reservoir.notes: System ready for next cycle.

tasks.description: Daily 2 weeks, then weekly.
tasks.name: Burp jars

tasks.description: 58-62%. Boveda 62 packs.
tasks.name: Monitor humidity

autoflower.description: Ruderalis-hybrid strains that flower automatically regardless of light schedule. Fixed timeline.

light_schedule.flower: 20/4 or 18/6 (same as veg — no change needed)
light_schedule.transition: Automatic — plant decides when to flower (usually week 3-4)

autoflower.name: Autoflower
autoflower.notes: Fixed timeline — stress cannot be recovered by extending veg. Start in final pot (no transplant shock). LST preferred over HST.

training_suitability.defoliation: light only — auto can't recover from heavy defol
training_suitability.topping: risky — only if plant is vigorous

photoperiod.description: Standard cannabis strains that require 12/12 light to flower. Grower controls timing.

light_schedule.transition: Switch to 12/12 when plant is ~50% of desired final height

photoperiod.name: Photoperiod
photoperiod.notes: Can be vegged as long as desired. Cloneable. More forgiving of stress (can extend veg to recover).

training_suitability.defoliation: good
training_suitability.topping: excellent

issues.cause: Mineral deposits or debris
issues.solution: Soak in vinegar/H2O2. Check inline filter.

issues.cause: Pressure drop or partial clogs
issues.solution: Pressure-compensating emitters. Shorten runs. Clean all.

issues.cause: Clogged impeller or air-lock
issues.solution: Clean pump intake. Ensure submerged. Prime.

issues.cause: Clogged siphon elbow or drain blockage
issues.solution: Clear siphon. Check for root intrusion. Snake drain.

issues.cause: Insufficient slope or blockage
issues.solution: Minimum 1/4 in/ft slope. Clear blockage. Larger pipe.

issues.cause: Siphon installed wrong
issues.solution: Reinstall at correct height (1-2 inch pool).

issues.cause: Plants taking more water than nutrients
issues.solution: Top off with plain water. Reduce base EC.

issues.cause: Inadequate buffering or root rot
issues.solution: Larger reservoir. Fresh nutrient change. Check roots.

issues.cause: Uneven feed or genetics
issues.solution: Check individual emitter flow. Adjust per-plant.

issues.cause: Recirculating system spreading pathogens
issues.solution: Switch to DTW. H2O2. UV sterilizer. Isolate affected.

issues.cause: Light exposure to solution
issues.solution: Light-proof all lines/reservoir. H2O2 clean.

issues.cause: Overwatering or dead roots
issues.solution: Isolate bucket. Beneficial microbes. H2O2 drench.

cons: RO system cost and waste water
water_source_profiles.name: Reverse Osmosis (RO) Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Gold standard for precision growing. Always add CalMag first when using RO.
pros: Blank slate — total control over mineral content

dechlorination.chloramine: Does NOT gas off — must use Campden tablets or activated carbon filter
dechlorination.chlorine: Let sit uncovered 24 hours (gasses off) or use dechlorinator drops
dechlorination.how_to_tell: Check city water report. Most modern cities use chloramine.

water_source_profiles.name: Municipal Tap Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Good enough for most grows. Dechlorinate and pH-adjust. Get a water report from your city.

cons: May contain iron, sulfur, or heavy metals
water_source_profiles.name: Well Water
water_source_profiles.notes: MUST get a water test before using. High iron or sulfur can cause lockout. Great if your well has clean water with moderate minerals.

water_source_profiles.name: Distilled Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Same as RO but purchased instead of filtered. Only practical for 1-2 plant grows.

advanced_techniques.description: Elevating CO2 from ambient (400ppm) to 1000-1500ppm increases photosynthesis rate by 20-40% when light and temperature are also increased.

methods.notes: Most common. Tank refills every 2-4 weeks for a tent.
Produces heat and humidity — better for large rooms with AC.
Produces ~100-200ppm boost. Only useful for small sealed spaces. Not worth it for most.

advanced_techniques.name: CO2 Supplementation

ppfd_co2_correlation.notes: Ambient CO2 sufficient at low light
Slight benefit from supplementation
Moderate benefit — cost-effective threshold
Strong benefit — diminishing returns above this
Maximum practical benefit for most grows
Commercial intensity — requires perfect environment

requirements: Sealed grow space (CO2 escapes quickly from leaky rooms)
High light intensity (>600 PPFD — plants can't use extra CO2 without sufficient light)
Higher temperature tolerance (82-86°F optimal with CO2 vs 75-80°F without)
CO2 controller or timer

advanced_techniques.description: Manipulating environmental and irrigation parameters to push the plant toward vegetative or generative (flowering/fruiting) growth.
advanced_techniques.name: Crop Steering
advanced_techniques.notes: Crop steering is most effective in hydro/coco where irrigation frequency is highly controllable. Soil growers have less precision due to soil buffering.

vegetative_steering.goal: More leaf/stem growth, larger plant framework

best_practices: Spray during lights-off or first/last 30 minutes of light cycle (stomata open, no light burn)
Use fine mist sprayer — coverage is key
pH foliar solution to 6.2-6.5 (leaf surface slightly higher pH than root zone)
EC should be very low (0.2-0.5) — leaves burn easily
Never foliar spray during flower (moisture in buds → mold/bud rot)
Best for micronutrient deficiency correction (CalMag, iron, magnesium)

common_foliar_products.frequency: Weekly preventive, 3-day intervals for active infestations

advanced_techniques.description: Spraying nutrient solution directly onto leaves for rapid uptake. Bypasses root zone entirely.
advanced_techniques.name: Foliar Feeding

advanced_techniques.description: Strengthens cell walls, increases stem thickness, improves heat/drought/pest resistance.
important_notes: ALWAYS add silica to water FIRST before other nutrients (raises pH significantly)
Let silica mix for 5-10 minutes before adding base nutrients
Silica is incompatible with concentrated nutrient solutions — never mix undiluted
Discontinue in late flower (last 2-3 weeks)
advanced_techniques.name: Silica (Potassium Silicate)

advanced_techniques.description: Introduces beneficial bacteria/fungi to the root zone to outcompete pathogens, improve nutrient uptake, and protect against root diseases.
important_notes: Do NOT use hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) with beneficial microbes — it kills them
Choose either sterile (H2O2) OR beneficial (Hydroguard) approach — not both
Beneficials need warmish water (65-75°F) to thrive
Reapply after any reservoir change or system cleaning
advanced_techniques.name: Beneficial Microbes & Root Inoculants

products.name: Hydroguard
products.notes: The standard for DWC/RDWC. Add at every res change.

products.dosing: 1 tsp/gal (water-in) or dust roots at transplant
products.name: Great White
products.notes: Best for soil/coco. Less effective in pure hydro.

products.name: Recharge
products.notes: Feeds existing soil microbes. Great for living soil.

products.name: Mammoth P
products.notes: Most effective in flower when P demand is highest.

advanced_techniques.description: Removing select fan leaves to improve light penetration, airflow, and direct energy to bud sites.
advanced_techniques.name: Defoliation (Strategic Leaf Removal)
rules: Never remove more than 20-30% of leaves at once (except schwazze technique)
Only defoliate healthy, vigorous plants — never stressed ones
Autoflowers: very light defoliation only — they can't recover from heavy removal
Always leave the top 2-3 fan leaves on each branch (they feed the bud below)

timing.method: Remove lower leaves that get no light (lollipop)
Major defoliation day 1 and day 21 of flower (schwazzing)
Selective tuck or remove leaves blocking bud sites

alternatives.notes: Powder. Contains mycorrhizae + trichoderma + bacteria. Apply at transplant.
Phosphorus-liberating bacteria. Use in flower. Compatible with Hydroguard.
Mycorrhizae + trichoderma + bacteria. Organic. Can cloud water — use sparingly in DWC.
Enzyme-based. Breaks down dead root matter. Not alive — compatible with H2O2 if needed.

compatibility_notes: NEVER use H2O2 with beneficial bacteria — H2O2 kills ALL microbes (good and bad)
Choose either H2O2 sterile approach OR beneficial microbe approach — not both
Hydroguard is the standard DWC recommendation (beneficial approach)
If you must use H2O2 (emergency rot treatment), wait 24h before re-adding beneficials
Mammoth P is safe to combine with Hydroguard (different organisms, complementary)

primary_recommendation.frequency: Every reservoir change AND every top-off
primary_recommendation.notes: The gold standard for DWC root protection. Colonizes root zone, outcompetes pathogens.

flush.notes: Frequent plain water changes to clear salts.

late_flower.notes: Maintaining quality. Watch for lockout signs.

mid_flower.notes: Peak demand. Consider every 4 days for large plants.

seedling.notes: Low nutrient demand. Change less frequently.

factors_that_reduce_do: Higher water temperature (above 72°F DO drops rapidly)
improvement_methods: Larger/more air stones (target 1 large stone or multiple small per bucket)
Commercial air pump (minimum 4 LPM per bucket)
Water chiller (68°F water holds ~8.5 ppm DO vs 75°F holds ~7.5 ppm)
Replace air stones when bubbles become large/fewer (mineral buildup)
Shorter air hose runs — air pump close to bucket

emergency_drain_protocol.action: Immediate full drain. H2O2 root dip (3ml/gal 3%). Refill with fresh solution + double Hydroguard.
emergency_drain_protocol.trigger: Root rot detected (brown, slimy roots, foul smell)

emergency_drain_protocol.action: Full drain. Scrub bucket. Refill with fresh calibrated solution.
Drain 50%, refill with plain pH'd water to dilute.
Immediate full drain. Clean bucket with H2O2. Fresh solution + Hydroguard.
Drain. Scrub bucket with H2O2. Ensure light exclusion. Refill. Add Hydroguard.
emergency_drain_protocol.trigger: Algae visible (green slime on roots/bucket walls)

top_off_decision_tree.description: How to decide between plain water top-off vs nutrient top-off based on EC drift.

ec_dropping.action: Top off with full-strength or slightly concentrated nutrient solution.
ec_dropping.meaning: Plants consuming more nutrients than water. Under-fed.
ec_dropping.target: Bring EC back to target for current stage.

ec_rising.action: Top off with plain pH'd water only.
ec_rising.meaning: Plants drinking more water than nutrients. Solution is concentrating.
ec_rising.target: Bring EC back to starting level.

ec_stable.action: Top off with 1/2 strength nutrient solution.
ec_stable.meaning: Plants consuming water and nutrients at equal rates. Ideal balance.

top_off_decision_tree.golden_rule: When in doubt, top off with plain pH'd water. You can always add nutrients; removing them requires a full change.

cooling_methods.notes: The only reliable method. Size: 1/10 HP per 5 gal bucket.
Requires constant rotation. Causes pH/EC swings. Emergency only.
Reflective insulation wrap. Prevents room heat transfer.

cooling_methods.method: Keep bucket off floor in tent
cooling_methods.notes: Heat rises. Floor is often coolest spot — use elevated stand only if floor has radiant heat.
Lower ambient temp during lights-on keeps water cooler.

danger_zone_f.notes: Root rot risk increases exponentially above 75°F

equipment.description: Food-safe bucket with lid and net pot hole. Dark colored to prevent algae. 5-gal minimum, 10-gal preferred for larger plants.
equipment.name: DWC Bucket (5-10 gal)

equipment.description: Sits in bucket lid. Holds growing media and plant.
equipment.name: Net Pot (6 inch)

equipment.description: Must run 24/7. Size depends on bucket count. Min 4W per bucket. EcoPlus and Vivosun are reliable.
equipment.name: Air Pump

equipment.description: Large disc or cylinder air stone. More bubbles = more dissolved oxygen. Replace every 1-2 grows.
equipment.name: Air Stone (large)

equipment.description: Connects air pump to air stone. Standard 1/4 inch.
equipment.name: Air Tubing

equipment.description: Fill net pot around Rapid Rooter. Rinse thoroughly before use to remove dust.
equipment.name: Hydroton / Clay Pebbles

equipment.description: Digital pH pen preferred. Calibrate regularly. Apera or BlueLab are reliable.
equipment.name: pH Pen or Test Kit

equipment.description: Measures nutrient strength. Get one that reads EC and PPM.
equipment.name: EC/TDS Meter

equipment.description: General Hydroponics pH Up and Down. Small amounts adjust pH. pH Down is much stronger than pH Up.
equipment.name: pH Up & pH Down

equipment.description: FloraGro, FloraMicro, FloraBloom. Industry standard for DWC. Add Micro first, then Gro, then Bloom.
equipment.name: Nutrients (GH Flora Trio)

equipment.description: Beneficial bacteria (Bacillus amyloliquefaciens). Prevents root rot. Essential for DWC. 2 ml/gal at every res change and top-off.
equipment.name: Hydroguard

equipment.description: LED recommended. Size depends on grow space. Target 30-40 watts/sq ft for flower.
equipment.name: Grow Light

equipment.description: Mechanical or digital timer for 18/6 → 12/12 light schedules. Must be reliable.
equipment.name: Light Timer

equipment.description: Monitor temp and humidity. Wireless with app is convenient.
equipment.name: Thermometer / Hygrometer

equipment.description: Essential if using RO or soft water. Prevents calcium and magnesium deficiencies.
equipment.name: CalMag Supplement

equipment.description: Best germination method for DWC. Pre-moistened seed plugs.
equipment.name: Rapid Rooters

equipment.description: Submersible thermometer for reservoir. Target 65-72°F.
equipment.name: Water Thermometer

equipment.description: For checking trichome maturity at harvest. Digital microscope also works.
equipment.name: Jeweler's Loupe (60x)

equipment.description: Controls heat, humidity, and smell. Size to your grow space.
equipment.name: Exhaust Fan + Carbon Filter

equipment.description: Oscillating fan for air movement. Strengthens stems and prevents hot spots.
equipment.name: Circulation Fan

equipment.description: Strengthens cell walls and stems. Add before other nutrients. Raises pH.
equipment.name: Silica Supplement (Armor Si)

equipment.description: Battery-operated siphon pump for easy reservoir changes.
equipment.name: Water Transfer Pump

equipment.description: Drip from reservoir to seedling until roots reach water. Saves 1-2 weeks in seedling stage.
equipment.name: Top-Feed Pump Kit

equipment.description: Measures DO levels. Target 6+ ppm. Nice to have but not necessary if air stone is strong.
equipment.name: Dissolved Oxygen Meter

equipment.description: Keeps reservoir at ideal temp. Only needed if grow room is hot (consistently above 80°F).
equipment.name: Water Chiller

equipment.description: Extra phosphorus and potassium during peak flower. Increases bud density.
equipment.name: PK Booster (Liquid Koolbloom)

equipment.description: Netting to spread canopy evenly. Great for maximizing light coverage.
equipment.name: Trellis Net (SCROG)

equipment.description: Retractable hangers to support heavy branches in flower.
equipment.name: Plant Yoyos

equipment.description: For curing. Wide-mouth quart jars. Boveda 62% humidity packs.
equipment.name: Mason Jars + Boveda Packs

flush_timing.coco: 7-14 days reduced nutrients → plain water last 3-5 days
flush_timing.notes: Flushing is debated — some studies show no difference. At minimum, reduce nutrients in final week.
flush_timing.soil: 14-21 days plain water (soil holds nutrients longer)

other_harvest_indicators: Most pistils (hairs) have darkened and curled in
Calyxes are swollen and dense
Fan leaves are yellowing and falling off naturally
Trichome stalks are visible and heads are enlarged
Breeder's recommended flowering time has passed

trichome_ratios.notes: Harvest early for more THC-dominant, less CBN. Energetic, racy high. Slight reduction in yield.
Standard harvest window. Mix of THC and CBN. Full-spectrum effects.
Extended harvest for max CBN conversion. Heavy body high. Good for pain/sleep. Slightly degraded THC.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Micro → Gro → Bloom (always Micro first)
nutrient_brands.name: General Hydroponics Flora Trio

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Part A → Epsom → Part B (dissolve each fully before adding next)
nutrient_brands.name: Jack's 321
nutrient_brands.notes: Jack's 321 is 3.6g Part A, 2.4g Cal Nit, 1.2g Epsom per gallon at full strength. Extremely cost-effective for large grows.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Core → Bloom
nutrient_brands.name: Athena Pro Line
nutrient_brands.notes: Commercial-grade. Simple 2-part with integrated CalMag. Popular in commercial cannabis facilities.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: A → B (separate for veg and bloom)
nutrient_brands.name: Advanced Nutrients pH Perfect (Sensi)
nutrient_brands.notes: Self-adjusting pH technology (claims no pH adjustment needed). Expensive but popular for beginners who struggle with pH.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Shake VERY well before use (thick concentrate settles). One bottle at a time.
nutrient_brands.name: General Hydroponics FloraNova
nutrient_brands.notes: Simplest option — one bottle per phase. Great for beginners. Shake well — concentrate separates.
nutrient_brands.type: 1-part (two bottles — one for veg, one for bloom)

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Dissolve powder in warm water, stir well
nutrient_brands.name: Greenleaf Nutrients Mega Crop
nutrient_brands.notes: One powder does everything — includes CalMag. Incredibly cost-effective at ~$0.02/gal. Community favorite on r/microgrowery.

environment.container: Glass mason jars (quart size). Fill 75% full.

humidity_packs.boveda_58: For drier preference or long-term storage.
humidity_packs.boveda_62: Maintains 62% RH. Set-and-forget. Replace when crispy.
humidity_packs.grove_bags: Heat-sealed bags with built-in humidity regulation. No burping needed.

indicators_of_good_cure: Smooth, flavorful smoke — no harshness or hay smell
Buds are slightly sticky but not wet
Strong terpene aroma when jar is opened
Ash burns to white/light gray (not black)
method: Trim buds to final shape (dry trim or finish wet trim)
Place in mason jars — fill 3/4 full to allow air exchange
Week 1: burp 2-3x daily for 10-15 minutes (release moisture and gases)
Week 2-4: burp 1x daily for 5-10 minutes
Month 2+: burp 1x weekly or seal completely
Cure for minimum 2 weeks, ideal is 4-8 weeks. Some strains improve for 6+ months.

common_mistakes: Drying too fast (>3 days) — harsh smoke, hay smell, terpene loss
Drying too slow (>14 days) — mold risk, diminishing returns
Fan blowing directly on buds — uneven drying, crispy exterior/moist interior
Temperature too high (>70°F) — terpene evaporation, harsh taste

environment.airflow: Gentle circulation — fan NOT pointed at buds. Just keep air moving.

method: Cut branches 12-18 inches long
Optionally remove large fan leaves (wet trim) or leave intact (dry trim)
Hang upside down on line/rack with 6+ inches between branches
Small branches should snap (not bend) when done — 5-7 days minimum
Stems should snap cleanly; if they bend, keep drying

long_term_storage.degradation: THC slowly converts to CBN over time. Light, heat, air, and moisture accelerate degradation.
long_term_storage.method: Sealed mason jars or vacuum-sealed bags with Boveda 58% in dark, cool place
long_term_storage.notes: Properly cured and stored cannabis can remain excellent for 12+ months. Freezing is debated — can damage trichomes if not done carefully.

golden_rules: Air pump runs 24/7 — never turn off
Check pH daily — #1 cause of DWC issues
Hydroguard at every res change and top-off
Water level 1-2 inches below net pot
Weekly full reservoir changes minimum
Start nutrients at 1/4 strength and work up
If in doubt, less is more with nutrients
Clean bucket at every reservoir change
Keep reservoir dark — no light = no algae
When EC rises between checks, plant wants water not food
quick_reference.hydroguard_dose: 2 ml/gal at every reservoir change AND every top-off
quick_reference.reservoir_change_schedule: Weekly in veg, every 5-7 days in flower, every 3 days during flush
quick_reference.top_off_rule: EC rising = top off with plain pH'd water. EC dropping = top off with 1/4 strength nutrient water.

change_schedule.top_off: Daily with plain pH'd water when EC rises. With dilute nutrient when EC drops.
change_schedule.top_off_rule: EC rising = plants drinking more water than nutrients → top off with plain water. EC dropping = plants eating more nutrients than water → top off with dilute nutrient mix.

reservoir_management.description: Reservoir management for active hydroponic systems (DWC, RDWC, NFT, Ebb & Flow, Drip, Aeroponics).

dissolved_oxygen.why_important: Roots need oxygen to function. Low DO → root rot, nutrient lockout, slow growth.

sizing_guidelines.notes: Larger reservoirs = more stable pH/EC, less frequent changes, more forgiving of mistakes.

beneficial.cons: Slightly cloudy water (normal). Doesn't work above 75°F water.
beneficial.pros: Continuous protection. Outcompetes pathogens. Set-and-forget per res change.

sterile_vs_beneficial.recommendation: Beneficial approach (Hydroguard) for most growers. Sterile only if you have persistent issues.

sterile.cons: Must reapply every 2-3 days. Kills beneficial microbes too. Degrades in hours.
sterile.pros: Kills all pathogens. Clean roots. No biofilm.

water_temperature.too_cold_risk: Slowed metabolism, nutrient lockout, stunted growth
water_temperature.too_warm_risk: Root rot, low dissolved oxygen, pathogen growth

scale_tiers.description: First-time grower or single-plant hobbyist. Manual management, learning-focused.
scale_tiers.name: Solo / Single Plant
scale_tiers.notes: Hands-on learning experience. Manual checks 1-2x daily. Ideal for beginners.

scale_tiers.description: Hobbyist tent grow. Light automation helpful but not required.
scale_tiers.name: Small Tent (2-8 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Timer-controlled lights, humidity controller. Sensor monitoring recommended.

scale_tiers.description: Dedicated grower with multiple tents/rooms. Automation saves significant time.
scale_tiers.name: Multi-Tent (9-24 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Multiple environments to manage. Automated alerts essential. Consider perpetual harvest.

scale_tiers.description: Licensed commercial operation. Full automation, compliance tracking, batch management.
scale_tiers.name: Commercial Room (25-100 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: SOPs required. Staff training. Regulatory compliance. Insurance requirements.

scale_tiers.description: Large-scale production facility. Enterprise automation, SCADA integration, dedicated staff.
scale_tiers.name: Warehouse Scale (100+ plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Multiple rooms, dedicated HVAC, industrial nutrient dosing, video surveillance.

stages.autoflower_notes: Same germination process. Autoflower seeds tend to be slightly faster to crack.

common_problems.cause: Too cold, too dry, or bad seed
common_problems.solution: Ensure 75-80°F. Keep medium moist. Try a different seed after 7 days.

common_problems.cause: Too wet, no air circulation
common_problems.solution: Reduce moisture. Ensure Rapid Rooter is moist not soaked.

stages.description: Seed cracks open and taproot emerges. Use Rapid Rooters, paper towel method, or direct-in-cube. Keep warm, moist, and dark.

environment.notes: Keep seeds in darkness. A heat mat at 78°F speeds germination. No direct light until sprout emerges.

environment_overrides.notes: Germinate inside the greenhouse on a heat mat. Greenhouses provide enough shelter for germination. Ensure overnight temps stay above 65°F — use a seedling heat mat if nights are cool.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse germination works well with a heat mat and humidity dome.

environment_overrides.notes: Germinate indoors or in a sheltered area even for outdoor grows. Seeds need consistent warmth that outdoor temps rarely provide. Start indoors, transplant to outdoor DWC once seedling has 2-3 true leaf sets.

extra_tasks.description: Outdoor DWC must start after last frost. Photoperiod plants flower when daylight drops below ~14 hours. In northern hemisphere, start seeds indoors in April-May for outdoor transplant in May-June.
extra_tasks.name: Plan outdoor timing

outdoor.notes: Always germinate indoors regardless of final grow location. Outdoor conditions are too unpredictable for delicate seeds.

health_checks: Is the taproot visible and white?
Is the Rapid Rooter moist but not soaking wet?
stages.name: Germination

nutrients.approach: None — seeds contain all energy needed to germinate.

stages.photoperiod_notes: Standard germination. No difference from autoflower at this stage.

reservoir.notes: No reservoir needed yet. If pre-filling buckets, use plain pH'd water only.

tasks.description: Place seeds in cup of room-temperature water for 12-24 hours until they sink. Transfer to Rapid Rooter or paper towel.
tasks.name: Soak seeds

tasks.description: After 24-72 hours, look for white taproot emerging from seed shell. Transfer to net pot once taproot is 0.5-1 inch.
tasks.name: Check for taproot

tasks.description: Fill bucket with plain pH'd water (5.8). Set water level 1 inch below net pot bottom. Turn on air stone.
tasks.name: Prepare DWC bucket

transition_signals: Seed shell has cracked and cotyledons are emerging

stages.autoflower_notes: Shorter seedling phase — autos move fast. Start in final container (no transplant). Keep light at 20/4.

common_problems.cause: Light too far away or too dim
common_problems.solution: Lower light to 20-24 inches. Increase intensity gradually.

common_problems.cause: Normal — cotyledons deplete as true leaves take over. Only worry if true leaves yellow.
common_problems.solution: No action needed unless true leaves affected.

common_problems.cause: Stem sitting in water, too wet at base
common_problems.solution: Lower water level so only roots touch water, not the stem. Maintain air gap.

common_problems.cause: Normal for first few days. Net pot too high or water too low.
common_problems.solution: Top-feed with turkey baster 2-3x daily until roots reach. Raise water level temporarily.

common_problems.cause: EC too high for seedling
common_problems.solution: Dilute reservoir. Use 1/4 strength max. Seedlings need almost nothing.

stages.description: First true leaves develop. Roots reach down into reservoir. Keep light gentle, humidity high, nutrients very light.

environment.notes: Keep light at 24-30 inches above canopy. Seedlings are fragile — too much light causes bleaching.

environment_overrides.light_hours: Natural daylight through glazing (slightly reduced PPFD vs full sun)
environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse provides good seedling conditions. The glazing naturally reduces light intensity ~10-30%. Open vents during hot days to prevent overheating. Use shade cloth if temps exceed 85°F.

extra_problems.cause: Closed greenhouse in sun traps heat
extra_problems.solution: Open vents, doors, or side panels. Use shade cloth (30-50%) on roof.

extra_tasks.description: Greenhouses heat up fast in sun. Open vents/doors when above 80°F. Seedlings prefer 72-80°F.
extra_tasks.name: Monitor greenhouse temp

extra_tasks.description: Greenhouse warmth heats water. Insulate bucket. Target 65-75°F.
extra_tasks.name: Check reservoir temp

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouses are excellent for DWC seedlings — protected from weather and pests while getting natural light.

environment_overrides.light_hours: Natural daylight (14-18 hours depending on season)
environment_overrides.light_ppfd: Variable — full sun is ~2000 PPFD but seedlings need shade
environment_overrides.notes: Keep seedling in partial shade outdoors. Direct summer sun is too intense for seedlings. Use a shade cloth (50%) or place bucket under a tree canopy for first 7-10 days. Gradually increase sun exposure.

extra_problems.cause: Too much direct sun too quickly
extra_problems.solution: Move to shade immediately. Gradually increase sun exposure over a week.

extra_problems.cause: Uncovered bucket in rain
extra_problems.solution: Always cover reservoir opening around the stem. Use a cut pool noodle or neoprene collar plus a rain shield.

extra_problems.cause: Outdoor environment exposes plant to insects immediately
extra_problems.solution: Use neem oil spray preventively. Check daily. Consider BT (Bacillus thuringiensis) for caterpillars.

extra_tasks.description: Use 50% shade cloth or natural shade. Gradually expose to more sun over 7-10 days (hardening off).
extra_tasks.name: Shade seedling from direct sun

extra_tasks.description: Morning and afternoon check. Outdoor sun heats water rapidly. If above 75°F, add frozen water bottle or move to shade.
extra_tasks.name: Check reservoir temp (twice daily)

extra_tasks.description: Check for aphids, gnats, caterpillars. Outdoor seedlings are vulnerable. Use neem oil preventively.
extra_tasks.name: Pest inspection

extra_tasks.description: Plan for rain (cover reservoir to prevent dilution), wind (stake or protect seedling), and temperature swings.
extra_tasks.name: Check weather forecast

outdoor.notes: Outdoor seedlings need hardening off (gradual sun exposure). Most growers start seedlings indoors for 1-2 weeks before moving outside.

reservoir_overrides.notes: Reservoir heats up fast in direct sun. Wrap bucket in reflective insulation (Reflectix). Place bucket in shaded area. Paint bucket white if dark-colored. Water temp is the #1 outdoor DWC challenge.

health_checks: Are the cotyledons (round leaves) still green and healthy?
Are the first true leaves (serrated) developing?
Is the stem strong and upright (not stretchy/leggy)?
Are roots visible growing into the reservoir?
stages.name: Seedling

nutrients.approach: Very light nutrients — 1/4 strength. Seedlings are extremely sensitive to overfeeding. When in doubt, use less.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Beneficial bacteria to prevent root rot. Essential in DWC.

stages.photoperiod_notes: Standard seedling phase. Can start in small container and transplant later.

reservoir.notes: Water level should be touching bottom of net pot to keep Rapid Rooter moist. Top-feed if roots haven't reached water.

tasks.description: Test reservoir pH. Target 5.5-6.0. Seedlings are very pH-sensitive. Adjust with pH Up/Down as needed.
tasks.name: Check pH

tasks.description: Ensure water touches bottom of net pot. Top-feed with a turkey baster if roots haven't reached water yet.
tasks.name: Check water level

tasks.description: Peek through empty net pot holes. Look for white roots growing down from Rapid Rooter into water.
tasks.name: Check root progress

tasks.description: Verify air stone is bubbling vigorously. No bubbles = roots will drown.
tasks.name: Check air stone

tasks.description: Keep light 24-30 inches above seedling. Watch for stretching (too far) or bleaching (too close).
tasks.name: Monitor light distance

stages.autoflower_notes: Very short window — only 1-2 weeks. Begin LST immediately when 4th node appears. Do NOT top unless plant is extremely vigorous. Light at 20/4.

common_problems.cause: Small reservoir volume, rapid root growth changing chemistry
common_problems.solution: Top off more frequently. Larger reservoir = more stable pH. Don't over-correct — small adjustments only.

common_problems.cause: Root rot (Pythium). Water too warm, not enough oxygen, no beneficial bacteria.
common_problems.solution: Add/increase Hydroguard (2ml/gal). Reduce water temp below 72°F. Ensure air stone is vigorous. Trim dead roots.

common_problems.cause: EC too low, reservoir depleted, or pH out of range locking out N
common_problems.solution: Check pH first. If pH is fine, increase nutrient strength slightly. Change reservoir with fresh nutes.

common_problems.cause: RO/soft water lacks calcium and magnesium. DWC has no media to buffer.
common_problems.solution: Add CalMag supplement (1-2 ml/gal). Always add CalMag first, before base nutrients.

stages.description: Rapid leaf and root growth. Plant develops structure. Begin LST training. Ramp up nutrients. Roots should be a thick white mass.

environment.notes: Plants can handle stronger light now. Lower light to 18-24 inches. Watch for light stress (leaves tacoing upward).

environment_overrides.notes: Open vents and doors during hot days. Greenhouses can exceed 100°F if not ventilated. Use HAF (horizontal air flow) fans. 30% shade cloth on the roof prevents overheating while still allowing strong growth.

extra_problems.cause: Insufficient ventilation in sunny weather
extra_problems.solution: Add automatic vent openers. Use exhaust fans. Apply 30-50% shade cloth in peak summer.

extra_tasks.description: Open side vents, roof vents, or endwall doors when temp exceeds 80°F. Autovent openers are worth the investment.
extra_tasks.name: Manage greenhouse ventilation

extra_tasks.description: Greenhouses attract whiteflies, fungus gnats, and spider mites. Use sticky traps for monitoring. Introduce beneficial insects (ladybugs, lacewings).
extra_tasks.name: Check for greenhouse pests

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouses provide excellent early veg conditions — protected from weather while getting strong natural light.

reservoir_overrides.notes: Greenhouse warmth heats reservoirs. Insulate bucket. Consider placing on a concrete floor (thermal mass) to keep water cooler.

environment_overrides.notes: Full sun is ideal now — acclimated plants thrive in direct sunlight. Reservoir temperature management becomes critical. Insulate buckets with Reflectix wrap. Paint buckets white. Consider burying bucket partially in ground for thermal mass.

extra_problems.cause: Direct sun on bucket, high ambient temps
extra_problems.solution: Insulate bucket, partially bury in ground, use frozen water bottles, consider a small aquarium chiller for persistent heat.

extra_problems.cause: Moths lay eggs on leaves; caterpillars eat foliage and bore into stems
extra_problems.solution: Apply BT (Bacillus thuringiensis) spray weekly as prevention. Hand-pick caterpillars. Check inside stems if you see frass.

extra_problems.cause: Strong gusts snap branches or topple buckets
extra_problems.solution: Stake plant to bamboo supports. Place bucket in sheltered location or against a wall/fence. Use a tomato cage.

extra_tasks.description: Wrap bucket in Reflectix or white reflective material. Keep bucket in shade while plant gets sun. Bury lower half in ground for thermal stability.
extra_tasks.name: Insulate & shade reservoir

extra_tasks.description: Check for aphids, spider mites, caterpillars, slugs, and whiteflies. Inspect both sides of leaves. Outdoor grows face constant pest pressure.
extra_tasks.name: Pest scouting

extra_tasks.description: Monitor forecast for storms, heatwaves, and cold snaps. Have a plan to move or protect the bucket if severe weather hits.
extra_tasks.name: Weather check

extra_tasks.description: Cover the reservoir gap around the stem with a collar or cut pool noodle. Rain dilutes nutrients and raises water level.
extra_tasks.name: Rain protection

outdoor.notes: Outdoor early veg is the prime growing season — long days and strong sun drive explosive growth. Focus on reservoir temp management and pest prevention.

reservoir_overrides.notes: Outdoor reservoirs need more frequent changes — heat and sunlight accelerate bacterial growth. Increase Hydroguard to 2.5 ml/gal in summer. Top off more often — evaporation is faster outdoors.

health_checks: Are leaves a healthy deep green? (not lime/yellow = hungry, not dark green/clawing = overfed)
Is growth noticeably faster than the seedling stage?
Any signs of nutrient deficiency? (yellowing lower leaves = N deficiency, brown spots = CalMag)
Any pests visible? Check undersides of leaves.
stages.name: Early Vegetative

nutrients.approach: Half-strength nutrients. More nitrogen (Gro) for leaf/stem development. Always add Micro first when using GH Flora Trio.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root rot prevention. Add at every reservoir change AND every top-off.

supplements.name: Silica (Armor Si)
supplements.purpose: Strengthens stems and cell walls. Add BEFORE other nutrients. Raises pH.

stages.photoperiod_notes: Standard early veg. Good time to start training (topping, LST). Light at 18/6 or 20/4.

reservoir.notes: Start weekly reservoir changes. Plants drink fast now — top off every 1-2 days with 1/4 strength nutrient water.

tasks.description: Test reservoir pH daily. Target 5.5-6.2. pH swings are common in early veg as roots establish. Let it range naturally within bounds.
tasks.name: Check pH

tasks.description: Measure EC/PPM. If EC rises between checks, plant is drinking more water than nutrients — top off with plain pH'd water. If EC drops, plant is hungry — top off with nutrient water.
tasks.name: Check EC

tasks.description: Add pH'd water (with 1/4 strength nutes if EC is dropping) to maintain water level 1-2 inches below net pot. Add Hydroguard to new water.
tasks.name: Top off reservoir

tasks.description: Drain and refill with fresh nutrient solution. Add Hydroguard. This prevents salt buildup and nutrient imbalances.
tasks.name: Full reservoir change

tasks.description: Verify 65-72°F. If above 72°F, consider insulating bucket, adding frozen water bottles, or placing in cooler area. Warm water holds less dissolved oxygen.
tasks.name: Check water temp

tasks.description: Peek through empty net pot hole. Roots should be white and fluffy. Brown/slimy/smelly = root rot.
tasks.name: Root inspection

tasks.description: Once plant has 4-5 nodes, begin gently bending main stem to side and securing with plant ties. This exposes lower branches to light.
tasks.name: Begin LST training

training.description: Gently bend main stem away from center and secure. This breaks apical dominance and creates multiple tops.
Cut the main stem above the 3rd-5th node to create two main tops. Plant will recover in 2-3 days.

transition_signals: Root mass is thick and white

stages.autoflower_notes: May not exist for autos — many transition directly from early veg to flower around day 21-28. Watch for pistils. Cannot extend veg.

common_problems.cause: EC too high, too much Gro in the mix
common_problems.issue: Nitrogen toxicity (dark green leaves, clawing tips)
common_problems.solution: Reduce nutrient strength by 10-15%. Reduce Gro relative to other bottles.

common_problems.cause: Normal in late veg — big plant drinks fast
common_problems.solution: Top off daily. Use a larger reservoir (5-gal bucket minimum, 10-gal preferred for big plants).

common_problems.cause: Light reaching reservoir water through net pot or lid gaps
common_problems.solution: Block all light to reservoir. Use dark bucket, cover net pot gaps with hydroton or neoprene collar.

common_problems.cause: Light too close, temps too high, low humidity
common_problems.solution: Raise light. Increase ventilation. Add humidity if below 40%.

stages.description: Plant fills out canopy. Continue training. Increase nutrients to near full strength. Prepare for flip to flower by establishing structure.

environment.notes: Maximum veg growth rate. Plants drink heavily — reservoir depletion is fast. May need daily top-offs.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse growers can use light deprivation (light dep) tarps to force flowering by covering the greenhouse to create 12/12. Without light dep, plants follow natural photoperiod like outdoor. Light dep gives you indoor-like control with natural light.

extra_problems.cause: Missing even one day of light dep can revert plant to veg or cause hermaphroditism
