extra_problems.solution: Automate with motorized tarps or timers. Be consistent — every single day.

extra_tasks.description: If you want to control flowering time, install blackout tarps. Cover greenhouse to create 12 hours of uninterrupted darkness. Must be done consistently every day.
extra_tasks.name: Consider light deprivation

extra_tasks.description: Peak summer greenhouses can hit 110°F+. Use shade cloth, exhaust fans, evaporative cooling if available.
extra_tasks.name: Manage greenhouse heat

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse late veg is similar to outdoor but with better protection. Light dep is the key advantage for controlling flowering.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor photoperiod plants veg until natural daylight drops below ~14 hours (mid-late summer). You cannot control veg length — the sun decides when flowering starts. Autoflowers skip this and flower on their own timeline regardless of light. Plants can get VERY large outdoors — plan for 4-8+ foot tall plants.

extra_problems.cause: Long outdoor veg period with unlimited root space in DWC
extra_problems.solution: Top aggressively. Use aggressive LST or a SCROG frame. Consider starting seeds later (June) for shorter veg.

extra_problems.cause: Outdoor plants attract herbivores
extra_problems.solution: Use fencing or chicken wire around plants. Consider companion planting with strong-smelling herbs.

extra_tasks.description: Large outdoor plants in summer heat can drink 2-3+ gal/day. Check morning and evening. EC rising = add plain water.
extra_tasks.name: Top off reservoir (twice daily in heat)

extra_tasks.description: Outdoor DWC plants grow tall. Install tomato cage, bamboo stakes, or trellis. Plan for 4-8 foot plants with heavy branches.
extra_tasks.name: Structural support

extra_tasks.description: Apply neem oil or insecticidal soap every 7-10 days during veg. Stop all sprays 2+ weeks before flower. This is your last chance for preventive spraying.
extra_tasks.name: IPM spray (preventive)

outdoor.notes: Late veg outdoors is the longest phase — the plant grows until nature triggers flowering. Focus on structure, support, and pest prevention.

reservoir_overrides.notes: Large outdoor plants may drink 2-3+ gallons/day in peak summer. Use the largest reservoir possible (10+ gal). Top off at least daily, sometimes twice in heat waves.

health_checks: Is the canopy even with multiple tops at the same height?
Are leaves a healthy deep green? (not too dark = N toxicity)
Is the plant drinking at a consistent rate?
Any pest signs? (thrips, spider mites, fungus gnats)
Is the plant at 1/3-1/2 of desired final height?
stages.name: Late Vegetative

nutrients.approach: 65-75% strength. High nitrogen for vigorous vegetative growth. Plant should be deep green but not dark/clawing (nitrogen toxicity).

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Always. Every change, every top-off.

supplements.name: Silica (Armor Si)
supplements.purpose: Stem strength. Add first before other nutes.

stages.photoperiod_notes: Grower-controlled duration. Veg until plant is ~50%% of desired final height. Can extend indefinitely. SCROG/mainline/heavy training all viable.

reservoir.notes: Plants drink 0.5-1+ gallon/day now. Top off daily. Change reservoir weekly without exception.

tasks.description: Daily check. Reservoir chemistry changes fast with big root mass. EC rising = needs more water. EC dropping = needs more nutes.
tasks.name: Check pH & EC

tasks.description: Daily — plants drink heavily. Use 1/4 strength nutrient water for top-offs. Add Hydroguard.
tasks.name: Top off reservoir

tasks.description: Weekly. Drain, clean bucket, refill with fresh mix. Add Hydroguard 2ml/gal.
tasks.name: Full reservoir change

tasks.description: Keep canopy even by bending tall branches and tucking leaves. The goal is a flat, even canopy with many tops at the same height.
tasks.name: Continue LST / tucking

tasks.description: Remove large fan leaves blocking light to lower bud sites. Don't remove more than 20% at once. Healthy plants recover in 1-2 days.
tasks.name: Defoliation (optional)

tasks.description: Plant will roughly double in height after flip. Flip when plant is 1/3 to 1/2 of desired final height. Consider flower stretch.
tasks.name: Assess flip readiness

tasks.description: Monitor daily. Critical in late veg as lights + big plants generate heat.
tasks.name: Check water temp

training.description: Continue bending and tying branches to maintain flat canopy.
All tops should be at equal height by now. Last chance to even out canopy.
Gently crush and bend stems to create knuckles. Strengthens branches for heavy buds.
Some growers strip all fan leaves before flip. Controversial but can increase light to bud sites.

transition_signals: Plant is 1/3-1/2 of desired final height
Ready to flip light schedule to 12/12

stages.autoflower_notes: Auto-triggered — no light change needed. Plant will stretch 50-100%% in height. Continue LST during stretch. Keep at 20/4.

common_problems.cause: Genetics, light too far, or too much N
common_problems.solution: Supercrop tall branches. Raise PPFD. Reduce nitrogen slightly.

common_problems.cause: Light leaks, stress, genetics
common_problems.solution: Remove male parts immediately. Fix light leaks. Monitor closely.

common_problems.cause: Light leaks preventing flower trigger
common_problems.issue: No signs of flowering after 2 weeks
common_problems.solution: Verify absolute darkness during 12-hour off period. Check for light leaks.

stages.description: First 1-3 weeks after flipping to 12/12. Plant stretches dramatically (can double in height). Begin transitioning nutrients from Gro to Bloom.

environment.notes: Switch to 12/12 light schedule. Total darkness in the 12 off-hours is CRITICAL — any light leaks can prevent flowering or cause hermaphroditism.

environment_overrides.notes: If using light dep, you control the flip just like indoor. Cover greenhouse with blackout tarp to create 12/12. If following natural light, transition happens same as outdoor but with better weather protection.

extra_problems.cause: Covering greenhouse traps heat
extra_problems.issue: Heat buildup under light dep tarp
extra_problems.solution: Cover in evening when temps drop. Ensure some passive ventilation under the tarp.

extra_tasks.description: Cover greenhouse at the same time every evening. 12 hours of TOTAL darkness required. Even small light leaks through tarp edges matter.
extra_tasks.name: Light dep schedule (if using)

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse with light dep gives the best of both worlds — natural light + controlled flowering.

environment_overrides.light_hours: Decreasing natural daylight (~12-14 hours, triggering flower)
environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor transition is triggered by shortening days in late summer (Aug-Sept in northern hemisphere). You don't 'flip' — nature does. The stretch is the same but watch for light pollution from street lights, porch lights, or neighbors that could interrupt the dark period and prevent proper flowering.

extra_problems.cause: Nearby artificial lights interrupting dark period
extra_problems.solution: Move plant to dark location, block light source, or cover with light-dep tarp at night.

extra_problems.cause: Unexpected frost or near-freezing temps during transition
extra_problems.solution: Cover plant with frost cloth at night. Move bucket indoors overnight if possible. DWC buckets are portable.

extra_tasks.description: Ensure no artificial light reaches the plant during dark hours. Street lights, porch lights, even car headlights can prevent flowering. Move plant if needed.
extra_tasks.name: Eliminate light pollution

extra_tasks.description: Last chance for preventive sprays. Apply BT for caterpillars — they love to bore into forming buds. After buds form, no more spraying.
extra_tasks.name: Final pest prevention

extra_tasks.description: Stretch + future bud weight means branches need strong support now. Add bamboo stakes, tomato cages, or trellis netting.
extra_tasks.name: Reinforce supports

outdoor.notes: Outdoor transition happens naturally. The biggest risks are light pollution and early fall weather. DWC's portability is an advantage — you can move buckets.

health_checks: Did the plant start stretching after flip?
Any light leaks in the grow space during dark period?
Are pre-flowers (white pistils) appearing at nodes?
Any signs of male pollen sacs or hermaphroditism? (bananas, nanners)
Is stretch manageable or does canopy need supercropping?
stages.name: Transition / Stretch

nutrients.approach: Transition mix — reduce Gro, increase Bloom gradually over 2 weeks. Plant still stretches and needs some nitrogen.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root protection.

stages.photoperiod_notes: Triggered by switching to 12/12. Stretch phase — plant doubles in height. Last chance for heavy defoliation.

reservoir.notes: Transition nutrients gradually. Plant still needs nitrogen during stretch but phosphorus demand is increasing.

tasks.description: Switch light timer to 12 hours on, 12 hours off. Ensure ZERO light leaks during dark period. Even phone screens or indicator LEDs can cause issues.
tasks.name: Flip to 12/12

tasks.description: Enter grow space during dark period after 30 min. You should see nothing — seal any light sources.
tasks.name: Check for light leaks

tasks.description: Daily check. Nutrient demand shifts during transition — watch for swings.
tasks.name: Check pH & EC

tasks.description: Supercrop or tie down branches that are getting too tall. Keep canopy as even as possible for uniform bud development.
tasks.name: Manage stretch

tasks.description: Look for tiny white hairs (pistils) at branch nodes. These confirm female sex and onset of flowering.
tasks.name: Watch for pre-flowers

tasks.description: Weekly with transition nutrient mix.
tasks.name: Full reservoir change

tasks.description: Daily. Plants drink even more during stretch.
tasks.name: Top off reservoir

training.description: Pinch and bend tall branches to control height and create knuckles.
Remove lower 1/3 of growth that won't receive light. Directs energy to top buds.
Remove large fan leaves blocking bud sites. Known as 'schwazze day 21'.

transition_signals: White pistils clearly visible at most nodes
Bud sites forming at branch tips

stages.autoflower_notes: Bud sites forming. Reduce nutrient strength to 75%% of photoperiod levels. Light stays at 20/4 (more DLI = more yield).

common_problems.cause: Humidity too high, poor air circulation, dense buds
common_problems.solution: Cut affected area plus 1 inch. Lower humidity below 50%. Increase fans. Space buds out.

common_problems.cause: pH too high (above 6.2 locks out P), or insufficient P in reservoir
common_problems.solution: Lower pH to 5.8-6.0. Increase Bloom nutrients. Consider PK booster.

common_problems.cause: EC too low, K locked out by pH
common_problems.solution: Check pH. Increase bloom nutrients slightly.

common_problems.cause: Heat stress, light too intense/close
common_problems.solution: Lower light intensity or raise light. Improve ventilation.

stages.description: Weeks 3-5 of flower. Bud sites develop and begin filling in. White pistils everywhere. Switch to full bloom nutrients. Critical period for bud formation.

environment.notes: Push light intensity — buds need energy to fatten. But don't cook them. Keep leaf surface temp under 85°F.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse provides rain protection — huge advantage during flower. Control humidity by ventilating during the day and closing up at night (but ensure darkness for 12/12). Use HAF fans to keep air moving around buds.

extra_problems.cause: Temperature differential between inside and outside causes condensation
extra_problems.issue: Condensation on greenhouse glazing dripping on buds
extra_problems.solution: Increase air circulation. Use anti-drip greenhouse film. Open vents briefly in morning to equalize temps.

extra_tasks.description: Open vents during day for airflow. Close at dusk for light dep if needed. Use dehumidifier if greenhouse RH consistently above 60% during flower.
extra_tasks.name: Humidity management

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse flower is excellent — rain protection plus natural light. Humidity management is the main challenge.

environment_overrides.notes: You cannot control outdoor humidity — but you can manage airflow. If in a humid climate, space plants apart, remove inner leaves for airflow, and inspect for bud rot after every rain event. Dew-covered buds in the morning are normal but prolonged wetness causes mold.

extra_problems.cause: Water trapped inside dense buds, humid conditions
extra_problems.solution: Cut affected area + 1 inch. Shake water off buds after rain. Improve airflow by selective defoliation. Consider harvesting early if persistent rain is forecast.

extra_problems.cause: Moths lay eggs on buds; larvae eat from inside
extra_problems.solution: Apply BT (Bacillus thuringiensis) — safe and organic. Check buds for dark frass droppings. Remove affected buds and caterpillars by hand.

extra_problems.cause: Fall temperatures dropping
extra_problems.solution: Cover with frost cloth at night. Move DWC bucket to sheltered area. A light frost won't kill but hard frost destroys buds.

extra_tasks.description: After rain, gently shake water off buds. Inspect for gray mold (botrytis) within 24-48 hours. Early detection saves the crop.
extra_tasks.name: Post-rain bud inspection

extra_tasks.description: Budworms (caterpillars) are the #1 outdoor flower pest. They bore into buds and cause rot from inside. Inspect buds for frass (dark droppings). Apply BT spray if found — safe up to harvest.
extra_tasks.name: Caterpillar patrol (BT spray)

extra_tasks.description: Gently shake plant each morning to remove dew from buds. Prolonged moisture = bud rot.
extra_tasks.name: Shake off morning dew

outdoor.notes: Outdoor flowering is a race against fall weather and pests. Stay vigilant with bud rot and caterpillar patrols.

health_checks: Are buds visibly growing week over week?
Any signs of bud rot? (brown/mushy spots inside buds)
Any nutrient deficiency? (P deficiency = purple stems, K deficiency = brown leaf edges)
Are roots still white and healthy?
stages.name: Early Flower

nutrients.approach: Full bloom mix. High P and K for bud development. Nitrogen is still needed but reduced. Bud structure and trichome density are established now.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root protection. Never skip.

supplements.name: Liquid Koolbloom (PK booster)
supplements.purpose: Extra phosphorus and potassium for bud development. Optional but effective.

stages.photoperiod_notes: Pistils lengthening, calyxes swelling. Full-strength bloom nutrients. 12/12 mandatory — any light leak can cause hermaphroditism.

reservoir.notes: Full bloom nutrients. Phosphorus and potassium are now the priority. Change reservoir weekly without fail.

tasks.description: Daily. Flowering plants are more sensitive to pH issues. Keep 5.5-6.2. Rotate pH within range to maximize nutrient uptake.
tasks.name: Check pH & EC

tasks.description: Daily with 1/4 strength bloom water. Buds are thirsty.
tasks.name: Top off reservoir

tasks.description: Weekly. Critical during flower — salt buildup causes lockouts and reduces bud quality.
tasks.name: Full reservoir change

tasks.description: Take photos to track bud growth week over week. Look for signs of growth stalling or deficiencies.
tasks.name: Monitor bud development

tasks.description: Keep below 55% to prevent bud rot. Use dehumidifier if needed. Increase air circulation.
tasks.name: Lower humidity

tasks.description: Use magnifying glass or jeweler's loupe. Trichomes should be forming on buds and sugar leaves.
tasks.name: Check trichome development

training.description: Remove large fan leaves blocking bud sites. Do NOT defoliate after week 6 of flower.
training.when: Once at start of this phase

transition_signals: Buds filling in and starting to stack
Trichomes covering buds and sugar leaves
Pistils starting to change from white to orange

stages.autoflower_notes: Peak bud production. Maintain 75%% nutrient strength. Buds will be smaller but more numerous. Don't defoliate heavily — autos can't recover.

common_problems.cause: EC too high
common_problems.solution: Reduce EC by 10-15%. Some tip burn is acceptable — means you're near the max the plant can handle.

common_problems.cause: Heavy buds without support
common_problems.solution: Use trellis net (SCROG), bamboo stakes, or plant yoyos to support branches.

common_problems.cause: Light too intense or too close
common_problems.solution: Raise light. Reduce intensity by 10-15%.

common_problems.cause: Heat/light stress at bud tips
common_problems.solution: Lower temps. Raise light. Improve air circulation above canopy.

stages.description: Weeks 5-7. Buds ripen and trichome production peaks. Peak nutrient demand. Dense resin development and terpene expression define quality here.

environment.notes: Maximum light intensity but watch for bleaching on closest buds. A 5-10°F night temp drop can enhance terpene and color production.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse mid-flower is ideal — rain protection with natural light. Fall temp drops are moderated by greenhouse thermal mass. May need supplemental heating if nighttime temps drop below 55°F consistently.

extra_tasks.description: If overnight greenhouse temps drop below 55°F, use a small space heater or heat mat under the bucket to maintain warmth.
extra_tasks.name: Consider supplemental heating

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse mid-flower is straightforward. The structure does most of the work protecting buds from weather.

environment_overrides.notes: Mid-flower outdoors is typically September-October in northern hemisphere. Days are getting shorter and cooler. Night temps dropping is actually beneficial for terpene and color development. However, fall rain and humidity are the enemy. Be prepared for emergency harvest if prolonged rain is forecast.

extra_problems.cause: Fall weather patterns
extra_problems.issue: Multi-day rain event during peak flower
extra_problems.solution: Cover with tarp. If no cover available and buds are 80%+ ready, harvest early. A slightly early harvest is better than a moldy crop.

extra_problems.cause: Fall approaching
extra_problems.solution: Move DWC bucket to sheltered spot at night. Cold slows growth but enhances colors/terpenes. Below 40°F risks damage.

extra_tasks.description: Check 10-day forecast daily. If a multi-day rain event is coming and buds are close to ready, consider early harvest rather than risking total bud rot loss.
extra_tasks.name: Weather monitoring (critical)

extra_tasks.description: Rig a simple tarp or clear plastic rain cover above the plant (not touching buds). Keeps rain off while allowing airflow. This single step saves many outdoor grows.
extra_tasks.name: Rain cover / tarp

extra_tasks.description: Caterpillars are still active. Check daily as bud rot from caterpillar damage intensifies in dense mid-flower buds.
extra_tasks.name: Continued caterpillar patrol

outdoor.notes: Outdoor mid-flower is the most stressful period — balancing peak bud development against fall weather. Have an emergency harvest plan ready.

health_checks: Are buds noticeably heavier each week?
Roots still white? (Brown roots in late flower is more common — Hydroguard is critical now)
stages.name: Mid Flower (Bulk)

nutrients.approach: Peak bloom. Maximum P and K. Some growers push EC higher here — watch for tip burn as upper limit signal.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root protection.

supplements.name: Liquid Koolbloom (PK booster)
supplements.purpose: Peak P/K for maximum bud density.

stages.photoperiod_notes: Peak nutrient demand. Full-strength bloom. Trichomes developing. Defoliation at day 21 of flower (schwazze) optional for canopy penetration.

reservoir.notes: Peak nutrient consumption. Change reservoir every 5-7 days. Plants may drink 1-2+ gallons per day.

tasks.description: Daily. Peak nutrient demand means chemistry changes fast.
tasks.name: Check pH & EC

tasks.description: Daily with 1/4 strength bloom. In hot weather, may need twice daily.
tasks.name: Top off reservoir

tasks.description: Every 5-7 days. Don't skip — salt buildup kills bud quality.
tasks.name: Full reservoir change

tasks.description: Use loupe. Trichomes should be mostly clear to milky. Not ready yet.
tasks.name: Check trichomes

tasks.description: Install plant yoyos, trellis net, or bamboo stakes. Heavy buds can snap branches.
tasks.name: Support heavy branches

tasks.description: Inspect densest buds for brown/gray spots. Check where buds meet stem.
tasks.name: Bud rot patrol

transition_signals: Trichomes transitioning from clear to milky

stages.autoflower_notes: Ripening phase. Reduce nutrients. Watch trichomes with loupe — autos ripen slightly faster. Begin checking for harvest window.

common_problems.cause: Harvesting too early based on pistil color alone
common_problems.solution: Wait. Pistil color is unreliable. Only trichome color determines harvest readiness.

common_problems.cause: Waited too long
common_problems.solution: Harvest immediately. THC degrades to CBN (sleepy effect) with excessive amber.

common_problems.cause: Humidity spike, dense buds, poor circulation
common_problems.solution: Cut affected buds immediately. Harvest early if widespread. Better to lose a few days than the whole crop.

common_problems.cause: Overfeeding in late flower, salt lockout, or root issues
common_problems.solution: Check pH. Consider flushing. If close to harvest window, harvest.

stages.description: Weeks 7-9+. Buds ripen and mature. Trichomes transition from milky to amber. Natural leaf fade occurs. Begin reducing nutrients.

environment.notes: Cooler temps (especially at night) can enhance colors and terpene production. A 10-15°F day/night swing is beneficial.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse late flower benefits from frost protection. The structure buys you 2-4 extra weeks compared to fully outdoor. Use supplemental heating on cold nights to keep above 55°F.

extra_problems.cause: Warm inside, cool outside creates condensation that drips on plants
extra_problems.solution: Ventilate briefly each morning. Use anti-drip greenhouse film. Increase air circulation.

extra_tasks.description: As fall progresses, greenhouse overnight temps may drop below optimal. Use a thermostat-controlled heater set to 55°F minimum.
extra_tasks.name: Supplemental heating

extra_tasks.description: Fall greenhouse humidity rises as outside temps cool. Run dehumidifier or increase ventilation during warmest part of day.
extra_tasks.name: End-of-season humidity watch

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse extends the season significantly. Supplemental heating is the key investment for late flower.

environment_overrides.notes: Late flower outdoors is October-November in many regions. First frost is the hard deadline — everything must be harvested before a hard freeze. Cool nights (50-60°F) enhance purple colors and terpene profiles. This is when outdoor DWC shines — the natural temp swings produce exceptional flavor.

extra_problems.cause: Growing season too short for the strain
extra_problems.issue: First frost before trichomes are ready
extra_problems.solution: Harvest immediately before frost. Next season, choose faster-finishing strains (8 week flower or autoflowers) or start earlier.

extra_problems.cause: Fall weather patterns
extra_problems.solution: Increase airflow around buds. Remove lower, shaded buds that are rot-prone. Consider early harvest of lower buds while tops finish.

extra_tasks.description: Monitor forecasts for first frost date. A light frost (32°F briefly) is survivable but stresses the plant. A hard freeze kills it. Have a harvest plan 1-2 weeks before expected first frost.
extra_tasks.name: Frost watch

extra_tasks.description: Cool, damp fall mornings are prime bud rot conditions. Check every dense cola daily. Remove any affected buds immediately — it spreads fast.
extra_tasks.name: Daily bud rot check

extra_tasks.description: If nighttime temps are below 45°F, bring DWC bucket into garage or sheltered area overnight. This is a major advantage of DWC — portability.
extra_tasks.name: Bring bucket in at night (optional)

outdoor.notes: Late flower outdoors is a race against frost. The natural temperature swings produce incredible terpene profiles but the weather risk is real.

health_checks: What percentage of trichomes are milky vs amber vs clear?
Natural fan leaf fade occurring? (yellowing from bottom up = normal)
Any bud rot? Check dense buds daily.
Does the grow space smell extremely strong? (peak terpene production)
stages.name: Late Flower / Ripening

nutrients.approach: Reduce to 50% strength. Plant is winding down. Natural yellowing of fan leaves is expected — the plant is using stored nutrients.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Still protecting roots until the end.

stages.photoperiod_notes: Ripening and maturation. Reduce nutrient strength. Monitor trichome ratio for desired effect (milky vs amber).

reservoir.notes: Reduce nutrient strength. Plant is nearing finish — less demand. Some yellowing of fan leaves is normal and desirable (natural fade).

tasks.description: Use 60x loupe. Harvest window: mostly milky with 10-30% amber. More amber = more couch-lock. More milky = more uplifting.
tasks.name: Check trichomes daily

tasks.description: Daily. Keep in range but plant needs less now.
tasks.name: Check pH & EC

tasks.description: Still daily. Plant still drinks even as it fades.
tasks.name: Top off reservoir

tasks.description: Critical now — dense, ripe buds are most vulnerable.
tasks.name: Bud rot patrol

tasks.description: Based on trichome readings, breeder's flowering time, and desired effect, set target harvest window.
tasks.name: Plan harvest date

stages.autoflower_notes: Shorter flush due to shorter overall cycle. 3-5 days plain water is sufficient. Some growers skip flush on autos entirely.

common_problems.cause: Normal — plant is cannibalizing fan leaves for remaining nutrients
common_problems.solution: No action needed. This is expected and desired.

stages.description: Final 3-10 days before harvest. Run plain pH'd water only. Allows plant to use up stored nutrients for smoother smoke. Controversial but widely practiced.

environment.notes: Reduce light intensity slightly. Plant is finishing — no need to push growth anymore.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse flush proceeds like indoor — weather protected. Maintain ventilation to prevent humidity buildup during these final days.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse provides stable conditions for a full flush period.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor flush is simple — just switch to plain pH'd water in the reservoir. The plant continues to receive natural light while flushing. Watch weather — if frost or heavy rain is imminent, skip the flush and harvest immediately.

extra_problems.cause: Incoming frost/rain
extra_problems.issue: Weather forces early harvest during flush
extra_problems.solution: Harvest immediately. A shorter flush won't noticeably affect quality — but mold will ruin it.

extra_tasks.description: If a hard frost, multi-day rain, or storm is forecast within 3-5 days, harvest now regardless of flush status. A clean harvest beats a moldy one.
extra_tasks.name: Weather-contingent harvest plan

outdoor.notes: Outdoor flush may be shortened or skipped entirely based on weather. Don't risk the crop for a flush.

health_checks: Are fan leaves yellowing and fading? (Good — plant is using stored nutes)
Are buds still developing or have they stopped growing?
stages.name: Flush

nutrients.approach: No nutrients. Plain pH'd water. Some growers use a flushing agent (FloraKleen) to help mobilize residual salts.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root protection even during flush.

supplements.name: FloraKleen (optional)
supplements.purpose: Flushing agent that helps dissolve residual mineral salts.

stages.photoperiod_notes: Standard 7-10 day flush with plain pH'd water. Optional — debated whether it improves taste.

reservoir.notes: Plain pH'd water only. No nutrients. Change every 3 days to keep water fresh. Still add Hydroguard to protect roots.

tasks.description: Drain reservoir completely. Refill with plain pH'd water (5.8). Add Hydroguard. No nutrients.
tasks.name: Drain & refill with plain water

tasks.description: Continue daily checks. Harvest when trichome ratio is where you want it.
tasks.name: Monitor trichomes

tasks.description: Clean trimming scissors, set up drying area, prepare drying rack/lines. Ensure drying space can maintain 60°F/60% humidity.
tasks.name: Prepare harvest equipment

tasks.description: Some growers give 48 hours of total darkness before harvest. Believed to increase trichome production. Not scientifically proven but widely practiced.
tasks.name: 48-hour darkness (optional)

transition_signals: Plant has used up stored nutrients

stages.autoflower_notes: Typically harvest entire plant at once (uniform canopy). Expected yield: 1-4 oz per plant.

common_problems.cause: Over-trimming removes trichome-covered sugar leaves
common_problems.solution: For personal use, leave some sugar leaves. For appearance, trim tight but save trim for edibles/hash.

stages.description: Cut plant, remove from bucket, trim, and hang to dry. Technique matters — this is where months of work come together.

environment.notes: Harvest in darkness or minimal light. Some growers harvest at end of dark period for highest trichome content.

environment_overrides.notes: Harvest inside the greenhouse, then move to a dedicated drying space. Don't dry in the greenhouse — temperature and humidity are too variable and light degrades trichomes.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse harvest is straightforward. Move all drying/curing to a controlled indoor space.

environment_overrides.notes: Harvest outdoors then bring everything INSIDE for drying and curing. Never dry outdoors — dust, pests, humidity, and UV degrade quality. A garage, shed, closet, or spare room with controlled conditions works.

extra_problems.cause: Outdoor buds may contain small insects, caterpillars, or eggs
extra_problems.solution: Do a thorough inspection during trim. Water cure or gentle rinse (bud washing) removes pests, dust, and debris without harming trichomes.

extra_tasks.description: Outdoor plants are best harvested at first light, before the sun warms terpenes and causes them to volatilize. Cooler temps preserve trichome quality.
extra_tasks.name: Harvest at dawn

extra_tasks.description: Scrub bucket, lid, net pot, and air stone with hydrogen peroxide or bleach solution. Rinse thoroughly. Store clean for next grow.
extra_tasks.name: Clean bucket for next season

outdoor.notes: Bud washing (gentle H2O2/water rinse) is highly recommended for outdoor harvests. It removes pests, dust, spray residue, and bird droppings.

stages.name: Harvest
stages.photoperiod_notes: Can selectively harvest (top buds first, lower buds 1 week later). Expected yield: 2-16 oz per plant.

tasks.description: Cut main stem at base. If wet trimming, trim fan leaves and sugar leaves immediately. If dry trimming, leave sugar leaves on.
tasks.name: Cut plant at base

tasks.description: Pull net pot from bucket. Drain. Clean bucket and air stone for next grow.
tasks.name: Remove from DWC bucket

tasks.description: Wet trim: remove all fan leaves, most sugar leaves, hang individual branches. Dry trim: hang whole plant or large branches. Dry trim = slower dry = better quality.
tasks.name: Wet trim or whole-plant hang

tasks.description: Note: date, wet weight, number of days in flower, strain, trichome ratio at harvest, any issues. This data improves future grows.
tasks.name: Record harvest details

transition_signals: Plant is cut, trimmed, and hanging

stages.autoflower_notes: Smaller buds may dry slightly faster. Monitor closely after day 5.

common_problems.cause: Too warm, too dry, or too much airflow
common_problems.issue: Drying too fast (crispy outside, wet inside)
common_problems.solution: Lower temp. Raise humidity. Remove direct fan on buds. Aim for 10-14 day dry.

common_problems.cause: Dried too fast — chlorophyll didn't break down properly
common_problems.solution: Prevention only — can't fix after. Slow, cool, dark drying prevents this. Curing may help slightly.

common_problems.cause: Humidity too high, dense buds, poor airflow
common_problems.solution: Remove affected buds. Lower humidity. Increase gentle air circulation.

stages.description: Hang-dry buds in dark, controlled environment. Slow drying = better flavor, smell, and smoothness. This step makes or breaks final quality.

environment.notes: DARK room. Gentle air circulation (not blowing directly on buds). 60°F / 60% RH is the gold standard. Slower = better.

environment_overrides.notes: Do NOT dry in the greenhouse. Move to a proper indoor drying space with controlled temp and humidity. Greenhouses have too much temperature and humidity variation.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse-grown buds dry the same as indoor. Just use a proper indoor drying room.

environment_overrides.notes: Dry INDOORS in a controlled space — never outdoors. A closet, spare room, or basement with 60°F/60% RH works. If in a hot/dry climate, drying may go too fast — use a humidifier. If humid climate, run a dehumidifier.

extra_problems.cause: Home heating system drying out the air
extra_problems.solution: Use a room away from heating vents. Add a small humidifier to maintain 55-65% RH. Monitor closely.

extra_tasks.description: Dip harvested branches in 3 buckets: 1) H2O2 solution (1 cup 3% per 5 gal), 2) lemon juice + baking soda water, 3) plain water rinse. Shake off excess and hang. Removes outdoor contaminants.
extra_tasks.name: Bud wash before hanging (recommended for outdoor)

outdoor.notes: The drying environment is the same regardless of grow method — 60°F / 60% RH in darkness. Bud washing is the main extra step for outdoor harvests.

health_checks: Is the drying room completely dark?
Air circulating gently (not blowing on buds directly)?
Any smell of ammonia? (too wet/fast = chlorophyll not breaking down)
stages.name: Drying
stages.photoperiod_notes: Larger/denser buds need full 10-14 days. Don't rush — slow dry = better terpenes.

tasks.description: Bend a small stem. If it snaps cleanly, buds are dry enough. If it bends, keep drying. If it crumbles, over-dried.
tasks.name: Check drying progress

tasks.description: Keep 60-68°F, 55-65% RH. Use hygrometer in drying area. Adjust dehumidifier/humidifier as needed.
tasks.name: Monitor temp & humidity

tasks.description: Inspect hanging buds for any gray/white fuzzy mold. Remove affected buds immediately.
tasks.name: Check for mold

transition_signals: Small stems snap when bent (don't bend)
Buds feel dry on outside but slightly spongy when squeezed

stages.autoflower_notes: Autos benefit equally from curing. Minimum 2 weeks, ideal 4+ weeks.

common_problems.cause: Buds weren't dry enough before jarring
common_problems.solution: Remove affected buds. Remove all buds from jar, dry for 12-24 more hours, rejar.

common_problems.cause: Too much moisture — anaerobic bacteria starting
common_problems.solution: Leave jar open for several hours. May need to lay buds on drying rack for 12-24 hours.

common_problems.cause: Over-dried before jarring or jar left open too long
common_problems.solution: Add Boveda 62% humidity pack. It will slowly restore moisture.

stages.description: Buds placed in sealed glass jars. Moisture equalizes. Chlorophyll breaks down. Terpenes mature. Flavor and smoothness dramatically improve over 2-8+ weeks.

environment.notes: Store jars in cool, dark place. 62% RH inside jars is ideal. Use Boveda 62% humidity packs for set-and-forget curing.

environment_overrides.notes: Curing is identical regardless of grow environment. Store jars in a cool, dark indoor space — not in the greenhouse.

greenhouse.notes: No greenhouse-specific curing differences.

environment_overrides.notes: Curing is identical regardless of grow environment. Store jars in cool, dark, indoor space. Outdoor-grown flower often develops exceptional terpene profiles from natural UV and temperature swings during the grow.

outdoor.notes: No outdoor-specific curing differences. All curing happens indoors in jars.

health_checks: Do buds smell better each week? (hay → floral/fruity/dank)
Any mold visible on buds in jars?
Are buds getting smoother to smoke/vape each week?
stages.name: Curing
stages.photoperiod_notes: Standard cure. Many strains continue improving for 2-6 months in jars.

tasks.description: Place dried buds loosely in glass Mason jars. Fill 3/4 full. Add Boveda 62% pack. Close lid.
tasks.name: Jar buds

tasks.description: Open jars for 5-15 minutes, 2-3 times per day for first 2 weeks. This releases excess moisture and gases. If ammonia smell, leave open longer — buds were too wet.
tasks.name: Burp jars (week 1-2)

tasks.description: Reduce to once daily for 5 minutes.
tasks.name: Burp jars (week 3-4)

tasks.description: Once every few days or weekly. After 4+ weeks, buds are stable.
tasks.name: Burp jars (week 5+)

tasks.description: Use mini hygrometer in jar. Target 60-65% RH. If above 70%, leave open for an hour. If below 55%, buds are over-dried (add Boveda pack).
tasks.name: Check jar humidity

tasks.description: Weigh dry, cured buds for final yield numbers. Compare to wet weight and growing conditions for future optimization.
tasks.name: Record final weights

container_progression.commercial_large: Nitrogen-flushed drums (5-50 lb), sealed bins in vault
container_progression.commercial_medium: Turkey bags in sealed bins, nitrogen-flushed
container_progression.industrial: Climate-controlled vault rooms, nitrogen generators, automated monitoring

degradation_timeline.0_3_months: Peak quality. Minimal degradation. Ideal consumption window.
degradation_timeline.12_18_months: Significant quality decline. Consider processing into extracts/edibles.
degradation_timeline.18_plus_months: Not recommended for flower sales. Convert to extracts, edibles, or topicals.
degradation_timeline.3_6_months: Slight terpene reduction (~10-15%). Potency stable. Still premium.
degradation_timeline.6_12_months: Noticeable terpene loss (~20-30%). THC down ~5%. Still good quality.

inventory_management.batch_tracking: Seed-to-sale tracking per state regulations. Lot numbers, weights, test results.
inventory_management.fifo: First in, first out. Oldest batches ship first.
inventory_management.hold_requirements: Retain testing samples per compliance. Typical: 10g per batch, stored 1 year minimum.
inventory_management.insurance_storage: Maintain documented temperature/humidity logs for insurance claims.

testing_schedule.360_days: Full panel — decision point: sell, process, or destroy
testing_schedule.initial: Full panel at harvest (potency, terpenes, pesticides, heavy metals, microbials)

common_problems.cause: Heat, light, oxygen, or time. THC converts to CBN at ~5% per year under ideal conditions, much faster with heat/light.
common_problems.issue: THC degrading to CBN (increased sedation, reduced potency)
common_problems.solution: Ensure complete darkness, cool temps (60°F ideal), minimal oxygen (nitrogen flush). Accept ~5%/year loss as baseline. If faster: check for light leaks and temp spikes.

common_problems.cause: Temperature above 70°F volatilizes terpenes. Oxygen oxidizes them. Frequent container opening.
common_problems.solution: Keep below 65°F. Minimize container opening. Nitrogen-sealed containers for long-term. Terpenes degrade faster than cannabinoids.

common_problems.cause: Humidity above 65% or flower wasn't properly dried/cured before storage
common_problems.solution: Check humidity immediately. Remove affected material. Increase airflow. Ensure flower is at 58-62% RH before sealing. Commercial: reject and destroy moldy batches per compliance requirements.

common_problems.cause: Normal moisture equilibration. 1-3% weight loss in first month of storage is typical.
common_problems.solution: Account for in inventory. Boveda/Integra packs minimize loss. Sealed containers reduce ongoing loss.

stages.description: Post-cure long-term storage. After 4-8 weeks of active curing, flower transitions to storage. For home growers this is jars in a dark closet. For commercial operations this is climate-controlled vault storage with nitrogen-sealed containers, batch tracking, FIFO rotation, and compliance testing holds. Proper storage preserves potency and terpenes for 6-12+ months. Improper storage degrades THC to CBN (sedative), destroys terpenes, and invites mold.

environment.notes: DARK. Cool. Stable temperature. Zero light exposure — UV degrades cannabinoids and terpenes. Commercial vaults: 58-62°F, 55-60% RH, complete darkness. Home: dark closet, cool room. Never garage (temp swings) or fridge (humidity issues).

environment_overrides.notes: Do NOT store in greenhouse. Store in climate-controlled indoor space.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse temperature swings make it unsuitable for storage.

environment_overrides.notes: Storage is always indoor. No outdoor-specific differences.

outdoor.notes: All storage is indoor in controlled environment.

stages.name: Long-Term Storage

nutrients.approach: None.

stages.photoperiod_notes: Properly cured and stored, quality remains excellent for 12+ months.

tasks.description: Home: mason jars with Boveda 58% or 62% packs. Commercial: nitrogen-sealed bags (grove bags, turkey bags, CVault containers) or nitrogen-flushed drums for bulk. Remove as much oxygen as possible — oxygen degrades cannabinoids.
tasks.name: Transfer to long-term containers

tasks.description: Label every container: strain, harvest date, cure start date, storage date, weight, batch number. Commercial: full seed-to-sale tracking. FIFO rotation — oldest stock ships first.
tasks.name: Label and track batches

tasks.description: Inspect for mold, smell for off odors (ammonia, mustiness). Check humidity packs — replace when exhausted. Spot-check trichome color (amber increase = degradation progressing). Commercial: test potency/terpenes at 30/90/180 day intervals.
tasks.name: Monthly quality checks

tasks.description: Monitor vault/storage room temp and humidity. Dehumidifier + AC as needed. No light leaks. Commercial: environmental monitoring with alerts.
tasks.name: Maintain environment

tasks.description: Commercial: first in, first out. Track storage duration per batch. Flag batches approaching 12 months for priority sale/processing.
tasks.name: Rotate stock (FIFO)

tasks.description: Commercial: maintain testing hold samples per state/local regulations. Store separately. Track chain of custody.
tasks.name: Compliance testing holds

autoflower.description: Ruderalis-hybrid strains that flower automatically regardless of light schedule. Fixed timeline.

light_schedule.flower: 20/4 or 18/6 (same as veg — no change needed)
light_schedule.transition: Automatic — plant decides when to flower (usually week 3-4)

autoflower.name: Autoflower
autoflower.notes: Fixed timeline — stress cannot be recovered by extending veg. Start in final pot (no transplant shock). LST preferred over HST.

training_suitability.defoliation: light only — auto can't recover from heavy defol
training_suitability.topping: risky — only if plant is vigorous

photoperiod.description: Standard cannabis strains that require 12/12 light to flower. Grower controls timing.

light_schedule.transition: Switch to 12/12 when plant is ~50% of desired final height

photoperiod.name: Photoperiod
photoperiod.notes: Can be vegged as long as desired. Cloneable. More forgiving of stress (can extend veg to recover).

training_suitability.defoliation: good
training_suitability.topping: excellent

total_grow_days.breakdown: Germination (3-7d) + Seedling (7-14d) + Veg (28-63d) + Flower (56-70d) + Dry (7-14d) + Cure (14-60d)

solutions: Add Hydroguard immediately (2 ml/gal)
Lower water temp below 72°F
Upgrade air stone or add second air stone
Trim dead/brown root material with clean scissors
Full reservoir change with fresh, clean solution
If severe, use hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) at 3ml/gal of 3% solution as emergency treatment — then switch to Hydroguard 24 hours later

causes: Dark nutrients discolor roots over time
solutions: No action needed if roots are firm and not slimy. Smell test — healthy roots have no bad odor.
problems.symptom: Roots stained brown but not slimy

solutions: Raise water level to touch bottom of net pot
Top-feed with turkey baster 2-3x daily
Ensure 75-80°F ambient temp
Consider top-feed pump setup

problems.diagnosis: Reservoir too small or rapidly changing conditions
solutions: Use larger reservoir (10-gal)
More frequent but smaller pH adjustments
Add a small amount of tap water for mineral buffering
Don't over-correct — let pH drift naturally within 5.5-6.2
problems.symptom: pH swings rapidly up and down

causes: Plants consuming nutrients at different rates
problems.diagnosis: Normal in DWC — aeration raises pH
solutions: Use pH Down to bring back into range
This is normal — just manage it
A small amount of pH Down goes a long way

causes: Not topping off with plain water
solutions: Reduce EC by 15-20%
Top off with plain pH'd water when EC rises
Slight tip burn is actually optimal — means plant is near max capacity

causes: pH out of range blocking N uptake
solutions: Check pH first (5.5-6.2)
Increase Gro portion of nutrients
Full reservoir change with fresh mix

causes: RO or soft water lacks minerals
DWC has no media to buffer minerals
solutions: Add CalMag supplement (1-2 ml/gal)
Add CalMag before base nutrients when mixing
Almost all DWC growers need CalMag
Use opaque dark-colored bucket
Cover net pot gaps with hydroton or neoprene collar
Seal any lid gaps with tape or neoprene
Keep reservoir completely dark
Insulate bucket with reflective material
Place frozen water bottles in reservoir
Use a water chiller (if chronic issue)
Move air pump outside grow space and pipe air in
Ensure Hydroguard is used — warm water + no beneficials = guaranteed root rot

cons: RO system cost and waste water
water_source_profiles.name: Reverse Osmosis (RO) Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Gold standard for precision growing. Always add CalMag first when using RO.
pros: Blank slate — total control over mineral content

dechlorination.chloramine: Does NOT gas off — must use Campden tablets or activated carbon filter
dechlorination.chlorine: Let sit uncovered 24 hours (gasses off) or use dechlorinator drops
dechlorination.how_to_tell: Check city water report. Most modern cities use chloramine.

water_source_profiles.name: Municipal Tap Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Good enough for most grows. Dechlorinate and pH-adjust. Get a water report from your city.

cons: May contain iron, sulfur, or heavy metals
water_source_profiles.name: Well Water
water_source_profiles.notes: MUST get a water test before using. High iron or sulfur can cause lockout. Great if your well has clean water with moderate minerals.

water_source_profiles.name: Distilled Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Same as RO but purchased instead of filtered. Only practical for 1-2 plant grows.

advanced_techniques.description: Elevating CO2 from ambient (400ppm) to 1000-1500ppm increases photosynthesis rate by 20-40% when light and temperature are also increased.

methods.notes: Most common. Tank refills every 2-4 weeks for a tent.
Produces heat and humidity — better for large rooms with AC.
Produces ~100-200ppm boost. Only useful for small sealed spaces. Not worth it for most.

advanced_techniques.name: CO2 Supplementation

ppfd_co2_correlation.notes: Ambient CO2 sufficient at low light
Slight benefit from supplementation
Moderate benefit — cost-effective threshold
Strong benefit — diminishing returns above this
Maximum practical benefit for most grows
Commercial intensity — requires perfect environment

requirements: Sealed grow space (CO2 escapes quickly from leaky rooms)
High light intensity (>600 PPFD — plants can't use extra CO2 without sufficient light)
Higher temperature tolerance (82-86°F optimal with CO2 vs 75-80°F without)
CO2 controller or timer

advanced_techniques.description: Manipulating environmental and irrigation parameters to push the plant toward vegetative or generative (flowering/fruiting) growth.
advanced_techniques.name: Crop Steering
advanced_techniques.notes: Crop steering is most effective in hydro/coco where irrigation frequency is highly controllable. Soil growers have less precision due to soil buffering.

vegetative_steering.goal: More leaf/stem growth, larger plant framework

best_practices: Spray during lights-off or first/last 30 minutes of light cycle (stomata open, no light burn)
Use fine mist sprayer — coverage is key
pH foliar solution to 6.2-6.5 (leaf surface slightly higher pH than root zone)
EC should be very low (0.2-0.5) — leaves burn easily
Never foliar spray during flower (moisture in buds → mold/bud rot)
Best for micronutrient deficiency correction (CalMag, iron, magnesium)

common_foliar_products.frequency: Weekly preventive, 3-day intervals for active infestations

advanced_techniques.description: Spraying nutrient solution directly onto leaves for rapid uptake. Bypasses root zone entirely.
advanced_techniques.name: Foliar Feeding

advanced_techniques.description: Strengthens cell walls, increases stem thickness, improves heat/drought/pest resistance.
important_notes: ALWAYS add silica to water FIRST before other nutrients (raises pH significantly)
Let silica mix for 5-10 minutes before adding base nutrients
Silica is incompatible with concentrated nutrient solutions — never mix undiluted
Discontinue in late flower (last 2-3 weeks)
advanced_techniques.name: Silica (Potassium Silicate)

advanced_techniques.description: Introduces beneficial bacteria/fungi to the root zone to outcompete pathogens, improve nutrient uptake, and protect against root diseases.
important_notes: Do NOT use hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) with beneficial microbes — it kills them
Choose either sterile (H2O2) OR beneficial (Hydroguard) approach — not both
Beneficials need warmish water (65-75°F) to thrive
Reapply after any reservoir change or system cleaning
advanced_techniques.name: Beneficial Microbes & Root Inoculants

products.name: Hydroguard
products.notes: The standard for DWC/RDWC. Add at every res change.

products.dosing: 1 tsp/gal (water-in) or dust roots at transplant
products.name: Great White
products.notes: Best for soil/coco. Less effective in pure hydro.

products.name: Recharge
products.notes: Feeds existing soil microbes. Great for living soil.

products.name: Mammoth P
products.notes: Most effective in flower when P demand is highest.

advanced_techniques.description: Removing select fan leaves to improve light penetration, airflow, and direct energy to bud sites.
advanced_techniques.name: Defoliation (Strategic Leaf Removal)
rules: Never remove more than 20-30% of leaves at once (except schwazze technique)
Only defoliate healthy, vigorous plants — never stressed ones
Autoflowers: very light defoliation only — they can't recover from heavy removal
Always leave the top 2-3 fan leaves on each branch (they feed the bud below)

timing.method: Remove lower leaves that get no light (lollipop)
Major defoliation day 1 and day 21 of flower (schwazzing)
Selective tuck or remove leaves blocking bud sites

equipment.description: Rigid, waterproof tray. Must be PERFECTLY level. ABS plastic or injection-molded. 2x4 ft for small grows (4-6 plants), 4x4 ft for larger (8-16 plants). Must have drain fitting hole and overflow fitting hole.
equipment.name: Flood Table / Tray (2x4 ft or 4x4 ft)

equipment.description: Supports the flood table at working height. Must be rigid and level. Adjustable legs help achieve perfect leveling.
equipment.name: Table Stand / Frame

equipment.description: Installed in table's drain hole. Connects via tubing to reservoir below. Screen/guard to prevent media and roots from entering.
equipment.name: Drain Fitting + Extension

equipment.description: Safety fitting set at maximum flood height. If pump sticks ON, overflow fitting prevents water from going higher. Set at 2/3 media height. NON-NEGOTIABLE.
equipment.name: Overflow Fitting

equipment.description: Sits below the flood table. Collects drain-back. Size at 2-3x the table flood volume for pH/EC stability. Food-safe, opaque, dark-colored.
equipment.name: Reservoir (20-50 gal)

equipment.description: Pumps from reservoir up to flood table. Size to fill table to target height in 5-10 minutes. Larger tables need bigger pumps.
equipment.name: Water Pump (submersible)

equipment.description: Controls pump on/off cycles. Digital timers are more reliable than mechanical. Minimum 15-minute resolution. Must be programmed for media-specific flood frequency.
equipment.name: Timer (digital preferred)

equipment.description: Hydroton (fast drain, frequent floods), Rockwool cubes/slabs (high retention, fewer floods), Perlite (moderate), or Coco in pots (high retention). Each media changes flood frequency dramatically.
equipment.name: Growing Media (choose one)

equipment.description: Individual pots sitting on the flood table. Net pots for hydroton (roots grow out bottom). Solid pots with drainage holes for coco/perlite. 1-3 gallon for small plants, 3-5 gallon for larger.
equipment.name: Pots / Net Pots / Containers

equipment.description: Measure at reservoir 30 minutes after drain-back for most accurate readings.
equipment.name: pH Pen

equipment.description: Measure at reservoir after drain-back. Also check EC of drain-back water to assess root zone conditions.
equipment.name: EC/TDS Meter

equipment.description: Adjust at reservoir.
equipment.name: pH Up & pH Down

equipment.description: Mix in reservoir. Delivered to plants during each flood cycle.
equipment.name: Nutrients (GH Flora Trio)

equipment.description: Root protection. Flood tables are warm, moist environments ideal for Pythium.
equipment.name: Hydroguard

equipment.description: Oxygenate reservoir water between floods.
equipment.name: Air Pump + Air Stone (for reservoir)

equipment.description: Size for table footprint canopy area.
equipment.name: Grow Light

equipment.description: Separate from flood timer. Reliable 18/6 → 12/12 scheduling.
equipment.name: Light Timer

equipment.description: Monitor grow room conditions.
equipment.name: Thermometer / Hygrometer

equipment.description: Verify table is perfectly level. Check BOTH directions. Re-check after loading with heavy pots.
equipment.name: Spirit Level

equipment.description: Prevents roots and media from entering the drain fitting. Saves pump and prevents clogs.
equipment.name: Drain Guard / Screen

equipment.description: Timer failure = either constant flooding (overflow) or drought. Keep a spare timer ready.
equipment.name: Backup Timer

equipment.description: Even canopy across the flood table.
equipment.name: SCROG / Trellis Net

equipment.description: Essential with RO water.
equipment.name: CalMag Supplement

equipment.description: Strengthen stems. Helpful for heavy flower support.
equipment.name: Silica Supplement

equipment.description: Air movement above table prevents humidity pockets.
equipment.name: Oscillating Fans

equipment.description: Odor and humidity control.
equipment.name: Exhaust Fan + Carbon Filter

equipment.description: Elevate pots slightly above table surface. Improves drainage and prevents roots from growing to drain fitting.
equipment.name: Pot Risers

equipment.description: Maintains reservoir level. Helpful with large plants consuming heavily.
equipment.name: Auto-Top-Off (float valve in reservoir)

equipment.description: Auto-dosing for reservoir pH and EC.
equipment.name: pH/EC Controller

equipment.description: Controls reservoir temp. Less critical than in DWC since roots get air between floods.
equipment.name: Water Chiller

equipment.description: Run multiple flood tables from one reservoir. Requires larger pump and sequencing valves.
equipment.name: Multi-Tray Manifold

equipment.description: Alerts if water detected outside the table. Catches leaks and overflows.
equipment.name: Flood Alarm / Leak Sensor

failure_modes.consequence: Continuous flooding. Roots submerged indefinitely = drowning + root rot within hours.
failure_modes.prevention: Overflow fitting (critical safety). Secondary timer as backup cutoff. Smart outlet with max-on-time limit.

failure_modes.consequence: Tray won't drain. Standing water. Root rot. Potential overflow onto floor.
failure_modes.failure: Drain clog (media particles blocking drain fitting)
failure_modes.prevention: Screen over drain fitting (keeps media out). Regular cleaning. Check drain speed monthly.

failure_modes.consequence: No flooding. Media dries out. 4-12 hours to damage depending on media retention.
failure_modes.prevention: Backup pump. Check pump weekly (listen for unusual sounds). Replace annually.

failure_modes.consequence: Uneven flooding. Low-end plants overwatered. High-end plants underwatered. Algae at low end.
failure_modes.prevention: Level check monthly (trays can shift). Use robust stand. Shim immediately if off-level.

failure_modes.consequence: Tray only partially floods. Pump runs dry (burns out). Plants at edges get no nutrients.
failure_modes.failure: Reservoir too small (runs dry during flood)
failure_modes.prevention: Reservoir volume = 2x tray flood volume. Mark 'low water' line on reservoir. Float valve for auto top-off.

early_veg.notes: Increasing frequency. Roots establishing in media.

flush.notes: Frequent flooding with plain water to leach salts from media.

late_flower.notes: Slightly reduce as plant matures. Promotes ripening.

late_veg.notes: Aggressive growth needs more frequent nutrient delivery.

seedling.notes: Minimal flooding. Media stays moist longer. Over-flooding = damping off.

day_vs_night.daytime: All floods occur during lights-on. Active transpiration drives nutrient uptake.
day_vs_night.last_flood: 1 hour before lights off (allows media surface to dry slightly, reducing fungal risk).
day_vs_night.nighttime: ZERO floods during lights-off for most growers. Exception: very hot environments where media dries fast.

flood_height.danger_zone: Flooding to surface = algae, fungus gnats, stem rot at base.
flood_height.overflow_protection: MANDATORY overflow fitting set 1 inch below media surface. Prevents pump malfunction from over-flooding.
flood_height.target: 2/3 up the media depth. Never above the top surface.

growstones.best_for: Balance between retention and aeration. Lighter than hydroton.
growstones.flood_frequency_adjustment: Standard timing. Good moisture holding capacity.
growstones.preparation: Rinse thoroughly. Can be dusty. Pre-soak 12h.

hydroton_expanded_clay.best_for: Most ebb & flow setups. Fast drain cycle. Good oxygen retention.
hydroton_expanded_clay.flood_frequency_adjustment: Needs more frequent flooding due to low retention. +1-2 floods/day vs rockwool.
hydroton_expanded_clay.preparation: Soak and rinse to remove dust. Pre-soak in pH 5.5 water for 24h.

perlite_vermiculite_mix.best_for: Budget option. Good retention + aeration. Use in net pots with screen bottom.
perlite_vermiculite_mix.flood_frequency_adjustment: High retention = fewer floods. Good for forgetful timers.
perlite_vermiculite_mix.preparation: Wet before use (perlite dust is harmful to breathe). Mix ratio: 70% perlite / 30% vermiculite.

rockwool_cubes_slabs.best_for: Commercial ebb & flow tables. Consistent saturation. Precise irrigation.
rockwool_cubes_slabs.flood_frequency_adjustment: Fewer floods needed. -1-2 floods/day vs hydroton. Watch for over-saturation.
rockwool_cubes_slabs.preparation: Soak in pH 4.5 solution for 24h to condition. Rinse. Verify pH stable at 5.5-6.0.

independent_systems.best_for: Multiple strains or growth stages running simultaneously.
independent_systems.description: Each tray has its own dedicated reservoir and pump.

cons: Nutrient consistency issues (first tray gets freshest solution)
parallel_flooding.description: All trays flood simultaneously from one reservoir/pump.
parallel_flooding.pump_sizing: Sum of all individual tray GPH requirements.

sequential_flooding.description: Trays flood one at a time using solenoid valves or individual pumps.
sequential_flooding.implementation: Timer + solenoid valves on each tray feed line. Open one at a time.

pump_and_timer_configuration.drain_time_considerations: Total cycle = pump-on time + drain time. Drain typically takes 3-5 min for a 4x4 tray. Roots are submerged during drain. Account for this in total wet time.

pump_sizing.notes: Account for head height (tray above reservoir). Add 25% for every 2 feet of vertical lift.
pump_sizing.rule: Pump must fill tray to flood height within 2-3 minutes. Longer fill time = uneven nutrient distribution.

timer_requirements.advanced: Smart timer with programmable flood/drain sequences and alerts
timer_requirements.critical_note: Timer controls ON time only. Drain happens automatically when pump turns off (gravity).
timer_requirements.minimum_resolution: 1 minute (analog timer acceptable for simple schedules)
timer_requirements.preferred: Digital timer with 1-minute increments and multiple daily events

drain_fitting_size.critical: Drain must be larger than fill fitting. Gravity drain is slower than pump fill — undersized drain = overflow.
drain_fitting_size.large_trays: 1 inch minimum for 4x4+. Dual drains for 4x8.

tray_engineering.flood_fill_fitting: Usually 1/2 to 3/4 inch. Connects to pump in reservoir below.
tray_engineering.leveling: Tray MUST be perfectly level. Use shims and spirit level. Unlevel tray = uneven flooding = some plants underwater while others dry.

2x2_ft.best_for: Single large plant or few small plants.

flush_timing.coco: 7-14 days reduced nutrients → plain water last 3-5 days
flush_timing.notes: Flushing is debated — some studies show no difference. At minimum, reduce nutrients in final week.
flush_timing.soil: 14-21 days plain water (soil holds nutrients longer)

other_harvest_indicators: Most pistils (hairs) have darkened and curled in
Calyxes are swollen and dense
Fan leaves are yellowing and falling off naturally
Trichome stalks are visible and heads are enlarged
Breeder's recommended flowering time has passed

trichome_ratios.notes: Harvest early for more THC-dominant, less CBN. Energetic, racy high. Slight reduction in yield.
Standard harvest window. Mix of THC and CBN. Full-spectrum effects.
Extended harvest for max CBN conversion. Heavy body high. Good for pain/sleep. Slightly degraded THC.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Micro → Gro → Bloom (always Micro first)
nutrient_brands.name: General Hydroponics Flora Trio

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Part A → Epsom → Part B (dissolve each fully before adding next)
nutrient_brands.name: Jack's 321
nutrient_brands.notes: Jack's 321 is 3.6g Part A, 2.4g Cal Nit, 1.2g Epsom per gallon at full strength. Extremely cost-effective for large grows.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Core → Bloom
nutrient_brands.name: Athena Pro Line
nutrient_brands.notes: Commercial-grade. Simple 2-part with integrated CalMag. Popular in commercial cannabis facilities.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: A → B (separate for veg and bloom)
nutrient_brands.name: Advanced Nutrients pH Perfect (Sensi)
nutrient_brands.notes: Self-adjusting pH technology (claims no pH adjustment needed). Expensive but popular for beginners who struggle with pH.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Shake VERY well before use (thick concentrate settles). One bottle at a time.
nutrient_brands.name: General Hydroponics FloraNova
nutrient_brands.notes: Simplest option — one bottle per phase. Great for beginners. Shake well — concentrate separates.
nutrient_brands.type: 1-part (two bottles — one for veg, one for bloom)

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Dissolve powder in warm water, stir well
nutrient_brands.name: Greenleaf Nutrients Mega Crop
nutrient_brands.notes: One powder does everything — includes CalMag. Incredibly cost-effective at ~$0.02/gal. Community favorite on r/microgrowery.

environment.container: Glass mason jars (quart size). Fill 75% full.

humidity_packs.boveda_58: For drier preference or long-term storage.
humidity_packs.boveda_62: Maintains 62% RH. Set-and-forget. Replace when crispy.
humidity_packs.grove_bags: Heat-sealed bags with built-in humidity regulation. No burping needed.

indicators_of_good_cure: Smooth, flavorful smoke — no harshness or hay smell
Buds are slightly sticky but not wet
Strong terpene aroma when jar is opened
Ash burns to white/light gray (not black)
method: Trim buds to final shape (dry trim or finish wet trim)
Place in mason jars — fill 3/4 full to allow air exchange
Week 1: burp 2-3x daily for 10-15 minutes (release moisture and gases)
Week 2-4: burp 1x daily for 5-10 minutes
Month 2+: burp 1x weekly or seal completely
Cure for minimum 2 weeks, ideal is 4-8 weeks. Some strains improve for 6+ months.

common_mistakes: Drying too fast (>3 days) — harsh smoke, hay smell, terpene loss
Drying too slow (>14 days) — mold risk, diminishing returns
Fan blowing directly on buds — uneven drying, crispy exterior/moist interior
Temperature too high (>70°F) — terpene evaporation, harsh taste

environment.airflow: Gentle circulation — fan NOT pointed at buds. Just keep air moving.

method: Cut branches 12-18 inches long
Optionally remove large fan leaves (wet trim) or leave intact (dry trim)
Hang upside down on line/rack with 6+ inches between branches
Small branches should snap (not bend) when done — 5-7 days minimum
Stems should snap cleanly; if they bend, keep drying

long_term_storage.degradation: THC slowly converts to CBN over time. Light, heat, air, and moisture accelerate degradation.
long_term_storage.method: Sealed mason jars or vacuum-sealed bags with Boveda 58% in dark, cool place
long_term_storage.notes: Properly cured and stored cannabis can remain excellent for 12+ months. Freezing is debated — can damage trichomes if not done carefully.

flood_engineering.drain_time: Complete drain-back in 10-20 minutes. If slower, check drain fitting.
flood_engineering.fill_time: Table should fill to target height in 5-10 minutes. Faster = pump too large (turbulence). Slower = pump too small.
flood_engineering.flood_height: 2/3 up the media in the pot. NEVER above the stem line.
flood_engineering.overflow_fitting_height: Set at 2/3 media height. This is your safety — if pump sticks ON, overflow prevents flooding above this line.
flood_engineering.soak_time: Once at target height, hold for 2-3 minutes for media saturation, then pump off and drain.

flood_frequency_matrix.description: Floods per day by media type and stage. Lights-on floods only unless noted.

golden_rules: The flood table MUST be perfectly level — even 1/8 inch slope means uneven flooding
Overflow fitting is NON-NEGOTIABLE — it's your safety if the pump sticks ON
Match flood frequency to your media type — hydroton and rockwool are VERY different
Read pH/EC 30 minutes AFTER drain-back for accurate readings
Complete drain-back after EVERY flood — standing water causes root rot
Timer reliability is critical — stuck ON = overflow, stuck OFF = drought
Flood height: 2/3 up the media, NEVER touching the stem
Clean the table surface at every reservoir change — salt deposits accumulate
Top-water pots occasionally with plain pH'd water to flush surface salt buildup
Ebb & Flow is forgiving — plants survive hours without a flood (unlike NFT)
quick_reference.hydroguard_dose: 2 ml/gal at every reservoir change AND top-off
quick_reference.ph_ec_reading_tip: Read pH/EC at reservoir 30 minutes after drain-back for most accurate readings
quick_reference.reservoir_change_schedule: Every 7 days in veg, every 5 days in flower, every 3 days during flush
quick_reference.top_off_rule: EC rising = top off with plain pH'd water. EC dropping = top off with 1/4 strength nutrient water.

change_schedule.top_off: Daily with plain pH'd water when EC rises. With dilute nutrient when EC drops.
change_schedule.top_off_rule: EC rising = plants drinking more water than nutrients → top off with plain water. EC dropping = plants eating more nutrients than water → top off with dilute nutrient mix.

reservoir_management.description: Reservoir management for active hydroponic systems (DWC, RDWC, NFT, Ebb & Flow, Drip, Aeroponics).

dissolved_oxygen.why_important: Roots need oxygen to function. Low DO → root rot, nutrient lockout, slow growth.

sizing_guidelines.notes: Larger reservoirs = more stable pH/EC, less frequent changes, more forgiving of mistakes.

beneficial.cons: Slightly cloudy water (normal). Doesn't work above 75°F water.
beneficial.pros: Continuous protection. Outcompetes pathogens. Set-and-forget per res change.

sterile_vs_beneficial.recommendation: Beneficial approach (Hydroguard) for most growers. Sterile only if you have persistent issues.

sterile.cons: Must reapply every 2-3 days. Kills beneficial microbes too. Degrades in hours.
sterile.pros: Kills all pathogens. Clean roots. No biofilm.

water_temperature.too_cold_risk: Slowed metabolism, nutrient lockout, stunted growth
water_temperature.too_warm_risk: Root rot, low dissolved oxygen, pathogen growth

scale_tiers.description: First-time grower or single-plant hobbyist. Manual management, learning-focused.
scale_tiers.name: Solo / Single Plant
scale_tiers.notes: Hands-on learning experience. Manual checks 1-2x daily. Ideal for beginners.

scale_tiers.description: Hobbyist tent grow. Light automation helpful but not required.
scale_tiers.name: Small Tent (2-8 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Timer-controlled lights, humidity controller. Sensor monitoring recommended.

scale_tiers.description: Dedicated grower with multiple tents/rooms. Automation saves significant time.
scale_tiers.name: Multi-Tent (9-24 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Multiple environments to manage. Automated alerts essential. Consider perpetual harvest.

scale_tiers.description: Licensed commercial operation. Full automation, compliance tracking, batch management.
scale_tiers.name: Commercial Room (25-100 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: SOPs required. Staff training. Regulatory compliance. Insurance requirements.

scale_tiers.description: Large-scale production facility. Enterprise automation, SCADA integration, dedicated staff.
scale_tiers.name: Warehouse Scale (100+ plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Multiple rooms, dedicated HVAC, industrial nutrient dosing, video surveillance.

common_problems.cause: Too cold, too wet, or bad seed
common_problems.solution: Ensure 75-80°F. Starter medium should be moist not soaking. Try a different seed after 7 days.

common_problems.cause: Surface or stand is uneven
common_problems.solution: Shim the table or stand until a spirit level reads level in both directions. This is critical — fix it before any plants go on.

stages.description: Seed cracks open and taproot emerges. For Ebb & Flow, start seeds in Rapid Rooters, rockwool cubes, or directly in the chosen media. Seeds are NOT on the flood table yet — germinate separately in a humidity dome.

environment.notes: Keep seeds in darkness. Heat mat at 78°F. No light until sprout emerges. Humidity dome over propagation tray.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse germination: use heat mat and humidity dome. Greenhouse temp swings may be too large without supplemental heating.

greenhouse.notes: Heat mat recommended in greenhouse.

environment_overrides.notes: Start seeds indoors. Outdoor flood tables are exposed to rain and temp swings — seedlings are too fragile.

outdoor.notes: Always germinate indoors for Ebb & Flow.

flood_cycle.notes: Seeds are NOT on the flood table yet. They are in a propagation tray with humidity dome. Germinate off-system.

health_checks: Is the taproot visible and white?
Is the starter medium moist but not soaking?
Is the flood table level and leak-tested?
stages.name: Germination

nutrients.approach: None — seeds contain all energy needed to germinate.

reservoir.notes: No reservoir running yet. Pre-soak growing media while seeds germinate. Leak-test the flood table, fittings, and pump.

tasks.description: Soak seeds 12-24 hours, then place in Rapid Rooters or pre-soaked rockwool cubes in a humidity dome. If using hydroton: start in Rapid Rooter first, transplant later.
tasks.name: Start seeds

tasks.description: After 24-72 hours, look for white taproot emerging. Do not disturb the seed — just look.
tasks.name: Check for taproot

tasks.description: While seeds germinate: set up flood table, verify it's PERFECTLY level, test pump and timer, check overflow fitting height, verify drain-back.
tasks.name: Prepare flood table

tasks.description: Use a spirit level. The table must be perfectly level in BOTH directions. Even 1/8 inch slope means uneven flooding — one end floods deeper than the other.
tasks.name: Level the flood table

tasks.description: Rinse hydroton thoroughly (pH 5.5 water). Soak rockwool in pH 5.5 water for 1 hour. Rinse perlite to remove dust.
tasks.name: Pre-soak growing media

common_problems.cause: Flood height too high — water touching the stem, or too frequent floods keeping media soggy
common_problems.issue: Damping off (stem collapse at media line)
common_problems.solution: Lower flood height. Reduce flood frequency. Improve airflow. Remove affected seedlings — damping off is fatal.

common_problems.cause: Media not supporting the seedling, or flood turbulence knocking it over
common_problems.solution: Pack media gently around seedling base. Use slower fill rate. Support with a small stake temporarily.

common_problems.cause: Table not level, or drain fitting clogged
common_problems.issue: Standing water on table after drain
common_problems.solution: Re-level table. Clean drain fitting. Verify siphon break on overflow fitting.

common_problems.cause: Light reaching wet surfaces
common_problems.solution: Cover exposed media surface with hydroton or perlite. Use opaque pots. Block light from reaching table surface.

stages.description: Seedling develops first true leaves. Can be placed on the flood table in final media/pots now, with very gentle flood cycles. Alternatively, keep on propagation tray and hand-water until more established. If placing on the flood table: use very shallow, infrequent floods.

environment.notes: Gentle light. Remove humidity dome after 3-5 days. First floods should be shallow — water must NOT touch the stem above the media line.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: good environment for seedlings. Warmer temps may require more frequent floods if media dries faster.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse: monitor media moisture more closely in warm conditions.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor: rain can flood the table uncontrollably. Cover the flood table or ensure drainage can handle rain volume.

extra_problems.cause: Uncovered outdoor table
extra_problems.solution: Install permanent rain cover. Ensure overflow fitting can handle sudden water influx.

extra_tasks.description: Rain flooding the table dilutes nutrients and can overflow. Cover is mandatory for outdoor Ebb & Flow.
extra_tasks.name: Install rain cover over flood table

outdoor.notes: Outdoor Ebb & Flow: rain cover is mandatory.

coco.notes: Coco holds moisture excellently. 1 flood/day maximum. Let coco dry slightly between floods.

hydroton.notes: Hydroton drains fast. 2 floods/day for seedlings. Flood just enough to wet the bottom half of the pot.

perlite.notes: Perlite drains moderately. 2 floods/day. Similar to hydroton but retains slightly more moisture.

rockwool.notes: Rockwool retains moisture well. 1 flood/day is sufficient. Over-flooding rockwool drowns seedlings.

flood_cycle.notes: Very gentle floods. Water level should reach only 1.5-2 inches up the pot/media — just enough to wet the bottom root zone. No floods at night — seedlings don't need it and excess moisture promotes damping off.

health_checks: No standing water on table after drain-back?
stages.name: Seedling

nutrients.approach: Quarter strength. Seedlings are tiny. The flood cycle delivers nutrients to roots during each flood — no continuous feeding needed.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Protect young roots. Flood tables are warm and moist — ideal for Pythium.

reservoir.notes: Very dilute nutrient solution. Reservoir should be sized at minimum 2x the flood table volume. Read pH/EC 30 minutes after last flood (drain-back stabilizes readings).

tasks.description: Transfer seedlings in their starter medium into net pots or pots filled with chosen media. Place on flood table. Ensure flood height does NOT reach the stem.
tasks.name: Place seedlings on flood table

tasks.description: Run pump manually. Verify flood height is correct (1.5-2 inches). Time the fill and drain. Verify complete drain-back to reservoir.
tasks.name: Run first test flood

tasks.description: Program timer for 1-2 floods per day during lights-on only. Each flood = pump on for 10-15 minutes (enough to fill and soak).
tasks.name: Set timer for floods

tasks.description: Read reservoir pH/EC 30 minutes after a flood. Drain-back carries information about root zone conditions.
tasks.name: Check pH/EC after drain-back

tasks.description: After each flood, all water must drain back to reservoir. Standing water on the table = level problem or clogged drain.
tasks.name: Verify complete drainage

tasks.description: Watch for stretching, yellowing, or damping off. Reduce flood frequency if media stays too wet.
tasks.name: Monitor seedling health

transition_signals: Roots visible at bottom of pot (if net pots)

common_problems.cause: Flooding too frequently for the media type, or poor drainage
common_problems.issue: Media staying too wet between floods
common_problems.solution: Reduce flood frequency. Check that pots have adequate drainage holes. Verify table drains completely. Switch to a faster-draining media if persistent.

common_problems.cause: Nutrient solution wicking up and evaporating, depositing salts on media surface
common_problems.solution: Top-water with plain pH'd water occasionally to flush surface salts down. Happens more with perlite and coco.

common_problems.cause: Table not perfectly level — some plants getting deeper floods than others
common_problems.solution: Re-level the table. Even small variations (1/8 inch) cause uneven flood depth across a 4-foot table.

common_problems.cause: Pump undersized for table volume, or tubing too small
common_problems.issue: Pump not filling table in time
common_problems.solution: Upgrade pump. Table should fill to target height in 5-10 minutes max. Use larger tubing if restricted.

stages.description: Rapid early vegetative growth. Roots are expanding into the media. Flood frequency increases as roots grow and water demand rises. This is where the flood table's versatility shines — you can mix pot sizes and even media types on the same table.

environment.notes: Increase light intensity for veg growth. Plants are establishing — flood frequency ramps up.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: may need more frequent floods in warm conditions as media dries faster.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse: adjust flood frequency based on temperature.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor early veg: natural photoperiod controls veg length. Rain cover over flood table is mandatory.

extra_problems.cause: Standing water, warm moist environment attracts fungus gnats
