extra_problems.solution: Ensure complete drainage. No standing water between floods. Yellow sticky traps. Mosquito dunks in reservoir if gnats persist.

extra_tasks.description: Verify rain hasn't overwhelmed the table. Check reservoir EC — rain dilutes it.
extra_tasks.name: Check table after rain

outdoor.notes: Outdoor Ebb & Flow: rain cover and pest management are essential.

coco.notes: Coco holds moisture. 2 floods/day. Let top of coco dry slightly between floods.

hydroton.notes: Hydroton drains completely. 4 floods/day during lights-on. Don't let hydroton dry out completely — roots are growing.

perlite.notes: Perlite dries moderately. 3 floods/day. Balanced between hydroton and rockwool.

rockwool.notes: Rockwool still retains well. 2 floods/day. Watch for waterlogging — reduce to 1 if cubes feel heavy.

flood_cycle.notes: Increase flood frequency from seedling stage. Flood height reaches 2-3 inches — about 2/3 up the media in the pot. Root zone gets fully saturated during floods, then oxygenated during drain. This wet/dry cycle is the core advantage of Ebb & Flow.

health_checks: Roots white and growing out pot bottoms?
No standing water on table between floods?
stages.name: Early Veg

nutrients.approach: Half strength. Plants are actively growing and consuming nutrients during each flood. Ebb & Flow's periodic feeding is efficient — roots absorb during floods and oxygenate during drain.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root protection. Warm flood tables are prime territory for Pythium.

reservoir.notes: Plants consuming more nutrients now. Read pH/EC 30 minutes after flood drain-back for most accurate readings. Aerate reservoir between floods.

tasks.description: Bump from seedling 1-2x to early veg 2-4x per day. Adjust based on media type — see media-specific recommendations.
tasks.name: Increase flood frequency

tasks.description: Read reservoir 30 minutes after a flood. Adjust as needed.
tasks.name: Check pH/EC after drain-back

tasks.description: After every flood, ALL water must drain back. Check for pooling. Check drain fitting for clogs.
tasks.name: Verify complete drainage

tasks.description: Full change every 7 days.
tasks.name: Reservoir change

tasks.description: Media should be moist but not soaking between floods. If still soggy when next flood hits, reduce frequency. If bone dry, increase frequency.
tasks.name: Check media moisture between floods

tasks.description: Lift a pot and check roots. White roots growing out the bottom = healthy. Brown/slimy = reduce flood frequency or treat.
tasks.name: Inspect roots

training.description: Bend and tie down main stem. Ebb & Flow pots provide good anchoring — tie to pot rim.

transition_signals: Roots growing through pot drainage holes

common_problems.cause: Roots growing out of pots and across the table toward the drain
common_problems.solution: Gently trim roots near the drain fitting. Elevate pots on risers to keep root growth away from the drain. Install a drain guard screen.

common_problems.cause: Mechanical timer tab broke, or digital timer glitch
common_problems.solution: Install an overflow fitting as a failsafe — it prevents water from rising above a set height even if the pump runs continuously. Check timer weekly. Consider digital timer with backup.

common_problems.cause: Timer malfunction, power outage, or tripped breaker
common_problems.solution: Plants survive hours without a flood (Ebb & Flow advantage over NFT), but fix immediately. Run a manual flood while diagnosing. Check power and timer.

common_problems.cause: Media affecting pH during flood (especially new rockwool or hydroton), or small reservoir relative to table volume
common_problems.solution: Pre-soak new media in pH 5.5 water. Use a larger reservoir (2-3x table volume). Let media stabilize over first few floods.

stages.description: Rapid vegetative growth. Plants double or triple in size. Flood frequency reaches peak veg levels. Root systems fill pots and start growing out drainage holes onto the table surface. This is normal for Ebb & Flow — roots on the table get fed during floods and oxygenated between them.

environment.notes: Full veg light intensity. Plants growing fast. Flood frequency at veg maximum. Monitor table drainage — roots growing on table can partially block the drain fitting.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse veg: excellent growth. May need more frequent floods in warm conditions.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse: monitor media moisture closely in warm weather.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor veg: natural photoperiod. May need more frequent floods on hot days as media dries faster.

extra_problems.cause: Outdoor environment + moist media = gnat paradise
extra_problems.solution: Yellow sticky traps. Mosquito dunks (BT) in reservoir. Let media surface dry between floods.

extra_tasks.description: Above 85°F, add 1-2 extra floods. Media dries much faster in heat.
extra_tasks.name: Increase floods on hot days

outdoor.notes: Outdoor: adjust flood frequency for weather conditions.

coco.notes: Coco: 3 floods/day. Coco holds moisture well but large veg plants drink heavily.

hydroton.notes: Peak veg hydroton: flood every 3 hours during lights-on (6 floods/18hr). Hydroton dries fast with large plants.

perlite.notes: Perlite: 4 floods/day. Every 4.5 hours during lights-on.

rockwool.notes: Rockwool: 3 floods/day. Morning, midday, afternoon. Don't over-flood — rockwool stays wet.

flood_cycle.notes: Peak veg flood frequency. The exact number depends heavily on media type, pot size, plant size, and room temperature. Watch the media — if it's dry before the next flood, add a flood. If it's still soaking wet, remove one. No night floods in veg.

health_checks: No standing water on table between floods?
stages.name: Late Veg

nutrients.approach: Three-quarter strength for active veg growth. Nitrogen-heavy ratio. Ebb & Flow's periodic feeding allows roots to absorb then breathe — very efficient nutrient uptake.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root zone protection. Flood tables stay warm and moist between floods.

supplements.name: Silica (Armor Si)
supplements.purpose: Strengthen stems for heavy flower later.

reservoir.notes: Plants consuming more. Reservoir level drops faster between changes. Top off between changes. Read pH/EC after drain-back.

tasks.description: Daily. Read 30 minutes after flood. Vigorous veg = fast EC changes.
tasks.name: Check pH/EC after drain-back

tasks.description: Check after each flood. Roots growing on table can block drain fitting. Clear any root growth near the drain.
tasks.name: Verify complete drainage

tasks.description: Watch media moisture. Increase floods if media is drying out between cycles. Decrease if staying too wet.
tasks.name: Adjust flood frequency

tasks.description: Full change every 7 days. Flood table systems accumulate salt deposits on the table surface.
tasks.name: Reservoir change

tasks.description: Wipe down exposed table surface to remove salt deposits and algae during reservoir changes.
tasks.name: Clean table surface

tasks.description: Top plants and train to fill the table canopy. Ebb & Flow pots provide good anchor points.
tasks.name: Training (LST/topping)

tasks.description: Verify timer is cycling correctly. Stuck ON = continuous flood (overflow risk). Stuck OFF = drought.
tasks.name: Check timer reliability

training.description: Cut main stem above 5th node. Creates two main colas.
Bend and tie branches to pot rims. Ebb & Flow pots are excellent LST anchor points.
Trellis net over flood table creates even canopy.

common_problems.cause: Genetics (sativa-dominant) or too much light/dark temp difference
common_problems.solution: Supercrop: gently pinch and bend stems at 90 degrees. Ebb & Flow pots provide good support for recovery.

common_problems.cause: Fast-draining media + large stretching plants + 12hr dark period
common_problems.solution: Add one night flood 4-6 hours into dark period. This is normal for hydroton with large plants.

common_problems.cause: Light leak during dark period
common_problems.solution: Check EVERY light source during dark period. Indicator LEDs, timer LEDs, light leaks through vents. Even brief exposure prevents flowering.

stages.description: Light flips to 12/12. The plant stretches 50-200% in height. Flood frequency adjusts — water demand increases during stretch but you may want to start adding a night flood to support the rapid growth. Nutrient ratio shifts from nitrogen to phosphorus/potassium.

environment.notes: Switch to 12/12. Dark period must be UNINTERRUPTED. Stretch will be dramatic — have support structures ready.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: use blackout curtains for 12-hour dark periods.

extra_tasks.description: Ensure 12 hours of complete darkness.
extra_tasks.name: Deploy blackout curtains

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse: blackout system critical for reliable flowering.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor: natural photoperiod triggers flowering. Transition is gradual over 2-3 weeks.

extra_tasks.description: Street lights, porch lights can disrupt flowering. Shield plants from artificial light at night.
extra_tasks.name: Protect from light pollution

outdoor.notes: Outdoor: natural photoperiod triggers transition more gradually.

coco.notes: Coco: 3 during lights-on. Coco retains through the night.

hydroton.notes: Hydroton: 4 during lights-on + 1 night flood during stretch. Plants drink heavily.

rockwool.notes: Rockwool: 3 during lights-on. No night flood needed — rockwool retains enough.

flood_cycle.notes: Transition introduces the first night flood for fast-draining media (hydroton, perlite). Stretching plants drink heavily. Adjust frequency based on media drying speed. Monitor media moisture at end of dark period — if bone dry, add a night flood.

health_checks: Night flood needed? (Check media at end of dark period)
No light leaks during dark period?
Timer cycling correctly with new schedule?
stages.name: Transition (Flip to Flower)

nutrients.approach: Shift ratio: reduce nitrogen (FloraGro), increase phosphorus/potassium (FloraBloom). Transition is gradual.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root protection through transition.

reservoir.notes: Transition nutrient mix — shift from grow to bloom. Plants consuming heavily during stretch.

tasks.description: Set timer to 12 on / 12 off. Verify dark period is complete darkness — any light leak can prevent flowering.
tasks.name: Switch to 12/12 light

tasks.description: For fast-draining media (hydroton, perlite): check media moisture at end of dark period. If bone dry, add one night flood 4-6 hours into dark period.
tasks.name: Consider adding night flood

tasks.description: Daily. Nutrient ratio shifting. pH may swing more.
tasks.name: Check pH/EC

tasks.description: Plants grow 2-4 inches/day during stretch. Trellis net or yoyos essential.
tasks.name: Support stretching branches

tasks.description: Transition from veg to bloom nutrient ratio.
tasks.name: Reservoir change

tasks.description: Verify timer is set correctly for the new flood schedule (including any night floods). Double-check dark period has no unintended floods with lights.
tasks.name: Check timer programming

training.description: Pinch and bend stems at 90 degrees. Heals with reinforced knuckle.
Remove all growth below bottom 1/3 of plant. Directs energy to top buds.

common_problems.cause: Bloom nutrients depositing from flood/drain cycles
common_problems.issue: Salt crust on table surface and pot rims
common_problems.solution: Clean table during every reservoir change. Rinse pot rims. Top-water pots with plain pH'd water occasionally to flush salts through.

common_problems.cause: That pot staying too wet (poor drainage), or sitting in a low spot on the table
common_problems.solution: Check pot drainage holes. Re-level table. Treat reservoir with H2O2 (3ml/gal) for 24 hours, then re-dose Hydroguard.

common_problems.cause: Root growth, media debris, or salt buildup in drain fitting
common_problems.solution: Install a drain guard screen. Clear fitting at every reservoir change. Trim roots growing near drain.

common_problems.cause: Overflow fitting shifting, or pump performance changing
common_problems.solution: Check overflow fitting is at correct height. Check pump output — impeller may need cleaning.

stages.description: Stretch ends, bud sites established, first pistils (white hairs) visible. Nutrient demand shifts fully to bloom. Flood frequency may decrease slightly from peak veg — flowering plants prefer slightly drier conditions between floods. Night floods continue for fast-draining media.

environment.notes: Increase light intensity. Drop humidity below 55% — bud rot prevention starts now. Ebb & Flow's wet/dry cycling keeps roots healthy and oxygenated during flower.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: run dehumidifier to keep humidity below 55%.

extra_tasks.description: Keep humidity below 55% during flower.
extra_tasks.name: Run dehumidifier

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse Ebb & Flow in flower: dehumidification is critical.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor early flower: rain protection mandatory. Rain flooding the table disrupts nutrient levels and can cause overflow.

extra_problems.cause: Moths laying eggs on flowers
extra_problems.solution: BT (Bacillus thuringiensis) spray weekly. Inspect buds for frass.

extra_tasks.description: Rain on an outdoor flood table is a major problem — check reservoir EC and table drainage after any rain.
extra_tasks.name: Check table after rain

outdoor.notes: Outdoor Ebb & Flow in flower: rain protection is non-negotiable.

coco.notes: Coco: 3 during lights-on. Coco retains through night.

hydroton.notes: Hydroton: 4 during lights-on + 1 night. Flowering plants drink heavily but prefer drier between floods than veg.

rockwool.notes: Rockwool: 3 during lights-on. No night flood. Rockwool retains plenty.

flood_cycle.notes: Flower flood frequency is similar to late veg but the goal shifts: allow more dryback between floods. Flowering plants prefer a slightly drier root zone — this stress-signal promotes flower production. Don't eliminate floods; just let media dry more between them.

health_checks: Bud sites developing at all nodes?
stages.name: Early Flower

nutrients.approach: Full bloom transition. Heavy phosphorus and potassium. Reduce nitrogen. Ebb & Flow's periodic feeding delivers nutrients then lets roots oxygenate — excellent for bud development.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root protection during flower.

supplements.name: PK Booster (Liquid Koolbloom)
supplements.purpose: Start PK supplementation as bud sites develop.

reservoir.notes: Switch to 5-day reservoir changes during flower. Bloom nutrients cause more salt buildup on table and media surfaces.

tasks.description: Daily. Bloom nutrients and heavy feeding cause faster pH/EC changes.
tasks.name: Check pH/EC after drain-back

tasks.description: Check after floods. Root growth on table may be blocking drain. Clear as needed.
tasks.name: Verify complete drainage

tasks.description: Every 5 days. Wipe down table surface during change to remove salt deposits.
tasks.name: Reservoir change + table cleaning

tasks.description: Lift a pot. Roots should be white with healthy branching. Brown/slimy = root rot starting.
tasks.name: Inspect roots

tasks.description: Day 21 of flower: remove large fan leaves blocking bud sites. Improves light penetration and airflow.
tasks.name: Defoliation (Day 21)

tasks.description: Verify overflow fitting is clear and at correct height. A stuck-on pump during flower can damage plants.
tasks.name: Check overflow fitting

training.description: Remove large fan leaves blocking bud sites. Opens canopy for light and airflow.

common_problems.cause: Peak flower consumption from multiple large plants
common_problems.solution: Top off daily. Consider a larger reservoir (3x table volume). Auto-top-off float valve helps.

common_problems.cause: Concentrated bloom nutrients depositing from repeated flood/drain cycles
common_problems.issue: Heavy salt crust on table and pots
common_problems.solution: Clean table at every reservoir change. Top-water pots weekly with plain water. Flush between floods.

common_problems.cause: Humidity pocket, dense bud, poor airflow
common_problems.solution: Remove ALL affected material (cut 2 inches below rot). Increase airflow. Lower humidity.

common_problems.cause: Aggressive root growth on table reaching drain
common_problems.issue: Drain fitting completely blocked by roots
common_problems.solution: Install a drain guard cage. Trim roots near drain. Elevate pots slightly on risers.

stages.description: Peak bud production. Buds swell rapidly. Water and nutrient consumption hits maximum. Flood frequency stays at flower levels but water consumption per flood increases — reservoir depletes faster. Salt buildup on the table and media accelerates with heavy bloom nutrients.

environment.notes: Maximum light intensity. Drop humidity to 45%. Temperature differential (day/night) enhances terpene and color production.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: run dehumidifier 24/7 during mid flower.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse: dehumidification is critical.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor: rain protection mandatory. Rain on flood table disrupts nutrient levels.

extra_problems.cause: Outdoor humidity + dew on dense buds
extra_problems.solution: Shake plants mornings. Add fans if power available.

extra_tasks.description: Remove moisture from buds each morning.
extra_tasks.name: Shake plants after rain/dew

outdoor.notes: Outdoor mid flower: dew and rain are primary threats.

coco.notes: Coco: 2-3 during lights-on + 1 night. Coco may finally need night floods during peak flower.

hydroton.notes: Hydroton: 4 during lights-on + 1 night. Peak water demand. May need to top off reservoir between changes.

rockwool.notes: Rockwool: 2-3 during lights-on + 1 night flood. Even rockwool dries faster now with large flowering plants.

flood_cycle.notes: Most media types now benefit from a night flood during peak flower. Plants are consuming heavily. Allow good dryback between daytime floods — flowering plants prefer periodic stress. Reservoir depletes faster — top off between changes.

stages.name: Mid Flower (Bulk Phase)

nutrients.approach: Full bloom strength. Maximum phosphorus and potassium. Slight tip burn = plants at maximum capacity (optimal).

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Non-negotiable during flower.

supplements.name: PK Booster (Liquid Koolbloom)
supplements.purpose: Peak PK supplementation for bud density.

reservoir.notes: Peak consumption. Reservoir level drops fast between changes. Top off daily. Change every 5 days. Clean table surface at each change.

tasks.description: Daily. Peak consumption means fast EC drops.
tasks.name: Check pH/EC after drain-back

tasks.description: May need daily top-offs. Large flowering plants consume heavily.
tasks.name: Top off reservoir

tasks.description: Every 5 days. Wipe table surface to remove salt deposits. Bloom nutrients deposit heavily.
tasks.name: Reservoir change + table cleaning

tasks.description: Check all bud sites. Dense canopies trap humidity. Part dense buds and look inside.
tasks.name: Bud rot inspection

tasks.description: Daily. Root growth on table can block drain. Critical during flower — standing water causes root rot.
tasks.name: Verify complete drainage

tasks.description: Install SCROG net, plant yoyos, or bamboo stakes. Heavy buds need support.
tasks.name: Support heavy branches

tasks.description: Once per week, hand-water each pot from the top with plain pH'd water to flush salt buildup down through the media.
tasks.name: Top-water pots with plain water

training.description: Final light defoliation. Remove only leaves blocking bud sites.

common_problems.cause: Light stress or heat stress
common_problems.solution: Raise light slightly. Lower room temp.

common_problems.cause: Dense buds + humidity spike
common_problems.solution: Harvest immediately if rot spreads. Better slightly early than losing buds.

stages.description: Final bud maturation and trichome ripening. Reduce nutrients. Trichomes transition from clear→cloudy→amber. Flood frequency can decrease slightly — reduced nutrient strength means less salt buildup. Allow more dryback between floods.

environment.notes: Cooler temps + large day/night differential enhances color and terpene production.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: cool fall nights enhance colors and terpenes.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse: cool nights beneficial if above freezing.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor: watch for early frost. Harvest before first frost.

extra_problems.cause: Late-season temperature drop
extra_problems.solution: Harvest immediately or protect with row covers. Bring pots inside if possible — Ebb & Flow pots are portable.

extra_tasks.description: Frost kills plants. Harvest before first frost or cover with row covers.
extra_tasks.name: Monitor frost forecast

outdoor.notes: Outdoor: Ebb & Flow advantage — pots can be brought inside for emergencies.

coco.notes: Coco: 2 during lights-on. Coco retains through night at reduced nute levels.

hydroton.notes: Hydroton: 3 during lights-on + 1 night. Slightly reduced from peak.

rockwool.notes: Rockwool: 2 during lights-on. Reduced nutes = less salt, rockwool retains well.

flood_cycle.notes: Reduce flood frequency slightly. Plants are finishing — they consume less. More dryback between floods promotes final trichome production. Continue night floods only for fast-draining media.

stages.name: Late Flower (Ripening)

nutrients.approach: Reduced strength. Taper down nutrients. Stop PK boosters. Yellowing fan leaves is normal and desired.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Continue until flush begins.

reservoir.notes: Reduce nutrient strength. Plants finishing. Continue 5-day reservoir changes.

tasks.description: 60x loupe or digital microscope. Clear = not ready. Milky = peak THC. Amber = more sedative. Most harvest at 10-20% amber.
tasks.name: Check trichomes daily

tasks.description: Daily. Reduced nutes, pH management continues.
tasks.name: Check pH/EC after drain-back

tasks.description: Continue 5-day changes.
tasks.name: Reservoir change + table cleaning

tasks.description: Dense mature buds are most susceptible. Check all plants daily.
tasks.name: Bud rot patrol

tasks.description: Yellow/dying leaves are normal. Remove to prevent mold.
tasks.name: Remove dying fan leaves

common_problems.cause: Salt deposits in media not fully flushing with floods alone
common_problems.issue: EC of reservoir not dropping to near-zero
common_problems.solution: Top-water pots from above with plain water. Increase flood frequency. Extend flush duration.

common_problems.cause: Normal — plant finishing its life cycle
common_problems.solution: Normal. Maintain flood schedule. Don't add nutrients.

stages.description: Run plain pH'd water through the flood table. No nutrients. Ebb & Flow flush is effective — each flood/drain cycle washes salts from the media. Multiple floods per day with plain water progressively removes nutrients from root zone.

environment.notes: Maintain 12/12. Environment stays the same. Continue bud rot monitoring.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: maintain ventilation. Dying leaves harbor mold.

greenhouse.notes: Remove fallen leaves promptly.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor: rain actually helps during flush.

outdoor.notes: Rain acceptable during flush — aids flushing.

coco.notes: Coco: increase to 4 floods/day + 1 night. Coco holds salts — needs aggressive flushing.

hydroton.notes: Hydroton: increase floods during flush. More floods = faster salt removal from fast-draining media.

rockwool.notes: Rockwool: 3 floods/day + 1 night. More than usual to actively flush salts from the retentive media.

flood_cycle.notes: INCREASE flood frequency during flush — opposite of normal flower guidance. Each plain-water flood washes salts from the media. More floods = faster, more thorough flush. Also top-water pots with plain water every 2-3 days to flush from the top down.

health_checks: Reservoir EC rising after floods? (Salts flushing — good.)
stages.name: Flush

nutrients.approach: Zero nutrients. Plain pH'd water. Each flood/drain cycle progressively removes salts from media.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Continue through flush. Root rot in final week wastes the entire grow.

reservoir.notes: Plain pH'd water only. Change every 3 days — drain-back water carries salts flushed from media. Monitor EC of reservoir after floods — rising EC means salts are being flushed out (good). Continue Hydroguard.

tasks.description: Every 3 days. Drain reservoir, refill with plain pH'd water. The reservoir EC will rise as salts flush from media.
tasks.name: Drain and refill reservoir with plain water

tasks.description: Every 2-3 days, hand-water each pot from the top with plain pH'd water to flush salts from the upper media that floods don't reach.
tasks.name: Top-water pots with plain water

tasks.description: Check EC after floods — rising EC means salts flushing from media (good). When EC stops rising, flush is working.
tasks.name: Monitor reservoir EC

tasks.description: Plants continue maturing during flush.
tasks.name: Continue trichome checks

tasks.description: Continue daily checks.
tasks.name: Monitor for bud rot

tasks.description: Sharp trimming scissors, drying rack/lines, mason jars, hygrometers, Boveda packs.
tasks.name: Prepare harvest equipment

transition_signals: Reservoir EC stays low after floods

common_problems.cause: Hidden rot inside dense colas
common_problems.solution: Cut 1+ inches beyond rot. Discard affected material. Inspect remaining buds.

common_problems.cause: Months of bloom nutrients depositing
common_problems.solution: Soak with vinegar solution for 1 hour. Scrub with brush. H2O2 solution for sanitization.

stages.description: Cut plants, trim, and hang to dry. Ebb & Flow harvest advantage: individual pots can be removed from the table independently. You can harvest plants at different times — just remove the ready pots and keep flooding the rest. Very flexible harvest scheduling.

environment.notes: Optional 24-48 hours of darkness before harvest. Cut at base. Pots lift right off the table.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse can serve as drying space if humidity is controllable.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse: usable as drying space with ventilation.

environment_overrides.notes: Harvest before frost. Ebb & Flow advantage: pots are portable — bring inside if frost threatens.

outdoor.notes: Outdoor: pot portability is a major Ebb & Flow advantage.

flood_cycle.notes: Stop floods after last plant is removed. If harvesting in stages, continue flooding for remaining plants.

health_checks: Trichomes at target maturity when cut?
Any bud rot discovered during trimming?
stages.name: Harvest

nutrients.approach: None.

reservoir.notes: Drain reservoir after last harvest. Clean table, fittings, reservoir, and pump.

tasks.description: 24-48 hours of darkness before harvest.
tasks.name: Optional dark period

tasks.description: Remove pots from table. Cut plants at base. Ebb & Flow advantage: pots are portable — harvest one at a time over multiple days if needed.
tasks.name: Harvest plants

tasks.description: Wet trim: trim immediately. Dry trim: hang whole plants. Wet is faster; dry often produces better flavor.
tasks.name: Wet trim or whole-plant hang

tasks.description: Remove all pots. Scrub table surface — salt deposits, root debris, and algae accumulate over the grow. Clean drain and overflow fittings.
tasks.name: Clean flood table

tasks.description: Disassemble pump, clean impeller. Scrub reservoir. Replace tubing if degraded.
tasks.name: Clean pump and reservoir

tasks.description: Check drain fitting, overflow fitting, and pump connections for wear or cracks.
tasks.name: Inspect fittings

common_problems.cause: Temp too high, humidity too low, fans on buds
common_problems.solution: Lower temp. Raise humidity. No direct airflow on buds.

common_problems.cause: Humidity too high, poor airflow
common_problems.solution: Remove moldy material. Increase ventilation. Lower humidity.

stages.description: Hang whole plants or branches in a dark, cool, ventilated space. Target slow dry over 10-14 days.

environment.notes: Complete darkness. Gentle airflow (not on buds). 60°F / 60% humidity. Slower dry = better cure.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse NOT ideal for drying. Dry indoors.

greenhouse.notes: Avoid drying in greenhouse.

environment_overrides.notes: Dry indoors. Never dry outdoors.

outdoor.notes: Always dry indoors.

stages.name: Drying

nutrients.approach: None.

reservoir.notes: System cleaned and stored.

tasks.description: Small stems snap (not bend) when dry. Large stems still slightly flexible. 7-14 days. Do NOT rush.
tasks.name: Check drying progress

tasks.description: Keep 60-65°F and 55-65% humidity.
tasks.name: Monitor temp and humidity

tasks.description: Inspect hanging buds daily.
tasks.name: Check for mold

transition_signals: Outside of bud dry to touch

common_problems.cause: Chlorophyll breaking down. Normal first 1-2 weeks.
common_problems.solution: Continue curing. Terpenes emerge after 2-3 weeks.

common_problems.cause: Buds jarred too wet.
common_problems.solution: Remove buds, dry on paper bag 12-24 hours. Re-jar.

common_problems.cause: Over-dried or low jar humidity.
common_problems.solution: Add Boveda 62% pack. Rehydrates in 3-5 days.

stages.description: Place dried buds in mason jars. Burp daily for first 2 weeks, then weekly. Curing converts chlorophyll into smooth compounds and develops terpenes. Minimum 2-week cure; 4-8 weeks ideal.

environment.notes: Cool, dark place. Boveda 62% packs. Light degrades THC.

environment_overrides.notes: Do not cure in greenhouse — temp swings degrade quality.

greenhouse.notes: Cure indoors.

environment_overrides.notes: Cure indoors. Same process regardless of grow environment.

outdoor.notes: Environment-independent.

stages.name: Curing

nutrients.approach: None.

reservoir.notes: N/A.

tasks.description: Open jars 5-15 min. Week 1-2: daily. Week 3-4: every 2-3 days. After week 4: weekly. Ammonia = too wet.
tasks.name: Burp jars

tasks.description: Mini hygrometer per jar. Target 58-65%.
tasks.name: Check jar humidity

tasks.description: Check when burping. Any mold = remove immediately.
tasks.name: Inspect for mold

container_progression.commercial_medium: Turkey bags in sealed bins, nitrogen-flushed
container_progression.industrial: Climate-controlled vault, nitrogen generators, automated monitoring

degradation_timeline.18_plus_months: Convert to extracts, edibles, or topicals.

testing_schedule.360_days: Full panel — sell, process, or destroy decision

common_problems.cause: Heat, light, oxygen, or time
common_problems.solution: Darkness, cool temps (60°F), minimal oxygen. ~5%/year baseline.

common_problems.cause: Temps above 70°F, oxygen, frequent opening
common_problems.solution: Below 65°F. Nitrogen-sealed. Minimize opening.

common_problems.cause: Humidity above 65% or improper cure
common_problems.solution: Verify 58-62% RH before sealing. Remove affected material.

common_problems.cause: Normal moisture equilibration
common_problems.solution: Boveda packs. Sealed containers.

stages.description: Post-cure long-term storage. Ebb & Flow systems produce consistent yields cycle after cycle — inventory accumulates fast with perpetual harvests. Storage planning is critical for commercial operations running continuous flood tables. Proper storage preserves potency and terpenes for 6-12+ months.

environment.notes: DARK. Cool. Stable. Zero light — UV destroys cannabinoids and terpenes. Commercial: 58-62°F, 55-60% RH, nitrogen atmosphere.

environment_overrides.notes: Do NOT store in greenhouse.

greenhouse.notes: Climate-controlled indoor space only.

environment_overrides.notes: Storage is always indoor.

outdoor.notes: Indoor controlled environment.

stages.name: Long-Term Storage

nutrients.approach: None.

tasks.description: Home: mason jars with Boveda 58-62%. Commercial: nitrogen-sealed grove bags, CVault, or nitrogen-flushed drums.
tasks.name: Transfer to long-term containers

tasks.description: Strain, harvest date, storage date, weight, batch number. Commercial: seed-to-sale, FIFO rotation.
tasks.name: Label and track batches

tasks.description: Inspect for mold, off odors. Check humidity packs. Commercial: potency/terpene testing at 30/90/180 days.
tasks.name: Monthly quality checks

tasks.description: Monitor vault temp/humidity. No light leaks. Commercial: automated alerts.
tasks.name: Maintain environment

tasks.description: First in, first out. Flag batches approaching 12 months.
tasks.name: Rotate stock (FIFO)

tasks.description: Commercial: retain samples per regulations.
tasks.name: Compliance testing holds

autoflower.description: Ruderalis-hybrid strains that flower automatically regardless of light schedule. Fixed timeline.

light_schedule.flower: 20/4 or 18/6 (same as veg — no change needed)
light_schedule.transition: Automatic — plant decides when to flower (usually week 3-4)

autoflower.name: Autoflower
autoflower.notes: Fixed timeline — stress cannot be recovered by extending veg. Start in final pot (no transplant shock). LST preferred over HST.

training_suitability.defoliation: light only — auto can't recover from heavy defol
training_suitability.topping: risky — only if plant is vigorous

photoperiod.description: Standard cannabis strains that require 12/12 light to flower. Grower controls timing.

light_schedule.transition: Switch to 12/12 when plant is ~50% of desired final height

photoperiod.name: Photoperiod
photoperiod.notes: Can be vegged as long as desired. Cloneable. More forgiving of stress (can extend veg to recover).

training_suitability.defoliation: good
training_suitability.topping: excellent

total_grow_days.breakdown: Germination (3-7d) + Seedling (7-14d) + Veg (24-56d) + Flower (56-70d) + Dry (7-14d) + Cure (14-60d)

causes: Table not level (water pools at low spots)
Drain fitting clogged with roots or media
solutions: Re-level the table (check BOTH directions)
Clear drain fitting — remove root debris and media particles
Check drain tubing for kinks, fix routing
Install a siphon break on the drain line if gravity drain is slow
problems.symptom: Table not draining completely — standing water after pump shuts off

causes: Heavy pots causing table to flex/sag
solutions: Re-level with spirit level in both directions
Add center support under table to prevent sag from heavy pots
Use shims to fine-tune level

causes: Drain partially blocked causing water to rise higher
problems.diagnosis: Flood height set too high, or overflow fitting too low
solutions: Reduce pump flow rate
Raise overflow fitting height (but never above 2/3 media level)
Clear drain fitting to improve outflow during fill
problems.symptom: Overflow fitting activating during normal floods

solutions: Overflow fitting should prevent disaster (this is why it's required)
Replace timer immediately. Switch to digital if using mechanical
Check timer programming after any power outage
Keep a backup timer ready
problems.symptom: Timer stuck ON — pump running continuously

solutions: Ebb & Flow plants survive hours without a flood (much more forgiving than NFT)
Run a manual flood immediately while diagnosing
Check power, breaker, timer. Replace timer if faulty
Consider a smart plug with monitoring that alerts on power loss
problems.symptom: Timer stuck OFF — no floods happening

solutions: Clean pump impeller
Check for and bleed air locks in supply tubing
Upgrade to larger pump if undersized
Replace aging pump — they lose capacity over time
problems.symptom: Pump not filling table to target height

causes: Flood frequency too low for hydroton's fast drainage
Small pots with minimal media volume
problems.diagnosis: Not enough floods for hydroton, or pots too small
solutions: Increase flood frequency (hydroton needs 4-6 floods/day in veg, plus night floods in flower)
Use larger pots (3-5 gallon) for more media buffer
Consider mixing perlite into hydroton for slightly better retention
problems.symptom: Hydroton drying out too fast between floods

causes: Too many floods per day for rockwool
Rockwool cubes sitting in standing water on table
Flood height too high for rockwool's retention
solutions: Reduce flood frequency (rockwool needs only 1-3 floods/day)
Elevate rockwool on risers so it's not sitting in any residual water
Lower flood height — rockwool wicks water upward, doesn't need deep flooding
problems.symptom: Rockwool staying too wet, roots browning

causes: Nutrients wicking up through coco and evaporating at surface
problems.diagnosis: Normal wicking action but needs management
solutions: Top-water with plain pH'd water every 3-5 days to flush surface salts down
Cover coco surface with a layer of hydroton to reduce evaporation
This is cosmetic unless severe — EC at root level is what matters
problems.symptom: Coco developing salt crust on surface

cons: RO system cost and waste water
water_source_profiles.name: Reverse Osmosis (RO) Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Gold standard for precision growing. Always add CalMag first when using RO.
pros: Blank slate — total control over mineral content

dechlorination.chloramine: Does NOT gas off — must use Campden tablets or activated carbon filter
dechlorination.chlorine: Let sit uncovered 24 hours (gasses off) or use dechlorinator drops
dechlorination.how_to_tell: Check city water report. Most modern cities use chloramine.

water_source_profiles.name: Municipal Tap Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Good enough for most grows. Dechlorinate and pH-adjust. Get a water report from your city.

cons: May contain iron, sulfur, or heavy metals
water_source_profiles.name: Well Water
water_source_profiles.notes: MUST get a water test before using. High iron or sulfur can cause lockout. Great if your well has clean water with moderate minerals.

water_source_profiles.name: Distilled Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Same as RO but purchased instead of filtered. Only practical for 1-2 plant grows.

advanced_techniques.description: Elevating CO2 from ambient (400ppm) to 1000-1500ppm increases photosynthesis rate by 20-40% when light and temperature are also increased.

methods.notes: Most common. Tank refills every 2-4 weeks for a tent.
Produces heat and humidity — better for large rooms with AC.
Produces ~100-200ppm boost. Only useful for small sealed spaces. Not worth it for most.

advanced_techniques.name: CO2 Supplementation

ppfd_co2_correlation.notes: Ambient CO2 sufficient at low light
Slight benefit from supplementation
Moderate benefit — cost-effective threshold
Strong benefit — diminishing returns above this
Maximum practical benefit for most grows
Commercial intensity — requires perfect environment

requirements: Sealed grow space (CO2 escapes quickly from leaky rooms)
High light intensity (>600 PPFD — plants can't use extra CO2 without sufficient light)
Higher temperature tolerance (82-86°F optimal with CO2 vs 75-80°F without)
CO2 controller or timer

advanced_techniques.description: Manipulating environmental and irrigation parameters to push the plant toward vegetative or generative (flowering/fruiting) growth.
advanced_techniques.name: Crop Steering
advanced_techniques.notes: Crop steering is most effective in hydro/coco where irrigation frequency is highly controllable. Soil growers have less precision due to soil buffering.

vegetative_steering.goal: More leaf/stem growth, larger plant framework

best_practices: Spray during lights-off or first/last 30 minutes of light cycle (stomata open, no light burn)
Use fine mist sprayer — coverage is key
pH foliar solution to 6.2-6.5 (leaf surface slightly higher pH than root zone)
EC should be very low (0.2-0.5) — leaves burn easily
Never foliar spray during flower (moisture in buds → mold/bud rot)
Best for micronutrient deficiency correction (CalMag, iron, magnesium)

common_foliar_products.frequency: Weekly preventive, 3-day intervals for active infestations

advanced_techniques.description: Spraying nutrient solution directly onto leaves for rapid uptake. Bypasses root zone entirely.
advanced_techniques.name: Foliar Feeding

advanced_techniques.description: Strengthens cell walls, increases stem thickness, improves heat/drought/pest resistance.
important_notes: ALWAYS add silica to water FIRST before other nutrients (raises pH significantly)
Let silica mix for 5-10 minutes before adding base nutrients
Silica is incompatible with concentrated nutrient solutions — never mix undiluted
Discontinue in late flower (last 2-3 weeks)
advanced_techniques.name: Silica (Potassium Silicate)

advanced_techniques.description: Introduces beneficial bacteria/fungi to the root zone to outcompete pathogens, improve nutrient uptake, and protect against root diseases.
important_notes: Do NOT use hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) with beneficial microbes — it kills them
Choose either sterile (H2O2) OR beneficial (Hydroguard) approach — not both
Beneficials need warmish water (65-75°F) to thrive
Reapply after any reservoir change or system cleaning
advanced_techniques.name: Beneficial Microbes & Root Inoculants

products.name: Hydroguard
products.notes: The standard for DWC/RDWC. Add at every res change.

products.dosing: 1 tsp/gal (water-in) or dust roots at transplant
products.name: Great White
products.notes: Best for soil/coco. Less effective in pure hydro.

products.name: Recharge
products.notes: Feeds existing soil microbes. Great for living soil.

products.name: Mammoth P
products.notes: Most effective in flower when P demand is highest.

advanced_techniques.description: Removing select fan leaves to improve light penetration, airflow, and direct energy to bud sites.
advanced_techniques.name: Defoliation (Strategic Leaf Removal)
rules: Never remove more than 20-30% of leaves at once (except schwazze technique)
Only defoliate healthy, vigorous plants — never stressed ones
Autoflowers: very light defoliation only — they can't recover from heavy removal
Always leave the top 2-3 fan leaves on each branch (they feed the bud below)

timing.method: Remove lower leaves that get no light (lollipop)
Major defoliation day 1 and day 21 of flower (schwazzing)
Selective tuck or remove leaves blocking bud sites

equipment.description: Must be completely light-proof to prevent algae. Black or dark-colored buckets, totes, or storage bins. 5 gal for small plants, 10+ gal for large. No transparent or translucent containers — even white can let light through.
equipment.name: Opaque Container (5-10 gal)

equipment.description: Sits in lid opening. Holds the plant and growing medium above the reservoir. Must fit snugly in the container lid — no light gaps.
equipment.name: Net Pot (6 inch)

equipment.description: Must seal tightly. Cut or drill a hole for the net pot. Seal all gaps — light entering the reservoir causes algae and root damage.
equipment.name: Container Lid (with net pot hole)

equipment.description: Inert growing medium in the net pot. Supports the plant and wicks moisture to young roots. Rinse thoroughly before use to remove dust.
equipment.name: Hydroton / Clay Pebbles

equipment.description: Accurate pH measurement is critical. Digital pen preferred (calibrate monthly). Liquid test kits work but are less precise. Target range: 5.5-6.2.
equipment.name: pH Pen or Test Kit

equipment.description: Measures nutrient concentration. Essential for Kratky because stagnant water concentrates nutrients as it evaporates. Check EC at every top-off.
equipment.name: EC/TDS Meter

equipment.description: GH pH Up (potassium hydroxide) and pH Down (phosphoric acid). Kratky pH drifts up over time — you'll use pH Down more often.
equipment.name: pH Up & pH Down

equipment.description: FloraMicro, FloraGro, FloraBloom — 3-part nutrient system. Mix in order: Micro first, then Gro, then Bloom. Never mix concentrates directly together.
equipment.name: Nutrients (GH Flora Trio)

equipment.description: Beneficial bacteria (Bacillus amyloliquefaciens). CRITICAL for Kratky — no air pump means stagnant water. Hydroguard prevents root rot. 2 ml/gal at every fill and top-off. Non-negotiable.
equipment.name: Hydroguard

equipment.description: Full-spectrum LED recommended. Size depends on canopy area. Minimum 30 watts/sq ft actual draw for flower. Quantum boards (Samsung LM301B/H diodes) are efficient and cool-running.
equipment.name: Grow Light

equipment.description: Reliable timer for photoperiod control. Digital preferred over mechanical. 18/6 for veg, 12/12 for flower. Timer failure during flower causes hermaphroditism.
equipment.name: Light Timer

equipment.description: Monitor air temp and humidity. Digital with min/max recording. Place at canopy level. Consider one with probe for reservoir water temp too.
equipment.name: Thermometer / Hygrometer

equipment.description: Fills the gap between the plant stem and net pot opening. Blocks light from entering the reservoir. Also prevents evaporation and mosquito access. Kratky-specific essential item.
equipment.name: Neoprene Collar / Seal

equipment.description: Calcium and magnesium supplement. Needed with most LED lights and RO/soft water. 1-2 ml/gal depending on stage.
equipment.name: CalMag Supplement

equipment.description: Starter plugs for germination. Place seed in Rapid Rooter, then into net pot with hydroton when taproot emerges.
equipment.name: Rapid Rooters

equipment.description: Separate thermometer for reservoir water. Kratky water temp creeps up because there's no air pump cooling. Keep 62-72°F.
equipment.name: Water Thermometer

equipment.description: For checking trichome maturity during flower. 60x minimum magnification. Digital USB microscopes work even better.
equipment.name: Jeweler's Loupe (60x)

equipment.description: Odor control and air exchange. Essential if growing in a tent or enclosed space. Size to exchange room volume every 1-3 minutes.
equipment.name: Exhaust Fan + Carbon Filter

equipment.description: Air movement strengthens stems and prevents mold. Don't blow directly on plants — aim at walls for indirect circulation.
equipment.name: Circulation Fan

equipment.description: Strengthens cell walls and stems. Add first when mixing nutrients (before anything else). Stop during mid-to-late flower.
equipment.name: Silica Supplement (Armor Si)

equipment.description: Mark the initial fill line and the air gap line on the container. Kratky-specific: knowing exactly where the air gap starts is critical for proper top-offs.
equipment.name: Marker / Tape for Water Lines

equipment.description: Accurate nutrient measurement. Small containers in Kratky mean small volumes — precision matters.
equipment.name: Syringe Set (1ml, 5ml, 10ml)

equipment.description: Cotton rope or felt strip to bridge the gap between net pot bottom and water level during seedling stage. Keeps medium moist until roots reach the water. Remove once roots touch.
equipment.name: Wick Material (for seedling stage)

equipment.description: Drop into reservoir to lower water temp in hot conditions. Cheap alternative to a water chiller. Replace every 12 hours.
equipment.name: Frozen Water Bottles

equipment.description: Flushing agent that dissolves mineral salt buildup. Useful in Kratky where salts accumulate over the grow cycle.
equipment.name: FloraKleen

equipment.description: Phosphorus/potassium booster for early-to-mid flower. Increases bud density. Use for 3-4 weeks then stop.
equipment.name: PK Booster (Liquid Koolbloom)

equipment.description: Support and canopy management. Stretch over the canopy at the start of flower. Tuck branches through during the stretch.
equipment.name: Trellis Net (SCROG)

equipment.description: Retractable plant supports. Hook to branches and hang from above. Support heavy buds during flower.
equipment.name: Plant Yoyos

equipment.description: Wide-mouth quart mason jars for curing. Boveda 62% packs maintain perfect humidity hands-free.
equipment.name: Mason Jars + Boveda Packs (62%)

equipment.description: If the reservoir develops issues (light leak, crack, smell), you can swap the net pot to a fresh container. Kratky makes this easy — just lift and move.
equipment.name: Backup Container + Lid

flush_timing.coco: 7-14 days reduced nutrients → plain water last 3-5 days
flush_timing.notes: Flushing is debated — some studies show no difference. At minimum, reduce nutrients in final week.
flush_timing.soil: 14-21 days plain water (soil holds nutrients longer)

other_harvest_indicators: Most pistils (hairs) have darkened and curled in
Calyxes are swollen and dense
Fan leaves are yellowing and falling off naturally
Trichome stalks are visible and heads are enlarged
Breeder's recommended flowering time has passed

trichome_ratios.notes: Harvest early for more THC-dominant, less CBN. Energetic, racy high. Slight reduction in yield.
Standard harvest window. Mix of THC and CBN. Full-spectrum effects.
Extended harvest for max CBN conversion. Heavy body high. Good for pain/sleep. Slightly degraded THC.

container_sizing.large_cannabis_photo: 5-10 gallons (but DWC is better for large plants)

critical_rules: NEVER refill to original level — the air gap roots will drown
Top off only 50% of consumed volume if plants are struggling
Solution should be consumed by harvest — if not, container was too large
Start with correct container size: 1-5 gallon per plant depending on grow duration
limitations: Best for short grows (lettuce, herbs, autoflowers)
Long photo period grows may outdrink the container
No way to adjust EC/pH mid-grow without disturbing roots
Single-use solution — can't recirculate or top off easily
kratky_specific.principle: Plants grow roots into a static nutrient solution. As plants drink, an air gap forms above the water line — roots in the air gap absorb oxygen. No pump, no air stone, no electricity needed.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Micro → Gro → Bloom (always Micro first)
nutrient_brands.name: General Hydroponics Flora Trio

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Part A → Epsom → Part B (dissolve each fully before adding next)
nutrient_brands.name: Jack's 321
nutrient_brands.notes: Jack's 321 is 3.6g Part A, 2.4g Cal Nit, 1.2g Epsom per gallon at full strength. Extremely cost-effective for large grows.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Core → Bloom
nutrient_brands.name: Athena Pro Line
nutrient_brands.notes: Commercial-grade. Simple 2-part with integrated CalMag. Popular in commercial cannabis facilities.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: A → B (separate for veg and bloom)
nutrient_brands.name: Advanced Nutrients pH Perfect (Sensi)
nutrient_brands.notes: Self-adjusting pH technology (claims no pH adjustment needed). Expensive but popular for beginners who struggle with pH.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Shake VERY well before use (thick concentrate settles). One bottle at a time.
nutrient_brands.name: General Hydroponics FloraNova
nutrient_brands.notes: Simplest option — one bottle per phase. Great for beginners. Shake well — concentrate separates.
nutrient_brands.type: 1-part (two bottles — one for veg, one for bloom)

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Dissolve powder in warm water, stir well
nutrient_brands.name: Greenleaf Nutrients Mega Crop
nutrient_brands.notes: One powder does everything — includes CalMag. Incredibly cost-effective at ~$0.02/gal. Community favorite on r/microgrowery.

late_grow.notes: May need to add water if container runs low. Add to BELOW current air root zone.

week_1_2.notes: Plant barely drinking. Solution level drops slowly.

week_3_4.notes: Roots developing air roots (fuzzy white) in gap. DO NOT refill.

week_5_8.notes: Large air gap. Long water roots below. Healthy air roots above. System is working.

air_gap_management.concept: As the plant drinks, water level drops creating an air gap. Roots in the air gap absorb oxygen. Roots in water absorb nutrients. This self-regulating balance IS the Kratky method.

initial_setup.air_gap: None at start. The plant creates it by drinking.
initial_setup.critical_rule: NEVER refill to original level once air gap forms. Air roots will drown.
initial_setup.water_level: Touching the bottom of the net pot (roots just submerged 1-2 inches).

refill_rules.how_much: Fill to JUST below the lowest air roots. NEVER submerge air roots.
refill_rules.warning: Each refill is an opportunity to crash the system. Many Kratky growers prefer to size container large enough to last the entire grow with zero refills.
refill_rules.what: Fresh nutrient solution at current stage EC.
refill_rules.when: Only when water level drops so low that water roots can't reach it (plant wilting).

container_sizing.lid_requirements: Sturdy lid that supports plant weight. Cut net pot hole (3-inch standard). Seal any gaps (light exclusion + evaporation prevention).

material.critical: 100% light-proof. ANY light entering the reservoir = algae bloom. Wrap/paint if using translucent containers.

large_plants.notes: Full cannabis flower cycle. Safer margin.

lettuce_herbs.notes: Small plants, short grows. Mason jar works.

container_sizing.rule_of_thumb: 1 gallon of solution per week of grow time as minimum. Bigger is always safer.

failure_modes_passive.consequence: Air roots suffocate. Root rot begins within 24-48h. Plant collapses.
failure_modes_passive.failure: Refilling above air roots (drowning air roots)
failure_modes_passive.prevention: Mark the LOWEST air root level on container exterior. Never fill above this line.
failure_modes_passive.recovery: Drain back to safe level immediately. Damaged air roots may not recover — watch for rot.

failure_modes_passive.consequence: Water roots dry out and die. Plant wilts. If caught early, recoverable.
failure_modes_passive.prevention: Size container appropriately. Use opaque container so you can't see level (use weight or lift test).
failure_modes_passive.recovery: Add fresh solution to just below air roots. Plant may recover in 4-12 hours if caught in time.

failure_modes_passive.consequence: Green slime on roots and container walls. Consumes oxygen. Competes with roots.
failure_modes_passive.prevention: 100% light exclusion. No gaps. Dark container or wrapped/painted.
failure_modes_passive.recovery: Drain. Clean container. Wrap for light exclusion. Refill with fresh solution.

failure_modes_passive.consequence: As water is consumed, remaining solution concentrates. Burns roots, locks out nutrients.
failure_modes_passive.prevention: Start with moderate EC (1.0-1.2). Larger container = more dilute solution over time.
failure_modes_passive.recovery: Add plain pH'd water (one of the ONLY times plain water is OK in Kratky — to dilute concentrated remnants).

ph_drift.acceptable_drift: 5.8 → 6.5 over the grow is normal and fine.
ph_drift.buffer_tip: Add a small amount of pH-down to initial mix to give it room to drift up. Starting at 5.5-5.8 is ideal.
ph_drift.expected_direction: pH rises over time (plant uptake of anions).
ph_drift.intervention_needed: Only if pH exceeds 7.0 or drops below 5.0 (uncommon in Kratky).

progressive_feeding.description: For longer grows (cannabis, tomatoes), adjust nutrients at key refill points.
progressive_feeding.notes: Only applicable if you're doing refills. For true single-mix Kratky, use a balanced formula.

single_mix_approach.description: Mix nutrients once at setup. Plant grows start to finish on that one batch.

ec_target.notes: Start middle-strength. As plant drinks, EC rises (water consumed faster than nutrients). Starting too high = lockout by end.

ph_target.notes: pH will drift up over time (normal). Starting at 5.8 gives it room to drift to 6.5 without issues.

single_mix_approach.simplicity: This is the beauty of Kratky. Mix once, plant once, harvest. No daily monitoring needed for simple crops.

checklist_for_zero_maintenance: Container sized for entire grow (no refills needed)
Sturdy lid that won't sag under plant weight
EC at 1.0-1.2 (safe for entire grow without concentration issues)
pH at 5.8 (room to drift up naturally)
Net pot with hydroton + starter plug (stable support)
Grow in moderate temperature environment (no reservoir overheating)
true_set_and_forget.kratky_advantage: No pumps. No timers. No electricity required for the hydro system. Only power need is grow lights and environment control. Perfect for off-grid or backup grows.
true_set_and_forget.philosophy: Kratky is the laziest hydro method. Embrace it. The goal is zero intervention from planting to harvest.
what_can_go_wrong_anyway: Extreme heat (above 85°F room temp) — solution heats, dissolved oxygen drops, root rot risk
Light leak developing over time (lid warps, tape peels) — algae
Unexpectedly aggressive plant drinks container dry before harvest
Power outage affecting environment (not the Kratky system itself — it has no power requirements!)

environment.container: Glass mason jars (quart size). Fill 75% full.

humidity_packs.boveda_58: For drier preference or long-term storage.
humidity_packs.boveda_62: Maintains 62% RH. Set-and-forget. Replace when crispy.
humidity_packs.grove_bags: Heat-sealed bags with built-in humidity regulation. No burping needed.

indicators_of_good_cure: Smooth, flavorful smoke — no harshness or hay smell
Buds are slightly sticky but not wet
Strong terpene aroma when jar is opened
Ash burns to white/light gray (not black)
method: Trim buds to final shape (dry trim or finish wet trim)
Place in mason jars — fill 3/4 full to allow air exchange
Week 1: burp 2-3x daily for 10-15 minutes (release moisture and gases)
Week 2-4: burp 1x daily for 5-10 minutes
Month 2+: burp 1x weekly or seal completely
Cure for minimum 2 weeks, ideal is 4-8 weeks. Some strains improve for 6+ months.

common_mistakes: Drying too fast (>3 days) — harsh smoke, hay smell, terpene loss
Drying too slow (>14 days) — mold risk, diminishing returns
Fan blowing directly on buds — uneven drying, crispy exterior/moist interior
Temperature too high (>70°F) — terpene evaporation, harsh taste

environment.airflow: Gentle circulation — fan NOT pointed at buds. Just keep air moving.

method: Cut branches 12-18 inches long
Optionally remove large fan leaves (wet trim) or leave intact (dry trim)
Hang upside down on line/rack with 6+ inches between branches
Small branches should snap (not bend) when done — 5-7 days minimum
Stems should snap cleanly; if they bend, keep drying

long_term_storage.degradation: THC slowly converts to CBN over time. Light, heat, air, and moisture accelerate degradation.
long_term_storage.method: Sealed mason jars or vacuum-sealed bags with Boveda 58% in dark, cool place
long_term_storage.notes: Properly cured and stored cannabis can remain excellent for 12+ months. Freezing is debated — can damage trichomes if not done carefully.

golden_rules: 1. NEVER submerge air roots. Once the air gap forms, it's sacred. Top off BELOW the air root zone only.
2. Hydroguard at EVERY fill and EVERY top-off. No exceptions. Stagnant water without beneficial bacteria = root rot.
3. Container must be 100% light-proof. Even small light leaks cause algae that chokes roots.
4. Check EC before topping off. Rising EC = use plain water. Dropping EC = use nutrients.
5. pH will drift UP in Kratky (no bubbles to stabilize). Check and adjust at every top-off.
6. Start with a FULL reservoir. The initial water level sets the air gap as the plant drinks down.
7. Don't panic when water level drops. The air gap forming IS Kratky working correctly.
8. Keep the container sealed. Evaporation wastes water, raises EC, and lets pests in.
9. Use opaque containers ONLY. No clear, no white, no translucent. Black or dark-colored.
10. Kratky's biggest advantage is simplicity and portability. Don't over-complicate it.
quick_reference.hydroguard_dose: 2 ml/gal at every fill and every top-off. Non-negotiable in Kratky — no air pump means no dissolved oxygen, making Hydroguard the primary defense against root rot.
nutrient_mixing_order: 1. Fill container with water to target level
2. Add Silica (Armor Si) — stir and wait 15 min
quick_reference.reservoir_change_schedule: Kratky does NOT use scheduled reservoir changes. Top off only — never refill above the air gap line. Exceptions: full swap at transition to bloom nutrients, and at flush. Partial swap if EC is chronically above 2.0 or root zone smells.
quick_reference.top_off_rule: Check EC before every top-off. EC rising = water evaporating, concentrating salts → top off with plain pH'd water. EC dropping = plant consuming nutrients → top off with nutrients at stage-appropriate strength. NEVER refill above the air root zone. Add Hydroguard to every top-off.

scale_tiers.description: First-time grower or single-plant hobbyist. Manual management, learning-focused.
scale_tiers.name: Solo / Single Plant
scale_tiers.notes: Hands-on learning experience. Manual checks 1-2x daily. Ideal for beginners.

scale_tiers.description: Hobbyist tent grow. Light automation helpful but not required.
scale_tiers.name: Small Tent (2-8 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Timer-controlled lights, humidity controller. Sensor monitoring recommended.

scale_tiers.description: Dedicated grower with multiple tents/rooms. Automation saves significant time.
scale_tiers.name: Multi-Tent (9-24 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Multiple environments to manage. Automated alerts essential. Consider perpetual harvest.

scale_tiers.description: Licensed commercial operation. Full automation, compliance tracking, batch management.
scale_tiers.name: Commercial Room (25-100 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: SOPs required. Staff training. Regulatory compliance. Insurance requirements.

scale_tiers.description: Large-scale production facility. Enterprise automation, SCADA integration, dedicated staff.
scale_tiers.name: Warehouse Scale (100+ plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Multiple rooms, dedicated HVAC, industrial nutrient dosing, video surveillance.

common_problems.cause: Too cold, too dry, or bad seed
common_problems.solution: Ensure 75-80°F. Keep medium moist. Try a different seed after 7 days.

common_problems.cause: Too wet, no air circulation
common_problems.solution: Reduce moisture. Ensure Rapid Rooter is moist not soaked.

stages.description: Seed cracks open and taproot emerges. Use Rapid Rooters, paper towel method, or direct-in-cube. Keep warm, moist, and dark. Identical to DWC — the Kratky difference starts at seedling stage.

environment.notes: Keep seeds in darkness. A heat mat at 78°F speeds germination. No direct light until sprout emerges.

environment_overrides.notes: Germinate inside the greenhouse on a heat mat. Greenhouses provide enough shelter for germination. Ensure overnight temps stay above 65°F — use a seedling heat mat if nights are cool.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse germination works well with a heat mat and humidity dome.

environment_overrides.notes: Germinate indoors or in a sheltered area even for outdoor grows. Seeds need consistent warmth that outdoor temps rarely provide. Start indoors, transplant to outdoor Kratky once seedling has 2-3 true leaf sets.

extra_tasks.description: Outdoor Kratky must start after last frost. Photoperiod plants flower when daylight drops below ~14 hours. In northern hemisphere, start seeds indoors in April-May for outdoor transplant in May-June.
extra_tasks.name: Plan outdoor timing

outdoor.notes: Always germinate indoors regardless of final grow location.

health_checks: Is the taproot visible and white?
Is the Rapid Rooter moist but not soaking wet?
stages.name: Germination

nutrients.approach: None — seeds contain all energy needed to germinate.

reservoir.notes: No reservoir needed yet. If pre-filling container, use plain pH'd water only. No air stone needed — this is Kratky.

tasks.description: Place seeds in cup of room-temperature water for 12-24 hours until they sink. Transfer to Rapid Rooter or paper towel.
tasks.name: Soak seeds

tasks.description: After 24-72 hours, look for white taproot emerging from seed shell. Transfer to net pot once taproot is 0.5-1 inch.
tasks.name: Check for taproot

tasks.description: Fill opaque container with plain pH'd water (5.8). Set water level to just touch the bottom of the net pot. NO air stone — leave the lid sealed tight.
tasks.name: Prepare Kratky container

transition_signals: Seed shell has cracked and cotyledons are emerging

common_problems.cause: Water level too low or Rapid Rooter dried out
common_problems.solution: Raise water level to just touch the bottom of the net pot. Use a wick (strip of felt or cotton) from the net pot down to the water to bridge the gap.

common_problems.cause: Light leaking into container through gaps
common_problems.solution: Seal ALL light leaks. Cover net pot gap with neoprene collar or hydroton. Use opaque/black container. Algae competes with roots for oxygen in stagnant water — more dangerous in Kratky than DWC.

common_problems.cause: Anaerobic bacteria in stagnant water
common_problems.solution: Drain, clean, refill with fresh pH'd nutrient solution + Hydroguard. Ensure container is sealed properly. This is the biggest Kratky risk — no aeration means anaerobic bacteria can establish quickly.

common_problems.cause: Normal Kratky behavior — no aeration to stabilize pH
common_problems.solution: Adjust with small amounts of pH Down. In Kratky, pH naturally drifts up. Check every 2-3 days. Don't over-correct.

stages.description: First true leaves develop. Roots grow down from net pot into the nutrient solution. The initial air gap begins to form as the seedling drinks. This is where Kratky diverges from DWC — there is no air stone, so the forming air gap is the ONLY oxygen source for roots.

environment.notes: Keep light at 24-30 inches above canopy. Seedlings are fragile — too much light causes bleaching.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse provides good seedling conditions. Ensure container stays cool — greenhouses can overheat. Place container on ground (thermal mass) rather than on a bench in direct sun.

extra_tasks.description: Place the container where it gets shade even though the plant gets light. Kratky containers must stay cool — no circulation to manage heat.
extra_tasks.name: Shade the container

greenhouse.notes: Keep Kratky container shaded in the greenhouse. Plant gets light, container stays cool.

environment_overrides.notes: Keep seedlings indoors or in sheltered area. They're too fragile for direct outdoor sun. Begin hardening off at the end of this stage — 1-2 hours of morning sun, increasing daily over a week.

extra_problems.cause: Stagnant Kratky water heats up faster than aerated DWC water
extra_problems.solution: Insulate container. Keep in shade while plant gets partial sun. Bury lower half in ground. Kratky without aeration is more sensitive to heat — above 75°F water promotes bacteria.

extra_tasks.description: Start exposing seedling to outdoor conditions gradually. Day 1-2: 1 hour morning sun. Day 3-4: 2-3 hours. Day 5-7: 4-6 hours. Bring inside at night.
extra_tasks.name: Begin hardening off (end of stage)

extra_tasks.description: Wrap Kratky container in Reflectix or white material. Outdoor sun heats containers much faster with no circulation to dissipate heat.
extra_tasks.name: Insulate container for outdoor use

outdoor.notes: Outdoor Kratky seedlings need extra protection. No aeration means water quality degrades faster in heat.

health_checks: Are roots growing down into the water?
Is the water level dropping (air gap forming)?
Any algae visible on the water surface?
Do roots smell clean (no foul odor)?
Are cotyledons still green? First true leaves appearing?
stages.name: Seedling

nutrients.approach: Very light nutrients in initial fill. Kratky philosophy: set the initial mix right and don't touch it. For cannabis seedlings, 1/4 strength is safe.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Beneficial bacteria — critical in stagnant Kratky water. Prevents root rot without aeration.

reservoir.notes: Water level should START touching the bottom of the net pot. As the seedling drinks, the water drops and an air gap forms. Do NOT refill to the top — this air gap is critical. Add Hydroguard at initial fill to prevent stagnant water issues.

tasks.description: Verify water is touching net pot bottom. Seedling roots are short and need direct contact. Once roots are 2+ inches into water, the air gap can begin forming.
tasks.name: Check water level

tasks.description: Use a marker to draw a line on the outside of the container at the current water level. This is your 'never refill above' line. As water drops, mark the air root zone.
tasks.name: Mark initial water line

tasks.description: Briefly lift the lid to check if roots are growing down through the net pot into the water. You should see white roots within 5-7 days.
tasks.name: Check for root growth

tasks.description: pH tends to drift UP in Kratky (no aeration stabilizing it). Check every 2-3 days. Adjust with pH Down if above 6.2.
tasks.name: Monitor pH

tasks.description: Check for light leaks around the lid and net pot. Stagnant water + light = algae explosion. Cover any gaps with tape or neoprene collar.
tasks.name: Ensure container is sealed and dark

transition_signals: Roots visible 2+ inches below net pot
Air gap beginning to form (water level has dropped 1+ inch)

common_problems.cause: Water level hasn't dropped enough or container isn't sealed (humidity escaping)
common_problems.solution: Ensure lid is sealed tightly. The air gap must be humid (near 100% RH inside the container). If water hasn't dropped, the plant may be too small — give it time.

common_problems.cause: Container lid not sealed — humid air escaping
common_problems.solution: Seal the container better. Air roots need humid air (near 100% RH). If the container breathes, the air gap dries out and air roots die. Use tape or gasket material around the lid.

common_problems.cause: Water too warm, no Hydroguard, organic debris
common_problems.solution: Keep water below 72°F. Add Hydroguard at 2 ml/gal. Remove any dead plant material from water. In severe cases, do a full water change (this breaks pure Kratky philosophy but saves the plant).

common_problems.cause: Normal Kratky pH drift — nutrient uptake creates alkaline byproducts
common_problems.solution: Add pH Down carefully. Phosphoric acid-based pH Down also adds phosphorus. Don't chase pH — a slow drift from 5.8 to 6.5 over a week is acceptable. Only correct if above 6.5.

stages.description: Rapid leaf and root growth. The air gap is actively forming as the plant drinks. You should see two distinct root zones developing: submerged 'water roots' (white, slimy-smooth) and exposed 'air roots' (fuzzy, branching laterally in the humid air gap). Both are healthy and essential. This dual root system is the core of Kratky.

environment.notes: Increase light intensity as plant grows. Can bring light closer (18-24 inches). Watch for light stress — if leaf edges curl up, raise light.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse Kratky works well — protected from rain and wind. Main concern is heat buildup heating the stagnant water. Place container on ground (thermal mass), not on a bench in sun.

extra_problems.cause: Stagnant water surface attracts egg-laying gnats
extra_problems.solution: Seal container lid completely — no gaps. Add a layer of hydroton on top of net pot. Use yellow sticky traps. Mosquito dunks (BTI) in the water are safe and effective.

extra_tasks.description: Open vents when temp exceeds 80°F. Kratky water has no aeration buffer — the whole system depends on stable temps.
extra_tasks.name: Manage greenhouse ventilation

extra_tasks.description: Greenhouses attract whiteflies, fungus gnats, and spider mites. Use sticky traps. Fungus gnats are especially attracted to stagnant Kratky water surfaces.
extra_tasks.name: Check for greenhouse pests

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse Kratky is a solid choice — weather protection with good natural light. Seal the container tight to prevent pests.

reservoir_overrides.notes: Greenhouse warmth heats Kratky water fast. Floor placement + insulation is critical. Consider a shade cloth over the container area (not the plant).

environment_overrides.notes: Full sun drives fast growth. Kratky containers outdoors are MORE vulnerable to heat than DWC — no aeration cooling. Double-insulate: Reflectix wrap + paint container white. Consider burying lower half in ground for thermal mass.

extra_problems.cause: No aeration + warm temps = bacteria bloom
extra_problems.issue: Stagnant water going anaerobic in heat
extra_problems.solution: If water smells bad, do an emergency change. Refill to the air root line (NOT to top). Add extra Hydroguard (3 ml/gal). Insulate better. If chronic, consider adding a small solar-powered air pump (breaks pure Kratky but saves the plant).

extra_problems.cause: As water level drops, container gets top-heavy
extra_problems.solution: Place rocks or weights at the base. Use a wider, lower-profile container (storage tote vs tall bucket). Anchor to the ground.

extra_tasks.description: Wrap in Reflectix. Keep container in shade while plant gets sun. Bury lower half in ground. Kratky has zero tolerance for overheated water — no bubbles to add oxygen.
extra_tasks.name: Insulate & shade container

extra_tasks.description: Check for aphids, spider mites, caterpillars, slugs. Outdoor grows face constant pest pressure. Inspect both sides of leaves.
extra_tasks.name: Pest scouting

extra_tasks.description: Cover the gap around the stem and net pot. Rain diluting the reservoir is worse in Kratky — you can't just 'change the res' without disrupting the air gap.
extra_tasks.name: Rain protection for container

outdoor.notes: Outdoor Kratky is higher risk than outdoor DWC due to no aeration. Temperature management is absolutely critical.

reservoir_overrides.notes: Outdoor Kratky water temp is the #1 concern. Without aeration, warm stagnant water goes anaerobic FAST. Keep below 72°F at all costs. Insulate, shade, or bury the container.

health_checks: Can you see both air roots (fuzzy, above water) and water roots (smooth, below)?
Is the air gap growing as the plant drinks?
Any foul smell when you open the lid?
Is pH staying in the 5.5-6.5 range?
Are leaves a healthy green with no yellowing?
stages.name: Early Vegetative

nutrients.approach: Half-strength veg nutrients. In true Kratky you don't touch the reservoir. For cannabis (a heavy feeder), use 'modified Kratky' — top off when water gets low but never above air roots. Mix top-off water at half strength.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Essential in stagnant Kratky water. Add at initial fill AND every top-off.

supplements.name: Silica (Armor Si)
supplements.purpose: Strengthens cell walls. Add first, before other nutrients. Especially useful in Kratky where roots have less oxygen support.

reservoir.notes: Air gap should be 2-4 inches and growing. The fuzzy air roots in this gap are oxygen roots — they MUST stay in humid air, NOT submerged. If you top off, NEVER go above the lowest air roots. Add Hydroguard to any top-off water.

tasks.description: Verify the air gap is growing. Mark the current water line. You should see fuzzy air roots above the water and white water roots below. Both are healthy.
tasks.name: Check water level & air gap

tasks.description: pH drifts up in Kratky. Check every 2-3 days. Adjust gently with pH Down. Large swings are worse than slightly high pH. Range 5.5-6.5 is acceptable.
tasks.name: Monitor pH

tasks.description: If water level has dropped more than 50% of original volume, top off to halfway between current level and the lowest air roots. Use half-strength nutrients + Hydroguard. Mark the new 'never fill above' line.
tasks.name: First top-off (if needed)

tasks.description: Briefly lift lid. Healthy Kratky roots: white/cream water roots below waterline, fuzzy lateral air roots above. Bad signs: brown slime, foul smell, mushy roots.
tasks.name: Inspect roots

tasks.description: Low Stress Training is ideal for Kratky. Bend and tie branches to spread the canopy. More even light = better yields without pushing the root system harder.
tasks.name: Begin LST training

training.description: Bend main stem and tie to container edge or use plant clips. Opens canopy for even light. Start when plant has 4-5 nodes. Ideal for Kratky — doesn't stress the root system.
training.when: 4-5 nodes, when stem is still flexible

training.description: Cut the main stem above the 4th or 5th node. Creates two main colas. Let plant recover for 5-7 days before additional training. Topping is fine in Kratky — the root system handles it.

transition_signals: Distinct air root and water root zones visible

common_problems.cause: Container too small for the plant size
common_problems.issue: Water level dropping too fast (daily top-off)
common_problems.solution: Upgrade to a larger container (10-15 gal). Transplant carefully — keep the air root zone intact. Match the water level to the existing air gap line. Larger container = more stable system.

common_problems.cause: Plant drinking water faster than nutrients — evaporation concentrating salts
common_problems.solution: Top off with PLAIN pH'd water (no nutrients) when EC is rising. Only add nutrients when EC is dropping. This is the same rule as DWC.

common_problems.cause: Plant consuming nutrients faster than water
common_problems.solution: Top off with full-strength nutrient solution. The plant is hungry. This usually means healthy, vigorous growth.

common_problems.cause: Container not sealed — humid air escaping, or water level rose above air roots during top-off
common_problems.solution: Check seal on container lid. NEVER top off above the air root zone. If you accidentally submerged air roots, drain water back down immediately. Air roots that were submerged may die but new ones will form.

common_problems.cause: Stagnant water + warm temps + organic matter
common_problems.solution: Carefully remove biofilm with a paper towel. Add extra Hydroguard. If severe, do a full reservoir change — fill to the existing air gap line, not to top. Add Hydroguard immediately.

stages.description: Full vegetative growth. Plant is drinking heavily — in Kratky, this means the water level drops fast and top-offs become routine. The air gap is well-established (4-8+ inches). You should see a thick mass of fuzzy air roots in the gap and a healthy mat of water roots below. This is 'modified Kratky' territory for cannabis — pure set-and-forget won't sustain a plant this thirsty.

environment.notes: Maximum veg light intensity. LED at 16-20 inches. Plants should be vigorously growing with 5-6+ nodes.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse Kratky can use light deprivation tarps to control flowering time. Without light dep, plants follow natural photoperiod. Keep container on ground for thermal mass. Ventilate to prevent overheating.

extra_problems.cause: Missing coverage interrupts the dark period
extra_problems.solution: Automate with motorized tarps. One missed day can revert to veg or cause hermaphroditism.

extra_tasks.description: Install blackout tarps if you want to control flowering time. 12 hours of uninterrupted darkness required. Must be done consistently every day.
extra_tasks.name: Consider light deprivation

extra_tasks.description: Shade cloth, exhaust fans, open vents. Kratky water temps must stay below 72°F — monitor closely in summer.
extra_tasks.name: Manage greenhouse heat
