greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse Kratky is excellent for veg — protected from weather with good natural light. Light dep gives indoor-like control.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor Kratky plants veg until natural daylight drops below ~14 hours. The plant can get very large — plan accordingly with container size. A 10-gal minimum is recommended for outdoor Kratky cannabis. Autoflowers bypass photoperiod concerns.

extra_problems.cause: Hot day, large plant, missed top-off
extra_problems.solution: Refill immediately to the air gap line. If water roots dried for less than a few hours, the plant will recover. If the air gap dried out too, mist the air roots to rehydrate. Prevention: use the largest container possible.

extra_problems.cause: Deer, rabbits, or rodents
extra_problems.solution: Fence or chicken wire around the plant. Container is portable — move to sheltered area if needed.

extra_tasks.description: Large outdoor Kratky plants drink fast. Check morning and evening. Never let the water level reach zero — the water roots will die. Maintain at least 2 inches of water at all times.
extra_tasks.name: Top off daily (or twice daily in heat)

extra_tasks.description: Outdoor Kratky plants grow tall. Install a tomato cage, bamboo stakes, or trellis. The container gets lighter as water drops — brace it.
extra_tasks.name: Structural support

extra_tasks.description: Apply neem oil or insecticidal soap every 7-10 days during veg. Stop all sprays 2+ weeks before flower.
extra_tasks.name: IPM spray (preventive)

outdoor.notes: Outdoor Kratky in late veg needs daily attention. The passive system's simplicity breaks down when the plant is a heavy drinker in summer heat.

reservoir_overrides.notes: Large outdoor plants may drink 1-3+ gal/day in peak summer. Use the largest container possible. Top off at least daily in heat. EC and pH management is harder outdoors — check at every top-off.

health_checks: How quickly is water level dropping? (Indicates plant health and size)
Is the air gap maintained at 4-8+ inches?
Are air roots still fuzzy and healthy?
Any bad smell from the container?
Is pH drifting excessively (more than 0.5 in 2 days)?
Are leaves showing any deficiency signs?
stages.name: Late Vegetative

nutrients.approach: Three-quarter strength veg nutrients for top-offs. Cannabis in late veg drinks 0.5-1.5 gal/day depending on size. In a 5-gal bucket, you may top off every 2-3 days. In a 10-gal, every 4-5 days. Larger container = less maintenance = true Kratky spirit.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Every top-off. Non-negotiable in Kratky.

supplements.name: Silica (Armor Si)
supplements.purpose: Strengthens stems for heavy flower. Add first before other nutrients.

reservoir.notes: Water consumption increases dramatically. Top-off when water level drops to 25-30% of container capacity. ALWAYS top off BELOW the air root zone. Mix top-off at 75% strength. Add Hydroguard to every top-off. The air gap should be maintained — never reduce it.

tasks.description: When water drops to 25-30% of container, add nutrient solution to halfway between current level and lowest air roots. Never submerge air roots. Add Hydroguard.
tasks.name: Top off reservoir

tasks.description: Check pH of existing water before and after adding top-off. Adjust entire volume if needed. pH Down is your friend in Kratky.
tasks.name: Check pH at top-off

tasks.description: Healthy air roots: fuzzy, white/cream, branching laterally. Healthy water roots: white/cream, may be slightly stained from nutrients. Bad: brown mush, foul smell, slimy.
tasks.name: Inspect roots

tasks.description: Keep bending and tying new growth. In Kratky, an even canopy is even more important — you need maximum efficiency from a root system with less oxygen than DWC.
tasks.name: Continue LST / training

tasks.description: If using a 5-gal bucket and topping off daily, switch to a 10-gal tote. Larger reservoir = more stable pH/EC, less frequent top-offs, and more Kratky-like behavior.
tasks.name: Consider container upgrade

tasks.description: Stop topping/training 1-2 weeks before planned flip. Let plant recover before the stretch.
tasks.name: Plan for flower transition

training.description: Continue bending and tying. Kratky plants benefit more from LST than DWC — the root system has less oxygen support, so keeping an efficient canopy matters more.
Install trellis net 8-12 inches above container. Tuck branches under net as they grow through. Excellent for Kratky — maximizes yield per plant.
Remove lower leaves and small branches that won't reach the canopy. Redirect energy to top growth. Don't overdo it — the plant needs leaves to drink and drive the Kratky cycle.

transition_signals: Plant is 1/3-1/2 of desired final height
Ready to flip light schedule to 12/12
Root system is well-established with healthy air and water roots

common_problems.cause: Heavy stretch drinking + undersized container
common_problems.solution: Top off to the air root line before lights out. If this keeps happening, upgrade to a larger container. Emergency: refill to air root line immediately. Roots that dry out completely will die.

common_problems.cause: Light leak during dark period, or stress from dried-out roots
common_problems.solution: Check for ANY light leaks. If you must open the container during dark period for top-off, use a green headlamp only. If hermie is confirmed, remove pollen sacs immediately.

common_problems.cause: Concentrated nutrients from evaporation or too-strong mix
common_problems.solution: Top off with plain pH'd water (no nutrients) until EC drops. Kratky concentrates nutrients as water evaporates — monitor EC at every top-off.

stages.description: Light flipped to 12/12. Plant stretches 50-200% in height. Water consumption spikes during the stretch — this is the most demanding phase for Kratky maintenance. The plant may drink 1-2+ gallons/day. Top-off discipline is critical: keep the air gap sacred, never submerge air roots, and switch nutrient mix toward bloom ratios.

environment.notes: Flip to 12/12. Light intensity stays high. The 12 hours of uninterrupted darkness must be ABSOLUTE — light leaks cause hermaphroditism. This is even more critical in Kratky since you may need to open the container for top-offs during the dark period — use a green headlamp only.

environment_overrides.notes: If using light dep, cover greenhouse with blackout tarp at the same time every evening. 12 hours of total darkness required. If following natural light, transition happens same as outdoor but with better weather protection.

extra_problems.cause: Covering greenhouse traps heat
extra_problems.issue: Heat buildup under light dep tarp
extra_problems.solution: Cover in evening when temps drop. Ensure some passive ventilation under the tarp. Kratky water temps spike in trapped heat.

extra_tasks.description: Cover greenhouse at the same time every evening. 12 hours of TOTAL darkness. Even small light leaks through tarp edges matter.
extra_tasks.name: Light dep schedule (if using)

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse with light dep gives indoor-like control. Kratky water temp management under tarps is the main challenge.

environment_overrides.light_hours: Decreasing natural daylight (~12-14 hours, triggering flower)
environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor transition happens naturally as days shorten in late summer. Kratky container maintenance intensifies — the plant drinks the most during stretch. Check for light pollution from street lights or porch lights that could interrupt the dark period.

extra_problems.cause: Street lights or porch lights nearby
extra_problems.solution: Move the container to a darker location. Kratky portability is an advantage here.

extra_problems.cause: Stretch + high temps + no aeration cooling
extra_problems.solution: Top off immediately. Use a larger container (15+ gal). Add frozen water bottles to cool the refill water.

extra_tasks.description: Ensure no artificial light reaches the plant during dark hours. Move container if needed — Kratky containers are portable.
extra_tasks.name: Eliminate light pollution

extra_tasks.description: Last chance for preventive sprays. Apply BT (Bacillus thuringiensis) for caterpillars. After buds form, no more spraying.
extra_tasks.name: Final pest prevention

extra_tasks.description: Stretch + future bud weight requires strong support. Add stakes, cage, or trellis.
extra_tasks.name: Reinforce supports

extra_tasks.description: Outdoor stretch in summer heat = peak water demand. Check morning and evening. Never let water level reach zero.
extra_tasks.name: Top off twice daily

outdoor.notes: Outdoor Kratky transition is the most maintenance-intensive period. Top-off discipline is critical. Consider the switch to a larger container before this stage.

health_checks: Is the plant stretching? (Should be gaining 1-2 inches/day at peak)
Are pistils (white hairs) appearing at nodes?
Is water level dropping fast? (Normal — stretch = peak water demand)
Are air roots still healthy and undisturbed?
Any signs of stress from the light flip?
stages.name: Transition (Stretch)

nutrients.approach: Transition mix: reduce Gro, increase Bloom. Some growers do a full reservoir swap here — drain existing veg solution (keeping the air gap intact), refill with bloom-ratio nutrients to the air gap line. This gives the plant a clean start for flower.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Every top-off and reservoir swap. Non-negotiable.

supplements.name: Silica (Armor Si)
supplements.purpose: Last week to add silica — stop before mid-flower.

reservoir.notes: Begin transitioning nutrient mix from veg to bloom ratios. Water consumption peaks during stretch — top off more frequently. Consider doing a full reservoir swap to bloom nutrients: drain to air gap line, refill with bloom-ratio mix. This is the one time a full change is recommended in Kratky.

tasks.description: Set timer to 12 hours on / 12 hours off. Ensure complete darkness during off period — check for indicator lights, light leaks, and timer reliability.
tasks.name: Flip light schedule to 12/12

tasks.description: Drain existing veg solution down to just above the water roots (keeping air gap intact). Refill to the air root line with bloom-ratio nutrients + Hydroguard. This gives a clean nutritional start for flower.
tasks.name: Consider full reservoir swap

tasks.description: Plant may drink 1-2+ gal/day during stretch. Check water level daily. Top off with bloom-ratio nutrients below the air root zone. Add Hydroguard to every top-off.
tasks.name: Top off frequently (stretch demand)

tasks.description: The stretch can double plant height. Use plant ties, bamboo stakes, or trellis net. Branches that aren't supported will bend or break under future bud weight.
tasks.name: Support stretching branches

tasks.description: Remove small lower branches and leaves that won't get light. This redirects energy to top colas. In Kratky, efficiency matters — the root system has less oxygen support than DWC.
tasks.name: Lollipop bottom growth

training.description: Tuck branches under the trellis net as they stretch through. Keep canopy even. Stop tucking once buds start forming (usually end of week 2 of 12/12).
Gently crush and bend tall branches to even the canopy without cutting. Creates a knuckle that strengthens the branch. Use carefully — Kratky plants may recover slower than DWC due to less oxygen.
training.when: During stretch for branches outgrowing the canopy

transition_signals: White pistils clearly visible at most nodes
Bud sites forming at branch tips

common_problems.cause: Water evaporating faster than plant drinks — concentrating salts in stagnant reservoir
common_problems.solution: Top off with plain pH'd water only until EC drops to target range. In Kratky, evaporation concentration is more severe than DWC because there's no water movement.

common_problems.cause: Humidity too high, poor airflow, dense buds
common_problems.solution: Remove affected bud + 1 inch of healthy tissue. Increase airflow with fans. Lower humidity below 50%. In Kratky, the sealed container adds ambient humidity to the grow space — ensure good room ventilation.

common_problems.cause: Nutrient-rich stagnant water + warm temps
common_problems.solution: Add extra Hydroguard (3 ml/gal for one top-off). If smell is severe, do a partial water swap: drain to just above water roots, refill with fresh bloom nutrients to the air gap line. Add Hydroguard immediately.

stages.description: Buds are forming and stacking. Trichome production begins. Water consumption remains high but stabilizes after the stretch. In Kratky, this is when nutrient management matters most — the plant needs heavy P and K for bud production, and the stagnant reservoir concentrates salts over time. Monitor EC closely at every top-off.

environment.notes: Maximum light intensity during flower. Lower humidity to prevent bud rot. The 4-6°F day/night temperature difference helps terpene and trichome production.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse provides rain protection — huge advantage during flower. Control humidity by ventilating during the day. Kratky containers in greenhouses need extra attention — the enclosed space + stagnant water = high humidity environment. Run fans.

extra_problems.cause: Temperature differential + Kratky humidity contribution
extra_problems.issue: Condensation on glazing dripping on buds
extra_problems.solution: Increase air circulation. Open vents briefly in morning. Consider anti-drip greenhouse film.

extra_tasks.description: Open vents during day. Run HAF fans constantly. Use dehumidifier if RH consistently above 55% during flower. Kratky sealed containers contribute moisture.
extra_tasks.name: Humidity management

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse flower is excellent. Monitor humidity closely — Kratky containers release some moisture into the enclosed space.

environment_overrides.notes: Humidity is uncontrollable outdoors. In humid climates, space plants apart, defoliate for airflow, and inspect for bud rot after every rain. Kratky containers add localized humidity — keep container sealed tight so moisture doesn't escape upward into the buds.

extra_problems.cause: Water trapped in dense buds + stagnant Kratky humidity
extra_problems.solution: Cut affected area + 1 inch. Improve airflow. Consider harvesting early if persistent rain is forecast.

extra_problems.cause: Moths lay eggs on buds
extra_problems.solution: Apply BT (Bacillus thuringiensis). Check buds for frass. Remove affected buds by hand.

extra_problems.cause: Fall temperatures dropping
extra_problems.solution: Cover with frost cloth at night. Move Kratky container to shelter — portability is a key advantage.

extra_tasks.description: After rain, shake water off buds. Inspect for gray mold within 24-48 hours. Kratky moisture + rain = double humidity risk.
extra_tasks.name: Post-rain bud inspection

extra_tasks.description: Budworms bore into buds and cause rot from inside. Inspect for frass (dark droppings). Apply BT if found — safe up to harvest.
extra_tasks.name: Caterpillar patrol (BT spray)

extra_tasks.description: Gently shake plant each morning to remove dew from buds.
extra_tasks.name: Shake off morning dew

outdoor.notes: Outdoor early flower faces weather pressure. Kratky's portability is an advantage — you can move the container to shelter.

health_checks: Are buds visibly growing and forming?
Any yellowing or burnt tips? (Adjust EC accordingly)
Container sealed? Any algae or smell?
stages.name: Early Flower

nutrients.approach: Full-strength bloom nutrients. The plant is focused entirely on bud production now. Heavy phosphorus (P) and potassium (K) drive bud size and density. Reduce nitrogen. In Kratky, nutrients concentrate as water evaporates — watch EC carefully.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Every top-off. Stagnant flower-stage water is nutrient-rich and warm — prime rot conditions.

supplements.name: Liquid Koolbloom (optional)
supplements.purpose: PK booster for early flower. Increases bud density. Use for first 3-4 weeks of flower only.

reservoir.notes: Bloom-ratio nutrients. Check EC at every top-off: if EC is rising, top off with plain pH'd water. If EC is dropping, top off with full-strength bloom nutrients. The air gap should be well-established and stable (6-10+ inches). Maintain it.

tasks.description: Check water level every 1-2 days. Top off below air roots with bloom-ratio nutrients. Check EC first — if rising, use plain water. If dropping, use nutrients.
tasks.name: Top off with bloom nutrients

tasks.description: Kratky concentrates nutrients as water evaporates. EC above 1.8 = too concentrated. Dilute by topping off with plain pH'd water. EC below 0.8 = plant is hungry, top off with full-strength nutrients.
tasks.name: Monitor EC carefully

tasks.description: Remove fan leaves that are blocking light to bud sites. Don't remove more than 20% of leaves at once. Kratky plants recover from defoliation slower than DWC — be conservative.
tasks.name: Defoliation (targeted)

tasks.description: Check dense bud sites for any gray/brown rot. Bud rot in Kratky grows can be worse because the grower is already fighting humidity from the stagnant reservoir. Good airflow is critical.
tasks.name: Inspect for bud rot

tasks.description: Use plant yoyos, string, or bamboo to support branches as buds gain weight. Kratky containers lose weight as water drops — anchor the container too.
tasks.name: Support heavy buds

transition_signals: Buds filling in and starting to stack
Trichomes covering buds and sugar leaves
Pistils starting to change from white to orange

common_problems.cause: Light too close / too intense, or heat stress
common_problems.solution: Raise light 2-4 inches. Check leaf surface temperature. In Kratky, root stress from warm stagnant water can compound heat stress from above — fix both.

common_problems.cause: Weeks of top-offs concentrating mineral salts in stagnant water
common_problems.solution: If EC is chronically high (above 2.0), do a partial water swap: drain to just above water roots, refill to air gap line with half-strength bloom nutrients + Hydroguard. This resets salt levels.

common_problems.cause: Insufficient light, low P/K, or root issues
common_problems.solution: Check light intensity (target 800+ PPFD). Ensure bloom nutrients are heavy on P and K. Inspect roots — if brown and smelly, root health is compromising nutrient uptake. Add Hydroguard and consider a water swap.

stages.description: Buds are bulking and packing on weight. Trichome production is at maximum. This is where Kratky simplicity pays off — the root system is mature, the air gap is stable, and the plant has settled into a steady drinking pattern. Top-offs become routine and predictable. The biggest risk is nutrient concentration from evaporation.

environment.notes: Peak light intensity. The 8-12°F day/night temp swing enhances terpene production and can bring out purple/red colors. Keep humidity below 50% — bud density creates moisture traps.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse mid-flower is ideal. Rain protection with natural light. Fall temp drops are moderated. May need supplemental heating if overnight temps drop below 55°F consistently.

extra_tasks.description: If overnight greenhouse temps drop below 55°F, use a small space heater. Kratky water will also cool down — check water temp.
extra_tasks.name: Consider supplemental heating

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse mid-flower is straightforward. The structure does the work.

environment_overrides.notes: Mid-flower outdoors is typically September-October. Cooler nights enhance terpenes but watch for fall rain and humidity. Kratky portability lets you move the container under cover during rain events — use it.

extra_problems.cause: Fall weather patterns
extra_problems.solution: Move container to covered area. Kratky's biggest advantage is portability. A slightly early harvest beats a moldy crop.

extra_problems.cause: Fall approaching
extra_problems.solution: Move container indoors at night. Cool enhances colors but below 40°F risks damage. Kratky portability is the key advantage here.

extra_tasks.description: Check 10-day forecast daily. If prolonged rain is coming and buds are close to ready, consider early harvest. Move Kratky container under cover for rain events.
extra_tasks.name: Weather monitoring (critical)

extra_tasks.description: When rain is forecast, move the Kratky container under an overhang, carport, or tarp. The plant can handle a day of lower light — it can't handle bud rot.
extra_tasks.name: Rain cover / move container

extra_tasks.description: Caterpillars are still active. Check daily — their damage causes bud rot from inside.
extra_tasks.name: Caterpillar patrol continues

outdoor.notes: Outdoor mid-flower benefits from Kratky portability. Move the container to protect from weather — something you can't do with in-ground grows.

health_checks: Are buds still growing and packing on weight?
Trichomes milky or still clear? (Check with loupe)
Any signs of bud rot or mold in dense colas?
Is the natural fade beginning (lower leaves yellowing)?
EC in the reservoir stable or climbing?
stages.name: Mid/Peak Flower

nutrients.approach: Full-strength bloom. Heavy P and K for bud density. This is the feeding peak — the plant is eating and drinking at maximum capacity. Watch for the natural fade to begin near the end of this stage (lower fan leaves yellowing).

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Every top-off. Bloom-stage water is nutrient-rich and perfect for bacteria — Hydroguard keeps the good ones dominant.

supplements.name: Liquid Koolbloom (optional)
supplements.purpose: PK booster through mid-flower. Stop 2 weeks before harvest.

reservoir.notes: Peak bloom nutrients. The plant has a predictable drinking pattern now. Top off on the same schedule. EC management is crucial — Kratky concentrates nutrients with no water movement to dilute. If EC creeps above 1.8, dilute with plain water.

tasks.description: By now you know the drinking pattern. Top off on schedule. Check EC first: rising = plain water, dropping = nutrients. Add Hydroguard every time.
tasks.name: Routine top-off

tasks.description: Use jeweler's loupe or digital microscope. Clear = not ready. Milky/cloudy = peak THC. Amber = more CBN (couch-lock). Most growers harvest at mostly milky with 10-20% amber.
tasks.name: Check trichomes weekly

tasks.description: Lower fan leaves naturally yellow as the plant redirects nutrients to buds. Remove them to improve airflow and prevent rot. This is normal.
tasks.name: Remove yellowing fan leaves

tasks.description: Flower stage attracts thrips and spider mites. In Kratky, fungus gnats are attracted to the container's moisture. Check undersides of leaves and bud sites.
tasks.name: Monitor for pests

transition_signals: Trichomes transitioning from clear to milky

common_problems.cause: Strain is slow, light leaks, or environmental stress
common_problems.issue: Buds not finishing (still throwing white pistils)
common_problems.solution: Check for light leaks. Some strains take 10-12 weeks. If light leak is causing re-vegging, fix immediately. Patience.

common_problems.cause: Nutrient deficiency or too much flushing too soon
common_problems.solution: If trichomes aren't ready yet, don't start flushing. Maintain light feeding. The fade should be gradual, not sudden.

common_problems.cause: Salts accumulated in stagnant Kratky water over weeks
common_problems.solution: A proper flush (7-14 days of plain water) resolves this. Kratky actually makes flushing easy — just swap the water and let the plant finish.

stages.description: Buds are ripening and finishing. Trichomes maturing from clear → milky → amber. The plant's water consumption slows as it finishes. In Kratky, this is a calm phase — the air gap is stable, top-offs are less frequent, and the plant is winding down. Begin planning the flush.

environment.notes: Can reduce light intensity slightly as plant finishes. Wider day/night temp swing (10-15°F) enhances terpene development and brings out colors. Keep humidity low — dense buds trap moisture.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse provides frost protection. The structure buys you 2-4 extra weeks vs fully outdoor. Use supplemental heating on cold nights to maintain above 55°F.

extra_problems.cause: Warm inside, cool outside = condensation
extra_problems.solution: Ventilate briefly each morning. Use HAF fans. Anti-drip greenhouse film helps.

extra_tasks.description: Thermostat-controlled heater set to 55°F minimum overnight. Kratky water also cools — check water temp.
extra_tasks.name: Supplemental heating

extra_tasks.description: Fall greenhouse humidity rises. Run dehumidifier or ventilate during warmest part of day.
extra_tasks.name: End-of-season humidity watch

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse extends the season significantly. Supplemental heating is the key investment.

environment_overrides.notes: Late flower outdoors is October-November in many regions. First frost is the hard deadline. Kratky's portability is your biggest asset — you can bring the container inside overnight or during weather events. The natural temperature swings of fall produce exceptional terpene profiles.

extra_problems.cause: Growing season too short
extra_problems.issue: First frost before trichomes are ready
extra_problems.solution: Harvest immediately before frost. Next season, choose faster-finishing strains or start earlier. Autoflowers avoid this problem entirely.

extra_problems.cause: Fall weather patterns
extra_problems.solution: Move container to drier area. Increase airflow. Consider early harvest of lower buds while tops finish.

extra_tasks.description: Monitor forecasts for first frost. Move Kratky container inside overnight if temps below 45°F. A hard freeze kills the plant and ruins buds.
extra_tasks.name: Frost watch

extra_tasks.description: Cool, damp fall mornings are prime bud rot conditions. Check every dense cola daily.
extra_tasks.name: Daily bud rot check

extra_tasks.description: If nighttime temps drop below 45°F, bring the Kratky container inside a garage or shed overnight. This is the #1 advantage of Kratky over DWC — true portability.
extra_tasks.name: Move container inside at night

outdoor.notes: Late flower outdoors is a race against frost. Kratky portability lets you move the plant to safety — use this advantage aggressively.

health_checks: Trichome ratio: what percentage is milky vs amber?
Is the natural fade progressing (fan leaves yellowing)?
Any bud rot in dense colas?
Are buds feeling dense and sticky?
stages.name: Late Flower / Ripening

nutrients.approach: Reduce to 3/4 strength bloom. Stop all supplements except Hydroguard. The plant is finishing and excess nutrients will remain in the buds (harsh smoke). Begin the fade — the plant should start yellowing fan leaves naturally.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Continue until flush. Root protection doesn't stop.

reservoir.notes: Reduce nutrient strength. The plant is finishing and doesn't need heavy feeding. Water consumption slows. Top-offs become less frequent. Begin planning the flush — you'll need to swap to plain water soon.

tasks.description: Trichome maturity determines harvest timing. Watch for milky → amber transition. Your target amber percentage is personal preference: 10% amber = energetic high, 30% = more sedative.
tasks.name: Check trichomes (daily now)

tasks.description: Plant drinks less as it finishes. Top off less frequently but still maintain water above the root zone. Never let roots dry completely.
tasks.name: Reduced top-offs

tasks.description: The natural fade accelerates. Yellow and dead fan leaves can fall into the reservoir — remove them. In Kratky, organic debris in stagnant water is especially problematic.
tasks.name: Remove dead/dying fan leaves

tasks.description: Most growers flush 7-14 days before harvest. In Kratky, flush = drain to above water roots, refill to air gap line with plain pH'd water. Mark your calendar.
tasks.name: Plan flush timing

common_problems.cause: Normal — plant is cannibalizing fan leaves for remaining nutrients
common_problems.solution: No action needed. This is expected and desired. The yellowing and fading IS the flush working.

common_problems.cause: Stagnant plain water + decomposing root material
common_problems.issue: Foul smell from reservoir during flush
common_problems.solution: Do more frequent water changes (every 3 days instead of 5). Add extra Hydroguard. Remove any fallen leaves from the water.

stages.description: Remove all nutrients and let the plant use up stored reserves. In Kratky, this is straightforward: drain the nutrient solution down to just above the water roots, refill to the air gap line with plain pH'd water. The plant will consume stored nutrients from its tissues over the next 7-14 days, resulting in cleaner, smoother smoke.

environment.notes: Reduce light intensity slightly. Plant is finishing — no need to push growth anymore.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse flush proceeds like indoor — weather protected. Maintain ventilation to prevent humidity buildup.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse provides stable conditions for a full flush.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor flush is simple — drain nutrients, refill with plain water. If frost or heavy rain is imminent, skip the flush and harvest immediately. A clean harvest beats a moldy one. Kratky portability means you can move the container to shelter for the flush period.

extra_problems.cause: Incoming frost/rain
extra_problems.issue: Weather forces early harvest during flush
extra_problems.solution: Harvest immediately. A shorter flush won't noticeably affect quality — but mold will ruin everything.

extra_tasks.description: If a hard frost, multi-day rain, or storm is forecast within 3-5 days, harvest now regardless of flush status. Move container inside for the flush if weather is marginal.
extra_tasks.name: Weather-contingent harvest plan

outdoor.notes: Outdoor flush may be shortened or skipped based on weather. Move the Kratky container inside to finish flushing if needed.

health_checks: Are fan leaves yellowing and fading? (Good — plant is using stored nutrients)
Are buds still developing or have they stopped growing?
stages.name: Flush

nutrients.approach: No nutrients. Plain pH'd water only. Some growers add FloraKleen to help dissolve residual salts in the stagnant reservoir. The Kratky flush is actually very effective — the still water allows salts to settle and the plant can clean itself out efficiently.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root protection even during flush. Stagnant water still needs beneficial bacteria.

supplements.name: FloraKleen (optional)
supplements.purpose: Flushing agent that dissolves residual mineral salts. Especially useful in Kratky where salts accumulate over the grow.

reservoir.notes: Drain existing nutrient solution to just above water roots. Refill to the air gap line with plain pH'd water + Hydroguard. Repeat every 5 days for a thorough flush. The plant will yellow and fade — this is expected and desired.

tasks.description: Carefully drain nutrient solution to just above water roots (don't disturb air roots). Refill to the air gap line with plain pH'd water (5.8) + Hydroguard. Mark the water line.
tasks.name: Drain and refill with plain water

tasks.description: Continue daily trichome checks. Harvest when ratio is where you want it. Don't extend flush past 14 days — diminishing returns.
tasks.name: Monitor trichomes (daily)

tasks.description: Clean trimming scissors, set up drying area, prepare drying rack/lines. Ensure drying space can maintain 60°F / 60% RH in darkness.
tasks.name: Prepare harvest equipment

tasks.description: Some growers give 48 hours of total darkness before harvest. Believed to increase trichome production. Not scientifically proven but widely practiced.
tasks.name: 48-hour darkness (optional)

transition_signals: Plant has used up stored nutrients

common_problems.cause: Over-trimming removes trichome-covered sugar leaves
common_problems.solution: For personal use, leave some sugar leaves. For appearance, trim tight but save trim for edibles/hash.

common_problems.cause: Roots grew through net pot and anchored to container walls
common_problems.solution: Gently work the root mass free. Kratky air roots can grip container walls. If stuck, cut roots near the wall — you're done growing anyway.

stages.description: Cut plant, remove from container, trim, and hang to dry. Kratky harvest is simpler than DWC — no air pump to disconnect, no air stone to clean. Just pull the net pot from the container, drain, and clean.

environment.notes: Harvest in darkness or minimal light. Some growers harvest at end of dark period for highest trichome content.

environment_overrides.notes: Harvest inside the greenhouse, then move to a dedicated drying space. Don't dry in the greenhouse — too variable.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse harvest is straightforward. Move to indoor drying space.

environment_overrides.notes: Harvest outdoors then bring everything INSIDE for drying and curing. Never dry outdoors. Bud washing is highly recommended for outdoor Kratky harvests.

extra_problems.cause: Outdoor buds may contain insects or eggs
extra_problems.solution: Bud washing: dip branches in 3 buckets — H2O2 solution, lemon juice + baking soda water, plain water rinse. Shake off excess and hang.

extra_tasks.description: Outdoor plants are best harvested at first light, before the sun warms terpenes. Cooler temps preserve trichome quality.
extra_tasks.name: Harvest at dawn

extra_tasks.description: Scrub, sanitize, and store container for next season. Kratky containers need thorough cleaning — stagnant water leaves more mineral deposits.
extra_tasks.name: Clean container for storage

outdoor.notes: Bud washing is especially recommended for outdoor Kratky harvests. Removes pests, dust, and any spray residue.

stages.name: Harvest

tasks.description: Cut main stem at base. If wet trimming, trim fan leaves and sugar leaves immediately. If dry trimming, leave sugar leaves on for slower, higher-quality dry.
tasks.name: Cut plant at base

tasks.description: Pull net pot from container. No air stone or pump to disconnect — just lift and drain. The root mass (both air roots and water roots) will be impressive.
tasks.name: Remove from Kratky container

tasks.description: Drain all water. Scrub container and lid with hydrogen peroxide or dilute bleach solution. Rinse thoroughly. Kratky containers accumulate more mineral deposits than DWC — soak in vinegar solution to dissolve.
tasks.name: Clean container for next grow

tasks.description: Wet trim: remove all fan leaves, most sugar leaves, hang individual branches. Dry trim: hang whole plant or large branches. Dry trim = slower dry = better quality.
tasks.name: Wet trim or whole-plant hang

tasks.description: Note: date, wet weight, days in flower, strain, trichome ratio, container size, total top-offs performed, any issues. This data improves future Kratky grows.
tasks.name: Record harvest details

transition_signals: Plant is cut, trimmed, and hanging

common_problems.cause: Too warm, too dry, or too much airflow
common_problems.issue: Drying too fast (crispy outside, wet inside)
common_problems.solution: Lower temp. Raise humidity. Remove direct fan on buds. Aim for 10-14 day dry.

common_problems.cause: Dried too fast — chlorophyll didn't break down
common_problems.solution: Prevention only — can't fix after. Slow, cool, dark drying prevents this. Curing may help slightly.

common_problems.cause: Humidity too high, dense buds, poor airflow
common_problems.solution: Remove affected buds. Lower humidity. Increase gentle air circulation.

stages.description: Hang trimmed buds in a dark room at 60°F and 60% humidity. The slow dry preserves terpenes and chlorophyll breaks down properly. Identical process regardless of grow method — drying is drying.

environment.notes: 60°F / 60% RH — the 60/60 rule. Total darkness. Gentle air circulation (not directly on buds). A 10-14 day dry is ideal. Faster = hay smell. Slower = better flavor.

environment_overrides.notes: Do NOT dry in the greenhouse. Move to a proper indoor drying space with controlled temp and humidity.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse-grown buds dry the same as indoor. Use a proper drying room.

environment_overrides.notes: Dry INDOORS in a controlled space — never outdoors. A closet, spare room, or basement with 60°F/60% RH works.

extra_problems.cause: Home heating system drying the air
extra_problems.solution: Use a room away from heating vents. Add a humidifier. Monitor closely.

extra_tasks.description: Dip harvested branches in 3 buckets: 1) H2O2 solution, 2) lemon + baking soda water, 3) plain rinse. Shake off excess and hang. Removes outdoor contaminants.
extra_tasks.name: Bud wash before hanging (recommended)

outdoor.notes: Drying is the same regardless of grow method. Bud washing is the main extra step for outdoor harvests.

health_checks: Any smell of ammonia? (Too wet/fast = chlorophyll not breaking down)
stages.name: Drying

tasks.description: Hang branches on drying lines or use a drying rack. Space buds so air circulates between them. Darkness is essential — light degrades THC.
tasks.name: Hang buds to dry

tasks.description: 60°F/60% RH target. Use humidifier if too dry, dehumidifier if too humid. Small adjustments matter. Check twice daily.
tasks.name: Monitor temperature and humidity

tasks.description: After day 5, start checking daily. Small stems should snap (not bend). Buds should feel dry on outside but slightly spongy when squeezed. Don't over-dry.
tasks.name: Check drying progress

tasks.description: Use a small fan pointed at the wall (not at buds). Air should circulate without directly blowing on buds. No oscillating fans aimed at drying buds.
tasks.name: Maintain gentle airflow

transition_signals: Small stems snap when bent (don't bend)
Buds feel dry on outside but slightly spongy when squeezed

common_problems.cause: Buds weren't dry enough before jarring
common_problems.solution: Remove affected buds. Remove all buds from jar, dry for 12-24 more hours, rejar.

common_problems.cause: Too much moisture — anaerobic bacteria starting
common_problems.solution: Leave jar open for several hours. May need to lay buds on drying rack for 12-24 hours.

common_problems.cause: Over-dried before jarring or jar left open too long
common_problems.solution: Add Boveda 62% humidity pack. It will slowly restore moisture.

stages.description: Buds placed in sealed glass jars. Moisture equalizes. Chlorophyll breaks down. Terpenes mature. Flavor and smoothness dramatically improve over 2-8+ weeks. Identical process regardless of grow method.

environment.notes: Store jars in cool, dark place. Room temperature is fine but cooler is better for long cures. Never let jars get direct sunlight — UV degrades THC.

environment_overrides.notes: Curing is identical regardless of grow environment. Store jars in a cool, dark indoor space — not in the greenhouse.

greenhouse.notes: No greenhouse-specific curing differences.

environment_overrides.notes: Curing is identical regardless of grow environment. Store jars in cool, dark, indoor space. Kratky-grown flower from outdoor grows often develops unique terpene profiles from the passive root system's interaction with natural temperature swings.

outdoor.notes: No outdoor-specific curing differences. All curing happens indoors in jars.

health_checks: When you open a jar, do you smell terpenes (good) or ammonia (bad)?
Are buds maintaining the right moisture (slightly springy, not wet)?
Any white fuzz (mold) on any buds?
Are buds getting smoother to smoke each week?
stages.name: Curing

tasks.description: Place dried buds loosely in wide-mouth mason jars (fill 75% full). Don't pack tight — buds need air space. Close lids.
tasks.name: Jar buds

tasks.description: Open jars for 5-15 minutes, 2-3 times per day for first 2 weeks. This releases moisture and prevents mold. If buds feel wet, leave jars open for 1 hour.
tasks.name: Burp jars (week 1-2)

tasks.description: Reduce burping to once per day for 5-10 minutes. Buds should feel consistently dry on outside.
tasks.name: Burp jars (week 3-4)

tasks.description: Burp once per week. Add Boveda 62% pack for hands-off humidity control. Buds continue improving for months. Peak quality at 6-8 weeks.
tasks.name: Long-term cure (week 5+)

container_progression.commercial_medium: Turkey bags in sealed bins, nitrogen-flushed
container_progression.industrial: Climate-controlled vault, nitrogen generators, automated monitoring

degradation_timeline.12_18_months: Significant decline. Consider processing into extracts.
degradation_timeline.18_plus_months: Convert to extracts, edibles, or topicals.

testing_schedule.360_days: Full panel — sell, process, or destroy decision

common_problems.cause: Heat, light, oxygen, or time
common_problems.solution: Ensure darkness, cool temps (60°F), minimal oxygen. ~5%/year loss is baseline under ideal conditions.

common_problems.cause: Temps above 70°F, oxygen, frequent opening
common_problems.solution: Keep below 65°F. Nitrogen-sealed containers. Minimize opening.

common_problems.cause: Humidity above 65% or improper dry/cure
common_problems.solution: Check humidity. Remove affected material. Ensure 58-62% RH before sealing.

common_problems.cause: Normal moisture equilibration (1-3% first month)
common_problems.solution: Boveda packs minimize loss. Sealed containers reduce ongoing loss.

stages.description: Post-cure long-term storage. After 4-8 weeks of active curing, flower transitions to storage. Kratky's simplicity ends at harvest — storage is identical across all grow methods. Proper storage preserves potency and terpenes for 6-12+ months. THC degrades to CBN (~5%/year under ideal conditions, much faster with heat/light/oxygen).

environment.notes: DARK. Cool. Stable. Zero light — UV destroys cannabinoids and terpenes. Commercial: 58-62°F, 55-60% RH, complete darkness, nitrogen atmosphere. Home: dark closet, cool room.

environment_overrides.notes: Do NOT store in greenhouse — temp swings.

greenhouse.notes: Store in climate-controlled indoor space.

environment_overrides.notes: Storage is always indoor.

outdoor.notes: Indoor controlled environment.

stages.name: Long-Term Storage

nutrients.approach: None.

tasks.description: Home: mason jars with Boveda 58-62% packs. Commercial: nitrogen-sealed grove bags, CVault containers, or nitrogen-flushed drums for bulk. Remove oxygen — it degrades cannabinoids.
tasks.name: Transfer to long-term containers

tasks.description: Strain, harvest date, storage date, weight, batch number. Commercial: full seed-to-sale tracking, FIFO rotation.
tasks.name: Label and track batches

tasks.description: Inspect for mold, off odors. Check humidity packs. Spot-check trichomes (amber increase = degradation). Commercial: potency/terpene testing at 30/90/180 days.
tasks.name: Monthly quality checks

tasks.description: Monitor temp/humidity. No light leaks. Commercial: environmental monitoring with alerts.
tasks.name: Maintain environment

tasks.description: First in, first out. Flag batches approaching 12 months for priority sale or processing.
tasks.name: Rotate stock (FIFO)

tasks.description: Commercial: retain testing samples per regulations. Track chain of custody.
tasks.name: Compliance testing holds

autoflower.description: Ruderalis-hybrid strains that flower automatically regardless of light schedule. Fixed timeline.

light_schedule.flower: 20/4 or 18/6 (same as veg — no change needed)
light_schedule.transition: Automatic — plant decides when to flower (usually week 3-4)

autoflower.name: Autoflower
autoflower.notes: Fixed timeline — stress cannot be recovered by extending veg. Start in final pot (no transplant shock). LST preferred over HST.

training_suitability.defoliation: light only — auto can't recover from heavy defol
training_suitability.topping: risky — only if plant is vigorous

photoperiod.description: Standard cannabis strains that require 12/12 light to flower. Grower controls timing.

light_schedule.transition: Switch to 12/12 when plant is ~50% of desired final height

photoperiod.name: Photoperiod
photoperiod.notes: Can be vegged as long as desired. Cloneable. More forgiving of stress (can extend veg to recover).

training_suitability.defoliation: good
training_suitability.topping: excellent

total_grow_days.breakdown: Germination (3-7d) + Seedling (7-14d) + Veg (28-63d) + Flower (56-70d) + Dry (7-14d) + Cure (14-60d)

causes: Light leak allowing algae to colonize roots
Organic debris (dead leaves) decomposing in reservoir
solutions: Add Hydroguard immediately (3 ml/gal for emergency dose)
Do a partial water swap: drain to above water roots, refill with fresh pH'd water + Hydroguard to the air gap line
Cool the water — frozen water bottles if needed
Seal all light leaks in the container
Remove any fallen leaves or debris from reservoir
If severe, trim off dead brown roots (leave healthy white ones)

causes: FloraMicro and other nutrients naturally stain roots brown
More visible in Kratky because roots sit in the same solution longer
solutions: No action needed — this is cosmetic
Healthy roots are firm and slightly fuzzy even when stained
Root rot roots are SLIMY and SMELL — stained roots are neither

causes: Water level too far below the net pot
No wicking material to bridge the gap
Humidity too low around the root zone
problems.diagnosis: Seedling roots haven't bridged the gap to the water surface
solutions: Ensure water level is 1 inch below the net pot bottom during seedling stage
Use a wick (cotton rope) from the net pot to the water
Ensure the container lid is sealed to trap humidity around the root zone
Some growers hand-water the hydroton from above until roots reach the water
problems.symptom: Roots not reaching water / slow root growth

causes: Plants consuming nutrients causes pH to rise — normal in all hydro but worse in Kratky because no air bubbles to stabilize pH
Low buffering capacity in the solution
problems.diagnosis: Normal Kratky pH behavior, but needs management
solutions: Check and adjust pH at every top-off (use pH Down)
Use RO water if source water is very alkaline
Accept slight drift between top-offs — plants tolerate 5.5-6.5 range
Don't chase pH daily if you can't top off — adjust at each top-off event

problems.diagnosis: Uncommon in Kratky — indicates a serious issue
solutions: Check roots for rot (slimy = rot, firm = healthy)
If root rot: treat root issue first (see Root Issues above)
Do a full water swap with fresh, properly mixed nutrients + Hydroguard
Check source water pH before adding anything

causes: Water evaporating faster than plant uptake — concentrating dissolved salts
solutions: Top off with plain pH'd water (no nutrients) until EC drops to target
Seal container lid tightly to reduce evaporation
If chronic (EC above 2.0 repeatedly): do a partial water swap — drain to above water roots, refill with half-strength nutrients + Hydroguard
Move container to cooler location if possible

causes: EC climbed due to evaporation concentration (most common in Kratky)
problems.diagnosis: EC too high — salts burning leaf margins
solutions: Top off with plain pH'd water to dilute
If EC is above 2.0, do a partial water swap
Reduce nutrient strength at next mix
Damaged tips won't heal — watch for progression on new growth

causes: Nutrient solution too dilute (EC too low)
pH out of range preventing nitrogen uptake
Plant outgrowing the available nutrients in the reservoir
solutions: Check EC — if low, top off with full-strength nutrients
Check pH — nitrogen absorption drops above 6.5
In large plants with small containers, top-offs may not provide enough nitrogen — consider a partial water swap with fresh veg nutrients
problems.symptom: Yellowing lower leaves in veg (not during flush)

causes: Light gaps around the net pot or lid
Using a white or translucent container
solutions: Wrap container in black duct tape or aluminum foil
Seal all gaps around the net pot with neoprene collar or tape
Replace with a proper opaque container
Algae competes with roots for oxygen and nutrients — more damaging in Kratky than DWC because there's no aeration to offset it
problems.symptom: Green algae growth in reservoir or on roots

causes: Hot grow room or direct sunlight on container
No air pump cooling (DWC's air pump provides evaporative cooling that Kratky lacks)
Dark container absorbing heat from grow light
problems.diagnosis: Heat stress risk + increased pathogen risk
solutions: Frozen water bottles in the reservoir (replace every 12 hours)
Move container away from light heat
Insulate container with reflective material (Reflectix)
Increase Hydroguard dose to 3 ml/gal when water is warm
If chronic: consider a small water chiller or move to a cooler room

cons: RO system cost and waste water
water_source_profiles.name: Reverse Osmosis (RO) Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Gold standard for precision growing. Always add CalMag first when using RO.
pros: Blank slate — total control over mineral content

dechlorination.chloramine: Does NOT gas off — must use Campden tablets or activated carbon filter
dechlorination.chlorine: Let sit uncovered 24 hours (gasses off) or use dechlorinator drops
dechlorination.how_to_tell: Check city water report. Most modern cities use chloramine.

water_source_profiles.name: Municipal Tap Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Good enough for most grows. Dechlorinate and pH-adjust. Get a water report from your city.

cons: May contain iron, sulfur, or heavy metals
water_source_profiles.name: Well Water
water_source_profiles.notes: MUST get a water test before using. High iron or sulfur can cause lockout. Great if your well has clean water with moderate minerals.

water_source_profiles.name: Distilled Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Same as RO but purchased instead of filtered. Only practical for 1-2 plant grows.

advanced_techniques.description: Elevating CO2 from ambient (400ppm) to 1000-1500ppm increases photosynthesis rate by 20-40% when light and temperature are also increased.

methods.notes: Most common. Tank refills every 2-4 weeks for a tent.
Produces heat and humidity — better for large rooms with AC.
Produces ~100-200ppm boost. Only useful for small sealed spaces. Not worth it for most.

advanced_techniques.name: CO2 Supplementation

ppfd_co2_correlation.notes: Ambient CO2 sufficient at low light
Slight benefit from supplementation
Moderate benefit — cost-effective threshold
Strong benefit — diminishing returns above this
Maximum practical benefit for most grows
Commercial intensity — requires perfect environment

requirements: Sealed grow space (CO2 escapes quickly from leaky rooms)
High light intensity (>600 PPFD — plants can't use extra CO2 without sufficient light)
Higher temperature tolerance (82-86°F optimal with CO2 vs 75-80°F without)
CO2 controller or timer

advanced_techniques.description: Manipulating environmental and irrigation parameters to push the plant toward vegetative or generative (flowering/fruiting) growth.
advanced_techniques.name: Crop Steering
advanced_techniques.notes: Crop steering is most effective in hydro/coco where irrigation frequency is highly controllable. Soil growers have less precision due to soil buffering.

vegetative_steering.goal: More leaf/stem growth, larger plant framework

best_practices: Spray during lights-off or first/last 30 minutes of light cycle (stomata open, no light burn)
Use fine mist sprayer — coverage is key
pH foliar solution to 6.2-6.5 (leaf surface slightly higher pH than root zone)
EC should be very low (0.2-0.5) — leaves burn easily
Never foliar spray during flower (moisture in buds → mold/bud rot)
Best for micronutrient deficiency correction (CalMag, iron, magnesium)

common_foliar_products.frequency: Weekly preventive, 3-day intervals for active infestations

advanced_techniques.description: Spraying nutrient solution directly onto leaves for rapid uptake. Bypasses root zone entirely.
advanced_techniques.name: Foliar Feeding

advanced_techniques.description: Strengthens cell walls, increases stem thickness, improves heat/drought/pest resistance.
important_notes: ALWAYS add silica to water FIRST before other nutrients (raises pH significantly)
Let silica mix for 5-10 minutes before adding base nutrients
Silica is incompatible with concentrated nutrient solutions — never mix undiluted
Discontinue in late flower (last 2-3 weeks)
advanced_techniques.name: Silica (Potassium Silicate)

advanced_techniques.description: Introduces beneficial bacteria/fungi to the root zone to outcompete pathogens, improve nutrient uptake, and protect against root diseases.
important_notes: Do NOT use hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) with beneficial microbes — it kills them
Choose either sterile (H2O2) OR beneficial (Hydroguard) approach — not both
Beneficials need warmish water (65-75°F) to thrive
Reapply after any reservoir change or system cleaning
advanced_techniques.name: Beneficial Microbes & Root Inoculants

products.name: Hydroguard
products.notes: The standard for DWC/RDWC. Add at every res change.

products.dosing: 1 tsp/gal (water-in) or dust roots at transplant
products.name: Great White
products.notes: Best for soil/coco. Less effective in pure hydro.

products.name: Recharge
products.notes: Feeds existing soil microbes. Great for living soil.

products.name: Mammoth P
products.notes: Most effective in flower when P demand is highest.

advanced_techniques.description: Removing select fan leaves to improve light penetration, airflow, and direct energy to bud sites.
advanced_techniques.name: Defoliation (Strategic Leaf Removal)
rules: Never remove more than 20-30% of leaves at once (except schwazze technique)
Only defoliate healthy, vigorous plants — never stressed ones
Autoflowers: very light defoliation only — they can't recover from heavy removal
Always leave the top 2-3 fan leaves on each branch (they feed the bud below)

timing.method: Remove lower leaves that get no light (lollipop)
Major defoliation day 1 and day 21 of flower (schwazzing)
Selective tuck or remove leaves blocking bud sites

equipment.description: Bigger = better for living soil. No-till beds or large fabric pots (minimum 15 gal for no-till).
equipment.name: Large fabric pots (15-30 gal) or raised bed

equipment.description: High-quality compost + peat/coco + aeration. BuildASoil, KIS, or custom mix.
equipment.name: Living soil base mix

equipment.description: Slow-release organic inputs that biology breaks down. Applied as top-dress.
equipment.name: Dry amendments (neem, kelp, crustacean)

equipment.description: High-quality vermicompost. Microbial inoculant + slow-release nutrition.
equipment.name: Worm castings

equipment.description: Straw, leaves, or cover crop residue. Protects soil surface and feeds biology.
equipment.name: Mulch material

equipment.description: Clover, crimson clover, tillage radish, rye. Fixes nitrogen, prevents erosion, feeds microbes.
equipment.name: Cover crop seeds

equipment.description: Endo-mycorrhizae (Glomus intraradices). Apply at planting. Extends root network 100x.
equipment.name: Mycorrhizal inoculant

equipment.description: 5-gallon bucket + air pump + air stone. Brews aerated compost tea in 24-48h.
equipment.name: Compost tea brewer

equipment.description: Continuous supply of fresh castings. Red wigglers (Eisenia fetida).
equipment.name: Worm bin

equipment.description: Probe to check soil moisture at depth. Avoid over/under watering.
equipment.name: Moisture meter

equipment.description: Monitor soil temp during cooking phase. Below 70°F = safe to plant.
equipment.name: Soil thermometer

equipment.description: View soil biology: bacteria, fungi, protozoa, nematodes. Verify soil health.
equipment.name: Microscope (400x)

equipment.description: Korean Natural Farming ferments. Fermented plant juice, fruit juice, lactic acid bacteria.
equipment.name: KNF inputs (FPJ, FFJ, LAB)

equipment.description: Broad-spectrum minerals. Slow-release via biological breakdown.
equipment.name: Rock dust (glacial/basalt)

equipment.description: Charged biochar provides microbial habitat. Lasts decades in soil.
equipment.name: Biochar

flush_timing.coco: 7-14 days reduced nutrients → plain water last 3-5 days
flush_timing.notes: Flushing is debated — some studies show no difference. At minimum, reduce nutrients in final week.
flush_timing.soil: 14-21 days plain water (soil holds nutrients longer)

other_harvest_indicators: Most pistils (hairs) have darkened and curled in
Calyxes are swollen and dense
Fan leaves are yellowing and falling off naturally
Trichome stalks are visible and heads are enlarged
Breeder's recommended flowering time has passed

trichome_ratios.notes: Harvest early for more THC-dominant, less CBN. Energetic, racy high. Slight reduction in yield.
Standard harvest window. Mix of THC and CBN. Full-spectrum effects.
Extended harvest for max CBN conversion. Heavy body high. Good for pain/sleep. Slightly degraded THC.

cover_cropping.management: Let grow 2-4 weeks. Chop at soil surface before planting cannabis. Leave residue as mulch. Never pull roots.
cover_cropping.purpose: Keeps soil alive between cycles. Fixes nitrogen. Prevents erosion. Feeds biology. Aerates with roots.

FFJ.name: Fermented Fruit Juice
FFJ.use: Soil drench in flower. P-K boost + beneficial microbes.

FPJ.ingredients: Fresh plant growth tips + brown sugar
FPJ.name: Fermented Plant Juice
FPJ.use: Foliar or soil drench. Growth stimulant.

LAB.ingredients: Rice wash water → ferment → milk separation
LAB.name: Lactic Acid Bacteria
LAB.use: Soil inoculant. Powerful decomposer. Suppresses pathogens.

OHN.ingredients: Garlic, ginger, licorice, cinnamon, angelica in alcohol
OHN.name: Oriental Herbal Nutrient

cycle_workflow: 1. Harvest: cut plant at soil surface, leave roots
2. Top-dress: add fresh amendments (neem, kelp, crustacean, compost)
3. Plant cover crop: scatter seed on surface, water in
4. Rest: 2-4 weeks while amendments break down and cover crop grows
5. Chop cover crop: cut at soil surface, leave as mulch
6. Plant: transplant next cannabis seedling directly
no_till_methodology.principle: Never disturb the soil. Build once, improve forever. Each cycle adds to the ecosystem.

soil_improvement_over_time.cycle_1: Good. Soil cooking period establishes baseline biology.
soil_improvement_over_time.cycle_2: Better. Previous roots decomposed. Biology more diverse.
soil_improvement_over_time.cycle_3_plus: Excellent. Self-sustaining ecosystem. Water-only with no issues.

bacteria.role: Decompose fresh organic matter. Mineralize nutrients. First responders.

earthworms.feed_with: Organic matter on surface. They self-regulate population.
earthworms.role: Aerate soil. Produce castings (perfect balanced fertilizer). Mix layers.

fungi.feed_with: Woody mulch, humic acids, old growth forest duff
fungi.role: Decompose woody/complex organics. Mycorrhizae extend root network. Move nutrients long distances.

mycorrhizae.feed_with: Plant alive! They feed off root exudates. Don't use excessive P fertilizer (reduces colonization).
mycorrhizae.role: Symbiotic root fungi. Trade phosphorus and water to plant in exchange for sugars. Extend root reach 100-1000x.

nematodes.role: Eat bacteria/fungi/other nematodes. Release nutrients. Beneficial species dominate healthy soil.

protozoa.feed_with: They follow bacteria — feed bacteria and protozoa come.
protozoa.role: Eat bacteria, releasing plant-available nutrients (bacterial grazing cycle).

amendments_per_cubic_foot.purpose: Slow-release N, pest deterrent, biology food
Chitin (triggers plant immune response), slow-release N-P-Ca

components.purpose: Oxygen to roots, drainage, prevents compaction

cooking_instructions.method: Mix all components. Moisten to 'wrung-out sponge' consistency. Cover loosely. Let sit 2-4 weeks.
cooking_instructions.readiness_test: Plant a lettuce seed. If it grows normally, soil is ready. If it burns/dies, wait longer.
cooking_instructions.temperature: Soil may heat to 100-130°F during cooking (composting action). Wait until it cools to < 80°F before planting.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Micro → Gro → Bloom (always Micro first)
nutrient_brands.name: General Hydroponics Flora Trio

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Part A → Epsom → Part B (dissolve each fully before adding next)
nutrient_brands.name: Jack's 321
nutrient_brands.notes: Jack's 321 is 3.6g Part A, 2.4g Cal Nit, 1.2g Epsom per gallon at full strength. Extremely cost-effective for large grows.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Core → Bloom
nutrient_brands.name: Athena Pro Line
nutrient_brands.notes: Commercial-grade. Simple 2-part with integrated CalMag. Popular in commercial cannabis facilities.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: A → B (separate for veg and bloom)
nutrient_brands.name: Advanced Nutrients pH Perfect (Sensi)
nutrient_brands.notes: Self-adjusting pH technology (claims no pH adjustment needed). Expensive but popular for beginners who struggle with pH.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Shake VERY well before use (thick concentrate settles). One bottle at a time.
nutrient_brands.name: General Hydroponics FloraNova
nutrient_brands.notes: Simplest option — one bottle per phase. Great for beginners. Shake well — concentrate separates.
nutrient_brands.type: 1-part (two bottles — one for veg, one for bloom)

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Dissolve powder in warm water, stir well
nutrient_brands.name: Greenleaf Nutrients Mega Crop
nutrient_brands.notes: One powder does everything — includes CalMag. Incredibly cost-effective at ~$0.02/gal. Community favorite on r/microgrowery.

environment.container: Glass mason jars (quart size). Fill 75% full.

humidity_packs.boveda_58: For drier preference or long-term storage.
humidity_packs.boveda_62: Maintains 62% RH. Set-and-forget. Replace when crispy.
humidity_packs.grove_bags: Heat-sealed bags with built-in humidity regulation. No burping needed.

indicators_of_good_cure: Smooth, flavorful smoke — no harshness or hay smell
Buds are slightly sticky but not wet
Strong terpene aroma when jar is opened
Ash burns to white/light gray (not black)
method: Trim buds to final shape (dry trim or finish wet trim)
Place in mason jars — fill 3/4 full to allow air exchange
Week 1: burp 2-3x daily for 10-15 minutes (release moisture and gases)
Week 2-4: burp 1x daily for 5-10 minutes
Month 2+: burp 1x weekly or seal completely
Cure for minimum 2 weeks, ideal is 4-8 weeks. Some strains improve for 6+ months.

common_mistakes: Drying too fast (>3 days) — harsh smoke, hay smell, terpene loss
Drying too slow (>14 days) — mold risk, diminishing returns
Fan blowing directly on buds — uneven drying, crispy exterior/moist interior
Temperature too high (>70°F) — terpene evaporation, harsh taste

environment.airflow: Gentle circulation — fan NOT pointed at buds. Just keep air moving.

method: Cut branches 12-18 inches long
Optionally remove large fan leaves (wet trim) or leave intact (dry trim)
Hang upside down on line/rack with 6+ inches between branches
Small branches should snap (not bend) when done — 5-7 days minimum
Stems should snap cleanly; if they bend, keep drying

long_term_storage.degradation: THC slowly converts to CBN over time. Light, heat, air, and moisture accelerate degradation.
long_term_storage.method: Sealed mason jars or vacuum-sealed bags with Boveda 58% in dark, cool place
long_term_storage.notes: Properly cured and stored cannabis can remain excellent for 12+ months. Freezing is debated — can damage trichomes if not done carefully.

critical_rules: NEVER use synthetic fertilizers, H2O2, or fungicides — kills biology
NEVER pull roots out at harvest (no-till principle)
Always keep soil covered (mulch or cover crop)
Let soil cook 2-4 weeks before first planting
Bigger pot = better results (minimum 15 gal for no-till)
Trust the process — living soil starts slow but catches up
key_challenges: 2-4 week soil build/cook time before planting
Fungus gnats attracted to organic matter
quick_reference.maintenance_level: Very low — water only during grow. Soil building is the investment.
quick_reference.method_summary: Feed the soil, not the plant. Build a thriving microbiome that delivers nutrition on demand. Water-only growing after initial soil build.

watering_approach.frequency: Every 2-4 days depending on pot size, plant size, and environment
watering_approach.method: Wet-dry cycle: water thoroughly, then let top inch dry before next watering

scale_tiers.description: First-time grower or single-plant hobbyist. Manual management, learning-focused.
scale_tiers.name: Solo / Single Plant
scale_tiers.notes: Hands-on learning experience. Manual checks 1-2x daily. Ideal for beginners.

scale_tiers.description: Hobbyist tent grow. Light automation helpful but not required.
scale_tiers.name: Small Tent (2-8 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Timer-controlled lights, humidity controller. Sensor monitoring recommended.

scale_tiers.description: Dedicated grower with multiple tents/rooms. Automation saves significant time.
scale_tiers.name: Multi-Tent (9-24 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Multiple environments to manage. Automated alerts essential. Consider perpetual harvest.

scale_tiers.description: Licensed commercial operation. Full automation, compliance tracking, batch management.
scale_tiers.name: Commercial Room (25-100 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: SOPs required. Staff training. Regulatory compliance. Insurance requirements.

scale_tiers.description: Large-scale production facility. Enterprise automation, SCADA integration, dedicated staff.
scale_tiers.name: Warehouse Scale (100+ plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Multiple rooms, dedicated HVAC, industrial nutrient dosing, video surveillance.

common_problems.cause: Soil still actively composting (too fresh)
common_problems.solution: Let soil cook longer. Test: plant lettuce seed — if it grows, soil is ready.

common_problems.cause: Too wet + fungal imbalance in new soil
common_problems.solution: Add more aeration. Reduce watering. Dust seed with mycorrhizae.

stages.description: Germinate in moist paper towel or directly in living soil. Soil should have been cooking (composting) for 2-4 weeks minimum before planting. Microbes are already active.

environment_overrides.notes: Living soil beds thrive in greenhouses.
Direct sow in established bed works great.

health_checks: Soil temp safe (not hot from composting)?
stages.name: Germination

nutrients.approach: None needed — soil provides. Germinate in plain water or directly in soil.

soil.notes: Soil should have been built and cooking 2-4 weeks prior. Biology active. No hot spots.

tasks.description: Soil has been cooking. No ammonia smell. Worms active if present.
tasks.name: Verify soil readiness

tasks.description: Paper towel method or direct sow in soil at 1/4 inch depth.
tasks.name: Germinate seeds

tasks.description: Light mist. Don't disturb soil structure.
tasks.name: Moisten surface

common_problems.cause: Normal for living soil — biology establishing
common_problems.solution: Patience. Living soil starts slow but accelerates in veg. Don't add synthetic fertilizer.

common_problems.cause: Organic matter attracting gnats
common_problems.solution: Yellow sticky traps. Top layer of pumice/perlite. BTI. Predatory mites.

stages.description: Seedling grows in living soil. Mycorrhizal colonization beginning. Only water — no feeding needed. Soil biology does the work.

environment_overrides.notes: Protected environment for biology establishment.
Natural soil biology enhanced by outdoor conditions.

stages.name: Seedling

nutrients.approach: Water only. Soil biology feeds the plant.

soil.notes: Water only. Soil provides all nutrition via microbial activity.

tasks.description: Small amounts around seedling. Don't flood. Maintain even moisture.
tasks.name: Water gently

tasks.description: Healthy soil shows white mycelium threads. Good sign!
tasks.name: Observe soil surface

tasks.description: Thin layer of straw or leaves. Protects biology.
tasks.name: Mulch lightly

tasks.description: No tilling, poking, or compacting. Let biology establish.
tasks.name: Avoid disturbing soil

common_problems.cause: Normal — living soil is slower in early veg
common_problems.solution: Trust the process. Growth explodes in late veg. Don't add salts.

common_problems.cause: Soil may need more nitrogen — microbes not mineralizing enough
common_problems.solution: Neem meal top-dress. Alfalfa tea. Don't panic — minor yellowing is normal.

stages.description: Mycorrhizal network fully colonized. Plant growth accelerates. Still water-only in well-built soil. Can add botanical teas or KNF inputs as supplemental biology boosts.

environment_overrides.notes: Controlled watering ideal for wet-dry cycles.
Outdoor living soil benefits from natural rainfall diversity.

extra_tasks.description: Rainwater ideal for living soil — microbe-friendly.
extra_tasks.name: Collect rainwater

stages.name: Early Vegetative

nutrients.approach: Water only in well-built soil. Optional: compost tea or aloe vera foliar.

soil.notes: Biology in full swing. Mycorrhizae extending root network 100x. Water-only or light teas.

tasks.description: Wet-dry cycle: water when top inch dry. Let biology breathe.
tasks.name: Water to maintain moisture

tasks.description: Aerated compost tea as soil drench. Boosts biology.
tasks.name: Compost tea (optional)

tasks.description: If cover crop growing, let it. Feeds soil. Chop and drop before it competes.
tasks.name: Observe cover crop

tasks.description: Gentle LST. Avoid high-stress techniques that damage biology recovery.
tasks.name: Begin training

common_problems.cause: No wet-dry cycle — anaerobic conditions
common_problems.solution: Let soil dry between waterings. Biology needs oxygen. Add perlite if soil too dense.

common_problems.cause: pH issue or biology crash from fungicide/H2O2
common_problems.solution: NEVER use H2O2, fungicides, or synthetic in living soil. Re-inoculate with compost tea.

stages.description: Explosive growth. Living soil plants catch up and often surpass synthetic-fed plants at this stage. Mycorrhizal network at peak. Optional top-dress for heavy feeders.

environment_overrides.notes: Ventilate well in late veg.
Peak growing season. Living soil at its best outdoors.

stages.name: Late Vegetative

nutrients.approach: Water only. Optional: neem/kelp/crustacean meal top-dress for heavy feeders.

soil.notes: Peak biological activity. Optional top-dress if heavy feeder strain.

tasks.description: Water thoroughly, then let dry to top inch. Oxygen for roots + microbes.
tasks.name: Water wet-dry cycles

tasks.description: 1-2 tbsp neem + kelp meal per gallon of soil. Only if plant shows hunger.
tasks.name: Top-dress (if needed)

tasks.description: Cut cover crop at soil level. Leave on surface as mulch. Feeds soil.
tasks.name: Chop and drop cover crop

tasks.description: Gentle LST/SCROG. Living soil plants are vigorous.
tasks.name: Train canopy

common_problems.cause: Soil phosphorus locked or depleted
common_problems.solution: Bone meal top-dress takes 1-2 weeks. Liquid fish bone meal for faster availability.

stages.description: Flip to 12/12. Plants stretch. In living soil, transition is smooth — biology adapts to plant's changing demands. Optional bloom top-dress.

environment_overrides.notes: Blackout covers if needed.
Natural photoperiod. Top-dress as days shorten.

stages.name: Transition (Stretch)

nutrients.approach: Top-dress: bone meal + langbeinite (or bloom dry amendment). Water only otherwise.

soil.notes: Top-dress bloom amendments now. Microbes will mineralize by the time buds form.

tasks.description: Switch to 12/12.
tasks.name: Flip light schedule

tasks.description: Bone meal (P), langbeinite (K-Mg-S), or all-purpose bloom dry amendment.
tasks.name: Bloom top-dress

tasks.description: LST as needed.
tasks.name: Manage stretch

tasks.description: Stretching plants drink more. Maintain wet-dry cycle.
tasks.name: Water increase

common_problems.cause: Calcium demand spikes in flower, biology slow to deliver
common_problems.solution: Gypsum top-dress. Dolomite lime. Cal-mag foliar (organic).

stages.description: Bud sites forming. Living soil delivers broad-spectrum nutrition — terpene profiles are often superior due to diverse mineral availability from biological breakdown.

environment_overrides.notes: Airflow critical as buds develop.
Watch for caterpillars in buds.

stages.name: Early Flower

nutrients.approach: Water only. Soil provides. Optional: coconut water (natural cytokinins) as drench.

soil.notes: Bloom amendments mineralizing. Biology delivering P-K to roots on demand.

tasks.description: Flowering plants need consistent moisture. Don't over-dry.
tasks.name: Water consistently

tasks.description: 1 tbsp/gal raw coconut water. Natural cytokinins boost flower sites.
tasks.name: Coconut water drench (optional)

tasks.description: Remove lower fan leaves. Light defoliation only.
tasks.name: Defoliate for airflow

common_problems.cause: Soil depleted — first cycle or undersized pot
common_problems.solution: Top-dress bloom amendments. Larger pot next cycle (15+ gal for no-till). Build soil richer.

common_problems.cause: Living soil + mulch = higher humidity near soil surface
common_problems.solution: Defoliate. Good airflow. Remove mulch from directly under buds if humidity high.

stages.description: Peak bud development. Living soil shows its strength — complex terpene profiles from diverse mineral availability. Soil biology at peak activity supporting heavy feeding.

environment_overrides.notes: Monitor humidity carefully.
Rain = bud rot risk. Cover in rain.

stages.name: Mid Flower (Bulk Phase)

nutrients.approach: Water only in established beds. First-cycle soil may benefit from compost tea or top-dress.

soil.notes: Peak demand. Biology working hard. May need supplemental tea if soil is first cycle.

tasks.description: Don't let soil dry excessively during peak flower. Every 2-3 days.
tasks.name: Water consistently

tasks.description: Dense buds = bud rot risk. Defoliate for airflow.
tasks.name: Monitor bud development

tasks.description: Only if plant showing hunger. Aerated compost tea as booster.
tasks.name: Compost tea (if needed)

tasks.description: Stakes or trellis for large buds.
tasks.name: Support heavy colas

common_problems.cause: Rich soil still feeding plant
common_problems.solution: This is fine — living soil doesn't need traditional flush. Quality is already excellent.

stages.description: Final ripening. Living soil plants often produce superior terpene profiles during this phase due to slow, balanced nutrient release from biology.

environment_overrides.notes: Drop night temps for color/terpenes.
Cool nights enhance color development.

stages.name: Late Flower (Ripening)

nutrients.approach: Water only. Let plant and soil reach natural equilibrium.

soil.notes: Biology slowing as plant demands decrease. Natural fade from soil depletion.

tasks.description: 60x loupe. 70-80% milky, 10-20% amber.
tasks.name: Check trichomes

tasks.description: Slight drought stress can enhance terpene production.
tasks.name: Reduce watering slightly

tasks.description: Daily check of dense buds.
tasks.name: Inspect for rot

common_problems.cause: Habit from synthetic growing
common_problems.solution: Living soil doesn't need flush. No salt buildup. Smoke is already smooth from organic growing.

stages.description: Living soil doesn't require traditional flushing. The soil IS the flush — biology self-regulates. Just water only (which you've been doing anyway). Many growers skip this entirely.

environment_overrides.notes: Harvest at optimal trichome maturity.
Harvest before frost. Don't wait too long.

stages.name: Flush

nutrients.approach: Same as always: water only. Living soil doesn't accumulate synthetic salts.

soil.notes: No flush needed in living soil — this IS water-only growing. Continue as normal.

tasks.description: Same as you've been doing. No change needed.
tasks.name: Continue water only

tasks.description: Continue checking ripeness daily.
tasks.name: Monitor trichomes

tasks.description: Gather trimming supplies. Plan harvest day.
tasks.name: Prep for harvest

common_problems.cause: Habit from other methods
common_problems.solution: ALWAYS leave roots in no-till. They are food for the next cycle's microbes.

stages.description: Chop plant. In no-till living soil: leave roots in soil! They decompose and feed the next cycle. Immediately plant cover crop or let soil rest.

environment_overrides.notes: Immediate cover crop for year-round cycling.
Harvest before frost. Cover crop protects soil over winter.

stages.name: Harvest

nutrients.approach: None.

soil.notes: DO NOT remove roots. Cut at soil surface. Roots decompose and become food for next cycle. This is the no-till principle.

tasks.description: LEAVE ROOTS IN SOIL. Cut stem at base, don't pull.
tasks.name: Cut plant at soil surface

tasks.description: Wet or dry trim preference.
tasks.name: Trim

tasks.description: Scatter clover/rye/radish seed on surface. Keeps soil alive between cycles.
tasks.name: Plant cover crop immediately

tasks.description: Add fresh amendments (neem, kelp, crustacean, compost) for next plant.
tasks.name: Top-dress for next cycle

common_problems.cause: Dried too fast (chlorophyll not broken down)
common_problems.solution: Slower dry next time. Can still recover with long cure (4-8 weeks).

stages.description: Standard slow dry. Living soil flower is known for exceptionally smooth smoke when dried and cured properly.

environment_overrides.notes: Dry indoors, not in greenhouse.
Always dry indoors in controlled environment.

stages.name: Drying

nutrients.approach: None.

soil.notes: Soil resting with cover crop growing. Preparing for next cycle.

tasks.description: Slow dry. 60-65°F, 55-62% RH, complete darkness.
tasks.name: Hang whole plant or branches

tasks.description: Check daily. Adjust if drying too fast/slow.
tasks.name: Monitor conditions

tasks.description: Small stems snap cleanly = ready.
tasks.name: Stem snap test

transition_signals: Buds slightly crispy outside, moist inside

common_problems.cause: Living soil flower needs longer cure for full expression
common_problems.solution: Set aside a jar for 8 weeks. Compare to week-2 jar. You'll never rush again.

stages.description: Living soil flower benefits enormously from extended cure (6-8 weeks). The complex terpene profiles from biological growing continue developing in the jar.

environment_overrides.notes: Cure indoors.
Cure indoors in stable environment.

stages.name: Curing

nutrients.approach: None.

soil.notes: Soil cycling with cover crop. Top-dress breakdown continuing.

tasks.description: Daily 2 weeks, then weekly. Living soil flower improves dramatically with time.
tasks.name: Burp jars

tasks.description: 58-62%. Boveda 62 packs.
tasks.name: Monitor humidity

tasks.description: Living soil flower peaks at 6-8 weeks cure. Don't rush.
tasks.name: Extended cure

autoflower.description: Ruderalis-hybrid strains that flower automatically regardless of light schedule. Fixed timeline.

light_schedule.flower: 20/4 or 18/6 (same as veg — no change needed)
light_schedule.transition: Automatic — plant decides when to flower (usually week 3-4)

autoflower.name: Autoflower
autoflower.notes: Fixed timeline — stress cannot be recovered by extending veg. Start in final pot (no transplant shock). LST preferred over HST.
