training_suitability.defoliation: light only — auto can't recover from heavy defol
training_suitability.topping: risky — only if plant is vigorous

photoperiod.description: Standard cannabis strains that require 12/12 light to flower. Grower controls timing.

light_schedule.transition: Switch to 12/12 when plant is ~50% of desired final height

photoperiod.name: Photoperiod
photoperiod.notes: Can be vegged as long as desired. Cloneable. More forgiving of stress (can extend veg to recover).

training_suitability.defoliation: good
training_suitability.topping: excellent

issues.cause: Over-watering killed aerobic biology. Anaerobic conditions.
issues.solution: Let dry thoroughly. Aerate top layer gently. Add fresh compost on surface. May need to start over if severe.

issues.cause: Soil too dry, too hot, or biology crashed
issues.solution: Maintain moisture. Add fresh mulch. Re-inoculate with quality compost. Mycelium returns when conditions right.

issues.cause: Chlorinated water, H2O2, fungicide, or excessive heat killed microbes
issues.solution: Stop the cause. Compost tea drench. Re-inoculate with mycorrhizae. Fresh worm castings top-dress. Takes 1-2 weeks to recover.

issues.cause: Soil nitrogen depleted or biology slow to mineralize
issues.solution: Neem meal or alfalfa meal top-dress. Compost tea. Larger pot next cycle.

issues.cause: Cold soil slowing P availability or soil P depleted
issues.solution: Bone meal top-dress. Ensure soil temp > 65°F (biology needs warmth). Mycorrhizae help P access.

issues.cause: pH too extreme, compacted soil, or root-bound in small pot
issues.solution: Living soil should self-buffer pH. Aerate gently. Transplant to larger pot if root-bound.
issues.symptom: Overall slow growth despite rich soil

issues.cause: Organic matter + moisture = gnat paradise
issues.solution: Top layer of coarse pumice (deters egg laying). Yellow sticky traps. BTI (Mosquito Bits). Stratiolaelaps predatory mites.

issues.cause: Lack of beneficial predators
issues.solution: Introduce beneficial insects (ladybugs, lacewings). Neem oil foliar (doesn't harm soil biology). IPM focus.

issues.cause: Contaminated soil or pots from previous grow
issues.solution: Beneficial nematodes drench (Steinernema feltiae). Neem cake in soil. Fresh clean soil for worst cases.

issues.cause: Peat breaking down, insufficient aeration amendment
issues.solution: Top-dress with pumice or rice hulls. Add more perlite/pumice in rebuild. Cover crop roots help aerate.

issues.cause: Peat-heavy soil dried out too much
issues.solution: Add aloe vera or yucca extract as wetting agent. Water slowly. Re-wet surface layer.

issues.cause: Organic matter decomposing (normal)
issues.solution: Top up with fresh compost + castings each cycle. Add 1-2 inches on top. This is normal maintenance.

cons: RO system cost and waste water
water_source_profiles.name: Reverse Osmosis (RO) Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Gold standard for precision growing. Always add CalMag first when using RO.
pros: Blank slate — total control over mineral content

dechlorination.chloramine: Does NOT gas off — must use Campden tablets or activated carbon filter
dechlorination.chlorine: Let sit uncovered 24 hours (gasses off) or use dechlorinator drops
dechlorination.how_to_tell: Check city water report. Most modern cities use chloramine.

water_source_profiles.name: Municipal Tap Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Good enough for most grows. Dechlorinate and pH-adjust. Get a water report from your city.

cons: May contain iron, sulfur, or heavy metals
water_source_profiles.name: Well Water
water_source_profiles.notes: MUST get a water test before using. High iron or sulfur can cause lockout. Great if your well has clean water with moderate minerals.

water_source_profiles.name: Distilled Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Same as RO but purchased instead of filtered. Only practical for 1-2 plant grows.

advanced_techniques.description: Elevating CO2 from ambient (400ppm) to 1000-1500ppm increases photosynthesis rate by 20-40% when light and temperature are also increased.

methods.notes: Most common. Tank refills every 2-4 weeks for a tent.
Produces heat and humidity — better for large rooms with AC.
Produces ~100-200ppm boost. Only useful for small sealed spaces. Not worth it for most.

advanced_techniques.name: CO2 Supplementation

ppfd_co2_correlation.notes: Ambient CO2 sufficient at low light
Slight benefit from supplementation
Moderate benefit — cost-effective threshold
Strong benefit — diminishing returns above this
Maximum practical benefit for most grows
Commercial intensity — requires perfect environment

requirements: Sealed grow space (CO2 escapes quickly from leaky rooms)
High light intensity (>600 PPFD — plants can't use extra CO2 without sufficient light)
Higher temperature tolerance (82-86°F optimal with CO2 vs 75-80°F without)
CO2 controller or timer

advanced_techniques.description: Manipulating environmental and irrigation parameters to push the plant toward vegetative or generative (flowering/fruiting) growth.
advanced_techniques.name: Crop Steering
advanced_techniques.notes: Crop steering is most effective in hydro/coco where irrigation frequency is highly controllable. Soil growers have less precision due to soil buffering.

vegetative_steering.goal: More leaf/stem growth, larger plant framework

best_practices: Spray during lights-off or first/last 30 minutes of light cycle (stomata open, no light burn)
Use fine mist sprayer — coverage is key
pH foliar solution to 6.2-6.5 (leaf surface slightly higher pH than root zone)
EC should be very low (0.2-0.5) — leaves burn easily
Never foliar spray during flower (moisture in buds → mold/bud rot)
Best for micronutrient deficiency correction (CalMag, iron, magnesium)

common_foliar_products.frequency: Weekly preventive, 3-day intervals for active infestations

advanced_techniques.description: Spraying nutrient solution directly onto leaves for rapid uptake. Bypasses root zone entirely.
advanced_techniques.name: Foliar Feeding

advanced_techniques.description: Strengthens cell walls, increases stem thickness, improves heat/drought/pest resistance.
important_notes: ALWAYS add silica to water FIRST before other nutrients (raises pH significantly)
Let silica mix for 5-10 minutes before adding base nutrients
Silica is incompatible with concentrated nutrient solutions — never mix undiluted
Discontinue in late flower (last 2-3 weeks)
advanced_techniques.name: Silica (Potassium Silicate)

advanced_techniques.description: Introduces beneficial bacteria/fungi to the root zone to outcompete pathogens, improve nutrient uptake, and protect against root diseases.
important_notes: Do NOT use hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) with beneficial microbes — it kills them
Choose either sterile (H2O2) OR beneficial (Hydroguard) approach — not both
Beneficials need warmish water (65-75°F) to thrive
Reapply after any reservoir change or system cleaning
advanced_techniques.name: Beneficial Microbes & Root Inoculants

products.name: Hydroguard
products.notes: The standard for DWC/RDWC. Add at every res change.

products.dosing: 1 tsp/gal (water-in) or dust roots at transplant
products.name: Great White
products.notes: Best for soil/coco. Less effective in pure hydro.

products.name: Recharge
products.notes: Feeds existing soil microbes. Great for living soil.

products.name: Mammoth P
products.notes: Most effective in flower when P demand is highest.

advanced_techniques.description: Removing select fan leaves to improve light penetration, airflow, and direct energy to bud sites.
advanced_techniques.name: Defoliation (Strategic Leaf Removal)
rules: Never remove more than 20-30% of leaves at once (except schwazze technique)
Only defoliate healthy, vigorous plants — never stressed ones
Autoflowers: very light defoliation only — they can't recover from heavy removal
Always leave the top 2-3 fan leaves on each branch (they feed the bud below)

timing.method: Remove lower leaves that get no light (lollipop)
Major defoliation day 1 and day 21 of flower (schwazzing)
Selective tuck or remove leaves blocking bud sites

channel_length_ft.notes: Beyond 12 ft, nutrient depletion at far end becomes an issue. Use multiple shorter channels.

channel_width_inches.notes: Width determines root mat spread and nutrient film coverage.

channel_specifications.cover_requirement: Light-proof covers mandatory. Light exposure = algae. Use opaque lids with net pot holes.

slope_percent.notes: 1:30 to 1:50 ratio. Too steep = roots dry. Too flat = pooling.

flower.notes: Full flow. Root mat developed. Heavy feeding.

flush.notes: Maximum flow with plain water to flush salt buildup.

seedling.notes: Gentle flow. Root tips just touching film.

veg.notes: Roots establishing in channel. Moderate flow.

film_depth_mm.notes: The 'Nutrient Film' should be 1-3mm deep. Deeper = roots submerged = less oxygen.

flow_rate_management.flow_check_frequency: Daily visual check. Weekly measured check with graduated container + timer.
flow_rate_management.flow_uniformity: All channels must receive equal flow. Use manifold with individual channel valves for balancing.

target_lpm.notes: Liters per minute per channel. Measured at drain end.

propagation_to_nft_transfer.acclimation_period: 3-5 days for roots to establish in channel. Growth may pause temporarily. Normal.
common_mistakes: Transferring too early (roots too short to reach film)
Not checking root contact after transfer
Reducing flow rate during establishment
Removing starter plug (leave it — roots grow through it)

ideal_root_length_inches.notes: Roots must be long enough to reach the nutrient film from net pot.

transfer_protocol: Start seedling/clone in starter plug under dome
Once roots are 2-4 inches, place in net pot with hydroton support
Insert net pot into NFT channel
Temporarily increase flow rate for first 24h (ensures roots contact film)
Verify root-to-film contact within 24h (gently lift cover and check)
If roots aren't touching: shim net pot lower, or increase flow briefly

backup_system_recommendations: Dedicated backup pump, pre-plumbed with valves (manual switchover in <1 min)
UPS on primary pump (30-60 min runtime minimum)
Float switch alarm on reservoir (alerts if pump stops returning water)
Battery-powered backup pump for extended outages
detection: No flow visible at drain end
immediate_actions: If no backup: manually pour nutrient solution through channels every 10 min
Seal channel ends to create temporary DWC (emergency — holds water in channel)
Cover channels to trap humidity and slow root drying
pump_failure_protocol.time_to_damage: 15-60 minutes depending on ambient temp and root mat moisture retention

dam_prevention: Channel slope maintained precisely (no flat spots where roots accumulate)
Periodic root trimming if mat blocks >50% of channel cross-section
Wider channels for large plants (6-inch minimum for aggressive rooters)
Shorter channel runs (fewer plants per channel = less mat buildup)
root_mat_management.healthy_root_mat: White/cream colored, thin mat along channel floor. Does NOT dam the flow.
root_mat_management.problematic_root_mat: Thick, dense mat that blocks flow to downstream plants. Brown sections = rot.

root_rot_in_nft.cause: Stagnant pools behind root dams. Lack of oxygen in pooled sections.

cleaning_protocol: End of grow: remove all plants and net pots
Run plain water flush for 30 minutes
Fill channels with pH 3.0 solution (citric acid). Soak 2 hours
Flush with clean water until runoff is pH neutral
Inspect for algae, biofilm, or residue. Repeat if needed.
prevention: Weekly flush with plain pH'd water (run for 1 hour, drain, refill with nutrients)
Maintain proper flow rate (too slow = more evaporation = more salt deposits)
Full channel clean between grows (disassemble, scrub, flush with vinegar or pH-down solution)

equipment.description: Food-safe, opaque channels. 4-inch wide for leafy greens, 6-inch for large plants. PVC pipe (4-6 inch split lengthwise), corrugated roofing channels, or purpose-built NFT rails. Max 10-12 feet per channel for even EC distribution.
equipment.name: NFT Channels (PVC, corrugated, or commercial rail)

equipment.description: Seal the high end of each channel. Must be watertight. PVC caps, silicone-sealed flat stock, or commercial NFT end caps.
equipment.name: Channel End Caps

equipment.description: Holds channels at correct slope (1:30 to 1:40). Must be rigid and level side-to-side. PVC frame, 2x4 lumber frame, or commercial NFT table.
equipment.name: Channel Support / Stand

equipment.description: Collects return flow from all channels. Size at 2-3x total channel volume for stability. Food-safe, opaque, dark-colored.
equipment.name: Reservoir (20-50 gal)

equipment.description: Pumps from reservoir to channel inlets. Size at 2x total flow demand. Must run 24/7 — pump failure = plant death in minutes.
equipment.name: Water Pump (submersible)

equipment.description: NOT optional for NFT — it's essential. Pump failure kills plants faster than any other hydro method. Keep a tested backup ready to swap in <5 minutes.
equipment.name: Backup Pump

equipment.description: Distributes flow from pump to each channel. Use a manifold with individual valves per channel for flow balancing.
equipment.name: Supply Tubing + Manifold

equipment.description: Channel drain ends feed back to reservoir. Use screen filters on returns to catch root debris.
equipment.name: Return Drains

equipment.description: Sit in channel holes. Hold rockwool cube + plant. 3-inch for leafy greens, 6-inch for large plants.
equipment.name: Net Pots (3-inch or 6-inch)

equipment.description: Starting medium for NFT. 1.5-inch starter cubes for germination, transfer cube-and-all into net pot for channel placement.
equipment.name: Rockwool Cubes

equipment.description: Measure at reservoir. Apera or BlueLab recommended.
equipment.name: pH Pen

equipment.description: Measure at reservoir AND at channel drain ends to check for EC gradient.
equipment.name: EC/TDS Meter

equipment.description: Adjust at reservoir.
equipment.name: pH Up & pH Down

equipment.description: Mix in reservoir. Continuous flow distributes to all channels.
equipment.name: Nutrients (GH Flora Trio)

equipment.description: Root protection. Dense root mats in NFT channels are susceptible to Pythium.
equipment.name: Hydroguard

equipment.description: Oxygenate reservoir water. NFT film is thin and oxygenated by exposure, but reservoir water needs aeration.
equipment.name: Air Pump + Air Stone (for reservoir)

equipment.description: Size for total canopy area across all channels.
equipment.name: Grow Light

equipment.description: Reliable timer for 18/6 → 12/12.
equipment.name: Light Timer

equipment.description: Monitor grow room conditions.
equipment.name: Thermometer / Hygrometer

equipment.description: Measure flow rate at each channel inlet and drain. Essential for diagnosing flow restriction from root mats.
equipment.name: Flow Rate Meter / Sight Tube

equipment.description: Seal around net pots in channel holes. Blocks light (prevents algae) and prevents splashing.
equipment.name: Neoprene Collars

equipment.description: Catch root fragments and debris before they reach the pump.
equipment.name: Inline Screen Filters (return lines)

equipment.description: NFT plants have no structural media support. Trellis net is nearly mandatory for flowering — supports heavy buds and creates even canopy.
equipment.name: SCROG / Trellis Net

equipment.description: Strengthens stems. NFT plants lack the structural support of media-grown plants.
equipment.name: Silica Supplement

equipment.description: Essential with RO water. Add to reservoir.
equipment.name: CalMag Supplement

equipment.description: Controls reservoir temp. NFT film warms up in channels under lights — chilled reservoir water compensates.
equipment.name: Water Chiller

equipment.description: Air movement above channels prevents humidity pockets between plants.
equipment.name: Oscillating Fans

equipment.description: Odor and humidity control. Size for grow room.
equipment.name: Exhaust Fan + Carbon Filter

equipment.description: Keeps pump running during power outages. NFT plants die in minutes without flow — this is the most important backup for any NFT system.
equipment.name: UPS / Battery Backup (for pump)

equipment.description: Maintains reservoir level automatically. Helpful with multiple large flowering plants consuming heavily.
equipment.name: Auto-Top-Off (float valve)

equipment.description: Automatically maintains pH and EC in reservoir.
equipment.name: pH/EC Controller (auto-dosing)

equipment.description: Precision slope setting for channels. Especially useful for multi-tier or long-channel setups.
equipment.name: Slope Level / Laser Level

equipment.description: Mesh inserts in channel bottom to keep root mat elevated slightly above channel floor. Helps maintain film flow under the mat.
equipment.name: Root Guards / Channel Screens

equipment.description: Alerts if reservoir temp exceeds 72°F. High water temp + NFT root mats = rapid Pythium development.
equipment.name: Water Temp Alarm

flush_timing.coco: 7-14 days reduced nutrients → plain water last 3-5 days
flush_timing.notes: Flushing is debated — some studies show no difference. At minimum, reduce nutrients in final week.
flush_timing.soil: 14-21 days plain water (soil holds nutrients longer)

other_harvest_indicators: Most pistils (hairs) have darkened and curled in
Calyxes are swollen and dense
Fan leaves are yellowing and falling off naturally
Trichome stalks are visible and heads are enlarged
Breeder's recommended flowering time has passed

trichome_ratios.notes: Harvest early for more THC-dominant, less CBN. Energetic, racy high. Slight reduction in yield.
Standard harvest window. Mix of THC and CBN. Full-spectrum effects.
Extended harvest for max CBN conversion. Heavy body high. Good for pain/sleep. Slightly degraded THC.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Micro → Gro → Bloom (always Micro first)
nutrient_brands.name: General Hydroponics Flora Trio

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Part A → Epsom → Part B (dissolve each fully before adding next)
nutrient_brands.name: Jack's 321
nutrient_brands.notes: Jack's 321 is 3.6g Part A, 2.4g Cal Nit, 1.2g Epsom per gallon at full strength. Extremely cost-effective for large grows.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Core → Bloom
nutrient_brands.name: Athena Pro Line
nutrient_brands.notes: Commercial-grade. Simple 2-part with integrated CalMag. Popular in commercial cannabis facilities.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: A → B (separate for veg and bloom)
nutrient_brands.name: Advanced Nutrients pH Perfect (Sensi)
nutrient_brands.notes: Self-adjusting pH technology (claims no pH adjustment needed). Expensive but popular for beginners who struggle with pH.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Shake VERY well before use (thick concentrate settles). One bottle at a time.
nutrient_brands.name: General Hydroponics FloraNova
nutrient_brands.notes: Simplest option — one bottle per phase. Great for beginners. Shake well — concentrate separates.
nutrient_brands.type: 1-part (two bottles — one for veg, one for bloom)

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Dissolve powder in warm water, stir well
nutrient_brands.name: Greenleaf Nutrients Mega Crop
nutrient_brands.notes: One powder does everything — includes CalMag. Incredibly cost-effective at ~$0.02/gal. Community favorite on r/microgrowery.

environment.container: Glass mason jars (quart size). Fill 75% full.

humidity_packs.boveda_58: For drier preference or long-term storage.
humidity_packs.boveda_62: Maintains 62% RH. Set-and-forget. Replace when crispy.
humidity_packs.grove_bags: Heat-sealed bags with built-in humidity regulation. No burping needed.

indicators_of_good_cure: Smooth, flavorful smoke — no harshness or hay smell
Buds are slightly sticky but not wet
Strong terpene aroma when jar is opened
Ash burns to white/light gray (not black)
method: Trim buds to final shape (dry trim or finish wet trim)
Place in mason jars — fill 3/4 full to allow air exchange
Week 1: burp 2-3x daily for 10-15 minutes (release moisture and gases)
Week 2-4: burp 1x daily for 5-10 minutes
Month 2+: burp 1x weekly or seal completely
Cure for minimum 2 weeks, ideal is 4-8 weeks. Some strains improve for 6+ months.

common_mistakes: Drying too fast (>3 days) — harsh smoke, hay smell, terpene loss
Drying too slow (>14 days) — mold risk, diminishing returns
Fan blowing directly on buds — uneven drying, crispy exterior/moist interior
Temperature too high (>70°F) — terpene evaporation, harsh taste

environment.airflow: Gentle circulation — fan NOT pointed at buds. Just keep air moving.

method: Cut branches 12-18 inches long
Optionally remove large fan leaves (wet trim) or leave intact (dry trim)
Hang upside down on line/rack with 6+ inches between branches
Small branches should snap (not bend) when done — 5-7 days minimum
Stems should snap cleanly; if they bend, keep drying

long_term_storage.degradation: THC slowly converts to CBN over time. Light, heat, air, and moisture accelerate degradation.
long_term_storage.method: Sealed mason jars or vacuum-sealed bags with Boveda 58% in dark, cool place
long_term_storage.notes: Properly cured and stored cannabis can remain excellent for 12+ months. Freezing is debated — can damage trichomes if not done carefully.

channel_engineering.material: PVC pipe (split), corrugated roofing, or commercial NFT rail. Must be opaque and food-safe.
channel_engineering.slope: 1:30 to 1:40 (1 inch drop per 30-40 inches of channel length)

flow_rate_guide.pump_sizing: 2x total channel demand. E.g., 4 channels × 2 LPM = 8 LPM demand → use 16+ LPM pump.

golden_rules: Pump runs 24/7 — pump failure kills plants in MINUTES, not hours
ALWAYS have a backup pump tested and ready
Check drain flow at every channel DAILY — root mats block flow
Channel slope: 1:30 to 1:40. Too steep = thin film. Too flat = pooling
Max channel length 10-12 feet — longer channels get EC gradient problems
Start seedlings in rockwool cubes OFF the channels — never plant directly in NFT
Roots must be 3+ inches before channel placement
Monitor EC at inlet AND drain end — difference reveals salt accumulation
Flush channels with plain water at every reservoir change
NFT plants have NO structural support — SCROG net is mandatory for flower
quick_reference.hydroguard_dose: 2 ml/gal at every reservoir change AND every top-off

pump_failure_protocol.immediate_action: Switch to backup pump. If no backup, manually pour nutrient water into channel inlets.
pump_failure_protocol.prevention: Keep tested backup pump ready. UPS/battery backup for pump circuit. Pump failure alarm if available.
pump_failure_protocol.time_to_death_minutes: 2-5 minutes without flow in warm conditions

quick_reference.reservoir_change_schedule: Every 7 days in veg, every 5 days in flower, every 3 days during flush
quick_reference.top_off_rule: EC rising = top off with plain pH'd water. EC dropping = top off with 1/4 strength nutrient water.

change_schedule.top_off: Daily with plain pH'd water when EC rises. With dilute nutrient when EC drops.
change_schedule.top_off_rule: EC rising = plants drinking more water than nutrients → top off with plain water. EC dropping = plants eating more nutrients than water → top off with dilute nutrient mix.

reservoir_management.description: Reservoir management for active hydroponic systems (DWC, RDWC, NFT, Ebb & Flow, Drip, Aeroponics).

dissolved_oxygen.why_important: Roots need oxygen to function. Low DO → root rot, nutrient lockout, slow growth.

sizing_guidelines.notes: Larger reservoirs = more stable pH/EC, less frequent changes, more forgiving of mistakes.

beneficial.cons: Slightly cloudy water (normal). Doesn't work above 75°F water.
beneficial.pros: Continuous protection. Outcompetes pathogens. Set-and-forget per res change.

sterile_vs_beneficial.recommendation: Beneficial approach (Hydroguard) for most growers. Sterile only if you have persistent issues.

sterile.cons: Must reapply every 2-3 days. Kills beneficial microbes too. Degrades in hours.
sterile.pros: Kills all pathogens. Clean roots. No biofilm.

water_temperature.too_cold_risk: Slowed metabolism, nutrient lockout, stunted growth
water_temperature.too_warm_risk: Root rot, low dissolved oxygen, pathogen growth

scale_tiers.description: First-time grower or single-plant hobbyist. Manual management, learning-focused.
scale_tiers.name: Solo / Single Plant
scale_tiers.notes: Hands-on learning experience. Manual checks 1-2x daily. Ideal for beginners.

scale_tiers.description: Hobbyist tent grow. Light automation helpful but not required.
scale_tiers.name: Small Tent (2-8 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Timer-controlled lights, humidity controller. Sensor monitoring recommended.

scale_tiers.description: Dedicated grower with multiple tents/rooms. Automation saves significant time.
scale_tiers.name: Multi-Tent (9-24 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Multiple environments to manage. Automated alerts essential. Consider perpetual harvest.

scale_tiers.description: Licensed commercial operation. Full automation, compliance tracking, batch management.
scale_tiers.name: Commercial Room (25-100 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: SOPs required. Staff training. Regulatory compliance. Insurance requirements.

scale_tiers.description: Large-scale production facility. Enterprise automation, SCADA integration, dedicated staff.
scale_tiers.name: Warehouse Scale (100+ plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Multiple rooms, dedicated HVAC, industrial nutrient dosing, video surveillance.

channel.notes: Seeds are NOT in channels yet. They are in rockwool cubes in a propagation tray. Do not place seeds directly in NFT channels.

common_problems.cause: Too cold, rockwool too wet, or bad seed
common_problems.solution: Ensure 75-80°F. Rockwool should be moist not soaking. Try a different seed after 7 days.

common_problems.cause: Rockwool is naturally alkaline (~7.0)
common_problems.solution: Pre-soak in pH 5.5 water for at least 1 hour. This is mandatory for NFT rockwool starts.

stages.description: Seed cracks open and taproot emerges. For NFT, always start seeds in rockwool cubes (not directly in channels). The cube will travel with the plant into the channel later. Use 1.5-inch rockwool starter cubes, pre-soaked in pH 5.5 water.

environment.notes: Keep seeds in darkness. Heat mat at 78°F. No light until sprout emerges. Rockwool cubes in a humidity dome.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse germination: use a heat mat and humidity dome. Greenhouse temps may swing too much without supplemental heating.

greenhouse.notes: Use heat mat in greenhouse for consistent germ temps.

environment_overrides.notes: Start seeds indoors regardless. NFT seedlings are too fragile for outdoor conditions. Transfer to outdoor channels only after roots are well established.

outdoor.notes: Always germinate indoors for NFT.

health_checks: Is the taproot visible and white?
Is the rockwool cube moist but not dripping?
Is the NFT system leak-tested and slope-verified?
stages.name: Germination

nutrients.approach: None — seeds contain all energy needed to germinate.

reservoir.notes: No reservoir running yet. Pre-soak rockwool cubes in pH 5.5 plain water. System should be assembled, leak-tested, and slope-verified while seeds germinate.

tasks.description: Pre-soak 1.5-inch starter cubes in pH 5.5 water for 1 hour. Shake out excess — cubes should be moist, not dripping. Never squeeze rockwool.
tasks.name: Soak rockwool cubes

tasks.description: Place one seed per cube, pointy end down, 1/4 inch deep. Cover with a small piece of torn rockwool. Place cubes in propagation tray with humidity dome.
tasks.name: Plant seeds

tasks.description: After 24-72 hours, look for white taproot emerging from bottom of cube. Do NOT disturb the cube — just look.
tasks.name: Check for taproot

tasks.description: While seeds germinate: run the pump, verify slope, check for leaks at channel joints and end caps. Fix everything now — you cannot fix leaks with plants in channels.
tasks.name: Leak-test NFT system

tasks.description: Use a level. Target 1:30 to 1:40 slope (approximately 1 inch drop per 3 feet). Too steep = film too thin. Too flat = pooling and root drowning.
tasks.name: Verify channel slope

transition_signals: Taproot visible through bottom of cube
Sprout emerging from top of cube
First set of cotyledon leaves visible

channel.notes: Seedlings are NOT in main channels yet. They stay on the propagation tray until roots are 3+ inches through the cube bottom. Some growers use a small nursery NFT channel for propagation.

common_problems.cause: Light too far away or too dim
common_problems.solution: Lower light to 6-8 inches. Increase intensity. Support stretched seedlings with a small stake.

common_problems.cause: Cube too wet (roots don't need to search for water) or too dry
common_problems.solution: Let cube dry slightly between waterings. Roots grow when searching for moisture. Check cube isn't sitting in standing water.

common_problems.cause: Light hitting wet rockwool
common_problems.solution: Cover cube tops with hydroton or a small piece of black plastic. Block light from reaching wet surfaces.

common_problems.cause: Fungal infection from too much moisture
common_problems.issue: Damping off (stem collapses at soil line)
common_problems.solution: Improve airflow. Reduce watering. Remove dome if still on. Discard affected seedlings — damping off is fatal.

stages.description: Seedling grows in rockwool cube on a propagation tray with gentle nutrient film or hand-watering. NOT in main NFT channels yet. Roots must grow through the bottom of the cube and be at least 3 inches long before channel placement. This propagation phase is critical for NFT — placing seedlings in channels too early means roots can't reach the film.

environment.notes: Keep under gentle light (T5 fluorescent or LED at low power). Humidity dome for first 3-5 days, then remove to harden off. Seedling needs to develop strong roots BEFORE entering the channel.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse propagation works well. Use shade cloth if light is too intense for seedlings.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse propagation: protect from intense midday sun.

environment_overrides.notes: Keep seedlings indoors until they have strong root systems. Outdoor NFT channels have more temperature variation — seedlings need robust roots to survive.

outdoor.notes: Propagate indoors, transfer to outdoor channels only when roots are well developed.

health_checks: Are roots emerging from the bottom of the rockwool cube?
Are cotyledon leaves healthy and green?
Rockwool cube moist but not waterlogged?
stages.name: Seedling (Propagation Tray)

nutrients.approach: Very light feeding. Seedlings are tiny and in rockwool — they need almost nothing. Hand-water cubes with dilute solution every 1-2 days, or run a gentle recirculating nursery tray.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Protect young roots from Pythium. Critical even at propagation stage.

reservoir.notes: If hand-watering cubes: use very dilute nutrient solution. If using a nursery tray with recirculating film: run at 0.3 EC. Main system reservoir should be mixed and ready for when seedlings are transferred.

tasks.description: Hand-water rockwool cubes with 1/4 strength nutrient solution when top of cube feels dry. Do NOT let cubes sit in standing water.
tasks.name: Water/feed cubes

tasks.description: Gently lift cube and check for roots emerging from the bottom. Roots must be 3+ inches before channel placement.
tasks.name: Check root growth

tasks.description: Days 3-5: crack the humidity dome lid. Days 5-7: remove dome entirely. Seedlings need to acclimate to ambient humidity before channel life.
tasks.name: Harden off from dome

tasks.description: If seedlings are stretching (tall thin stems), light is too far away. Lower light or increase intensity.
tasks.name: Monitor for stretch

tasks.description: Run pump and flow through main channels with plain pH'd water. Verify flow rate at each channel. Fix any issues before adding plants.
tasks.name: Run main NFT system empty

transition_signals: Roots 3+ inches through bottom of cube

channel.channel_slope: 1:30 to 1:40 (1 inch drop per 30-40 inches of channel length)
channel.notes: Start with lower flow rate. Young roots are delicate — too much flow can wash them down-channel. Film should just barely cover the bottom of the channel. Verify that the film reaches each plant position.

common_problems.cause: Roots not reaching the film, or flow too low at that position
common_problems.solution: Check root contact with film. Increase flow slightly. Verify slope is correct — flat spots cause flow gaps.

common_problems.cause: Flow rate too low, channel not level side-to-side, or slope issues
common_problems.issue: Film not reaching all plant positions
common_problems.solution: Increase pump output. Level channel side-to-side (must be perfectly flat laterally). Check for leaks reducing flow.

common_problems.cause: Flow rate too high for young roots
common_problems.solution: Reduce flow rate. Young roots need gentle flow. Increase gradually as roots establish.

common_problems.cause: Light reaching the nutrient film through channel holes or gaps
common_problems.solution: Block all light entry. Use neoprene inserts around net pots. Cover unused holes. Use opaque channel material.

stages.description: Transfer seedlings from propagation tray into NFT channels. This is the most critical transition in NFT growing. Roots must immediately contact the nutrient film. Place rockwool cube into net pot, set in channel hole. Roots grow into the film and begin rapid vegetative growth. Flow rate starts low and increases as root mass develops.

environment.notes: Ramp up light intensity as plants establish in channels. Slightly higher humidity during first few days after transplant helps reduce transplant shock.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse channel placement: good environment for transplant. Temperature may be warmer — ensure adequate ventilation.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse NFT: ideal transplant environment with natural light.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor channel placement: do this in the evening or on a cloudy day to reduce transplant shock. Protect channels from direct rain — rain dilutes the film and disrupts flow.

extra_problems.cause: Outdoor rain entering open channel holes
extra_problems.solution: Use rain covers over channels. Neoprene collars around net pots help. Never run NFT channels without rain protection outdoors.

extra_tasks.description: Provide shade cloth for 2-3 days after channel placement. Full sun + transplant shock can kill seedlings.
extra_tasks.name: Shade new transplants

outdoor.notes: Outdoor NFT: rain protection for channels is mandatory.

health_checks: Are all plant positions receiving film flow?
New growth visible within 3-5 days of channel placement?
Root tips extending into the film?
Any signs of transplant shock beyond 48 hours?
stages.name: Early Veg (Channel Placement)

nutrients.approach: Half strength. Plants are establishing in channels and ramping up nutrient uptake. Too strong = burns delicate new root tips touching the film for the first time.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root protection during the vulnerable transplant phase.

reservoir.notes: First time running system with plants. Monitor pH closely — will swing more with young plants. Aerate reservoir heavily. NFT reservoir should be 2-3x the volume of the total channel capacity for stability.

tasks.description: Set rockwool cube into net pot → into channel hole. Roots MUST hang below the net pot and touch the flowing film. If roots are too short, wait longer in propagation.
tasks.name: Place seedlings in channels

tasks.description: After placement, check every plant position. The film must flow past each set of roots. Adjust flow rate if any position is dry.
tasks.name: Verify film contact at all positions

tasks.description: Daily. New plants in channels will cause pH and EC swings. Adjust gently at the reservoir.
tasks.name: Check pH/EC

tasks.description: Some wilting in the first 24-48 hours is normal. If wilting persists beyond 48 hours, check that roots are in the film and flow is adequate.
tasks.name: Monitor transplant stress

tasks.description: Full change every 7 days. Drain reservoir, clean if needed, refill with fresh solution.
tasks.name: Reservoir change

tasks.description: Verify nutrient solution is flowing out the drain end of every channel. If flow stops, roots may already be blocking.
tasks.name: Check flow at end of channel

training.description: Bend and tie down main stem to expose lower branches to light. Start gentle — NFT plants are anchored only by the net pot, not buried media.

transition_signals: Roots visibly growing along channel bottom

channel.notes: Increase flow rate as root mass grows. Root mats will restrict flow — you may need to increase pump output to maintain target flow at the drain end. Check drain flow rate DAILY. If flow at the drain drops below 50% of inlet flow, roots are blocking.

common_problems.cause: Root mat blocking flow — the #1 NFT problem
common_problems.issue: Reduced flow at drain end of channel
common_problems.solution: Gently lift root mat and flush debris. Increase pump flow rate. For severe blockage: temporarily remove plants, flush channel, replace. Consider root trimming for aggressive root growers.

common_problems.cause: Root mat acting as a dam
common_problems.issue: Standing water pooling behind root mass
common_problems.solution: This is dangerous — standing water in NFT leads to root rot. Clear the blockage immediately. Lift roots, restore flow. May need to trim roots.

common_problems.cause: Nutrient concentration increasing along channel length
common_problems.issue: Salt crusting at end of channels
common_problems.solution: Flush channels with plain pH'd water every 3-4 days. Reduce channel length if persistent. Move to bi-directional flow if available.

common_problems.cause: NFT provides no structural support — plant anchored only by net pot and roots
common_problems.solution: Use plant yoyos, clips, or a trellis net over the channels. Silica supplement strengthens stems.

stages.description: Rapid vegetative growth. Plants double or triple in size. Root mats develop along channel bottoms — this is where NFT gets tricky. Roots will naturally form a mat that can restrict or block flow entirely. Monitor flow at the drain end of every channel. Increase flow rate as root mass grows.

environment.notes: Full veg light. Plants growing fast. Channel root mats becoming significant. Watch for flow restriction.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse veg: excellent growth with natural light. Supplemental lighting extends veg period if desired. Ventilate to prevent excessive heat near channel surfaces.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse NFT: ventilation prevents heat buildup at channel level.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor veg: natural photoperiod controls veg length (will flower when days shorten below ~14 hours). Monitor weather — heavy rain can flood channels.

extra_problems.cause: Open channel holes attract pests seeking moisture
extra_problems.solution: Neoprene collars on all net pot holes. Inspect channels for pests weekly. Diatomaceous earth around channel supports.

extra_tasks.description: Verify rain hasn't entered channels and diluted nutrient solution. Check reservoir EC after any rain event.
extra_tasks.name: Check channel covers after rain

outdoor.notes: Outdoor NFT: rain and pest protection for channels is essential.

health_checks: Is flow reaching the drain end of every channel?
Roots white and healthy (not brown or slimy)?
EC difference between inlet and drain acceptable (<0.3)?
stages.name: Late Veg

nutrients.approach: Three-quarter strength for active vegetative growth. Nitrogen-heavy ratio. Plants in NFT channels have direct root contact with nutrients — they feed efficiently.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root zone protection. Root mats in NFT are dense — perfect environment for Pythium if water temp rises.

supplements.name: Silica (Armor Si)
supplements.purpose: Strengthen stems. NFT plants can grow fast and may be less structurally strong without heavy media anchoring.

reservoir.notes: Plants consuming more now. EC will drop faster between checks. Reservoir size matters — larger reservoir = more stability. Change weekly. Aerate heavily.

tasks.description: MOST IMPORTANT NFT TASK. Measure or visually verify flow at the drain end of every channel. If flow is reduced, root mats are blocking. Immediate action needed.
tasks.name: Check drain flow (all channels)

tasks.description: Daily at reservoir. Vigorous veg = high nutrient consumption.
tasks.name: Check pH/EC

tasks.description: Full change every 7 days.
tasks.name: Reservoir change

tasks.description: Gently lift a net pot and check roots. Healthy root mat is white and flows along the channel. Brown/slimy roots or standing water behind the root mass = problem.
tasks.name: Check for root mat blockage

tasks.description: Measure EC at inlet and drain end of each channel. If drain EC is significantly higher than inlet (>0.3 EC difference), salt is accumulating along the channel.
tasks.name: EC inlet vs drain check

tasks.description: Top plants and train horizontal to fill the channel canopy. NFT plants have less anchoring than soil — use plant ties and clips, not weights.
tasks.name: Training (LST/topping)

tasks.description: Verify pump running at rated capacity. Test backup pump monthly. Pump failure = root death in minutes.
tasks.name: Check pump and backup

training.description: Cut main stem above 5th node to create two main colas. NFT's even nutrient delivery grows both colas equally.
Bend and tie branches to spread canopy along the channel. Use soft plant ties — do not weight branches (no media to anchor weights).
Trellis net above channels provides structural support NFT plants need and creates even canopy.

channel.notes: Root mass grows aggressively during stretch. Increase flow rate proactively — don't wait for blockage. Check drain flow twice daily during transition. This is the peak period for root mat development.

common_problems.cause: Root mat completely blocked the channel
common_problems.issue: Flow stopped at one channel during stretch
common_problems.solution: IMMEDIATE action: gently lift root mass and restore flow. If root mat is too dense, carefully trim roots at channel bottom (never more than 20%). Consider upgrading to wider channels for future grows.

common_problems.cause: Genetics (sativa-dominant) or too much difference between light and dark temps
common_problems.solution: Supercrop: gently pinch and bend stems at 90 degrees. They'll recover and form a knuckle. Works well in NFT with trellis support.

common_problems.cause: Light leak during dark period, or plant still acclimating
common_problems.issue: Pre-flowers not appearing after 2 weeks of 12/12
common_problems.solution: Check EVERY light source during dark period — indicator LEDs, phone charging lights, light through cracks. Even brief light exposure can prevent flowering.

stages.description: Light flips to 12/12. The plant stretches 50-200% in height during this period. In NFT, the stretch phase is when root mats grow most aggressively — channel flow management becomes critical. Root mass can double during transition. Nutrient ratio shifts from nitrogen-heavy to phosphorus/potassium-heavy.

environment.notes: Switch to 12/12. Dark period must be UNINTERRUPTED — any light leak during dark period can cause hermaphrodites or revert to veg. Stretch will be dramatic. Have support structures ready.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse transition: use blackout curtains for 12-hour dark periods if greenhouse has any light leaks.

extra_tasks.description: Ensure 12 hours of complete darkness. Greenhouse light leaks from nearby structures or passing cars can prevent flowering.
extra_tasks.name: Deploy blackout curtains

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse: blackout system critical for reliable flowering.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor NFT: photoperiod plants flower naturally as days shorten. No light switch needed. Transition happens over 2-3 weeks instead of abruptly. Monitor root growth closely during this period.

extra_tasks.description: Street lights, porch lights, even a bright moon can disrupt flowering. Shield channels and plants from any artificial light at night.
extra_tasks.name: Protect from light pollution

outdoor.notes: Outdoor: natural photoperiod triggers transition more gradually.

health_checks: Is the stretch progressing normally (50-200% height increase)?
Flow reaching drain end of all channels?
Roots white and healthy despite rapid mat growth?
No light leaks during dark period?
Support structures adequate for stretch growth?
stages.name: Transition (Flip to Flower)

nutrients.approach: Shift ratio: reduce nitrogen (FloraGro), increase phosphorus/potassium (FloraBloom). Not full bloom strength yet — transition is gradual.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root mat density peaks during transition — Pythium risk increases.

reservoir.notes: Transition nutrient mix — shift from grow to bloom. Plants consuming heavily during stretch. Monitor EC closely. Reservoir changes every 7 days.

tasks.description: Set timer to 12 hours on / 12 hours off. Verify dark period is complete darkness — check for indicator LEDs, light leaks through vents, etc.
tasks.name: Switch to 12/12 light

tasks.description: TWICE daily during transition. Root mass growth accelerates dramatically. Flow restriction can develop in hours during peak stretch.
tasks.name: Check drain flow (all channels)

tasks.description: Bump pump output 20-30% at the start of transition. Root mats will grow into this extra capacity within days.
tasks.name: Increase flow rate proactively

tasks.description: Daily. Nutrient ratio is shifting. pH may swing more during transition.
tasks.name: Check pH/EC

tasks.description: Plants can grow 2-4 inches per day during stretch. Trellis net or plant yoyos are essential.
tasks.name: Support stretching branches

tasks.description: Transition from veg to bloom nutrient ratio.
tasks.name: Reservoir change

tasks.description: Lift net pots and check root health. White and branching = good. Brown, slimy, or matted solid = problem.
tasks.name: Root mat inspection

training.description: Pinch and bend stems at 90 degrees. The stem heals with a reinforced knuckle and the height is controlled.
Remove all growth below the bottom 1/3 of the plant. These sites won't produce quality buds. Directs energy to top buds and improves airflow in channels.

channel.notes: Maximum flow rate. Root mats are at peak density. If you haven't already increased pump output, do it NOW. Flow restriction during flower means nutrient deficiencies that directly impact bud development.

common_problems.cause: EC gradient — plants at channel end receiving higher concentration
common_problems.solution: Flush channels more frequently. Increase flow rate. Reduce channel length for future grows. Monitor EC at inlet vs drain.

common_problems.cause: Local flow restriction creating stagnant zone behind root mat
common_problems.issue: Root rot in one channel position
common_problems.solution: Clear blockage immediately. Treat system with H2O2 (3ml/gal of 3%) for 24 hours, then re-dose Hydroguard. Monitor all other positions.

common_problems.cause: Bloom nutrients depositing between the film line and channel edge
common_problems.solution: Flush channels during every reservoir change. Use a soft brush to clean channel walls at res changes.

common_problems.cause: Heavy flower development with no structural support from media
common_problems.solution: SCROG net is essential for NFT flower. Plant yoyos for individual branches. Silica supplement strengthens stems.

stages.description: Stretch ends, bud sites established, first pistils (white hairs) visible at every node. Nutrient demand shifts fully to bloom (P/K heavy). Root mats are now at their maximum density in NFT channels — flow management is critical. NFT's continuous nutrient delivery produces excellent early bud development.

environment.notes: Increase light intensity for flower. Drop humidity below 55% — bud rot prevention starts now. Temperature differential (day vs night) enhances terpene development.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse early flower: run dehumidifier to keep humidity below 55%. Greenhouse traps moisture from multiple transpiring plants.

extra_tasks.description: Keep humidity below 55% during flower. Dense NFT canopies above channels trap moisture.
extra_tasks.name: Run dehumidifier

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse NFT in flower: dehumidification is critical.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor early flower: protect buds from rain. NFT channels must have rain covers. Wet buds + warm temps = bud rot.

extra_problems.cause: Moths laying eggs on flowers
extra_problems.solution: BT (Bacillus thuringiensis) spray weekly. Inspect buds for frass (caterpillar droppings).

extra_tasks.description: Cover channels and canopy to prevent rain from hitting bud sites. This is mandatory for outdoor NFT in flower.
extra_tasks.name: Install rain covers

outdoor.notes: Outdoor NFT in flower: rain protection is non-negotiable.

health_checks: Bud sites developing at all nodes?
White pistils visible at every bud site?
stages.name: Early Flower

nutrients.approach: Full bloom transition. Heavy phosphorus and potassium. Reduce nitrogen. NFT delivers nutrients directly to roots — plants respond quickly to ratio changes.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Dense root mats + warm temps + bloom organics = high Pythium risk.

supplements.name: PK Booster (Liquid Koolbloom)
supplements.purpose: Start PK supplementation as bud sites develop. Begin at half dose.

reservoir.notes: Switch to 5-day reservoir changes during flower. Bloom nutrients cause more salt buildup in channels. Aerate reservoir heavily — O2 demand increases with root mass.

tasks.description: Daily. Root mats at maximum density. Any flow reduction = immediate attention. Bud development depends on consistent nutrient delivery.
tasks.name: Check drain flow (all channels)

tasks.description: Daily. Bloom nutrients and heavy feeding cause faster pH and EC swings.
tasks.name: Check pH/EC

tasks.description: Every 5 days during flower. Flush channels with plain water during each change to remove salt buildup.
tasks.name: Reservoir change

tasks.description: Lift net pots. Roots should be white with branching. Brown/slimy = rot starting. Act immediately if any site shows rot.
tasks.name: Inspect roots at 2-3 channel positions

tasks.description: Day 21 of flower: remove large fan leaves blocking bud sites. Improves light penetration and airflow through the channel canopy.
tasks.name: Defoliation (Day 21)

tasks.description: At each reservoir change, run plain pH'd water through channels for 15-30 minutes before adding nutrient solution. Clears salt deposits.
tasks.name: Flush channels with plain water

training.description: Remove large fan leaves blocking bud sites. Opens canopy for light and airflow. Don't over-defoliate — leave sugar leaves near buds.

channel.notes: Peak flow demand. Root mats may need flow rates above the standard 2 LPM range. If drain flow drops below 50% of inlet, increase pump output immediately. Salt deposits accelerate — flush channels at every reservoir change.

common_problems.cause: Peak flower consumption from multiple large plants
common_problems.solution: Install auto-top-off float valve. Upgrade to a larger reservoir (3-5x channel volume minimum).

common_problems.cause: Heavy bloom nutrients depositing in channels, especially at root mat edges
common_problems.solution: Flush channels with plain water at EVERY reservoir change. Use soft brush during changes. Consider mid-week flush.

common_problems.cause: Humidity pocket, dense bud, poor airflow at that position
common_problems.issue: Bud rot at one channel position
common_problems.solution: Remove ALL affected material (cut 2 inches below rot). Increase airflow. Lower room humidity. Check all other positions.

common_problems.cause: Heavy buds + no structural media support
common_problems.solution: SCROG net is mandatory for NFT in mid flower. Add individual plant yoyos. Silica supplement strengthens stems.

stages.description: Peak bud production. Buds swell rapidly and pack on weight. NFT's continuous nutrient delivery produces excellent bud density. Water and nutrient consumption hits maximum. Root mats are fully established — flow management is a daily battle. Salt accumulation in channels accelerates with heavy bloom nutrients.

environment.notes: Maximum light intensity. Drop humidity to 45% to prevent bud rot. Temperature differential (day/night) enhances terpene and color production.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: run dehumidifier 24/7 during mid flower.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse NFT mid flower: dehumidification is critical.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor mid flower: rain protection over channels and canopy is absolutely mandatory. Rain + dense buds = bud rot.

extra_problems.cause: Outdoor humidity + dew on dense flower clusters
extra_problems.solution: Shake plants mornings. Add fans if power available. Defoliate for airflow.

extra_tasks.description: Gently shake each plant in the morning to remove dew from buds.
extra_tasks.name: Shake plants after rain/dew

outdoor.notes: Outdoor NFT mid flower: dew and rain are the primary threats.

health_checks: Flow reaching drain end of all channels?
Buds swelling uniformly across all channel positions?
stages.name: Mid Flower (Bulk Phase)

nutrients.approach: Full bloom strength. Maximum phosphorus and potassium. NFT delivers peak nutrition directly to roots. Watch for slight tip burn — that means plants are at maximum capacity (optimal).

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Non-negotiable during flower. Root rot now = total loss.

supplements.name: PK Booster (Liquid Koolbloom)
supplements.purpose: Peak PK supplementation for bud density.

reservoir.notes: Peak consumption. Multiple large flowering plants can consume reservoir quickly. Top off between changes. Reservoir change every 5 days — flush channels with plain water during each change.

tasks.description: Daily — CRITICAL. Root mats + salt buildup + heavy bloom nutrients = maximum flow restriction risk. Any reduction = immediate action.
tasks.name: Check drain flow (all channels)

tasks.description: Daily at reservoir. Peak consumption means fast EC drops.
tasks.name: Check pH/EC

tasks.description: May need daily top-offs. Multiple large flowering plants consume heavily.
tasks.name: Top off reservoir

tasks.description: Every 5 days. Run plain pH'd water through channels for 30 minutes before refilling with nutrient solution.
tasks.name: Reservoir change + channel flush

tasks.description: Measure EC at both ends of each channel. If drain EC is >0.5 higher than inlet, salt accumulation is excessive — flush more frequently.
tasks.name: EC inlet vs drain check

tasks.description: Check all bud sites for brown/gray rot. Dense NFT canopies trap humidity. Part dense buds and look inside.
tasks.name: Bud rot inspection

tasks.description: NFT plants have zero media anchoring. Heavy buds will topple plants without SCROG net or yoyos. Check support daily.
tasks.name: Support heavy branches

training.description: Final light defoliation. Remove only leaves directly blocking bud sites. Do not over-defoliate in late flower.

channel.notes: Maintain flow. Root mats are established but stable. Reduced nutrients mean less salt buildup. Continue monitoring drain flow.

common_problems.cause: EC gradient effect — plants at end of channel received slightly different nutrition over time
common_problems.solution: NFT: harvest by position. Cut ready plants first, leave others to ripen longer. Cap their channel holes.

common_problems.cause: Light stress (too close) or heat stress
common_problems.solution: Raise light slightly. Lower room temp.

common_problems.cause: Dense mature buds + any humidity spike
common_problems.solution: Harvest immediately if rot is spreading. Better slightly early than losing buds.

stages.description: Final bud maturation and trichome ripening. Reduce nutrients. Trichomes transition from clear→cloudy→amber. In NFT, reduced nutrient strength means less salt accumulation — channels stay cleaner. Flow management eases slightly as plants consume less.

environment.notes: Cooler temps + large day/night differential enhances color and terpene production. NFT's continuous delivery helps all plants ripen at similar rates.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: cool fall nights enhance colors and terpenes. Monitor for frost if unheated.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse NFT: cool nights beneficial if above freezing.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor late flower: watch for early frost. NFT channels and plumbing freeze before air temp reaches 32°F due to thin water film. Harvest before first frost.

extra_problems.cause: Late-season temperature drop
extra_problems.solution: Harvest immediately. NFT's thin film freezes faster than any other hydro method. No insulation can protect a running NFT system from a hard freeze.

extra_tasks.description: NFT is extremely frost-vulnerable — the thin film freezes quickly. Harvest before first frost. No recovery if channels freeze.
extra_tasks.name: Monitor frost forecast

outdoor.notes: Outdoor NFT: harvest timing is frost-date-dependent.

health_checks: Flow stable at all channel positions?
stages.name: Late Flower (Ripening)

nutrients.approach: Reduced strength. Taper down nutrients. Stop PK boosters. Allow plant to use internal reserves. Yellowing fan leaves is normal and desired.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Continue until flush begins.

reservoir.notes: Reduce nutrient strength. Plants are finishing. Water consumption decreases slightly. Continue 5-day reservoir changes.

tasks.description: Use 60x loupe or digital microscope. Clear = not ready. Milky/cloudy = peak THC. Amber = more sedative. Most growers harvest at 10-20% amber.
tasks.name: Check trichomes daily

tasks.description: Daily. Reduced nutes, but pH management continues.
tasks.name: Check pH/EC

tasks.description: Daily. Root mats are stable but still need monitoring.
tasks.name: Check drain flow

tasks.description: Continue 5-day changes with channel flushing.
tasks.name: Reservoir change + channel flush

tasks.description: Dense, mature buds are most susceptible. Check all positions daily.
tasks.name: Bud rot patrol

tasks.description: Yellow/dying fan leaves are normal now. Remove to prevent mold and improve airflow.
tasks.name: Remove dying fan leaves

channel.notes: Keep flow running at full rate. The continuous film actively washes salts from roots and channel surfaces. NFT flush is faster and more thorough than any reservoir-based system.

common_problems.cause: Severe salt buildup in channel walls or root mat trapping salts
common_problems.solution: Increase flow rate. Drain and refill more frequently (every 2 days). Gently agitate roots if accessible.

common_problems.cause: Root function declining naturally near end of life
common_problems.solution: Normal. Ensure flow continues. Don't add nutrients.

stages.description: Run plain pH'd water through the NFT system. No nutrients. NFT is the MOST effective system for flushing — the continuous thin film washes salts from roots faster than any other method. Plants use remaining internal nutrient stores, producing cleaner-burning, better-tasting flower.

environment.notes: Maintain 12/12. Environment stays the same. Continue monitoring for bud rot.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse flush: maintain ventilation. Dying leaves can harbor mold.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse: remove fallen leaves promptly.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor flush: rain actually helps. Let rain supplement the flushing process.

outdoor.notes: Outdoor: rain acceptable during flush.

stages.name: Flush

nutrients.approach: Zero nutrients. Plain pH'd water. NFT's continuous film delivers the most thorough flush of any hydro method.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Continue through flush. Root rot in final week wastes the entire grow.

reservoir.notes: Plain pH'd water only. Change every 3 days. Continue Hydroguard — root health matters until harvest day. Monitor EC of return water — it should drop rapidly with NFT's continuous flow.

tasks.description: Drain reservoir, refill with plain pH'd water. Run pump to flush all channels. NFT's continuous flow flushes faster than standing systems.
tasks.name: Drain and refill with plain water

tasks.description: Check EC of water returning from channels. Should drop rapidly toward 0. NFT typically flushes in 7 days vs 10-14 for DWC.
tasks.name: Monitor EC of drain water

tasks.description: Plants continue maturing during flush. Check trichomes to time harvest.
tasks.name: Continue trichome checks

tasks.description: Continue daily checks. Flush period is still high-risk.
tasks.name: Monitor for bud rot

tasks.description: Sharp trimming scissors, drying rack/lines, mason jars, hygrometers, Boveda packs.
tasks.name: Prepare harvest equipment

channel.notes: Stop pump after last plant is harvested. Clean channels thoroughly — root mats leave debris and salt deposits.

common_problems.cause: Dense root growth bonded to channel walls over months of growth
common_problems.solution: Soak with H2O2 solution for several hours. Use a long-handled brush. For PVC channels, a pressure washer on low works well.

common_problems.cause: Hidden rot inside dense colas
common_problems.solution: Cut at least 1 inch beyond visible rot. Discard affected material. Inspect remaining buds carefully.

stages.description: Cut plants, trim, and hang to dry. NFT harvest advantage: plants lift cleanly from channel holes — no digging or pot removal. You can harvest individual channel positions at different times if ripeness varies (due to EC gradient along channel). Stop pump, cut plants, clean system.

environment.notes: Optional 24-48 hours of darkness before harvest. Cut at base. NFT plants lift out of channels easily.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse can serve as drying space if humidity is controllable.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse: usable as drying space with proper ventilation.

environment_overrides.notes: Harvest before frost. Bring plants inside to trim in a clean environment.

outdoor.notes: Outdoor: harvest timing may be forced by weather.

health_checks: Were trichomes at target maturity when cut?
Any bud rot discovered during trimming?
Are channels fully cleaned of root debris?
stages.name: Harvest

nutrients.approach: None.

reservoir.notes: Drain reservoir after last harvest. Run H2O2 solution (5ml/gal of 3%) through all channels for cleaning.

tasks.description: 24-48 hours of darkness before harvest. Debated but many growers report stickier trichomes.
tasks.name: Optional dark period

tasks.description: Cut at base. Lift plant + net pot from channel — comes out clean. If channel positions are at different maturities, harvest ready ones and cap their holes.
tasks.name: Harvest plants

tasks.description: Wet trim: trim immediately. Dry trim: hang whole plants. Wet is faster; dry often produces better flavor.
tasks.name: Wet trim or whole-plant hang

tasks.description: Remove all root debris from channels. Flush with H2O2 solution (5ml/gal of 3%). Scrub channel walls to remove salt deposits. Rinse with plain water. Dry completely.
tasks.name: Clean NFT channels

tasks.description: Disassemble pump, clean impeller. Scrub reservoir. Run H2O2 through all tubing.
tasks.name: Clean pump and reservoir

tasks.description: Check for warping, cracks, or worn seals. Replace degraded end caps. NFT channels take more abuse from root pressure than reservoir systems.
tasks.name: Inspect channel slope and fittings

common_problems.cause: Temp too high, humidity too low, fans on buds
common_problems.solution: Lower temp. Raise humidity. No direct airflow on buds.

common_problems.cause: Humidity too high, poor airflow, or hidden bud rot
common_problems.solution: Remove moldy material. Increase ventilation. Lower humidity.

stages.description: Hang whole plants or branches in a dark, cool, ventilated space. Target slow dry over 10-14 days. NFT produces dense buds that may need slightly longer drying time.

environment.notes: Complete darkness. Gentle airflow (not blowing directly on buds). 60°F / 60% humidity is the sweet spot. Slower dry = better cure.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse is NOT ideal for drying — too much humidity fluctuation. Dry indoors if possible.

greenhouse.notes: Avoid drying in greenhouse.

environment_overrides.notes: Dry indoors in a controlled environment. Never dry outdoors.

outdoor.notes: Always dry indoors.

stages.name: Drying

nutrients.approach: None.

reservoir.notes: System cleaned and stored.

tasks.description: Small stems should snap (not bend) when dry. Large stems still slightly flexible. Takes 7-14 days. Do NOT rush with fans or heat.
tasks.name: Check drying progress

tasks.description: Keep 60-65°F and 55-65% humidity. Too fast = harsh smoke. Too slow = mold risk.
tasks.name: Monitor temp and humidity

tasks.description: Inspect hanging buds daily. Dense buds are especially mold-prone. Remove affected buds immediately.
tasks.name: Check for mold

transition_signals: Outside of bud dry to touch

common_problems.cause: Chlorophyll still breaking down. Normal first 1-2 weeks.
common_problems.solution: Continue curing. Terpenes emerge after 2-3 weeks.

common_problems.cause: Buds jarred too wet. Anaerobic bacteria.
common_problems.solution: Remove buds, lay on paper bag 12-24 hours. Re-jar when drier.

common_problems.cause: Over-dried or jar humidity too low
common_problems.solution: Add Boveda 62% pack. Rehydrates in 3-5 days.

stages.description: Place dried buds in mason jars. Burp daily for first 2 weeks, then weekly. Curing converts chlorophyll into smooth-smoking compounds and develops terpene profiles. Minimum 2-week cure; 4-8 weeks is ideal.

environment.notes: Store jars in a cool, dark place. Boveda 62% packs maintain perfect humidity. Light degrades THC.

environment_overrides.notes: Do not cure in greenhouse — temp swings degrade quality.

greenhouse.notes: Cure indoors.

environment_overrides.notes: Cure indoors. Identical process regardless of grow environment.

outdoor.notes: Curing is environment-independent.

stages.name: Curing

nutrients.approach: None.

reservoir.notes: N/A.

tasks.description: Open jars 5-15 min. Week 1-2: daily. Week 3-4: every 2-3 days. After week 4: weekly. Ammonia smell = too wet, leave lid off 1 hour.
tasks.name: Burp jars

tasks.description: Use mini hygrometer in each jar. Target 58-65%. Above 65% = too wet. Below 55% = add Boveda 62%.
tasks.name: Check jar humidity

tasks.description: Check buds visually when burping. Any mold = remove immediately.
tasks.name: Inspect for mold

container_progression.commercial_medium: Turkey bags in sealed bins, nitrogen-flushed
container_progression.industrial: Climate-controlled vault, nitrogen generators, automated monitoring

degradation_timeline.18_plus_months: Convert to extracts, edibles, or topicals.

testing_schedule.360_days: Full panel — sell, process, or destroy decision

common_problems.cause: Heat, light, oxygen, or time
common_problems.solution: Darkness, cool temps (60°F), minimal oxygen. ~5%/year baseline.

common_problems.cause: Temps above 70°F, oxygen, frequent opening
common_problems.solution: Below 65°F. Nitrogen-sealed. Minimize opening.

common_problems.cause: Humidity above 65% or improper cure
common_problems.solution: Verify 58-62% RH before sealing. Remove affected material.

common_problems.cause: Normal moisture equilibration
common_problems.solution: Boveda packs. Sealed containers.

stages.description: Post-cure long-term storage. NFT systems with their fast growth cycles may produce multiple harvests per year — storage planning is critical for inventory management. Proper storage preserves potency and terpenes for 6-12+ months.

environment.notes: DARK. Cool. Stable. Zero light — UV destroys cannabinoids and terpenes. Commercial: 58-62°F, 55-60% RH, nitrogen atmosphere.

environment_overrides.notes: Do NOT store in greenhouse.

greenhouse.notes: Climate-controlled indoor space only.

environment_overrides.notes: Storage is always indoor.

outdoor.notes: Indoor controlled environment.

stages.name: Long-Term Storage

nutrients.approach: None.

tasks.description: Home: mason jars with Boveda 58-62%. Commercial: nitrogen-sealed grove bags, CVault, or nitrogen-flushed drums.
tasks.name: Transfer to long-term containers

tasks.description: Strain, harvest date, storage date, weight, batch number. Commercial: seed-to-sale tracking, FIFO rotation.
tasks.name: Label and track batches

tasks.description: Inspect for mold, off odors. Check humidity packs. Commercial: potency/terpene testing at 30/90/180 days.
tasks.name: Monthly quality checks

tasks.description: Monitor vault temp/humidity. No light leaks. Commercial: automated alerts.
tasks.name: Maintain environment

tasks.description: First in, first out. Flag batches approaching 12 months.
tasks.name: Rotate stock (FIFO)

tasks.description: Commercial: retain samples per regulations.
tasks.name: Compliance testing holds

autoflower.description: Ruderalis-hybrid strains that flower automatically regardless of light schedule. Fixed timeline.

light_schedule.flower: 20/4 or 18/6 (same as veg — no change needed)
light_schedule.transition: Automatic — plant decides when to flower (usually week 3-4)

autoflower.name: Autoflower
autoflower.notes: Fixed timeline — stress cannot be recovered by extending veg. Start in final pot (no transplant shock). LST preferred over HST.

training_suitability.defoliation: light only — auto can't recover from heavy defol
training_suitability.topping: risky — only if plant is vigorous

photoperiod.description: Standard cannabis strains that require 12/12 light to flower. Grower controls timing.

light_schedule.transition: Switch to 12/12 when plant is ~50% of desired final height

photoperiod.name: Photoperiod
photoperiod.notes: Can be vegged as long as desired. Cloneable. More forgiving of stress (can extend veg to recover).

training_suitability.defoliation: good
training_suitability.topping: excellent

total_grow_days.breakdown: Germination (3-7d) + Seedling/Propagation (7-14d) + Veg (24-56d) + Flower (56-70d) + Dry (7-14d) + Cure (14-60d)

solutions: IMMEDIATE: Switch to backup pump (<5 min response time)
EMERGENCY: If no backup, manually pour nutrient solution into channel inlets every 5 minutes until pump is replaced
Diagnose: Check power, check impeller for debris, check breaker
PREVENT: UPS/battery backup for pump circuit. Pump failure alarm. Always have a tested backup pump.

causes: Root mass forming a dam across channel
Root debris caught at channel restriction point
solutions: Gently lift root mass at the blockage point to restore flow
Increase pump flow rate to push through partial restriction
For severe blockage: temporarily remove plants, flush channel with pressurized water, replace
Trim roots carefully if mat is too dense (never remove more than 20%)
Install root guards/screens to keep mat elevated above channel floor
problems.symptom: Reduced or no flow at drain end of channel

solutions: Adjust manifold valves until all channels have equal flow
Check each supply line for air locks or kinks
Flush individual channels
Clean pump intake and impeller
Treat with H2O2 (3ml/gal of 3%) for 24 hours — kills all bacteria
After 24 hours: full drain, clean, refill, re-dose Hydroguard at 3ml/gal
Lower water temp below 70°F (chiller may be needed)
Clear any flow restrictions that created stagnant zones
Maintain Hydroguard at every res change going forward

causes: Light leaking through channel holes or gaps
solutions: Block ALL light entry into channels
Install neoprene collars around every net pot
Cover unused channel holes
Use opaque black channel material only
Flush channels with plain water at every reservoir change
Increase flow rate to reduce concentration gradient
Reduce channel length for future grows (max 10-12 feet)
Consider bi-directional flow (alternating inlet/drain ends)
Monitor EC at inlet vs drain regularly
problems.symptom: Plants at end of channel showing nutrient burn while inlet plants are fine

problems.diagnosis: Salt deposit from nutrient film evaporation at the waterline
solutions: Clean during every reservoir change with a soft brush
Reduce EC if excessive
Keep channel temps stable (reservoir chiller helps)
problems.symptom: White salt crust on channel walls and net pot edges

cons: RO system cost and waste water
water_source_profiles.name: Reverse Osmosis (RO) Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Gold standard for precision growing. Always add CalMag first when using RO.
pros: Blank slate — total control over mineral content

dechlorination.chloramine: Does NOT gas off — must use Campden tablets or activated carbon filter
dechlorination.chlorine: Let sit uncovered 24 hours (gasses off) or use dechlorinator drops
dechlorination.how_to_tell: Check city water report. Most modern cities use chloramine.

water_source_profiles.name: Municipal Tap Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Good enough for most grows. Dechlorinate and pH-adjust. Get a water report from your city.

cons: May contain iron, sulfur, or heavy metals
water_source_profiles.name: Well Water
water_source_profiles.notes: MUST get a water test before using. High iron or sulfur can cause lockout. Great if your well has clean water with moderate minerals.

water_source_profiles.name: Distilled Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Same as RO but purchased instead of filtered. Only practical for 1-2 plant grows.

advanced_techniques.description: Elevating CO2 from ambient (400ppm) to 1000-1500ppm increases photosynthesis rate by 20-40% when light and temperature are also increased.

methods.notes: Most common. Tank refills every 2-4 weeks for a tent.
Produces heat and humidity — better for large rooms with AC.
Produces ~100-200ppm boost. Only useful for small sealed spaces. Not worth it for most.

advanced_techniques.name: CO2 Supplementation

ppfd_co2_correlation.notes: Ambient CO2 sufficient at low light
Slight benefit from supplementation
Moderate benefit — cost-effective threshold
Strong benefit — diminishing returns above this
Maximum practical benefit for most grows
Commercial intensity — requires perfect environment

requirements: Sealed grow space (CO2 escapes quickly from leaky rooms)
High light intensity (>600 PPFD — plants can't use extra CO2 without sufficient light)
Higher temperature tolerance (82-86°F optimal with CO2 vs 75-80°F without)
CO2 controller or timer

advanced_techniques.description: Manipulating environmental and irrigation parameters to push the plant toward vegetative or generative (flowering/fruiting) growth.
advanced_techniques.name: Crop Steering
advanced_techniques.notes: Crop steering is most effective in hydro/coco where irrigation frequency is highly controllable. Soil growers have less precision due to soil buffering.

vegetative_steering.goal: More leaf/stem growth, larger plant framework

best_practices: Spray during lights-off or first/last 30 minutes of light cycle (stomata open, no light burn)
Use fine mist sprayer — coverage is key
pH foliar solution to 6.2-6.5 (leaf surface slightly higher pH than root zone)
EC should be very low (0.2-0.5) — leaves burn easily
Never foliar spray during flower (moisture in buds → mold/bud rot)
Best for micronutrient deficiency correction (CalMag, iron, magnesium)

common_foliar_products.frequency: Weekly preventive, 3-day intervals for active infestations

advanced_techniques.description: Spraying nutrient solution directly onto leaves for rapid uptake. Bypasses root zone entirely.
advanced_techniques.name: Foliar Feeding

advanced_techniques.description: Strengthens cell walls, increases stem thickness, improves heat/drought/pest resistance.
important_notes: ALWAYS add silica to water FIRST before other nutrients (raises pH significantly)
Let silica mix for 5-10 minutes before adding base nutrients
Silica is incompatible with concentrated nutrient solutions — never mix undiluted
Discontinue in late flower (last 2-3 weeks)
advanced_techniques.name: Silica (Potassium Silicate)

advanced_techniques.description: Introduces beneficial bacteria/fungi to the root zone to outcompete pathogens, improve nutrient uptake, and protect against root diseases.
important_notes: Do NOT use hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) with beneficial microbes — it kills them
Choose either sterile (H2O2) OR beneficial (Hydroguard) approach — not both
Beneficials need warmish water (65-75°F) to thrive
Reapply after any reservoir change or system cleaning
advanced_techniques.name: Beneficial Microbes & Root Inoculants

products.name: Hydroguard
products.notes: The standard for DWC/RDWC. Add at every res change.

products.dosing: 1 tsp/gal (water-in) or dust roots at transplant
products.name: Great White
products.notes: Best for soil/coco. Less effective in pure hydro.

products.name: Recharge
products.notes: Feeds existing soil microbes. Great for living soil.

products.name: Mammoth P
products.notes: Most effective in flower when P demand is highest.

advanced_techniques.description: Removing select fan leaves to improve light penetration, airflow, and direct energy to bud sites.
advanced_techniques.name: Defoliation (Strategic Leaf Removal)
rules: Never remove more than 20-30% of leaves at once (except schwazze technique)
Only defoliate healthy, vigorous plants — never stressed ones
Autoflowers: very light defoliation only — they can't recover from heavy removal
Always leave the top 2-3 fan leaves on each branch (they feed the bud below)

timing.method: Remove lower leaves that get no light (lollipop)
Major defoliation day 1 and day 21 of flower (schwazzing)
Selective tuck or remove leaves blocking bud sites

air_pots.best_for: Experienced growers willing to water frequently for maximum root development.

fabric_pots.best_for: Primary recommendation for outdoor container growing. Superior root structure.

plastic_pots.best_for: Hot climates (moisture retention) or windy areas (stability).
cons: Root circling if no air holes
Can overheat in direct sun (black pots)
plastic_pots.sun_protection: Use WHITE or light-colored pots outdoors. Black plastic in direct sun can reach 120°F+ at root zone. Wrap with reflective material if dark colored.

mobility_aids.lightweight_soil_mix: Use more perlite/pumice in outdoor container mix to reduce weight for mobility.
mobility_aids.plant_dollies: Rolling platforms for large pots. Essential for 10+ gallon containers.
mobility_aids.wheeled_bases: Built-in wheels on pot saucers. Budget option.

use_cases: Chase sunlight as sun angle changes seasonally
Move to shelter during storms, hail, extreme heat
Move indoors for frost protection (extend season 2-4 weeks)
Rearrange for optimal spacing as plants grow
Move to darkness for light-dep flowering technique
Bring inside to inspect/treat pest issues
Move away from construction, lawn chemicals, or other disturbances

weight_considerations.note: Plan mobility BEFORE filling and planting. Saturated containers are extremely heavy.
