mobility_advantage.why_it_matters: Containers can MOVE. This is the #1 advantage over in-ground outdoor growing.

frost_protocol: Move containers indoors or to garage when frost expected (advantage of containers!)
If too heavy to move: cover with frost cloth/row cover at night
Group containers against south-facing wall (thermal mass radiates heat at night)
Water soil before frost (wet soil holds more heat than dry)
cold_management.spring_strategy: Containers warm faster than ground in spring (advantage for early start). Place on dark surface in spring to absorb heat. Switch to light surface/elevated in summer.

cooling_strategies: Light-colored containers (white/tan) — reflects heat vs absorbing
Double-potting (pot inside larger pot with air gap for insulation)
Shade cloth on pot sides (not plant canopy) during peak afternoon heat
Elevate pots off dark surfaces (concrete, asphalt) — use pot feet or wooden pallets
Mulch surface heavily (3-4 inches) to insulate top of root zone
Group pots together (outer pots shade inner pot sides)
Water in morning (evaporative cooling) and again if soil temp exceeds 90°F

root_zone_temperature.problem: Outdoor containers expose root zones to ambient temperature extremes. Ground soil insulates; containers do not.

automation_options.drip_irrigation: Timer-controlled drip emitters (2 GPH) per pot. Most reliable for vacations/busy schedules.
automation_options.ollas: Buried terracotta pots that seep water into soil. Low-tech. Self-regulating. Great for smaller containers.
automation_options.self_watering_bases: Reservoir trays that wick water up into pot. Good for short absences (1-3 days).
automation_options.wicking_beds: DIY self-watering container with internal reservoir. Can sustain plants 3-5 days without attention.

fall.notes: Cooling down. Plants finishing. Reduce slightly.

peak_summer.notes: Hot + windy + large plants = extreme water demand. 10-gal fabric pot may need 2-3 gallons daily.

spring.notes: Cool temps, small plants, low demand.

deep_vs_shallow.correct: Water until 15-20% runoff from bottom. Ensures FULL root zone saturation. All roots get water.
deep_vs_shallow.incorrect: Light surface watering that doesn't reach bottom roots. Creates shallow root zone. Plant suffers in heat.

watering_outdoor_containers.frequency_challenge: Outdoor containers dry much faster than indoor. Wind, sun, heat all accelerate evaporation. Budget for DAILY watering in peak summer, sometimes twice daily.

wind_management.problem: Containers elevate plants above ground. Combined with no root anchoring in deep soil, outdoor containers are vulnerable to wind.

stability: Low, wide containers are more stable than tall narrow ones
Place against walls or fences on windward side
Stakes/cages anchored into pot provide support without catching wind
wind_protection: Shade cloth (30-50%) as windbreak — allows some airflow while reducing force
Temporary fencing or lattice on windward side
Companion tall plants (sunflowers, corn) as living windbreak
Move containers to sheltered location during storms

wind_management.training_for_wind_resistance: Top plants early for bushy, wide profile instead of tall single-cola. Lower center of gravity = less wind catch. LST (Low Stress Training) spreads branches laterally.

equipment.name: Fabric pots (15-25 gallon)
equipment.notes: THE outdoor container choice. Air-prune roots, 10-20°F cooler than plastic, breathable. Tan or white preferred for heat reflection. Smart Pot, Root Pouch, GeoPot are quality brands.

equipment.name: Air-Pots (alternative)
equipment.notes: Excellent aeration and air-pruning. More expensive. Work well outdoors.

equipment.name: Saucers (heavy-duty)
equipment.notes: Catch runoff. Use plant caddies underneath saucers.

equipment.name: Plant caddies / dollies
equipment.notes: ESSENTIAL for mobility. Heavy-duty rolling platforms for 15-25 gallon pots. The mobility tool. Budget for one per plant.

equipment.name: Quality potting soil or super soil
equipment.notes: Pre-mixed or build your own. Must be well-draining. Add 30% perlite for outdoor containers (drainage is critical).

equipment.name: Perlite (extra)
equipment.notes: 30% perlite minimum in outdoor containers. Drainage + aeration.

equipment.name: Mulch (straw or cover crop)
equipment.notes: Top of container. 3-4 inches. Retains moisture, reduces heat, suppresses weeds. Living mulch (clover) also works.

equipment.name: Drip irrigation (per-container)
equipment.notes: Drip ring or stake emitters for each container. Timer. Essential at outdoor container scale — hand watering daily is unsustainable.

equipment.name: Watering wand (backup)
equipment.notes: For manual watering and flushing.

equipment.name: Frost cloth / row cover
equipment.notes: For covering containers in place when too heavy to move.

equipment.name: Tarps
equipment.notes: Quick rain protection. Throw over plants/containers.

equipment.name: Shade cloth (30-50%)
equipment.notes: For heat waves above 95°F.

equipment.name: Wind stakes / guy wires
equipment.notes: Secure tall plants in containers against wind. Tip-over prevention.

equipment.name: Tomato cages or bamboo stakes
equipment.notes: Branch support. Top-heavy outdoor container plants need reinforcement.

equipment.name: Trellis netting
equipment.notes: ScrOG for outdoor containers if space allows.

equipment.name: BT (Bacillus thuringiensis)
equipment.notes: Weekly in flower for caterpillars.

equipment.name: Neem oil
equipment.notes: Veg only.

equipment.name: Weather app / station
equipment.notes: Forecasts drive mobility decisions.

equipment.name: Soil moisture meter
equipment.notes: Critical for containers — they dry fast.

equipment.name: Jeweler's loupe (60-100x)
equipment.notes: Trichome inspection.

equipment.name: Infrared thermometer
equipment.notes: Check container surface temp and root zone temp. Very useful for heat management.

flush_timing.coco: 7-14 days reduced nutrients → plain water last 3-5 days
flush_timing.notes: Flushing is debated — some studies show no difference. At minimum, reduce nutrients in final week.
flush_timing.soil: 14-21 days plain water (soil holds nutrients longer)

other_harvest_indicators: Most pistils (hairs) have darkened and curled in
Calyxes are swollen and dense
Fan leaves are yellowing and falling off naturally
Trichome stalks are visible and heads are enlarged
Breeder's recommended flowering time has passed

trichome_ratios.notes: Harvest early for more THC-dominant, less CBN. Energetic, racy high. Slight reduction in yield.
Standard harvest window. Mix of THC and CBN. Full-spectrum effects.
Extended harvest for max CBN conversion. Heavy body high. Good for pain/sleep. Slightly degraded THC.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Micro → Gro → Bloom (always Micro first)
nutrient_brands.name: General Hydroponics Flora Trio

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Part A → Epsom → Part B (dissolve each fully before adding next)
nutrient_brands.name: Jack's 321
nutrient_brands.notes: Jack's 321 is 3.6g Part A, 2.4g Cal Nit, 1.2g Epsom per gallon at full strength. Extremely cost-effective for large grows.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Core → Bloom
nutrient_brands.name: Athena Pro Line
nutrient_brands.notes: Commercial-grade. Simple 2-part with integrated CalMag. Popular in commercial cannabis facilities.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: A → B (separate for veg and bloom)
nutrient_brands.name: Advanced Nutrients pH Perfect (Sensi)
nutrient_brands.notes: Self-adjusting pH technology (claims no pH adjustment needed). Expensive but popular for beginners who struggle with pH.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Shake VERY well before use (thick concentrate settles). One bottle at a time.
nutrient_brands.name: General Hydroponics FloraNova
nutrient_brands.notes: Simplest option — one bottle per phase. Great for beginners. Shake well — concentrate separates.
nutrient_brands.type: 1-part (two bottles — one for veg, one for bloom)

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Dissolve powder in warm water, stir well
nutrient_brands.name: Greenleaf Nutrients Mega Crop
nutrient_brands.notes: One powder does everything — includes CalMag. Incredibly cost-effective at ~$0.02/gal. Community favorite on r/microgrowery.

outdoor_specific.description: Outdoor-specific guidance for both soil and container grows.

challenges: Must be consistent — one missed day can reveg
light_deprivation.description: Manually reducing light hours to force flowering earlier than natural photoperiod.
light_deprivation.method: Cover plants with lightproof tarp at the same time daily (e.g., 8pm → 8am = 12 hours dark)

common_outdoor_pests.damage: Bore into buds, cause rot from inside

common_outdoor_pests.prevention: Good airflow, defoliation, potassium bicarbonate spray
common_outdoor_pests.treatment: Remove affected leaves, sulfur spray, PM Wash

common_outdoor_pests.prevention: Airflow, defoliation, harvest before extended rain
common_outdoor_pests.treatment: Remove affected buds immediately, increase airflow

ipm_schedule.active_infestation: Every 3 days until controlled. Rotate products to prevent resistance.
ipm_schedule.preventive: Weekly neem oil or BT spray during veg. Biweekly during early flower. Stop all sprays 2-3 weeks before harvest.

northern_hemisphere.flower_trigger: Mid-August to September (days shorten below 14 hours)
northern_hemisphere.veg_period: June-August (long days keep plants in veg)

weather_challenges.frost: Cover with frost cloth if unexpected frost. Harvest immediately if hard freeze coming. Plants die below 28°F.
weather_challenges.heat_wave: Shade cloth (30-50%), extra watering, mulch to retain moisture. Plants struggle above 95°F.
weather_challenges.rain: Extended rain during flower = bud rot risk. Shake plants after rain, increase airflow, consider light dep for early harvest.
weather_challenges.wind: Stake/cage tall plants. Strong wind can snap branches. Wind also dries out soil faster.

environment.container: Glass mason jars (quart size). Fill 75% full.

humidity_packs.boveda_58: For drier preference or long-term storage.
humidity_packs.boveda_62: Maintains 62% RH. Set-and-forget. Replace when crispy.
humidity_packs.grove_bags: Heat-sealed bags with built-in humidity regulation. No burping needed.

indicators_of_good_cure: Smooth, flavorful smoke — no harshness or hay smell
Buds are slightly sticky but not wet
Strong terpene aroma when jar is opened
Ash burns to white/light gray (not black)
method: Trim buds to final shape (dry trim or finish wet trim)
Place in mason jars — fill 3/4 full to allow air exchange
Week 1: burp 2-3x daily for 10-15 minutes (release moisture and gases)
Week 2-4: burp 1x daily for 5-10 minutes
Month 2+: burp 1x weekly or seal completely
Cure for minimum 2 weeks, ideal is 4-8 weeks. Some strains improve for 6+ months.

common_mistakes: Drying too fast (>3 days) — harsh smoke, hay smell, terpene loss
Drying too slow (>14 days) — mold risk, diminishing returns
Fan blowing directly on buds — uneven drying, crispy exterior/moist interior
Temperature too high (>70°F) — terpene evaporation, harsh taste

environment.airflow: Gentle circulation — fan NOT pointed at buds. Just keep air moving.

method: Cut branches 12-18 inches long
Optionally remove large fan leaves (wet trim) or leave intact (dry trim)
Hang upside down on line/rack with 6+ inches between branches
Small branches should snap (not bend) when done — 5-7 days minimum
Stems should snap cleanly; if they bend, keep drying

long_term_storage.degradation: THC slowly converts to CBN over time. Light, heat, air, and moisture accelerate degradation.
long_term_storage.method: Sealed mason jars or vacuum-sealed bags with Boveda 58% in dark, cool place
long_term_storage.notes: Properly cured and stored cannabis can remain excellent for 12+ months. Freezing is debated — can damage trichomes if not done carefully.

golden_rules: Fabric pots in tan/white. The #1 equipment decision.
Heat management from day 1. Root zone below 85°F always.
Plant caddies under every container. Mobility is the advantage.
Drip irrigation with timer. Hand-watering daily is unsustainable.
Mulch the top of every container. 3-4 inches.
Keep plants bushy, not tall. Wind tips top-heavy containers.
Map your mobility routes: sun → cover → inside.
Rain coming during flower? MOVE. One rain dodge can save a crop.
Frost warning? Bring inside. In-ground growers can't do this.
15-gallon fabric pot is the sweet spot: good yield, still mobile.
30% perlite in container mix. Drainage is non-negotiable.
Budget for plant caddies. One per plant. Non-negotiable equipment.

heat_management_guide.description: Container heat management is THE #1 outdoor container challenge. Root zone above 85°F = stress. Above 95°F = root death.
heat_management_guide.problem: Containers in direct sun absorb massive heat. A black plastic pot in 90°F air = 120-130°F pot surface. Even fabric pots reach 100-110°F in extreme heat.

tier_2_recommended: Group containers (mutual shading of pot sides)
Position against walls for afternoon shade on pots
Pot-in-pot: nest in larger container with air gap or insulation
tier_3_extreme_heat: Move to afternoon shade during heat waves (>95°F)
Shade cloth on containers (not plants) during heat waves

mobility_strategies.description: Container mobility is the defining advantage of outdoor container growing.
mobility_strategies.planning: Map your routes: sun position → cover position → indoor position. Keep paths clear. Test with full (wet) pots before flower.
tools: Two-person lift for 20+ gallon wet pots
use_cases: Rain protection: move under cover before rain (bud rot prevention)
Frost protection: bring inside for cold nights
Sun optimization: reposition as shadows shift seasonally
Light dep: move to dark space for early flowering
Stealth: move out of sight when needed
Wind protection: move to sheltered area before storms
Pest isolation: move affected plant away from others
Indoor finish: bring inside last 1-2 weeks if weather turns

ph_range.notes: Same as indoor soil. Adjust if using coco (5.5-6.5) or peat (5.8-6.5).

air_pot.cons: Expensive, harder to find large sizes, can dry fast
air_pot.pros: Excellent air-pruning, good drainage, rigid structure

pot_material_guide.description: Container material selection is the first and most important decision for outdoor container growing.

fabric.best_for: Hot climates, any outdoor container grow
fabric.colors: Tan > White > Gray >> Brown >> Black. AVOID BLACK FABRIC — still absorbs significant heat.
fabric.cons: Dries faster (more watering), can tip in wind, less structural rigidity
fabric.pros: Air-prunes roots, 10-20°F cooler than plastic, excellent drainage, lightweight dry, folds for storage

light_plastic.colors: White > Tan > Green >> Brown >> Black. NEVER black plastic in sun.
light_plastic.cons: No air-pruning, root circling, hotter than fabric
light_plastic.pros: Cheap, retains moisture longer, easy to find

terracotta_ceramic.best_for: Patio aesthetics where mobility is secondary
terracotta_ceramic.cons: Heavy (hard to move — defeats mobility purpose), fragile, expensive at large sizes
terracotta_ceramic.pros: Thermal mass (slower temp swings), beautiful, heavy (wind-stable)
terracotta_ceramic.rating: Not recommended for serious grows (mobility loss)

15_gallon.best_for: SWEET SPOT. Good size, still moveable.

20_gallon.best_for: Serious production. Getting heavy to move.

25_gallon.best_for: Maximum production. Needs dolly to move.

30_plus_gallon.best_for: Stationary grows (too heavy for mobility). Approaching in-ground yields.

pot_sizing_guide.description: Pot size determines plant size, yield, and watering frequency.

watering_guide.description: Outdoor containers dry 2-3x faster than in-ground. The #2 challenge after heat.

frequency_by_temp.above_90F: Twice daily, or install drip irrigation

tips: Drip irrigation with timer is nearly essential at scale
Mulch top 3-4 inches to reduce evaporation
Water early morning — evaporative cooling + full water for hot day
Larger pots = more water buffer = less frequent watering
Fabric pots dry fastest (trade-off for root health benefits)
watering_guide.why_faster: Containers lose moisture from ALL surfaces: top (evaporation), sides (sun heating, wind), bottom (gravity). In-ground only loses from top.

scale_tiers.description: First-time grower or single-plant hobbyist. Manual management, learning-focused.
scale_tiers.name: Solo / Single Plant
scale_tiers.notes: Hands-on learning experience. Manual checks 1-2x daily. Ideal for beginners.

scale_tiers.description: Hobbyist tent grow. Light automation helpful but not required.
scale_tiers.name: Small Tent (2-8 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Timer-controlled lights, humidity controller. Sensor monitoring recommended.

scale_tiers.description: Dedicated grower with multiple tents/rooms. Automation saves significant time.
scale_tiers.name: Multi-Tent (9-24 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Multiple environments to manage. Automated alerts essential. Consider perpetual harvest.

scale_tiers.description: Licensed commercial operation. Full automation, compliance tracking, batch management.
scale_tiers.name: Commercial Room (25-100 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: SOPs required. Staff training. Regulatory compliance. Insurance requirements.

scale_tiers.description: Large-scale production facility. Enterprise automation, SCADA integration, dedicated staff.
scale_tiers.name: Warehouse Scale (100+ plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Multiple rooms, dedicated HVAC, industrial nutrient dosing, video surveillance.

common_problems.cause: Black plastic absorbs maximum solar heat — pot surface can reach 120°F+
common_problems.solution: Use fabric pots (tan/white preferred), light-colored plastic, or wrap black pots in reflective material. Container heat is THE #1 outdoor container problem.

stages.description: Start seeds INDOORS. Same as outdoor soil — germinate in controlled conditions, then harden off. While seeds germinate: select containers, prepare medium, plan outdoor placement.

environment.notes: INDOORS. Heat mat, humidity dome.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse germination works.

greenhouse.notes: Good option.

environment_overrides.notes: Start indoors.

outdoor.notes: Indoor germination.

medium.notes: Start seeds in solo cups with seed-starting mix indoors. While germinating: prepare final containers — select pot material/color/size, fill with medium, stage in planned outdoor location to test sun exposure.

stages.name: Germination

nutrients.approach: None — seeds contain all energy.

tasks.description: Same as outdoor soil. Soak, plant, heat mat, dome.
tasks.name: Start seeds indoors

tasks.description: Choose pot material: fabric (best heat management, air pruning), light-colored plastic (reflects heat), or Air-Pots (excellent aeration). AVOID: black plastic pots — they absorb massive heat and cook roots.
tasks.name: Select containers

tasks.description: Final pot size: 10-15 gallon for moderate plants, 15-25 gallon for large plants, 25-30+ gallon for maximum production. Bigger = more water buffer = less frequent watering.
tasks.name: Size containers

tasks.description: Fill containers with chosen medium (super soil, coco/perlite, peat blend). Pre-moisten. Stage in planned outdoor location.
tasks.name: Prepare medium

tasks.description: Map sun exposure: minimum 6 hours direct sun, ideal 8-10+. South-facing is best (Northern Hemisphere). Note shade patterns throughout the day.
tasks.name: Plan placement

common_problems.cause: Day length shorter than indoor 18/6
common_problems.solution: Don't move out until days are 14+ hours.

stages.description: Seedlings develop indoors or in greenhouse. Harden off before moving containers outside. Same hardening off protocol as outdoor soil: gradual sun exposure over 2 weeks.

environment.notes: Indoor grow lights on 18/6. Keep on long light schedule to prevent premature flowering when moved outside.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse seedlings may not need full hardening off if staying in greenhouse.

greenhouse.notes: Easier transition.

environment_overrides.notes: Harden off into outdoor conditions.

outdoor.notes: Standard.

medium.mobility_plan: Move seedlings outside gradually during hardening off. Bring in at night.
medium.notes: Seedlings in solo cups indoors. Begin hardening off 2 weeks before plant-out. Same protocol as outdoor soil: gradually increase outdoor time. With containers, the advantage is you can bring them in easily.

stages.name: Seedling

nutrients.approach: Seed-starting mix provides base nutrition.

tasks.description: 18/6 schedule. Strong stems.
tasks.name: Grow seedlings under lights

tasks.description: 2 weeks before plant-out. Gradually increase outdoor exposure. Container advantage: easy to move in/out.
tasks.name: Harden off

tasks.description: Set empty containers in planned locations. Observe sun exposure, wind, and access for watering. Adjust placement before plants are in them.
tasks.name: Test container placement

common_problems.cause: Container absorbing solar heat — root zone above 85°F
common_problems.issue: Root zone overheating (wilting in afternoon, slow growth)
common_problems.solution: Implement ALL heat management strategies. Switch to fabric pots if using plastic. Elevate. Shade pot surfaces. Water early morning for evaporative cooling. This is the #1 outdoor container problem.

common_problems.cause: Small pot + sun + wind = extreme evaporation
common_problems.solution: Transplant to larger pot sooner. Mulch top. Water twice daily if needed. Larger containers = more water buffer.

common_problems.cause: Top-heavy plant in lightweight pot
common_problems.solution: Weight the saucer (rocks/bricks). Stake to fixed object. Use wider, lower pots. Keep plants bushy, not tall.

stages.description: Transplant to intermediate pots and move outside. Or transplant directly to final containers if using autoflowers. Containers are NOW in full sun — heat management begins immediately. Root zone temperature is the critical factor.

environment.notes: Full sun outdoors. Container surfaces absorb heat — monitor root zone temp.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: less heat stress on containers, less wind.

greenhouse.notes: Protected environment.

environment_overrides.notes: Standard outdoor.

outdoor.notes: Heat management critical.

heat_management.description: Container heat is the #1 outdoor container problem. Root zone above 85°F causes stress. Above 95°F kills roots.
strategies: Use fabric pots (air cooling on all surfaces)
Pot-in-pot: set container inside a larger container with air gap
Elevate on bricks/stand for bottom airflow
Shade cloth on containers (not plants) during heat waves
Group containers to shade each other's sides
Water early morning — evaporation cools root zone

medium.mobility_plan: Keep containers on plant caddies/dollies for easy moving. Can move for sun optimization, weather protection, or stealth. The container advantage.
medium.notes: Containers in direct sun: the pot surface reaches ambient temp + 20-40°F. A black pot at 90°F air temp = 120-130°F pot surface = cooked roots. Heat management is NON-NEGOTIABLE. Fabric pots are 10-20°F cooler than plastic. White fabric is coolest.

pot_size_gal.notes: Transplant to intermediate. Final pot move in late veg.

stages.name: Early Vegetative (Move Outside)

nutrients.approach: Quarter strength. Container medium depletes faster than in-ground — begin light feeding earlier.

supplements.name: Mycorrhizae
supplements.purpose: Apply to roots at transplant.

tasks.description: Transplant to intermediate pots. Place in planned outdoor location. Implement heat management immediately.
tasks.name: Transplant and move outside

tasks.description: Elevate pots. Mulch top. Group for side shading. Use fabric/light colored pots. This is day-1 outdoor container work.
tasks.name: Implement heat management

tasks.description: Containers outdoors dry FAST. Check twice daily in hot weather. Water when top 1-2 inches dry. May need daily watering even in small pots.
tasks.name: Establish watering schedule

tasks.description: Wind tips top-heavy plants in pots. Weight containers (rocks in saucers), use plant caddies, or stake to fixed objects.
tasks.name: Secure containers

tasks.description: Top, LST at 4-5 nodes. Keep bushy, not tall — top-heavy outdoor containers tip in wind.
tasks.name: Begin training

training.description: Top at 4-5 nodes. Keep bushy for wind stability.
Bend outward for wide, low profile.

common_problems.cause: Container too small for vigorous outdoor plant
common_problems.solution: Transplant to larger container if still in veg. Fabric pots air-prune and reduce circling. Once root-bound: the plant's maximum size is limited.

common_problems.cause: Root zone overheating, or root-bound plant can't absorb enough water
common_problems.solution: Check root zone temp (feel pot sides — if hot to touch, roots are stressed). Move to shade. Increase pot size. Water twice daily.

common_problems.cause: Container medium concentrates salts faster than in-ground
common_problems.solution: Flush with plain water at 3x pot volume. Resume feeding at lower strength. This is common in containers — less soil volume means faster salt accumulation.

stages.description: Transplant to final container (10-25 gallon). Peak summer growth. Container plants are smaller than in-ground (limited root space) but still impressive — 4-8 feet tall in 15-25 gallon pots. Water demand peaks — may need twice-daily watering in 15+ gallon pots during heat waves.

environment.notes: Peak summer. Maximum water demand. Heat management is a daily task.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: less heat stress, can use light dep.

greenhouse.notes: More control.

environment_overrides.notes: Peak summer management.

outdoor.notes: Heat management daily.

heat_management.description: Peak heat season. All strategies must be active.
strategies: Fabric pots are 10-20°F cooler than plastic
Water early morning for evaporative cooling
Move to partial afternoon shade during heat waves (>95°F)

medium.mobility_plan: MOBILITY ADVANTAGE: move to afternoon shade during heat waves. Move under cover before rain. Move inside during cold snaps. Rotate for even sun exposure. This is why outdoor containers exist.
medium.notes: Water demand at peak: 1-3 gallons per plant per day for 15-gallon pots. During heat waves: 3-5 gallons or twice-daily watering. Containers dry from ALL surfaces (top, sides, bottom) unlike in-ground which only dries from top. This is 2-3x faster drying than in-ground. Drip irrigation strongly recommended at this stage.

pot_material.notes: Fabric pots air-prune roots and stay cooler. The outdoor container standard.

pot_size_gal.notes: 10-gal for autos/small plants. 15-gal standard. 25-gal for maximum production. Bigger = more water buffer.

stages.name: Late Vegetative (Peak Growth)

nutrients.approach: Half strength. Container medium depletes faster than in-ground. Feed more frequently than outdoor soil (in-ground). Every other watering or through drip.

tasks.description: Move to final 10-25 gallon pot. Pre-moisten medium. Apply mycorrhizae to root ball.
tasks.name: Transplant to final container

tasks.description: Containers dry fast. Check twice daily in hot weather. Water when top 1-2 inches dry. Deep drench to 10-15% runoff each time.
tasks.name: Daily (or twice-daily) watering

tasks.description: Monitor root zone temp. Move to shade during heat waves. Water early morning.
tasks.name: Active heat management

tasks.description: Container medium depletes fast. Half-strength nutrients every other watering.
tasks.name: Feed every other watering

tasks.description: Keep bushy and low for wind stability. Top multiple times. LST.
tasks.name: Training and canopy management

tasks.description: Check forecasts. Move or anchor containers before wind/rain events.
tasks.name: Secure containers for storms

training.description: Top 2-3 times for wide bushy structure.
Bend branches outward. Keep plant wider than tall.

common_problems.cause: Tall stretching plant caught by wind
common_problems.solution: Stake to fixed object. Weight saucer. Keep plants bushy.

common_problems.cause: Street lights, porch lights during dark period
common_problems.solution: Move containers to dark area during night. Or use opaque cover. MOBILITY ADVANTAGE.

stages.description: Natural photoperiod triggers flowering. Container plants stretch less than in-ground (root space limits overall size) but still add 50-100% height. MOBILITY ADVANTAGE: can move containers to optimize light exposure during shorter fall days. Move to catch maximum sun.

environment.notes: Late summer → early fall. Days shortening. Move containers to maximize remaining sun hours.

environment_overrides.notes: Light dep for early trigger.

greenhouse.notes: Light dep advantage.

environment_overrides.notes: Natural trigger.

outdoor.notes: Standard.

heat_management.description: Heat stress easing. Container temp management shifts from cooling to warming in late fall.
strategies: Heat management less critical as temps drop
May need to warm containers as nights cool (dark-colored pots become an advantage in fall)

medium.mobility_plan: Move containers to maximize sun exposure. As sun angle drops, buildings/trees cast longer shadows — reposition for maximum light. This is the killer container advantage in fall.
medium.notes: Shift to bloom feeding. Water demand may decrease slightly as temps drop. But container drying continues — wind accelerates fall drying. Don't assume cooler = less watering.

pot_material.notes: Final pot. Heat management easing as temps drop.

stages.name: Transition (Natural Trigger)

nutrients.approach: Transition ratio. Every other watering.

tasks.description: Transition nutrient ratio. Every other watering.
tasks.name: Shift to bloom feeding

tasks.description: As shadows lengthen, move containers to catch maximum sun. South-facing walls reflect additional light.
tasks.name: Reposition for sun optimization

tasks.description: Supercrop, reinforce supports. Top-heavy stretching plants in pots are a wind hazard.
tasks.name: Manage stretch

tasks.description: Open canopy. Bud rot prevention starts now.
tasks.name: Defoliate for airflow

tasks.description: Weekly during flower initiation.
tasks.name: BT spray for caterpillars

tasks.description: Have a plan to move containers under cover for rain/frost.
tasks.name: Prepare for weather events

training.description: Bend tall stems for height control.

common_problems.cause: 15-25 gallon pots with wet soil weigh 50-100+ lbs
common_problems.solution: Plant caddies with wheels. Dolly. Position containers near cover from the start. Or use lighter medium (coco/perlite instead of soil).

common_problems.cause: Didn't move containers before rain, or can't move them
common_problems.solution: Use tarps over containers if too heavy to move. Shake plants after rain. Defoliate heavily.

stages.description: Buds forming. Container advantage: can bring plants inside or under cover during rain events. This is a MASSIVE advantage over in-ground growing for bud rot prevention. Every rain event is a decision: cover/move the containers or risk the rain.

environment.notes: Early fall. Cooler. Move containers to maximize sun and avoid rain.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: already rain-protected.

greenhouse.notes: Already protected.

environment_overrides.notes: Rain dodge with mobility.

outdoor.notes: Mobility is everything.

strategies: Warming may become needed — dark pots absorb heat
Move against south-facing wall for thermal mass

medium.mobility_plan: RAIN PROTECTION: move containers under cover (garage, porch, overhang) during rain events. This is the #1 outdoor container advantage during flower. One rain dodge can save your entire crop.
medium.notes: Full bloom feeding. Container medium depleting — may need more frequent feeding than in-ground. Water demand moderate (not peak summer levels). Rain protection via mobility is the defining container strategy during flower.

stages.name: Early Flower

nutrients.approach: Full bloom. Every other watering.

tasks.description: Every other watering at 3/4 strength.
tasks.name: Full bloom feeding

tasks.description: Monitor forecast. Move containers under cover before rain. Move back to full sun after rain. This is the container advantage.
tasks.name: Rain protection protocol

tasks.description: Defoliate. Shake after any moisture exposure. Inspect every 2 days.
tasks.name: Bud rot prevention

tasks.description: Weekly for caterpillars.

tasks.description: Stakes, cages. Top-heavy buds + wind.

training.description: Aggressive defoliation for airflow.

common_problems.cause: Wet 20-gallon pots weigh 80-100+ lbs
common_problems.issue: Can't move heavy containers fast enough before storm
common_problems.solution: Pre-position near cover from start of flower. Keep dollies under pots. Have tarps ready. A tarp over containers in place is faster than moving them.

common_problems.cause: Humidity, morning dew
common_problems.solution: Shake plants at sunrise. Defoliate aggressively. Move to area with morning sun (faster dew evaporation).

stages.description: Peak bud development. Container mobility is your weapon against weather. Every rain event: decide — move or cover. Every frost warning: bring inside or cover. Container growers lose far less to weather than in-ground growers.

environment.notes: Fall weather. Move containers to maximize sun angle. South-facing walls reflect extra light.

environment_overrides.notes: Already protected.

greenhouse.notes: Protected.

environment_overrides.notes: Mobility is the advantage.

outdoor.notes: Move for weather.

heat_management.description: Heat not the issue now — cold nights becoming concern.
strategies: Move against south-facing wall for overnight thermal mass
Dark pots now an advantage — absorb daytime heat

medium.mobility_plan: CRITICAL: move for rain, frost, and sun optimization. Heavy pots: use dollies or have a partner. Plan routes from sun position to cover.
medium.notes: Reduce watering. Plants drinking less in fall. Container drying slower in cooler temps. But don't let them dry out completely — roots still active. Feed at reduced strength.

stages.name: Mid Flower (Peak Bloom)

nutrients.approach: Peak bloom reducing. Every other watering.

common_problems.issue: Plant too large to bring inside
common_problems.solution: Bring in sideways through wide doors. Or prune lower branches to fit. Or set up in garage with shop light.

medium.mobility_plan: BRING INSIDE if frost threatens. Move to garage, basement, or spare room with a shop light on 11/13 schedule. Finish indoors for 1-2 weeks if needed. THE container endgame advantage.
medium.notes: Minimal watering. Plants barely drinking. Stop all feeding.

stages.name: Curing

nutrients.approach: None.

tasks.description: Standard. Mason jars or grove bags.
tasks.name: Trim and jar

tasks.description: 2-3x/day week 1, 1x/day week 2.
tasks.name: Burp containers

tasks.description: Daily inspection.
tasks.name: Monitor for mold

container_progression.commercial_medium: Turkey bags in sealed bins, nitrogen-flushed
container_progression.industrial: Climate-controlled vault, nitrogen generators, automated monitoring

degradation_timeline.0_3_months: Peak quality. Outdoor sun-driven terps at their best.
degradation_timeline.18_plus_months: Convert to extracts, edibles, or topicals.

testing_schedule.360_days: Full panel — sell, process, or destroy decision
testing_schedule.initial: Full panel at harvest (extra microbial testing for outdoor)

common_problems.cause: Outdoor flower carries more environmental mold spores
common_problems.solution: Extra inspection first 30 days. Nitrogen-sealed containers reduce risk.

common_problems.cause: Heat, light, oxygen, or time
common_problems.solution: Darkness, cool temps (60°F), minimal oxygen. ~5%/year baseline.

common_problems.cause: Temps above 70°F, oxygen, frequent opening
common_problems.solution: Below 65°F. Nitrogen-sealed. Minimize opening.

common_problems.cause: Normal moisture equilibration
common_problems.solution: Boveda packs. Sealed containers.

stages.description: Post-cure long-term storage. Outdoor container yields are smaller than in-ground (root space limited) but still substantial — 0.5-3 lbs per 15-25 gallon container. Storage is manageable. Container-grown outdoor flower benefits from both sun-driven terpene development and container-optimized root zones. Extra mold vigilance for outdoor-exposed flower.

environment.notes: DARK. Cool. Stable. Zero light — UV destroys cannabinoids and terpenes. Commercial: 58-62°F, 55-60% RH, nitrogen atmosphere.

environment_overrides.notes: Do NOT store in greenhouse.

greenhouse.notes: Climate-controlled indoor space only.

environment_overrides.notes: Storage is always indoor. Extra mold checks for outdoor flower.

outdoor.notes: Indoor controlled environment.

stages.name: Long-Term Storage

nutrients.approach: None.

tasks.description: Home: mason jars with Boveda 58-62%. Commercial: nitrogen-sealed grove bags, CVault, or nitrogen-flushed drums. Container yields are more manageable than in-ground.
tasks.name: Transfer to long-term containers

tasks.description: Outdoor flower had environmental mold spore exposure. Inspect more frequently first month.
tasks.name: Extra mold inspection for outdoor flower

tasks.description: Strain, harvest date, storage date, weight, batch number, container size/medium. Commercial: seed-to-sale, FIFO.
tasks.name: Label and track batches

tasks.description: Inspect for mold, off odors. Check humidity packs. Commercial: potency/terpene testing at 30/90/180 days.
tasks.name: Monthly quality checks

tasks.description: Monitor vault temp/humidity. No light leaks. Commercial: automated alerts.
tasks.name: Maintain environment

tasks.description: First in, first out. Flag batches approaching 12 months.
tasks.name: Rotate stock (FIFO)

tasks.description: Commercial: retain samples per regulations.
tasks.name: Compliance testing holds

autoflower.description: Ruderalis-hybrid strains that flower automatically regardless of light schedule. Fixed timeline.

light_schedule.flower: 20/4 or 18/6 (same as veg — no change needed)
light_schedule.transition: Automatic — plant decides when to flower (usually week 3-4)

autoflower.name: Autoflower
autoflower.notes: Fixed timeline — stress cannot be recovered by extending veg. Start in final pot (no transplant shock). LST preferred over HST.

training_suitability.defoliation: light only — auto can't recover from heavy defol
training_suitability.topping: risky — only if plant is vigorous

photoperiod.description: Standard cannabis strains that require 12/12 light to flower. Grower controls timing.

light_schedule.transition: Switch to 12/12 when plant is ~50% of desired final height

photoperiod.name: Photoperiod
photoperiod.notes: Can be vegged as long as desired. Cloneable. More forgiving of stress (can extend veg to recover).

training_suitability.defoliation: good
training_suitability.topping: excellent

issues.cause: Root zone overheating. Pot surface absorbing solar radiation.
issues.fix: Feel pot sides — hot to touch = root stress. Implement all heat management: fabric pots, elevate, mulch, shade pot surfaces, water early AM. This is the #1 outdoor container problem.
issues.symptom: Wilting in afternoon despite wet soil

issues.cause: Root death from sustained heat above 95°F
issues.fix: Move to shade immediately. Cool root zone with water. May need to transplant to new medium if roots are dead. Prevention is everything.

issues.cause: Mineral deposits from evaporation through fabric sides
issues.fix: Normal with fabric pots. Cosmetic only. Rinse off if extreme.

issues.cause: Container drying too fast. Outdoor conditions accelerate moisture loss 2-3x.
issues.fix: Larger pot. Mulch. Drip irrigation. Water twice daily in heat. Saucers hold some water (but don't let roots sit).
issues.symptom: Wilting every afternoon (not heat — just dry)

issues.cause: Poor drainage or container sitting in standing water
issues.fix: Elevate container. Empty saucers after watering. Add perlite to mix. Ensure drainage holes are clear.

issues.cause: Top-heavy plant caught by wind
issues.fix: Weight saucer with rocks/bricks. Stake to fixed object. Use wider, lower containers. Keep plants bushy via training. Group containers for mutual stability.

issues.cause: Unsupported branches in exposed container location
issues.fix: Cage or stake all branches. Move to sheltered location before storms. Supercrop if still in veg.

issues.cause: Container too small for plant
issues.fix: Transplant to larger container if in veg. If in flower: too late, manage with more frequent feeding. Fabric pots reduce this (air-pruning).
issues.symptom: Root-bound (slow growth, roots circling out drainage holes)

issues.cause: Small soil volume depletes fast. Salt buildup from concentrated feeding.
issues.fix: Flush with 3x volume, resume at lower strength. Feed more frequently at lower dose. Container medium exhausts faster than in-ground.

cons: RO system cost and waste water
water_source_profiles.name: Reverse Osmosis (RO) Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Gold standard for precision growing. Always add CalMag first when using RO.
pros: Blank slate — total control over mineral content

dechlorination.chloramine: Does NOT gas off — must use Campden tablets or activated carbon filter
dechlorination.chlorine: Let sit uncovered 24 hours (gasses off) or use dechlorinator drops
dechlorination.how_to_tell: Check city water report. Most modern cities use chloramine.

water_source_profiles.name: Municipal Tap Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Good enough for most grows. Dechlorinate and pH-adjust. Get a water report from your city.

cons: May contain iron, sulfur, or heavy metals
water_source_profiles.name: Well Water
water_source_profiles.notes: MUST get a water test before using. High iron or sulfur can cause lockout. Great if your well has clean water with moderate minerals.

water_source_profiles.name: Distilled Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Same as RO but purchased instead of filtered. Only practical for 1-2 plant grows.

advanced_techniques.description: Elevating CO2 from ambient (400ppm) to 1000-1500ppm increases photosynthesis rate by 20-40% when light and temperature are also increased.

methods.notes: Most common. Tank refills every 2-4 weeks for a tent.
Produces heat and humidity — better for large rooms with AC.
Produces ~100-200ppm boost. Only useful for small sealed spaces. Not worth it for most.

advanced_techniques.name: CO2 Supplementation

ppfd_co2_correlation.notes: Ambient CO2 sufficient at low light
Slight benefit from supplementation
Moderate benefit — cost-effective threshold
Strong benefit — diminishing returns above this
Maximum practical benefit for most grows
Commercial intensity — requires perfect environment

requirements: Sealed grow space (CO2 escapes quickly from leaky rooms)
High light intensity (>600 PPFD — plants can't use extra CO2 without sufficient light)
Higher temperature tolerance (82-86°F optimal with CO2 vs 75-80°F without)
CO2 controller or timer

advanced_techniques.description: Manipulating environmental and irrigation parameters to push the plant toward vegetative or generative (flowering/fruiting) growth.
advanced_techniques.name: Crop Steering
advanced_techniques.notes: Crop steering is most effective in hydro/coco where irrigation frequency is highly controllable. Soil growers have less precision due to soil buffering.

vegetative_steering.goal: More leaf/stem growth, larger plant framework

best_practices: Spray during lights-off or first/last 30 minutes of light cycle (stomata open, no light burn)
Use fine mist sprayer — coverage is key
pH foliar solution to 6.2-6.5 (leaf surface slightly higher pH than root zone)
EC should be very low (0.2-0.5) — leaves burn easily
Never foliar spray during flower (moisture in buds → mold/bud rot)
Best for micronutrient deficiency correction (CalMag, iron, magnesium)

common_foliar_products.frequency: Weekly preventive, 3-day intervals for active infestations

advanced_techniques.description: Spraying nutrient solution directly onto leaves for rapid uptake. Bypasses root zone entirely.
advanced_techniques.name: Foliar Feeding

advanced_techniques.description: Strengthens cell walls, increases stem thickness, improves heat/drought/pest resistance.
important_notes: ALWAYS add silica to water FIRST before other nutrients (raises pH significantly)
Let silica mix for 5-10 minutes before adding base nutrients
Silica is incompatible with concentrated nutrient solutions — never mix undiluted
Discontinue in late flower (last 2-3 weeks)
advanced_techniques.name: Silica (Potassium Silicate)

advanced_techniques.description: Introduces beneficial bacteria/fungi to the root zone to outcompete pathogens, improve nutrient uptake, and protect against root diseases.
important_notes: Do NOT use hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) with beneficial microbes — it kills them
Choose either sterile (H2O2) OR beneficial (Hydroguard) approach — not both
Beneficials need warmish water (65-75°F) to thrive
Reapply after any reservoir change or system cleaning
advanced_techniques.name: Beneficial Microbes & Root Inoculants

products.name: Hydroguard
products.notes: The standard for DWC/RDWC. Add at every res change.

products.dosing: 1 tsp/gal (water-in) or dust roots at transplant
products.name: Great White
products.notes: Best for soil/coco. Less effective in pure hydro.

products.name: Recharge
products.notes: Feeds existing soil microbes. Great for living soil.

products.name: Mammoth P
products.notes: Most effective in flower when P demand is highest.

advanced_techniques.description: Removing select fan leaves to improve light penetration, airflow, and direct energy to bud sites.
advanced_techniques.name: Defoliation (Strategic Leaf Removal)
rules: Never remove more than 20-30% of leaves at once (except schwazze technique)
Only defoliate healthy, vigorous plants — never stressed ones
Autoflowers: very light defoliation only — they can't recover from heavy removal
Always leave the top 2-3 fan leaves on each branch (they feed the bud below)

timing.method: Remove lower leaves that get no light (lollipop)
Major defoliation day 1 and day 21 of flower (schwazzing)
Selective tuck or remove leaves blocking bud sites

equipment.name: Soil test kit (or lab test)
equipment.notes: Test native soil pH, NPK, and micronutrients BEFORE amending. Lab tests are $15-30 and worth every penny.

equipment.name: Compost (bulk)
equipment.notes: High-quality compost for bed building. Buy by the cubic yard for outdoor scale.

equipment.name: Worm castings
equipment.notes: Best amendment for soil biology. Mix into planting holes and use as top dress.

equipment.name: Perlite / pumice
equipment.notes: For drainage in heavy clay soils.

equipment.name: Dry amendments
equipment.notes: Neem, kelp, alfalfa, bone meal, bat guano, langbeinite, azomite. For building soil.

equipment.name: Cover crop seed
equipment.notes: Crimson clover, winter rye, vetch. Plant after harvest for off-season soil building.

equipment.name: Drip irrigation system
equipment.notes: Timer + mainline + emitters. Essential for outdoor scale. Hand watering 20+ plants is unsustainable.

equipment.name: Soaker hoses (alternative)
equipment.notes: Simpler than drip. Lay under mulch.

equipment.name: Rain gauge
equipment.notes: Know how much rain your plants received. Adjust irrigation accordingly.

equipment.name: Stakes / cages / trellis
equipment.notes: Outdoor plants get MASSIVE. Heavy-duty support from day 1. T-posts + wire for large operations.

equipment.name: Frost cloth / row cover
equipment.notes: Emergency frost protection. Keep on hand from September onward.

equipment.name: Shade cloth (30-50%)
equipment.notes: For heat waves above 95°F.

equipment.name: Deer fencing (8 foot)
equipment.notes: Essential in deer country. 8-foot minimum — deer jump 6 feet easily.

equipment.name: Chicken wire (rabbit)
equipment.notes: 2-foot ring around plants for rabbit protection.

equipment.name: BT (Bacillus thuringiensis)
equipment.notes: THE outdoor caterpillar/budworm control. Spray weekly during flower. Organic. Safe.

equipment.name: Neem oil
equipment.notes: Veg only — don't spray on buds. Aphids, mites, whiteflies.

equipment.name: Yellow sticky traps
equipment.notes: Monitoring tool.

equipment.name: Weather station or app
equipment.notes: Track temperature, humidity, rain, frost forecasts. THE outdoor monitoring tool.

equipment.name: Soil moisture meter
equipment.notes: In-ground moisture monitoring.

equipment.name: Jeweler's loupe (60-100x)
equipment.notes: Trichome inspection.

equipment.name: Straw or wood chip mulch
equipment.notes: 3-4 inches around plants. Retains moisture, suppresses weeds, regulates soil temp, feeds biology. The outdoor secret weapon.

equipment.name: Companion plant starts/seeds
equipment.notes: Basil, marigolds, clover, lavender. Started at same time as cannabis or before.

flush_timing.coco: 7-14 days reduced nutrients → plain water last 3-5 days
flush_timing.notes: Flushing is debated — some studies show no difference. At minimum, reduce nutrients in final week.
flush_timing.soil: 14-21 days plain water (soil holds nutrients longer)

other_harvest_indicators: Most pistils (hairs) have darkened and curled in
Calyxes are swollen and dense
Fan leaves are yellowing and falling off naturally
Trichome stalks are visible and heads are enlarged
Breeder's recommended flowering time has passed

trichome_ratios.notes: Harvest early for more THC-dominant, less CBN. Energetic, racy high. Slight reduction in yield.
Standard harvest window. Mix of THC and CBN. Full-spectrum effects.
Extended harvest for max CBN conversion. Heavy body high. Good for pain/sleep. Slightly degraded THC.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Micro → Gro → Bloom (always Micro first)
nutrient_brands.name: General Hydroponics Flora Trio

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Part A → Epsom → Part B (dissolve each fully before adding next)
nutrient_brands.name: Jack's 321
nutrient_brands.notes: Jack's 321 is 3.6g Part A, 2.4g Cal Nit, 1.2g Epsom per gallon at full strength. Extremely cost-effective for large grows.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Core → Bloom
nutrient_brands.name: Athena Pro Line
nutrient_brands.notes: Commercial-grade. Simple 2-part with integrated CalMag. Popular in commercial cannabis facilities.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: A → B (separate for veg and bloom)
nutrient_brands.name: Advanced Nutrients pH Perfect (Sensi)
nutrient_brands.notes: Self-adjusting pH technology (claims no pH adjustment needed). Expensive but popular for beginners who struggle with pH.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Shake VERY well before use (thick concentrate settles). One bottle at a time.
nutrient_brands.name: General Hydroponics FloraNova
nutrient_brands.notes: Simplest option — one bottle per phase. Great for beginners. Shake well — concentrate separates.
nutrient_brands.type: 1-part (two bottles — one for veg, one for bloom)

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Dissolve powder in warm water, stir well
nutrient_brands.name: Greenleaf Nutrients Mega Crop
nutrient_brands.notes: One powder does everything — includes CalMag. Incredibly cost-effective at ~$0.02/gal. Community favorite on r/microgrowery.

preparation: Dig 3x3 ft hole, 2 ft deep per plant
Amend with compost (30%), perlite (10%), worm castings (10%)

raised_beds.soil_recipe: 1/3 quality compost, 1/3 peat/coco, 1/3 perlite/pumice. Amend with dry organic fertilizer.

in_ground_vs_raised_bed.recommendation: Raised beds for beginners and areas with poor native soil. In-ground for experienced growers with good soil and long growing seasons.

aphids.damage: Suck sap. Excrete honeydew (attracts ants, causes sooty mold).
aphids.signs: Clusters on new growth and stems. Curling leaves. Sticky residue.
aphids.treatment: Ladybugs (natural predator). Neem oil. Strong water spray. Insecticidal soap.

caterpillars_budworms.damage: Bore into buds. Cause bud rot from inside. Devastating in flower.
caterpillars_budworms.signs: Frass (black droppings) on buds, small holes in buds, browning bud sections.
caterpillars_budworms.treatment: BT (Bacillus thuringiensis) spray weekly during flower. Pick off manually. Inspect buds daily.

deer_rabbits.damage: Eat entire plants, especially young seedlings.
deer_rabbits.prevention: Fencing (8 ft for deer, buried 6 inches for rabbits). Companion plants (strong-smelling herbs).

spider_mites.damage: Suck cell contents. Yellow stippling on leaves. Webs in severe infestation.
spider_mites.signs: Tiny dots on leaf undersides. Fine webbing. Yellow speckled leaves.
spider_mites.treatment: Neem oil or insecticidal soap (veg only). Predatory mites (Phytoseiulus persimilis). Water spray to knock off.

bud_rot_botrytis.cause: High humidity + dense buds + rain/dew exposure.
bud_rot_botrytis.critical: Bud rot spreads rapidly. ONE rotted bud can destroy entire plant if not caught. Daily inspection mandatory in late flower.
bud_rot_botrytis.prevention: Shake plants after rain. Ensure airflow through canopy. Choose mold-resistant strains for humid climates. Defoliate inner canopy.
bud_rot_botrytis.treatment: Remove affected buds IMMEDIATELY (cut 1 inch below rot). Check neighboring buds. Cannot be saved — remove and destroy.

powdery_mildew.cause: High humidity + moderate temps + poor airflow.
powdery_mildew.prevention: Defoliate for airflow. Don't water leaves. Potassium bicarbonate spray preventatively.
powdery_mildew.treatment: Remove affected leaves. Neem oil or potassium bicarbonate spray. Improve airflow.

prevention_first: Companion planting (basil, marigolds, lavender deter many pests)
Neem oil foliar spray (weekly during veg, stop 3 weeks before harvest)
Diatomaceous earth around stem base (deters crawling insects)
Inspect plants DAILY during outdoor season (early detection is key)
Beneficial insect habitat (ladybugs, lacewings, parasitic wasps)

hardening_off.description: Gradually acclimating indoor-started plants to outdoor conditions.
protocol: Day 1-2: 2 hours outside in shade, protected from wind
Day 7-8: Full day outside, bring in at night if cold
Day 9-10: Leave outside 24h (if nighttime temps above 50°F)
hardening_off.skipping_risks: Sunburn, wind damage, transplant shock. Plants not adapted to UV intensity will bleach/burn leaves.

seasonal_timing.last_frost_rule: Do NOT plant outdoors until 2 weeks AFTER last frost date for your area.

photoperiod_awareness.flower_trigger: Plants begin flowering when days shorten below ~14 hours (varies by latitude/strain).
photoperiod_awareness.light_dep_option: Cover plants with blackout tarp to force flower earlier. Creates artificial short days. Allows multiple harvests per season.
photoperiod_awareness.veg_trigger: Long days (14+ hours) keep plants vegetative.

proximity.access_frequency: Plan for daily visits during peak season (watering, pest checks, training).
proximity.security: Hidden from public view, roads, neighbors. Fenced to prevent animal/human access.
proximity.water_source: Within hose reach. Outdoor plants in peak summer can need 5+ gallons EACH per day.

soil_assessment.drainage_test: Dig 12-inch hole. Fill with water. Should drain within 4 hours. Longer = amend heavily or raised bed.
soil_assessment.ph_test: Test native soil pH. Cannabis prefers 6.0-7.0 in soil. Amend with lime (raise) or sulfur (lower).
soil_assessment.texture_test: Squeeze moist soil. Sandy = crumbles (needs organic matter). Clay = stays balled (needs perlite/sand). Loam = ideal.

sunlight_requirements.afternoon_shade_benefit: Partial afternoon shade in hot climates (95°F+) prevents heat stress.
sunlight_requirements.direction: South-facing (Northern Hemisphere). Unobstructed southern sky exposure.
sunlight_requirements.morning_sun_priority: Morning sun (east exposure) dries dew faster, reducing mold/mildew risk.

wind_considerations.light_breeze: Beneficial. Strengthens stems. Improves transpiration. Deters some pests.
wind_considerations.strong_wind: Damaging. Breaks branches, dries soil rapidly, causes wind burn on leaves.

container_vs_ground.in_ground: Water deeply, less frequently. Roots access deeper moisture. 2-3 times per week in peak summer.
container_vs_ground.raised_bed: More frequent than in-ground, less than containers. Monitor moisture at 4-6 inch depth.

drought_strategy.deep_watering: Water deeply (saturate full root zone) less often. Trains roots deep. Shallow watering = shallow roots = drought vulnerability.
drought_strategy.water_schedule_peak_summer: In-ground: every 2-3 days. Raised bed: daily to every other day. Morning watering preferred (less evaporation).

benefits: Feeds soil biology as it decomposes

rain_considerations.advantage: Free water. Contains dissolved nitrogen (lightning-fixed). Ideal pH for most soils.
rain_considerations.rain_during_flower: Shake plants gently after rain to dislodge water from buds. Bud rot risk highest after rain events. Tarps/canopies during late flower.
rain_considerations.too_much_rain: Waterlogged soil = root rot. Improve drainage. Raised beds help. Cover with plastic during extended rain events.

outdoor_specific.description: Outdoor-specific guidance for both soil and container grows.

challenges: Must be consistent — one missed day can reveg
light_deprivation.description: Manually reducing light hours to force flowering earlier than natural photoperiod.
light_deprivation.method: Cover plants with lightproof tarp at the same time daily (e.g., 8pm → 8am = 12 hours dark)

common_outdoor_pests.damage: Bore into buds, cause rot from inside

common_outdoor_pests.prevention: Good airflow, defoliation, potassium bicarbonate spray
common_outdoor_pests.treatment: Remove affected leaves, sulfur spray, PM Wash

common_outdoor_pests.prevention: Airflow, defoliation, harvest before extended rain
common_outdoor_pests.treatment: Remove affected buds immediately, increase airflow

ipm_schedule.active_infestation: Every 3 days until controlled. Rotate products to prevent resistance.
ipm_schedule.preventive: Weekly neem oil or BT spray during veg. Biweekly during early flower. Stop all sprays 2-3 weeks before harvest.

northern_hemisphere.flower_trigger: Mid-August to September (days shorten below 14 hours)
northern_hemisphere.veg_period: June-August (long days keep plants in veg)

weather_challenges.frost: Cover with frost cloth if unexpected frost. Harvest immediately if hard freeze coming. Plants die below 28°F.
weather_challenges.heat_wave: Shade cloth (30-50%), extra watering, mulch to retain moisture. Plants struggle above 95°F.
weather_challenges.rain: Extended rain during flower = bud rot risk. Shake plants after rain, increase airflow, consider light dep for early harvest.
weather_challenges.wind: Stake/cage tall plants. Strong wind can snap branches. Wind also dries out soil faster.

environment.container: Glass mason jars (quart size). Fill 75% full.

humidity_packs.boveda_58: For drier preference or long-term storage.
humidity_packs.boveda_62: Maintains 62% RH. Set-and-forget. Replace when crispy.
humidity_packs.grove_bags: Heat-sealed bags with built-in humidity regulation. No burping needed.

indicators_of_good_cure: Smooth, flavorful smoke — no harshness or hay smell
Buds are slightly sticky but not wet
Strong terpene aroma when jar is opened
Ash burns to white/light gray (not black)
method: Trim buds to final shape (dry trim or finish wet trim)
Place in mason jars — fill 3/4 full to allow air exchange
Week 1: burp 2-3x daily for 10-15 minutes (release moisture and gases)
Week 2-4: burp 1x daily for 5-10 minutes
Month 2+: burp 1x weekly or seal completely
Cure for minimum 2 weeks, ideal is 4-8 weeks. Some strains improve for 6+ months.

common_mistakes: Drying too fast (>3 days) — harsh smoke, hay smell, terpene loss
Drying too slow (>14 days) — mold risk, diminishing returns
Fan blowing directly on buds — uneven drying, crispy exterior/moist interior
Temperature too high (>70°F) — terpene evaporation, harsh taste

environment.airflow: Gentle circulation — fan NOT pointed at buds. Just keep air moving.

method: Cut branches 12-18 inches long
Optionally remove large fan leaves (wet trim) or leave intact (dry trim)
Hang upside down on line/rack with 6+ inches between branches
Small branches should snap (not bend) when done — 5-7 days minimum
Stems should snap cleanly; if they bend, keep drying

long_term_storage.degradation: THC slowly converts to CBN over time. Light, heat, air, and moisture accelerate degradation.
long_term_storage.method: Sealed mason jars or vacuum-sealed bags with Boveda 58% in dark, cool place
long_term_storage.notes: Properly cured and stored cannabis can remain excellent for 12+ months. Freezing is debated — can damage trichomes if not done carefully.

marigolds.benefit: Repels nematodes, whiteflies. Trap crop for aphids.

sunflowers.benefit: Trap crop for aphids, attracts pollinators, windbreak

white_clover.benefit: Nitrogen fixation, living mulch, moisture retention

gdd_guide.description: Growing Degree Days (GDD) track heat accumulation. More accurate than calendar days for predicting plant development. Cannabis base temp: 50°F.
gdd_guide.example: Day with high 85°F, low 55°F: GDD = ((85+55)/2) - 50 = 20 GDD

golden_rules: Start seeds indoors. Always. Outdoor germination is unreliable.
Harden off seedlings before plant-out. 2 weeks of gradual outdoor exposure.
Don't plant out until 2 weeks after last frost AND day length is 14+ hours.
Soil test before amending. Know what you're starting with.
Mulch 3-4 inches. The single best thing you can do for outdoor soil growing.
Companion plant. Basil and marigolds are non-negotiable outdoor companions.
Deep, infrequent watering. Encourage deep roots. Not daily shallow watering.
BT spray weekly during flower. Budworms are the #1 outdoor flower pest.
Shake plants after every rain and every dewy morning. Prevents bud rot.
Weather decides harvest timing, not trichomes. Be ready to harvest early.
Plant cover crop after harvest. Build soil for next year.
One outdoor plant can yield 1-5+ pounds. Plan your drying space accordingly.

hardiness_zone_guide.description: USDA Hardiness zones determine growing season length.

zone_4_5.notes: Short season. Choose fast-finishing cultivars (8-9 week flower).

zone_6_7.notes: Good season length. Most cultivars work.

zone_8_9.notes: Long season. Can grow sativas. Multiple harvests possible with light dep.

ph_range.notes: Same as indoor soil. Test native soil and amend to range.

seasonal_calendar.april: Start seeds indoors 4-6 weeks before plant-out. Begin site prep.
seasonal_calendar.august: Transition begins as days shorten. Shift to bloom feeding. Caterpillar season starts.
seasonal_calendar.december_january: Rest. Plan next year. Review what worked.
seasonal_calendar.february_march: Plan. Order seeds. Indoor germination. Soil test. Order amendments.
seasonal_calendar.june_july: Peak vegetative growth. Training, feeding, pest management. Water heavily.
seasonal_calendar.may: Harden off seedlings. Plant out after last frost + 2 weeks. Install irrigation.
seasonal_calendar.november: Late-season harvest (warm climates). Cover crop growing. Soil building.
seasonal_calendar.october: Peak-late flower. Harvest most cultivars. Frost protection. Cover crop planting.
seasonal_calendar.september: Early-mid flower. Bud rot prevention. BT spray. Frost watch begins.

water_requirements.notes: In-ground plants with deep roots need less frequent watering than containers. Deep and infrequent > shallow and daily.

scale_tiers.description: First-time grower or single-plant hobbyist. Manual management, learning-focused.
scale_tiers.name: Solo / Single Plant
scale_tiers.notes: Hands-on learning experience. Manual checks 1-2x daily. Ideal for beginners.

scale_tiers.description: Hobbyist tent grow. Light automation helpful but not required.
scale_tiers.name: Small Tent (2-8 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Timer-controlled lights, humidity controller. Sensor monitoring recommended.

scale_tiers.description: Dedicated grower with multiple tents/rooms. Automation saves significant time.
scale_tiers.name: Multi-Tent (9-24 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Multiple environments to manage. Automated alerts essential. Consider perpetual harvest.

scale_tiers.description: Licensed commercial operation. Full automation, compliance tracking, batch management.
scale_tiers.name: Commercial Room (25-100 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: SOPs required. Staff training. Regulatory compliance. Insurance requirements.

scale_tiers.description: Large-scale production facility. Enterprise automation, SCADA integration, dedicated staff.
scale_tiers.name: Warehouse Scale (100+ plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Multiple rooms, dedicated HVAC, industrial nutrient dosing, video surveillance.

compost_tea.description: Brewed aerated compost tea (ACT) is a microbial inoculant that boosts soil biology.
compost_tea.frequency: Every 2-4 weeks during veg and early flower.
compost_tea.method: Brew with air pump for 24-48 hours. Use immediately (microbes die without oxygen). Apply as soil drench.
compost_tea.notes: Only useful for organic/living soil grows. Synthetic nutrients kill the microbes in the tea.

soil_specific.description: Soil-specific management for traditional soil grows.

components: Base soil (1/3 peat or coco, 1/3 aeration, 1/3 compost/EWC)
Dry amendments (kelp meal, neem meal, crab meal, bone meal, rock dust)
Cover crop or mulch layer (straw, rice hulls)
living_soil.description: A self-sustaining soil ecosystem where you water only — the soil provides all nutrients through microbial activity.
living_soil.re_amend_schedule: Top-dress with dry amendments every 3-4 weeks. Let soil biology break down amendments into plant-available form.

cons: Harder to dial in for max yield
Can attract pests (gnats love organic matter)
organic.description: Feed the soil, not the plant. Build a living ecosystem of microbes that break down organic matter into plant-available nutrients.

synthetic.description: Feed the plant directly with mineral salts. Fast-acting, precise, measurable with EC meter.

top_dressing.description: Applying dry organic amendments to the soil surface. Microbes and watering break them down over 2-4 weeks.
top_dressing.schedule: Top-dress every 3-4 weeks. Adjust recipe based on growth stage (more N in veg, more P/K in flower).

wet_dry_cycle.description: Soil grows benefit from a wet/dry cycle — water thoroughly, then let the top 1-2 inches dry before watering again.
wet_dry_cycle.how_to_check: Lift the pot — heavy = still wet, light = time to water. Or finger test: top inch dry = water.
underwatering_signs: Dry/crusty soil pulling away from pot edges

common_problems.cause: Excited grower starts in January for a June plant-out
common_problems.solution: Count backward from last frost date. Start seeds 4-6 weeks before last frost. Starting too early = leggy, root-bound seedlings.

stages.description: Start seeds INDOORS regardless of outdoor plan. Outdoor germination is unreliable — temperature fluctuations, pests, moisture inconsistency. Germinate in controlled conditions, then harden off seedlings before moving outside.

environment.notes: INDOORS. Heat mat, humidity dome. Seeds need consistent 78°F.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse germination works with heat mat.

greenhouse.notes: Good option.

environment_overrides.notes: Start indoors. Always.

outdoor.notes: Indoor germination.

medium.notes: Start in solo cups with quality seed-starting mix indoors. While seeds germinate: prepare the outdoor site (soil testing, amendment, bed prep). The in-ground soil needs weeks of prep.

stages.name: Germination

nutrients.approach: None — seeds contain all energy. Use seed-starting mix.

tasks.description: Soak 12-24 hours, plant in pre-moistened seed-starting mix in solo cups. Heat mat at 78°F. Humidity dome.
tasks.name: Start seeds indoors

tasks.description: While seeds germinate: test native soil pH and nutrients (soil test kit or send to lab). Begin amending: compost, worm castings, perlite, lime if needed. Build raised beds or prepare in-ground holes. This takes 2-6 weeks to 'cook.'
tasks.name: Prepare outdoor site

tasks.description: Decide on companion plants: basil (repels aphids/mites), marigolds (nematodes), clover (nitrogen fixation), lavender (pollinators + pest deterrent). Start companion plants at the same time.
tasks.name: Plan companion planting

tasks.description: Plants cannot go outside until 2 weeks AFTER last frost date. Know your zone.
tasks.name: Check local last frost date

common_problems.cause: Light too far away or too dim
common_problems.solution: Lower light. Increase intensity. Brush stems gently daily to stimulate thickness.

common_problems.cause: Outdoor day length shorter than indoor 18/6 triggered flowering
common_problems.solution: Don't move outside until day length is 14+ hours. Supplement with outdoor lights for first 1-2 weeks if needed.

stages.description: Seedlings develop indoors under grow lights. Longer seedling phase than indoor grows — you're waiting for weather to be safe. Begin hardening off 1-2 weeks before target plant-out date. Hardening off = gradually exposing seedlings to outdoor conditions.

environment.notes: Indoor grow lights. Keep on 18/6 or 20/4 schedule. Seedlings need consistent light to prevent premature flowering when moved outside.

environment_overrides.notes: Seedlings can stay in greenhouse. Still harden off if moving to open ground.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse seedlings.

environment_overrides.notes: Seedlings indoors, hardening off into outdoor conditions.

outdoor.notes: Harden off before plant-out.

medium.notes: Seedlings in solo cups or small pots indoors. Continue preparing outdoor site. Hardening off protocol: Day 1-3: 2 hours outside in shade. Day 4-6: 4 hours with some sun. Day 7-9: 6 hours full sun. Day 10-14: full day outside, bring in at night. Then plant out.

weather_triggers.action: Begin hardening off 2 weeks before safe plant-out date (2 weeks after last frost)

stages.name: Seedling

nutrients.approach: Seed-starting mix provides base nutrition. No supplemental feeding yet.

tasks.description: 18/6 light schedule. Prevent stretch. Build strong stems.
tasks.name: Grow seedlings under lights

tasks.description: 2 weeks before plant-out: gradually expose to outdoor conditions. Increase sun exposure daily. Reduce watering slightly to toughen stems.
tasks.name: Begin hardening off

tasks.description: Final amendments. Dig planting holes 2x2x2 feet minimum. Fill with amended soil mix. Water in.
tasks.name: Continue site prep

tasks.description: Plant basil, marigolds, clover around planned cannabis locations. Established companions before cannabis arrives.
tasks.name: Install companion plants

tasks.description: Drip lines, soaker hoses, or irrigation plan. Outdoor plants need reliable water.
tasks.name: Install irrigation

common_problems.cause: Root disturbance, sun shock, temperature change
common_problems.solution: Normal for 2-5 days. Shade cloth if severe. Water well. Do NOT fertilize during shock. Mycorrhizae helps recovery.

common_problems.cause: Wildlife browsing young plants
common_problems.solution: Fencing. Deer: 8-foot fence minimum. Rabbits: 2-foot chicken wire. Repellent sprays (temporary).

common_problems.cause: Planted out too early when days were too short
common_problems.solution: Next year: wait until days are 14+ hours. This year: the plant may reveg (confusing) or stay small.

stages.description: PLANT OUT into prepared ground. The plant transitions from indoor conditions to full outdoor growing. Roots expand into native/amended soil. Growth accelerates dramatically with full sun (1000+ PPFD, 40+ DLI). Outdoor plants can grow 2-6 inches per DAY in ideal conditions.

light_dli.notes: Full sun outdoor DLI is 40-65 — double indoor targets.

light_hours.notes: Natural photoperiod. Long days keep plant in veg.

light_ppfd.notes: Full sun. Way more than any indoor light.

environment.notes: Full sun outdoors. The plant now receives more light than any indoor setup can provide. Growth will explode. Temperature and humidity are uncontrolled — the plant must adapt.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: protected from weather. May need supplemental airflow.

greenhouse.notes: Weather-protected.

environment_overrides.notes: Default outdoor environment.

outdoor.notes: Standard outdoor.

companion_plants.benefit: Repels aphids, whiteflies, spider mites. Aromatic companion.
companion_plants.name: Basil

companion_plants.benefit: Nitrogen fixation. Living mulch. Retains moisture.
companion_plants.name: White clover (ground cover)

hardiness_zone.notes: Determines growing season length. Zone 5: ~150 days. Zone 7: ~200 days. Zone 9+: ~270 days.

medium.notes: In-ground planting. Dig hole 2x2x2 feet. Fill with amended soil mix (native soil + compost + perlite + worm castings + dry amendments). Mulch 3-4 inches around plant (straw, wood chips). Mulch retains moisture, suppresses weeds, and regulates soil temp. Install drip irrigation.

weather_triggers.action: Check drainage. Amend with perlite if pooling. Cover if prolonged.
Mulch heavily. Water early morning. Shade cloth if sustained.
Cover with row cover or frost cloth overnight.

stages.name: Early Vegetative (Plant-Out)

nutrients.approach: In amended ground: soil provides most nutrition. Supplement with compost tea 1-2x/month. If using synthetic: very light feeding (1/4 strength) through drip.

supplements.name: Mycorrhizae
supplements.purpose: Apply to root ball at plant-out. Establishes critical fungal network for outdoor growing.

tasks.description: Transplant hardened seedling into prepared ground hole. Water in heavily. Apply mycorrhizae to root ball. Mulch 3-4 inches around plant. Install drip emitter.
tasks.name: Plant out

tasks.description: Stake or cage immediately. Outdoor plants get HUGE — support from day 1.
tasks.name: Install plant support

tasks.description: 3-4 inches of straw or wood chip mulch. Retains moisture, suppresses weeds, feeds soil biology.
tasks.name: Mulch

tasks.description: Drip irrigation: 1-2 gallons per plant per day initially. Adjust based on rain and heat. Deep infrequent watering > frequent shallow watering.
tasks.name: Establish irrigation schedule

tasks.description: Wilting is normal for 2-3 days after plant-out. If persistent: shade cloth for a few days.
tasks.name: Monitor for transplant shock

tasks.description: Start LST, topping once established (2 weeks after plant-out). Outdoor plants respond well to aggressive training.
tasks.name: Begin training

tasks.description: Deer fencing if in area. Chicken wire for rabbits. Row cover available for emergency.
tasks.name: Install pest barriers

training.description: Top at 5-6 nodes once established. Outdoor plants benefit from wide, bushy structure.
Bend branches outward for bush shape. More light penetration.

common_problems.cause: Temps above 95°F — plant can't transpire fast enough
common_problems.issue: Heat stress (taco leaves, wilting in afternoon)
common_problems.solution: Shade cloth (30-50%). Morning watering. Mulch. Foliar spray in evening (water only). Plants recover overnight.

common_problems.cause: Caterpillars (budworms are the outdoor enemy)
common_problems.solution: BT (Bacillus thuringiensis) spray weekly. Hand-pick caterpillars. Inspect INSIDE developing buds — budworms hide inside and cause rot.

common_problems.cause: Sativa genetics + unlimited light + in-ground roots
common_problems.solution: Supercrop tall branches. Top aggressively. Some outdoor plants reach 10-12 feet — this may be a neighbor/visibility issue.

stages.description: Peak summer growth. The longest stage outdoors — often 6-8 weeks of explosive vegetative growth under full sun. Plants can reach 6-10 feet tall and 4-8 feet wide. This is where outdoor growing shows its advantage: unlimited light and root space produce plants that dwarf any indoor grow.

gdd_trigger.notes: 600 GDD from plant-out. Plant at full vegetative pace.

light_hours.notes: Long summer days. Plant stays vegetative.

environment.notes: Peak summer. Full sun. Plant may need 5-10 gallons of water per day at peak. Shade cloth at 95°F+.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: can use light dep to trigger early flowering.

greenhouse.notes: Light dep option.

environment_overrides.notes: Standard outdoor. Full sun.

outdoor.notes: Peak summer management.

companion_plants.benefit: Nitrogen fixation + living mulch between plants
companion_plants.name: Cover crop (clover, vetch)

companion_plants.benefit: Attract pollinators, provide partial shade, attract aphids away from cannabis (trap crop)
companion_plants.name: Sunflowers

hardiness_zone.notes: Zone determines how long this stage lasts. Longer season = bigger plants.

medium.notes: Peak water demand. In-ground plants develop massive root systems — 3-4 foot deep taproots. Water deeply and infrequently (every 2-3 days at 5-10 gallons) rather than shallowly and daily. Deep watering encourages deep roots. Topdress with compost monthly. Maintain mulch layer — it breaks down and needs refreshing.

weather_triggers.action: Double irrigation. Deep soak. Mulch heavily.
Shade cloth. Morning-only watering. Foliar spray evening.
Assess damage. Remove broken branches cleanly. Plants recover well in veg.
Reinforce stakes. Check supports. Supercrop broken branches.

stages.name: Late Vegetative (Summer Growth)

nutrients.approach: In amended ground: monthly compost top dress + biweekly compost tea. Synthetic: 1/2 strength through drip every other irrigation.

tasks.description: 5-10 gallons per plant per day at peak summer. Drip system or deep hand watering. Early morning watering preferred.
tasks.name: Maintain irrigation

tasks.description: 1-2 inches of compost around base. Scratch in lightly. Water in. Refresh mulch on top.
tasks.name: Monthly compost top dress

tasks.description: Continue topping, LST. Open interior for airflow. Outdoor plants get DENSE — defoliate interior.
tasks.name: Training and canopy management

tasks.description: Weekly inspection: aphids (undersides of leaves), caterpillars (frass/droppings), spider mites (stippling), grasshoppers, Japanese beetles.
tasks.name: Pest scouting

tasks.description: Pull weeds around plants. Companion cover crops help suppress weeds.
tasks.name: Weed management

tasks.description: Plants getting heavy. Add stakes, cages, or trellis netting.
tasks.name: Reinforce supports

training.description: Outdoor plants can be topped 3-4 times for wide bushy structure.
Bend tall branches. Creates knuckles that strengthen.

common_problems.cause: Sativa genetics + vigor
common_problems.issue: Extreme stretch (plant too tall for support)
common_problems.solution: Supercrop aggressively. Add taller supports. You cannot top during stretch — too late.

common_problems.cause: Corn earworm / budworm laying eggs on buds
common_problems.solution: BT spray weekly. Hand-inspect every bud. Remove caterpillars. Cut out damaged bud sections. This is THE outdoor battle.

common_problems.cause: Street lights, porch lights, nearby buildings
common_problems.solution: Any light during dark period disrupts flowering. Move plants if possible. Screen with opaque barriers.

stages.description: Natural photoperiod triggers flowering as day length drops below ~14 hours. The stretch begins. Outdoor stretch can be DRAMATIC — plants may add 50-100% height.
