This is the most critical transition outdoors: the plant shifts from vegetative to reproductive, and you can't control the timing (unless using light dep).

gdd_trigger.notes: ~1000 GDD. Natural photoperiod triggers flowering as days shorten.

light_hours.notes: Dropping below 14 hours triggers transition. Gradual — not instant like indoor 12/12.

light_ppfd.notes: Sun angle lowering. Slightly less intensity than peak summer.

environment.notes: Late summer → early fall. Days getting shorter. Nights getting cooler. Humidity may be rising. This is when outdoor growing gets interesting — you're racing against fall weather.

environment_overrides.notes: Light dep triggers flowering earlier — more time before frost.

extra_tasks.description: Pull tarps to enforce 12/12 starting 4-6 weeks before natural trigger. Gives more time for flowering before fall weather.
extra_tasks.name: Light dep schedule

greenhouse.notes: Light dep is the greenhouse advantage.

environment_overrides.notes: Natural photoperiod triggers transition.

outdoor.notes: Standard outdoor.

hardiness_zone.notes: Zone determines how much time you have before frost. Zone 5: flower must finish by mid-October. Zone 9: November-December.

medium.notes: Shift to bloom feeding. Top dress with bloom amendments (bone meal, bat guano, langbeinite) or switch synthetic ratio. The plant's water needs may actually DECREASE as days shorten and temps drop. Adjust irrigation. Monitor weather forecasts obsessively — fall weather determines outdoor harvest quality.

weather_triggers.action: Cover plants with frost cloth. Cannabis dies at 28°F. Damaged below 40°F.
Pre-treat with anti-fungal. Prepare tarps. Bud rot season begins.
Defoliate heavily for airflow. This is critical for bud rot prevention.

stages.name: Transition (Natural Trigger)

nutrients.approach: Transition ratio. Reduce N, increase PK. Top dress with bloom amendments. Through drip: transition feed.

tasks.description: Top dress: bone meal + bat guano + langbeinite. Synthetic: transition ratio through drip.
tasks.name: Shift to bloom feeding

tasks.description: Outdoor stretch can add 3-5 feet. Supercrop, reinforce supports.
tasks.name: Manage stretch

tasks.description: CRITICAL outdoors. Dense canopy + fall humidity = bud rot. Open up the interior.
tasks.name: Defoliate for airflow

tasks.description: Budworms are the #1 outdoor flower pest. BT spray weekly. Inspect buds.
tasks.name: Scout for caterpillars intensely

tasks.description: Track frost dates, rain forecasts, humidity. Plan harvest timing.
tasks.name: Monitor weather forecasts

tasks.description: Drying space ready. Trim supplies. The harvest may be forced by weather.
tasks.name: Prepare harvest supplies

training.description: Aggressive bending for height control.

common_problems.cause: Rain + humidity + dense buds. THE outdoor problem.
common_problems.solution: Remove affected bud + 2 inches of healthy tissue. Increase airflow. Spray with potassium bicarbonate or Regalia. If widespread: consider early harvest to save what you can.

common_problems.cause: Budworms hiding inside buds, causing internal rot
common_problems.solution: BT spray. Hand-inspect by gently pulling buds apart. Brown frass (caterpillar droppings) = budworm present. Remove immediately.

common_problems.cause: Night temps below 50°F
common_problems.solution: Some purple is genetic. But if growth slows dramatically: cover overnight with row cover. The plant can handle brief cold but sustained cold slows ripening.

stages.description: Buds forming. The race against fall weather begins in earnest. Bud rot prevention becomes the #1 priority. Every rain event, every humid night, every morning dew is a potential disaster. Outdoor flower quality depends almost entirely on weather management.

light_ppfd.notes: Sun lower in sky. Fewer peak hours.

environment.notes: Early fall. Cooler nights bringing out colors. But humidity and rain bring bud rot. Every grower decision from here is about protecting buds from weather.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: protected from rain. Major advantage.

greenhouse.notes: Rain protection is the greenhouse advantage in flower.

environment_overrides.notes: Standard outdoor. Weather management is everything.

outdoor.notes: Bud rot prevention is #1.

health_checks: Weather forecast favorable for next 2 weeks?

hardiness_zone.notes: Track first frost date. You need 6-10 more weeks of frost-free weather.

medium.notes: Bloom feeding continues. Reduce irrigation as temps drop — plants drinking less. Watch for overwatering in cool weather. In-ground plants may need no irrigation if getting rain (but rain brings rot risk — the eternal outdoor tradeoff).

weather_triggers.action: Shake plants after rain to remove water from buds. Inspect for rot 24-48 hours after every rain.
Cover with frost cloth or harvest if frost is hard (below 28°F).
Shake plants at sunrise to remove dew from buds.

stages.name: Early Flower

nutrients.approach: Full bloom. Heavy PK. Top dress or synthetic through drip.

tasks.description: Top dress bloom amendments. Synthetic through drip.
tasks.name: Full bloom feeding

tasks.description: Defoliate heavily. Shake plants after rain/dew. Inspect buds every 2 days. Remove ANY suspicious buds immediately. One rotten bud can destroy a cola in 24 hours.
tasks.name: Bud rot prevention protocol

tasks.description: Weekly BT spray. Budworms cause bud rot by damaging buds from the inside. Stop BT 2 weeks before harvest.
tasks.name: BT spray for caterpillars

tasks.description: Remove standing water from buds. Do this every morning with heavy dew.
tasks.name: Shake plants after rain/dew

tasks.description: Stakes, trellis, netting. Outdoor buds get heavy.
tasks.name: Support heavy branches

training.description: Remove more leaves than you would indoors. Airflow is critical outdoor.

common_problems.cause: Rain + humidity + dense outdoor buds
common_problems.solution: Remove all affected material. If more than 20% of buds are affected: HARVEST NOW. Save what you can. Outdoor bud rot can destroy an entire crop in 48 hours of rain.

common_problems.cause: Unexpected frost before buds are ready
common_problems.solution: Light frost (32-35°F): cover with frost cloth, plants survive. Hard frost (below 28°F): harvest immediately. Frost-damaged buds degrade rapidly.

stages.description: Peak bud development outdoors. The most anxious time for outdoor growers — buds are fattening beautifully but weather threats are maximum. Every day the buds survive is a win. Bud rot, caterpillars, and early frost are the three threats.

environment.notes: Fall weather. Cooler nights developing colors. Morning dew is heavy. Rain events are high-anxiety. Every sunny day is a gift.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: rain protection. Huge advantage.

greenhouse.notes: Protected.

environment_overrides.notes: Standard outdoor. Weather anxiety is normal.

outdoor.notes: Bud rot prevention is everything.

hardiness_zone.notes: Track days until first frost. You need 3-5 more weeks.

medium.notes: Reduce irrigation significantly. Plants drinking less. Cool temps + rain may provide all water needed. Overwatering in fall causes root issues. Continue bloom feeding but at reduced strength. The plant is finishing.

weather_triggers.action: Consider tarping plants or early harvest. Extended rain during peak bloom = guaranteed rot.
Cover or harvest. Hard frost (28°F) kills. Light frost (32-35°F) damages.
Shake plants at sunrise. Every morning. Non-negotiable.

stages.name: Mid Flower (Peak Bloom)

nutrients.approach: Peak bloom. Reducing as plant finishes. Top dress winding down.

tasks.description: Inspect every bud on every plant. Pull apart large colas gently. Brown, mushy spots = rot. Remove immediately + 2 inches of healthy tissue.
tasks.name: Daily bud rot inspection

tasks.description: Remove dew from buds. Every morning. This simple act prevents enormous amounts of rot.
tasks.name: Shake plants at sunrise

tasks.description: Last BT spray 2 weeks before expected harvest.
tasks.name: BT spray (final weeks)

tasks.description: Track extended forecasts. If 3+ day rain event is coming and buds are close to done: harvest early. Better to harvest slightly early than lose to rot.
tasks.name: Weather-based harvest planning

tasks.description: Heavy buds + wet from rain = branch breaks.
tasks.name: Support branches

common_problems.cause: Frost or extended rain before trichomes are ready
common_problems.solution: Harvest. An early harvest at 80% milky trichomes is vastly better than a rotted or frost-killed crop. This is the outdoor reality.

stages.description: Buds ripening. Nature is putting on the fall show — colors, aromas, and trichome development peak. But so do weather risks. Many outdoor growers harvest during this stage rather than waiting for perfect trichome ratios — weather decides, not the grower.

environment.notes: Late fall. Cool nights producing colors. Frost risk increasing daily.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: more time before weather forces harvest.

greenhouse.notes: Extended window.

environment_overrides.notes: Weather controls harvest timing.

outdoor.notes: Nature decides.

hardiness_zone.notes: Frost imminent in zones 5-6. Zones 8-9 have more time.

medium.notes: Minimal irrigation. Plants barely drinking. Natural senescence — fan leaves dropping. This is beautiful and natural. Stop all feeding. Plain water only if soil is very dry.

weather_triggers.action: Harvest. Don't gamble.
Harvest if buds are close. Rain + late flower = rot.

stages.name: Late Flower (Ripening)

nutrients.approach: Stop feeding. Let the plant use stored nutrients. Natural outdoor senescence.

tasks.description: Critical. Every day.
tasks.name: Daily bud rot inspection

tasks.description: Track milky → amber. But be ready to harvest based on weather, not trichomes.
tasks.name: Trichome checks

tasks.description: Monitor forecasts twice daily. Have frost cloth and harvest supplies ready.
tasks.name: Frost watch

tasks.description: Outdoor harvest timing: trichome readiness vs weather threat. If frost or rain threatens: harvest. Better 90% ready than 100% rotten.
tasks.name: Harvest decision point

common_problems.cause: Frost or rain approaching
common_problems.issue: Can't flush long enough (weather forcing harvest)
common_problems.solution: Harvest. Outdoor flush is a luxury, not a requirement. In-ground organic plants don't need formal flushing.

stages.description: Outdoor soil often doesn't need a formal flush — the plant naturally stops feeding as it senesces. In amended ground, there are no synthetic salts to flush. The plant consumes stored nutrients. Many outdoor growers simply stop all inputs and let nature finish.

environment.notes: Late fall. Plant finishing. Frost very close.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: more time for natural finish.

greenhouse.notes: Extended window.

environment_overrides.notes: Natural senescence.

outdoor.notes: Nature's flush.

hardiness_zone.notes: Flush may be cut short by weather.

medium.notes: No inputs. Just water if bone dry. Outdoor organic in-ground plants flush themselves naturally. Synthetic outdoor grows: rain often provides natural flush. Don't stress about flush timing outdoors — weather controls everything.

weather_triggers.action: Harvest immediately. Flush is secondary to saving the crop.

stages.name: Flush (Natural Senescence)

nutrients.approach: None. Natural senescence.

tasks.description: No feeding. Water only if soil is very dry.
tasks.name: Stop all inputs

tasks.description: Still the #1 risk.
tasks.name: Continue daily bud rot checks

tasks.description: Drying space ready. Trim supplies. Frost cloth on standby.
tasks.name: Prepare for harvest

common_problems.cause: Large outdoor plants yield pounds, not ounces
common_problems.issue: Too much material to process at once
common_problems.solution: Stagger harvest over 2-3 days. Hang whole branches rather than trimming immediately. Wet trim is faster for large volumes.

stages.description: Outdoor harvest is a multi-day event for large plants. A single outdoor plant can yield 1-5+ pounds — you can't process that in an afternoon. Plan for 2-3 day harvest windows. Weather may force staggered harvests — take the most vulnerable/ripe plants first.

environment.notes: Harvest in morning after dew evaporates. Dry day preferred.

environment_overrides.notes: More controlled timing.

greenhouse.notes: More flexibility.

environment_overrides.notes: Harvest before frost.

outdoor.notes: Weather dictates.

medium.notes: After harvest: chop plant at base. Leave roots in ground — they decompose and add organic matter. Plant cover crop (crimson clover, winter rye) immediately in the vacated ground for off-season soil building.

stages.name: Harvest

nutrients.approach: None.

tasks.description: Cut at base or branch by branch. Large outdoor plants: harvest in sections. Most ripe branches first.
tasks.name: Harvest plants

tasks.description: Outdoor buds MUST be inspected during trim. Hidden rot is common.
tasks.name: Inspect every bud for rot

tasks.description: Immediately after harvest: scatter cover crop seed (crimson clover, winter rye, vetch). Water in. This builds soil for next season.
tasks.name: Plant cover crop

tasks.description: Remove stakes, cages, irrigation. Chop remaining stalks. Leave roots to decompose in ground.
tasks.name: Clean up site

common_problems.cause: Pounds of wet material releasing moisture
common_problems.issue: Drying space too humid from volume
common_problems.solution: Bigger dehumidifier. Stage the hanging (don't hang everything at once). Increase airflow.

stages.description: Dry indoors. The volume from outdoor plants demands dedicated drying space. Garages, spare rooms, or dedicated dry rooms. 60°F / 60% RH / dark. Outdoor-grown flower may have more stem moisture and take slightly longer to dry.

environment.notes: INDOORS. Dark. 60/60. Large volume needs good airflow planning.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse can work for drying if dark and temp-controlled.

greenhouse.notes: Possible if controlled.

environment_overrides.notes: Dry indoors.

outdoor.notes: Always indoors.

medium.notes: No outdoor involvement. Drying is indoor environmental control.

stages.name: Drying

nutrients.approach: None.

tasks.description: Space so they don't touch. Large volume needs planning.
tasks.name: Hang branches

tasks.description: Dehumidifier and AC/heater as needed. Large volumes release lots of moisture.
tasks.name: Maintain 60/60

tasks.description: Bend stems. Snap = ready.
tasks.name: Check drying progress

tasks.description: Outdoor buds have more exposure to mold spores. Check daily.
tasks.name: Inspect for mold

common_problems.cause: Too wet when jarred
common_problems.solution: Remove, dry 12-24 hours, rejar.

common_problems.cause: Mold spores from outdoor growing surviving into cure
common_problems.solution: Inspect carefully when burping. Remove affected buds immediately.

stages.description: Mason jar cure. Outdoor-grown, soil-fed flower often has exceptional terpene profiles that develop beautifully during cure. Many connoisseurs consider outdoor organic soil-grown flower the gold standard for flavor.

environment.notes: In-jar 58-62% RH. Dark, cool. Large volume may need turkey bags or grove bags instead of jars.

environment_overrides.notes: Cure indoors.

greenhouse.notes: Stable indoor space.

environment_overrides.notes: Cure indoors.

outdoor.notes: Indoor cure.

medium.notes: Post-harvest.

stages.name: Curing

nutrients.approach: None.

tasks.description: Large volume: grove bags or turkey bags may be more practical than mason jars.
tasks.name: Trim and jar/bag

tasks.description: 2-3x/day week 1, 1x/day week 2.
tasks.name: Burp containers

tasks.description: Outdoor buds: extra mold vigilance.
tasks.name: Monitor for mold

container_progression.commercial_medium: Turkey bags in sealed bins, nitrogen-flushed
container_progression.home_large: Grove bags or turkey bags (more practical for outdoor volumes)
container_progression.home_small: Mason jars with Boveda packs (you'll need LOTS for outdoor yields)
container_progression.industrial: Climate-controlled vault, nitrogen generators, automated monitoring

degradation_timeline.0_3_months: Peak quality. Outdoor organic terps continuing to develop.
degradation_timeline.12_18_months: Significant decline. Process into extracts or edibles.
degradation_timeline.18_plus_months: Convert to extracts, edibles, or topicals.
degradation_timeline.3_6_months: Slight terpene reduction (~10-15%). Still premium outdoor.

testing_schedule.360_days: Full panel — sell, process, or destroy decision
testing_schedule.initial: Full panel at harvest (extra microbial testing for outdoor)

common_problems.cause: Outdoor flower carries more environmental mold spores than indoor
common_problems.solution: Extra inspection first 30 days. Remove any suspicious buds. Nitrogen-sealed containers reduce mold risk. If in doubt: test.

common_problems.cause: Heat, light, oxygen, or time
common_problems.solution: Darkness, cool temps (60°F), minimal oxygen. ~5%/year baseline.

common_problems.cause: Temps above 70°F, oxygen, frequent opening
common_problems.solution: Below 65°F. Nitrogen-sealed. Minimize opening.

common_problems.cause: Outdoor yields are 5-20x larger than indoor per plant
common_problems.solution: Plan storage BEFORE harvest. Grove bags and turkey bags for large volumes. Dedicated vault room for commercial.

common_problems.cause: Normal moisture equilibration
common_problems.solution: Boveda packs. Sealed containers.

stages.description: Post-cure long-term storage. Outdoor soil grows produce MASSIVE yields — a single plant can produce 1-5+ lbs. Storage planning is critical. Outdoor flower has higher mold spore exposure from its growing environment, so extra vigilance during storage is essential. Organic outdoor soil flower is prized by connoisseurs for exceptional terpene complexity — protect that investment.

environment.notes: DARK. Cool. Stable. Zero light — UV destroys cannabinoids and terpenes. Commercial: 58-62°F, 55-60% RH, nitrogen atmosphere. Outdoor flower needs extra mold vigilance due to field exposure.

environment_overrides.notes: Do NOT store in greenhouse.

greenhouse.notes: Climate-controlled indoor space only.

environment_overrides.notes: Storage is always indoor. Extra mold vigilance for field-grown flower.

outdoor.notes: Indoor controlled environment.

health_checks: Extra mold checks for outdoor flower?
stages.name: Long-Term Storage

nutrients.approach: None.

tasks.description: Home: mason jars with Boveda 58-62%. Commercial: nitrogen-sealed grove bags, CVault, or nitrogen-flushed drums. OUTDOOR YIELDS ARE HUGE — plan container volume for pounds, not ounces.
tasks.name: Transfer to long-term containers

tasks.description: Outdoor flower had more environmental mold spore exposure than indoor. Inspect more frequently in first month of storage. Any suspicious buds: remove immediately.
tasks.name: Extra mold inspection for outdoor flower

tasks.description: Strain, harvest date, storage date, weight, batch number, grow method (organic/synthetic). Commercial: seed-to-sale, FIFO.
tasks.name: Label and track batches

tasks.description: Inspect for mold, off odors. Check humidity packs. Commercial: potency/terpene testing at 30/90/180 days.
tasks.name: Monthly quality checks

tasks.description: Monitor vault temp/humidity. No light leaks. Commercial: automated alerts.
tasks.name: Maintain environment

tasks.description: First in, first out. Outdoor harvests are annual — entire year's production stored at once.
tasks.name: Rotate stock (FIFO)

tasks.description: Commercial: retain samples per regulations. Outdoor flower may need additional microbial testing.
tasks.name: Compliance testing holds

autoflower.description: Ruderalis-hybrid strains that flower automatically regardless of light schedule. Fixed timeline.

light_schedule.flower: 20/4 or 18/6 (same as veg — no change needed)
light_schedule.transition: Automatic — plant decides when to flower (usually week 3-4)

autoflower.name: Autoflower
autoflower.notes: Fixed timeline — stress cannot be recovered by extending veg. Start in final pot (no transplant shock). LST preferred over HST.

training_suitability.defoliation: light only — auto can't recover from heavy defol
training_suitability.topping: risky — only if plant is vigorous

photoperiod.description: Standard cannabis strains that require 12/12 light to flower. Grower controls timing.

light_schedule.transition: Switch to 12/12 when plant is ~50% of desired final height

photoperiod.name: Photoperiod
photoperiod.notes: Can be vegged as long as desired. Cloneable. More forgiving of stress (can extend veg to recover).

training_suitability.defoliation: good
training_suitability.topping: excellent

issues.cause: Rain + humidity + dense outdoor buds. THE outdoor problem.
issues.fix: Remove affected material + 2 inches. Defoliate for airflow. Shake after rain/dew. If widespread: harvest early. Prevention > cure.

issues.cause: Temperature below 32°F
issues.fix: Light frost: cover with frost cloth. Hard frost (below 28°F): harvest immediately. Frost-damaged buds degrade fast.

issues.cause: Temps above 95°F
issues.fix: Shade cloth (30-50%). Morning watering. Mulch heavily. Plants recover overnight.

issues.cause: Strong wind or storms
issues.fix: Reinforce supports. Supercrop broken branches if possible. Stake everything.

issues.cause: Corn earworm, cabbage looper, or other caterpillars
issues.fix: BT spray weekly. Hand-inspect buds. Remove caterpillars. Cut out damaged bud sections. THE outdoor pest battle.

issues.cause: Deer eating plants
issues.fix: 8-foot fence. Repellent sprays (temporary). Young plants are most vulnerable.

issues.cause: Aphid colony on undersides of leaves
issues.fix: Blast with water hose. Neem oil. Ladybugs (release at dusk). Companion plants (basil, marigolds).

issues.cause: Grasshoppers — worse in dry years
issues.fix: Nosema locustae (biological control). Row cover for small plants. Difficult to control at scale.

issues.cause: Native soil too clay, too sandy, wrong pH, or nutrient-depleted
issues.fix: Soil test first. Amend heavily: compost, perlite for clay, vermiculite for sand. Build raised beds if native soil is hopeless.

issues.cause: Soil pH out of range locking out nutrients
issues.fix: Check pH. Add lime if too acidic (<6.0). Add sulfur if too alkaline (>7.0). pH is the #1 soil nutrition issue.

cons: RO system cost and waste water
water_source_profiles.name: Reverse Osmosis (RO) Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Gold standard for precision growing. Always add CalMag first when using RO.
pros: Blank slate — total control over mineral content

dechlorination.chloramine: Does NOT gas off — must use Campden tablets or activated carbon filter
dechlorination.chlorine: Let sit uncovered 24 hours (gasses off) or use dechlorinator drops
dechlorination.how_to_tell: Check city water report. Most modern cities use chloramine.

water_source_profiles.name: Municipal Tap Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Good enough for most grows. Dechlorinate and pH-adjust. Get a water report from your city.

cons: May contain iron, sulfur, or heavy metals
water_source_profiles.name: Well Water
water_source_profiles.notes: MUST get a water test before using. High iron or sulfur can cause lockout. Great if your well has clean water with moderate minerals.

water_source_profiles.name: Distilled Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Same as RO but purchased instead of filtered. Only practical for 1-2 plant grows.

advanced_techniques.description: Elevating CO2 from ambient (400ppm) to 1000-1500ppm increases photosynthesis rate by 20-40% when light and temperature are also increased.

methods.notes: Most common. Tank refills every 2-4 weeks for a tent.
Produces heat and humidity — better for large rooms with AC.
Produces ~100-200ppm boost. Only useful for small sealed spaces. Not worth it for most.

advanced_techniques.name: CO2 Supplementation

ppfd_co2_correlation.notes: Ambient CO2 sufficient at low light
Slight benefit from supplementation
Moderate benefit — cost-effective threshold
Strong benefit — diminishing returns above this
Maximum practical benefit for most grows
Commercial intensity — requires perfect environment

requirements: Sealed grow space (CO2 escapes quickly from leaky rooms)
High light intensity (>600 PPFD — plants can't use extra CO2 without sufficient light)
Higher temperature tolerance (82-86°F optimal with CO2 vs 75-80°F without)
CO2 controller or timer

advanced_techniques.description: Manipulating environmental and irrigation parameters to push the plant toward vegetative or generative (flowering/fruiting) growth.
advanced_techniques.name: Crop Steering
advanced_techniques.notes: Crop steering is most effective in hydro/coco where irrigation frequency is highly controllable. Soil growers have less precision due to soil buffering.

vegetative_steering.goal: More leaf/stem growth, larger plant framework

best_practices: Spray during lights-off or first/last 30 minutes of light cycle (stomata open, no light burn)
Use fine mist sprayer — coverage is key
pH foliar solution to 6.2-6.5 (leaf surface slightly higher pH than root zone)
EC should be very low (0.2-0.5) — leaves burn easily
Never foliar spray during flower (moisture in buds → mold/bud rot)
Best for micronutrient deficiency correction (CalMag, iron, magnesium)

common_foliar_products.frequency: Weekly preventive, 3-day intervals for active infestations

advanced_techniques.description: Spraying nutrient solution directly onto leaves for rapid uptake. Bypasses root zone entirely.
advanced_techniques.name: Foliar Feeding

advanced_techniques.description: Strengthens cell walls, increases stem thickness, improves heat/drought/pest resistance.
important_notes: ALWAYS add silica to water FIRST before other nutrients (raises pH significantly)
Let silica mix for 5-10 minutes before adding base nutrients
Silica is incompatible with concentrated nutrient solutions — never mix undiluted
Discontinue in late flower (last 2-3 weeks)
advanced_techniques.name: Silica (Potassium Silicate)

advanced_techniques.description: Introduces beneficial bacteria/fungi to the root zone to outcompete pathogens, improve nutrient uptake, and protect against root diseases.
important_notes: Do NOT use hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) with beneficial microbes — it kills them
Choose either sterile (H2O2) OR beneficial (Hydroguard) approach — not both
Beneficials need warmish water (65-75°F) to thrive
Reapply after any reservoir change or system cleaning
advanced_techniques.name: Beneficial Microbes & Root Inoculants

products.name: Hydroguard
products.notes: The standard for DWC/RDWC. Add at every res change.

products.dosing: 1 tsp/gal (water-in) or dust roots at transplant
products.name: Great White
products.notes: Best for soil/coco. Less effective in pure hydro.

products.name: Recharge
products.notes: Feeds existing soil microbes. Great for living soil.

products.name: Mammoth P
products.notes: Most effective in flower when P demand is highest.

advanced_techniques.description: Removing select fan leaves to improve light penetration, airflow, and direct energy to bud sites.
advanced_techniques.name: Defoliation (Strategic Leaf Removal)
rules: Never remove more than 20-30% of leaves at once (except schwazze technique)
Only defoliate healthy, vigorous plants — never stressed ones
Autoflowers: very light defoliation only — they can't recover from heavy removal
Always leave the top 2-3 fan leaves on each branch (they feed the bud below)

timing.method: Remove lower leaves that get no light (lollipop)
Major defoliation day 1 and day 21 of flower (schwazzing)
Selective tuck or remove leaves blocking bud sites

cross_contamination_protocol.overview: RDWC's shared water means any pathogen at one site reaches all sites within one pump cycle (minutes). Early detection and immediate isolation are critical.
prevention_checklist: Hydroguard at every reservoir change AND every top-off
Water temp below 70°F at all times (ideally 65-68°F)
Ball valves on every site (ability to isolate in seconds)
Inspect roots at every reservoir change
Inline filter on return line to catch debris before pump
Clean plumbing at every reservoir change
Sanitize hands/tools between touching different site's roots

quarantine_procedure.details: Brown/slimy roots, foul smell, wilting plant at one site while others look healthy.
Close ball valves on the affected site's supply AND return lines. This disconnects it from the loop immediately.
Is infection at one site only, or has it spread? Check roots at ALL other sites for any discoloration.
If caught early (1 site only): add Hydroguard at 3ml/gal (1.5x normal dose) to central reservoir. Drop water temp to 65°F.
If multiple sites affected: H2O2 shock (3ml/gal of 3% H2O2). This kills ALL bacteria (good and bad). Full reservoir change after 24h, then re-inoculate with Hydroguard.
Isolated site: either remove the plant entirely, or treat individually with H2O2 root dip and keep isolated for 48h before reconnecting.
Daily root inspections for 14 days after incident. Any recurrence → remove the affected plant entirely.

system_flush_procedure.action: Remove all organic debris from buckets and lines
Fill system with H2O2 solution (5ml/gal of 3%) and run pump for 30 minutes
Rinse with plain water — fill and drain 2x
Refill with fresh nutrient solution + Hydroguard at 3ml/gal
Monitor pH/EC every 4 hours for 48h to confirm stability

uv_sterilization.description: Inline UV sterilizer on return line kills pathogens before water returns to reservoir.
uv_sterilization.maintenance: Replace UV bulb every 6 months. Clean quartz sleeve monthly.
uv_sterilization.note: UV also kills beneficial bacteria — if using Hydroguard, add it directly to each site bucket instead of reservoir when UV is inline.
uv_sterilization.placement: On return line, AFTER inline filter, BEFORE central reservoir.
uv_sterilization.sizing_rule: 25-40 watts per 100 gallons of system volume.

equipment.description: The heart of RDWC. Where you mix nutrients, adjust pH/EC, and manage the entire system. Larger = more stable. Food-safe, opaque, dark-colored.
equipment.name: Central Control Reservoir (20-50 gal)

equipment.description: One per plant. Connected to central res via supply and return plumbing. 5-gal minimum, 10-gal preferred.
equipment.name: Site Buckets (5-10 gal each)

equipment.description: Pumps from central res to site buckets. Size at 2x total flow demand. Example: 4 sites × 100 GPH = 400 GPH demand → use 800+ GPH pump.
equipment.name: Water Pump (submersible)

equipment.description: Supply lines (pump → sites) and return lines (sites → central res). Use opaque black tubing. PVC is more durable. Vinyl is easier to install.
equipment.name: Plumbing (PVC or vinyl tubing)

equipment.description: Waterproof connections through bucket walls. Bulkheads are more reliable. Uniseals are cheaper. Must be leak-proof.
equipment.name: Bulkhead Fittings or Uniseals

equipment.description: Control flow to each site individually. Essential for balancing flow and isolating sick sites.
equipment.name: Ball Valves (per site)

equipment.description: One per site bucket. Holds media and plant.
equipment.name: Net Pots (6 inch)

equipment.description: Air stone in central res AND each site bucket. DO levels critical across entire system. Size air pump for total stone count.
equipment.name: Air Pump + Air Stones

equipment.description: Fill net pots. Rinse thoroughly.
equipment.name: Hydroton / Clay Pebbles

equipment.description: Measure at central reservoir. Apera or BlueLab recommended.
equipment.name: pH Pen

equipment.description: Measure at central reservoir.
equipment.name: EC/TDS Meter

equipment.description: Adjust at central reservoir only.
equipment.name: pH Up & pH Down

equipment.description: Mix in central reservoir. Recirculation distributes to all sites.
equipment.name: Nutrients (GH Flora Trio)

equipment.description: Add to central reservoir. Even more critical than single DWC — root rot spreads to all sites.
equipment.name: Hydroguard

equipment.description: Size for total canopy area (all sites combined). Target 30-40W/sqft for flower.
equipment.name: Grow Light

equipment.description: Reliable timer for 18/6 → 12/12 schedule.
equipment.name: Light Timer

equipment.description: Monitor grow room conditions.
equipment.name: Thermometer / Hygrometer

equipment.description: Controls temp for entire system from central res. Size for total system volume. Almost essential for systems >4 sites.
equipment.name: Water Chiller

equipment.description: Float valve in central res connected to a plain-water reservoir. RDWC systems consume enormous volumes — manual top-off is tedious with 4+ sites.
equipment.name: Auto-Top-Off System

equipment.description: Install on return lines to catch root fragments and debris before they reach the pump.
equipment.name: Inline Screen Filters

equipment.description: Pump failure = dead plants within hours. Keep a spare pump ready.
equipment.name: Backup Pump

equipment.description: Essential with RO water. Add to central reservoir.
equipment.name: CalMag Supplement

equipment.description: Strengthens stems to support heavy RDWC growth.
equipment.name: Silica Supplement

equipment.description: Trellis net spanning all sites for even canopy.
equipment.name: SCROG Net

equipment.description: Air movement between sites prevents humidity pockets.
equipment.name: Oscillating Fans

equipment.description: Odor and humidity control. Size for grow room.
equipment.name: Exhaust Fan + Carbon Filter

equipment.description: Automatically maintains pH and EC in central reservoir. Expensive but saves daily manual adjustment on large systems.
equipment.name: pH/EC Controller (auto-dosing)

equipment.description: Alerts when central reservoir is low. Prevents pump running dry.
equipment.name: Water Level Sensor

equipment.description: Keeps pump and air running during power outages. RDWC plants die faster than DWC without circulation.
equipment.name: UPS/Battery Backup

equipment.description: Monitor DO across the system. Target 6+ ppm.
equipment.name: Dissolved Oxygen Meter

equipment.description: Mesh screens at bucket drain ports to prevent roots entering plumbing.
equipment.name: Root Guards/Screens

immediate_response: Switch to backup pump immediately
If no backup: add air stones to every site bucket (oxygenation buys time)
Lower room temperature to slow metabolism
failure_modes.prevention: Keep a tested backup pump ready. Run pump on UPS. Install pre-filter screen.
root_cause_fix: Replace pump if motor is burned
Install inline pre-filter to prevent future clog
failure_modes.time_to_damage: 2-6 hours (depending on air stones and water temp)

detection: Plant wilting at one site only
immediate_response: Close ball valve for affected site
Manually top off that site bucket with reservoir water
Clear the clog: disconnect line, flush with pressurized water
failure_modes.prevention: Root guard screens at every drain. Clean lines at every res change. Regular flow-rate inspections.
root_cause_fix: Root fragments blocking line — install root guard screen at drain port
Mineral deposit — soak line in pH-down solution or vinegar
Kink in flexible tubing — replace with rigid PVC
failure_modes.time_to_damage: 6-12 hours (site has its own air stone as buffer)

detection: Water level dropping in reservoir without explanation
immediate_response: Shut off circulation pump to stop flow to the leak
Identify leak location
Temporary fix: plumber's tape, pipe clamp, or bucket under drip
Permanent fix: drain section, replace fitting/gasket
failure_modes.prevention: Leak test before every grow. Water sensors on floor. Inspect fittings monthly. Use drip trays.
root_cause_fix: Gasket degradation — replace with EPDM gaskets rated for nutrient solution
Bucket wall cracked — replace bucket entirely
Vibration loosened connection — add pipe clamps and anti-vibration mounts
failure_modes.time_to_damage: Varies: slow leak loses reservoir overnight, burst fitting is immediate

immediate_response: UPS/battery kicks in (if installed) — keeps pump + air running for 1-4 hours
If no UPS: manually agitate water in each bucket (pour back and forth)
Open tent/room for airflow — reduce heat buildup without exhaust fans
Do NOT open reservoir to 'aerate' — splash introduces pathogens
failure_modes.prevention: UPS for pump + air pump. Generator for extended outages. Notification alert on power loss.
root_cause_fix: Install UPS rated for pump + air pump wattage × 4 hours
Consider generator for grows in areas with frequent outages
Wire critical equipment (pump, air) to dedicated UPS circuit
failure_modes.time_to_damage: 4-8 hours without circulation/aeration (faster in warm water)

detection: Water level in one site rising above normal
failure_modes.failure: Return line overflow (site bucket fills faster than it drains)
immediate_response: Reduce flow to that site via ball valve
Check return line for clog or kink
Clear obstruction from return fitting inside bucket (often root mass)
failure_modes.prevention: Overflow drain in every bucket (safety outlet 2" above normal level). Root guards on all return ports.
root_cause_fix: Return pipe too small for flow rate — upsize
Root mass blocking return port — install root guard mesh
Return line has uphill section creating air lock — re-route for continuous downhill grade
failure_modes.time_to_damage: Minutes to overflow, then continuous water on floor

dead_zone_prevention: Use 45° elbows instead of 90° where possible — reduces turbulence dead spots
Aim supply inlet at far wall of bucket to create circular flow
Return drain should be on opposite side of supply inlet
Add a small air stone directly under the net pot to agitate dead zones
Periodically check under net pots for stagnant buildup

flow_monitoring.inline_flow_meter: Install on each site's supply line. Compare readings to detect partial clogs early.
flow_monitoring.level_check: All site buckets should be within 0.5 inch of each other. Uneven levels = flow imbalance.
flow_monitoring.temperature_uniformity: All sites should read within 2°F of each other. Outlier = poor circulation at that site.
flow_monitoring.visual_indicators: Ripples on water surface at each site should be visible. No ripples = flow issue.

0.5_inch.notes: Only for drip-feed supplemental lines. Not suitable for main supply.

2.0_inch.notes: Commercial. 30+ sites or high-flow current culture.

flush.notes: Maximum flow to flush nutrients from root zone.

late_veg.notes: Strong flow to support rapid growth.

mid_flower.notes: Roots dense — flow may need rebalancing.

cons: First site gets most flow, last site gets least
Any clog stops all downstream sites
daisy_chain.description: Water flows from pump → site 1 → site 2 → ... → return to reservoir.
daisy_chain.recommended_for: Only for 2-3 site builds where simplicity is paramount. Not recommended.

manifold.description: Central pump feeds a T/cross manifold splitting equally to all sites.
manifold.recommended_for: Systems with 4+ sites. Best overall approach.

flush_timing.coco: 7-14 days reduced nutrients → plain water last 3-5 days
flush_timing.notes: Flushing is debated — some studies show no difference. At minimum, reduce nutrients in final week.
flush_timing.soil: 14-21 days plain water (soil holds nutrients longer)

other_harvest_indicators: Most pistils (hairs) have darkened and curled in
Calyxes are swollen and dense
Fan leaves are yellowing and falling off naturally
Trichome stalks are visible and heads are enlarged
Breeder's recommended flowering time has passed

trichome_ratios.notes: Harvest early for more THC-dominant, less CBN. Energetic, racy high. Slight reduction in yield.
Standard harvest window. Mix of THC and CBN. Full-spectrum effects.
Extended harvest for max CBN conversion. Heavy body high. Good for pain/sleep. Slightly degraded THC.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Micro → Gro → Bloom (always Micro first)
nutrient_brands.name: General Hydroponics Flora Trio

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Part A → Epsom → Part B (dissolve each fully before adding next)
nutrient_brands.name: Jack's 321
nutrient_brands.notes: Jack's 321 is 3.6g Part A, 2.4g Cal Nit, 1.2g Epsom per gallon at full strength. Extremely cost-effective for large grows.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Core → Bloom
nutrient_brands.name: Athena Pro Line
nutrient_brands.notes: Commercial-grade. Simple 2-part with integrated CalMag. Popular in commercial cannabis facilities.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: A → B (separate for veg and bloom)
nutrient_brands.name: Advanced Nutrients pH Perfect (Sensi)
nutrient_brands.notes: Self-adjusting pH technology (claims no pH adjustment needed). Expensive but popular for beginners who struggle with pH.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Shake VERY well before use (thick concentrate settles). One bottle at a time.
nutrient_brands.name: General Hydroponics FloraNova
nutrient_brands.notes: Simplest option — one bottle per phase. Great for beginners. Shake well — concentrate separates.
nutrient_brands.type: 1-part (two bottles — one for veg, one for bloom)

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Dissolve powder in warm water, stir well
nutrient_brands.name: Greenleaf Nutrients Mega Crop
nutrient_brands.notes: One powder does everything — includes CalMag. Incredibly cost-effective at ~$0.02/gal. Community favorite on r/microgrowery.

bulkhead.description: Two-piece fitting that clamps through a drilled hole with a rubber gasket.
bulkhead.recommended_for: All permanent installations. Use PVC bulkheads for 1"+ connections.

cons: Can leak if hole is off-size
uniseal.description: Flexible rubber grommet that seals around pipe pushed through a drilled hole.
uniseal.recommended_for: Budget builds, temporary setups, or sites under 1" pipe diameter.

current_culture.best_for: Medium-large systems (6-50+ sites), commercial, expandable setups
current_culture.description: Pump-driven circulation — water flows continuously through interconnected buckets in a loop. All buckets at same height.

hybrid_manifold.best_for: Any size — recommended default for new builds
hybrid_manifold.description: Central pump feeds a manifold splitting to individual site lines. Each site returns independently to reservoir.
pros: Individual site control via ball valves

waterfall.best_for: Small-medium systems (2-8 sites), hobbyists, limited space
waterfall.description: Gravity-fed return — site buckets sit higher than central reservoir. Water flows from pump to sites, drains back by gravity.

overflow_protection.air_gap_overflow: Install an overflow drain 1-2 inches above normal water line in each site bucket. Routes to a catch basin or floor drain.
overflow_protection.alarm_system: Water leak sensors on floor under each bucket. Triggers alert via notification system.
overflow_protection.float_valve_shutoff: Float valve in central reservoir cuts pump power if water drops below minimum (prevents pump dry-run).

pipe_sizing_by_site_count.notes: 3/4" supply is adequate for small systems. 1" return ensures gravity drain keeps up.
Step up to 1" supply. 1.5" return handles higher volume.
1.25" supply with manifold. 2" return is almost mandatory.
1.5" main supply trunk splitting to 1" branches per site. Dual return lines recommended.
Commercial scale. 2" main supply manifold, zone-based return trunks, dedicated pumps per zone.

at_every_reservoir_change.details: Run plain pH'd water through entire system for 15 minutes before refilling with nutrient solution.
Check all bulkheads and connections for seepage. Tighten or reseal as needed.
Remove pump, clear debris from intake screen. Check impeller for mineral buildup.
Check screens at bucket drain ports. Trim any roots growing into screens.

between_grows.details: Fill with H2O2 solution (5ml/gal) and run 1 hour. Drain and rinse 3x with plain water.
All rubber gaskets in bulkheads. They degrade from nutrient exposure. Cheap insurance.
Look for discoloration, stiffening, or mineral deposits inside. Replace any degraded sections.
After 2 grows, inspect impeller for wear. Consider replacing pump every 3-4 grows proactively.
Fill with plain water and run 24 hours before adding plants. Mark water level — any drop = leak.

monthly.details: Run diluted H2O2 (3ml/gal of 3%) through lines for 30 min to clear biofilm. Rinse twice after.
Open and close every ball valve fully. Prevents seizure from mineral deposits.
Re-check all threaded connections. Thermal cycling and vibration can loosen over time.
Mineral-clogged air stones produce fewer/larger bubbles. Replace if output visually reduced.

steps: Drain ALL water from lines, reservoir, and site buckets
Blow compressed air through all lines to clear remaining water
Disconnect and store pump indoors (freezing kills submersible pumps)
Cap all open fittings to prevent debris/insects entering
Store flexible tubing indoors (cold makes vinyl brittle)
Inspect and replace any UV-degraded outdoor tubing in spring

weekly.details: Verify ripples visible at all site buckets. Compare water levels across sites (should be within 0.5 inch).
Remove and rinse pre-filter screens on return lines. Root fragments and debris accumulate fast in flower.

environment.container: Glass mason jars (quart size). Fill 75% full.

humidity_packs.boveda_58: For drier preference or long-term storage.
humidity_packs.boveda_62: Maintains 62% RH. Set-and-forget. Replace when crispy.
humidity_packs.grove_bags: Heat-sealed bags with built-in humidity regulation. No burping needed.

indicators_of_good_cure: Smooth, flavorful smoke — no harshness or hay smell
Buds are slightly sticky but not wet
Strong terpene aroma when jar is opened
Ash burns to white/light gray (not black)
method: Trim buds to final shape (dry trim or finish wet trim)
Place in mason jars — fill 3/4 full to allow air exchange
Week 1: burp 2-3x daily for 10-15 minutes (release moisture and gases)
Week 2-4: burp 1x daily for 5-10 minutes
Month 2+: burp 1x weekly or seal completely
Cure for minimum 2 weeks, ideal is 4-8 weeks. Some strains improve for 6+ months.

common_mistakes: Drying too fast (>3 days) — harsh smoke, hay smell, terpene loss
Drying too slow (>14 days) — mold risk, diminishing returns
Fan blowing directly on buds — uneven drying, crispy exterior/moist interior
Temperature too high (>70°F) — terpene evaporation, harsh taste

environment.airflow: Gentle circulation — fan NOT pointed at buds. Just keep air moving.

method: Cut branches 12-18 inches long
Optionally remove large fan leaves (wet trim) or leave intact (dry trim)
Hang upside down on line/rack with 6+ inches between branches
Small branches should snap (not bend) when done — 5-7 days minimum
Stems should snap cleanly; if they bend, keep drying

long_term_storage.degradation: THC slowly converts to CBN over time. Light, heat, air, and moisture accelerate degradation.
long_term_storage.method: Sealed mason jars or vacuum-sealed bags with Boveda 58% in dark, cool place
long_term_storage.notes: Properly cured and stored cannabis can remain excellent for 12+ months. Freezing is debated — can damage trichomes if not done carefully.

quick_reference.flow_rate_guide: 100-200 GPH per site in seedling/veg, 300-500 GPH per site in flower. Pump should be 2x total demand.
golden_rules: ALL adjustments at the central reservoir — never dose individual sites
Flow balance is everything — check site bucket levels regularly
Root rot at one site threatens ALL sites — inspect every site at every res change
Pump runs 24/7 — pump failure kills plants faster than DWC (no individual air stones as backup)
Air stones in central res AND every site bucket
Keep a backup pump ready at all times
Leak-test the entire system before every grow
Ball valves on every site line — you must be able to isolate any site
Auto-top-off is nearly mandatory for systems with 4+ sites
Clean plumbing at every reservoir change — bloom nutrients deposit heavily
quick_reference.hydroguard_dose: 2 ml/gal at every reservoir change AND every top-off — add to central reservoir
quick_reference.reservoir_change_schedule: Every 7 days in veg, every 5 days in flower, every 3 days during flush
quick_reference.top_off_rule: EC rising = top off with plain pH'd water. EC dropping = top off with 1/4 strength nutrient water. Always add Hydroguard to top-off water.

rdwc_system_tiers.automation_level: Manual pH/EC. Timer-based light. Manual top-off.
rdwc_system_tiers.chiller: Optional — 1/10 HP if room temp exceeds 75°F
rdwc_system_tiers.label: Hobby Small (2-4 sites)
rdwc_system_tiers.notes: Great starting point. Master plumbing and flow balance before scaling up.

rdwc_system_tiers.air_pump_recommendation: 35-50 LPM pump or dual smaller pumps
rdwc_system_tiers.automation_level: Auto-top-off recommended. pH controller optional. Smart plugs for monitoring.
rdwc_system_tiers.label: Hobby Medium (5-8 sites)
rdwc_system_tiers.notes: The sweet spot for serious hobbyists. Auto-top-off becomes nearly mandatory at this scale.
rdwc_system_tiers.plumbing: 1" supply trunk, 1.5" return, PVC recommended over vinyl

rdwc_system_tiers.air_pump_recommendation: 60-80 LPM commercial pump or regenerative blower
rdwc_system_tiers.automation_level: Auto-top-off, pH/EC controller, environmental automation. Monitoring alerts essential.
rdwc_system_tiers.label: Enthusiast (9-16 sites)
rdwc_system_tiers.notes: Dedicated grow room required. Consider zone-based plumbing (2 zones of 8). Backup pump is mandatory.
rdwc_system_tiers.plumbing: 1.25" supply manifold, 2" return trunks, all PVC, zone valves
rdwc_system_tiers.pump_recommendation: 1200-2000 GPH inline pump or dual submersibles

rdwc_system_tiers.automation_level: Full automation: pH/EC dosing, auto-top-off, environmental control, monitoring + alerts, data logging.
rdwc_system_tiers.chiller: Required — 1/2 to 1 HP or HVAC integration
rdwc_system_tiers.label: Commercial Small (17-30 sites)
rdwc_system_tiers.notes: Requires commercial space, proper drainage, backup power. Multiple zones allow partial harvest/clean.
rdwc_system_tiers.plumbing: 1.5" supply trunk, 2" return, zone-based with isolation valves per zone
rdwc_system_tiers.pump_recommendation: Commercial inline pump (3000+ GPH) or zone-split with dedicated pumps

rdwc_system_tiers.air_pump_recommendation: Multiple regenerative blowers or centralized compressed air system
rdwc_system_tiers.automation_level: Enterprise automation: SCADA-like monitoring, automated dosing, predictive alerts, compliance logging, seed-to-sale integration.
rdwc_system_tiers.chiller: Dedicated HVAC loop or industrial water chiller (1+ HP per 200 gal)
rdwc_system_tiers.label: Commercial Large (31-50+ sites)
rdwc_system_tiers.notes: Requires dedicated facility, licensed cultivation. Zone isolation mandatory for pathogen control. Redundancy on all critical systems.
rdwc_system_tiers.plumbing: 2"+ PVC trunk lines, zone manifolds, automated valve control, flow meters on each zone
rdwc_system_tiers.pump_recommendation: Multiple zone pumps or centralized commercial pump with manifold distribution

change_schedule.top_off: Daily with plain pH'd water when EC rises. With dilute nutrient when EC drops.
change_schedule.top_off_rule: EC rising = plants drinking more water than nutrients → top off with plain water. EC dropping = plants eating more nutrients than water → top off with dilute nutrient mix.

reservoir_management.description: Reservoir management for active hydroponic systems (DWC, RDWC, NFT, Ebb & Flow, Drip, Aeroponics).

dissolved_oxygen.why_important: Roots need oxygen to function. Low DO → root rot, nutrient lockout, slow growth.

sizing_guidelines.notes: Larger reservoirs = more stable pH/EC, less frequent changes, more forgiving of mistakes.

beneficial.cons: Slightly cloudy water (normal). Doesn't work above 75°F water.
beneficial.pros: Continuous protection. Outcompetes pathogens. Set-and-forget per res change.

sterile_vs_beneficial.recommendation: Beneficial approach (Hydroguard) for most growers. Sterile only if you have persistent issues.

sterile.cons: Must reapply every 2-3 days. Kills beneficial microbes too. Degrades in hours.
sterile.pros: Kills all pathogens. Clean roots. No biofilm.

water_temperature.too_cold_risk: Slowed metabolism, nutrient lockout, stunted growth
water_temperature.too_warm_risk: Root rot, low dissolved oxygen, pathogen growth

scale_tiers.description: First-time grower or single-plant hobbyist. Manual management, learning-focused.
scale_tiers.name: Solo / Single Plant
scale_tiers.notes: Hands-on learning experience. Manual checks 1-2x daily. Ideal for beginners.

scale_tiers.description: Hobbyist tent grow. Light automation helpful but not required.
scale_tiers.name: Small Tent (2-8 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Timer-controlled lights, humidity controller. Sensor monitoring recommended.

scale_tiers.description: Dedicated grower with multiple tents/rooms. Automation saves significant time.
scale_tiers.name: Multi-Tent (9-24 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Multiple environments to manage. Automated alerts essential. Consider perpetual harvest.

scale_tiers.description: Licensed commercial operation. Full automation, compliance tracking, batch management.
scale_tiers.name: Commercial Room (25-100 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: SOPs required. Staff training. Regulatory compliance. Insurance requirements.

scale_tiers.description: Large-scale production facility. Enterprise automation, SCADA integration, dedicated staff.
scale_tiers.name: Warehouse Scale (100+ plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Multiple rooms, dedicated HVAC, industrial nutrient dosing, video surveillance.

common_problems.cause: Too cold, too dry, or bad seed
common_problems.solution: Ensure 75-80°F on heat mat. Keep Rapid Rooter moist. Try a different seed after 7 days.

common_problems.cause: Improper seal, cross-threaded fittings
common_problems.solution: Use Teflon tape on threaded connections. Uniseal or bulkhead fittings must be hand-tight plus 1/4 turn. Test with plain water first.

common_problems.cause: Air locks in lines, different line lengths, pump too weak
common_problems.solution: Bleed air from lines. Use ball valves to balance flow. Ensure pump GPH exceeds total system demand by 2x.

stages.description: Seed cracks open and taproot emerges. Use Rapid Rooters or paper towel method. RDWC system is not used yet — germinate separately, transplant to net pots once seedling is established.

environment.notes: Keep seeds in darkness on a heat mat at 78°F. The RDWC system should be assembled, leak-tested, and sterilized during this stage.

environment_overrides.notes: Germinate inside greenhouse on heat mat. Test system plumbing while seeds germinate.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse provides good shelter for RDWC plumbing.

environment_overrides.notes: Germinate indoors regardless. RDWC plumbing must be UV-resistant for outdoor use. Test all connections under full sun exposure.

extra_tasks.description: Ensure all tubing, fittings, and exposed pipes are UV-resistant or wrapped in reflective insulation. UV degrades standard vinyl tubing.
extra_tasks.name: UV-proof plumbing check

outdoor.notes: Always germinate indoors. Outdoor RDWC plumbing requires UV-rated materials.

health_checks: Is the taproot visible and white?
Is the RDWC system leak-free after 24-hour test?
Are flow rates even across all site buckets?
stages.name: Germination

nutrients.approach: None — seeds contain all energy needed to germinate.

reservoir.notes: No reservoir running yet. Use this time to leak-test plumbing, calibrate flow rates, and verify even distribution to all sites.

tasks.description: Place seeds in cup of room-temp water for 12-24 hours. Transfer to Rapid Rooter once they sink.
tasks.name: Soak seeds

tasks.description: After 24-72 hours, look for white taproot. Transfer to net pot once taproot is 0.5-1 inch.
tasks.name: Check for taproot

tasks.description: Fill system with plain water. Run pump for 24 hours. Check every connection, bulkhead, and return line for leaks. Fix before adding nutrients.
tasks.name: Leak-test RDWC system

tasks.description: Measure flow to each site bucket. All sites must receive equal flow. Adjust ball valves to balance. Uneven flow = uneven pH/EC.
tasks.name: Calibrate flow rates

tasks.description: Run H2O2 (3ml/gal of 3%) through entire system for 4 hours. Drain and rinse. This kills any bacteria from previous grows.
tasks.name: Sterilize system

transition_signals: Seed shell cracked and cotyledons emerging

common_problems.cause: Flow imbalance — some sites getting more/less nutrient solution
common_problems.solution: Check and balance flow rates with ball valves. Verify all return lines are flowing freely.

common_problems.cause: Site buckets filling to capacity, evaporation, or a slow leak
common_problems.solution: Check for leaks at all connections. Verify float valves are working. Top off regularly.

common_problems.cause: Water level too low in those site buckets
common_problems.issue: Roots not reaching water in some sites
common_problems.solution: Adjust site bucket water level. Top-feed those seedlings with a turkey baster 2-3x daily until roots reach water.

common_problems.cause: Pump clogged, power outage, or undersized pump
common_problems.solution: Clean pump intake. Use a backup pump. Size pump at 2x total flow demand. Consider a battery backup for power outages.

stages.description: First true leaves develop. Place seedlings in net pots in RDWC site buckets. Roots must reach the recirculating water. Keep nutrients very light — the large system volume makes it easy to overfeed seedlings.

environment.notes: Keep light 24-30 inches above canopy. RDWC advantage: large water volume = very stable pH/EC for delicate seedlings.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse light is naturally diffused. Good for seedlings. Monitor pump and res temps — greenhouses warm water fast.

extra_tasks.description: Open vents above 80°F. Seedlings and water both suffer in overheated greenhouses.
extra_tasks.name: Monitor greenhouse temp

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse RDWC benefits from the shelter but watch water temps.

environment_overrides.notes: Keep seedlings in partial shade outdoors. RDWC plumbing must be insulated from sun to prevent algae and heating.

extra_problems.cause: Light penetrating clear/translucent tubing
extra_problems.solution: Replace with black opaque tubing or wrap in light-proof insulation.

extra_tasks.description: Wrap all exposed tubing in reflective insulation. Sunlight on plumbing = algae growth + water heating.
extra_tasks.name: Insulate plumbing

extra_tasks.description: 50% shade cloth over seedlings for first 7-10 days outdoors.
extra_tasks.name: Shade seedlings

outdoor.notes: Outdoor RDWC requires all plumbing to be opaque and insulated.

health_checks: Is water level equal across all site buckets?
Is the pump running and recirculating?
Are roots growing toward the water in each site?
stages.name: Seedling

nutrients.approach: 1/4 strength in central reservoir. The large total volume means each seedling gets consistent, dilute nutrition. Mix in central reservoir only — never add nutrients to individual site buckets.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Beneficial bacteria. Add to central reservoir — it distributes to all sites via recirculation.

reservoir.notes: Run pump 24/7. Water level in site buckets should touch bottom of net pots. Adjust float valves if installed. Monitor central reservoir level — it drops as site buckets fill.
reservoir.total_system_volume_gal: Calculate: central res + all site buckets + plumbing volume

tasks.description: Test pH at the central control reservoir. This is where you measure and adjust — not at individual sites. Target 5.5-6.0.
tasks.name: Check pH at central reservoir

tasks.description: Measure EC at central res. RDWC advantage: one measurement covers all sites if flow is balanced.
tasks.name: Check EC at central reservoir

tasks.description: Check that water level is equal in all site buckets. Uneven levels = flow imbalance. Adjust ball valves.
tasks.name: Verify even flow

tasks.description: Look through net pot holes for white roots growing down. Top-feed seedlings whose roots haven't reached the water yet.
tasks.name: Check for root progress

tasks.description: Top off central reservoir with plain pH'd water as needed. Evaporation + plant uptake drops the level.
tasks.name: Monitor central res level

tasks.description: Measure at central reservoir. One chiller controls temp for entire system. Target 65-72°F.
tasks.name: Check water temp

transition_signals: Roots dangling into water in all site buckets
Vigorous daily growth across all sites

common_problems.cause: Reduced flow to that site, clogged line, or unhealthy roots at that site
common_problems.issue: One site growing slower than others
common_problems.solution: Check flow rate to slow site. Inspect return line for clogs. Check roots at that site for early rot.

common_problems.cause: RDWC's shared water means pathogens spread to all sites
common_problems.issue: Root rot at one site spreading to others
common_problems.solution: This is the #1 RDWC risk. Isolate sick plant if possible (cap its lines). Dose Hydroguard heavily. Consider H2O2 emergency treatment for entire system. Remove dead root material.

common_problems.cause: Root fragments, hydroton dust, or biofilm buildup
common_problems.solution: Flush line with pressurized water. Install inline screen filters on return lines. Clean at every reservoir change.

common_problems.cause: Pump impeller clogged, air lock, or pump wearing out
common_problems.solution: Clean pump intake and impeller. Bleed air from lines. Keep a backup pump ready.

stages.description: Rapid leaf and root growth across all sites. Begin training. RDWC advantage: uniform conditions across sites produces consistent canopy height — critical for even light distribution.

environment.notes: Lower light to 18-24 inches. All sites should be growing at similar rates if flow is balanced. Uneven growth = plumbing issue.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse RDWC grows fast in early veg. Ventilate to prevent overheating. Central reservoir should be shaded within the greenhouse.

extra_tasks.description: Open vents above 80°F. RDWC plants grow aggressively — they transpire heavily, raising humidity.
extra_tasks.name: Ventilate greenhouse

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse RDWC: manage humidity from heavy transpiration of multiple plants.

environment_overrides.notes: Full sun now. Insulate all plumbing. Central reservoir must be in shade or insulated — it's the thermal mass for the whole system.

extra_problems.cause: Direct sun on reservoir, hot ambient temps
extra_problems.solution: Move res to shade. Insulate with Reflectix. Add chiller if chronic. Res temp controls the whole system.

extra_tasks.description: Central res must be in complete shade or heavily insulated. It controls temp for all sites.
extra_tasks.name: Shade central reservoir

extra_tasks.description: Check every plant for pests. Outdoor RDWC sites may have uneven pest pressure — wind-facing sites get hit first.
extra_tasks.name: Pest scouting all sites

outdoor.notes: Outdoor RDWC: the central reservoir is the thermal anchor. Keep it cool and shaded.

health_checks: Are all sites growing at similar rates?
Are roots white and healthy at every site?
Is flow even across all sites?
Is the central reservoir level stable?
Any signs of nutrient deficiency at any site?
stages.name: Early Vegetative

nutrients.approach: Half-strength. Mix in central reservoir only. RDWC distributes evenly to all sites. More nitrogen (Gro) for vegetative growth.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Add to central reservoir. Recirculation distributes to all sites.

supplements.name: Silica (Armor Si)
supplements.purpose: Strengthens stems. Add before other nutrients. Especially important in RDWC — strong stems handle the vigorous growth.

reservoir.notes: Weekly full system changes. Drain from central reservoir, flush sites, refill. Large volume means more nutrient cost but much more stability. Monitor consumption rate — RDWC systems drink fast with multiple plants.

tasks.description: Daily pH check at central reservoir. RDWC's large volume drifts slower than single-bucket DWC.
tasks.name: Check pH at central res

tasks.description: Monitor EC. EC rising = top off with plain water. EC dropping = plants are hungry.
tasks.name: Check EC at central res

tasks.description: Drain central res and all site buckets. Flush lines with plain water. Refill with fresh nutrient solution. Add Hydroguard.
tasks.name: Full system reservoir change

tasks.description: Check water levels are equal across all sites. Measure flow rate at each return line if possible.
tasks.name: Verify even flow

tasks.description: Root fragments, hydroton dust, and biofilm can clog lines. Inspect intake screens and return line openings.
tasks.name: Check plumbing for debris

tasks.description: Check roots at every site bucket. One site with brown roots = cross-contamination risk to all sites.
tasks.name: Root inspection at each site

tasks.description: Once plants have 4-5 nodes, begin LST at all sites simultaneously for even canopy.
tasks.name: Begin training (LST)

training.description: Train all sites simultaneously. RDWC's uniform conditions make training easier — all plants respond similarly.
Top all plants at the same node for uniform canopy. RDWC's even nutrition supports fast recovery.

transition_signals: Plants have 5-6+ nodes at all sites
Root mass thick and white at all sites

common_problems.cause: Vigorous root growth in RDWC — roots follow water flow into plumbing
common_problems.solution: Install root guards (mesh screens) at bucket drain ports. Trim roots that intrude into lines at reservoir changes. Check weekly.

common_problems.cause: Multiple large plants consuming 5-10+ gallons/day
common_problems.solution: Upgrade central reservoir size. Install auto-top-off with float valve and a separate top-off reservoir. Fill top-off with pH'd water.

common_problems.cause: Multiple plants consuming at different rates
common_problems.solution: More frequent, smaller top-offs. Consider auto-dosing (pH and EC controllers) for large systems.

common_problems.cause: Poor flow to that site, or localized issue
common_problems.solution: CRITICAL: In RDWC this can spread to ALL sites. Increase flow. Add extra air stone to that site. Dose extra Hydroguard. If severe, isolate that site by capping its lines and treating separately.

stages.description: Maximum vegetative growth. RDWC plants grow explosively — roots interconnect through plumbing creating a massive shared root system. This is when RDWC shows its biggest advantage over single-bucket DWC: uniform, aggressive growth across all sites.

environment.notes: Plants drink heavily now — monitor central reservoir level daily. RDWC systems with 4+ sites can consume 5-10+ gallons per day in late veg.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse RDWC in late veg: humidity soars from multiple plants transpiring. Dehumidifier may be needed. Ventilate aggressively.

extra_tasks.description: Multiple RDWC plants transpire heavily. Humidity above 65% in late veg risks mold. Use exhaust fans and dehumidifier.
extra_tasks.name: Manage humidity

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse RDWC: humidity management becomes the primary challenge.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor RDWC in late veg: plants grow massive with full sun. Central res consumption is extreme — may need 10-20+ gal/day top-off. Auto-top-off is nearly mandatory.

extra_problems.cause: Outdoor heat + full sun + multiple large plants
extra_problems.solution: Install large (50+ gal) auto-top-off reservoir with float valve. Check daily.

extra_tasks.description: Hot sun degrades tubing over a full veg cycle. Inspect for soft spots, cracks, or discoloration.
extra_tasks.name: Check plumbing for heat degradation

extra_tasks.description: Large outdoor RDWC plants attract more pests. Apply preventive neem oil. Scout every 2 days.
extra_tasks.name: Pest management

outdoor.notes: Outdoor RDWC in late veg is the most water-intensive grow method. Plan for massive consumption.

health_checks: Root mass white and healthy at all sites?
Central reservoir holding level between top-offs?
Is flow balanced? (Check site bucket levels.)
Any roots growing into plumbing lines?
stages.name: Late Vegetative

nutrients.approach: 3/4 strength. Peak nitrogen phase. RDWC's large root mass absorbs nutrients efficiently. Watch EC closely — it can drop fast with multiple hungry plants.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Maintain at every change and top-off. More critical now — large root mass is more susceptible.

supplements.name: Silica (Armor Si)
supplements.purpose: Strong stems needed to support the heavy growth RDWC produces.

reservoir.notes: Water consumption accelerates dramatically. Top off central reservoir daily or multiple times daily. Consider an auto-top-off (float valve + reservoir) for large systems. Weekly full changes still critical.

tasks.description: Daily at central reservoir. Large volume drifts slowly but multiple plants can swing pH faster than single DWC.
tasks.name: Check pH

tasks.description: EC drops fast with multiple plants feeding. Top off with 1/4 strength if EC is dropping; plain water if rising.
tasks.name: Check EC

tasks.description: RDWC systems consume 5-10+ gal/day in late veg. Top off 1-2x daily. Add Hydroguard to top-off water.
tasks.name: Top off central reservoir

tasks.description: Drain, flush lines, refill. Clean any debris from site bucket screens.
tasks.name: Full system change

tasks.description: Roots may grow into return lines. Check flow at each site. Clear any root intrusions.
tasks.name: Check plumbing

tasks.description: Lift net pots and check roots at every site. One site with problems = threat to all sites.
tasks.name: Root inspection all sites

tasks.description: RDWC grows an even canopy — maintain it with continued LST/SCROG. All sites should be at similar height.
tasks.name: Canopy management

tasks.description: Remove lower growth that won't reach the canopy. Improves airflow around plumbing and bucket sites.
tasks.name: Defoliation (light)

training.description: Install a trellis net over all sites. Weave branches through as they grow. RDWC's even growth makes SCROG very effective.
Remove all growth below the SCROG net. Redirects energy to top colas.

transition_signals: Plants have been in veg 4-6+ weeks
Root mass is enormous and filling site buckets

common_problems.cause: Flow imbalance or one site has root issues
common_problems.solution: Check and balance flow rates. Inspect roots at the lagging site. May need to supercrop taller sites to level the canopy.

common_problems.cause: Stress, genetics, light leaks
common_problems.solution: CRITICAL in RDWC: pollen contaminates ALL sites. Remove the hermaphrodite immediately. Check all other plants daily. Fix light leaks.

common_problems.cause: Nutrient formula change + heavy plant activity during stretch
common_problems.solution: Normal during transition. Adjust gently. The pH will stabilize once stretch slows.

stages.description: Light flip to 12/12. Plants stretch 50-100% in height. In RDWC, all sites stretch uniformly if flow is balanced — this is the payoff of good plumbing. Shift nutrients from grow to bloom. The stretch is aggressive in RDWC due to the massive root system driving rapid growth.

environment.notes: Flip to 12/12. The stretch in RDWC is aggressive — plan canopy height accordingly. SCROG nets need to be in place before flip. Plants can gain 12-24 inches during stretch.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse may need blackout curtains for 12/12 control. Natural photoperiod + supplemental light control gives flexibility.

extra_tasks.description: If forcing 12/12 before natural photoperiod, use blackout curtains. Must be complete — any light leak causes problems.
extra_tasks.name: Deploy blackout curtains

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse RDWC: blackout curtains for light dep flowering.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor: flowering triggers naturally when daylight drops below ~14 hours. In RDWC, all sites transition together. Monitor for pests — flowering plants attract different pests than veg.

extra_tasks.description: No artificial light should reach plants during dark period. Street lights, security lights, and even moonlight can cause revegging or hermaphrodites.
extra_tasks.name: Verify dark period

outdoor.notes: Outdoor RDWC transition: natural photoperiod controls timing.

health_checks: Are all sites stretching at similar rates?
Are pistils (white hairs) appearing at the nodes?
Root mass still healthy across all sites?
Any hermaphrodite signs (pollen sacs)? Check every plant.
stages.name: Transition (Pre-Flower)

nutrients.approach: Begin transitioning from grow to bloom ratios. Reduce nitrogen, increase phosphorus and potassium. RDWC's uniform delivery ensures even transition across all sites.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Continue at every change and top-off.

reservoir.notes: Begin shifting pH target slightly higher (6.0) for phosphorus/potassium availability. Plants drink even more during the stretch. Top off frequently.

tasks.description: Switch to 12/12. RDWC plants stretch aggressively — ensure adequate headroom. The shared root system drives faster stretching than single DWC.
tasks.name: Flip light schedule

tasks.description: Daily. Shift target to 6.0 for better P/K uptake in flower.
tasks.name: Check pH

tasks.description: Measure plant height daily. RDWC stretch can be 50-100%. Adjust SCROG weaving. Supercrop any branches threatening to reach the light.
tasks.name: Monitor stretch

tasks.description: Transition to bloom ratio. Drain, flush lines, refill with new formula.
tasks.name: Full system change with bloom nutrients

tasks.description: 12/12 requires complete darkness during lights-off. Check for any light from equipment, indicators, or leaks around door/tent seams.
tasks.name: Check for light leaks

tasks.description: Remove fan leaves blocking bud sites. Open up lower canopy for airflow around plumbing.
tasks.name: Defoliation (strategic)

training.description: Pinch and bend overly tall branches. RDWC stretch is aggressive — supercropping controls height without removing growth.
Continue weaving new growth through SCROG net for even canopy.

transition_signals: Pistils visible at all bud sites

common_problems.cause: The worst RDWC scenario — pathogen in shared system during flower
common_problems.issue: Root rot detected at one site during flower
common_problems.solution: EMERGENCY: Isolate affected site if possible (cap lines). Dose heavy Hydroguard to entire system. Add hydrogen peroxide (3ml/gal of 3%) as emergency treatment — then switch back to Hydroguard 24 hours later. Full system change. Monitor all other sites hourly for 48 hours.

common_problems.cause: Humidity too high, airflow blocked, dense canopy
common_problems.solution: Remove all affected bud material (cut 2 inches below visible rot). Increase airflow. Lower humidity to 45%. Inspect all other sites — bud rot spores spread. Defoliate for airflow.

common_problems.cause: Bloom nutrients are heavier and leave more mineral deposits
common_problems.solution: Flush lines with plain pH'd water at every reservoir change. Use a pipe brush on accessible connections.

common_problems.cause: Flow imbalance, root issues at one site, or light distribution
common_problems.solution: Balance flow. Check roots. Adjust light height/angle. Rotate plants if possible.

stages.description: Bud sites form and begin developing. The RDWC advantage shines: uniform nutrient delivery produces consistent bud development across all sites. Water consumption peaks. Cross-contamination vigilance is critical — any pathogen now risks the entire harvest.

environment.notes: Lower humidity to prevent bud rot. RDWC grows dense canopies — airflow between sites is critical. Use oscillating fans pointing between plants.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse RDWC in flower: humidity control is the #1 challenge. Multiple plants transpiring heavily in an enclosed space. Dehumidifier is essential.

extra_tasks.description: Keep humidity below 55%. RDWC canopies in greenhouses trap moisture. Run dehumidifier during lights-off when humidity peaks.
extra_tasks.name: Run dehumidifier

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse RDWC in flower: dehumidifier is mandatory.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor RDWC in flower: rain is the biggest threat — water dilutes res and creates humidity. Build rain covers over sites. Central res must be covered.

extra_problems.cause: Uncovered sites overflow in heavy rain
extra_problems.solution: Emergency drain of excess water. Check EC (diluted = add nutrients). Cover all open surfaces.

extra_tasks.description: Build covers over all site buckets and central reservoir. Rain dilutes nutrients and can overflow sites. A tarp roof works — just ensure airflow.
extra_tasks.name: Rain protection

extra_tasks.description: Apply potassium bicarbonate spray weekly as PM prevention. Outdoor humidity swings are unavoidable.
extra_tasks.name: PM prevention

outdoor.notes: Outdoor RDWC in flower: rain protection is non-negotiable.

health_checks: Are buds forming at all sites?
Root health good at all sites? (Critical check.)
Any signs of bud rot or PM at any site?
stages.name: Early Flower

nutrients.approach: Full bloom strength. Heavy phosphorus and potassium for bud development. All sites receive identical nutrition from the central reservoir — this is RDWC's key advantage for consistent flower quality.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Continue religiously. Root rot during flower = catastrophic loss.

supplements.name: PK Booster (Liquid Koolbloom)
supplements.purpose: Extra phosphorus and potassium. Start low and increase. Watch for tip burn.

reservoir.notes: Increase res change frequency to every 5 days. Plants are feeding heavily. More frequent changes = cleaner root zone = better bud production. Monitor for salt buildup in plumbing connections.

tasks.description: Daily. Maintain 5.8-6.2 for optimal P/K uptake.
tasks.name: Check pH

tasks.description: Daily. Full-strength bloom nutes — watch for tip burn (EC too high) or light green new growth (too low).
tasks.name: Check EC

tasks.description: Every 5 days. Clean solution critical during flower. Flush plumbing at each change.
tasks.name: Full system change

tasks.description: Check every site for root health. Brown/slimy roots at ANY site require immediate action — the whole system is at risk.
tasks.name: Root inspection all sites

tasks.description: All sites should show similar bud development if flow is balanced. Uneven buds = check flow to that site.
tasks.name: Monitor bud development

tasks.description: Salt buildup at fittings during flower can restrict flow. Clean at reservoir changes.
tasks.name: Check plumbing connections

tasks.description: Remove leaves blocking bud sites and impeding airflow. RDWC canopies get dense — airflow prevents mold.
tasks.name: Defoliation (targeted)

training.description: Strategic removal of fan leaves. First defoliation when flower starts. Second at week 3. Improves light penetration to lower buds.

transition_signals: Buds swelling and white pistils abundant

common_problems.cause: Peak flower consumption with multiple large plants
