common_problems.solution: Install auto-top-off. Upgrade central reservoir to largest possible. Some commercial RDWC runs a 50-100 gal central res for 8+ sites.

common_problems.cause: Humidity pocket, dense bud, poor airflow at that site
common_problems.solution: Remove ALL affected material (cut 2 inches below rot). Increase airflow to that site. Check all other sites. Lower room humidity.

common_problems.cause: Heavy bloom nutrients depositing at connection points
common_problems.solution: Clean at every reservoir change. Use a pipe brush. Run hot water through lines during changes.

common_problems.cause: Salt buildup in pump, root debris in intake
common_problems.solution: Clean pump at every res change. Install pre-filter screen on intake. Consider upgrading pump for late flower demands.

stages.description: Peak bud production. Buds swell rapidly and pack on weight. RDWC's uniform nutrient delivery produces remarkably consistent bud size across all sites. Water and nutrient consumption hits maximum. This is the most resource-intensive phase.

environment.notes: Maximum light intensity. Drop humidity to 45% to prevent bud rot in dense RDWC canopies. Temperature differential (day/night) enhances terpene and color production.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: run dehumidifier 24/7 during mid flower. Greenhouse traps moisture from multiple transpiring plants.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse RDWC mid flower: dehumidification is critical.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor mid flower: protect from rain at all costs. Rain + dense buds = guaranteed bud rot. Build permanent rain covers over all sites.

extra_problems.cause: Outdoor humidity + dew on dense flower clusters
extra_problems.solution: Shake plants gently each morning. Add oscillating fans if power available. Defoliate for airflow.

extra_tasks.description: Gently shake each plant in the morning to remove dew. Moisture sitting on buds causes rot.
extra_tasks.name: Shake plants after rain/dew

outdoor.notes: Outdoor RDWC mid flower: rain and dew are the primary threats.

health_checks: Are buds swelling uniformly across all sites?
Root mass healthy and white at all sites?
Any signs of bud rot (brown/mushy patches)?
stages.name: Mid Flower (Bulk Phase)

nutrients.approach: Full bloom strength. Maximum phosphorus and potassium. RDWC delivers peak nutrition uniformly. Watch EC closely — slight tip burn is optimal (means plants are at max capacity).

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Non-negotiable during flower. Root rot now = total loss.

supplements.name: PK Booster (Liquid Koolbloom)
supplements.purpose: Peak PK supplementation for bud density. Increase to full dose.

reservoir.notes: Peak consumption. Multiple large flowering plants can drain 10-15+ gallons/day. Auto-top-off is strongly recommended. Change every 5 days — bloom nutrients leave more deposits.

tasks.description: Daily at central reservoir. Maintain 5.8-6.2.
tasks.name: Check pH

tasks.description: Daily. Slight tip burn on newest growth = perfect EC. Heavy burn = reduce 10%. No burn = can push slightly higher.
tasks.name: Check EC

tasks.description: May need 2-3x daily during peak flower. RDWC systems with 4+ flowering plants are extremely thirsty.
tasks.name: Top off central reservoir

tasks.description: Every 5 days. Flush all lines at each change. Salt buildup accelerates with heavy bloom nutrients.
tasks.name: Full system change

tasks.description: Check every site. This is the highest-risk period for root rot — warm res + heavy organics + peak oxygen demand.
tasks.name: Root inspection all sites

tasks.description: Check all bud sites at all plants for brown/gray rot. Dense RDWC canopies trap humidity. Part dense buds and look inside.
tasks.name: Bud rot inspection

tasks.description: RDWC produces heavy buds. Install plant yoyos or bamboo stakes. SCROG net helps distribute weight.
tasks.name: Support heavy branches

training.description: Final light defoliation. Remove only leaves directly blocking bud sites. Do not over-defoliate in late flower.

common_problems.cause: Slight genetic variation or light distribution difference
common_problems.issue: One site ripening faster than others
common_problems.solution: In RDWC, you can harvest individual sites at different times. Cap the harvested site's plumbing and continue running the system for remaining sites.

common_problems.cause: Light stress (too close) or heat stress
common_problems.solution: Raise light slightly. Lower room temp. Some foxtailing is genetic and harmless.

common_problems.cause: Dense mature buds + any humidity spike
common_problems.solution: Harvest immediately if rot is spreading. Better to harvest slightly early than lose buds to rot. Remove affected material before it spreads via shared system.

stages.description: Final bud maturation and trichome ripening. Reduce nutrients. Buds swell their last 10-20%. Trichomes transition from clear→cloudy→amber. In RDWC, all sites ripen at similar rates if system has been balanced.

environment.notes: Cooler temps + large day/night differential enhances color and terpene production. Some growers drop to 35% humidity. RDWC's uniform environment helps all sites ripen together.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: cool fall nights enhance colors and terpenes. Monitor for frost if greenhouse is unheated.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse RDWC: cool nights are beneficial if above freezing.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor late flower: watch for early frost. RDWC plumbing can freeze, cracking fittings. Harvest before first frost or bring system inside.

extra_problems.cause: Late-season temperature drop
extra_problems.solution: Harvest immediately or cover plants + insulate all plumbing. RDWC is especially vulnerable — water in plumbing expands when frozen.

extra_tasks.description: Frost kills plants and cracks plumbing. Harvest before first frost or protect with row covers. Have an emergency harvest plan.
extra_tasks.name: Monitor frost forecast

outdoor.notes: Outdoor RDWC: harvest timing is frost-date-dependent.

health_checks: Fan leaves yellowing naturally? (Good sign — plant using reserves)
Any bud rot? Check all sites daily.
Roots still healthy? (Almost done — keep them clean.)
stages.name: Late Flower (Ripening)

nutrients.approach: Reduced strength. Taper down nutrients. Stop PK boosters. Allow plant to begin using internal reserves. Some yellowing of fan leaves is normal and desired — the plant is redirecting energy to buds.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Continue until flush begins.

reservoir.notes: Reduce nutrient strength. Plants are finishing — they need less. Water consumption decreases slightly. Continue 5-day res changes to keep solution clean for the final stretch.

tasks.description: Use 60x loupe or digital microscope. Clear = not ready. Milky/cloudy = peak THC. Amber = more sedative. Most growers harvest at 10-20% amber.
tasks.name: Check trichomes daily

tasks.description: Daily. Reduced nutes. pH management continues.
tasks.name: Check pH/EC

tasks.description: Continue 5-day changes. Clean solution until flush.
tasks.name: Full system change

tasks.description: Dense, mature buds are most susceptible. Check all sites daily. Part large colas and inspect inside.
tasks.name: Bud rot patrol (all sites)

tasks.description: RDWC advantage: if balanced, all sites ripen together. Check the lagging site to determine harvest timing.
tasks.name: Check trichome maturity at all sites

tasks.description: Yellow/dying fan leaves are normal now. Remove them to prevent mold and improve airflow.
tasks.name: Remove dying fan leaves

common_problems.cause: Salt buildup in plumbing or site buckets not flushing completely
common_problems.solution: Drain and refill more frequently (every 2 days). Manually flush each site bucket. Run pump at higher flow.

common_problems.cause: Root function declining naturally near end of life
common_problems.solution: Normal — plant is finishing. Ensure water level is maintained. Don't add nutrients.

stages.description: Run plain pH'd water through the entire RDWC system. No nutrients. This allows plants to use remaining internal nutrient stores, producing cleaner-burning, better-tasting flower. RDWC advantage: the recirculating system flushes all sites simultaneously and thoroughly.

environment.notes: Maintain 12/12. Some growers reduce light slightly during flush. Continue monitoring environment — bud rot risk persists.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse flush: maintain ventilation. Dying fan leaves can harbor mold in humid conditions.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse: remove fallen leaves promptly to prevent mold.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor flush: rain actually helps flush. Let rain dilute the system during this phase. Still monitor for rot.

outdoor.notes: Outdoor: rain is acceptable during flush — it aids the flushing process.

stages.name: Flush

nutrients.approach: Zero nutrients. Plain pH'd water. The recirculating system flushes salts from all sites simultaneously — more efficient flush than single DWC.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Continue through flush. Root rot in final week wastes the entire grow.

reservoir.notes: Plain pH'd water only. Change every 3 days to flush accumulated salts. RDWC's recirculation rinses all sites continuously. Continue Hydroguard — root health matters until harvest day.

tasks.description: Drain entire system. Refill central res with plain pH'd water. No nutrients. Run pump to flush all lines and sites.
tasks.name: Drain and refill with plain water

tasks.description: Check EC of water returning from sites. EC should drop toward 0 over the flush period. If EC stays high, extend flush.
tasks.name: Monitor EC runoff

tasks.description: Plants continue maturing during flush. Check trichomes to time harvest precisely.
tasks.name: Continue trichome checks

tasks.description: Continue daily checks at all sites. Flush period is still high-risk for rot.
tasks.name: Monitor for bud rot

tasks.description: Sharp trimming scissors, drying rack/lines, mason jars, hygrometers, Boveda packs. Have everything ready before harvest day.
tasks.name: Prepare harvest equipment

common_problems.cause: Hidden rot inside dense colas
common_problems.solution: Cut at least 1 inch beyond visible rot. Discard affected material. Inspect remaining buds carefully.

common_problems.cause: Salt + root + biofilm buildup over the full grow cycle
common_problems.solution: Soak all removable parts in H2O2 solution overnight. Use pipe brushes. Replace any tubing that won't come clean.

stages.description: Cut plants, trim, and hang to dry. RDWC harvest consideration: you can harvest individual sites at different times if they ripened unevenly — cap the harvested site's plumbing lines and continue running the system for remaining sites. Drain the entire system only after the last site is harvested.

environment.notes: Some growers give 24-48 hours of darkness before harvest to boost trichome production. Cut plant at base. Wet trim or dry trim based on preference.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse can serve as drying space if humidity is manageable.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse: usable as drying space with proper ventilation.

environment_overrides.notes: Harvest before frost. Bring plants inside to trim in a clean, controlled environment.

outdoor.notes: Outdoor: harvest timing may be forced by weather.

health_checks: Were trichomes at target maturity when cut?
Any bud rot discovered during trimming? (Remove affected areas.)
Is the RDWC system fully cleaned and dried?
stages.name: Harvest

nutrients.approach: None.

reservoir.notes: Drain system after last site harvested. Run plain water + H2O2 through all lines to clean. Disassemble and clean all components.

tasks.description: 24-48 hours of darkness before harvest. Debated but many growers report stickier trichomes.
tasks.name: Optional dark period

tasks.description: Cut each plant at the base. If sites are at different maturities, harvest ready sites first — cap their plumbing and continue system for others.
tasks.name: Harvest sites

tasks.description: Wet trim: trim leaves immediately after cutting. Dry trim: hang whole plants and trim after drying. Wet trim is faster; dry trim often produces better flavor.
tasks.name: Wet trim or whole-plant hang

tasks.description: After last site is harvested: drain everything. Run H2O2 (5ml/gal of 3%) through all lines for 4 hours. Drain, rinse, disassemble, scrub all buckets. Dry completely before storage.
tasks.name: Clean RDWC system

tasks.description: Check all tubing, bulkheads, and fittings. Replace anything degraded. Root intrusions may have weakened connections.
tasks.name: Inspect plumbing for damage

common_problems.cause: Temp too high, humidity too low, fans blowing on buds
common_problems.issue: Buds drying too fast (crispy outside, wet inside)
common_problems.solution: Lower temp. Raise humidity (wet towel in room). No direct airflow on buds.

common_problems.cause: Humidity too high, not enough airflow, or bud rot hidden from grow phase
common_problems.solution: Remove moldy material immediately. Increase ventilation. Lower humidity. Inspect all remaining buds.

stages.description: Hang whole plants or branches in a dark, cool, ventilated space. Target slow dry over 10-14 days. RDWC yields tend to be larger — ensure adequate drying space for multi-site harvests.

environment.notes: Complete darkness. Gentle airflow (not blowing directly on buds). 60°F and 60% humidity is the sweet spot. Slower dry = better cure.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse is NOT ideal for drying — too much humidity fluctuation. Dry in a separate indoor space if possible.

greenhouse.notes: Avoid drying in greenhouse if possible.

environment_overrides.notes: Dry indoors in a controlled environment. Never dry outdoors.

outdoor.notes: Always dry indoors.

health_checks: Any mold or off-smell on drying buds?
Is the drying room holding 60°F / 60% RH?
stages.name: Drying

nutrients.approach: None.

reservoir.notes: System should be cleaned and stored.

tasks.description: Small stems should snap (not bend) when dry. Large stems still slightly flexible. This takes 7-14 days. Do NOT rush with fans or heat.
tasks.name: Check drying progress

tasks.description: Keep 60-65°F and 55-65% humidity. Too fast = harsh smoke. Too slow = mold risk.
tasks.name: Monitor temp and humidity

tasks.description: Inspect hanging buds daily. Dense RDWC colas are especially mold-prone during drying. Remove any affected buds immediately.
tasks.name: Check for mold

transition_signals: Outside of bud is dry to touch

common_problems.cause: Chlorophyll still breaking down. Normal in first 1-2 weeks.
common_problems.solution: Continue curing. The hay smell fades and terpenes emerge after 2-3 weeks.

common_problems.cause: Buds were jarred too wet. Anaerobic bacteria breaking down plant material.
common_problems.solution: Remove buds from jar immediately. Lay on paper bag or drying rack for 12-24 hours. Re-jar when drier.

common_problems.cause: Over-dried before jarring, or jar humidity too low
common_problems.solution: Add Boveda 62% pack. It slowly rehydrates buds to perfect humidity. Takes 3-5 days to stabilize.

stages.description: Place dried buds in mason jars. Burp daily for first 2 weeks, then weekly. Curing converts harsh chlorophyll into smooth-smoking compounds and develops terpene profiles. Minimum 2-week cure; 4-8 weeks is ideal.

environment.notes: Store jars in a cool, dark place. Boveda 62% packs maintain perfect humidity. Light degrades THC — complete darkness.

environment_overrides.notes: Do not cure in the greenhouse — temperature swings degrade quality. Cure indoors.

greenhouse.notes: Cure indoors, not in greenhouse.

environment_overrides.notes: Cure indoors in a cool, dark space. Identical process regardless of grow environment.

outdoor.notes: Curing is environment-independent.

health_checks: Buds smell improving (less hay, more terpenes)?
stages.name: Curing

nutrients.approach: None.

reservoir.notes: N/A — system cleaned and stored.

tasks.description: Open jars for 5-15 minutes. Week 1-2: daily. Week 3-4: every 2-3 days. After week 4: weekly. If ammonia smell when opening = too wet, leave lid off for 1 hour.
tasks.name: Burp jars

tasks.description: Use mini hygrometer in each jar. Target 58-65%. Above 65% = too wet (leave lid off). Below 55% = add Boveda 62% pack.
tasks.name: Check jar humidity

tasks.description: Check buds visually when burping. Any mold = remove affected buds immediately. Discard the whole jar if mold is widespread.
tasks.name: Inspect for mold

container_progression.commercial_medium: Turkey bags in sealed bins, nitrogen-flushed
container_progression.industrial: Climate-controlled vault, nitrogen generators, automated monitoring

degradation_timeline.18_plus_months: Convert to extracts, edibles, or topicals.

testing_schedule.360_days: Full panel — sell, process, or destroy decision

common_problems.cause: Heat, light, oxygen, or time
common_problems.solution: Darkness, cool temps (60°F), minimal oxygen. ~5%/year baseline.

common_problems.cause: Temps above 70°F, oxygen exposure, frequent opening
common_problems.solution: Keep below 65°F. Nitrogen-sealed containers. Minimize opening.

common_problems.cause: Humidity above 65% or improper dry/cure
common_problems.solution: Verify 58-62% RH before sealing. Remove affected material immediately.

common_problems.cause: Normal moisture equilibration (1-3% first month)
common_problems.solution: Boveda/Integra packs minimize loss. Sealed containers essential.

stages.description: Post-cure long-term storage for commercial and home operations. RDWC produces heavy yields — plan storage capacity accordingly. A 4-site RDWC system can produce 2-4+ lbs per cycle. Proper storage preserves potency and terpenes for 6-12+ months. THC degrades to CBN at ~5%/year under ideal conditions, much faster with heat, light, or oxygen exposure.

environment.notes: DARK. Cool. Stable. Zero light — UV destroys cannabinoids and terpenes. Commercial vaults: 58-62°F, 55-60% RH, complete darkness, nitrogen atmosphere.

environment_overrides.notes: Do NOT store in greenhouse.

greenhouse.notes: Climate-controlled indoor space only.

environment_overrides.notes: Storage is always indoor.

outdoor.notes: Indoor controlled environment.

stages.name: Long-Term Storage

nutrients.approach: None.

tasks.description: Home: mason jars with Boveda 58-62% packs. Commercial: nitrogen-sealed grove bags, CVault containers, or nitrogen-flushed drums. RDWC yields are high — plan container volume accordingly.
tasks.name: Transfer to long-term containers

tasks.description: Strain, harvest date, storage date, weight, batch number. Commercial: seed-to-sale tracking, FIFO rotation.
tasks.name: Label and track batches

tasks.description: Inspect for mold, off odors. Check humidity packs. Commercial: potency/terpene testing at 30/90/180 days.
tasks.name: Monthly quality checks

tasks.description: Monitor vault temp/humidity. No light leaks. Commercial: automated environmental monitoring with alerts.
tasks.name: Maintain environment

tasks.description: First in, first out. Flag batches approaching 12 months for priority sale or extraction.
tasks.name: Rotate stock (FIFO)

tasks.description: Commercial: retain testing samples per regulations. Track chain of custody.
tasks.name: Compliance testing holds

autoflower.description: Ruderalis-hybrid strains that flower automatically regardless of light schedule. Fixed timeline.

light_schedule.flower: 20/4 or 18/6 (same as veg — no change needed)
light_schedule.transition: Automatic — plant decides when to flower (usually week 3-4)

autoflower.name: Autoflower
autoflower.notes: Fixed timeline — stress cannot be recovered by extending veg. Start in final pot (no transplant shock). LST preferred over HST.

training_suitability.defoliation: light only — auto can't recover from heavy defol
training_suitability.topping: risky — only if plant is vigorous

photoperiod.description: Standard cannabis strains that require 12/12 light to flower. Grower controls timing.

light_schedule.transition: Switch to 12/12 when plant is ~50% of desired final height

photoperiod.name: Photoperiod
photoperiod.notes: Can be vegged as long as desired. Cloneable. More forgiving of stress (can extend veg to recover).

training_suitability.defoliation: good
training_suitability.topping: excellent

autoflower.description: Day-neutral genetics. Flowers based on age regardless of light schedule. Fixed timeline.
autoflower.rdwc_specific_notes: Stagger planting by 2-3 weeks per site for perpetual harvest. Different nutrient needs per site complicates shared reservoir — not ideal for beginners.
training_recommendations: LST only (no topping — recovery time too slow)

veg_duration_days.notes: Auto veg is short and non-adjustable. Plant starts flowering around day 21 regardless.

autoflower.yield_potential: Moderate — faster turnaround but smaller plants. Good for perpetual RDWC if you stagger planting.

fast_version.description: Photoperiod genetics with faster flower time (bred with auto genetics). Light-flip triggered but finishes 1-2 weeks faster.
fast_version.rdwc_specific_notes: Best of both worlds for RDWC. Full canopy control with faster turnaround. Ideal for uniform multi-site grows.
training_recommendations: Slightly shorter veg since flower is faster
fast_version.yield_potential: High — nearly photoperiod yields with faster finish. Excellent for RDWC.

photoperiod.description: Standard cannabis genetics. Veg duration controlled by grower (light schedule). Flower triggered by 12/12.
photoperiod.rdwc_specific_notes: With shared reservoir, all sites must flip to flower simultaneously. Cannot stagger harvest unless you isolate sites.
training_recommendations: Defoliate at day 21 and day 42 of flower

veg_duration_days.notes: RDWC plants veg aggressively — keep shorter veg than DWC to prevent overcrowding between sites.

photoperiod.yield_potential: High — full control of plant size via veg length. RDWC maximizes photoperiod potential.

examples.recommendation: Commercial 45-65 LPM pump (one pump, manifold to all stones)
60-80 LPM pump or dual 35 LPM pumps
Commercial 100+ LPM pump or regenerative blower

air_pump_sizing.rule: Minimum LPM = (site_count + 1) × 4 LPM per stone. +1 for central reservoir stone.

chiller_sizing.delta_temp_note: delta_temp = ambient room temp - target water temp. Typical indoor: 75°F room - 68°F target = 7°F delta.

examples.recommendation: 1 HP chiller or dedicated HVAC loop
Commercial inline pump or zone-split with multiple pumps

pump_sizing.head_loss_note: Add 20% for every foot of vertical lift. Add 10% for every 90° elbow.
pump_sizing.rule: Minimum pump GPH = site_count × target_gph_per_site × 2 (safety factor).

total_volume_formula.central_res_rule: central_res_gal = (site_count × bucket_gal) × 0.30 minimum. Bigger is always better.
total_volume_formula.plumbing_volume_estimate: Approximately 0.5 gal per site for typical tubing lengths (4-6 feet per run).

total_grow_days.breakdown: Germination (3-7d) + Seedling (7-14d) + Veg (28-63d) + Flower (56-70d) + Dry (7-14d) + Cure (14-60d)

solutions: Adjust ball valves until all site levels are equal
Flush each line individually with pressurized water
Bleed air from highest points in plumbing
Clean pump intake and impeller
problems.symptom: Uneven water levels across site buckets

solutions: Check pump intake immediately — root debris is the #1 cause
Clear main supply line
Swap to backup pump (you should have one ready)
Install inline pre-filter screen to prevent future blockages
problems.symptom: Pump running but no flow to sites

solutions: Drain system and reseal with new gasket
Apply marine-grade silicone if gasket replacement isn't enough
If bucket is cracked, replace the bucket

causes: Root rot (Pythium) at one site spread through recirculating water
solutions: ISOLATE: Cap the affected site's lines immediately to stop circulation
TREAT: Emergency H2O2 dose (3ml/gal of 3%) to entire system — kills pathogens AND beneficial bacteria
REBUILD: After 24 hours, do full system drain and refill. Re-dose Hydroguard at 3ml/gal (50% extra)
PREVENT: Lower water temp below 70°F. Maintain Hydroguard religiously. Install a chiller if you don't have one
MONITOR: Check roots at all sites daily for 2 weeks after incident
problems.symptom: Root rot spreading from one site to all sites

causes: Sites close together in same tent
solutions: Treat ALL plants, not just affected ones — shared environment means all are exposed
Increase airflow between sites
Remove severely affected plants entirely — they threaten the whole system
problems.symptom: Pest or mold spreading between sites

causes: Multiple plants consuming nutrients at different rates
Large root surface area affecting water chemistry
problems.diagnosis: Normal for RDWC — large root mass drives bigger swings
solutions: Adjust pH gently — don't over-correct
More frequent, smaller pH adjustments
Consider a pH controller for auto-dosing on large systems

causes: Multiple plants in peak growth or flower
problems.diagnosis: Multiple hungry plants depleting nutrients fast
solutions: Top off with nutrient water (1/4 to 1/2 strength) instead of plain water
More frequent reservoir changes with fresh full-strength solution
Increase central reservoir size for more buffer

cons: RO system cost and waste water
water_source_profiles.name: Reverse Osmosis (RO) Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Gold standard for precision growing. Always add CalMag first when using RO.
pros: Blank slate — total control over mineral content

dechlorination.chloramine: Does NOT gas off — must use Campden tablets or activated carbon filter
dechlorination.chlorine: Let sit uncovered 24 hours (gasses off) or use dechlorinator drops
dechlorination.how_to_tell: Check city water report. Most modern cities use chloramine.

water_source_profiles.name: Municipal Tap Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Good enough for most grows. Dechlorinate and pH-adjust. Get a water report from your city.

cons: May contain iron, sulfur, or heavy metals
water_source_profiles.name: Well Water
water_source_profiles.notes: MUST get a water test before using. High iron or sulfur can cause lockout. Great if your well has clean water with moderate minerals.

water_source_profiles.name: Distilled Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Same as RO but purchased instead of filtered. Only practical for 1-2 plant grows.

rainwater.contamination_warning: Filter through sediment filter minimum. First-flush diverter prevents roof contamination. Don't collect from treated/painted roofs.
rainwater.description: Collected rainwater — very low PPM, slightly acidic. Like soft RO water.
rainwater.rdwc_volume_note: Supply is weather-dependent. Need large storage capacity (500+ gal tank) to buffer dry periods. Good supplemental source.

calmag_dose_ml_per_gal.notes: RO water has zero calcium/magnesium. Must add CalMag FIRST before other nutrients.

cons: Slow production rate for large systems
reverse_osmosis.description: 0-20 PPM starting water. Pure blank slate for nutrients.
reverse_osmosis.ph_buffer_note: RO water has almost no buffering capacity — pH will swing more. Expect more frequent pH adjustments.
reverse_osmosis.rdwc_volume_note: RO systems produce 50-100 GPD. For 100+ gal systems, you need a high-output RO or to pre-fill a storage tank.

calmag_dose_ml_per_gal.notes: Usually not needed — tap water contains calcium and magnesium. Test first.

tap_municipal.chlorine_treatment: Let water sit 24 hours in open container (chlorine evaporates) OR use dechlorinator for chloramine (doesn't evaporate).
tap_municipal.description: City water — varies by location. Typically 100-400 PPM with chlorine/chloramine treatment.
tap_municipal.rdwc_volume_note: Easy fill — unlimited supply from tap. But chloramine in large volumes can overwhelm beneficial bacteria. Always dechlorinate.

starting_ec.notes: Test YOUR tap water. Subtract starting EC from target EC when mixing nutrients.

calmag_dose_ml_per_gal.notes: Usually high in calcium/magnesium already. Additional CalMag may cause lockout.

cons: Hard water can cause buildup in plumbing
well_water.description: Private well — varies wildly by geology. Can be excellent or problematic.
well_water.iron_warning: Iron above 0.3 PPM stains everything and feeds iron bacteria. Consider iron filter or RO for high-iron wells.
well_water.rdwc_volume_note: Unlimited supply, no chlorine. But well pumps have limited GPM — filling 100+ gal takes time. Consider a storage/staging tank.

starting_ec.notes: Get a full water test (iron, manganese, sulfur, hardness, pH, PPM).

advanced_techniques.description: Elevating CO2 from ambient (400ppm) to 1000-1500ppm increases photosynthesis rate by 20-40% when light and temperature are also increased.

methods.notes: Most common. Tank refills every 2-4 weeks for a tent.
Produces heat and humidity — better for large rooms with AC.
Produces ~100-200ppm boost. Only useful for small sealed spaces. Not worth it for most.

advanced_techniques.name: CO2 Supplementation

ppfd_co2_correlation.notes: Ambient CO2 sufficient at low light
Slight benefit from supplementation
Moderate benefit — cost-effective threshold
Strong benefit — diminishing returns above this
Maximum practical benefit for most grows
Commercial intensity — requires perfect environment

requirements: Sealed grow space (CO2 escapes quickly from leaky rooms)
High light intensity (>600 PPFD — plants can't use extra CO2 without sufficient light)
Higher temperature tolerance (82-86°F optimal with CO2 vs 75-80°F without)
CO2 controller or timer

advanced_techniques.description: Manipulating environmental and irrigation parameters to push the plant toward vegetative or generative (flowering/fruiting) growth.
advanced_techniques.name: Crop Steering
advanced_techniques.notes: Crop steering is most effective in hydro/coco where irrigation frequency is highly controllable. Soil growers have less precision due to soil buffering.

vegetative_steering.goal: More leaf/stem growth, larger plant framework

best_practices: Spray during lights-off or first/last 30 minutes of light cycle (stomata open, no light burn)
Use fine mist sprayer — coverage is key
pH foliar solution to 6.2-6.5 (leaf surface slightly higher pH than root zone)
EC should be very low (0.2-0.5) — leaves burn easily
Never foliar spray during flower (moisture in buds → mold/bud rot)
Best for micronutrient deficiency correction (CalMag, iron, magnesium)

common_foliar_products.frequency: Weekly preventive, 3-day intervals for active infestations

advanced_techniques.description: Spraying nutrient solution directly onto leaves for rapid uptake. Bypasses root zone entirely.
advanced_techniques.name: Foliar Feeding

advanced_techniques.description: Strengthens cell walls, increases stem thickness, improves heat/drought/pest resistance.
important_notes: ALWAYS add silica to water FIRST before other nutrients (raises pH significantly)
Let silica mix for 5-10 minutes before adding base nutrients
Silica is incompatible with concentrated nutrient solutions — never mix undiluted
Discontinue in late flower (last 2-3 weeks)
advanced_techniques.name: Silica (Potassium Silicate)

advanced_techniques.description: Introduces beneficial bacteria/fungi to the root zone to outcompete pathogens, improve nutrient uptake, and protect against root diseases.
important_notes: Do NOT use hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) with beneficial microbes — it kills them
Choose either sterile (H2O2) OR beneficial (Hydroguard) approach — not both
Beneficials need warmish water (65-75°F) to thrive
Reapply after any reservoir change or system cleaning
advanced_techniques.name: Beneficial Microbes & Root Inoculants

products.name: Hydroguard
products.notes: The standard for DWC/RDWC. Add at every res change.

products.dosing: 1 tsp/gal (water-in) or dust roots at transplant
products.name: Great White
products.notes: Best for soil/coco. Less effective in pure hydro.

products.name: Recharge
products.notes: Feeds existing soil microbes. Great for living soil.

products.name: Mammoth P
products.notes: Most effective in flower when P demand is highest.

advanced_techniques.description: Removing select fan leaves to improve light penetration, airflow, and direct energy to bud sites.
advanced_techniques.name: Defoliation (Strategic Leaf Removal)
rules: Never remove more than 20-30% of leaves at once (except schwazze technique)
Only defoliate healthy, vigorous plants — never stressed ones
Autoflowers: very light defoliation only — they can't recover from heavy removal
Always leave the top 2-3 fan leaves on each branch (they feed the bud below)

timing.method: Remove lower leaves that get no light (lollipop)
Major defoliation day 1 and day 21 of flower (schwazzing)
Selective tuck or remove leaves blocking bud sites

equipment.name: 1-inch starter cubes
equipment.notes: For germination. Pre-soak in pH 5.5 solution.

equipment.name: 4-inch grow cubes
equipment.notes: Seedling to early veg. Condition before use.

equipment.name: Rockwool slabs (Hugo or Expert)
equipment.notes: 6x36 inch standard. Hugo (larger pores) for cannabis preferred. Condition 24 hours before use. ONE GROW per slab.

equipment.name: Drip stakes (1 per plant)
equipment.notes: Insert into top of 4-inch cube on slab. Delivers shots.

equipment.name: Irrigation timer (multi-event)
equipment.notes: Must support 10-15 events per day in flower. Galcon, Titan, or Netafim. Simple on/off timers won't work.

equipment.name: Pump (submersible)
equipment.notes: Sized for your plant count. Consistent pressure.

equipment.name: Reservoir (opaque)
equipment.notes: Mix nutrients. Opaque to prevent algae. Size for 1-2 days.

equipment.name: Drip tubing + fittings
equipment.notes: 1/2 inch main line, 1/4 inch to stakes.

equipment.name: Drain-to-waste trays/channels
equipment.notes: Slabs sit in trays that catch runoff. Drain to waste — rockwool runoff is not recirculated.

equipment.name: pH meter (accurate to 0.1)
equipment.notes: Bluelab or Apera. Calibrate weekly.

equipment.name: EC/TDS meter
equipment.notes: Input AND runoff. Delta is critical.

equipment.name: Scale (under slab)
equipment.notes: THE rockwool metric. Weigh slabs to track dryback. Commercial: Aroya Teros or Grodan GroSens substrate sensors. Home: kitchen scale under slab.

equipment.name: Jeweler's loupe (60-100x)
equipment.notes: Trichome inspection.

equipment.name: Grow light (LED preferred)
equipment.notes: LED or HPS.

equipment.name: Exhaust fan + carbon filter
equipment.notes: Odor control and air exchange.

equipment.name: Oscillating fan(s)
equipment.notes: Canopy airflow.

equipment.name: Temperature/humidity controller
equipment.notes: Climate control.

equipment.name: Dehumidifier
equipment.notes: Essential in flower.

equipment.name: Base nutrient system
equipment.notes: GH Flora Trio, Athena Pro, Canna, etc.

equipment.name: CalMag supplement
equipment.notes: Rockwool has no cation exchange (unlike coco), but CalMag is still needed for LED grows and RO water.

equipment.name: pH Up/Down
equipment.notes: pH 5.5-6.0 for rockwool.

equipment.name: Hydroguard
equipment.notes: Root protection in warm moist rockwool.

equipment.name: PK booster
equipment.notes: Flower supplement.

equipment.name: Gloves (nitrile)
equipment.notes: Rockwool fibers irritate skin. Wear when handling dry rockwool.

equipment.name: Dust mask
equipment.notes: Rockwool dust irritates lungs. Wear when cutting or handling dry slabs/cubes.

flush_timing.coco: 7-14 days reduced nutrients → plain water last 3-5 days
flush_timing.notes: Flushing is debated — some studies show no difference. At minimum, reduce nutrients in final week.
flush_timing.soil: 14-21 days plain water (soil holds nutrients longer)

other_harvest_indicators: Most pistils (hairs) have darkened and curled in
Calyxes are swollen and dense
Fan leaves are yellowing and falling off naturally
Trichome stalks are visible and heads are enlarged
Breeder's recommended flowering time has passed

trichome_ratios.notes: Harvest early for more THC-dominant, less CBN. Energetic, racy high. Slight reduction in yield.
Standard harvest window. Mix of THC and CBN. Full-spectrum effects.
Extended harvest for max CBN conversion. Heavy body high. Good for pain/sleep. Slightly degraded THC.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Micro → Gro → Bloom (always Micro first)
nutrient_brands.name: General Hydroponics Flora Trio

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Part A → Epsom → Part B (dissolve each fully before adding next)
nutrient_brands.name: Jack's 321
nutrient_brands.notes: Jack's 321 is 3.6g Part A, 2.4g Cal Nit, 1.2g Epsom per gallon at full strength. Extremely cost-effective for large grows.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Core → Bloom
nutrient_brands.name: Athena Pro Line
nutrient_brands.notes: Commercial-grade. Simple 2-part with integrated CalMag. Popular in commercial cannabis facilities.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: A → B (separate for veg and bloom)
nutrient_brands.name: Advanced Nutrients pH Perfect (Sensi)
nutrient_brands.notes: Self-adjusting pH technology (claims no pH adjustment needed). Expensive but popular for beginners who struggle with pH.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Shake VERY well before use (thick concentrate settles). One bottle at a time.
nutrient_brands.name: General Hydroponics FloraNova
nutrient_brands.notes: Simplest option — one bottle per phase. Great for beginners. Shake well — concentrate separates.
nutrient_brands.type: 1-part (two bottles — one for veg, one for bloom)

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Dissolve powder in warm water, stir well
nutrient_brands.name: Greenleaf Nutrients Mega Crop
nutrient_brands.notes: One powder does everything — includes CalMag. Incredibly cost-effective at ~$0.02/gal. Community favorite on r/microgrowery.

environment.container: Glass mason jars (quart size). Fill 75% full.

humidity_packs.boveda_58: For drier preference or long-term storage.
humidity_packs.boveda_62: Maintains 62% RH. Set-and-forget. Replace when crispy.
humidity_packs.grove_bags: Heat-sealed bags with built-in humidity regulation. No burping needed.

indicators_of_good_cure: Smooth, flavorful smoke — no harshness or hay smell
Buds are slightly sticky but not wet
Strong terpene aroma when jar is opened
Ash burns to white/light gray (not black)
method: Trim buds to final shape (dry trim or finish wet trim)
Place in mason jars — fill 3/4 full to allow air exchange
Week 1: burp 2-3x daily for 10-15 minutes (release moisture and gases)
Week 2-4: burp 1x daily for 5-10 minutes
Month 2+: burp 1x weekly or seal completely
Cure for minimum 2 weeks, ideal is 4-8 weeks. Some strains improve for 6+ months.

common_mistakes: Drying too fast (>3 days) — harsh smoke, hay smell, terpene loss
Drying too slow (>14 days) — mold risk, diminishing returns
Fan blowing directly on buds — uneven drying, crispy exterior/moist interior
Temperature too high (>70°F) — terpene evaporation, harsh taste

environment.airflow: Gentle circulation — fan NOT pointed at buds. Just keep air moving.

method: Cut branches 12-18 inches long
Optionally remove large fan leaves (wet trim) or leave intact (dry trim)
Hang upside down on line/rack with 6+ inches between branches
Small branches should snap (not bend) when done — 5-7 days minimum
Stems should snap cleanly; if they bend, keep drying

long_term_storage.degradation: THC slowly converts to CBN over time. Light, heat, air, and moisture accelerate degradation.
long_term_storage.method: Sealed mason jars or vacuum-sealed bags with Boveda 58% in dark, cool place
long_term_storage.notes: Properly cured and stored cannabis can remain excellent for 12+ months. Freezing is debated — can damage trichomes if not done carefully.

crop_steering_guide.description: Crop steering is THE rockwool technique. Control vegetative vs generative growth through dryback, EC, and irrigation timing.

generative.effect: Pushes flowering: denser buds, more trichomes, faster ripening.

transition.effect: Gradual shift from veg to flower over 7-14 days.

vegetative.effect: Pushes vegetative growth: bigger leaves, taller stems, more branching.

cube_to_slab_guide.transplant_1_to_4: When roots emerge from 1-inch cube bottom. Place on top of 4-inch cube hole.
cube_to_slab_guide.transplant_4_to_slab: When roots emerge from 4-inch cube sides. Place cube on conditioned slab surface.

golden_rules: Condition rockwool before use. New rockwool pH is 7-8. Non-negotiable.
Slab weight is your #1 metric. Weigh daily. Everything flows from this number.
Morning pre-first-shot weight determines your dryback and steering strategy.
Never let slab weight drop below 35% — roots die fast in dry rockwool.
Crop steering is THE technique — master dryback and you master rockwool.
P1/P2/P3 shot phases structure your daily irrigation curve.
Shot-based irrigation: many small precise doses, not one big watering.
Rockwool is not reusable. One grow per slab. Dispose responsibly.
Wear gloves and mask when handling dry rockwool — fibers irritate.
Drain to waste — rockwool runoff should not be recirculated.

ph_range.notes: Rockwool pH range is tighter than coco. New rockwool is 7-8 — MUST condition.

shot_phases.P1_morning: Ramp-up — restore slab to target weight after overnight dryback. Multiple small shots.
shot_phases.P2_midday: Maintenance — keep slab at target weight during peak transpiration.
shot_phases.P3_afternoon: Dryback setup — reduce/stop shots to allow overnight dryback.
shot_phases.description: Daily irrigation is divided into three phases.

slab_conditioning_protocol.step_1: Unwrap slab from packaging. Wear gloves.
slab_conditioning_protocol.step_3: Submerge or flood slab in solution for 24 hours.
slab_conditioning_protocol.step_4: Drain completely. Flip slab. Re-flood for 8 hours.
slab_conditioning_protocol.step_5: Drain. Test runoff pH — must be below 6.0.
slab_conditioning_protocol.step_6: Cut drainage slits in bottom wrap (off-center, 2-3 slits).
slab_conditioning_protocol.step_7: Slab is ready for cube placement.
slab_conditioning_protocol.why: New rockwool pH is 7.0-8.0 (too alkaline for cannabis). The lime binder used in manufacturing raises pH. Conditioning neutralizes this. Skipping conditioning is the #1 rockwool mistake.

slab_weight_guide.danger_zone: Below 35% slab weight — roots begin dying
slab_weight_guide.dry_slab_0_pct: Weight of brand new dry slab (before conditioning)
slab_weight_guide.field_capacity_100_pct: Weight of fully saturated slab after draining (this is your 100% baseline)

scale_tiers.description: First-time grower or single-plant hobbyist. Manual management, learning-focused.
scale_tiers.name: Solo / Single Plant
scale_tiers.notes: Hands-on learning experience. Manual checks 1-2x daily. Ideal for beginners.

scale_tiers.description: Hobbyist tent grow. Light automation helpful but not required.
scale_tiers.name: Small Tent (2-8 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Timer-controlled lights, humidity controller. Sensor monitoring recommended.

scale_tiers.description: Dedicated grower with multiple tents/rooms. Automation saves significant time.
scale_tiers.name: Multi-Tent (9-24 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Multiple environments to manage. Automated alerts essential. Consider perpetual harvest.

scale_tiers.description: Licensed commercial operation. Full automation, compliance tracking, batch management.
scale_tiers.name: Commercial Room (25-100 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: SOPs required. Staff training. Regulatory compliance. Insurance requirements.

scale_tiers.description: Large-scale production facility. Enterprise automation, SCADA integration, dedicated staff.
scale_tiers.name: Warehouse Scale (100+ plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Multiple rooms, dedicated HVAC, industrial nutrient dosing, video surveillance.

common_problems.cause: Cube too wet (saturated), too cold, or bad seed
common_problems.solution: Squeeze out excess water. Cubes should be moist, not dripping. Ensure heat mat at 78°F. Try new seed after 7 days.

common_problems.cause: Insufficient conditioning — new rockwool pH is 7-8
common_problems.solution: Re-soak cubes in pH 5.0 solution for 1 hour. Test runoff. Repeat until below 6.0. This is the #1 rockwool mistake.

stages.description: Seed cracks open and taproot emerges. Start seeds in pre-soaked 1-inch rockwool starter cubes (pH conditioned to 5.5). Humidity dome. Darkness until sprout emerges.

environment.notes: Seeds in darkness. Heat mat at 78°F. Humidity dome over starter cubes.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse germination: heat mat + dome. Temperature swings may slow germination.

greenhouse.notes: Heat mat essential in greenhouse.

environment_overrides.notes: Not recommended. Rockwool is an indoor/greenhouse method.

outdoor.notes: Start indoors always.

health_checks: Cubes properly pH conditioned (test runoff pH < 6.0)?

medium.notes: Seeds are in 1-inch starter cubes, not on slabs yet. Pre-soak cubes in pH 5.5 nutrient solution (EC 0.4) before inserting seeds. Squeeze out excess — cubes should be moist, not dripping.

stages.name: Germination

nutrients.approach: Seeds contain all energy. Pre-soak cubes with very light nutrient solution (EC 0.4, pH 5.5) before inserting seeds.

tasks.description: Soak 1-inch rockwool cubes in pH 5.5 nutrient solution (EC 0.4) for 30-60 minutes. Gently squeeze out excess. Should be evenly moist. New rockwool is pH 7-8 — conditioning is NON-NEGOTIABLE.
tasks.name: Condition starter cubes

tasks.description: Place seed in cube hole, pointed end down. Cover lightly with a torn piece of rockwool. Do NOT push seed deep.
tasks.name: Insert seeds

tasks.description: Place cubes in tray under humidity dome. Heat mat at 78°F.
tasks.name: Humidity dome

tasks.description: After 24-72 hours, look for white taproot emerging from cube bottom.
tasks.name: Check for taproot

common_problems.cause: Cube too wet, poor airflow
common_problems.issue: Damping off (stem rots at cube surface)
common_problems.solution: Reduce watering. Improve airflow. Cube should dry slightly between waterings. Treat with Hydroguard.

common_problems.cause: 4-inch cube too dry or poor contact with starter cube
common_problems.solution: Ensure starter cube sits flush in the 4-inch cube hole. Moisten the contact area.

stages.description: First true leaves develop. Seedling in 1-inch cube, transplant to 4-inch cube when roots emerge from starter cube. Begin light hand-watering of the 4-inch cube. No slabs yet.

environment.notes: Gentle light. Gradually remove humidity dome over 3-5 days.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse seedlings: watch temperature swings.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse: heat mat recommended.

environment_overrides.notes: Keep seedlings indoors or in greenhouse.

outdoor.notes: Rockwool seedlings: indoor only.

medium.notes: Seedling in 4-inch cube. Hand-water when cube feels light. 1x/day is usually enough. DO NOT overwater cubes — seedling roots need oxygen. The 4-inch cube should feel damp but not saturated. Lift to check weight.

shot_volume_ml.notes: Hand-water 4-inch cube. Small volume — just enough to moisten.

stages.name: Seedling

nutrients.approach: Quarter strength. pH 5.5-5.8. Low EC for tender seedlings.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root protection in moist rockwool.

tasks.description: When roots emerge from 1-inch cube, place it on top of a pre-conditioned 4-inch cube. The 1-inch cube sits in the hole of the 4-inch cube. Roots grow down into the larger cube.
tasks.name: Transplant to 4-inch cube

tasks.description: Soak in pH 5.5, EC 0.6 solution for 1 hour. Drain but don't squeeze. Should be evenly moist.
tasks.name: Condition 4-inch cubes

tasks.description: Lift 4-inch cube. Heavy = still wet. Light = time to water. The rockwool skill starts here.
tasks.name: Daily weight check

tasks.description: Crack day 1, remove for hours day 2, fully remove by day 3-5.
tasks.name: Remove humidity dome gradually

common_problems.cause: Insufficient conditioning
common_problems.solution: Re-condition: run pH 5.0 solution through slab at 3x volume. Let sit 8 hours. Test runoff. Repeat.

common_problems.cause: Cube-slab contact poor, or slab too dry/wet
common_problems.solution: Ensure cube bottom is flat against slab. Moisten contact zone. Don't oversaturate slab — roots grow toward moisture gradient.

common_problems.cause: Light reaching wet rockwool surface
common_problems.solution: Cover slab with opaque wrap. No light on rockwool. Algae competes for nutrients and hosts fungus gnats.

stages.description: Transplant 4-inch cube to slab. This is where rockwool's precision irrigation begins. Condition slabs, cut drainage slits, place cubes, install drip stakes. Begin shot-based irrigation on the slab. Vegetative steering: low dryback, frequent shots.

environment.notes: Ramp light. Humidity moderate. Plant growing rapidly on slab.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse rockwool is the commercial standard. Excellent.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse: ideal for rockwool.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor rockwool not recommended. Rain destroys irrigation precision.

outdoor.notes: Keep rockwool indoors or in greenhouse.

health_checks: Roots growing from cube into slab?

medium.notes: VEGETATIVE STEERING on slab. Low dryback (5-10%), frequent shots, lower EC. This pushes vegetative growth. First shot 30 min after lights on (early — don't let morning dryback go too far). Last shot 2-3 hours before lights off. P1 phase (morning ramp-up): restore slab to field capacity. P2 (midday): maintenance shots. P3 (afternoon): allow dryback before dark.

shot_volume_ml.notes: Per drip stake per shot. Volume depends on slab size and plant count per slab.

slab_weight_pct.notes: Vegetative: keep slab wet. 70% field capacity. Weight is the metric.

stages.name: Early Vegetative

nutrients.approach: Half strength. Veg ratio (more nitrogen). EC 0.8-1.2.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root protection in warm rockwool.

supplements.name: Silica (Armor Si)
supplements.purpose: Stem strength. Add FIRST.

tasks.description: Soak new slabs in pH 5.0-5.5 nutrient solution (EC 1.0) for 24 hours. Drain. Re-soak 8 hours. Test runoff pH — must be below 6.0. This is NON-NEGOTIABLE. New rockwool pH is 7-8.
tasks.name: Condition slabs

tasks.description: Cut 2-3 slits in the bottom of the slab wrap, off-center (not directly below cubes). Slits allow drainage but not too fast.
tasks.name: Cut drainage slits

tasks.description: Place 4-inch cubes on slab surface. 1-2 plants per standard slab. Cube bottom in full contact with slab top.
tasks.name: Place cubes on slab

tasks.description: One drip stake per cube, inserted into the cube top. Connected to pump/timer system.
tasks.name: Install drip stakes

tasks.description: Program timer: 3 shots/day. First shot 30 min after lights on, last shot 2-3 hours before lights off, middle shot(s) evenly spaced.
tasks.name: Begin shot-based irrigation

tasks.description: Weigh slab at saturation (= 100% field capacity). Track daily weight to calculate dryback. Commercial: use substrate sensors (Aroya, GroSens). Home: kitchen scale under slab.
tasks.name: Weigh slabs daily

tasks.description: Start LST, topping at 4-5 nodes. Rockwool plants grow fast with precision irrigation.
tasks.name: Begin training

training.description: Bend and tie branches. Start at 4-5 nodes.
Cut above 4th or 5th node.

transition_signals: Roots visible in slab (check by lifting cube)

common_problems.cause: Salt accumulation in slab
common_problems.solution: Increase shot volume for more runoff. Or flush with low-EC solution.

common_problems.cause: Drip stake not centered, or slab tilted wrong
common_problems.solution: Check stake position. Level slab with slight tilt toward drainage slits.

stages.description: Plant reaches target size on slab. Ramp shot count. Continue vegetative steering. Root system now established throughout slab. Pre-flip: saturate slab (reset to field capacity) and flush salts.

environment.notes: Full veg intensity. Heavy transpiration.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: excellent for late veg rockwool.

greenhouse.notes: Commercial standard.

environment_overrides.notes: Not recommended outdoors.

outdoor.notes: Keep indoor/greenhouse.

medium.notes: Continue vegetative steering. 5 shots/day, low dryback. P1 (morning): 2-3 shots to restore field capacity quickly. P2 (midday): 1-2 maintenance shots. P3 (afternoon): 1 shot, then allow overnight dryback. Pre-flip: run a saturation event — flood slab to field capacity, flush with low-EC solution to reset salts.

shot_volume_ml.notes: Larger shots as plant drinks more.

slab_weight_pct.notes: Keep slab wet for veg push.

stages.name: Late Vegetative

nutrients.approach: Three-quarter strength. Veg ratio. EC 1.2-1.6.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root protection.

supplements.name: Silica (Armor Si)
supplements.purpose: Stem strength.

tasks.description: P1: 2-3 morning shots. P2: 1-2 midday. P3: 1 afternoon. First shot 30 min after lights on.
tasks.name: Ramp shot count to 5/day

tasks.description: Track dryback overnight. Should be 5-10% for veg steering. Commercial: substrate sensor graphs.
tasks.name: Daily slab weight tracking

tasks.description: Collect from drainage slits. EC delta <0.3, pH 5.5-6.0.
tasks.name: Check slab runoff EC/pH

tasks.description: 2-3 days before flip: run low-EC solution (EC 0.4) through slabs until runoff EC matches input. Then saturate to field capacity.
tasks.name: Pre-flip saturation flush

tasks.description: Complete topping, LST, SCROG filling.
tasks.name: Final training

tasks.description: Defoliate lower growth. Lollipop lower 1/3.
tasks.name: Canopy management

training.description: Install and fill 70-80% before flip.
Remove lower 1/3.

common_problems.cause: Increased dryback too fast
common_problems.solution: Ease back. Increase dryback by 1-2% every 2-3 days, not all at once. The plant needs to adapt.

common_problems.cause: Genetics, or transition too slow
common_problems.solution: Accelerate generative steering (increase dryback faster). Supercrop. Some cultivars stretch regardless.

stages.description: Flipped to 12/12. Explosive stretch. BEGIN transitioning from vegetative to generative steering. Gradually increase dryback, increase EC, reduce shot frequency slightly while maintaining slab at adequate moisture. This transition is THE art of rockwool growing.

environment.notes: Flip to 12/12. DIF of 10°F helps control stretch.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: light dep triggers flip.

greenhouse.notes: Light dep recommended.

environment_overrides.notes: Not recommended.

outdoor.notes: Indoor/greenhouse only.

health_checks: First shot timing delaying on schedule?

medium.notes: TRANSITION from veg to generative. Over 7-14 days: increase overnight dryback from 8% to 15%. Delay first shot from 30 min to 1 hour after lights on. Slightly increase EC. P1: delayed start, restore to 65% (not full field capacity). P2: maintenance. P3: allow deeper dryback. The goal: signal the plant to shift energy from vegetative growth to flower production.

slab_weight_pct.notes: Transitioning: slab running slightly drier than veg.

stages.name: Transition (Stretch)

nutrients.approach: Transition ratio. Reduce Gro, increase Bloom. EC 1.6-2.0.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root protection.

supplements.name: Silica (Armor Si)
supplements.purpose: Support stretching stems.

tasks.description: Zero light leaks. Rockwool operations don't change — just the photoperiod.
tasks.name: Flip to 12/12

tasks.description: Gradually increase overnight dryback: day 1-3 = 8-10%, day 4-7 = 10-12%, day 8-14 = 12-15%. Delay first shot timing by 10 min every 2-3 days.
tasks.name: Begin steering transition

tasks.description: Shift from veg to bloom ratio over 5-7 days. Increase EC by 0.1 every 2-3 days.
tasks.name: Transition nutrient ratio

tasks.description: Morning pre-first-shot weight = overnight dryback. This number drives your steering decisions.
tasks.name: Track slab weight daily

tasks.description: Supercrop, SCROG tuck daily.
tasks.name: Manage stretch

training.description: Bend tall stems. Recovery 2-3 days.
Daily tucking under net.

common_problems.cause: Slab weight dropped below 40% — root damage
common_problems.solution: EMERGENCY: run a saturation event. Slab to field capacity. Resume at 50-55% weight target. Roots in dry rockwool die fast.

common_problems.cause: EC too high for cultivar
common_problems.solution: Reduce EC by 0.2-0.3. Some cultivars can't handle 2.6.

common_problems.cause: Uneven irrigation — some roots getting more than others
common_problems.solution: Check drip stakes. Ensure even flow. May need slab leveling.

stages.description: Stretch ended. Buds forming. Full generative steering now: maximum dryback, highest EC, delayed first shot, lower slab weight targets. This is where rockwool's precision pays off — every parameter is optimized for flower production.

environment.notes: Peak light. Low humidity. CO2 to 1000-1200 ppm if available.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: excellent for rockwool flower with climate control.

greenhouse.notes: Ideal environment.

environment_overrides.notes: Not recommended.

outdoor.notes: Indoor/greenhouse.

medium.notes: FULL GENERATIVE STEERING. Dryback 15-25%. First shot delayed 1-2 hours after lights on (morning dryback is the strongest generative signal). EC 2.2-2.6. Slab weight 45-65%. P1: delayed start, restore to 55-65% (NOT full field capacity). P2: frequent small maintenance shots. P3: stop shots 3 hours before lights off, allow deep overnight dryback. The slab is your precision tool — every % of dryback drives generative response.

slab_weight_pct.notes: Generative: slab running drier. But never let it go below 40% — roots die.

stages.name: Early Flower

nutrients.approach: Full bloom. Heavy PK. EC 2.2-2.6.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root protection.

supplements.name: Liquid Kool Bloom
supplements.purpose: PK booster for bud formation.

tasks.description: Dryback 15-25%. First shot 1-2 hours after lights on. EC 2.2-2.6. Slab weight target 55%.
tasks.name: Full generative steering

tasks.description: Weigh slab at lights-on, before first shot. This is your generative signal. Target: 15-25% dryback from yesterday's final weight.
tasks.name: Track morning dryback precisely

tasks.description: High EC + generative stress = fast salt accumulation. Check every 2 days. Flush if delta >0.5.
tasks.name: Monitor runoff EC/pH

tasks.description: Remove fan leaves blocking bud sites. Open canopy interior.
tasks.name: Defoliate for airflow

tasks.description: Dense buds in humid environments = risk.
tasks.name: Check for bud rot / PM

training.description: Remove blocking fan leaves. Max 20% at once.
Remove growth below net/lower 1/3.

common_problems.cause: Slab weight dropped below 35% — rockwool below 35% kills roots
common_problems.solution: EMERGENCY saturation event. This is the rockwool danger zone. Roots in dry rockwool die faster than in any other medium.

common_problems.cause: EC concentrated in dry slab
common_problems.solution: Flush with low-EC solution. Resume at 80% previous EC.

stages.description: Peak bud development. Maximum generative steering: deepest dryback, highest EC, most aggressive morning delay. Every parameter optimized for bud density and weight. This is where rockwool's precision separates it from every other method.

environment.notes: Peak light. Low humidity. CO2 at 1200-1500 ppm if available.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: peak production.

greenhouse.notes: Ideal.

environment_overrides.notes: Not recommended.

outdoor.notes: Indoor/greenhouse.

medium.notes: PEAK GENERATIVE. Deepest dryback (20-30%). Longest morning delay (1.5-2 hours after lights on). Highest EC (2.4-2.8). Many small shots to maintain the 40-60% slab weight without ever saturating. P1: delayed start, small shots to restore to 50-55%. P2: frequent tiny maintenance shots. P3: stop 3-4 hours before lights off for deep overnight dryback. The morning pre-first-shot weight is your #1 daily metric.

slab_weight_pct.notes: Peak generative. Slab at lowest safe moisture. NEVER below 35%.

stages.name: Mid Flower (Peak Bloom)

nutrients.approach: Full bloom. Peak PK. EC 2.4-2.8.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root protection.

supplements.name: Liquid Kool Bloom
supplements.purpose: PK booster at peak.

tasks.description: Dryback 20-30%, delay first shot 1.5-2 hours, EC 2.4-2.8. Monitor plant response daily — wilting means back off.
tasks.name: Peak generative steering

tasks.description: Weigh slab at lights-on before first shot. This number drives all decisions.
tasks.name: Morning weight check

tasks.description: High EC + deep dryback = rapid salt concentration. Flush if delta >0.5.
tasks.name: Runoff EC/pH every 2 days

tasks.description: Dense buds. High risk.
tasks.name: Daily bud rot inspection

tasks.description: Trellis, yo-yos, stakes.
tasks.name: Support heavy branches

common_problems.cause: pH lockout or root issues, not natural fade
common_problems.solution: Check runoff pH. Gradual yellowing = good. Sudden browning = problem.

common_problems.cause: Light stress
common_problems.solution: Raise/dim lights.

stages.description: Buds ripening. Reduce EC and ease back on generative steering slightly. Fan leaf yellowing is natural. Taper toward flush.

environment.notes: Slightly reduced light. Cooler nights. Low humidity.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: maintain environment control.

greenhouse.notes: Continue precision.

environment_overrides.notes: Not recommended.

outdoor.notes: Indoor/greenhouse.

medium.notes: Still generative but tapering. Reduce EC. Fewer shots. Plant is finishing — it needs less. Natural leaf yellowing = plant consuming stored nutrients.

stages.name: Late Flower (Ripening)

nutrients.approach: Reducing. EC 2.0-2.4. Lower nitrogen. Maintain PK.

supplements.name: Hydroguard
supplements.purpose: Root protection.

supplements.name: Liquid Kool Bloom
supplements.purpose: Reduced PK.

tasks.description: EC 2.0-2.4. 7 shots/day. Plant drinking less.
tasks.name: Reduce EC and shot count

tasks.description: 60-100x loupe. Track clear → milky → amber.
tasks.name: Trichome checks

tasks.description: Daily.
tasks.name: Continue bud rot inspection

tasks.description: Start flush 7-14 days before target harvest.
tasks.name: Plan flush timing

common_problems.cause: Insufficient flush volume
common_problems.solution: Increase shot volume. More runoff.

stages.description: Pre-harvest flush. Run low-EC solution (pH 5.8, EC 0.0-0.2) through slabs to leach salts. Rockwool flushes efficiently — salts leach readily with high-volume shots. Saturate slab and maintain high moisture during flush.

environment.notes: Reduced light. Low humidity.

environment_overrides.notes: Manage humidity from saturated slabs.

greenhouse.notes: Watch humidity spike.

environment_overrides.notes: N/A.

outdoor.notes: Indoor/greenhouse.

medium.notes: FLUSH MODE. Saturate slab. Large shots with high runoff (30%+) to leach salts. Monitor runoff EC — flush is done when runoff EC drops below 0.3-0.5. Rockwool flushes efficiently.

stages.name: Flush (Pre-Harvest)

nutrients.approach: Plain pH'd water only (pH 5.8). EC 0.0-0.2. No nutrients.

tasks.description: Plain pH 5.8 water. No nutrients (rockwool doesn't have cation exchange like coco — no CalMag needed during flush).
tasks.name: Switch to flush solution

tasks.description: Flush complete when runoff EC drops below 0.3-0.5.
tasks.name: Monitor runoff EC daily

tasks.description: Harvest window approaching.
tasks.name: Trichome checks daily

tasks.description: High slab moisture during flush increases risk.
tasks.name: Bud rot inspection

common_problems.cause: Hidden rot in dense colas
common_problems.solution: Cut away rot + 1 inch. Salvage clean buds.

stages.description: Chop day. Cut plants, trim, hang to dry. Dispose of spent slabs — rockwool is NOT reusable (unlike coco). One grow per slab.

environment.notes: Some growers give 24-48 hours darkness before chop.

environment_overrides.notes: Use greenhouse space for drying.

greenhouse.notes: Drying space.

environment_overrides.notes: N/A.

outdoor.notes: N/A.

health_checks: No bud rot found during trim?

medium.notes: No more irrigation. Dispose of spent slabs — NOT compostable. Landfill or specialized rockwool recycling programs (Grodan). Wear gloves when handling dry rockwool — fibers irritate skin.

stages.name: Harvest

nutrients.approach: None.

tasks.description: Confirm target ratio.
tasks.name: Final trichome check

tasks.description: Cut main stem. Wet trim or whole-plant hang.
tasks.name: Harvest

tasks.description: Spent rockwool → landfill or recycling. NOT compostable. Wear gloves for dry handling.
tasks.name: Dispose of slabs

tasks.description: Clean drip system, lines, stakes. Sanitize with H2O2.
tasks.name: Clean equipment

common_problems.cause: Low humidity, high temp
common_problems.solution: Raise humidity, lower temp.

common_problems.cause: Dense buds + high humidity
common_problems.solution: Remove moldy buds. Lower humidity.

stages.description: Slow controlled drying. 60°F / 60% humidity / darkness. Rockwool-grown flower dries similarly to hydro-grown.

environment.notes: DARK. 60°F. 60% RH. Gentle airflow not directly on buds.

environment_overrides.notes: Too warm/bright. Dry in dark room.

greenhouse.notes: Separate dark room.

environment_overrides.notes: Dry indoors.

outdoor.notes: Always indoors.

medium.notes: No rockwool involvement. Drying is environmental control.

stages.name: Drying

nutrients.approach: None.

tasks.description: Space so they don't touch.
tasks.name: Hang plants/branches

tasks.description: 60°F, 60% RH, dark.
tasks.name: Maintain 60/60

tasks.description: Bend stem — snaps = ready.
tasks.name: Check drying progress

tasks.description: Daily.
tasks.name: Inspect for mold

common_problems.cause: Too wet when jarred
common_problems.solution: Remove, dry 12-24 hours, rejar.

common_problems.cause: Over-dried
common_problems.solution: Boveda 62 to rehydrate.

stages.description: Mason jar cure. Minimum 2 weeks, ideal 4-8 weeks.

environment.notes: In-jar: 58-62% RH (Boveda 62). Dark, cool storage.

environment_overrides.notes: Cure indoors.

greenhouse.notes: Stable indoor space.

environment_overrides.notes: Cure indoors.

outdoor.notes: Same process.

medium.notes: Post-harvest processing.

stages.name: Curing

nutrients.approach: None.

tasks.description: Trim sugar leaves. Jars 75% full.
tasks.name: Trim and jar

tasks.description: 2-3x/day week 1, 1x/day week 2.
tasks.name: Burp jars

tasks.description: Boveda 62%, 1 per oz.
tasks.name: Add Boveda packs

tasks.description: Check when burping.
tasks.name: Monitor for mold

container_progression.commercial_medium: Turkey bags in sealed bins, nitrogen-flushed
container_progression.industrial: Climate-controlled vault, nitrogen generators, automated monitoring

degradation_timeline.18_plus_months: Convert to extracts, edibles, or topicals.

testing_schedule.360_days: Full panel — sell, process, or destroy decision

common_problems.cause: Heat, light, oxygen, or time
common_problems.solution: Darkness, cool temps (60°F), minimal oxygen. ~5%/year baseline.

common_problems.cause: Temps above 70°F, oxygen, frequent opening
common_problems.solution: Below 65°F. Nitrogen-sealed. Minimize opening.

common_problems.cause: Humidity above 65% or improper cure
common_problems.solution: Verify 58-62% RH before sealing. Remove affected material.

common_problems.cause: Normal moisture equilibration
common_problems.solution: Boveda packs. Sealed containers.

stages.description: Post-cure long-term storage. Rockwool's precision crop steering produces premium, dense flower — that quality investment must be protected in storage. Commercial rockwool operations (the dominant commercial indoor method) often store hundreds of pounds. Proper storage preserves potency and terpenes for 6-12+ months.

environment.notes: DARK. Cool. Stable. Zero light — UV destroys cannabinoids and terpenes. Commercial: 58-62°F, 55-60% RH, nitrogen atmosphere.

environment_overrides.notes: Do NOT store in greenhouse.

greenhouse.notes: Climate-controlled indoor space only.

environment_overrides.notes: Storage is always indoor.

outdoor.notes: Indoor controlled environment.

stages.name: Long-Term Storage

nutrients.approach: None.

tasks.description: Home: mason jars with Boveda 58-62%. Commercial: nitrogen-sealed grove bags, CVault, or nitrogen-flushed drums.
tasks.name: Transfer to long-term containers

tasks.description: Strain, harvest date, storage date, weight, batch number, steering profile. Commercial: seed-to-sale, FIFO rotation.
tasks.name: Label and track batches

tasks.description: Inspect for mold, off odors. Check humidity packs. Commercial: potency/terpene testing at 30/90/180 days.
tasks.name: Monthly quality checks

tasks.description: Monitor vault temp/humidity. No light leaks. Commercial: automated alerts.
tasks.name: Maintain environment

tasks.description: First in, first out. Flag batches approaching 12 months.
tasks.name: Rotate stock (FIFO)

tasks.description: Commercial: retain samples per regulations.
tasks.name: Compliance testing holds

autoflower.description: Ruderalis-hybrid strains that flower automatically regardless of light schedule. Fixed timeline.

light_schedule.flower: 20/4 or 18/6 (same as veg — no change needed)
light_schedule.transition: Automatic — plant decides when to flower (usually week 3-4)

autoflower.name: Autoflower
autoflower.notes: Fixed timeline — stress cannot be recovered by extending veg. Start in final pot (no transplant shock). LST preferred over HST.

training_suitability.defoliation: light only — auto can't recover from heavy defol
training_suitability.topping: risky — only if plant is vigorous

photoperiod.description: Standard cannabis strains that require 12/12 light to flower. Grower controls timing.

light_schedule.transition: Switch to 12/12 when plant is ~50% of desired final height

photoperiod.name: Photoperiod
photoperiod.notes: Can be vegged as long as desired. Cloneable. More forgiving of stress (can extend veg to recover).

training_suitability.defoliation: good
training_suitability.topping: excellent

express_method.notes: More aggressive pH for faster conditioning. Use only if time-constrained. Check runoff pH before planting.

never_do: Never use rockwool without conditioning (plants will show iron deficiency within days)
Never squeeze rockwool dry (damages fiber structure, reduces air porosity)
Never reuse rockwool from a previous grow (pathogens, salt accumulation, degraded structure)

steps: Fill container with pH 4.5 nutrient solution (light veg formula at 0.5 EC)
Fully submerge rockwool cubes/slabs — squeeze gently to displace air
Soak for 24 hours minimum (48h for large slabs)
Do NOT rinse after soaking — go directly to planting
Check runoff pH — should be 5.5-6.0. If still above 6.5, soak again.

conditioning_protocol.why_condition: Rockwool is naturally alkaline (pH 7.5-8.0). Unconditioned rockwool locks out iron, manganese, and phosphorus.

grow_cubes.hole_prep: Pre-made hole in center for propagation cube. Ensure good contact.
grow_cubes.irrigation: Top-feed drip or hand water. Target 10-20% runoff.
grow_cubes.transplant_timing: When roots visible on all sides of grow cube.
grow_cubes.use: Vegetative growth. Transplant propagation cube INTO grow cube (cube-in-cube).

propagation_cubes.dome_humidity: Keep under humidity dome at 80-90% RH until roots emerge.
propagation_cubes.saturation: Fully saturate during conditioning. Drain by gravity (never squeeze).
propagation_cubes.transplant_timing: When roots visible on all 4 sides and emerging from bottom.
propagation_cubes.use: Seed germination and cloning. Single plant per cube.

slabs.drain_slits: Cut 2 drain slits per plant position on bottom edge of slab wrap. 1 inch long. Allows runoff.
slabs.initial_saturation: Condition slab, then let EC stabilize for 24h before placing plants.
slabs.use: Final growing position. Place grow cubes ON TOP of slab (roots grow down into slab).

disposal_methods: Landfill (rockwool is inert mineral fiber — does not decompose but is non-toxic)
Garden amendment (shred and mix into outdoor garden soil for aeration — lasts indefinitely)
Some municipalities accept in construction waste recycling
disposal_and_sustainability.environmental_notes: Rockwool manufacturing is energy-intensive (melting basalt rock at 3000°F). Offset by high yields and water efficiency in use.

ec_gradient.description: In rockwool slabs, EC is not uniform. Top layer (where drip hits) is lower EC. Bottom layer accumulates salts.
ec_gradient.management: Adequate runoff from drain slits flushes bottom layer. Minimum 15-20% runoff in flower.

slab_ec_monitoring.if_slab_ec_high: Increase irrigation frequency and runoff %. Flush with lower-EC solution.
slab_ec_monitoring.if_slab_ec_low: Increase input EC slightly. Plants are hungry.
slab_ec_monitoring.method: Extract solution from slab center with syringe. Measure EC.
slab_ec_monitoring.target: Slab EC should be within 0.5 of input EC.

ph_management_in_rockwool.buffering_behavior: Rockwool has minimal CEC (unlike coco). pH management is primarily about input pH and maintaining adequate runoff to prevent accumulation.

input_ph_range.notes: Rockwool tends to push pH up. Start lower than other media.

slab_ph_monitoring.if_slab_ph_high: Lower input pH by 0.2. Increase runoff % to flush alkaline solution out.
slab_ph_monitoring.if_slab_ph_low: Raise input pH by 0.2. Rare in rockwool — usually indicates over-acidification.
slab_ph_monitoring.method: Extract solution from slab using syringe. Measure pH of extracted solution.

water_content_management.concept: Rockwool has predictable water-holding capacity. Managing saturation % is key to crop steering.

measuring_saturation.sensor_method: Capacitance-based substrate sensor (Grodan GroSens, Teros 10). Most accurate.
measuring_saturation.visual_method: Lift slab edge — heavy = saturated. Light color on exposed rockwool = drying. Least accurate.
measuring_saturation.weight_method: Weigh fully saturated slab (100%). Weigh bone-dry slab (0%). Calculate current % by weight.

flower_maintenance.notes: Balanced. Not stressed but not vegetative.

generative_steering.notes: Allow dry-back to trigger flowering response.

propagation.notes: High saturation for root initiation. Humidity dome supplementing.

steering_with_rockwool.transition: Shift from vegetative to generative schedule over 3-5 days. Don't shock the plant with sudden change.

steps: Soak cubes/slabs in pH 5.5 nutrient solution (EC 0.5) for 24 hours
Drain and squeeze gently — do NOT wring out (damages fiber structure)
Check runoff pH — should be 5.5-6.0. If still high, soak again.
conditioning.why: Rockwool is naturally alkaline (pH 7.5-8.0). Must be soaked in pH 5.5 solution for 24h before use.

substrate_management.disposal: Rockwool is NOT biodegradable. Do not compost. Landfill or repurpose as insulation/drainage material.

dryback.description: Dryback % = how much the pot weight decreases between feedings. The #1 crop steering tool in coco.
dryback.how_to_measure: Weigh pot immediately after feeding (saturated weight) and before next feeding (dry weight). Dryback% = (saturated - dry) / saturated x 100
dryback.never_let_coco_dry_fully: Unlike soil, coco should NEVER fully dry out. Dry coco becomes hydrophobic (repels water), concentrates salts to toxic levels, and kills beneficial microbes.

danger_zone.effect: Salt concentration dangerous, anaerobic conditions possible, hydrophobic surface

vegetative_steering.effect: Keeps substrate wet = vegetative growth (leaves, stems)

irrigation.automation: Automated drip/pump timers make high-frequency fertigation practical. Manual watering 5x/day is unsustainable — invest in automation.

flush.notes: Flush with plain water for 3-7 days

late_veg.notes: Roots filling pot = more frequent feeds

mid_flower.notes: Maximum water/nutrient demand. High frequency essential.

seedling.notes: Keep coco moist but not soaked

irrigation.principle: In coco, every watering is a feeding. More frequent smaller feedings = better results than infrequent large ones.

runoff.description: Input EC vs Runoff EC is the #1 diagnostic tool in coco growing.

interpretation.runoff_ec_higher_than_input: Salt accumulation in media. Solutions: increase runoff percentage, do a flush feed, reduce input EC.
interpretation.runoff_ec_lower_than_input: Plant is eating more than you're providing. Solution: increase input EC.
interpretation.runoff_ec_matches_input: Perfect balance — nutrients in = nutrients consumed. Ideal state.

runoff.when_to_flush: Runoff EC is >0.5 higher than input EC consistently, or visible salt buildup on pot surface.

issues.cause: Slab pH too high — conditioning was insufficient
issues.fix: Check runoff pH. Re-condition: flush with pH 5.0 solution at 5x slab volume. Let sit 8 hours. Retest. This is the #1 rockwool problem.

issues.cause: Lime in rockwool slowly raising pH
issues.fix: Lower input pH to 5.5. Flush periodically with pH 5.0 solution. Normal in rockwool — requires ongoing management.

issues.cause: Dryback too low — still steering vegetative in flower
issues.fix: Increase overnight dryback to 20-25%. Delay first shot. Run slab drier.

issues.cause: Dryback too aggressive — slab too dry overnight
issues.fix: Reduce dryback target. Add a late-afternoon shot. Never let slab weight below 40%.
issues.symptom: Wilt/droop in morning before first shot

issues.cause: Slab weight dropped below 35% — oxygen stress and desiccation
issues.fix: EMERGENCY saturation event. Restore to field capacity. Treat with Hydroguard. Resume at higher slab weight target. Rockwool below 35% kills roots faster than any other medium.
issues.symptom: Root death (brown, slimy roots at cube-slab interface)

issues.cause: Drip stake placement wrong, slab tilted incorrectly, or drainage slits blocked
issues.fix: Check stake position (centered over plant). Ensure slight tilt toward drainage slits. Clear blocked slits.

issues.cause: Salt accumulation in slab from high-EC feeding with insufficient runoff
issues.fix: Increase shot volume for more runoff (target 20%+). Or flush with low-EC solution.
issues.symptom: Runoff EC climbing despite good input

issues.cause: Salt lockout — concentrated salts blocking uptake
issues.fix: Flush with low-EC solution (EC 0.2) until runoff matches input. Resume at 80% previous EC.

issues.cause: Light reaching wet rockwool
issues.fix: Cover ALL exposed rockwool with opaque material. No light on rockwool. Algae hosts pests and competes for nutrients.

cons: RO system cost and waste water
water_source_profiles.name: Reverse Osmosis (RO) Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Gold standard for precision growing. Always add CalMag first when using RO.
pros: Blank slate — total control over mineral content

dechlorination.chloramine: Does NOT gas off — must use Campden tablets or activated carbon filter
dechlorination.chlorine: Let sit uncovered 24 hours (gasses off) or use dechlorinator drops
dechlorination.how_to_tell: Check city water report. Most modern cities use chloramine.

water_source_profiles.name: Municipal Tap Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Good enough for most grows. Dechlorinate and pH-adjust. Get a water report from your city.

cons: May contain iron, sulfur, or heavy metals
