water_source_profiles.name: Well Water
water_source_profiles.notes: MUST get a water test before using. High iron or sulfur can cause lockout. Great if your well has clean water with moderate minerals.

water_source_profiles.name: Distilled Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Same as RO but purchased instead of filtered. Only practical for 1-2 plant grows.

advanced_techniques.description: Elevating CO2 from ambient (400ppm) to 1000-1500ppm increases photosynthesis rate by 20-40% when light and temperature are also increased.

methods.notes: Most common. Tank refills every 2-4 weeks for a tent.
Produces heat and humidity — better for large rooms with AC.
Produces ~100-200ppm boost. Only useful for small sealed spaces. Not worth it for most.

advanced_techniques.name: CO2 Supplementation

ppfd_co2_correlation.notes: Ambient CO2 sufficient at low light
Slight benefit from supplementation
Moderate benefit — cost-effective threshold
Strong benefit — diminishing returns above this
Maximum practical benefit for most grows
Commercial intensity — requires perfect environment

requirements: Sealed grow space (CO2 escapes quickly from leaky rooms)
High light intensity (>600 PPFD — plants can't use extra CO2 without sufficient light)
Higher temperature tolerance (82-86°F optimal with CO2 vs 75-80°F without)
CO2 controller or timer

advanced_techniques.description: Manipulating environmental and irrigation parameters to push the plant toward vegetative or generative (flowering/fruiting) growth.
advanced_techniques.name: Crop Steering
advanced_techniques.notes: Crop steering is most effective in hydro/coco where irrigation frequency is highly controllable. Soil growers have less precision due to soil buffering.

vegetative_steering.goal: More leaf/stem growth, larger plant framework

best_practices: Spray during lights-off or first/last 30 minutes of light cycle (stomata open, no light burn)
Use fine mist sprayer — coverage is key
pH foliar solution to 6.2-6.5 (leaf surface slightly higher pH than root zone)
EC should be very low (0.2-0.5) — leaves burn easily
Never foliar spray during flower (moisture in buds → mold/bud rot)
Best for micronutrient deficiency correction (CalMag, iron, magnesium)

common_foliar_products.frequency: Weekly preventive, 3-day intervals for active infestations

advanced_techniques.description: Spraying nutrient solution directly onto leaves for rapid uptake. Bypasses root zone entirely.
advanced_techniques.name: Foliar Feeding

advanced_techniques.description: Strengthens cell walls, increases stem thickness, improves heat/drought/pest resistance.
important_notes: ALWAYS add silica to water FIRST before other nutrients (raises pH significantly)
Let silica mix for 5-10 minutes before adding base nutrients
Silica is incompatible with concentrated nutrient solutions — never mix undiluted
Discontinue in late flower (last 2-3 weeks)
advanced_techniques.name: Silica (Potassium Silicate)

advanced_techniques.description: Introduces beneficial bacteria/fungi to the root zone to outcompete pathogens, improve nutrient uptake, and protect against root diseases.
important_notes: Do NOT use hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) with beneficial microbes — it kills them
Choose either sterile (H2O2) OR beneficial (Hydroguard) approach — not both
Beneficials need warmish water (65-75°F) to thrive
Reapply after any reservoir change or system cleaning
advanced_techniques.name: Beneficial Microbes & Root Inoculants

products.name: Hydroguard
products.notes: The standard for DWC/RDWC. Add at every res change.

products.dosing: 1 tsp/gal (water-in) or dust roots at transplant
products.name: Great White
products.notes: Best for soil/coco. Less effective in pure hydro.

products.name: Recharge
products.notes: Feeds existing soil microbes. Great for living soil.

products.name: Mammoth P
products.notes: Most effective in flower when P demand is highest.

advanced_techniques.description: Removing select fan leaves to improve light penetration, airflow, and direct energy to bud sites.
advanced_techniques.name: Defoliation (Strategic Leaf Removal)
rules: Never remove more than 20-30% of leaves at once (except schwazze technique)
Only defoliate healthy, vigorous plants — never stressed ones
Autoflowers: very light defoliation only — they can't recover from heavy removal
Always leave the top 2-3 fan leaves on each branch (they feed the bud below)

timing.method: Remove lower leaves that get no light (lollipop)
Major defoliation day 1 and day 21 of flower (schwazzing)
Selective tuck or remove leaves blocking bud sites

equipment.name: Quality potting soil
equipment.notes: Fox Farm Ocean Forest, Happy Frog, BuildASoil 3.0, or homemade super soil. Quality soil is the foundation.

equipment.name: Perlite (for mixing)
equipment.notes: Add 20-30% perlite to heavy soils for drainage. Not needed in already-aerated mixes.

equipment.name: Compost / worm castings
equipment.notes: Organic track: essential for soil biology. Top dress and tea ingredient.

equipment.name: Dry amendments (organic)
equipment.notes: Neem meal, kelp meal, alfalfa meal, bone meal, bat guano, langbeinite. For top dressing. Organic track.

equipment.name: Solo cups for seedlings
equipment.notes: Drill drain holes.

equipment.name: 1-3 gallon intermediate pots
equipment.notes: Intermediate step. Fabric or plastic.

equipment.name: 3-7 gallon final pots (fabric preferred)
equipment.notes: Fabric pots for air pruning. 3-gal autos, 5-gal standard, 7-gal large photos.

equipment.name: Saucers / drip trays
equipment.notes: Catch runoff. Don't let pots sit in standing water.

equipment.name: Watering can
equipment.notes: For hand watering. Soil doesn't need pumps or drip systems at home scale.

equipment.name: pH meter
equipment.notes: Less critical in organic (soil self-buffers) but essential for synthetic track. Soil pH: 6.0-7.0.

equipment.name: EC/TDS meter
equipment.notes: Useful for synthetic track. Less relevant for organic (teas have low EC but high nutrition).

equipment.name: Jeweler's loupe (60-100x)
equipment.notes: Trichome inspection.

equipment.name: Grow light (LED preferred)
equipment.notes: LED or HPS.

equipment.name: Exhaust fan + carbon filter
equipment.notes: Odor control.

equipment.name: Oscillating fan(s)
equipment.notes: Airflow.

equipment.name: Temperature/humidity controller
equipment.notes: Climate control.

equipment.name: Dehumidifier
equipment.notes: Essential in flower.

equipment.name: Base nutrient system (synthetic track)
equipment.notes: GH Flora Trio or equivalent. Only for synthetic track. Organic track uses soil amendments.

equipment.name: pH Up/Down
equipment.notes: pH 6.0-7.0 for soil.

equipment.name: Compost tea brewing kit
equipment.notes: 5-gallon bucket + air pump + air stone + compost + molasses. For organic track.

equipment.name: Mycorrhizae inoculant
equipment.notes: Apply directly to roots at transplant. Xtreme Gardening Mykos or equivalent.

equipment.name: Yellow sticky traps
equipment.notes: Fungus gnat monitoring. Soil grows WILL have gnat pressure — be ready.

equipment.name: BTI (Mosquito Bits)
equipment.notes: Add to water for fungus gnat larvae control. The #1 soil pest defense.

equipment.name: Diatomaceous earth
equipment.notes: Dust on soil surface for gnat control.

equipment.name: Neem oil
equipment.notes: Foliar spray for mites, aphids.

flush_timing.coco: 7-14 days reduced nutrients → plain water last 3-5 days
flush_timing.notes: Flushing is debated — some studies show no difference. At minimum, reduce nutrients in final week.
flush_timing.soil: 14-21 days plain water (soil holds nutrients longer)

other_harvest_indicators: Most pistils (hairs) have darkened and curled in
Calyxes are swollen and dense
Fan leaves are yellowing and falling off naturally
Trichome stalks are visible and heads are enlarged
Breeder's recommended flowering time has passed

trichome_ratios.notes: Harvest early for more THC-dominant, less CBN. Energetic, racy high. Slight reduction in yield.
Standard harvest window. Mix of THC and CBN. Full-spectrum effects.
Extended harvest for max CBN conversion. Heavy body high. Good for pain/sleep. Slightly degraded THC.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Micro → Gro → Bloom (always Micro first)
nutrient_brands.name: General Hydroponics Flora Trio

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Part A → Epsom → Part B (dissolve each fully before adding next)
nutrient_brands.name: Jack's 321
nutrient_brands.notes: Jack's 321 is 3.6g Part A, 2.4g Cal Nit, 1.2g Epsom per gallon at full strength. Extremely cost-effective for large grows.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Core → Bloom
nutrient_brands.name: Athena Pro Line
nutrient_brands.notes: Commercial-grade. Simple 2-part with integrated CalMag. Popular in commercial cannabis facilities.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: A → B (separate for veg and bloom)
nutrient_brands.name: Advanced Nutrients pH Perfect (Sensi)
nutrient_brands.notes: Self-adjusting pH technology (claims no pH adjustment needed). Expensive but popular for beginners who struggle with pH.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Shake VERY well before use (thick concentrate settles). One bottle at a time.
nutrient_brands.name: General Hydroponics FloraNova
nutrient_brands.notes: Simplest option — one bottle per phase. Great for beginners. Shake well — concentrate separates.
nutrient_brands.type: 1-part (two bottles — one for veg, one for bloom)

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Dissolve powder in warm water, stir well
nutrient_brands.name: Greenleaf Nutrients Mega Crop
nutrient_brands.notes: One powder does everything — includes CalMag. Incredibly cost-effective at ~$0.02/gal. Community favorite on r/microgrowery.

environment.container: Glass mason jars (quart size). Fill 75% full.

humidity_packs.boveda_58: For drier preference or long-term storage.
humidity_packs.boveda_62: Maintains 62% RH. Set-and-forget. Replace when crispy.
humidity_packs.grove_bags: Heat-sealed bags with built-in humidity regulation. No burping needed.

indicators_of_good_cure: Smooth, flavorful smoke — no harshness or hay smell
Buds are slightly sticky but not wet
Strong terpene aroma when jar is opened
Ash burns to white/light gray (not black)
method: Trim buds to final shape (dry trim or finish wet trim)
Place in mason jars — fill 3/4 full to allow air exchange
Week 1: burp 2-3x daily for 10-15 minutes (release moisture and gases)
Week 2-4: burp 1x daily for 5-10 minutes
Month 2+: burp 1x weekly or seal completely
Cure for minimum 2 weeks, ideal is 4-8 weeks. Some strains improve for 6+ months.

common_mistakes: Drying too fast (>3 days) — harsh smoke, hay smell, terpene loss
Drying too slow (>14 days) — mold risk, diminishing returns
Fan blowing directly on buds — uneven drying, crispy exterior/moist interior
Temperature too high (>70°F) — terpene evaporation, harsh taste

environment.airflow: Gentle circulation — fan NOT pointed at buds. Just keep air moving.

method: Cut branches 12-18 inches long
Optionally remove large fan leaves (wet trim) or leave intact (dry trim)
Hang upside down on line/rack with 6+ inches between branches
Small branches should snap (not bend) when done — 5-7 days minimum
Stems should snap cleanly; if they bend, keep drying

long_term_storage.degradation: THC slowly converts to CBN over time. Light, heat, air, and moisture accelerate degradation.
long_term_storage.method: Sealed mason jars or vacuum-sealed bags with Boveda 58% in dark, cool place
long_term_storage.notes: Properly cured and stored cannabis can remain excellent for 12+ months. Freezing is debated — can damage trichomes if not done carefully.

ec_by_stage.description: EC targets for SYNTHETIC track only. Organic track doesn't use EC — teas have low EC but high nutrition.

golden_rules: Water when the pot is LIGHT. Skip when heavy. The wet/dry cycle is everything in soil.
Overwatering is the #1 soil mistake. Roots need oxygen. Let the soil dry.
Organic: feed the soil, not the plant. Synthetic: feed the plant directly.
Soil pH: 6.0-7.0. Higher than hydro. Soil naturally buffers.
Soil is slower than hydro. Nutrient response takes 2-3 days. Plan ahead.
Fungus gnats are a soil fact of life. BTI in every watering. Yellow sticky traps always.
Living soil gets BETTER with reuse. The biology strengthens each cycle.
Trust the process. Soil-grown flower often has superior terpenes and flavor.
Synthetic: feed every OTHER watering. Not every watering (that's coco).
The soil IS the nutrient (organic track). Good soil = easy grow.

organic_vs_synthetic.recommendation: For beginners: start synthetic (simpler feedback loop). For flavor chasers: go organic. For best of both: start organic soil, supplement with light synthetic if deficiency appears.

ph_range.notes: Higher than hydro (5.5-6.0). Soil buffers pH naturally — less drift than soilless media.

fungus_gnats.prevention: Let top inch dry. BTI in every watering. Yellow sticky traps.
fungus_gnats.treatment: BTI + diatomaceous earth + nematodes. Severe: hydrogen peroxide drench (3% H2O2, 1:4 ratio with water).

root_aphids.prevention: Quarantine new plants. Inspect roots at transplant.
root_aphids.treatment: Botanigard (Beauveria bassiana), beneficial nematodes (Stratiolaelaps), pyrethrin drench. Root aphids are the hardest soil pest to eliminate.

spider_mites.prevention: Keep humidity up in veg. Inspect undersides of leaves.
spider_mites.treatment: Neem oil, Spinosad, predatory mites (Phytoseiulus persimilis).

thrips.prevention: Blue sticky traps. Inspect new growth.

super_soil_recipe.base: 8 large bags (1.5 cu ft) high-quality base soil (Light Warrior, Roots Original)
super_soil_recipe.cooking: Mix thoroughly. Moisten. Cover. Let sit 30 days, turning weekly. Microbes need time to break down amendments.
super_soil_recipe.description: DIY super soil / water-only soil recipe. Mix and 'cook' (compost) for 30 days before use.
super_soil_recipe.usage: Place in bottom 1/3 of final pot. Top 2/3 with mild base soil. Roots grow down into hot soil as they mature. Water only — no additional feeding needed for 60-90 days.

watering_guide.description: THE fundamental soil skill: the wet/dry cycle.
watering_guide.how_much: Water until 10-15% runoff from the bottom. Stop. Wait until pot is light again.
watering_guide.overwatering_signs: Droopy dark green leaves, slow growth, fungus gnats
watering_guide.rule: Water when the pot is LIGHT. Skip when HEAVY. Lift to check weight.
watering_guide.underwatering_signs: Wilting, dry crispy leaves, pot extremely light

scale_tiers.description: First-time grower or single-plant hobbyist. Manual management, learning-focused.
scale_tiers.name: Solo / Single Plant
scale_tiers.notes: Hands-on learning experience. Manual checks 1-2x daily. Ideal for beginners.

scale_tiers.description: Hobbyist tent grow. Light automation helpful but not required.
scale_tiers.name: Small Tent (2-8 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Timer-controlled lights, humidity controller. Sensor monitoring recommended.

scale_tiers.description: Dedicated grower with multiple tents/rooms. Automation saves significant time.
scale_tiers.name: Multi-Tent (9-24 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Multiple environments to manage. Automated alerts essential. Consider perpetual harvest.

scale_tiers.description: Licensed commercial operation. Full automation, compliance tracking, batch management.
scale_tiers.name: Commercial Room (25-100 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: SOPs required. Staff training. Regulatory compliance. Insurance requirements.

scale_tiers.description: Large-scale production facility. Enterprise automation, SCADA integration, dedicated staff.
scale_tiers.name: Warehouse Scale (100+ plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Multiple rooms, dedicated HVAC, industrial nutrient dosing, video surveillance.

application.foliar_spray: Strain through 400-mesh. Apply at lights-off to prevent leaf burn. Covers both leaf surfaces.
application.frequency: Every 2 weeks during veg. Monthly during flower (avoid on buds).
application.root_drench: Apply within 4 hours of brewing (microbes die without aeration). Full soil saturation.

brewing.aeration: Continuous vigorous aeration (aquarium pump + large air stone). 400+ LPH air pump minimum.

duration_hours.notes: 24h minimum. 36h maximum. Beyond 36h anaerobic bacteria can develop.

brewing.signs_of_failure: Foul/rotten smell = anaerobic. Discard immediately. Do NOT apply to plants.
brewing.signs_of_success: Earthy smell. Light foam on surface. Slightly brown color.

aloe_vera.notes: Saponins break surface tension. Contains growth enzymes. Natural wetting agent.

fish_hydrolysate.notes: Nitrogen source + feeds bacteria. Can smell — outdoor or exhaust-filtered use.

kelp_meal.dose: 1 tbsp per gallon (tea) or top dress 2 tbsp per pot
kelp_meal.notes: Growth hormones + trace minerals. Feeds fungi.

molasses.notes: Feeds bacteria. Use unsulfured blackstrap molasses only.

beneficial_bacteria.application: Compost tea, microbial inoculants, or worm castings.
beneficial_bacteria.function: Fix nitrogen, solubilize phosphorus, produce growth hormones, suppress pathogens.
beneficial_bacteria.maintenance: Feed regularly with sugars/molasses to maintain populations.

mycorrhizae.application: Dust on roots at transplant. Mix into soil. DO NOT use with high-phosphorus synthetic nutrients (kills colonization).
mycorrhizae.function: Extends root network 100x. Trades phosphorus and micronutrients for plant sugars.

trichoderma.application: Mix into soil at potting. Apply as root drench monthly.
trichoderma.compatibility: Compatible with mycorrhizae. Incompatible with most fungicides.
trichoderma.function: Colonizes root zone. Outcompetes and attacks pathogens (Pythium, Fusarium). Promotes root growth.

between_grows: Chop plant at soil level (leave roots to decompose — feeds biology)
Remove any large stem pieces from surface
Top dress with fresh worm castings (1 inch layer)
Add cover crop seeds (clover, barley) to surface
Water and let soil 'cook' for 2-4 weeks before replanting
Plant directly into the living soil for next run
no_till_living_soil.concept: Build soil once. Never disturb it. Reuse indefinitely. The soil food web does all the work.

initial_soil_recipe_per_cubic_foot.base: 1/3 peat or coco, 1/3 aeration (pumice/perlite/rice hulls), 1/3 compost/worm castings
minerals: 1/2 cup oyster shell flour (calcium + pH buffer)

application_method: Scratch soil surface lightly (top 1/4 inch) — don't disturb roots
Cover with thin layer of worm castings or compost (1/4 inch)
Water in gently — amendments activate as water passes through
Mulch on top (straw, rice hulls) to retain moisture and protect biology

flower_top_dress.pk_ratio: Higher phosphorus and potassium for flower development.

top_dressing_protocol.what_is_top_dressing: Adding dry amendments to soil surface. Breaks down slowly over 2-4 weeks as you water through it.
top_dressing_protocol.when_to_top_dress: Every 3-4 weeks throughout the grow. Start 3-4 weeks after potting (amendments in initial mix running out).

common_mistakes: Watering on a schedule instead of by weight/feel (the #1 soil growing mistake)
Light frequent watering that only wets the top (trains roots upward, not down)
Never letting soil dry back (chronically overwatered = root rot, fungus gnats, slow growth)
Using pots without drainage holes
Compacted soil that won't absorb water (water runs down sides of rootball)

10_gallon_plus.notes: Very long dry-backs. Great for living soil no-till.

1_gallon.notes: Dries fast. Frequent watering. Easy to under-water.

5_gallon.notes: Standard for most indoor soil grows.

7_gallon.notes: Good for larger plants. Less frequent watering.

wet_dry_cycle_mastery.importance: The wet/dry cycle is the MOST important skill in soil growing. It drives root development and oxygen exchange.

dry_back.description: Soil drying. Roots stretching to find moisture. Oxygen returning to pore spaces.

field_capacity.description: Excess water drained. Soil holds maximum water against gravity. Ideal nutrient uptake.

ready_to_water.description: Top 1-2 inches dry. Pot noticeably lighter (50-60% of saturated weight). Slight leaf droop at end of day.

saturation.description: After watering. Soil fully wetted. Nutrients dissolving and available.

pot_weight_method.beginner_shortcut: Use a chopstick/dowel. Insert 2 inches. Pull out. If dry/clean = water. If moist/dark = wait.
pot_weight_method.description: The gold standard for knowing when to water in soil.
pot_weight_method.method: Lift pot when fully saturated (note weight). Lift daily. Water when pot is 50-60% of saturated weight.

compost_tea.description: Brewed aerated compost tea (ACT) is a microbial inoculant that boosts soil biology.
compost_tea.frequency: Every 2-4 weeks during veg and early flower.
compost_tea.method: Brew with air pump for 24-48 hours. Use immediately (microbes die without oxygen). Apply as soil drench.
compost_tea.notes: Only useful for organic/living soil grows. Synthetic nutrients kill the microbes in the tea.

soil_specific.description: Soil-specific management for traditional soil grows.

components: Base soil (1/3 peat or coco, 1/3 aeration, 1/3 compost/EWC)
Dry amendments (kelp meal, neem meal, crab meal, bone meal, rock dust)
Cover crop or mulch layer (straw, rice hulls)
living_soil.description: A self-sustaining soil ecosystem where you water only — the soil provides all nutrients through microbial activity.
living_soil.re_amend_schedule: Top-dress with dry amendments every 3-4 weeks. Let soil biology break down amendments into plant-available form.

cons: Harder to dial in for max yield
Can attract pests (gnats love organic matter)
organic.description: Feed the soil, not the plant. Build a living ecosystem of microbes that break down organic matter into plant-available nutrients.

synthetic.description: Feed the plant directly with mineral salts. Fast-acting, precise, measurable with EC meter.

top_dressing.description: Applying dry organic amendments to the soil surface. Microbes and watering break them down over 2-4 weeks.
top_dressing.schedule: Top-dress every 3-4 weeks. Adjust recipe based on growth stage (more N in veg, more P/K in flower).

wet_dry_cycle.description: Soil grows benefit from a wet/dry cycle — water thoroughly, then let the top 1-2 inches dry before watering again.
wet_dry_cycle.how_to_check: Lift the pot — heavy = still wet, light = time to water. Or finger test: top inch dry = water.
underwatering_signs: Dry/crusty soil pulling away from pot edges

common_problems.cause: Soil too wet (drowning), too cold, or planted too deep
common_problems.solution: Soil should be damp, not waterlogged. Ensure 75-80°F. Seed only 1/4 inch deep. 1/2 inch is too deep.

common_problems.cause: Too wet, poor airflow, Pythium or Fusarium in soil
common_problems.issue: Damping off (seedling falls over at soil line)
common_problems.solution: Reduce watering. Improve airflow. Use clean or sterilized soil. Inoculate with mycorrhizae.

stages.description: Seed cracks open and taproot emerges. Start seeds in Rapid Rooters, paper towels, or directly in pre-moistened soil in solo cups. Soil germination is the simplest — just plant the seed and keep it moist.

environment.notes: Seeds in darkness or very dim light. Heat mat at 78°F. Humidity dome.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse germination works with heat mat and dome.

greenhouse.notes: Heat mat recommended.

environment_overrides.notes: Start seeds indoors. Outdoor soil germination is unreliable.

outdoor.notes: Always start seeds indoors.

medium.notes: Pre-moisten soil before planting seed. Soil should be damp like a wrung-out sponge — not soaking, not dry. In solo cups, make sure there are drain holes. Soil temperature matters more than air temp for germination.

organic_track.description: Use pre-amended soil (Fox Farm Ocean Forest, BuildASoil, or homemade super soil). No nutrients needed — the soil is the nutrient.

synthetic_track.description: Use any quality potting mix (Fox Farm Happy Frog, ProMix HP). No nutrients yet — seeds don't need them.

stages.name: Germination

nutrients.approach: None — seeds contain all energy needed. Soil provides gentle background nutrition.

tasks.description: Pre-moisten soil. If using super soil: mix base soil layer + hot soil layer in final pot (hot soil on bottom, base on top — roots grow into nutrients). For solo cup starts: use mild base soil only (Ocean Forest or lighter).
tasks.name: Prepare soil

tasks.description: 1/4 inch deep in pre-moistened soil. Pointed end down. Cover lightly. Humidity dome.
tasks.name: Plant seed

tasks.description: Mist soil surface if drying. Don't overwater — soil germination's biggest risk is drowning the seed.
tasks.name: Check daily

common_problems.cause: Watering too often — soil hasn't dried between waterings
common_problems.solution: STOP watering. Let the soil dry until the pot is noticeably lighter. Soil needs to dry — roots need oxygen. This is the #1 soil mistake and the #1 beginner mistake in all of growing.

common_problems.cause: Soil surface staying wet — gnats breed in top 1 inch
common_problems.solution: Let top inch dry completely. Yellow sticky traps. BTI (Mosquito Bits) in water. Diatomaceous earth on soil surface. This is a soil-specific problem.

common_problems.cause: Light too far away or too dim
common_problems.solution: Lower light or increase intensity. Support stem with toothpick.

stages.description: First true leaves develop. Seedling in solo cup or small pot with mild soil. The WET/DRY cycle begins here — soil MUST dry between waterings. Lift the pot to check weight. Water when light (dry), skip when heavy (wet). This is the fundamental soil skill.

environment.notes: Gentle light. Remove humidity dome gradually over 3-5 days. Seedlings in soil need LESS watering than coco or hydro — the soil holds moisture longer.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: watch for heat drying.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse: good environment for soil seedlings.

environment_overrides.notes: Outdoor: harden off before placing outside.

outdoor.notes: Harden seedlings before outdoor placement.

medium.notes: THE RULE: water when the pot is light, skip when heavy. Soil seedlings need to dry between waterings — overwatering is the #1 soil mistake. Root development is BETTER in a wet/dry cycle: roots grow searching for water during the dry period. Water in a small ring around the stem, not the entire soil surface.

organic_track.description: In pre-amended soil, the soil IS the food. No additional feeding for the first 2-4 weeks. Just water. This is the beauty of soil.

inputs.name: Mycorrhizae inoculant
inputs.purpose: Establish beneficial fungal network. Must contact roots directly.

synthetic_track.description: In mild potting mix, soil has 2-4 weeks of base nutrition. No feeding yet unless leaves are pale.

medium.watering_method: circle pour around stem, water when pot is light

stages.name: Seedling

nutrients.approach: None for either track — soil provides base nutrition. If leaves yellow early, add 1/4 strength (synthetic track only).

tasks.description: Lift pot daily. Heavy = wet, skip. Light = dry, water. THE most important soil skill. Soil should dry ~1 inch deep between waterings.
tasks.name: Learn the wet/dry cycle

tasks.description: When watering: pour slowly in a ring around the stem. Stop when you see runoff from the bottom. Do NOT water every day — only when the pot is light.
tasks.name: Water correctly

tasks.description: Crack dome day 1, remove for hours day 2, full remove by day 3-5.
tasks.name: Remove humidity dome gradually

tasks.description: Moist soil surface = fungus gnat breeding ground. Let top inch dry completely between waterings. Yellow sticky traps near pots.
tasks.name: Watch for fungus gnats

common_problems.cause: Watering the entire soil volume when roots only occupy a fraction
common_problems.solution: Water only the root zone initially. As roots expand, gradually water more of the pot. A 5-gallon pot with a solo-cup-sized root ball should NOT be fully drenched.

common_problems.cause: Soil's base nutrition depleted (3-4 weeks in mild mixes)
common_problems.solution: Synthetic: begin 1/4 strength feeding. Organic: top dress and/or compost tea.

common_problems.cause: Top inch of soil staying moist, organic matter in soil
common_problems.solution: BTI (Mosquito Bits) in EVERY watering. Yellow sticky traps. Let top inch go fully dry. Diatomaceous earth. Nematodes (Stratiolaelaps scimitus) for biological control.

common_problems.cause: Normal — soil is slower than hydro/coco
common_problems.solution: Expected. Soil grows run 1-2 weeks longer in veg. The tradeoff is simpler management and often better terpene profiles.

stages.description: Rapid growth begins. Transplant from solo cup to 1-3 gallon pot. Then transplant to final pot (3-7 gallon) when roots fill intermediate pot. Soil grows run longer in veg than hydro — soil is slower but produces different plant structure. Begin feeding at 3-4 weeks (synthetic track) or begin teas/top dress (organic track).

environment.notes: Ramp up light. Soil grows are slightly slower than hydro but produce robust plants.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse soil: excellent. Watch drying rate in warm conditions.

greenhouse.notes: Greenhouse is great for soil.

environment_overrides.notes: See outdoor_soil config for outdoor-specific guidance.

outdoor.notes: Outdoor soil is a separate grow type.

medium.notes: Wet/dry cycle continues — more critical as pots get larger. Larger pots take longer to dry. Water only when pot is noticeably lighter. Overwatering in large soil pots is the most common veg mistake. ORGANIC TRACK: feed the soil, not the plant. SYNTHETIC TRACK: feed every other watering (not every watering like coco).

organic_track.description: In super soil or living soil: the soil feeds the plant. Supplemental inputs are teas and top dressings that feed the SOIL BIOLOGY, not the plant directly. The microbes make nutrients plant-available.

inputs.name: Compost tea (aerated)
inputs.purpose: Feeds microbial population. Brew 24-48 hours with compost + molasses + air pump.

inputs.name: Top dress (dry amendments)
inputs.purpose: Slow-release nutrition. Neem meal, kelp meal, alfalfa meal, bone meal. Scratch into top inch, water in. Takes 1-2 weeks to break down.

inputs.name: Mycorrhizae
inputs.purpose: Extends root reach. Mycorrhizal fungi create a secondary root network.

synthetic_track.description: Begin bottled nutrients when soil's base nutrition runs out (usually 3-4 weeks). Start at 1/4 strength, ramp to 1/2. Feed every other watering — alternate feed/water/feed/water.

medium.watering_method: full pot drench when light, wait until dry

stages.name: Early Vegetative

nutrients.approach: Synthetic track: 1/4 strength every other watering. Organic track: teas and top dress. Soil pH: 6.0-7.0.

supplements.name: Mycorrhizae
supplements.purpose: Apply directly to roots at transplant. Not a liquid nutrient.

tasks.description: When roots fill solo cup, transplant to 1-3 gallon pot. Handle root ball gently. Don't break roots. If using super soil: put hot soil on bottom, mild base soil on top.
tasks.name: Transplant to intermediate pot

tasks.description: Larger pots = longer dry-down time. May go 3-5 days between waterings in a 3-gallon pot. This is NORMAL in soil.
tasks.name: Continue wet/dry cycle

tasks.description: Synthetic: start at 1/4 strength when lower leaves lighten. Organic: first compost tea at week 3-4. Top dress every 2-3 weeks.
tasks.name: Begin feeding (if needed)

tasks.description: When roots fill intermediate pot (drying in 2-3 days), transplant to final 3-7 gallon pot.
tasks.name: Transplant to final pot

tasks.description: LST, topping at 4-5 nodes. Soil plants recover from training slower than hydro — allow extra time.
tasks.name: Begin training

tasks.description: Yellow sticky traps. BTI in every watering. Let top inch dry. This is ongoing in soil.
tasks.name: Fungus gnat prevention

training.description: Bend and tie. Start at 4-5 nodes.
Cut above 4th-5th node. Recovery 3-5 days in soil (slower than hydro).

common_problems.cause: Roots completely fill the pot — root-bound
common_problems.solution: If still in veg: transplant to larger pot. If ready to flip: accept it and flip. Root-bound plants drink fast but grow slow.

common_problems.cause: Soil pH drifting from synthetic fertilizer salts
common_problems.solution: Check runoff pH. Should be 6.0-7.0. If low: flush with pH 6.8 water. Organic track rarely has pH issues — soil biology self-regulates.

stages.description: Plant reaches target size in final pot. Full veg feeding on either track. Soil grows have a longer veg than hydro but build robust root systems. Pre-flip: no flush needed in soil (unlike hydro) — just reduce nitrogen in the last feeding before flip.

environment.notes: Full veg intensity. Plant transpiring well. Soil drying faster as root system expands.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse soil: excellent.

greenhouse.notes: Great for soil late veg.

environment_overrides.notes: See outdoor_soil config.

outdoor.notes: Outdoor soil: separate config.

medium.notes: The plant should be drinking the entire pot volume now. Full drenches to runoff. In a 5-gallon fabric pot, expect 3-5 day wet/dry cycles. If the pot takes more than 5 days to dry, the plant is underwatered or root-bound. Pre-flip: last top dress should be bloom-oriented (bone meal, bat guano) for organic. Synthetic: reduce Gro in last feeding.

organic_track.description: Peak organic feeding. Top dress every 2 weeks with veg amendments (high N: alfalfa, neem, kelp). Weekly compost tea. The soil biology should be fully established now — you're feeding the microbes, they feed the plant.

inputs.name: Top dress (veg blend)
inputs.purpose: Alfalfa meal + neem meal + kelp meal. Scratch in, water in.

inputs.name: Malted barley top dress
inputs.purpose: Enzymes that support nutrient cycling. Unique to organic soil.

synthetic_track.description: Half to three-quarter strength. Feed every other watering. Water/feed/water/feed. Never feed every watering in soil — the soil holds nutrients longer than coco.

medium.watering_method: full pot drench to 10-15% runoff when light

stages.name: Late Vegetative

nutrients.approach: Synthetic track: half strength every other watering. Organic track: soil biology does the work. pH: 6.2-6.8.

tasks.description: Full drench when light. Wait until dry. 3-5 day cycles typical in 5-gal pots.
tasks.name: Establish full wet/dry cycle

tasks.description: Synthetic: every other watering. Organic: weekly tea + biweekly top dress.
tasks.name: Feed on schedule

tasks.description: Complete topping, LST, SCROG filling.
tasks.name: Final training

tasks.description: Defoliate lower growth. Lollipop lower 1/3.
tasks.name: Canopy management

tasks.description: Organic: final top dress with bloom amendments (bone meal, bat guano). Synthetic: reduce Gro in last feeding.
tasks.name: Pre-flip preparation

training.description: Install and fill 70-80% before flip.
Remove lower 1/3.

common_problems.cause: Too much nitrogen at flip — soil holds nitrogen longer than hydro
common_problems.issue: Nitrogen excess during transition (dark green, clawing)
common_problems.solution: Reduce or stop N. Soil buffers nutrients — excess N takes 1-2 weeks to clear. Flush if severe.

common_problems.cause: Light leaks, or residual nitrogen pushing veg growth
common_problems.solution: Check for ANY light during dark period. Even indicator LEDs can disrupt flowering.

stages.description: Flipped to 12/12. Stretch phase. Soil plants stretch less aggressively than hydro but still double in height for some cultivars. Transition nutrient ratio from veg to bloom. Organic track: bloom top dress should be breaking down now.

environment.notes: Flip to 12/12. DIF of 10°F helps control stretch.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: light dep for flip control.

greenhouse.notes: Light dep recommended.

environment_overrides.notes: See outdoor_soil config.

outdoor.notes: Separate config.

medium.notes: Wet/dry cycle continues. Plant drinking more during stretch. Pots may dry faster (every 2-3 days now). This is good — the plant is transpiring heavily. Don't fight the faster cycle by watering less — just water to saturation when dry.

organic_track.description: Bloom top dress (applied pre-flip) should be breaking down. Continue weekly compost tea. Add bloom-specific inputs.

inputs.name: Compost tea (bloom)
inputs.purpose: Continue feeding soil biology. Can add bat guano to tea for PK.

inputs.application: If not done pre-flip, do now
inputs.name: Bloom top dress
inputs.purpose: Bone meal, bat guano, kelp. Scratch in, water in.

synthetic_track.description: Transition ratio: reduce Gro, increase Bloom. Feed every other watering at 3/4 strength.

medium.watering_method: full drench to runoff when pot is light

stages.name: Transition (Stretch)

nutrients.approach: Transition ratio. Reduce N, increase PK. Synthetic: every other watering. Organic: soil biology handles the transition naturally.

tasks.description: Zero light leaks during dark period.
tasks.name: Flip to 12/12

tasks.description: Synthetic: shift ratio over 5-7 days. Organic: bloom top dress + bloom tea.
tasks.name: Transition nutrients

tasks.description: Stretch increases water uptake. Pots dry faster. Water when light — don't skip because 'it hasn't been 3 days yet.'
tasks.name: Water to demand

tasks.description: Supercrop, tuck into SCROG.
tasks.name: Manage stretch

tasks.description: Watch nodes. Remove males.
tasks.name: Check for preflowers/sex

training.description: Bend tall stems.
Daily tucking.

common_problems.cause: Normal for soil — slower than hydro but often denser/terper final product
common_problems.solution: Patience. Soil-grown flower develops differently. Trust the timeline.

common_problems.cause: Soil-dwelling pest — more common in soil than any other method
common_problems.issue: Root aphids (white insects on roots/soil surface)
common_problems.solution: BTI won't work on root aphids. Use Botanigard (Beauveria bassiana) as a drench. Nematodes (Stratiolaelaps). Severe: pyrethrin drench. Root aphids are the most devastating soil pest.

common_problems.cause: Synthetic salts accumulating in soil
common_problems.solution: Check runoff pH. If below 6.0: flush with pH 6.8 water. Organic track almost never has this issue.

stages.description: Buds forming. Full bloom feeding on either track. Soil-grown flower develops terpenes differently — many growers consider soil-grown flower to have superior flavor/aroma. Continue wet/dry cycle.

environment.notes: Peak light. Low humidity. CO2 1000-1200 ppm if available.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: manage humidity carefully.

greenhouse.notes: Humidity control critical.

environment_overrides.notes: See outdoor_soil config.

outdoor.notes: Separate config.

medium.notes: Wet/dry cycle continues. Flower development in soil is steady but not as fast as hydro — buds develop density over a longer period. Trust the process. Organic soil: the microbial network is delivering a broader spectrum of minerals than any bottle can provide.

organic_track.description: Peak organic bloom feeding. Top dress + teas. Some growers add compost worm castings as a top mulch layer.

inputs.name: Bloom top dress
inputs.purpose: Bat guano (high P), bone meal, langbeinite (0-0-22). Scratch in, water in.

inputs.name: Compost tea (bloom-boosted)
inputs.purpose: Add bat guano or bloom nutrients to tea. Feeds biology + provides PK.

inputs.name: Worm castings mulch
inputs.purpose: Slow-release nutrition + moisture retention + microbial activity.

synthetic_track.description: Full bloom strength. Feed every other watering. High PK. EC 1.8-2.2 for input solution.

medium.watering_method: full drench to 10-15% runoff when pot is light

stages.name: Early Flower

nutrients.approach: Full bloom. Heavy PK. Synthetic: every other watering at 3/4 strength. Organic: soil biology + teas + top dress.

supplements.name: Liquid Kool Bloom (synthetic track)
supplements.purpose: PK booster.

tasks.description: Synthetic: 3/4 strength every other watering. Organic: bloom top dress + weekly bloom tea.
tasks.name: Full bloom feeding

tasks.description: Remove fan leaves blocking bud sites. Open canopy interior.
tasks.name: Defoliate for airflow

tasks.description: Soil grows can have higher humidity at soil surface — check plant base too.
tasks.name: Check for bud rot / PM

tasks.description: Water to runoff when pot is light. Don't over- or under-water during flower.
tasks.name: Continue wet/dry cycle

tasks.description: Fungus gnats, root aphids, spider mites. Soil grows have more pest pressure than hydro.
tasks.name: Monitor for pests

training.description: Remove blocking fan leaves. Max 20% at once.
Remove lower 1/3.

common_problems.cause: Synthetic: too strong. Organic: too much top dress breaking down at once
common_problems.solution: Synthetic: reduce strength. Organic: flush with plain water once, then resume teas only (no top dress for 2 weeks).

common_problems.cause: Humidity + density
common_problems.solution: Remove affected buds + 1 inch margin. Lower humidity. Improve airflow. Defoliate.

stages.description: Peak bud development. Maximum bloom feeding. Soil-grown buds may be denser and terper than hydro at this stage. Continue wet/dry cycle — don't overwater chasing bigger buds. The soil food web is at full capacity delivering a complex mineral profile.

environment.notes: Peak light. Low humidity. CO2 1200-1500 ppm if available.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: dehumidification maximum.

greenhouse.notes: Humidity control.

environment_overrides.notes: See outdoor_soil config.

outdoor.notes: Separate config.

medium.notes: Wet/dry cycle unchanged. Don't increase watering frequency to 'push' buds — soil doesn't work that way. The wet/dry cycle is what drives root health and microbial activity. More water ≠ bigger buds in soil.

organic_track.description: Continue bloom tea + top dress. The soil is at peak biological activity.

synthetic_track.description: Full bloom at 75-100% strength. Feed every other watering. PK booster.

medium.watering_method: full drench to 10-15% runoff when pot is light

stages.name: Mid Flower (Peak Bloom)

nutrients.approach: Peak bloom. High PK. Synthetic: 85% every other watering. Organic: soil biology at peak.

supplements.name: Liquid Kool Bloom (synthetic)
supplements.purpose: PK booster at peak.

tasks.description: Synthetic: 85% every other watering + PK booster. Organic: top dress + bloom tea.
tasks.name: Peak bloom feeding

tasks.description: Soil-grown buds can be dense. Check daily.
tasks.name: Daily bud rot inspection

tasks.description: Trellis, yo-yos, stakes.
tasks.name: Support heavy branches

tasks.description: Don't change what's working.
tasks.name: Continue wet/dry cycle

common_problems.cause: pH lockout or root issue, not natural fade
common_problems.solution: Check: gradual yellowing from bottom up = good. Sudden browning or spotting = problem.

stages.description: Buds ripening. Fan leaf yellowing is natural and desired. Reduce feeding. Organic track: stop top dressing, continue light teas. Synthetic: reduce strength.

environment.notes: Cooler nights for color. Low humidity.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: maintain control.

greenhouse.notes: Continue climate control.

environment_overrides.notes: See outdoor_soil config.

outdoor.notes: Separate config.

medium.notes: Plant drinking less. Wet/dry cycle may extend to 4-6 days. This is normal — the plant is finishing. Don't increase watering. Fan leaf yellowing = natural nutrient fade.

organic_track.description: Stop top dressing. Light compost tea only. Let the plant consume stored nutrients and soil reserves. Natural senescence.

inputs.name: Light compost tea (no amendments)
inputs.purpose: Keep biology alive but don't push nutrition.

synthetic_track.description: Reduce to 50% strength. Still feed every other watering. Tapering toward flush.

medium.watering_method: full drench when light, plant drinking less

stages.name: Late Flower (Ripening)

nutrients.approach: Reducing. Synthetic: 50% every other watering. Organic: light tea only.

tasks.description: Synthetic: 50%. Organic: teas only, no top dress.
tasks.name: Reduce feeding

tasks.description: 60-100x loupe. Track milky → amber.
tasks.name: Trichome checks

tasks.description: Daily.
tasks.name: Continue bud rot inspection

tasks.description: Soil flush debate: organic growers often don't flush at all (soil already clean). Synthetic: flush 7-14 days before harvest.
tasks.name: Plan flush timing

common_problems.cause: Soil holds salts more than coco/rockwool
common_problems.issue: Soil not flushing (runoff EC stays high)
common_problems.solution: More volume per flush. Soil flushes slower than soilless media — allow 10-14 days.

stages.description: Pre-harvest flush. THE GREAT DEBATE: organic soil growers often skip the flush entirely (the soil is already 'clean' — no synthetic salts to flush). Synthetic track: flush with plain water for 7-14 days. The plant consumes stored nutrients either way.

environment.notes: Reduced light.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse: maintain control.

greenhouse.notes: Continue.

environment_overrides.notes: See outdoor_soil config.

outdoor.notes: Separate config.

medium.notes: Soil flush debate: organic soil often doesn't need flushing (no synthetic salts). Synthetic soil should be flushed. The plant will consume stored nutrients either way — flush just removes excess salts from the root zone. Continue wet/dry cycle during flush.

organic_track.description: OPTIONAL for organic. Many organic growers just water normally — the soil has no synthetic salts to flush. If you want to flush: plain water for the final 7 days.

synthetic_track.description: Plain pH'd water only. No nutrients. Water to 20-30% runoff. Flush until runoff EC drops below 0.5.

medium.watering_method: plain water to runoff when pot is light

stages.name: Flush (Pre-Harvest)

nutrients.approach: Plain pH'd water. No nutrients. pH 6.5.

tasks.description: Daily.

common_problems.cause: Hidden rot
common_problems.solution: Cut away + 1 inch. Salvage clean buds.

stages.description: Chop day. Spent soil can be recycled — add to compost, re-amend and reuse (organic), or dispose (synthetic). Living soil can be reused indefinitely with re-amending.

environment.notes: Optional 24-48 hours darkness before chop.

environment_overrides.notes: N/A.

health_checks: No bud rot found during trim?

medium.notes: Organic living soil gets BETTER with reuse. The mycorrhizal network, beneficial bacteria, and fungal hyphae persist and strengthen. This is the organic advantage — your soil improves every cycle.

organic_track.description: Spent living soil is REUSABLE. Re-amend with compost, dry amendments, and worm castings. Let 'cook' for 2-4 weeks. Indefinitely reusable — the biology only gets better with age.

synthetic_track.description: Spent synthetic soil is depleted and salt-laden. Compost it or dispose. Not ideal for reuse without heavy flushing and re-amending.

common_problems.cause: Low humidity, high temp
common_problems.solution: Raise humidity, lower temp.

common_problems.cause: Dense buds + humidity
common_problems.solution: Remove moldy buds. Lower humidity.

stages.description: Slow controlled drying. 60°F / 60% humidity / darkness. Soil-grown flower is often slightly less dense than hydro-grown, which can actually make drying easier and more even.

environment.notes: DARK. 60°F. 60% RH. Gentle airflow not on buds.

environment_overrides.notes: Too warm. Separate dark room.

container_progression.commercial_medium: Turkey bags in sealed bins, nitrogen-flushed
container_progression.industrial: Climate-controlled vault, nitrogen generators, automated monitoring

degradation_timeline.18_plus_months: Convert to extracts, edibles, or topicals.

testing_schedule.360_days: Full panel — sell, process, or destroy decision

common_problems.cause: Heat, light, oxygen, or time
common_problems.solution: Darkness, cool temps (60°F), minimal oxygen. ~5%/year baseline.

common_problems.cause: Temps above 70°F, oxygen, frequent opening
common_problems.solution: Below 65°F. Nitrogen-sealed. Minimize opening.

common_problems.cause: Humidity above 65% or improper cure
common_problems.solution: Verify 58-62% RH before sealing. Remove affected material.

common_problems.cause: Normal moisture equilibration
common_problems.solution: Boveda packs. Sealed containers.

stages.description: Post-cure long-term storage. Soil-grown flower — especially organic soil — is prized for complex terpene profiles that continue to develop in early storage. Many connoisseurs consider properly stored organic soil flower the gold standard. Proper storage preserves potency and terpenes for 6-12+ months.

environment.notes: DARK. Cool. Stable. Zero light — UV destroys cannabinoids and terpenes. Commercial: 58-62°F, 55-60% RH, nitrogen atmosphere.

environment_overrides.notes: Do NOT store in greenhouse.

greenhouse.notes: Climate-controlled indoor space only.

environment_overrides.notes: Storage is always indoor.

outdoor.notes: Indoor controlled environment.

stages.name: Long-Term Storage

nutrients.approach: None.

tasks.description: Home: mason jars with Boveda 58-62%. Commercial: nitrogen-sealed grove bags, CVault, or nitrogen-flushed drums.
tasks.name: Transfer to long-term containers

tasks.description: Strain, harvest date, storage date, weight, batch number, soil type (organic/synthetic). Commercial: seed-to-sale, FIFO rotation.
tasks.name: Label and track batches

tasks.description: Inspect for mold, off odors. Check humidity packs. Commercial: potency/terpene testing at 30/90/180 days.
tasks.name: Monthly quality checks

tasks.description: Monitor vault temp/humidity. No light leaks. Commercial: automated alerts.
tasks.name: Maintain environment

tasks.description: First in, first out. Flag batches approaching 12 months.

tasks.description: Commercial: retain samples per regulations.
tasks.name: Compliance testing holds

autoflower.description: Ruderalis-hybrid strains that flower automatically regardless of light schedule. Fixed timeline.

light_schedule.flower: 20/4 or 18/6 (same as veg — no change needed)
light_schedule.transition: Automatic — plant decides when to flower (usually week 3-4)

autoflower.name: Autoflower
autoflower.notes: Fixed timeline — stress cannot be recovered by extending veg. Start in final pot (no transplant shock). LST preferred over HST.

training_suitability.defoliation: light only — auto can't recover from heavy defol
training_suitability.topping: risky — only if plant is vigorous

photoperiod.description: Standard cannabis strains that require 12/12 light to flower. Grower controls timing.

light_schedule.transition: Switch to 12/12 when plant is ~50% of desired final height

photoperiod.name: Photoperiod
photoperiod.notes: Can be vegged as long as desired. Cloneable. More forgiving of stress (can extend veg to recover).

training_suitability.defoliation: good
training_suitability.topping: excellent

issues.cause: Watering too frequently — soil not drying between waterings
issues.fix: STOP watering. Wait until pot is noticeably light. Roots need oxygen. This is the #1 soil problem.

issues.cause: Underwatering — soil too dry
issues.fix: Water to runoff. If soil is hydrophobic (water runs off surface): add water slowly in stages. Let absorb between pours.

issues.cause: Chronic overwatering leading to anaerobic conditions and root rot
issues.fix: Transplant to dry soil. Cut dead roots. Treat with Hydroguard. Fix watering schedule.

issues.cause: Soil base nutrition depleted (3-4 weeks in)
issues.fix: Synthetic: begin feeding. Organic: top dress with high-N amendment (alfalfa, blood meal) + compost tea.

issues.cause: Synthetic salts accumulating, lowering pH below 6.0
issues.fix: Check runoff pH. Flush with pH 6.8 water until runoff is 6.0+. Resume feeding at lower strength. Organic track rarely has this issue.

issues.cause: Too-strong synthetic feeding, or super soil too 'hot'
issues.fix: Synthetic: reduce strength. Super soil: transplant to milder soil layer, or flush with plain water.

issues.cause: Moist soil surface — gnats breed in top 1 inch
issues.fix: BTI (Mosquito Bits) in every watering. Yellow sticky traps. Let top inch dry. Diatomaceous earth on surface. Nematodes for severe infestations.

issues.cause: Root aphid infestation — the most damaging soil pest
issues.fix: Botanigard drench (Beauveria bassiana). Beneficial nematodes. Pyrethrin drench for severe cases. May need to discard soil. Quarantine affected plants immediately.

issues.cause: Spider mite infestation
issues.fix: Neem oil spray. Spinosad. Predatory mites (P. persimilis). Raise humidity in veg. Daily inspection.
issues.symptom: Tiny dots on leaf undersides (spider mites)

cons: RO system cost and waste water
water_source_profiles.name: Reverse Osmosis (RO) Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Gold standard for precision growing. Always add CalMag first when using RO.
pros: Blank slate — total control over mineral content

dechlorination.chloramine: Does NOT gas off — must use Campden tablets or activated carbon filter
dechlorination.chlorine: Let sit uncovered 24 hours (gasses off) or use dechlorinator drops
dechlorination.how_to_tell: Check city water report. Most modern cities use chloramine.

water_source_profiles.name: Municipal Tap Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Good enough for most grows. Dechlorinate and pH-adjust. Get a water report from your city.

cons: May contain iron, sulfur, or heavy metals
water_source_profiles.name: Well Water
water_source_profiles.notes: MUST get a water test before using. High iron or sulfur can cause lockout. Great if your well has clean water with moderate minerals.

water_source_profiles.name: Distilled Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Same as RO but purchased instead of filtered. Only practical for 1-2 plant grows.

advanced_techniques.description: Elevating CO2 from ambient (400ppm) to 1000-1500ppm increases photosynthesis rate by 20-40% when light and temperature are also increased.

methods.notes: Most common. Tank refills every 2-4 weeks for a tent.
Produces heat and humidity — better for large rooms with AC.
Produces ~100-200ppm boost. Only useful for small sealed spaces. Not worth it for most.

advanced_techniques.name: CO2 Supplementation

ppfd_co2_correlation.notes: Ambient CO2 sufficient at low light
Slight benefit from supplementation
Moderate benefit — cost-effective threshold
Strong benefit — diminishing returns above this
Maximum practical benefit for most grows
Commercial intensity — requires perfect environment

requirements: Sealed grow space (CO2 escapes quickly from leaky rooms)
High light intensity (>600 PPFD — plants can't use extra CO2 without sufficient light)
Higher temperature tolerance (82-86°F optimal with CO2 vs 75-80°F without)
CO2 controller or timer

advanced_techniques.description: Manipulating environmental and irrigation parameters to push the plant toward vegetative or generative (flowering/fruiting) growth.
advanced_techniques.name: Crop Steering
advanced_techniques.notes: Crop steering is most effective in hydro/coco where irrigation frequency is highly controllable. Soil growers have less precision due to soil buffering.

vegetative_steering.goal: More leaf/stem growth, larger plant framework

best_practices: Spray during lights-off or first/last 30 minutes of light cycle (stomata open, no light burn)
Use fine mist sprayer — coverage is key
pH foliar solution to 6.2-6.5 (leaf surface slightly higher pH than root zone)
EC should be very low (0.2-0.5) — leaves burn easily
Never foliar spray during flower (moisture in buds → mold/bud rot)
Best for micronutrient deficiency correction (CalMag, iron, magnesium)

common_foliar_products.frequency: Weekly preventive, 3-day intervals for active infestations

advanced_techniques.description: Spraying nutrient solution directly onto leaves for rapid uptake. Bypasses root zone entirely.
advanced_techniques.name: Foliar Feeding

advanced_techniques.description: Strengthens cell walls, increases stem thickness, improves heat/drought/pest resistance.
important_notes: ALWAYS add silica to water FIRST before other nutrients (raises pH significantly)
Let silica mix for 5-10 minutes before adding base nutrients
Silica is incompatible with concentrated nutrient solutions — never mix undiluted
Discontinue in late flower (last 2-3 weeks)
advanced_techniques.name: Silica (Potassium Silicate)

advanced_techniques.description: Introduces beneficial bacteria/fungi to the root zone to outcompete pathogens, improve nutrient uptake, and protect against root diseases.
important_notes: Do NOT use hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) with beneficial microbes — it kills them
Choose either sterile (H2O2) OR beneficial (Hydroguard) approach — not both
Beneficials need warmish water (65-75°F) to thrive
Reapply after any reservoir change or system cleaning
advanced_techniques.name: Beneficial Microbes & Root Inoculants

products.name: Hydroguard
products.notes: The standard for DWC/RDWC. Add at every res change.

products.dosing: 1 tsp/gal (water-in) or dust roots at transplant
products.name: Great White
products.notes: Best for soil/coco. Less effective in pure hydro.

products.name: Recharge
products.notes: Feeds existing soil microbes. Great for living soil.

products.name: Mammoth P
products.notes: Most effective in flower when P demand is highest.

advanced_techniques.description: Removing select fan leaves to improve light penetration, airflow, and direct energy to bud sites.
advanced_techniques.name: Defoliation (Strategic Leaf Removal)
rules: Never remove more than 20-30% of leaves at once (except schwazze technique)
Only defoliate healthy, vigorous plants — never stressed ones
Autoflowers: very light defoliation only — they can't recover from heavy removal
Always leave the top 2-3 fan leaves on each branch (they feed the bud below)

timing.method: Remove lower leaves that get no light (lollipop)
Major defoliation day 1 and day 21 of flower (schwazzing)
Selective tuck or remove leaves blocking bud sites

equipment.description: Commercial tower garden or DIY PVC tower with stacked plant sites around central column.
equipment.name: Vertical tower structure

equipment.description: Base reservoir. Smaller than other hydro due to tower's compact footprint.
equipment.name: Reservoir (20-50 gal)

equipment.description: Pumps solution from reservoir to tower top. Gravity returns it down through sites.
equipment.name: Submersible pump

equipment.description: Fit into tower sites. Hold plant + minimal media or clay pebbles.
equipment.name: Net pots (3-inch)

equipment.description: Top of tower distributes solution evenly to all sides before gravity flow.
equipment.name: Distribution cap/shower head

equipment.description: Controls pump cycles. Critical for vertical flow management.
equipment.name: Timer

equipment.description: Minimal media in net pots for support. Hydroton/clay pebbles standard.
equipment.name: Growing media (clay pebbles)

equipment.description: Monitor solution. Small reservoirs change fast.
equipment.name: pH/EC meters

equipment.description: Vertical LED strips illuminate lower tower levels. Critical for even development.
equipment.name: Side lighting (LED strips)

equipment.description: Circular trellis at each level supports outward-growing branches.
equipment.name: Trellis rings

equipment.description: Slow rotation (1 RPM or less) ensures even light exposure.
equipment.name: Rotation motor/base

equipment.description: Aerate reservoir for dissolved oxygen.
equipment.name: Air pump + stone

equipment.description: Stabilizes tower. Prevents tipping from plant weight.
equipment.name: Ballast weight/base

equipment.description: Auto-top-off for small reservoir.
equipment.name: Float valve

equipment.description: Support heavy buds from above. Prevent plants pulling from sites.
equipment.name: Plant supports (yo-yos)

flush_timing.coco: 7-14 days reduced nutrients → plain water last 3-5 days
flush_timing.notes: Flushing is debated — some studies show no difference. At minimum, reduce nutrients in final week.
flush_timing.soil: 14-21 days plain water (soil holds nutrients longer)

other_harvest_indicators: Most pistils (hairs) have darkened and curled in
Calyxes are swollen and dense
Fan leaves are yellowing and falling off naturally
Trichome stalks are visible and heads are enlarged
Breeder's recommended flowering time has passed

trichome_ratios.notes: Harvest early for more THC-dominant, less CBN. Energetic, racy high. Slight reduction in yield.
Standard harvest window. Mix of THC and CBN. Full-spectrum effects.
Extended harvest for max CBN conversion. Heavy body high. Good for pain/sleep. Slightly degraded THC.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Micro → Gro → Bloom (always Micro first)
nutrient_brands.name: General Hydroponics Flora Trio

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Part A → Epsom → Part B (dissolve each fully before adding next)
nutrient_brands.name: Jack's 321
nutrient_brands.notes: Jack's 321 is 3.6g Part A, 2.4g Cal Nit, 1.2g Epsom per gallon at full strength. Extremely cost-effective for large grows.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Core → Bloom
nutrient_brands.name: Athena Pro Line
nutrient_brands.notes: Commercial-grade. Simple 2-part with integrated CalMag. Popular in commercial cannabis facilities.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: A → B (separate for veg and bloom)
nutrient_brands.name: Advanced Nutrients pH Perfect (Sensi)
nutrient_brands.notes: Self-adjusting pH technology (claims no pH adjustment needed). Expensive but popular for beginners who struggle with pH.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Shake VERY well before use (thick concentrate settles). One bottle at a time.
nutrient_brands.name: General Hydroponics FloraNova
nutrient_brands.notes: Simplest option — one bottle per phase. Great for beginners. Shake well — concentrate separates.
nutrient_brands.type: 1-part (two bottles — one for veg, one for bloom)

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Dissolve powder in warm water, stir well
nutrient_brands.name: Greenleaf Nutrients Mega Crop
nutrient_brands.notes: One powder does everything — includes CalMag. Incredibly cost-effective at ~$0.02/gal. Community favorite on r/microgrowery.

environment.container: Glass mason jars (quart size). Fill 75% full.

humidity_packs.boveda_58: For drier preference or long-term storage.
humidity_packs.boveda_62: Maintains 62% RH. Set-and-forget. Replace when crispy.
humidity_packs.grove_bags: Heat-sealed bags with built-in humidity regulation. No burping needed.

indicators_of_good_cure: Smooth, flavorful smoke — no harshness or hay smell
Buds are slightly sticky but not wet
Strong terpene aroma when jar is opened
Ash burns to white/light gray (not black)
method: Trim buds to final shape (dry trim or finish wet trim)
Place in mason jars — fill 3/4 full to allow air exchange
Week 1: burp 2-3x daily for 10-15 minutes (release moisture and gases)
Week 2-4: burp 1x daily for 5-10 minutes
Month 2+: burp 1x weekly or seal completely
Cure for minimum 2 weeks, ideal is 4-8 weeks. Some strains improve for 6+ months.

common_mistakes: Drying too fast (>3 days) — harsh smoke, hay smell, terpene loss
Drying too slow (>14 days) — mold risk, diminishing returns
Fan blowing directly on buds — uneven drying, crispy exterior/moist interior
Temperature too high (>70°F) — terpene evaporation, harsh taste

environment.airflow: Gentle circulation — fan NOT pointed at buds. Just keep air moving.

method: Cut branches 12-18 inches long
Optionally remove large fan leaves (wet trim) or leave intact (dry trim)
Hang upside down on line/rack with 6+ inches between branches
Small branches should snap (not bend) when done — 5-7 days minimum
Stems should snap cleanly; if they bend, keep drying

long_term_storage.degradation: THC slowly converts to CBN over time. Light, heat, air, and moisture accelerate degradation.
long_term_storage.method: Sealed mason jars or vacuum-sealed bags with Boveda 58% in dark, cool place
long_term_storage.notes: Properly cured and stored cannabis can remain excellent for 12+ months. Freezing is debated — can damage trichomes if not done carefully.

critical_rules: Side lighting is almost mandatory for even bud development
Rotate tower (or use multiple light angles) for even exposure
Train plants OUTWARD — never let them grow straight up (shading)
Small reservoir = check twice daily in flower
Fewer plants with better light > more plants starving for light

feed_schedule.flower: 15 min on / 15 min off or continuous

quick_reference.maintenance_level: Medium-high — light management is ongoing challenge. Small reservoirs need frequent attention.
quick_reference.method_summary: Plants grow from stacked sites around a vertical column. Solution pumped to top, flows down via gravity past all root zones. Maximizes plants per square foot.

scale_tiers.description: First-time grower or single-plant hobbyist. Manual management, learning-focused.
scale_tiers.name: Solo / Single Plant
scale_tiers.notes: Hands-on learning experience. Manual checks 1-2x daily. Ideal for beginners.

scale_tiers.description: Hobbyist tent grow. Light automation helpful but not required.
scale_tiers.name: Small Tent (2-8 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Timer-controlled lights, humidity controller. Sensor monitoring recommended.

scale_tiers.description: Dedicated grower with multiple tents/rooms. Automation saves significant time.
scale_tiers.name: Multi-Tent (9-24 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Multiple environments to manage. Automated alerts essential. Consider perpetual harvest.

scale_tiers.description: Licensed commercial operation. Full automation, compliance tracking, batch management.
scale_tiers.name: Commercial Room (25-100 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: SOPs required. Staff training. Regulatory compliance. Insurance requirements.

scale_tiers.description: Large-scale production facility. Enterprise automation, SCADA integration, dedicated staff.
scale_tiers.name: Warehouse Scale (100+ plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Multiple rooms, dedicated HVAC, industrial nutrient dosing, video surveillance.

common_problems.cause: Temperature too low
common_problems.solution: Heat mat under propagation tray. 78°F target.

stages.description: Germinate in net pot inserts or starter plugs. Tower system not running yet — seedlings develop in propagation tray.

environment_overrides.notes: Propagation area in greenhouse works well.
Germinate indoors for control.

stages.name: Germination

nutrients.approach: Plain water for germination.

reservoir.notes: System off. Propagation only.

tasks.description: pH 5.5 water. Rapid rooters or rockwool.
tasks.name: Soak starter plugs

tasks.description: One per plug, pointed end down.
tasks.name: Plant seeds

tasks.description: Look for taproot emergence.
tasks.name: Check germination

common_problems.cause: Gravity pulls solution to lower sites faster
common_problems.solution: Increase pump cycle. Ensure distribution cap on top is clear. Consider only using lower sites for seedlings.

common_problems.cause: Net pot too small or plant not rooted enough
common_problems.solution: Use clay pebbles to stabilize. Wait until roots are more developed before transplanting.

stages.description: Seedlings in plugs under mild light. Once roots emerge from plug, transplant to tower sites. Start system on gentle cycle. Lower sites preferred for seedlings (more moisture).

environment_overrides.notes: Ideal environment for tower systems.
Protect from wind (towers are top-heavy).

stages.name: Seedling

nutrients.approach: 1/4 strength veg nutrients.

reservoir.notes: Light feed. Pump on 15/60 cycle.

tasks.description: Lower sites preferred — more moisture from gravity flow.
tasks.name: Place seedlings in tower

tasks.description: 15 min on / 60 min off. Gentle flow.
tasks.name: Start pump cycle

tasks.description: Check that solution reaches all levels. Lower = more, upper = less.
tasks.name: Verify all sites getting moisture

tasks.description: Small reservoir changes fast. Monitor closely.
tasks.name: Check pH/EC

transition_signals: Roots visible at net pot edges

common_problems.cause: Shaded by upper sites + less direct light
common_problems.solution: Aggressive training of upper plants outward. Side lighting. Rotate tower.

common_problems.cause: Aggressive root growth fills the internal space
common_problems.solution: Trim roots that extend into central flow path. More frequent pump cycles.

stages.description: Plants establishing in tower sites. Roots growing into central column. Increase feed strength. Vertical canopy management becomes critical — lower sites get less light.

environment_overrides.notes: Supplement lower sites with side lighting if needed.
Natural light from multiple angles helps vertical distribution.

stages.name: Early Vegetative

nutrients.approach: Half-strength veg. High nitrogen.

reservoir.notes: Half strength. More frequent cycles.

tasks.description: 15 on / 30 off. Roots need consistent moisture in tower.
tasks.name: Increase feed cycles

tasks.description: Rotate 90° every 2-3 days for even light exposure.
tasks.name: Rotate tower (if applicable)

tasks.description: Train plants outward, not upward. LST to prevent upper sites shading lower.
tasks.name: Manage vertical canopy

tasks.description: Roots growing into central column can block flow to lower sites.
tasks.name: Check for root clogs

transition_signals: Plants filling out from all sites

common_problems.cause: Multiple large plants in veg drink heavily from small reservoir
common_problems.solution: Larger external reservoir. Float valve auto-top-off. Consider fewer plants per tower.

common_problems.cause: Large plants creating imbalanced weight
common_problems.solution: Ballast base. Tether to wall/ceiling. Prune to maintain balance.

stages.description: Plants growing aggressively from all tower sites. Vertical canopy management critical. Light distribution is the main challenge. Reservoir consumption increases significantly.

environment_overrides.notes: Peak growth. Ventilate well.
Secure tower against wind. Stake or tether.

stages.name: Late Vegetative

nutrients.approach: 3/4 strength veg. High nitrogen for rapid growth.

reservoir.notes: Full veg strength. Monitor consumption — towers deplete fast.

tasks.description: Tower systems have small reservoirs. Multiple plants deplete fast.
tasks.name: Monitor reservoir daily

tasks.description: Train all plants outward and downward. Keep center column clear.
tasks.name: Aggressive canopy management

tasks.description: Root debris can clog pump intake.
tasks.name: Check pump and lines

tasks.description: Better yield from fewer strong plants than many struggling ones.
tasks.name: Consider removing weak sites

common_problems.cause: Stretch creates a canopy ceiling blocking light below
common_problems.issue: Upper sites completely shading lower sites
common_problems.solution: Supercrop upper sites. Add vertical side lighting. Consider removing top sites.

stages.description: Flip to 12/12. Plants stretch 50-100%. Critical to manage — vertical tower stretch can create massive shading issues. Aggressive training essential.

environment_overrides.notes: Supplement with side lighting.
Natural photoperiod. Sun angle helps vertical light distribution.

stages.name: Transition (Stretch)

nutrients.approach: 50% veg + 50% bloom transition.

reservoir.notes: Transition formula.

tasks.description: Switch to 12/12.
tasks.name: Flip light schedule

tasks.description: Bend/tie all growth outward. Supercrop if necessary. Shading kills lower sites.
tasks.name: Aggressive stretch management

tasks.description: Begin bloom formula.
tasks.name: Transition nutrients

tasks.description: LED strips on sides illuminate lower sites during stretch.
tasks.name: Add side lighting (optional)

common_problems.cause: Light not reaching lower tower sites
common_problems.solution: Side lighting (LED strips). Aggressive defoliation of upper canopy. Supercrop upper branches to let light through.

stages.description: Bud sites forming at all tower levels. Full bloom nutrients. Tower advantage: 360° access for inspection. Challenge: even light distribution to all bud sites.

environment_overrides.notes: Good natural light from multiple angles.
Sun angle changes help light distribution.

stages.name: Early Flower

nutrients.approach: Full bloom. High P-K.

reservoir.notes: Full bloom nutrients.

tasks.description: Switch completely to flower formula.
tasks.name: Full bloom nutrients

tasks.description: Remove fan leaves blocking bud sites on inner canopy.
tasks.name: Defoliate for light penetration

tasks.description: Rotate for even light. Or use multiple light sources.
tasks.name: Maintain tower rotation

common_problems.cause: Heavy buds + gravity + small net pots
common_problems.issue: Buds pulling plants out of tower
common_problems.solution: Support nets. Trellis rings around tower. Yo-yo supports from ceiling.

common_problems.cause: Dense canopy trapping moisture inside tower structure
common_problems.solution: Oscillating fan directed at tower. Defoliate aggressively. Space plants wider.

stages.description: Peak bud development across all tower levels. Maximum water/nutrient demand. Tower challenge: maintaining even bud development despite varying light exposure per level.

environment_overrides.notes: Dehumidify. Good circulation around tower.
Rain cover essential. Wind support.

stages.name: Mid Flower (Bulk Phase)

nutrients.approach: Full bloom + PK booster.

reservoir.notes: Peak demand. Reservoir may need daily top-offs.

tasks.description: Peak consumption from multiple flowering plants. Depletes fast.
tasks.name: Monitor reservoir 2x daily

tasks.description: Weeks 4-6 of flower.
tasks.name: PK booster

tasks.description: Net or ties to prevent buds from pulling plant out of tower site.
tasks.name: Support buds

tasks.description: Dense buds + internal tower humidity = risk.
tasks.name: Monitor for bud rot

common_problems.cause: Light differential between levels
common_problems.solution: Staggered harvest — take upper sites first. Leave lower to finish.

stages.description: Final ripening. Reduce nutrients. Tower allows 360° trichome inspection — easy access to all bud sites for maturity assessment.

environment_overrides.notes: Drop night temps.
Fall temps help ripening.

stages.name: Late Flower (Ripening)

nutrients.approach: 75% bloom. Stop PK booster.

reservoir.notes: Reducing strength.

tasks.description: Tower advantage: easy 360° access. Check upper and lower sites.
tasks.name: Check trichomes all levels

tasks.description: Drop EC. Preparing for flush.
tasks.name: Reduce nutrients

tasks.description: Upper sites may ripen faster (more light). Plan staggered harvest.
tasks.name: Plan harvest order

common_problems.cause: Sudden nutrient removal + continued high light
common_problems.solution: Normal response. Reduce light intensity slightly. Maintain pump cycle.

stages.description: Switch to plain water. Tower systems flush fast — no media (or minimal media) means roots are directly exposed to solution. Clean flush in 5-7 days.

environment_overrides.notes: Standard flush.
Continue pump cycle with plain water.

stages.name: Flush

nutrients.approach: Plain pH'd water only.

reservoir.notes: Plain water. Clean reservoir.

tasks.description: Drain nutrient reservoir. Refill clean.
tasks.name: Switch to plain water

tasks.description: Keep running to flush roots.
tasks.name: Maintain pump cycles

tasks.description: Yellowing fan leaves = good sign.
tasks.name: Monitor fade

common_problems.cause: Root mass grew into and around internal structure
common_problems.solution: Soak tower. Roots soften and pull out. Enzyme cleaner helps.

stages.description: Remove plants from tower sites. Can harvest level by level (upper first if they ripened earlier). Easy access from all sides — tower advantage for harvest.

environment_overrides.notes: Harvest at convenience.
Harvest before frost.

nutrients.approach: None.

reservoir.notes: Turn off pump. Drain system.

tasks.description: Remove all root material. H2O2 flush through tower.
tasks.name: Clean tower

tasks.description: Clean for next cycle.
tasks.name: Inspect pump and lines

common_problems.cause: Humidity too low or temp too high
common_problems.solution: Lower temp, raise humidity, whole-plant hangs.

environment_overrides.notes: Dry in separate controlled space.
Dry indoors.

common_problems.solution: Remove, dry 12-24h more, re-jar.

Cure indoors.
