autoflower.description: Ruderalis-hybrid strains that flower automatically regardless of light schedule. Fixed timeline.

light_schedule.flower: 20/4 or 18/6 (same as veg — no change needed)
light_schedule.transition: Automatic — plant decides when to flower (usually week 3-4)

autoflower.name: Autoflower
autoflower.notes: Fixed timeline — stress cannot be recovered by extending veg. Start in final pot (no transplant shock). LST preferred over HST.

training_suitability.defoliation: light only — auto can't recover from heavy defol
training_suitability.topping: risky — only if plant is vigorous

photoperiod.description: Standard cannabis strains that require 12/12 light to flower. Grower controls timing.

light_schedule.transition: Switch to 12/12 when plant is ~50% of desired final height

photoperiod.name: Photoperiod
photoperiod.notes: Can be vegged as long as desired. Cloneable. More forgiving of stress (can extend veg to recover).

training_suitability.defoliation: good
training_suitability.topping: excellent

issues.cause: Distribution cap clogged or uneven
issues.solution: Clean distribution cap. Ensure even flow to all sides. Check for root blockages.
issues.symptom: Upper sites getting all the water, lower sites dry

issues.cause: Pump clogged or line kinked
issues.solution: Clean pump intake filter. Check for kinks in delivery line. Verify pump is submerged.

issues.cause: Seals degraded or tower shifted
issues.solution: Re-seat sections. Apply food-grade silicone sealant. Check O-rings.

issues.cause: Insufficient light reaching lower levels
issues.solution: Add side LED lighting. Aggressive upper defoliation. Consider using fewer levels.

issues.cause: Uneven light — tower not being rotated
issues.solution: Rotate tower 90° every 2-3 days. Or add light from multiple angles.

issues.cause: Strong directional light source
issues.solution: More frequent rotation. Support plants with trellis rings. Multi-directional lighting.
issues.symptom: Plants growing toward light (phototropism) pulling from pots

issues.cause: Top-heavy from plant growth or wind
issues.solution: Ballast base with water/sand. Tether to wall or ceiling. Prune to balance weight.

issues.cause: Net pots too small or roots not established
issues.solution: Pack with clay pebbles. Use larger net pots. Support with plant ties.

issues.cause: Aggressive root growth filling interior space
issues.solution: Root trim through sites. Consider fewer plants for root space. More frequent pump cycles to prevent root desiccation.

issues.cause: Small reservoir with many plants = fast changes
issues.solution: Larger external reservoir. More frequent monitoring. Auto-dosing system.

issues.cause: Nutrients absorbed by upper roots first, depleted by lower
issues.solution: Increase nutrient strength. More pump cycles. Consider bottom-up feed supplement.
issues.symptom: Lower sites showing deficiency, upper fine

issues.cause: Light hitting wet surfaces
issues.solution: Block light from tower surface. Use opaque tower material. Wrap with reflective material.

cycle_timing.notes: Roots in tower have minimal media — need frequent moisture. Can go continuous in flower.

gravity_distribution_challenge.issue: Lower sites receive more solution (gravity accumulation). Upper sites can dry faster.
solutions: More frequent pump cycles
Larger distribution cap for upper coverage
Absorbent collar inserts at each level
Mist rather than drip at top

pump_to_top.delivery: Pump pushes solution from base reservoir up through central pipe to distribution cap at top.
pump_to_top.distribution_cap: Spreads solution evenly to all sides of tower. Critical for uniform coverage.
pump_to_top.gravity_return: Solution cascades down through tower interior, passing over roots at each level, and returns to reservoir at base.

lighting_strategy.optimal_setup: Overhead HPS/LED for intensity + 4 vertical LED strips for side coverage + rotation

primary_overhead.coverage: Mainly hits top 1-2 levels. Diminishes rapidly below.

rotation.benefit: Ensures all plant faces receive equal light over time.
rotation.method: Manual 90° turn every 2-3 days, or motorized slow rotation (< 1 RPM)

side_lighting.importance: Critical for even development. Without it, lower 50% of tower underperforms.
options: Multiple small panels at each level
side_lighting.placement: One vertical strip every 90° around tower (4 strips total) is ideal.

best_use_cases: High-plant-count legal limits (more plants = more advantage)
Leafy greens (lettuce, herbs — ideal for towers)
cannabis_specific_challenges: Cannabis gets too large for typical tower sites
Light intensity requirements exceed what side-lighting can provide
Better for SOG (Sea of Green) with many small plants than few large plants
space_efficiency.yield_reality: Per-plant yield is lower (less light per site), but yield per square foot can be higher with proper lighting.

commercial_options.name: Tower Garden (Juice Plus)
commercial_options.notes: Proven design. Expensive. Good support community.

commercial_options.name: Mr. Stacky / GreenStalk
commercial_options.notes: Affordable. Soil-based (not true aero). Multiple tiers.

bucket_stack.notes: Easier to build. Modular. Can add/remove sections.

pvc_tower.notes: Most common DIY approach. Cut holes at 120° rotation per level for optimal spacing.

cons: RO system cost and waste water
water_source_profiles.name: Reverse Osmosis (RO) Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Gold standard for precision growing. Always add CalMag first when using RO.
pros: Blank slate — total control over mineral content

dechlorination.chloramine: Does NOT gas off — must use Campden tablets or activated carbon filter
dechlorination.chlorine: Let sit uncovered 24 hours (gasses off) or use dechlorinator drops
dechlorination.how_to_tell: Check city water report. Most modern cities use chloramine.

water_source_profiles.name: Municipal Tap Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Good enough for most grows. Dechlorinate and pH-adjust. Get a water report from your city.

cons: May contain iron, sulfur, or heavy metals
water_source_profiles.name: Well Water
water_source_profiles.notes: MUST get a water test before using. High iron or sulfur can cause lockout. Great if your well has clean water with moderate minerals.

water_source_profiles.name: Distilled Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Same as RO but purchased instead of filtered. Only practical for 1-2 plant grows.

advanced_techniques.description: Elevating CO2 from ambient (400ppm) to 1000-1500ppm increases photosynthesis rate by 20-40% when light and temperature are also increased.

methods.notes: Most common. Tank refills every 2-4 weeks for a tent.
Produces heat and humidity — better for large rooms with AC.
Produces ~100-200ppm boost. Only useful for small sealed spaces. Not worth it for most.

advanced_techniques.name: CO2 Supplementation

ppfd_co2_correlation.notes: Ambient CO2 sufficient at low light
Slight benefit from supplementation
Moderate benefit — cost-effective threshold
Strong benefit — diminishing returns above this
Maximum practical benefit for most grows
Commercial intensity — requires perfect environment

requirements: Sealed grow space (CO2 escapes quickly from leaky rooms)
High light intensity (>600 PPFD — plants can't use extra CO2 without sufficient light)
Higher temperature tolerance (82-86°F optimal with CO2 vs 75-80°F without)
CO2 controller or timer

advanced_techniques.description: Manipulating environmental and irrigation parameters to push the plant toward vegetative or generative (flowering/fruiting) growth.
advanced_techniques.name: Crop Steering
advanced_techniques.notes: Crop steering is most effective in hydro/coco where irrigation frequency is highly controllable. Soil growers have less precision due to soil buffering.

vegetative_steering.goal: More leaf/stem growth, larger plant framework

best_practices: Spray during lights-off or first/last 30 minutes of light cycle (stomata open, no light burn)
Use fine mist sprayer — coverage is key
pH foliar solution to 6.2-6.5 (leaf surface slightly higher pH than root zone)
EC should be very low (0.2-0.5) — leaves burn easily
Never foliar spray during flower (moisture in buds → mold/bud rot)
Best for micronutrient deficiency correction (CalMag, iron, magnesium)

common_foliar_products.frequency: Weekly preventive, 3-day intervals for active infestations

advanced_techniques.description: Spraying nutrient solution directly onto leaves for rapid uptake. Bypasses root zone entirely.
advanced_techniques.name: Foliar Feeding

advanced_techniques.description: Strengthens cell walls, increases stem thickness, improves heat/drought/pest resistance.
important_notes: ALWAYS add silica to water FIRST before other nutrients (raises pH significantly)
Let silica mix for 5-10 minutes before adding base nutrients
Silica is incompatible with concentrated nutrient solutions — never mix undiluted
Discontinue in late flower (last 2-3 weeks)
advanced_techniques.name: Silica (Potassium Silicate)

advanced_techniques.description: Introduces beneficial bacteria/fungi to the root zone to outcompete pathogens, improve nutrient uptake, and protect against root diseases.
important_notes: Do NOT use hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) with beneficial microbes — it kills them
Choose either sterile (H2O2) OR beneficial (Hydroguard) approach — not both
Beneficials need warmish water (65-75°F) to thrive
Reapply after any reservoir change or system cleaning
advanced_techniques.name: Beneficial Microbes & Root Inoculants

products.name: Hydroguard
products.notes: The standard for DWC/RDWC. Add at every res change.

products.dosing: 1 tsp/gal (water-in) or dust roots at transplant
products.name: Great White
products.notes: Best for soil/coco. Less effective in pure hydro.

products.name: Recharge
products.notes: Feeds existing soil microbes. Great for living soil.

products.name: Mammoth P
products.notes: Most effective in flower when P demand is highest.

advanced_techniques.description: Removing select fan leaves to improve light penetration, airflow, and direct energy to bud sites.
advanced_techniques.name: Defoliation (Strategic Leaf Removal)
rules: Never remove more than 20-30% of leaves at once (except schwazze technique)
Only defoliate healthy, vigorous plants — never stressed ones
Autoflowers: very light defoliation only — they can't recover from heavy removal
Always leave the top 2-3 fan leaves on each branch (they feed the bud below)

timing.method: Remove lower leaves that get no light (lollipop)
Major defoliation day 1 and day 21 of flower (schwazzing)
Selective tuck or remove leaves blocking bud sites

fill_pipe.material: 1.5-2 inch PVC pipe, vertical, from surface down into reservoir
fill_pipe.position: Corner or edge of bed. Cap on top (mosquito prevention).
fill_pipe.purpose: Refill reservoir without disturbing soil surface.

layers_bottom_to_top.material: Waterproof container (tote, raised bed with pond liner, commercial SIP)
Gravel, scoria, perlite, or false floor with void space
layers_bottom_to_top.notes: This IS the water reservoir. Holds water below overflow drain.
Allows water through but keeps soil from filling reservoir voids.

layers_bottom_to_top.material: Fine perlite, sand, or wicking fabric bridge
layers_bottom_to_top.notes: Creates capillary bridge between reservoir and soil above.

layers_bottom_to_top.material: Quality potting soil, living soil, or soilless mix (must wick)
layers_bottom_to_top.notes: Top 4-6 inches should be ABOVE capillary saturation point.

layers_bottom_to_top.material: Straw, wood chips, rice hulls, or living cover crop
layers_bottom_to_top.notes: Prevents evaporation and salt migration to surface.

overflow_drain.position: At the TOP of the reservoir layer (where geotextile begins)
overflow_drain.purpose: Prevents water from rising into soil (waterlogging). Defines the maximum saturation line.
overflow_drain.sizing: 3/4 inch minimum fitting. Flush with inside wall of container.

capillary_break.cause: Reservoir fully emptying, or air pocket forming between layers.
capillary_break.consequence: Soil above break dries out. Plant wilts. Re-establishing requires heavy top-watering.
capillary_break.prevention: NEVER let reservoir fully empty. Keep at minimum 25% level. Ensure no air pockets in wicking layer.
capillary_break.what: Air gap that stops capillary action. Once broken, water cannot wick past the gap.

capillary_science.how_it_works: Water moves upward through small pore spaces in soil via capillary action (surface tension). Finer particles = higher wicking height but slower rate. Balance is key.

ideal_soil_recipe.notes: This mix wicks well (coco + compost) while providing aeration (perlite) in the upper zone where roots need oxygen.

coarse_gravel.notes: Does not wick. Used for reservoir voids only.

coco_coir.notes: Excellent wicking material. Great in mixes.

fine_sand.notes: Wicks highest but very slow. Too fine for most growing.

perlite.notes: Fast wicking but limited height. Good for reservoir layer, not grow layer alone.

potting_soil.notes: Ideal balance of height and speed for wicking beds.

failure_modes.response: Refill reservoir AND top-water heavily to re-establish capillary continuity. May take 24h to fully reconnect.
Clear blockage immediately. Drill second overflow as backup. Check monthly.
failure_modes.time_to_damage: Next heavy rain or over-fill causes waterlogging

failure_modes.response: Find and seal leak. Pond liner repair kit. May need to rebuild reservoir section.
failure_modes.time_to_damage: Days — reservoir empties faster than expected

failure_modes.response: Gently aerate top layer. Add fresh coco/perlite to surface. Rebuild every 3-4 cycles.

anaerobic_prevention: Don't add organic teas or particulate matter directly to reservoir (decomposition uses oxygen)
Optional: small aquarium air stone keeps water oxygenated
If reservoir smells sulfurous: drain completely, refill with fresh water
Use clean, filtered water for reservoir fills

nutrient_delivery_via_reservoir.caution: Nutrients concentrate as water evaporates. Start conservative. Flush regularly from top.
nutrient_delivery_via_reservoir.method: Add liquid nutrients to fill water at reduced strength (50-75% of label)

reservoir_management.sizing_rule: Minimum 1/3 of total bed volume should be reservoir. Bigger = less frequent refills.

flush_protocol: Every 2-3 weeks (or when white crust visible): water heavily from TOP
Use plain pH'd water, enough to flow out the overflow drain
This pushes accumulated salts downward and out
If crust is severe: scrape off surface layer, flush, then re-mulch
salt_management.mechanism: Water wicks UP carrying dissolved minerals. Water evaporates at surface. Minerals deposit as white crust. This is the #1 maintenance challenge of wicking beds.
prevention: Mulch layer (2-3 inches) — dramatically reduces surface evaporation and salt deposition
Lower nutrient concentration in reservoir (less minerals to deposit)
Regular top-water flushes push salts back down through soil
Organic growing with top-dress amendments avoids reservoir-based salt issues

equipment.description: DIY tote, raised bed, or commercial SIP planter. Must be waterproof on bottom half.
equipment.name: Wicking bed container

equipment.description: Gravel, scoria, or purpose-built false floor to create sub-surface water reservoir.
equipment.name: Reservoir layer material

equipment.description: Separates reservoir from soil above. Allows water through, keeps soil out of reservoir.
equipment.name: Geotextile fabric

equipment.description: Positioned at top of reservoir layer. Prevents waterlogging soil. CRITICAL safety feature.
equipment.name: Overflow drain fitting

equipment.description: Vertical pipe from surface down into reservoir for easy refilling without disturbing soil.
equipment.name: Fill pipe (PVC)

equipment.description: Quality potting soil or living soil mix. Must have capillary properties (not too coarse).
equipment.name: Growing medium

equipment.description: Clear tube connected to reservoir to visually check water level without disturbing anything.
equipment.name: Sight tube or dipstick

equipment.description: Straw, wood chips, or rice hulls. 2-3 inches on surface to reduce evaporation and salt migration.
equipment.name: Mulch material

equipment.description: Biological mosquito larvicide safe for plants. Essential for outdoor wicking beds.
equipment.name: BTI mosquito dunks

equipment.description: Liquid feed for reservoir or dry organic amendments for top-dressing.
equipment.name: Nutrients (liquid or organic)

equipment.description: Monitor reservoir water pH. Target 6.0-6.8 for soil-based wicking.
equipment.name: pH meter

equipment.description: Monitor nutrient concentration in reservoir.
equipment.name: EC meter

equipment.description: Fabric wicks or capillary matting if media alone doesn't wick well. Usually not needed with proper media.
equipment.name: Wicking material (optional)

equipment.description: Auto-refill from water line. Perfect for vacation growing or large beds.
equipment.name: Float valve

equipment.description: Prevents anaerobic conditions in reservoir. Useful if using organic nutrients in reservoir.
equipment.name: Small air stone + pump

flush_timing.coco: 7-14 days reduced nutrients → plain water last 3-5 days
flush_timing.notes: Flushing is debated — some studies show no difference. At minimum, reduce nutrients in final week.
flush_timing.soil: 14-21 days plain water (soil holds nutrients longer)

other_harvest_indicators: Most pistils (hairs) have darkened and curled in
Calyxes are swollen and dense
Fan leaves are yellowing and falling off naturally
Trichome stalks are visible and heads are enlarged
Breeder's recommended flowering time has passed

trichome_ratios.notes: Harvest early for more THC-dominant, less CBN. Energetic, racy high. Slight reduction in yield.
Standard harvest window. Mix of THC and CBN. Full-spectrum effects.
Extended harvest for max CBN conversion. Heavy body high. Good for pain/sleep. Slightly degraded THC.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Micro → Gro → Bloom (always Micro first)
nutrient_brands.name: General Hydroponics Flora Trio

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Part A → Epsom → Part B (dissolve each fully before adding next)
nutrient_brands.name: Jack's 321
nutrient_brands.notes: Jack's 321 is 3.6g Part A, 2.4g Cal Nit, 1.2g Epsom per gallon at full strength. Extremely cost-effective for large grows.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Core → Bloom
nutrient_brands.name: Athena Pro Line
nutrient_brands.notes: Commercial-grade. Simple 2-part with integrated CalMag. Popular in commercial cannabis facilities.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: A → B (separate for veg and bloom)
nutrient_brands.name: Advanced Nutrients pH Perfect (Sensi)
nutrient_brands.notes: Self-adjusting pH technology (claims no pH adjustment needed). Expensive but popular for beginners who struggle with pH.

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Shake VERY well before use (thick concentrate settles). One bottle at a time.
nutrient_brands.name: General Hydroponics FloraNova
nutrient_brands.notes: Simplest option — one bottle per phase. Great for beginners. Shake well — concentrate separates.
nutrient_brands.type: 1-part (two bottles — one for veg, one for bloom)

nutrient_brands.mixing_order: Dissolve powder in warm water, stir well
nutrient_brands.name: Greenleaf Nutrients Mega Crop
nutrient_brands.notes: One powder does everything — includes CalMag. Incredibly cost-effective at ~$0.02/gal. Community favorite on r/microgrowery.

environment.container: Glass mason jars (quart size). Fill 75% full.

humidity_packs.boveda_58: For drier preference or long-term storage.
humidity_packs.boveda_62: Maintains 62% RH. Set-and-forget. Replace when crispy.
humidity_packs.grove_bags: Heat-sealed bags with built-in humidity regulation. No burping needed.

indicators_of_good_cure: Smooth, flavorful smoke — no harshness or hay smell
Buds are slightly sticky but not wet
Strong terpene aroma when jar is opened
Ash burns to white/light gray (not black)
method: Trim buds to final shape (dry trim or finish wet trim)
Place in mason jars — fill 3/4 full to allow air exchange
Week 1: burp 2-3x daily for 10-15 minutes (release moisture and gases)
Week 2-4: burp 1x daily for 5-10 minutes
Month 2+: burp 1x weekly or seal completely
Cure for minimum 2 weeks, ideal is 4-8 weeks. Some strains improve for 6+ months.

common_mistakes: Drying too fast (>3 days) — harsh smoke, hay smell, terpene loss
Drying too slow (>14 days) — mold risk, diminishing returns
Fan blowing directly on buds — uneven drying, crispy exterior/moist interior
Temperature too high (>70°F) — terpene evaporation, harsh taste

environment.airflow: Gentle circulation — fan NOT pointed at buds. Just keep air moving.

method: Cut branches 12-18 inches long
Optionally remove large fan leaves (wet trim) or leave intact (dry trim)
Hang upside down on line/rack with 6+ inches between branches
Small branches should snap (not bend) when done — 5-7 days minimum
Stems should snap cleanly; if they bend, keep drying

long_term_storage.degradation: THC slowly converts to CBN over time. Light, heat, air, and moisture accelerate degradation.
long_term_storage.method: Sealed mason jars or vacuum-sealed bags with Boveda 58% in dark, cool place
long_term_storage.notes: Properly cured and stored cannabis can remain excellent for 12+ months. Freezing is debated — can damage trichomes if not done carefully.

critical_rules: NEVER let reservoir fully empty — breaks capillary continuity
Top-water flush every 2-3 weeks to prevent salt lockout
Overflow drain is non-negotiable — prevents soil waterlogging
Mulch surface to reduce evaporation and salt migration
Screen all openings against mosquitoes (standing water = breeding)
key_challenges: Cannot let reservoir fully empty (breaks capillary action)
Slower nutrient delivery than active hydro
quick_reference.maintenance_level: Very low — refill reservoir every 3-7 days, flush salts every 2-3 weeks.
quick_reference.method_summary: Passive self-watering via capillary action from sub-surface reservoir. Zero pumps, zero electricity.

nutrient_approach.hybrid: Organic soil + light liquid feed in reservoir
nutrient_approach.organic: Top-dress amendments (worm castings, kelp, bone meal) every 2-3 weeks
nutrient_approach.synthetic: Add to reservoir water at 50-75% recommended strength

scale_tiers.description: First-time grower or single-plant hobbyist. Manual management, learning-focused.
scale_tiers.name: Solo / Single Plant
scale_tiers.notes: Hands-on learning experience. Manual checks 1-2x daily. Ideal for beginners.

scale_tiers.description: Hobbyist tent grow. Light automation helpful but not required.
scale_tiers.name: Small Tent (2-8 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Timer-controlled lights, humidity controller. Sensor monitoring recommended.

scale_tiers.description: Dedicated grower with multiple tents/rooms. Automation saves significant time.
scale_tiers.name: Multi-Tent (9-24 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Multiple environments to manage. Automated alerts essential. Consider perpetual harvest.

scale_tiers.description: Licensed commercial operation. Full automation, compliance tracking, batch management.
scale_tiers.name: Commercial Room (25-100 plants)
scale_tiers.notes: SOPs required. Staff training. Regulatory compliance. Insurance requirements.

scale_tiers.description: Large-scale production facility. Enterprise automation, SCADA integration, dedicated staff.
scale_tiers.name: Warehouse Scale (100+ plants)
scale_tiers.notes: Multiple rooms, dedicated HVAC, industrial nutrient dosing, video surveillance.

common_problems.cause: Air pockets or media too coarse
common_problems.solution: Repack wicking layer. Use finer media above reservoir.

stages.description: Germinate separately from wicking bed. Paper towel or starter plug. Pre-saturate wicking bed reservoir so media is ready.

environment_overrides.notes: Greenhouse propagation works well.
Germinate indoors. Bed can saturate outdoors.

stages.name: Germination

nutrients.approach: None.

reservoir.notes: Fill reservoir with plain water. Let media saturate 24-48h before planting.

tasks.description: Paper towel method. 78°F, dark, moist.
tasks.name: Germinate seeds

tasks.description: Fill reservoir to overflow. Let media wick upward fully.
tasks.name: Pre-saturate wicking bed

tasks.description: Look for taproot emergence.
tasks.name: Check germination

common_problems.cause: Roots haven't reached wicking zone
common_problems.solution: Continue top-watering. Patience — takes 3-7 days.

common_problems.cause: Standing water accessible
common_problems.solution: Screen openings. BTI dunks in reservoir.

stages.description: Transplant seedling into wicking bed. Top-water for first 3-5 days until roots reach capillary zone, then let wicking take over.

environment_overrides.notes: Low-labor greenhouse setup.
Wicking beds ideal outdoors — self-watering.

extra_tasks.description: Seal reservoir access. BTI dunks.
extra_tasks.name: Mosquito prevention

stages.name: Seedling

nutrients.approach: 1/4 strength in reservoir or light organic top-dress.

reservoir.notes: Keep full. Top-water seedling directly until roots reach moisture zone.

tasks.description: Plant in top soil layer. Roots grow down to moisture zone.
tasks.name: Transplant to bed

tasks.description: Light watering from above for 3-5 days until wicking takes over.
tasks.name: Top-water daily

tasks.description: Use sight tube or dipstick. Never let it empty.
tasks.name: Check reservoir level

tasks.description: 1-2 inch mulch to prevent evaporation and salt crust.
tasks.name: Mulch surface

common_problems.cause: Minerals wick up and deposit via evaporation
common_problems.solution: Top-water flush. Mulch layer. Reduce reservoir nutrient concentration.

common_problems.cause: Nutrients not reaching roots (too dilute in reservoir)
common_problems.solution: Increase reservoir nutrient strength. Top-dress amendments.

stages.description: Roots have reached capillary zone. Plant is self-watering from reservoir. Low maintenance — just refill reservoir and watch for salt crust.

environment_overrides.notes: Minimal intervention needed.
Outdoor wicking beds are self-sufficient in early veg.

stages.name: Early Vegetative

nutrients.approach: Half-strength veg nutrients in reservoir OR organic top-dress (worm castings + kelp).

reservoir.notes: Small plants drink less. Check twice per week.

tasks.description: Check 2x/week via sight tube. Never let empty.
tasks.name: Refill reservoir

tasks.description: Flush from top every 2-3 weeks to clear surface salts.
tasks.name: Top-water flush

tasks.description: White crust on surface? Scrape off and flush.
tasks.name: Check for salt crust

tasks.description: LST or topping at 5th node.
tasks.name: Begin training

common_problems.cause: Large plant mass, hot conditions
common_problems.solution: Larger reservoir or more frequent refills. Float valve for auto-fill.

common_problems.cause: Stagnant water decomposing organic matter
common_problems.solution: Flush reservoir. Don't add organic teas directly. Small air stone optional.

stages.description: Plants growing aggressively. Reservoir consumption increases. Training to fill canopy. Wicking beds produce vigorous, healthy plants due to constant moisture.

environment_overrides.notes: Monitor reservoir more in warm greenhouse.
Peak summer consumption. Check daily in heat.

extra_tasks.description: Hot days = 2+ gal/day consumption.
extra_tasks.name: Daily reservoir check in heat

stages.name: Late Vegetative

nutrients.approach: 3/4 strength veg in reservoir. Top-dress organics every 2-3 weeks.

reservoir.notes: Larger plants drink more. May need refills every 3-4 days.

tasks.description: Every 3-4 days. Plants drinking more now.
tasks.name: Refill reservoir

tasks.description: Flush surface salts every 2 weeks.
tasks.name: Top-water flush

tasks.description: Worm castings + kelp meal on surface. Water in lightly.
tasks.name: Top-dress amendments

tasks.description: LST/SCROG to fill space. Consistent moisture = fast recovery from training.
tasks.name: Train canopy

common_problems.cause: Genetics or insufficient light
common_problems.solution: Supercrop tall branches. Increase light intensity.

stages.description: Flip to 12/12. Plants stretch 50-100%. Wicking bed handles increased water demand effortlessly. Begin transitioning nutrients to bloom.

environment_overrides.notes: Use blackout covers for photoperiod control if needed.
Natural photoperiod triggers transition. No manual flip needed.

stages.name: Transition (Stretch)

nutrients.approach: Transition to bloom nutrients. 50/50 veg/bloom in reservoir.

reservoir.notes: Stretching plants increase water uptake. Check every 2-3 days.

tasks.description: Switch to 12/12.
tasks.name: Flip light schedule

tasks.description: Stretching increases demand.
tasks.name: Refill reservoir

tasks.description: LST/supercropping if needed for height control.
tasks.name: Manage stretch

tasks.description: Begin transitioning to bloom formula in reservoir.
tasks.name: Switch nutrients

common_problems.cause: Reservoir too concentrated + salt buildup
common_problems.solution: Dilute reservoir. Top-water flush to clear salts.

stages.description: Bud sites forming. Increased nutrient demand. Wicking bed provides consistent moisture ideal for flower development. Switch fully to bloom nutrients.

environment_overrides.notes: Good airflow critical as buds develop.
Watch for caterpillars and bud rot as buds form.

stages.name: Early Flower

nutrients.approach: Full bloom nutrients in reservoir. High P-K. Organic: bone meal + langbeinite top-dress.

reservoir.notes: Flowering plants have peak water demand. Monitor closely.

tasks.description: Peak demand. Every 2-3 days.
tasks.name: Refill reservoir

tasks.description: Remove fan leaves blocking bud sites.
tasks.name: Defoliate for airflow

tasks.description: Continue flushing surface salts every 2 weeks.
tasks.name: Top-water flush

common_problems.cause: Dense buds + humidity from wicking surface
common_problems.solution: Defoliate. Good airflow. Mulch reduces surface evaporation. Keep humidity < 50%.

common_problems.cause: Peak consumption from large flowering plant
common_problems.solution: Larger reservoir. Float valve auto-fill. Top-feed supplement if needed.

stages.description: Peak bud development. Maximum nutrient and water demand. Wicking bed's consistent moisture shines here — no wet/dry stress means better bud quality.

environment_overrides.notes: Ideal rain protection. Monitor humidity.
Rain on buds = rot. Cover during rain events.

extra_tasks.description: Cover buds during rain.
extra_tasks.name: Rain protection

stages.name: Mid Flower (Bulk Phase)

nutrients.approach: Full bloom nutrients. Consider PK booster weeks 4-6 of flower.

reservoir.notes: Peak consumption. Large plants may need daily checks.

tasks.description: Every 2 days. Peak demand period.
tasks.name: Refill reservoir

tasks.description: Check for rot, pests, deficiencies.
tasks.name: Monitor bud development

tasks.description: Trellis or stakes for heavy colas.
tasks.name: Support heavy buds

common_problems.cause: Light too intense or too warm
common_problems.solution: Slightly reduce light intensity. Ensure night temps drop for color development.

stages.description: Final ripening. Trichomes maturing. Reduce nutrient strength. Wicking bed's gentle delivery suits this phase well — no dramatic swings.

environment_overrides.notes: Can manipulate temp drop for color/terpenes.
Fall temps help color development. Watch for early frost.

stages.name: Late Flower (Ripening)

nutrients.approach: Reduce to 75% bloom. Stop PK boosters. Some growers begin reducing for final flush.

reservoir.notes: Consumption may slightly decrease as plant ripens.

tasks.description: Magnification. Looking for milky with some amber.
tasks.name: Check trichomes

tasks.description: Lower reservoir strength. Preparing for flush.
tasks.name: Reduce nutrients

tasks.description: Daily inspection of dense buds.
tasks.name: Inspect for bud rot

common_problems.cause: Organic soil has too many stored nutrients
common_problems.solution: Continue flush longer. Living soil may not fully flush — that's OK.

stages.description: Final salt flush. Fill reservoir with plain pH'd water. Top-water flush heavily from above to push salts down and out through overflow. Wicking beds flush beautifully — top-water pushes through entire soil column.

environment_overrides.notes: Standard flush procedure.
Rain helps flush naturally. Let it rain on the bed.

stages.name: Flush

nutrients.approach: Plain water only. Flush all nutrients from reservoir and soil.

reservoir.notes: Plain pH'd water only. No nutrients. Drain and refill reservoir with clean water.

tasks.description: Empty nutrient water. Refill with plain pH'd water.
tasks.name: Drain and refill reservoir

tasks.description: Flush from top to push salts through entire bed and out overflow.
tasks.name: Heavy top-water flush

tasks.description: Yellowing fan leaves = plant using stored nutrients. Good sign.
tasks.name: Monitor plant fade

common_problems.cause: Impatience or unclear trichome reading
common_problems.solution: Use 60x loupe. Target 70-80% milky, 10-20% amber.

stages.description: Chop plant. Wicking bed can be left to rest or immediately prepared for next cycle (refill, amend soil, plant cover crop).

environment_overrides.notes: Harvest and dry indoors.
Harvest before frost. Bring inside to dry.

stages.name: Harvest

nutrients.approach: None.

reservoir.notes: Empty or leave with plain water for cover crop / next cycle prep.

tasks.description: Cut at base. Leave roots in soil (they decompose and feed biology).
tasks.name: Chop plant

tasks.description: Preference. Wet trim for faster dry, dry trim for slower cure.
tasks.name: Wet or dry trim

tasks.description: Top-dress fresh amendments. Plant cover crop. Or let rest.
tasks.name: Prep bed for next cycle

common_problems.cause: Humidity too low or temp too high
common_problems.solution: Slow down: lower temp, raise humidity, larger whole-plant hangs.

stages.description: Standard hang dry. 60°F, 60% RH, complete darkness. Slow dry preserves terpenes.

environment_overrides.notes: Dry indoors, not in greenhouse.
Always dry indoors in controlled environment.

stages.name: Drying

nutrients.approach: None.

reservoir.notes: Bed resting. Can prep for next cycle during this time.

tasks.description: 60-65°F, 55-62% RH. Complete darkness.
tasks.name: Check drying conditions

tasks.description: Small stems snap = ready for jars.
tasks.name: Stem snap test

transition_signals: Buds slightly crispy outside, still moist inside

common_problems.cause: Jarred too wet — anaerobic bacteria
common_problems.solution: Dump out, dry another 12-24h, re-jar.

stages.description: Jar cure. Burp daily for 2 weeks, then weekly. Minimum 2-week cure, 4-8 weeks ideal.

environment_overrides.notes: Cure indoors.
Cure indoors in stable environment.

stages.name: Curing

nutrients.approach: None.

reservoir.notes: Bed resting or already growing next cycle.

tasks.description: Daily for 2 weeks, then weekly.
tasks.name: Burp jars

tasks.description: 58-62% in jars. Boveda 62% packs help.
tasks.name: Monitor humidity

autoflower.description: Ruderalis-hybrid strains that flower automatically regardless of light schedule. Fixed timeline.

light_schedule.flower: 20/4 or 18/6 (same as veg — no change needed)
light_schedule.transition: Automatic — plant decides when to flower (usually week 3-4)

autoflower.name: Autoflower
autoflower.notes: Fixed timeline — stress cannot be recovered by extending veg. Start in final pot (no transplant shock). LST preferred over HST.

training_suitability.defoliation: light only — auto can't recover from heavy defol
training_suitability.topping: risky — only if plant is vigorous

photoperiod.description: Standard cannabis strains that require 12/12 light to flower. Grower controls timing.

light_schedule.transition: Switch to 12/12 when plant is ~50% of desired final height

photoperiod.name: Photoperiod
photoperiod.notes: Can be vegged as long as desired. Cloneable. More forgiving of stress (can extend veg to recover).

training_suitability.defoliation: good
training_suitability.topping: excellent

issues.cause: Reservoir too small for plant size or hot conditions
issues.solution: Larger reservoir, float valve auto-fill, or more frequent refills.

issues.cause: Anaerobic conditions from stagnant organic matter
issues.solution: Drain and refill with clean water. Add air stone. Avoid organic teas in reservoir.

issues.cause: Overflow drain too low or reservoir layer compromised
issues.solution: Check overflow height — should be at TOP of reservoir layer. Ensure no cracks/leaks below overflow.
issues.symptom: Reservoir won't fill (water runs out overflow immediately)

issues.cause: Capillary break — air gap between soil and reservoir, or media too coarse
issues.solution: Top-water heavily to re-establish capillary continuity. May need to re-pack media. Use finer-textured soil.
issues.symptom: Soil surface bone dry despite full reservoir

issues.cause: Overflow too high or media wicking too aggressively
issues.solution: Lower overflow height. Add coarser layer between reservoir and soil. Improve drainage in top layer.

issues.cause: Bed not level or wicking layer unevenly packed
issues.solution: Level the bed. May need to rebuild wicking layer for even distribution.
issues.symptom: Uneven moisture (wet on one side, dry on other)

issues.cause: Minerals wicking up and depositing via evaporation
issues.solution: Top-water flush. Scrape off crust. Mulch surface. Reduce reservoir nutrient concentration.
issues.symptom: White salt crust on soil surface

issues.cause: Salt buildup in soil from upward wicking
issues.solution: Heavy top-water flush (3x pot volume). Reset reservoir nutrients at lower concentration.

issues.cause: pH drift in reservoir or root zone
issues.solution: Check reservoir pH (target 6.0-6.8). Flush and reset.
issues.symptom: Deficiency symptoms despite nutrients in reservoir

issues.cause: Standing water accessible to egg-laying mosquitoes
issues.solution: Screen all openings with fine mesh. Add BTI dunks (safe for plants). Seal fill pipe cap.

issues.cause: Constantly moist surface attracts gnats
issues.solution: Mulch layer (deters egg-laying). Yellow sticky traps. Let surface dry between flushes. BTI in top-water.

issues.cause: Saturated zone too high — roots sitting in standing water
issues.solution: Check overflow drain position (defines saturation line). Top 4-6 inches MUST be above saturation.

cons: RO system cost and waste water
water_source_profiles.name: Reverse Osmosis (RO) Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Gold standard for precision growing. Always add CalMag first when using RO.
pros: Blank slate — total control over mineral content

dechlorination.chloramine: Does NOT gas off — must use Campden tablets or activated carbon filter
dechlorination.chlorine: Let sit uncovered 24 hours (gasses off) or use dechlorinator drops
dechlorination.how_to_tell: Check city water report. Most modern cities use chloramine.

water_source_profiles.name: Municipal Tap Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Good enough for most grows. Dechlorinate and pH-adjust. Get a water report from your city.

cons: May contain iron, sulfur, or heavy metals
water_source_profiles.name: Well Water
water_source_profiles.notes: MUST get a water test before using. High iron or sulfur can cause lockout. Great if your well has clean water with moderate minerals.

water_source_profiles.name: Distilled Water
water_source_profiles.notes: Same as RO but purchased instead of filtered. Only practical for 1-2 plant grows.
